Ancient Monuments Bill (S).
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
125 Ancient Monuments Bill-

4 MAY 1926
Committee.
T
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND AVA
in the Chair.
CLAUSE 1 (Appointment of Ministry of
Finance as guardian of ancient monuments).
(1) The owner of any monument which
appears to the Ministry of Finance to be an
ancient monument within the meaning of
this Act may by deed appoint that
Ministry, with its consent, guardian of the
monument.
(2) Where the owner of a monument
appoints the Ministry of Finance to be
guardian of a monument, any person
entitled to any estate or interest in the
monument, whether such estate or interest
is the subject of a settlement or not, may
join in the deed of appointment, or may at
any time after the execution of the deed of
appointment execute a separate deed, for
the purpose of making more effective the
appointment of the Ministry of Finance as
guardian of the monument.
(3) Every such deed, to the extent to
which it is expressed to operate, and can
operate, under this Act, binds every person
deriving title from any person executing it;
and where the site of a monument or any
estate or interest therein is the subject of
a settlement, and a person executing the
deed is a person having a power of sale of
the settled land, such deed binds every
person entitled to an estate, interest or
charge subsisting or to arise under the
settlement, but subject to, and with the
exception of,-
(i) an estate, interest or charge having
priority to the settlement;
(ii) any estate, interest or charge which
has been conveyed or created for
securing money actually raised at the
date of the deed;
(iii) all leases, grants of easements or
other rights or privileges, granted or
made before the date of the deed by
the person executing the deed or by
any of his predecessors in title or by
any trustees for him or them under
the settlement or under any statutory
power, or being otherwise binding on
the successors of the person executing
the deed.
(4) For the purposes of this Act the expression "owner," where used in relation
to a monument, means any person entitled
to any estate or interest in the site of the
monument who, by virtue of such estate
or interest, is entitled to the actual possession of the monument, and includes any
body corporate, local authority, corporation, trustees for charities, or commissioners or trustees for ecclesiastical, collegiate or other public purposes.
(5) Except as provided by this Act, the
right to the possession of a monument or
lands adjoining thereto shall not be affected
by the appointment of the Ministry of
Finance as guardian under this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: The first Amendment which
I have to move this afternoon is in Clause
1, page 1, line 8, after the word "Finance,"
(Ministry of Finance) to insert the words
"in this Act referred to as 'the Ministry.'
The object of moving the insertion of these
words is to enable us to get rid of the
repetition of the two words "of Finance"
throughout the Bill. The expression "Ministry of Finance" occurs 37 times in the
Bill, and I think the Committee will agree
there is no object in repeating two words 37
times when the insertion of seven or eight
words in the First Clause will remove the
necessity. After all, there ought to be some
little consistency in our practice, and in
searching for a precedent I found that our
Livestock Breeding Act of 1922, in which so
many of the Members of this Committee
take so great an interest, would have contained the expression "Ministry of Agriculture" 35 times had not the expedient been
taken of referring to it as "The Ministry"
and inserting in the Bill words such as I
now beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Before I reply to the specific Amendment of the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie), perhaps I may be allowed, by leave of the
House, to make a brief statement as regards
these Amendments. I think it will relieve
the task of the House if I make some statement as to the attitude of the Government
towards these Amendments. There are, I
think I am correct in saying, something over
a hundred on the Order Paper. In the first
place, the Committee will see that many of
these Amendments are purely matters of
drafting and deal-if the hon. Senator who
has tabled them will forgive me for saying
so-with minor matters which are not really
very important. I shall, therefore, confine
myself to the chief questions of principle
which the hon. Senator has raised. The
Government is not prepared to accept an extension to local authorities of powers practically equal to those of the Ministry of
Finance.
Mr. LESLIE: On a point of Order, is it
in order for the noble Viscount to deal with
the Amendments before they are moved?
The CHAIRMAN: I have considered that
question, and I think the noble Viscount is
quite in order.
127 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: As I was saying, the Government is
not prepared to accept an extension to local
authorities of powers practically equal to
those of the Ministry of Finance. The local
authorities themselves have made no such
demand, although the Bill has now been for
five months before the public. We do not
propose in this Bill to deprive the County
Councils of any powers which they have
already exercised, and I shall at a later
stage, if the House appears to be strongly in
favour of such a course, introduce an
Amendment designed to enable County Councils to become guardians of monuments
which, though not perhaps of national importance may be considered by them of great
local interest. With regard to the National
Trust, this body was consulted and expressed
no wish to be included in this Bill.
The Government cannot agree to the hon.
Senator's proposal that an intention to excavate in the neighbourhood of a listed
ancient monument shall not be notifiable to
the Ministry of Finance. If the hon. Senator
will read the annals of archæology he will
find that incalculable harm may be done by
injudicious excavation. As regards the
Amendments to Clause 6, we consider they
will be rendered unnecessary by an official
Amendment the Government is putting forward which will ensure that Parliament can
at the very outset, if it so chooses, summarily revoke a Preservation Order. The
Government is in agreement with the hon.
Senator's proposed Amendment in Clause 12.
The CHAIRMAN: I hope the noble Viscount is not going to deal with the individual
Amendments.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: As regards the specific Amendment
moved by the hon. Senator, this matter has
been considered by the drafting authority,
and it is considered that the form used in the
Bill is the established practice, and is better
than the words proposed by the hon. Senator,
which the Government is not prepared to
accept.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I should like to ask
the noble Viscount to tell me quite definitely
whether we are to understand that the
Government in future always intend to repeat
the full title of a Ministry throughout the
Bill. I cited to the Committee the precedent
of the Livestock Breeding Act in which the
course taken was not the course taken in this
Bill. I cannot, I confess, see any object in
printing and re-printing both in the Bills
and in the Statute Book seventy unnecessary
words. We are always talking about
economy, and surely here is an opportunity
to exercise economy. It seems to me that as
we have set a precedent in the Livestock
Breeding Act we may just as well follow it.
There are, of course, other precedents, and I
cannot understand why the noble Viscount
objects to this Amendment. Perhaps other
hon. Senators will give their opinions:
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Senator has asked me
whether we will continue this practice in
future Bills. Naturally, I cannot bind myself
as to what the Government may do in future
Bills, but we consider that in this Bill it will
be better to leave the words as they are.
Mr. LESLIE: That being so, I withdraw
the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Clause 1, page 1, line 10, leave out "that
Ministry" and insert "the Ministry."
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 1, at
the end of Sub-Section (1) to insert the following new Sub-Section :-
"(2) The owner of any monument which
appears to a Local Authority to be an
ancient monument within the meaning of
this Act, and is situate in or in the vicinity
of their area may, with the consent of the
Local Authority, constitute them by deed
guardians of the monument."
The words on the Paper are taken from the
English Act of 1913. In that Act they form
the Second Sub-Section of Section 3, and the
object of the Amendment which I have put
on the Paper is to enable a local authority
to continue to exercise in the future the
powers which it has been able to exercise in
the past. That is, to enable it to act as
guardian of what I would call a new monu.
ment, to acquire the guardianship of a monument which is not at the moment under their
guardianship. When speaking on the Second
Reading of this Bill, I pointed out that as the
funds at the disposal of the Government or
the Ministry of Finance are apparently
limited to £600 a year, and if the intention
was to preserve ancient monuments it seems
rather curious that instead of extending the
number of authorities who might help them,
the Government should restrict the probable
area of charge, the result being that if the
Ministry of Finance does not preserve public
monuments this Bill will effectually prevent
anybody else from doing so.
The proposal here is to extend to a local
authority-that is a Council of a County or
City or of an Urban District-the same
powers which have recently been given them
in England. The only difference is that in
England the powers are given to a Borough
Council, but not to an Urban Council. In
England a County Council can become the
129 Ancient Monuments Bill-

4 MAY 1926
Committee.
JE
guardian of a monument and so can the
Council of a City. I do not know what view
other hon. Senators entertain on this matter
but it seems to me that a County Council
will accept guardianship if allowed to do so
if in the opinion of the members of that
County Council there is a demand on the
part of their constituents that they should
do so.
If the constituents do not want their
County Council to become the guardian then
in all probability the County Council as an
elected body will not do it. I confess I cannot see any reason for confining this work
to the Ministry of Finance when the Ministry admittedly has not got the money to do
the work properly. My proposal means extension, and I should have thought that in
the interests of the preservation of monuments, and because it is based strictly on the
last Imperial Act, this Amendment would
have been accepted. I beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I do not
understand exactly what the hon. Senator
(Senator Leslie) means by the words "local
authority." So far as I can see his Amendments propose to strike out the words
"County Council" everywhere they occur
in the Bill, and to substitute the words "local
authority." I am at a loss to understand
the reason for doing so, and I shall vote
against the Amendment.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I would be prepared
to support the Amendment, because a case in
point occurs to me. I do not know whether
the ancient walls of Londonderry would be
considered an ancient monument, but, if so,
I certainly think the Corporation of Londonderry is the proper authority to maintain
those walls. That seems to me to be a perfect argument in support of the Amendment,
and I would be prepared to support it on
those grounds without going any further.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to oppose the
Amendment, and I do so advisedly, because
I do not consider that the local authorities
are competent to take the place of guardians
of these monuments, which must of necessity
be discovered and application made for their
preservation from all classes of the community who are far better qualified than local
representative men to seek and preserve
such monuments. In addition, I know that
throughout the country the local authorities
are not even wide awake, and when certain
Measures were delegated by the Imperial
Parliament to local authorities in which
their own economic interest and prosperity
were bound up-and I refer particularly to
the appointment of Inspectors under the
Agricultural and Technical Instruction Act
-they resisted, and bitterly resisted, everything calculated to benefit themselves as well
as the community as a whole.
I do not wish to take up the time of the
Committee, but I know of one instance
where an Inspector was sent to Ireland and
the local authorities without exception practically demanded his dismissal. They were
not sufficiently well educated to appreciate
the value of the theories this man was propounding to them in their own interests.
The man in question afterwards occupied
a Chair in the Queen's University and now
occupies a Chair in the University of Cork.
The CHAIRMAN: I am not quite certain
that the hon. Senator is not going far away
from the Amendment we are discussing.
Mr. DORMAN: I am trying to give an illustration-although it may be in a crude way
of how the local authorities acted when a
matter was put before them for their consideration. They resisted it, but I will not pursue
that matter any further. I hope the Committee will not consent to taking the power
from the Ministry, who will have the benefit
of the advice of the most competent authorities in Ireland.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to Senator Leslie, I
would say on behalf of the Government, that
it is not prepared to adopt this Sub-Section,
principally for the reason that there has
been no demand shown for it up to the
present by local authorities. As I said
before, the Government do propose if the
Committee desires it, to give certain powers
to the County Councils at a later stage of the
Bill. As regards the historic walls of Derry,
I understand, that, under the Public Health
Act, the Corporation of Derry has power to
look after those walls. As the local authorities show no desire at all to take over these
monuments or be saddled with them, the
Government do not propose to accept this
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: There are two or three
points that have been raised on this Amendment. Perhaps I may deal first with the
point raised py the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman (Colonel Sharmaa Crawford). He
asked what was meant by local authority,
and he rather complained that I had taken
out of the Bill the words "County Council"
and substituted "local authorities." I should
like to explain to the right hon. and
gallant Gentleman that the expression "local
authority" is a Council of a County or of a
City or of an Urban District. That is why
the words "County Council" were removed.
There was no disrespect intended to the
County Councils, who have already done a
certain amount of work in this direction.
The point was made that the local
authorities have not asked for these powers.
Have they had an opportunity? The
Association of County Councils was never

Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
.
[Mr. Leslie]
consulted before this Bill was brought in,
and was not given an opportunity of expressing an opinion. I do not know that there is
anything else to be said in reply. I must not
at this stage introduce any new argument,
but the point was made by Senator Dorman
that there was a suggestion of taking power
My
away from the Ministry of Finance.
It
Amendment does nothing of the kind.
gives a local authority the power of being
appointed the guardian if somebody will give
them the guardianship. It may be that the
possessor of a monument might be prepared
to entrust it to the guardianship of a County
Council, whom he knows, but not to the

whom he does not know.
Mr. LONG: I do not know that any possible harm could be done by the Government
accepting the Amendment. I can vouch for
the fact that local authorities have not had
an opportunity of pronouncing on this Bill,
and have not even heard of it. No opportunity has been given them of making any
demand for it. I support the Amendment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In all matters of this sort I am in
the hands of the Committee, and if the Committee desires to have this Amendment inserted I must accept the ruling of the Committee.
So far as the Government is concerned it does
not consider the Amendment is necessary for
the reason I have stated. We do propose to
grant the county councils certain powers
later on if the Committee desires it, but the
Government is not anxious to accept this
Amendment.
Did the
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
noble Viscount say he intended to move an

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I propose to move an Amendment on

become guardians of ancient monuments.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I cannot see why the

He complains that the county councils have
had this Bill before them for five months,
but he has not put his Amendment on the
the Paper.
There
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
will be a difficulty in my own county if this
Amendment is adopted. There is one of the
most important ancient monuments in Ireland at Dundrum, in County Down, and if
this Amendment is passed there will be
divided control of that monument, because
the Down County Council will come in as

will also come in as guardian.
Mr. LESLIE: There is nothing in this

On Question, Amendment negatived.

Mr. LESLIE: These Amendments

Amendments not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: Here are two small Amendments I will put together. The first one is
in page 2, line 6, to leave out "binds" (binds
every person) and insert "shall bind," and
the second is in page 2, line 10, to leave out
"binds" (such deed binds) and insert, "shall
bind." I do not know whether it is a misprint or a new idea, but to use the expression "binds" in dealing with a future matter
is not customary. I have studied the
English Law of Property Act, 1922, and I
find the words "shall bind" is always used
when referring to the future, and I think
we ought to follow that precedent.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The phraseology "binds" is that
used in the Settled Land Act, the draft of
which was supervised by that very wellknown Ulsterman, Lord Cairns, who was
Lord Chancellor of England. I think the
Committee may confidently assume that this
word is considered more suitable than the
words suggested by the hon. Senator, and for
that
the Government propose to
leave it in the Bill.
reason
Mr. LESLIE: I have read the Settled
Land Act, and my opinion was that we
should follow the latest authority.
But if
the Noble Viscount wants to go back nearly
half-a-century I do not propose to press the
Amendments.
Amendments by leave withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 2,
line 28, to leave out "means" (to a monument, means) and insert, "includes." This
is a much more difficult matter than that
we have just considered. This Amendment
is really one of three which come together.
The difficulty, as I explained on the Second
Reading, is this. As the Sub-Clause stands,
we have limited the expression "owner" to
the man who is in possession of the monument at the moment. That may be the
133 Ancient Monuments Bill-

4 MAY 1926
Committee.
intention. There is no strictly corresponding Clause or Section in the English Act of
1913. The nearest approach to it is Section
5, Sub-Section 1, of the English Act, where
the words are, "for the purpose of this part
of this Act." I wondered at first whether it
was a misprint here, but I see on looking
closely at the Bill that the type has evidently
been altered and I do not think that can be
the intention.
I think that the Sub-clause is not quite
adapted to our purpose as it stands, and indeed I have some doubt as to whether I
should not ask the Committee to leave it
out altogether in order that the draftsman
should bring up a new Sub-clause at a later
stage, so that it might come with the
other definitions towards the close of the Bill
as is the more ordinary practice.
The difficulty, of course, really arises from
the use of the word "owner." It is to deal
with that difficulty I make the suggestion.
In order that the Committee may follow I
am afraid I must ask them to join with me
in hunting this word "owner" in relation to
the monument through the Bill. We find
that the word "owner" again occurs in the
proviso to Sub-clause (1) of this same Clause,
and there it gives no difficulty whatever for
the Sub-clause, following the English Clause,
provides that if a man who was the owner
ceases to be the owner somebody else becomes the owner, and then the power to
maintain the monument may be determined
by notice. Therefore, no difficulty arises
there. The moment somebody else becomes
the owner who is not bound by the Bill the
power of the Ministry is at an end. That is
so also in England.
to
We find the expression again in Sub-clause
(1) of the fifth Clause, and that is where
the term "owner" seems to me to give some
difficulty, because Clause 5 is intended
work towards the protection of monuments
by getting a list. The second Sub-Clause provides that the owner of the monument shall
notify the Ministry before he proceeds to do
various things. Now that owner is again the
man in possession. Directly that man ceases
to be in possession a difficulty arises again,
because somebody else then becomes the
owner under the Act, and that somebody else
is not bound-if we stand by the definition
that I am now taking-is not bound by what
has happened before. Again we find the expression next when dealing with preservation orders in the second Sub-clause of
Section 7, where it provides that if owing
to the neglect of the owner of the monument
"the Ministry may," and so on. What is to
happen to the new owner, the man round the
corner?
Again in Clause 12 where we are dealing
with public access you get the owner, and
you get it again in Clause 13. The Ancient
Monuments Committee may if referred to by
the owner, that is, the man in possession,
give advice free of charge. If the man who
is the real owner, that is to say, the absolute
owner subject to a lease or tenancy, wants
advice apparently he cannot have it. We
get the owner again in the 15th Clause where
the Ministry may give advice, and superintendence if invited to do so by the owner.
Apparently the ultimate owner is not included there either. It therefore seems to
me that the Sub-clause as it stands is not
suited to our purpose. It breaks down somewhere.
I must confess that I am not at all certain
that my suggested alteration would make it
completely satisfactory. I therefore think
it might be better to make the motion any
way rather than to suggest that the Clause
should be cut out altogether. I think it
does require attention in some form or another. For the reasons I have given accordingly I beg to move the first Amendment,
that is, to leave out "means" and insert
"includes" to make "owner" cover both the
man in possession and the man coming in.
If we had provided under the Bill for a
change of ownership in the proviso at the beginning of line 5, page 3, I should not be in
any difficulty. As it is, I beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Member as far as I understand considers that the word "means" is
not sufficiently broad. He wishes to insert
the word "includes." We have inserted the
word "means," because the intention is to
define precisely the owner to the exclusion
of other interpretations. For that reason
we have inserted that word in the Clause.
Therefore, the Government are not inclined
to accept this Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask how it is proposed to meet the point I raised about the
gap in Clause 5?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I may tell the hon. Member that
this definition was considered carefully with
the best legal advice we have got. The best
legal advice is that the word was the most
suitable word to be inserted.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure that the Committee will wish the Amendment to be withdrawn. If necessary it can be considered
again. It follows of course that the remaining Amendments to Clause 1 fall also.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
The following Amendments to Clause 1
stood on the Order Paper in the name of
Mr. Leslie:-
Line 29, after "person" to insert "body
corporate, local authority, corporation,

trustees for charities, or commissioners or

Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Effect of Guardianship)—
(1) Where the Ministry of Finance has been
appointed guardian of any monument under
this Act, that Ministry shall maintain the
monument and shall have access by its
inspectors, agents or workmen to the monument for the purposes of inspecting it and
of bringing such materials and doing such
acts and things as may be required for its
maintenance:
Provided that, if a person who is not
bound by the deed by which the Ministry
of Finance was appointed guardian becomes
owner of the monument, the power of that
Ministry to maintain the monument may
be determind by notice in writing signed
by such person and served on that Ministry,
and such notice shall operate from the
date of the service thereof.
(2) The expression "maintenance" in this
section includes the fencing, repairing and
covering in of a monument and the doing
of any other act or thing which may be
required for the purpose of repairing the
monument or protecting it from decay or
injury, and the expression "maintain"
shall be construed accordingly.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 2, line 39, after "Finance" insert
"or a local authority."
Line 41, after " Ministry" insert "or the
local authority as the case may be."
Amendments not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: The first two Amendments
to Clause 2 have already been disposed of by

The next one is on page 3, line 1. I think
I must ask leave to withdraw that Amendment also to leave out "its" and insert
"their," also the remaining three local
authority Amendments on that page.

CLAUSE 3 (Purchase of Ancient monuments by agreement).
The Ministry of Finance may purchase
by agreement any monument which appears
to it to be an ancient monument within
the meaning of this Act.
The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie on the Order Paper :-
Page 3, line 20, after "Finance" to insert "or a local authority.
Mr. LESLIE: That goes also.
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Gift, devise or bequest of
Ancient Monuments to Ministry of Finance).
Any person may, by deed or will, give,
devise or bequeath to the Ministry of
Finance the estate or interest in any ancient
monument of which he may be seised or
possessed, and that Ministry may accept
any such gift, devise or bequest if, in the
opinion of that Ministry, it is expedient
to do so.

Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-

"or a local authority."
Line 26, after "Ministry" to insert "or a

Line 27, after "Ministry" to insert "or a
local authority."

The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Samuel Cunningham:-
Page 3, line 28, at end add "Property in
Northern Ireland bequeathed to the National
Trust for places of Historic Interest or
Natural Beauty will for Duties be treated as
if the National Trust, etc., is a Society

Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Notice to be given by owners
of certain monuments).
(1) The Ministry of Finance shall from
time to time cause to be prepared and published a list or lists containing
4 MAY 1926
Ancient Monuments Bill-

Committee.
J
whatever. It is scarcely possible to describe
anything of the kind which I understand the
Government has in mind as really of national
importance. The term is too strong.
(a) such monuments as are reported by
the Advisory Committee constituted under
this Act (in this Act referred to as "the
Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee") as being ancient monuments the
preservation of which is of national importance; and
(b) such other ancient monuments as the
Ministry of Finance think fit;
and that Ministry shall, when it is intended to include a monument in the list,
notify the owner of the monument of such
intention, and of the penalties which may
be incurred by a person guilty of an offence
under the next succeeding sub-section.
(2) Where the owner of any ancient monument which is included in any such list of
monuments as aforesaid, after being notified as provided in the last preceding subsection, proposes to demolish or remove in
whole or in part, alter structurally, or
make additions to, the monument, or make
excavations in the neighbourhood thereof,
he shall forthwith give notice of his intention to the Ministry of Finance, and
shall not, except in the case of urgent necessity, commence any work of demolition, removal, alteration, addition or excavation,
for a period of two months after having
given such notice; and any person guilty
of a contravention of or non-compliance
with this provision shall be liable, on conviction under the Summary Jurisdiction
(Ireland) Acts, to a fine not exceeding one
hundred pounds, or to imprisonment for a
term not exceeding three months, or to
both.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move on page 3,
line 32, to leave out "or lists" ("published
a list or lists"). I wanted to ask the noble
Viscount why the expression "or lists" was
inserted. I think there must be some meaning, because the English Act uses the
singular. I put down the Amendment to ask
the question, and so draw attention to this
matter.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The reason why we put in "or lists"
is because "or lists"" implies a succession.
There may be more than one. There have
been several lists in England.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
as
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 3, line
34, to leave out "are reported by" ("such
monuments
are reported by the
Advisory"). This and the next Amendment
practically go together. I approach here the
difficult expression "national importance"
about which I spoke on the Second Reading.
If the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee deal with those which are of national
importance, it seems to me that for practical
purposes your Committee will be of no service
The CHAIRMAN: I am sorry. The Amendment is to leave out "or reported by." Is
that your Amendment?
Mr. LESLIE: That is the first of the
Amendments.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD. It is a misprint.
Mr. LESLIE: It is a misprint. It should
be not "or" but "are." The difficulty is that
the term national importance is so very
strong that it practically shuts out anyone.
Also I do not think that it is for the Parliament of Northern Ireland to use the expression "national" if it can help it, because
after all if Ireland is not a nation neither is
Northern Ireland. It is necessary to find some
other expression which really does indicate
what we mean. It is rather difficult to find
anything in the ordinary Statute Book dealing with the matter. We find a reference in
the National Trust Act which incorporates
the National Trust for Places of Historic
Interest. I find there in Section 21, Subsection 4, an expression which it seems to me
would meet our case. The expression is that
which occurs in my next Amendment, that
is, "may by resolution determine to be
ancient monuments which are proper to be
held for preservation for the benefit of the
people." It is really in view of the longer
Amendment that I move the first to get rid
of the words "are reported by." I do not
think that I need say anything more
at the moment as regards this subsequent
Amendment on the same Clause, but I hope
that the authors may help us as to what they
mean or what they want to mean by national
importance, for unless we give our Advisory
Committee some line by which they can consider that expression they cannot help us
very much. I beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I do not quite follow why Senator
Leslie wishes these words, 'are reported by,"
left out. Apparently it refers to the following
Amendment, which deals with ancient monuments of national importance. The term
"national importance" is one which is used
in the English Act of 1913. I think it is a
word which is understood by everybody, and
I do not see that we should benefit the Bill
in any way, or make it any better, by using
the much longer expression. Therefore, I am
not prepared to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Again I can only say that
if the Government desires to do everything it
can to stifle its own Bill I can do nothing
but withdraw the Amendment.
139 Ancient Monuments Bill-

Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-

Page 4, line 1, after "Committee" (Monuments Advisory Committee), insert, may
by resolution determine to be ancient
monuments which are proper to be held for
preservation for the benefit of the people of
Northern Ireland."
Line 1. leave out from "Committee" to
"and" in line 3.
Amendments not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave out
from the beginning of line 6 (and that
Ministry shall), to "of" in line 8, and
insert:-
Provided that, before any monument is
included in any such list, the Ministry
shall give the owner of the monument (if
such owner's address be known or ascertainable) an opportunity of being heard, and
shall notify such owner of his right to
compensation, and”
These words are intended to ensure for the
owner, whatever class of owner he may be,
an opportunity of being heard before his
monument is put on the list. It may be
remembered that when these matters were
discussed in the House of Lords in 1912 and
1913 a great point was made of the fact that
it was apparently proposed to list a man's
monument without consulting him on the subject, and it was thought that that was not
right. When these matters were under consideration in England the question of compensation was not, perhaps, so necessarily important as it is here. In Northern Ireland,
as we know, we cannot take property of any
kind without compensation. If we cannot
take property neither can we take control of
that property without compensation. You
may be imposing a pecuniary loss on him
by assuming control of his property.
It might be a monument he could sell by
transporting elsewhere. It has been possible,
as the Committee will remember, to move
such things as Temple Bar and Cleopatra's
Needle. As the Committee will also remember, we are legislating here only for
Northern Ireland. We cannot
go beyond
that, and if a man wants to sell his monument it seems to me that before the monument is put on the list the man should be
given an opportunity of saying anything he
may have to say, and he should also have the
right to compensation if the monument is
put on the list without his consent.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: On second thoughts, perhaps, the
hon. Senator will see that this Amendment
is not necessary. It goes somewhat beyond
the English Act of 1913, and it seems to me
it will create delay. As I understand the
matter, it would not be an advantage to the
owner at all and the Government are not
prepared to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I cannot see that the
delay on which the noble Viscount lays such
stress is of such importance. After all there
is some sort of postal system in Northern
Ireland. I reminded the House the other
day that Lord Curzon drew attention to this
point in the House of Lords. However, if the
noble Viscount is very anxious not to insert
these words, and if nobody else is anxious
that they should be inserted I shall not
detain the Committee any longer.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: There is no great point in this
Amendment, but I am prepared to reconsider it with the object of seeing if some
other Amendment can be drawn up for the
Report Stage.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in lines 9
and 10, to leave out "the next succeeding
sub-section," and insert "this Act." This
raises another question. We provide in the
following Clause a penalty for offences
under the next succeeding sub-section. I
suggest that instead of dealing here with
offences under the next sub-section we should
ruake it an offence under this Act, because
that would provide for the possibility of
offences committed under Clause 7 when a
monument is under a Preservation Order.
As the matter stands at present, there is
no provision for imposing a penalty for
contravention of Clause 7 by a man who is
the owner of a nonument. Therefore, it is
desirable to alter the words as I suggest,
but if there is any objection it is not a
point I should desire to press.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I gather that what the hon. Member proposes is to fix a single general
penalty for all offences under the Act. If
that is so the Government cannot accept the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I will withdraw the Amendment. I am not very keen on it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 16
to leave out from "monument" to "he" in
line 17 (or make excavations in the neighbourhood thereof). There is no such provision in the English Act, and the English Act,
I think, ought to be referred to. The noble
Viscount refers to it now and again when
he likes it, and he does not refer to it when
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Committee.
4 MAY
he does not like it. In the English Act this
provision is not included, and I cannot help
thinking the reason it was not included in
the English Act was because it was not
wanted. There is quite sufficient protection
given elsewhere in the Bill to meet this case
of excavation. If you leave it as it stands
let us see what it says. "Or make excavations in the neighbourhood thereof." What
is meant by neighbourhood? Half an acre,
half a mile, or half a county? How is it
proposed to justify interference with the man
to whom the monument belongs if he wants
to conduct his own excavations? Why should
he not do so?
The English Act does not contain these
words, and I cannot see that we ought to
retain them. We must recognise some rights
of the owner. I know it may be said that
excavations, except under the control of
the Minister of Finance, may lead to awful
results, but I have yet to learn that the
Ministry of Finance is such a universally
received authority as to justify us setting
them up against the original rights of property. In moving the Amendment I direct
particular attention to the expression
"neighbourhood."
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: At any rate to water down this subsection very much. The learned societies
is to put an absolute embargo on any excavations.
Mr. LESLIE: Oh, no.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: At any rate, to water down this subsection very much. The learned societies
here are very strong on the point that excavation in injudicious hands may do irreparable damage to a monument, and
muddle up the neighbourhood to such an extent that scientific excavations would be impossible. As regards the expression "neighbourhood," I think the Committee may take
it that the Advisory Committee is not going
to strain at the meaning of that. It will
only mean the vicinity of the monument.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to support the inclusion of the Clause as it is. It is quite
possible that owing to an obsession a person
may do great harm to a monument, and
grievous harm has been done to one of the
most ancient monuments in Ireland by a
private owner. As a Socialist I do not stand
here as a supporter of property, or to speak
on behalf of property, but I do stand here to
try to show the absurdity of moving an
Amendment such as this.
One of the most ancient monuments in Ireland, which will be venerated by all Irishmen so long as Ireland retains the name of
Ireland, is where on the 1st May all the

lights of Ireland which were put out are relit under Druidical administration. The
owner of that monument was obsessed with
the British-Israelite movement, and got the
idea into his head that the Ark of the
Covenant was buried under Tara Hill.
The CHAIRMAN: I want to allow every
latitude for discussion, but I cannot see
that the Ark of the Covenant has anything
whatever to do with the Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I am
afraid I disagree with the Amendment on
the ground that such a monument is not the
property of one man, but the property of the
people of the country. I do think the owner
should be treated with all justice, but it is
much better that the country, the Government of the country and not the owner,
should be the chief object in these ancient
places.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount and his
allies are perhaps too strong for me.
Colonel CRAWFORD: I do not think it
is Parliamentary language to call us his
allies. We are not allies of the Government.
Mr. LESLIE : I did not wish to give any
offence. I was about to say more seriously
that the insertion of the words does not
really matter greatly, because the power of
the Ministry to deal with excavations is
limited. Therefore, I would ask leave to
withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 4,
line 17, to leave out from "forthwith" ("he
shall forthwith give notice") to "the" in
line 18 ("to the Ministry of Finance") and
to insert "so inform." This is a small
difficulty. I thought the Bill as we originally saw it had been altered in one place so
as to get rid of the juxtaposition of the
purpose and intention." I rather think
the point is a very small one. I think
having spoken here of the purpose to
demolish we ought not to go on to talk
about intention. Therefore, I suggest a
small alteration to leave out from "forthwith" and insert "so inform."

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This is a very small point, but still
I think the wording of the Government
draftsman is more suitable than the wording
of the hon. Senator.
Mr.
LESLIE : It is the inconsistency
I object to. That is all.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
143 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
Line 20, after "alteration" to insert "or."
Lines 20 and 21, to leave out "or excavation."
Amendments not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 21, to
leave out "two months" ("period of two
months after"), and insert "one month." I
suggest here that the Minister of Finance
ought to be able to make up his mind if he
has anything to say within a period of one
month, as had been originally proposed. The
Ministry should not take two months to deal
with Northern Ireland when Great Britain
manages to deal with Great Britain in one
month, and as the noble Viscount has just
reminded us how important haste is-that is
to say, we have not really got time to consult
the owner-I really cannot see why we want
two months instead of one month. You must
remember if a man really does want to commence any work of removal, alteration, or
addition, he may want to do it at once. I
presume, of course, that he will get compensation for the delay. But at all events I
suggest that we take out "two months" and
insert "one month," following the English
Act.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The reason why we have inserted
two months is that although it is not in the
Act, it was suggested by the authorities in
Great Britain as a more satisfactory period
than one month. That is the reason why we
considered two months better. There is not
very much in it. Two months is a better
period than one.

Mr. LESLIE: Really I am disposed to press
this matter. We really must have some regard to the different conditions. The
English authorities are not concerned with
legislation in Northern Ireland. I suggest
to the noble Viscount that one month "
should be inserted instead of "two months."
On Question, Amendment negatived.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Lines 21 and 22, leave out "having given
such notice" and insert "so informing the
Ministry."
Line 22, leave out from "notice" to the end
of sub-section (2).
Mr. LESLIE: This first Amendment has
already been dealt with, and the next one has
for practical purposes also been dealt with.
I may draw attention to it on the Report
Stage.
Amendments not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
New Clause:
The Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee may with the approval of the
Ministry publish from time to time a list
containing such monuments in Northern
Ireland as are vested in the Ministry, or
in a local authority or in the National
Trust, or of which the Ministry or a local
authority has been appointed guardian, or
in respect of which a Preservation Order
is in force.-(Mr. Leslie.)
Brought up, and read a First time.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move that this
Clause be read a Second time. The object of
it is to enable the Advisory Committee to let
the people in Northern Ireland know something about the monuments there really are.
As the matter stands at present we are in
the position that the ordinary person has no
knowledge of the monuments in Northern
Ireland which need the protection of the
Ministry or of the local authority or of the
National Trust. It appears to me that this
Clause would be of some use, and it is necessary. I have put it in as a new Clause, otherwise some other provisions I have suggested
would have become applicable to it. There
is no objection, I think, to including the
local authority in this new Clause. The local
authority may be the owners, that is to say,
they may have monuments vested in them
under the provisions of the Land Purchase
Acts. I think that this Clause might possibly be of some use, and subject to what
may be said by others, I do not see that it
does any serious harm.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I venture to think that this
Amendment is really not necessary. Any individual person who is interested in this
matter can get from the Finance Ministry a
list of the monuments which are of national
importance, and, in any case, if we were to
accept this Amendment the words " or in the
National Trust" could hardly be included.
The National Trust works, as the hon.
Senator knows, under Imperial Acts, and
has not expressed any desire to be included
in this Act of ours. I really do not think
that this new Clause is necessary.
Mr. LESLIE : The new Clause was intended to do something towards popularising
ancient monuments, but if the noble
Viscount objects I ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 6 (Orders placing ancient monuments under protection of Ministry of
Finance).
(1) If the Ancient Monuments Advisory
Committee report to the Ministry of
Finance that any monument is in danger
of destruction or removal or damage from
neglect or injudicious treatment, and that
the preservation of the monument is of
national importance, the Ministry of
Committee.
Ancient Monuments Bill-
4 MAY 1926
J.
r
Finance may, if it appears that the monument is an ancient monument within the
meaning of this Act, make an order (in
this Act referred to as a Preservation
Order) placing the monument under the
protection of that Ministry:
Provided that, if in any case the Ministry
of Finance considers that the making of
such an order is a matter of immediate
urgency, the order may be made without
receiving any such report as aforesaid.
(2) Where the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee have reason to believe
that any monument is in danger as aforesaid, and that the preservation of the
monument is of national importance, they
may themselves, or by any person authorised in writing by them, enter at any
reasonable time upon any premises for the
purposes of enabling them to determine by
inspection of the monument whether it is
proper for them to report to the Ministry
of Finance:
Provided that, unless the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee consider that
the inspection of the monument is a matter
of immediate urgency, they shall give not
less than seven clear days' notice to the occupier of the premises of their intention so
to enter upon them.
(3) A Preservation Order shall have effect
until revoked by the Ministry of Finance.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Page 4, line 32, to leave out "of national
importance" and insert "proper to be held
for preservation for the benefit of the people
of Northern Ireland."
Mr. LESLIE: This Amendment has already been dealt with and falls.
Amendment not moved.
The following Amendment also stood on
the Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Line 3, to leave out from "aforesaid" to
"they" in line 4.
Mr. LESLIE: I had intended to move an
Amendment there, but I think as things are
I had better not move that Amendment. We
will leave it as it is.
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 14, at
end ("so to enter upon them") to insert
(3) The Ministry shall forthwith notify the
owner of the monument and the occupier
of the premises wherein the monument is
situated of the making of a Preservation
Order under this section, and of his right
to apply for the revocation of such Order,
and of the penalties which may be incurred
under this Act.
(4) Any owner or occupier aggrieved by
the making of a Preservation Order under
this section may, within one month after
he has been notified under the last preceding sub-section, apply to the Ministry to
revoke such Order.
(5) On the receipt of such application the
Ministry shall either revoke the Order or
shall make a fresh Order providing that
the Order shall lapse eighteen months from
the time the first Order was made, unless
during such cighteen months the monument
shall be purchased by the Ministry by
agreement or a Bill for the compulsory
purchase of the monument shall be in
Parliament.
I suggest here these three sub-Clauses to
endeavour to meet the very difficult point of
taking dominion over another man's property
without the authority of Parliament. It will
be remembered that the English Act provides
that the Commissioners of Works with the
approval of the Treasury may make a Preservation Order in certain circumstances, and
that Preservation Order shall only be valid
for eighteen months unless in the meantime
it is confirmed by Parliament, and that the
Commissioners of Works may, if they so
desire, bring in a private Bill to confirm
the Preservation Order, and a private Bill so
brought in shall have the right to be referred
to a joint Committee, and the owner shall
be given an opportunity of being heard and
the ordinary procedure of a private Bill
Committee follows. When moving this sub-
Clause in England, Lord Beauchamp, who,
perhaps, we would not expect to find a very
strong supporter of the rights of private
property, pointed out that in his opinion
nothing short of the authority of Parliament gave complete protection. As the Bill
stands a Preservation Order can be made,
and apparently can be enforced. That is entirely unconstitutional, and I do not think
this Committee would be likely to allow that
to happen. I may say I endeavoured to find
out how many preservation Orders had been
made in England after the 1913 Act was
passed. I could not find one, but I since
learned that one was made in 1924.
I do
not think many preservation Orders are
likely to be made, but I do feel very strongly
it is quite important for us not to allow a
man to be deprived to some extent of his
property without the authority of Parliament. Accordingly, I move sub-Clauses first
of all, providing that where a preservation
Order is made the man whose monument it
applies to shall have the right to be notified
and shall subsequently have the right of
application to the Ministry for a revocation
of the Order, and, finally, if the Ministry
and the owner of the monument cannot agree,
that the Order shall not stay in force for
more than 18 months unless during that time
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
the monument is purchased or a Bill for its
purchase has been brought into Parliament.
I cannot agree that you can take a man's
monument unless you are prepared to pay.
That is the purpose of the sub-section I have
moved. Whether it may be accepted I do
not know. I do think the principle is of the
very greatest importance.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I agree with the principle embodied
in this Amend:nent. The hon. Senator
referred to the fact that there has only been
cne Preservation Order made since 1913, but
I think the reason for that must be very
obvious, namely, that Preservation Orders
are not made because of the necessity in
Great Britain of following them up by a
Bill before Parliament. There is an Amendment down in my name on behalf of the
Government, to insure that no injustice is
done to the owner of a monument, and I
consider that the Amendment, as set down
in my name is more suitable than the
Amendment moved by the hon. Senator.
My Amendment follows his Amendment on
the Paper, and does provide for Parliamentary sanction. Therefore, I would ask
the hon. Member to withdraw his Amendment and accept mine.
Mr. LESLIE: My Amendment puts the
onus of moving on the Minister, and the
noble Viscount's Amendment
proposes to
put the onus on the owner. The owner,
after all, is not represented in Parliament,
and the noble Viscount does not provide an
opportunity for him being heard, nor does
he provide anything in the nature of Private
Bill procedure. I do not know whether the
Committee would like me to withdraw this
Amendment in order that we might perhaps
re-consider it at a later stage. It is obvious
that some few words in the first of the new
sub-sections could not stand-the words dealing with penalties on account of alterations,
which the Government have not accepted in
Clause 5. This is a matter on which I would
ask hon. Senators to give us a little help, so
that we may see what really is their desire.
Mr. LONG: I think you might withdraw
your Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: The view seems to be that
the Amendment should be withdrawn.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, on page 5, lines 15
and 16, to leave out Sub-Section (3) and
insert:-

Every Preservation Order shall be laid
before both Houses of Parliament as soon
as may be after it is made, and if either
of those Houses within the statutory period
next after any Preservation Order is laid
before it by resolution annuls the same,
such Preservation Order shall forthwith
become void, but without prejudice to the
validity of anything which in the meantime
may have been done thereunder. Subject
as aforesaid, a Preservation Order shall
have effect until revoked by the Ministry
of Finance.
For the purpose of this sub-section the
expression "statutory period" means a
period comprising five days at least on
which the House in question has sat, but
not being in any case shorter in duration
than ten days, any days comprised in more
than one session of Parliament may be
reckoned for that purpose."
to
I have already explained, in answer
Senator Leslie, the necessity for this
Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 5, line 15, after "(3)" insert "subject
as aforesaid."
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount has incorporated this in his Amendment.
Amendment not moved.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Effect of Preservation Orders).
(1) While a Preservation Order is in
force, the monument to which the Order
relates shall not be demolished or removed,
nor shall any additions or alterations be
made thereto, or any work carried out in
connection therewith, except with the
written consent of the Ministry of Finance.
(2) If, while a Preservation Order is in
force, it appears to the Ministry of Finance
that, owing to the neglect of the owner of
the monument, the monument is liable to
fall into decay, the Ministry of Finance
may make an order appointing that Ministry guardian of the monument for the
period during which the Preservation Order
is in force, and in that case the provisions
of this Act shall, during that period, take
effect as if the Ministry of Finance had
been appointed guardian by virtue of a
deed executed by every person seized or
possessed of, or entitled to, an estate or
interest in the monument.
(3) Any Order made under the last preceding sub-section may be revoked at any
time by the Ministry of Finance.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Clause 7, page 5, line 19, after "nor," insert
"except in the case of urgent necessity."
Amendment not moved.
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Committee.
4 MAY 1926

Mr. LESLIE : I beg to move in page 5,
line 30, after "Act" (Act shall), to insert,
"other than those relating to public access.'
The Amendment I have put down has to deal
with a difficulty which will arise as regards
public access. If the Ministry proceeds to
make a guardianship order, apparently the
provisions as regards public access are to take
effect as if the Ministry had been appointed
by virtue of every estate existing in the
monument, that is, the person in possession
and the person who may come into possession
in the future. I do not know if that is the
intention. We have had some difficulty with
the word owner, but for the moment it seems
to me that we had better cover ourselves
here by saying "other than those relating to
public access," because we shall be dealing
with the question of public access again in
a few minutes, and I need not anticipate
what I am going to say about that.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: With all respect to the hon. Senator,
I do not think there is very much in his
Amendment. I can see no reason for inserting the words he suggests when, after all,
the matter is so serious that the State has
to step in and assume the whole responsibility. I do not see any necessity for the
Amendment, because the State only steps in
at the eleventh hour.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think it is important to pursue that matter at the moment,
and I ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 5, line 31, after "guardian," insert
"during that period."
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE : I beg to move, in page 5,
line 35, after "may" (may be revoked) to
insert "with the consent of the owner of the
monument." The point that occurred to me
was that if you allow the Ministry to come
in and make a Preservation Order, and if
it should so happen that the Ministry finds
its £600 a year is exhausted before it has
completed something that wants doing while
the Preservation Order is in force, the owner
cannot make any repairs and he cannot do
anything without the consent of the Ministry
of Finance. If during the Preservation Order
the monument falls into disrepair the Ministry, having no money to spend, can turn
round and revoke this Preservation Order,
and leave the monument in a worse state
than they found it. That is a possibility
under the Bill and, therefore, I suggest that
if a Preservation Order has been made it
should not be revoked without the consent
of the owner of the monument.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I think the contingency the hon.
Member has in mind is so unlikely that it
will never occur. At the same time, I cannot see why the owner's consent is necessary
to a revocation by the Ministry of its own
Order. I do not think the contingency the
hon. Member has in mind will ever occur.
Mr. LESLIE :
Obviously the noble Viscount does not intend to give the owner any
protection whatever. I will not pursue the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Penalty for injuring ancient
monuments).
If any person injures or defaces any
monument of which the Ministry of
Finance or a county council is the owner or
guardian, or which is the subject of a Preservation Order, or which has been included
in a list of monuments published by the
Ministry of Finance under this part of this
Act, that person shall, on conviction under
the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts,
be liable to a fine not exceeding five pounds,
and, in addition to the fine, to pay such
sums as the court by whom he is tried think
just for the purpose of repairing any damages caused by him, or to imprisonment for
a term not exceeding one month.
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Page 5, line 38, leave out "county council"
and insert "local authority or the National
Trust."
Page 6, line 4, leave out "five" and insert
"ten."
Line 8, leave out "one month" and insert
"three months."
Amendments not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Compensation for infringement
of private rights).
(1) Where, under the powers conferred by
this Act, any act is done involving interference with private rights of property, compensation shall be payable by the Ministry.
of Finance.
(2) If any dispute arises as to the amount
of such compensation, such question shall
be referred to, and determined by, an
official arbitrator under the Acquisition of
Land (Assessment of Compensation) Act,
1919, and sections three and six of that Act
shall apply accordingly.
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, on page 6,
line 17, at the end of the Clause, to insert
the following new Sub-section :--
"For the purposes of this section the inclusion of a monument in any list published under this Act, or the making of a
Preservation Order shall be deemed to be
an act done involving interference with
private rights of property."
I find some slight difficulty in Clause 9.
This is the Clause, as the Committee will
see, dealing with compensation. It does not
definitely state that compensation shall
be paid in respect of the inclusion of a monument in the list, or in respect of the making
of a Preservation Order. Yet both of these
proceedings would be to my mind proceedings which involve interference with the
private rights of property, and, therefore,
compensation ought to be payable. There is
not provided any means of setting in operation the compensation machinery, and I
think we could get over that. I, therefore,
suggest that "for the purposes of this Section
the inclusion of a monument in any list
published under this Act, or the making of a
Preservation Order, shall be deemed to
be an Act done involving interference with
private rights of property."
"
I am not sure that if something on the
lines of my suggestion was adopted, it is at
all clear that the expression 'act is done
would cover what I want. It is not an expression in the Public Health Act, and if you
look back to the earlier part of the Bill to
trace the expression "act" you find that "act"
means actually doing something yourself, not
making somebody else do it. "Doing such
acts and things as may be required for maintenance, and any other act or thing required
for the purpose of repairing a monument.”
The words are used almost in a manual
sense, and I think it is necessary for the
protection of the owner that words of the
kind I have suggested should be included.
VISCOUNT MASERREENE AND FER-
RARD: I oppose this Amendment, because
I think you may still leave it to the Tribunal to see that it is not an interference
with the rights of private property. It is
quite clear what the Tribunal would do,
and I think this Amendment is superfluous
and unnecessary.
Mr. LESLIE: I will not press the Amendment, but it seems to me to be another injustice to the owner.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 10 (Power to receive voluntary
contributions for maintenance of ancient
monuments.)
The Ministry of Finance may receive
voluntary contributions towards the cost
of the maintenance and preservation of
any monument which has become vested in
that Ministry, or of which that Ministry
has become the guardian under the provisions of this Act, and may enter into
an agreement with the owner of any such
monument, or with any other person, as to
the maintenance and preservation of the
monument and the cost thereof.
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 6, line 20, after "Ministry" insert "or
local authority".
Line 23. after "Ministry" in each case
where it occurs insert "or a local authority".
Amendments not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 11 (Powers of county councils
with respect to ancient monuments).
(1) The provisions of this Act with
respect to the maintenance of and access,
and penalties for injury, to ancient monuments shall apply in the case of any ancient
monument-
(a) of which a county council has, before the passing of this Act, become
guardian under sub-section (2) of section
nineteen of the Local Government (Ireland) Act, 1898; or
(b) which has been before the passing
of this Act, or thereafter becomes, vested
in a county council by virtue of an order
under sub-section (3) of section fourteen
of the Irish Land Act, 1903,
as if the county council were substituted
in those provisions for the Ministry of
Finance; and the expenses incurred by a
county council under this section shall be
raised by means of the poor rate as a
county at large charge.
(2) Where a county council is guardian
of an ancient monument, such council may,
with the consent of the owner of the monument, transfer the guardianship thereof
to the Ministry of Finance, and the provisions of this Act shall apply accordingly.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 11,
page 7, line 5, to leave out "and" (and the
expenses), and insert "provided that." This
Amendment is to meet a possible objection
which I think will probably have some substance in it. We see that in certain circumstances the Bill is to be read as if the
county council is to be substituted for the
Ministry of Finance, and it goes on to say
that the expenses incurred by a county
council shall be raised so and so. I think
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Committee.
4 MAY 1926
that if those provisions which deal with
access, maintenance, and expenses of county
councils are to be read as though the county
council were to be substituted for the Ministry we will have some little difficulty in
Clause 14, because in Clause 14 we find that
such expense as may be incurred by the
Ministry of Finance in the exercise of its
powers in connection with the maintenance
of ancient monuments are to be defrayed out
of moneys provided by Parliament. Whether
that is the intention of the Government I
do not know. I do not think it
is. I rather think that it is the
intention of the Government that county
council expenses should be borne as hitherto
by the county councils themselves. If that
is the intention I do think the Bill has expressed itself very badly indeed. I suggest
that we put the matter beyond dispute by
simply leaving out the word "and" and
putting in "provided that." I do not think
it can be suggested that the expression paying out of expenses coupled with the maintenance of ancient monuments is anything
else than a provision in respect of maintenance. If the noble Viscount can assure me
that the Government itself proposes to bear
the expenditure of the county council, of
course, my Amendment falls to the ground
as unnecessary, but I am afraid my Amendment is required. I beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am not quite clear what the effect
of these words would be in the Bill, and if
the hon. Member would leave it over till the
Report Stage I will go into the matter.
Mr. LESLIE: By leave of the Committee,
I will withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Line 9, to leave out "county council" and
insert "local authority."
Line 10, to leave out "council" and insert
"local authority."
Amendments not moved.
Colonel CRAWFORD: Before you put the
Clause to the Committee, I take this opportunity of expressing the hope that in the
further consideration of this Bill the powers
of the county council should be more considered than they are under the Bill. After all,
the chief local authority in the county is the
county council. The district councils are
fully represented not only by their chairmen,
but by members from each district. I think
that where the county council is anxious, as
my county council is anxious to do-and probably some of them are in a better position
to take charge of ancient monuments in their
district than the Ministry or Department,
which is to be formed, and might not have
the same information as we have, they
should have the power. I do hope in the
further consideration of the Bill the Leader
of the House will ask the Government to do
this, and we will be most happy to give any
advice. I am only speaking because my own
county council has taken the greatest interest in ancient monuments. I would hope
that the Government will consider the county
councils, because up to this they have not
been asked to say what their opinion is of
this Bill. I agree with the first part of the
Bill, but the county council should be given
more powers, and I trust the Government
will give a little more consideration to the
powers of these county councils, who wish to
do their duty in preserving what are con
sidered to be of the greatest interest, the
ancient monuments of Ireland.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the right hon. and
gallant Member, as I said before in the
previous part of this Committee Stage, I am
prepared on behalf of the Government to introduce a new Clause, a subsequent Clause,
on the Report Stage, giving the county
council more power, that is to say, enabling
them to become guardians of ancient monuments which are considered of national
importance. I am prepared to do so.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 12 (Public access to ancient
monuments).
The public shall have access to any monument which is vested in the Ministry of
Finance or a county council, or of which
that Ministry or a county council is
guardian, at such times and under such regulations as may from time to time be prescribed by that Ministry or such county
council respectively:
Provided that-
(i) this section shall not apply to any
monument of which the Ministry of
Finance or a county council has been
appointed or constituted the guardian before the commencement of this Act, except in cases where the consent of the
owner has been given to the public having
access to the monument either by the
deed appointing or constituting that
Ministry or such county council the
guardian, or where a right of access has
arisen through the acquiescence of the
owner or otherwise; and
(ii) where the deed appointing the
Ministry of Finance the guardian of the
monument is executed after the commencement of this Act, if the deed so
provides, the public shall not have access
to the monument without the consent of
the owner of the monument.
155 Ancient Monuments Bill-

The following Amendments stood on the
