Page 7, line 15, to leave out "county
council" and to insert "local authority."
Line 16, to leave out "county council" and
insert "local authority."
Amendments not moved.
VISCOUNT
MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move on page 7, line 17, to
leave out "regulations" ("under such regulations"), and insert "conditions." I think
"conditions" is a more general term, and has
a somewhat wider significance than regulations.
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask whether the noble
Viscount proposes to accept the following
Amendment, which stands in my name at
the top of the next page of Amendments.
The position is rather altered since I put my
Amendment down by the changing of the
word.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am not prepared to accept that
Amendment, because we consider "conditions"
enables us to make the change.
Mr. LESLIE: I really lay some stress on
that statement of the noble Viscount, that
in the opinion of the Government the new
word introduced does cover my proposal, that
is to say, there will be statutory provision
to charge for admission. I certainly support
the Amendment proposed by the noble Viscount. "Conditions" is much better than
"regulations."
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Page 7, line 17, after "regulations" ("under
such regulations "), to insert "which may
make provision among other things for the
route of access, and for imposing charges for
admission."
Mr. LESLIE: This Amendment falls after
what the noble Viscount said in moving his
Amendment.
Amendment not moved.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move in page 7, line 18,
after "by" ("prescribed by that Ministry")
to insert "regulations of." This comes in
consequence of the last Amendment.
The CHAIRMAN: The noble Viscount's
voice is getting lower and lower and lower.
I can hear not one word.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move after "by" to insert
"regulations of."
Amendment agreed to.
the
The following Amendment stood on
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 7, line 19, to leave out "county council" and insert "local authority."
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 21 to
leave out from "apply" ("shall not apply to
any") to the end of line 40, and insert "without the consent of the owner and the occupier
of the premises whereon the monument is
situated, and of the owner and occupier of
any other premises which it may be necessary to cross in order to obtain access to the
monument, and provided also that no right
of public access which may exist at the commencement of this Act shall be deemed to be
extinguished by reason only of the provisions
of this section."
This is an attempt on my part to deal with
the very difficult question of public access to
monuments. The Committee may appreciate
that the question of access to a monument
does not depend entirely on whether the land
one goes across to get to the monument belongs to the man to whom the monument
belongs or not. I confess I do not think that
the Clause, as it comes before us, is satisfactory. It seems to me to go very much
round the bush and not to arrive at the
proper point. The difficulties that particularly occur to me are these: What is to
happen, if this Clause is accepted, in Land
Purchase Act cases where a man has really
become the owner of a monument under the
Land Purchase Acts or where he does not?
What is to happen as regards the right of
public access, because the Land Purchase
Acts do not, as it seems to me, give any
right whatever of public access to a monument which is the subject of the Act. The
right of access given in the Land Purchase
Acts is the right of access which was given
by the original Act of 1882, and the access
given in the Act of 1882 was limited to access
of the Commissioner of Public Works for the
purpose of repairing a monument. It seems
to me if we pass the Clause as it stands we
are up against a difficulty there. Again, in
passing Preservation Orders there does not
seem to be any power under the Preservation
Order Clause to give public access. And
again we use an expression at the end of the
first Clause of the proviso which is quite
new to me. The section is not to apply where
the right of access has arisen through the
acquiescence of the owner. I really must ask
the noble Viscount, if he is going to oppose
my Amendment, what in the world he
means by acquiescence. We all know the
difficulties perpetually before us as to rights
of way. I think most of us are under the
impression that the mere voluntary courtesy
to A, B, C, or the whole alphabet, is no
157 Ancient Monuments Bill-

4 MAY 1926
Committee.
SO
foundation for a right of way. I think we
are also agreed that it is contrary to public
policy to make owners so extremely careful
that they are obliged to exclude everybody
for fear they may by acquiescence-to use the
word in the Bill-establish a right of way.
I do not think the Clause as it stands in the
Bill is at all satisfactory. I think that,
perhaps, the method I have adopted of making it a little more definite, that the consent
of the owner and occupier must be given,
may help us. It does not seem to me to
matter when this consent is given
long as it is given. Therefore, I think we
should add that one and also put in a saving
Clause, but no right of public access which
is already there should be interfered with.
I do not know whether the Committee would
wish to accept this Clause that I am suggesting as it stands. I must freely confess that
it is rather doubtful. I cannot think that
we can accept the expression "arising through
acquiescence of the owner," and I do not
think some of the other difficulties I have
raised have been met. I beg to move.
Colonel CRAWFORD: Before you put the
Question I hope the noble Leader of the
House will make the point clear, because the
case I have already mentioned, the case of
Nendrum, is partly under the county council
and partly under a private individual at
the present time. If the Clause does give
the right of access, probably the county council could get the owner out. There is divided
control. Something should be introduced to
enable them to do so.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In regard to the question asked by
the right hon, and gallant Member, I must
look into the matter of divided control and
give the right hon. Gentleman an answer at
a later date. As regards the Amendment of
the hon. Member, I confess I do not understand it. It does not seem to me to read
correctly. I think the Clause would be unintelligible if this were inserted. I may be
wrong, but so far I do not see what it would
mean. I have tried my best with the information I have got, and the legal advice at my
disposal, and they cannot make out what
actually it would mean. It would be better
if I went into this matter more fully. I
have not had time to consider this Clause,
but I will bring it up again. At the moment
I cannot gather what the Clause means.
Mr. LESLIE: I must, perhaps explain
that the Clause was meant to confer on the
public a definite right of access to a monument, but not to access without the consent
of the owner, but I will gladly avail myself
of the noble Viscount's offer to look further
into the matter.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I understand what the hon. Senator
means, but I think the Amendment could be.
better drafted.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 13 (Ancient Monuments Advisory
Committee).
(1) The Minister of Finance shall appoint
the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee, and that Committee shall consist
of members representing the bodies named
in the First Schedule to this Act, and such
other members (of whom the Deputy
Keeper of the Records of Northern Ireland
shall be one) as the said Minister may
appoint.
(2) The Governor of Northern Ireland
may, by Order in Council, alter the First
Schedule to this Act.
(3) The Ancient Monuments Advisory
Committee may, if so requested by the
owner of an ancient monument, give advice,
free of charge, except for out of pocket expenses, with reference to the treatment of
the monument.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 13,
page 8, line 2, to leave out "shall" (shall be
one), and insert "may."
This is a very small matter. The proposal is
that the Deputy Keeper of Records of
Northern Ireland shall always be a member
of the Advisory Committee. It is not usual,
I think, in Acts of Parliament to insert a
definite provision of that sort. I imagine the
proposal was made for two reasons, partly to
obtain the services of the present temporary
Keeper of Records, which can of course be
done without specially mentioning it in the
Bill, and to substitute the Schedule here for
the provision in the English Schedule, that a
member of the Historical Commission on
Ancient Monuments should be included.
It seems to me that if you leave it imperative there might conceivably be a difficulty at some future date, because it might
happen that the Deputy Keeper of Records
might not be the man best fitted to represent the Ministry on the Advisory Committee, and it might happen that the man
best fitted to represent the Ministry might
not be the best man to become Deputy
Keeper of Records. I suggest that a way out
of the difficulty is by leaving out “shall” and
substituting "may.”
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The reason the Government draftsman has put in the word "shall" instead of
may, is because the Deputy Keeper of Records
is the one person in Northern Ireland who is
sure to be a member of the Advisory Com-
159 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
mittee, both from an official and an expert
point of view. He is the one who can supply
official information, and if we put in the
word "may" the Ministry may appoint anyone else at their discretion. We desire that
the Deputy Keeper of Records should be the
one person to be appointed.
Mr. LESLIE: The knot the noble Viscount
seems to tie can always be untied, and I ask
leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I beg to
move, in page 8, line 2, after "one" (shall
be one) to insert "being a citizen of Northern
Ireland or Great Britain." The Clause says
the Ministry of Finance shall appoint an
Advisory Committee, and then the Schedule
gives the names of some associations that
are to be represented on that Committee.
Amongst these is the Royal Society of
Antiquarians of Ireland and the Royal Irish
Academy. I think that whatever representative is appointed for these societies should
not be a foreigner. I think there are some
members of these societies in Germany and
I want to prevent a foreigner being appointed a representative on this Committee
by the insertion of the words in the Amendment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am sorry I cannot accept the
Amendment of the right hon. Senator for the
reason that the Ministry of Finance may
desire temporarily to appoint on the Committee some eminent expert who was in
Northern Ireland at the time. The Ministry
might wish him to be a member of this
Committee temporarily, but if we insert the
Amendment we exclude any distinguished
person who is not a citizen of Northern Ireland or Great Britain from giving us the
benefit of his experience and ability. For
that reason I ask the right hon. Gentleman
to withdraw his Amendment.
Mr.
SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: After
this explanation, and taking it for granted
that it is not the intention of the Ministry
of Finance to appoint a foreigner on the
Committee except for special reasons, I withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 14 (Expenses of Ministry of
Finance and Advisory Committee).
There shall be paid out of moneys provided by Parliament such expenses as may
be incurred by the Ministry of Finance in
the exercise of its powers in connection
with-
(a) the inspection and maintenance of
ancient monuments, whether vested in
the Ministry of Finance or of which that
Ministry is guardian;
(b) the purchase by agreement of any
ancient monument; and
(c) the payment of compensation for
acts done under this Act involving interference with private rights of property;
and also such expenses of the Ancient
Monuments Advisory Committee as may
be sanctioned by the Ministry of Finance.
(d) the purchase or assistance towards
the purchase of archaeological objects of
which the finding has been reported to
the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee.
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 8, line 17, at end add "or which are
the subject of a Preservation Order."
Line 19, leave out "and."
Line 22, at end add "and (d) the purchase
or assistance towards the purchase of archæological objects."
Lines 25 to 28, leave out paragraph (d).
Line 28, at end insert the following new
sub-sections:-
"(2) The cost of any conveyances required for the purposes of this Act shall
be paid by the Ministry or the local authority concerned, and in so far as such cost
is payable by the Ministry it shall be paid
out of moneys provided by Parliament.
(3) Any such conveyances shall be exempt from Stamp Duty.
(4) No entertainments duty shall be payable in respect of any charge for admission
which may be imposed by virtue of section
twelve of this Act."
Mr. LESLIE : I think the Amendments
on Clause 14 might better be discussed on
the Report Stage, and I think they might be
withdrawn to-day. That applies to all the
Amendments on Clause 14.
Amendments not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 15 (Advice and superintendence
by Ministry of Finance).
(1) The Ministry of Finance may, if it
thinks fit, give advice with reference to the
treatment of any ancient monument, and
shall, as and when required, give that
advice with reference to the treatment of
any monument which is placed under their
protection by virtue of a Preservation
Order.
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Committee.
4 MAY 1926
(2) The Ministry of Finance may superintend any work in connection with any
ancient monument if invited to do so by
the owner, and shall superintend any such
work, whether required to do so by the
owner or not, in connection with any monument which is placed under their protection by virtue of a Preservation Order.
(3) Any such advice and superintendence
shall be given and afforded fee of charge,
except that a charge may be made for outof-pocket expenses in the case of monuments which have not been placed under
the protection of the Ministry of Finance
by virtue of a Preservation Order.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page 8,
line 33, to leave out "their protection" and
insert, "the protection of the Ministry." I
am rather afraid of making any suggestion-
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Might I say at once that I accept
the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 16 (Power of Councils to make
by-laws regulating Amendments, 7 Edw. 7,
c. 27), ordered to stand part of the Bill.
NEW CLAUSES.
The following new Clauses stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
After Clause 16, insert the following new
Clause:-
In this Act, unless the context otherwise
requires, the expression "local authority"
means the council of a county, county of a
city, or of an urban district.
After Clause 16, insert the following new
Clause:-
The expenses of a local authority under
this Act shall be raised in like manner as
that local authority raises funds for the
relief of the poor.
After Clause 16, insert the following new
Clause:-
The expression "the "National Trust"
means the National Trust for places of
historic interest or national beauty incorporated by the National Trust Act, 1907.
After Clause 16, insert the following new
Clause:-
Nothing in this Act shall, without the
consent of the owners and occupiers concerned apply (a) to any structure or monument which appears to the Ministry to be
occupied as a dwelling-house (otherwise
than by a person employed as the caretaken thereof or his family); or
(b) to any monument which has passed
under the provisions of Section Twentyfive of the Irish Church Act, 1869, or
under the provisions of the Irish Land
Act, 1903, or any enactments amending
the same; or
(c) to any premises or monuments
vested in the National Trust.
After Clause 16, insert the following new
Clause:-
Any person guilty of any contravention
of or non-compliance with the provisions
of this Act shall be liable, on conviction
under the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland)
Acts, to a fine not exceeding fifty pounds,
or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three months.
After Clause 16, insert the following new
Clause:-
"Any expenditure to be incurred by a
local authority under this Act shall be
limited to an amount from time to time
approved by the Ministry of Home Affairs,
and shall-
(a) in the case of the council of a county
(not being a county borough) be defrayed
out of the county fund, and be raised by
means of the poor rate over so much of
the county as does not comprise any
urban district; and
(b) in the case of a council of a county
borough or urban district, be defrayed in
the same manner as expenses incurred in
the administration of the Public Health
(Ireland) Acts, 1878 to 1918."
After Clause 16 insert the following new
Clause:-
"In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires, the expression local authority' means the council of a county, county
of a city, or of an urban district, and the
expression 'the National Trust' means the
National Trust for places of Historic Interest or National Beauty, incorporated by
the National Trust Act, 1907."
or
Mr. LESLIE: The whole of the Amendments on page 6 of the Paper go out, although
I may draw attention to the last proposal on

favour of that proposal. It will be necessary
in future to make provision for financing

THE SENATE

those authorities, but I think the noble

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-

Amendments not moved.
NEW CLAUSE.
"Nothing in the Act shall, without the
consent of the owner and occupier concerned, apply—
(a) to any structure or monument
which appears to the Ministry to be occupied as a dwelling-house (otherwise than
by a person employed as the caretaker
thereof or his family); or
(b) to any church, ecclesiastical building or structure which has been disposed
of under the provisions of sub-section
four of section twenty-five of the Irish
Church Act, 1869; or
(c) to any monument which since the
first day of November, 1903, has become,
or may after the commencement of this
Act become, vested in a purchaser under
the Land Purchase Acts; or
(d) to any premises or monument
vested in the National Trust.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move the insertion
of this new Clause after Clause 16. I am
not sure whether the words "and occupier"
are now necessary, having regard to what
happened to sub-Section 4. There are two
cases where the protection is required. We
must have the protection given in 1913 to the
owner of a dwelling-house. In the Act of
1913 not only are the Commissioners of
Works and the local authority definitely prohibited from acquiring a dwelling-house, but
in the Clause dealing with this matter, and
I think with the Preservation Order, the
dwelling occupied by a person is protected.
It must be obvious, I think, that however
much you interfere with a man's property
without his consent there are rights, and if
a man happens to be living in a house which
is of historic interest unless you put a
saving Clause in you are exposing that man
to interference by the Ministry, and I do
not think Parliament intends to go so far
as that.
Next it is intended to protect any building
which has already been offered to the public
and not accepted, and so passed into private
ownership. The Committee will remember
that the provisions of Section 25 of the Irish
Church Act were that if any church or
ecclesiastical building was not in use for
ecclesiastical purposes and yet if the Commissioners for the purpose of the Irish
Church thought it was worthy of preservation as a national monument, it should be
offered to the Commissioners of Works for
maintenance as a national monument. If the
Commissioners did not accept it it was to be
disposed of as the Commissioners of the Irish
Church thought fit.
It seems to me that we cannot now go back
on any arrangement made there, except with
the definite consent of the man who has become the owner of that church, ecclesiastical
building, or structure. The third case is
likely to be more extensive. In the case of
the Land Purchase Acts-that is to say, those
that have come into operation since 1st
November, 1903, the day when the Wyndham
Act came into operation--it has been provided
by Parliament that if any farm is included
in any estate to be sold, and on that farm
there is anything which, in the opinion of
the Land Commission, ought to be preserved
as an ancient monument, that monument
shall not pass with the farm to the tenant
purchaser, but shall be offered to the Commissioners of Works for maintenance as a
national monument. If they accept the
monument it shall be vested in them, but if
the Commissioners of Works-now the Ministry of Finance-do not accept the monument it shall then be offered to the county
council, and if the county council accept it
it shall then be vested in the county council
Failing those two authorities the monument passed to the man who was the purchaser of the farm. It is impossible to go
back on that arrangement now, however much
local antiquarians may desire to get dominion
over that monument. Under the Land
Purchase Acts the monument has twice been
offered to the public and not accepted.
I say that you cannot consistently with
honouring a bargain go back on that bargain,
except with his consent, and that is the
proposal I have made. As regards the
fourth paragraph of the Clause, that is, “(d)
to any premises or monument vested in the
National Trust," I think after what the
noble Viscount said about the National
Trust, the Committee might prefer that that
should be dropped. Therefore, I only move
the new Clause with paragraphs (a), (b),
and (c).
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am afraid I do not see my way to
accept the hon. Member's Amendment. We
do not consider that there is any necessity to
165 Ancient Monuments Bill-

4 MAY 1926
Committee.
exempt an inhabited house.
It is very
I
doubtful, indeed, whether there is any inhabited house in Northern Ireland that from
the point of view of this Bill would deserve
preservation as an ancient monument.
think I may say, without any offence to
the hon. Senator that he has an eye more
to Great Britain in this matter than to
Northern Ireland. In this sense we have no
town like Chester, York, or Edinburgh,
where there would be dwelling-houses that
should be preserved. We have no place like
that. In any case, I think we must agree
that habitation is the best form of preservation. As regards the point the hon.
Senator raised as to the Land Purchase Act,
I think it must be quite clear to all of us
if we are to take over an ancient monument
of a tenant or occupier of a farm, for instance, we shall have to pay unless we come
to some agreement with him. We could
hardly do all these things by voluntary
agreement. If a monument was affered
before, and not accepted, I think it must
be quite clear we could not take it over
without giving some terms to the tenant or
occupier. Therefore, I would ask the hon.
Member if he can see his way to withdraw
the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I greatly dislike to withdraw this Amendment. I did hope I should
have reached it at a time when the Committee was not quite so empty. I think.
perhaps, the noble Viscount, if I understood
correctly, will look into this matter again
and perhaps give us something on Report
to meet the point. I should have mentioned,
though I did not, as to how far, even without this Section, you are empowered to interfere with a monument, having regard to
the limitations of the Government of Ireland
Act.
If the noble Viscount will look into
this matter, I would prefer to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

CLAUSE 17 (Definition of monument"
and ancient monument." 45 and 46 Vic.,
c. 73).
In this Act the expression monument "
includes any site, structure or erection
(whether above or below the ground) other
than ecclesiastical building which is, for
the time being, used for ecclesiastical purposes; and the expression "ancient monument includes any monument or part or
remains of a monument, the preservation
of which is a matter of public interest, by
reason of the historic, architectural, traditional, artistic, or archaeological interest
attaching thereto, and the site of any such
monument or of any remains thereof, and
any part of the adjoining land which may
be required for the purpose of fencing,
covering in, or otherwise preserving the
monument from injury, or for the purpose
of preserving the amenities of the monument, and also includes the means of
access thereto.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 14 to
leave out "site" ("including any site, structure, or").
Clause 17 is the definition Clause and it
contains I am afraid, a good many matters
which will require our attention. The first
point which I would direct the attention of
the Committee to is the word "site" in line
14. The word "site" is quite new in this
sense, in the large body of statutes dealing
with ancient monuments, and it is in itself
a word capable of very great expansion.
For example, when the expression "monument" includes any site we are not given any
idea whatever as to how much is meant.
The word has been put in in order to meet
some case which it was thought would not
otherwise be covered. I would propose in a
later Amendment to the same Clause, to
bring back the reference to the Schedule of
the Ancient Monuments Protection Act, 1882.
which still survives in England. The
Schedule has been kept alive, although the
Act has been repealed. I think it is fairly
plain to all of us that the original intention
was to keep in this Clause the reference to
the Ancient Monuments Act of 1882. I think
that expression would come under the term
"site," which is not, as I say, sufficiently
definite for our purpose. I think I may
move that the word "site" be omitted as
going much further than we desire.
"
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The word "site" we consider to be
important, because, without the word "site"
I do not know that you could include the
ancient cave dwelling, for instance, or a
burying place. The hon. Member, I think,
said site" is far too general a term and
might lead us to taking over a large tract
of country. But really, I think the hon.
Member need have no fear on that score.
The Ministry of Finance are not going to go
seeking monuments, or to take up tracts of
country which they shall look after--as the
hon. Member rather seems to be suggesting.
I think he can rest assured that the word
"site" will not be abused in any way. Therefore, I would ask him to withdraw the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I have this difficulty, that
there are not enough Members to divide the
Committee.
The CHAIRMAN: Yes, there are.
Mr. LESLIE: Of course, if the Noble Viscount is adamant on the subject of site, it is
a little difficult to go on with some of the
other Amendments on this Clause. The noble
Viscount used cave as an illustration.
Reference to the Schedule of the Act of 1882,
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
which is what I want to keep in the Bill,
will show that the cave is definitely included
in the Schedule of that Act. Therefore, anything of like character to that can be dealt
with under the old Acts. The noble Viscount also refers to burial ground. A burial
ground may be of tremendous size, and,
although I suppose, £600 a year might run
to buying few of these large ones, I think
it is rather to large a power to give. However, I do not know, as nobody else has
expressed any view on the matter. Therefore, I will not press this to a division, ir
the noble Viscount really prefers.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 15, to
leave out "ground" ("or below the ground")
and insert "surface." This is to cover the
case where it might be under water.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This is a small matter.
We con
sider that "surface" would meet the point
rather than the word "ground." It is
merely a matter of wording. I am not
wedded to the term. I think "surface"
would meet the point.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Just a
slight concession.
Mr. LESLIE: I think my Amendment is
to take out "ground" and insert "surface
I rejoice to find the noble Viscount with me.
The CHAIRMAN: Then the Amendment
is accepted?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-

Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 15.
after "than" ("other than ecclesiastical") to
insert "an." It is a misprint.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
""
""
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 17,
after purposes ("ecclesiastical purposes
and ") to insert "or anything contained in
such ecclesiastical building." The point I
had in mind here was to protect monuments
that happen to be in churches. They are
not so protected in the English Act, but it
seems to me we ought to consider just a
little further. The power of protection of
the fabric and outer walls of an ecclesiastical
building, necessarily applies to everything
inside. I think we ought to insert these
words. But if the noble Viscount wishes
to consider the matter, this is a point which
might very well stand over for Report, because there are other considerations to come
in which might make that unnecessary. At
the moment I beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I would ask the hon. Member to withdraw it and bring the matter up again on
Report.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 18,
after "monument" ("any monument or
part") to insert "irrespective of age." This
is an Amendment which I do not think is
very likely to be accepted. I put it down
in order to get from the Government, if I
can, some definition as to what they expect
or desire the Committee and the public to
understand by the word "ancient."
"Ancient," as we know, is commonly used
simply as the antithesis of "modern," one
word being as vague and as indefinite as
the other. An ancient monument, for this
purpose, was first defined in the Act of 1881,
as I said the other day and it takes us back to
the eleventh century. Then we are carried
on by subsequent Acts until the Middle
Ages, and now, by inserting the expression,
historic, or architectural, or artistic interest,
we have really brought it down to the monument which was erected the week before
last. I do not know whether it will be the
desire of the Government to make any suggestion as to what they understand constitutes ancient, but I think it will be remembered that the term "ancient monument"
is left to the interpretation of the Advisory
Committee and the Ministry itself in the
earlier part of the Bill, and if we can do
anything to guide them, I think we ought
to do so. It is a point for discussion, and 1
beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I do not think the Government can
accept this Amendment. The term "ancient"
is a matter of interpretation. As far as the
British procedure goes I understand that
nothing is ancient after about the year 1740
or 1714. I think that is the rule they adopt
across the water, and while I do not say we
should follow it we will be guided to a certain extent by the experience across the
water.
Mr. LESLIE: I will withdraw the Amendment, but I will make a suggestion on the
Report Stage. There was a precedent established when we made our Records. I think
we made them old from before the year
1700.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
""
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in line 18,
after "monument' (includes any monument) to insert " specified in the Schedule
to the Ancient Monuments Protection Act,
1882, and any other monuments or things

VISCOUNT
MASSEREENE AND FER-

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave out
from the beginning of line 26 to "and" in
line 27.
This is a more serious matter because here
again you touch the private property of the
owner. It is a new proposal altogether, and
does not appear in the English Act. The
words that ought to come out are altogether
too vague. How far do you want to go? Who
is to be the authority as to whether the
amenities of a monument are in danger or
not? The words in the Section are terribly
vague, because I am afraid we will have to
remember that it is possible a Preservation
Order might be made in respect of this. I
think it is going much further than anything
done across the water. I beg to move.
is
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I think the word "amenities
really important. The hon. Member says it
has too wide a significance, but there might
be cases where that expression might be
very useful. It is quite true the term has
not been used before, but I do not think
the hon. Member need fear that any harm
will come from the use of those words.
Mr. LESLIE: It is not a question of amenities; it is a question of area.
It says "( any
part," etc. We are not told who is to

On Question, Amendment negatived.
"6

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in line 27,
to leave out after the word 'of " ("of
access thereto ") and insert "public and
other."
This again is a question of access. The
expression is exactly as it appears in the
English Act of 1913, and it still leaves us
in doubt as to the access provided in the
case of monuments which have passed under
the Land Purchase Acts. It seems to me
that we ought to clear that up in some
way; otherwise sooner or later somebody
will be landed in a law suit, and it is not
desirable that our Acts should not be plain.
As it stands I am not satisfied that anything to which the Land Purchase Acts
apply is covered by the access provided in
this Bill. I suggest we should make our
meaning clear, by inserting such words as
these.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I do not think these words are
necessary, but I will look into the matter
further.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, That the
Chairman do leave the Chair.

The House being resumed,
Ordered: That the House be again put into

McCALDIN'S DIVORCE BILL.
Brought from the Commɔns.
Adjournment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER
RARD: In rising to move that this House
do now adjourn, I have to inform the House
that owing to the serious state of affairs in
Great Britain, the Government are introducing an Emergency Powers Bill tomorrow, which they desire to pass through
all its stages on that day. Therefore, I must
crave the indulgence of the Senate to suspend Standing Orders, in order to pass this
Bill through the House to-morrow.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Six
o'clock.
Purchase of Property.
Purchase of Property.
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 5th May, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
McCaldin's Divorce Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time. (The Marquess of Dufferin and
Ava).
--
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate agree to
the said Bill without any Amendment.
Business of the House.
Moved: That Standing Order No. XXV
be dispensed with for the purpose of passing the Emergency Powers Bill.-(Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Property Purchased with Public Funds.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Senator Samuel
Cunningham.
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government will introduce legislation
by which the dimensions, terms, and prices
of all property purchased with public funds
must be gazetted before the title is completed.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I ask the
Question which stands in my name on the
ground of recent events which are well
known to all the inhabitants of Northern Ireland. I think it would be a great
advantage if we knew at once what property had been bought, so that, if necessary,
a protest could be entered against any misappropriation of public money. Yesterday,
on another question, I had the assurance
that the Government were considering these
points, and I hope also to receive the assurance that this special point with regard to
the purchase of property will receive the
consideration of the Government, and that
they will deal with it in the Bill they propose to bring before Parliament.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD. In reply to the right hon. Gentleman, I understand that the procedure suggested by him relates in the main to proposals for purchase of land, buildings, etc.,
by local authorities for Local Government
purposes to be financed from the rates. 1
am inclined to think, however, that on
learning the existing procedure he will
agree that the arrangements already in
force bring with sufficient prominence to
the notice of the ratepayers concerned
capital outlay proposed by Local Government Authorities.
In the first place the meetings of local
authorities at which any such proposals
would be discussed are usually open to the
public, and their proceedings are generally
reported in the public Press.
In the second place, in every case in which
a local authority proposes to purchase property, that proposal usually comes before the
central authority concerned in the shape of
an application for sanction to the borrowing
of the sums necessary to make the purchase,
and in all such cases, except under exceptional circumstances, the application forms
the subject of a Public Inquiry, held by the
officers of the Ministry concerned. At that
Inquiry it is customary for the authority
applying for sanction to the loan to be called
upon to explain publicly all details in connection with the works they propose to
embark on, including full particulars as to
any lands or buildings which it is proposed
to acquire.
In all the circumstances, therefore, it is
not considered that any further or essential
safeguards would be provided or secured by
making it a condition that particulars of
the works, including particulars of lands,
etc., to be acquired by a local authority,
should be published in the official Gazette,
a document which is not read, as
by the general body of ratepayers.
a rule,
As the hon. Senator may also have in his
mind procedure in connection with purchases of property by Government Departments, I may point out that it is almost the
invariable practice that no new work or purchase of land for State purposes is embarked
on until an opportunity has been afforded
for debate in another place by the inclusion
of the necessary estimated money provision
in the Estimates for the services of the year
with an indication, if practicable, of the
property proposed to be acquired. It has
on rare occasions happened that by reason
of urgency the Ministry of Finance has
sanctioned a purchase, not provided for in
the Estimates, but in such circumstances,
if the amount involved is material, it is
the practice to bring the matter to the
notice of Parliament by means of a Supplementary Estimate.
Furthermore, in the Appropriation Accounts of the expenditure of the preceding
year full details are given of all purchases
Afforestation.
Vising of Passports.
5 MAY 1926
of property made throughout that year with
reasons for any variations that may exist
between the actual expenditure and the provision made by Parliament. Should the
Comptroller and Auditor-General not be
satisfied with any such explanations, it is
open to him to bring the matter to the
notice of the Committee of Public Accounts,
and the Accounting Officer of the Vote will
have to justify fully to that Committee the
action of his Department. I am sure, therefore, that the hon. Senator will agree that
as regards purchases by the State ample
publicity is at present given.
I am
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
disappointed with the answer of the Government. Recent events-
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member is not
entitled to make another speech on the same
question.
Arising
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
out of the answer, I am sorry the Government does not see its way to take up this
question-
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member is not
entitled to make a second speech on that
particular question. He has only the right
to make a second speech by leave of the
House.
Afforestation in Northern Ireland.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Samuel Cunningham:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government has taken or intends to
take, any steps to carry out the recommendation as to afforestation submitted
on 3rd December, 1924, by the Commission
on the Natural and Industrial Resources of
Northern Ireland, in its Report, which
stressed the urgent necessity for action
by the State; whether since the supply
of home-grown timber is steadily declining,
they will adopt the comprehensive scheme
of afforestation for Northern Ireland contained in the Development Commission
Report, and make adequate monetary provision for carrying out the same, in order
ultimately to ensure to this country its
own supply of soft woods; and whether
they will take steps to ascertain where,
in Northern Ireland areas which are suitable for afforestation and which could be
leased, can be found such as are mentioned
in paragraph 12 of the Commission Report,
particularly in the Antrim Hills, the
Sperrin Mountains and the Mourne
Mountains.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: The second
question which stands in my name on the
Order Paper is one to which I wish to call
the attention of the House. At this time,
when there is so much unemployment, I
think it would be a great advantage if a
scheine such as that suggested in the Report
of the Development Commission could be
carried out. It would take men off the dole
and give them useful employment. During
the past few days we have seen what can
happen in regard to supplies, and if there
was another war like the last I think it
would be a great advantage to have more
home-grown timber. I hope the Government
will be in a position to give us a satisfactory
reply as to whether or not they are likely
to carry out the recommendations of the
Development Commission on this subject.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I regret that the answer I have to
give to the right hon. Gentleman is not as
full as I should like to give him, and it
may not altogether satisfy him. In answer
to his question I have to say that the recommendations as to afforestation submitted
by the Commission on the Natural and Industrial Resources of Northern Ireland are
still under consideration. Information as to
land suitable for forestry purposes continues
to be collected by the Ministry of Agriculture
and will be available for consideration when
additional funds for the acquisition of extra
land for forestry purposes are available.
Vising of Passports at Londonderry Port
The following question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Long:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
it is a fact that persons sailing from the
United States direct to the port of Londonderry are required to have their passports
visaed by an official of the Irish Free State
at the port of embarkation, and pay a fee
of ten dollars, and, if so, whether the
Northern Government acquiesce in this
arrangement.
Mr. LONG: I do not intend to ask the
question standing in my name at the present
time, and I ask permission to withdraw it.
Message from the Commons.
That they have passed the Economy (Exchequer Relief) Bill, to which they desire
the concurrence of this House.
Economy (Exchequer Relief) Bill.

time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)

Mines Bill-

Mines (Working Facilities, Support,

Order for the House to be put into Committee read.

itself into Committee on the said Bill.-

On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (Power to grant right to work
minerals which would otherwise be unworkable), and 2 (Power to grant right to work
minerals in case of agreement as to adjustment of boundaries between two mines),
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
ancillary
of
CLAUSE 3 (Power to grant
rights for facilitating the working
minerals).
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In the debate on the Second Reading of this Bill, Senator Leslie raised a
question as to what would happen in the
event of waste water from mines flowing into
streams. As this matter is referred to in
this Clause, I should like to inform the hon.
Member, that with regard to his question as
to the effect upon fishing interests of rights
to dispose of water, or other liquid matter,
obtained from mines being granted under
Section 1 of this Clause, I think we may
assume that the Tribunal would have regard
to the nature of the water to be disposed of,
and to the character of the stream into which
it was to be carried, before granting any
such rights. Also, I may explain that
Section 80 of the Fishery (Ireland) Act,
1842, prohibits the throwing, emptying, or
release into any river, lake, or portion of
tidal waters any dye-stuffs or other deleterious or poisonous liquid. This, I think,
covers the point the hon. Senator raised.
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the noble
Viscount for so kindly redeeming his promise, and for telling us the result of his
investigations. I feel quite sure that the
Section of the Fishery Act to which the noble
Viscount referred will, coupled with reasonable intelligence on the part of the Tribunal,
prevent any possible difficulty in this connection.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 4 (Limitation on power of
granting rights), 5 (Applications for rights),
6 Power of Ministry of Commerce to grant
rights after reference to the Tribunal), and
7 (Provisions in case of several applications
in respect of the same rights), ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Restrictions on working
minerals required for support).
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I should like to take this opportunity of informing Senator Leslie, who on
the Second Reading raised the question of
the protection of lateral support, that if he
will read Clause 8 of the Bill he will see
that this Clause gives power to impose restrictions upon the working of minerals required for the support of buildings or works,
and this support includes both vertical and
lateral support, as the text of the Clause
states.
Mr. LESLIE: Again I am obliged to the
noble Viscount. My question was really more
directed to secure lateral support for railways, but, as the noble Viscount told us the
other day, railways are not covered by this
Bill.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 9 (Determination and payment of compensation), 10 (Appeals to
County Court sitting with an assessor
as to amount of compensation), 11 (Constitution and procedure of the Tribunal),
12 (Power of Ministry of Commerce to state
special cases), 13 (Power of Ministry of Commerce to make rules), 14 (Effect of grant of
right), 15 (Provisions as to tenants for life,
&c.), 16 (Saving of rights under the Railways Clauses Act, &c.), 17 (Interpretation),
18 (Repeal of ss. 4 and 20 of 10 & 11 Geo. 5,
c. 50), 19 (Expenses under Part I of Act),
and 20 (Short title, commencement and construction), ordered to stand part of the Bill.

Pensions Bill-
Committee.
5 MAY 1926
CLAUSE 1 (Confirmation of Agreement as
to chargeability of certain pensions increased under 14 and 15 Geo. 5, c. 32), ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Additions to percentages by
which certain pensions may be increased, 10
and 11 Geo. 5, c. 36).
(1) As from the first day of April, nineteen hundred and twenty-six, the schedule
to the Pensions (Increase) Act, 1920 (in
this Act referred to as "the principal Act")
shall, as respects pensions not exceeding
one hundred pounds payable out of the
Petty Sessions Clerks Fund, or out of the
Teachers' Pension Fund, have effect as if
the percentages by which existing pensions
may be increased were-
*
*
Mr. DICKSON: I beg to move in Clause
2, page 2, lines 16 and 17, to leave out
the words "out of the Petty Sessions
Clerks' Fund, or." I do not move this
Amendment with the object of affecting the
Petty Sessions clerks who are employed full
time, such as those in the City of Belfast
and in some other places, but I refer particularly to country districts where the
Petty Sessions only sit once a month, and
where the Petty Sessions clerk is occupied
perhaps for one hour a month, or, if he has
two districts, а couple of hours in
the month. Why this man's pension
should be increased is more than I can
understand, any more than I can understand
why he should get a pension at all.
I do not think the country can afford to
increase the pensions of men who are not
full time officers, and are only occupied for
an hour or two in the month on these
duties. I really think it is absurd to
suggest increasing the pensions of such men.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The effect of the Amendment proposed by the hon. Member would be to
exclude Petty Sessions clerk pensioners from
the scope of the Bill. The hon. Senator
has another Amendment on the same subject. He states he has proposed the Amend
ment on the ground that these clerks are
almost all part time officials, and that,
therefore, they should not be given pension
rights. But this would be quite contrary
to the policy of previous Acts of Parliament.
While they are part time officials their
salaries are calculated on a part time basis
and are regulated by the amount of work
they perform in each district. The salaries
are not large, and as the persons appointed
are usually of middle age they are not
likely to earn large pensions owing to the
comparatively small number of years they
can serve.
The effect of this Bill, so far as the
Petty Sessions clerks in Northern Ireland
are concerned, is very small; I might almost say trifling. Only two cases will be
affected, and the amount of the increase will
be small, as in both cases the existing
pensions are small, and the Bill would only
give an increase of about 10 per cent. In
these circumstances I hope the hon. Member
will not press his Amendment
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: support the Amendment, and I quite agree with
what Senator Hill Dickson has said.
know of my own knowledge what very little
work some Petty Sessions clerks have to do.
I know of one case where the Petty Sessions
sits only once a month, and sometimes there
is no business at all and no magistrates
attend. The whole thing is ridiculous,
and I hope the Government will make some
differentiation between the full time Petty
Sessions clerks, who are entitled to a pension, and those in country districts to whom
I am referring. It would be a great injustice if increased pensions were given in
these cases. I hope the hon. Senator will
press the matter to a Division.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: My only reply to the hon. Senator
is what I have already stated.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Is it
not possible that some difference can be
made in the Bill between those Petty Sessions clerks who are fully employed and
those who are not? Some of these clerks
are entitled to the increased pension, but
a large proportion are not, and if some
differentiation could be made it would get
over the difficulty.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I will look into the matter and
see if we can bring up something on the
Report Stage.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
(2) For the purposes of this section-
The expression "Petty Sessions Clerks
Fund" means the Petty Sessions Clerks
(Northern Ireland) Fund, being the fund
established in Northern Ireland in pursuance of section twenty-eight of the Petty
Sessions Clerk (Ireland) Act, 1858, for the
purposes mentioned in the said Act, as
amended by subsequent enactments:

Ancient Monuments Bill.
House again in Committee (according to
Order).
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 18 (Finding
articles) -
of archæological
(1) The finder of any archæological
object shall, within fourteen days of such
finding, report the finding and character of
the object and the circumstances of the
finding to the Ancient Monuments Advisory
Committee.
(2) Any person, being a finder of an
archaeological object, who fails to report
the finding of such object as provided by
sub-section (1) of this section or who refuses to give information or gives false information of the character of the object
found, or of the circumstances of the finding, to the Ancient Monuments Advisory
Committee or to such person or persons as
may be prescribed by the Ministry of
Finance shall be liable on conviction under
the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts
to a fine not exceeding five pounds, or to
imprisonment for a term not exceeding one
month.
(3) In this section the expressior
archæological object" means an object
being a chattel whether in a manufactured
or unmanufactured state, which, by reason
of its archæological or historic interest, has
a value substantially greater than its in
trinsic value or the value of the materials
of which it is composed.
(4) The Ministry of Finance may, on the
recommendation of the Ancient Monuments
Advisory Committee, purchase or give
assistance towards the purchase of archaeological objects, the finding of which has
been reported under the section, and such
objects when purchased shall be deposited
for public exhibition in such places and
subject to such conditions, as the Ministry
may direct.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 9, line 29, to leave out "shall, within
fourteen days of such finding" and insert
"may."
Mr. LESLIE: I think it would perhaps
make for convenience if I ask leave to alter
slightly the first Amendment standing in my
name, so that for the moment I may confine
myself to the Motion to leave out the word
"shall" instead of proceeding further. I
think that it is not impossible that the main
part of our discussion of this Clause can be
bound by our decision on that suitable substitution. The object of taking out the word
"shall
""
The CHAIRMAN: May I ask is the hon.
Member leaving out the remainder of the
Amendment to insert "may"?
"
Mr. LESLIE: I was proposing to leave
out these words for the moment. I should
necessarily have to move to insert may
afterwards. The object of the proposal is
of course to render the whole Section
Clause permissive instead of as it is at present subject to a penalty. The Committee
will remember that this Clause, according to
notice given to the public by means of the
Press, was deliberately brought forth
in order to ascertain how public feeling regarded such an innovation in our general
law. To require that any individual should
be required under penalty to report the finding of something which was not treasure
trove, and not, therefore prima facie the
property of the Crown, but was simply some
object possibly of interest to antiquaries or
the reverse there is no precedent that I
know of for a Clause of this kind. But there
5 MAY 1926
Committee.

Ancient Monuments Bill—
is no reason whatever why if it really is desired to make it so that this House should
not take the power. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) The question is then whether we are
entitled to add another to the already large
list of offences dealt with by criminal law,
and whether there is sufficient justification
for doing it in this case. After all who is it
that takes any particular interest in the preservation of any such articles as those with
which this Clause purports to deal? It is
only, I think, a very small section of the
commuity.
Colonel CRAWFORD: No, no.
Mr. LESLIE: I repeat that I think it is
only a very small section of the community.
Colonel CRAWFORD: No.
Mr. LESLIE: And I have yet to learn that
it is in accordance with our Constitution
that the very small minority should be put
over the vast majority. There is not the
least reason why anybody should not proceed
to study archæology if they wish to do so.
The only point is they should not endeavour
to impose upon others the task of assisting
them in the cultivation of their particular
taste. Passing from that is it gravely
thought that in this twentieth century if you
want to get help from the people of Northern
Ireland you can only get it by exposing them
to the risk of fine and imprisonment if they
do not help? I confess I have myself much
higher confidence in our people than that
course of action would suggest. I believe if
you want articles of archæological interest
preserved, the right thing to do is to endeavour to get help in preserving them by
cultivating the spirit of politeness, and by
lectures in towns and villages, but do not
bring in a new penal Clause without further
justification. Moreover, if you do insert a
penal Clause in the Act it is generally desirable that you should so arrange matters
that there is some chance of your desires becoming effective. I think it is fairly plain
that although these penalties may be inserted
in the Bill, the chance of securing conviction
is remarkably small. That is perhaps rather
a side issue, and I need not dwell upon it.
I understand this Clause may be of some use
to antiquaries if it were made permissive instead of treating it as a compulsory penal
Clause. I object to its inclusion. Accordingly, as a first step, to render it permissive,
I beg to move that the word "shall" be left
out and the word "may" inserted.
Colonel CRAWFORD: I am sorry I am at
-a complete difference with him in every point
the hon. Senator has made in his speech. I
am under the impression-I will probably
be corrected afterwards-I am not certain of
that-but my opinion of this is that he is
quite wrong in the statement he made about
few people being interested in the discovery
and preservation of things which have lasted
for many years, the destruction of which is
such a disgrace to the country. Instead of
finding a minority, I think in this House you
will find a large majority. If this is put to
a vote it will be proved that the Committee
is entirely against him. In my humble
opinion taking out the word "shall," and
putting in the word "may," would destroy
the whole value of this Bill. We need not
be afraid of innocent persons being punished.
I am sure most people on whose land these
things may be found would only be too glad
to assist in every way, and it is only against
those who have no right to them that action
would be brought, and who would be rightly
punished. I am sure if I were on the Bench
when they were brought forward they would
be very heavily punished. I think the hon
Senator is quite wrong in his conclusions.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to support the retention of the word "shall" instead of the
proposed Amendment. I think I would be
right in saying most of these objects of
archæological interest are invariably discovered, not by the owners of the property
but by citizens interested in the preservation
of such objects in the interests of the community in the future. Circumstances might
arise that a member of one of these societies
mentioned for representation on the Committee to advise the Minister might discover
on certain property indications of a valuable
relic and if the word "shall" is taken out
here, it would debar him from restraining
the proprietor from proceeding to destroy
what he considered his property. In the
public interest, therefore, the removal of the
word "shall" would be more or less against
the object of the Bill. Therefore, I support
the retention of the word "shall."
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The speeches on this Amendment
made by the right hon. and gallant Senator,
Colonel Sharman Crawford, and the hon.
Senator Dorman really leave me very little
to say. They fully answered the speech of
the hon. Senator Leslie. But the hon. Senator Leslie, I gather, feels very strongly on
this matter, and thinks that "shall" is a
great infringement of private rights. After
all, what does this Clause do? It lays a very
trifling restraint on the liberty of the finder.
It merely delays the sale until he has reported, and it may, so it can be argued, do
a favour to the finder. What does it do? It
ensures that if he reports the matter he gets
expert information as to the value of the
find, and may indicate possible purchasers at
a fair value. Also, I may say, that this
Clause is not going to injure any innocent
person. There is an Amendment which
stands in my name, which comes on later, on
which I can raise that point. It is quite
THE SENATE
Committee.

Ancient Monuments Bill—
really what course the noble Viscount desires
to take on this matter, whether for the sake
of obtaining public interest through the newspapers he wishes to divide. Perhaps he would
tell us. I have not received sufficient support
from others.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
true if the word "may" is inserted instead of
"shall" it will absolutely destroy the whole
value of the Clause as the hon. and gallant
Senator says. Therefore, on behalf of the
Government, I resist the Amendment.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I wish to
ask, just for information, if I find an Edward
III. or Edward I. coin would I be bound
under that Clause to proceed within fourteen
days to inform the Advisory Committee about
it. It is just for information I want to ask
that.
Colonel CRAWFORD: Under the law at
present you are.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The right hon. Senator raised the
question of treasure trove. Treasure trove is
gold or silver bullion or coins, and under
the present law the right hon. Senator would
have to report that matter. But as regards
treasure trove, perhaps I may not be out of
order in stating that an arrangement is being
come to with the Crown to the effect that in
respect of treasure trove in Northern Ireland
the Crown is prepared to waive its rights on
condition that the treasure trove is deposited
in the care of a public body or museum in
Northern Ireland. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear).
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I would
like to thank the Leader of the House for
the information. I was under the impression
that treasure trove had to be gold. Treasury
notes are not treasure trove. (Laughter).
Mr. LESLIE: There seem to be one or two
small matters left for me to do. I must
apologise to the noble Viscount for not quite
catching what he said about gold and silver
coins. Secondly, as regards the suggestion
of, I think, the right hon. and gallant Gentleman that these alterations would upset the
whole balance of the Bill, I must point out
that this Clause is dealing not with ancient
monuments but with archæological objects.
The provisions of the Bill applicable to
ancient monuments are not necessarily applicable to archæological objects.
Colonel CRAWFORD:
quibble.
That is a mere
Mr. LESLIE: The hon. and gallant Gentleman says it is a mere quibble. I hope, as
we are all allowed to speak more than once,
he will elaborate that for us. I think also
that somewhat the same point was raised by
hon. Senator Dorman. I might point out
that he seemed to be under the impression
that it was the person to whom the object
was due who was under the liability under
the Act. The person to whom any object may
be passed is, I am told, not the finder. The
only person to whom the Clause of the Bill
would appear to apply is the person who
actually finds the object. I do not know
The CHAIRMAN: I think the question
whether the Committee divides would not
rest with the noble Viscount. It is entirely
with the hon. Member.
Mr. LESLIE: I must apologise for making
the discussion so conversational. That being
so, I think I had better withdraw and leavo
the defence of the liberties of the people to
the popularly elected Chamber to whom this
Bill will have to go.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
-
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Line 32, to leave out from the beginning of
the line to "or" in line 39.
Amendment not moved.
وو
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move on page 9, line 34,
after "who" ("object, who fails to") to insert
"without reasonable cause.' The object of
this Amendment is to allow some latitude,
to give a certain amount of freedom where an
individual may have some object which may
be of archæological interest, but from ignorance he may not be aware of the fact. We
do not want to penalise any innocent person.
We consider the words "without reasonable
cause" will fully cover this. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
:-
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 9, line 40, to leave out from
"Ministry" to the end of line 3 of page 10.
Page 10, line 4, to leave out "Section" and
insert "Act."
Line 7, to leave out "has " and insert

Line 11, to leave out from "may" to "purchase" in line 13.
Amendments not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 13,
after "purchase" ("Committee, purchase
or") to insert "by agreement." The object
of bringing that Amendment before the Committee is for the sake of symmetry, if I may
use the expression, with Clause 3 of the Bill.
I do not think it requires a speech. It is
obvious that if you allow the Ministry of
Finance to acquire an ancient monument by
purchase or agreement under Clause 3, and
if you are going to allow the Ministry to purchase an archæological object under Clause
18, it would be desirable to have purchase by
agreement in each case. I beg to move,
185 Ancient Monuments Bill-

5 MAY 1926
Committee.
FER-
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
RARD: This is a matter on which I do not
really mind very much one way or the other.
The Government consider the words "by
agreement" are really unnecessary, but if the
hon. Member insists I will agree.
Mr. LESLIE: I thank the noble Viscount.
I would not ask to insert it only for that
reason.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am prepared to accept the Amendment if the hon. Member wishes it.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, page 10, line 18, at
end (end of Sub-section (4)) to add the following new Sub-sections:-
(5) The Ministry of Finance may make
regulations--
(a) prescribing any officer of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary as the person to
whom reports are to be made or other
information given with respect to archæological objects;
(b) prescribing the body or bodies in
whose custod- archæological objects may
be deposited; and
(c) generally for carrying this section
into effect.
(6) Nothing in this section shall be construed as affecting treasure trove, provided
that regulations under this section may be
made so as to apply to any archæological
object which is treasure trove, if the rights
of the Crown in such object have been
waived.
The object of this sub-Section is really to
provide machinery for carrying the provisions into effect. We prescribe an officer of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary as the person
to whom reports are to be made. The Ministry of Finance can make regulations about
the body or bodies into whose custody archæological objects may be deposited, and generally carrying this section into effect. Perhaps
I should mention the reason why we put in
the Royal Ulster Constabulary. It is in
deference to a recommendation from the
other side in regard to treasure trove. It
was thought there that an officer of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary should be the proper
person to receive it. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Samuel
Cunningham :-
Page 10, line 18, at end to add the following new sub-section :-
:-
(5) Property held under this Act will be
exempt from taxes and rates.
Amendment not moved.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSES 19 (Repeal) and 20 (Short title)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
FIRST SCHEDULE.
The Ministry of Education for Northern
Ireland.
The Queen's University, Belfast.
The Royal Irish Academy.
The Royal Institute of British Architects.
The Royal Society of Antiquaries of Ireland.
The Belfast Natural History and Philosophical Society.
The Belfast Naturalists' Field Club.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, page 11, line
2, to insert "Trinity College, Dublin." As
I mentioned the other day it is a new idea
in schedules of this sort to give university
representation. University representation was
not given in England prior to 1913. Such representation is given in the National Trust
Act dealing with the constitution of the
council of the Trust. And if we are to
decide to give university representation in
Northern Ireland, to which I do not presume
to offer any objection, it seems to me that if
we include Queen's University, Belfast, we
cannot refrain from including also the old
University of Trinity College, Dublin, which
has so many distinguished representatives
in Northern Ireland. Accordingly, I suggest
we should add after Ministry of Education
for Northern Ireland the words "Trinity
College, Dublin.”
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I fully appreciate the motives which
caused Senator Leslie to wish to include
Trinity College in the Schedule of this
Bill. But I might point out if we include
Trinity College, a college outside the jurisdiction of Northern Ireland, it might raise
an awkward precedent with regard to including on some future day in some future Bill
other institutions in the Free State. I might
mention that it is practically certain that
there will be a certain graduate of Trinity
College on the Advisory Committee. For that
reason on behalf of the Government I resist
the Amendment.
Colonel CRAWFORD: Perhaps, as Trinity
College has been mentioned, I might say that
I, unfortunately, on account of age, am one of
the senior graduates of Trinity College in
this part of the world, and as representing
the rest of the graduates here I might say
they would not care at all to have this Clause
put in, and would be quite satisfied with
what the Leader of the House suggested, representation on the Advisory Committee. The
graduates of Trinity College here would not
care for it.
187 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 7, to
leave out "The Belfast Naturalists' Field
Club" and insert the "National Trust, The
Corporation of the City of Londonderry, The
Association of Ulster County Councils." This
Amendment is on somewhat different lines.
We have just declined to give representation
to Trinity College, Dublin, but it is given to
the Royal Irish Academy and the Royal
Society of Antiquaries and so on. The point
I do suggest is that there is no precedent that
I know of for including such an institution
as the Belfast Naturalists' Field Club in the
schedule of an Act of Parliament. In the
Bill before us we are providing an Advisory
Committee to advise the Ministry on the
question whether this or that monument is of
national importance. I am afraid I cannot
see that the inclusion of this Belfast Club
would help to inspire public confidence in
that Committee. I assume that the Belfast
Naturalists' Field Club differs from the
Natural History and Philosophical Society
in that it has nothing in the shape of a
assume that the
Charter, and I also
membership of this Field Club is not entirely
under its control. I confess I cannot see any
reason for including this Field Club in the
First Schedule, and accordingly for the sake
of discussion, I beg to move its omission.
Before sitting down I should like, if I may,
to say how pleased I am to see the Minister
in charge of the Bill paying his first visit
to the Senate. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Mr. DORMAN: I do not know what reason
animated the Government when they put the
Belfast Naturalists' Field Club in the Bill,
but I presume it was mainly because the
Belfast Naturalists' Field Club would be regarded as pioneers in this work and as being
a body of individuals interested in the antiquities in Northern Ireland, and possibly
they would be the means of discovering most
of the monuments which are worthy of being
preserved by the Government for the people
as a whole. I think it would be a great
mistake to obliterate such a body as this
which is a voluntary society, and because of
their enthusiasm in the interests of ancient
monuments devote their own time to research.
In my judgment they would be a very valuable body to co-operate with the Government
in the preservation of ancient monuments
in the future.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I should like to support what Senator Dorman has said. It does
not seem to me to be of much consequence
whether the body has a Charter or not. The
Belfast Naturalists' Field Club has been in
existence a very long time and has done valuable work. It is a very active body indeed,
and as Senator Dorman pointed out, it is
very likely to make discoveries of interesting
monuments, and it should not be disqualified
on the mere technical point of having no
Charter. I therefore hope the Committee
will allow the name of the Belfast
Naturalists' Field Club to remain in the
Schedule.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I think it is the feeling of the Committee that the name of the Belfast
Naturalists' Field Club should remain in
the First Schedule, and with every word that
has been spoken by Senator Dorman and
Senator Colonel Sinclair I am in entire agreement. As a matter of fact, the Belfast
Naturalists' Field Club has got an anti
quarian and archæological section which is
doing very useful work, and they were consulted to some extent with reference to this
Bill. To say the least it would be very ungracious on our part now to object to their
inclusion in this Schedule. They will be
very useful on the Advisory Committee, and
for these reasons the Government desire to
retain their name in the First Schedule.
Mr. LESLIE: After what has been said
by the noble Viscount and Senator Colonel
Sinclair, I ask leave to withdraw the Amendment, and I do not move the insertion of the
other words.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
First Schedule agreed to.
SECOND SCHEDULE.
:-
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Item 1, column 3, add "except the Schedule."
Item 3, leave out the reference.
After item 4 insert "7 Edwd. 7, ch. cxxxvi,
The National Trust Act, 1907, section 35."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not propose to move
any of the Amendments standing in my name
on the Second Schedule, because they are
consequential on other matters, but I would
like to ask a question as regards the Ancient
Monuments Protection Act of 1892. Will the
noble Viscount say what will happen under
this Bill to Section 2 of that Act? Section 2
was the Section that provided that moneys
from the Church Temporalities Account
might be used for the maintenance of monuments which had passed under the Irish
Church Act, but the Committee will
member that there was some alteration made
with regard to the method of accounting for
Church Temporalities receipts. I confess 1
do not know what will happen if we repeal
the whole of that Act, and being anxious,
as I am sure hon. Members will realise, to
obtain as much money as possible for the
maintenance of such ancient monuments as
may be ultimately decided upon, I am loath
to give up any money if it happens to be
available.
