MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Emergency
Powers Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
Emergency Powers Bill.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, That this Bill be now
read a First time.
Before
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
the Motion is put, I should like to point out
that we have not even got a copy of the Bill
before us, and if copies of the Bill have been
available in the other House I think this
House should have been treated with a little
more courtesy.
Mr. SPEAKER: I understand that Bills
are coming down in a few minutes, but as a
matter of fact on the First Reading it is not
necessary to have a print of the Bill.
I am
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
satisfied so long as the Bill will be before us
for the Second Reading.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I would ask the Minister of Commerce to move the Second Reading of this
Bill. I think this is the first time the right
hon. Gentleman has been in this House and
we welcome him very heartily. I am sure
we will listen with great interest to the
speech he will make on the Second Reading
of this Bill, and we hope to see him here on
future occasions.
(Mr.
The MINISTER OF COMMERCE
Barbour): In response to a question that was
asked in another place yesterday afternoon
I announced the Government's intention of
seeking powers to protect the interests of the
community owing to the conditions which
have arisen in consequence of the very serious
strike which has broken out on the other
side of the Channel. The Government here
felt it was necessary to immediately take
such steps as might seem to them desirable
in order to protect the interests of the community generally, and especially of those
who, through lack of financial means, are
least able to face the hardships which an
upheaval such as is occurring across the
Channel inevitably brings in its train even
to us who are at this comparatively remote
distance.
The peculiar feature of the present
position is that the difficulties which face
uз do not arise through any industrial dispute or disagreement within our own borders,
where, I am glad to say, amicable relations
continue in every section of industry. Unfortunately, however, our sources of coal and
certain imported foodstuffs have, for the
time being, been seriously interfered with,
and, although the general public throughout
Northern Ireland has taken the position
calmly, and risen to the occasion, there has
been a tendency on the part of some to
endeavour to secure as large stocks of supplies as possible, regardless of the interests
of the general community.
There are in our midst a number whose
means of subsistence are rot such as to enable them to take thought for the morrow
by laying in adequate stocks of fuel and food
while these were freely available. These
less fortunate members of the community
must be protected, not only against any
difficulty in securing their daily needs, which
would be likely to arise from panic purchases on an unnecessarily large scale by
those who can afford to do so, but also so
far as this is practicable by means of regulation against any unwarranted increase
in prices of essential commodities.
Turning to the Measure, which is now in
the hands of bon. Members, I hope hon.
Members will forgive me the brief time given
for its consideration. They will realise that
if the steps we have in view are to be a
success they must be taken now. They will
eventually give the Government very ample
powers, and it is axiomatic that only the
existence of very exceptional circumstances
would justify the Government asking for
powers to intervene in the general commercial life of the community.
In 1920 the Imperial Parliament enacted
a Measure entitled the Emergency Powers
somewhat
Act, to deal with a situation
similar to that which faces us to-day. That
Act we have adapted to local conditions.
Stated briefly, the Bill empowers the
Governor, when satisfied that a state of
emergency has arisen, to issue a proclama-
191 Emergency Powers Bill—

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[The Minister of Commerce (Mr. Barbour)}
tion that such emergency exists, and thereafter to make regulations for securing the
essentials of life to the community and for
regulating the supply and distribution of
food and other necessities.
I would like to draw the attention of hon.
Members to an addition that was made at
the last moment in another place. It comes
in in line 19, and I am sorry time did not
permit the addition being made in the copies
of the Bill circulated in this House. I am
glad to see it has been made in some of the
copies, but to ensure that it will come before
all Members of this House I will read the
addition that has been made, and which was
taken from the British Act. It was thought
when first framing the Bill that it would
not be necessary to insert this particular
proviso here, as it merely repeats a right that
is already secured, to those who wish to take
advantage of it, by legislation. To ensure
that their rights under the Trades Disputes
Act with regard to peaceful picketing shall
not be interfered with by any Regulations
that may be made under this Act it is repeated here that those powers are not overridden by the Emergency Powers Bill, the
Second Reading of which I am now moving.
In line 19 there have been inserted these
words:-
Provided that no such Regulation shall
make it an offence for any person or persons to take part in a strike or peacefully
to persuade any other person or persons
to take part in a strike.
Before sitting down I should like to take
advantage of this opportunity of appealing
to the people of Ulster to take to heart the
appeal of the Prime Minister of Great Britain
that the community at large should bear
with fortitude and resolution any inconveniences which they may be called upon to
face, and so facilitate and support the efforts
that the Government is making to safeguard
the supply of the essentials of life for all
classes of the community. I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to support the
Second Reading of this Measure, and to
thank the Government for inserting the
Amendment suggested in another place. I
am sure we all regret the introduction of
strife, and especially of strife which is calculated to interfere with the normal carrying
on of business. In this connection there
comes to my mind a remarkable statement
made by a celebrated Irishman who was an
authority on the subject of legislation. He
said Government was a device of human
wisdom for the supply of human needs. It
appears to me that this Measure is calculated
to enable the Government to function in
regard to the preservation of the supply of
the needs of the people inside the area of
Northern Ireland, regardless of what may be
taking place outside.
It is unfortunate that these circumstances
should arise, but they have arisen from
causes that I think authorities in the past
have recognised as being more or less justified.
One of the most celebrated economists
of the last century stated that the example
of any individual or body of individuals who
willingly consent to a reduction in their
wages or in their standard of comfort should
never be held up for public approbation,
but everything that could be done should be
done to make such conduct become disgraceful in the interests of the community as a
whole. I make this statement, because I think
there is a tendency in the community to cast
all the blame on one of the parties engaged
in this particular strife, and to exonerate the
other and most powerful party from all
blame. The standard of comfort of the
miners will eventually be considerably reduced. I will not give hon. Senators exact
particulars, but I can assure them that in
the vast majority of mining districts in
Great Britain-
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: On a point of order, has à dissert
ation on the standard of living of the miners
anything to do with this Bill?
Mr. SPEAKER: A little wider latitude is
given on a Second Reading, and I propose to
allow the hon. Member to proceed.
Mr. DORMAN: My object was to show
that the miners are not absolutely to blame.
because, as I understand the situation across
the water, they have consented to enter into
negotiations provided that the undertaking
suggested in the Report of the Mines Commission is carried out. I think this Bill is
necessary, because only on Monday night 1
was in a more fortunate suburban area of
this city, and I discovered coal in large quantities being delivered at 9-30 and 10 p.m.,
which shows that steps were necessary on the
part of the Government to prevent the more
fortunate section of the community depriving
their poorer neighbours of some of the necessaries and comforts of life. I thank the
Government for accepting the Amendment in
another place, and I support the Bill.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I would
also like to say how pleased I am
that the Government has taken steps
to see that the poorer people of
the community are provided for
during the time of this conflict between the
Communists and the Government. I am
sorry at the same time that the Government
found it necessary to insert an Amendment in
the Bill such has been inserted. It amounts
to this, in my opinion, that it goes out to
the people of Northern Ireland to tell them
that they are entitled to go on strike the same
as those on the other side. It is a reminder
to the working men of the North of Ireland
that they also are entitled to join in it.
193 Emergency Powers Bill-

5 MAY 1926
Second Reading.
We know that an Emergency Powers Act
does not prevent them from doing anything
legal and constitutional. I fail to see the
necessity for putting an Amendment like that
in. We should be thankful-extremely thankful-that the people in Northern Ireland, the
trade unionists and those connected with the
labour unions, are so wise that they are
taking no part in this conflict. I say I do not.
agree with it all. The hon. Member who has
just sat down may claim that he is a representative of the workers, but I can say this-
I have been connected with the trade union
movement for the past 30 years, and been a
very active member, and I think I have done
all I possibly could to assist the worker to
improve his position and maintain his condition by collective bargaining. At the
present time I am a paying member of one
of the largest societies, and proud of it. But
this is not a trade dispute or an industrial
dispute. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
When I was more actively connected with
the society, and as the head of the society,
when we had a dispute between employers
and workers, the workman was entitled to
withdraw his labour. What is the position
to-day? It is not a dispute between the
mineowners and miners. It is a penalty
being inflicted upon the community. The
whole community is being held up. Workers
of every description at the present time are
put to the greatest possible inconvenience.
Food is in no way guaranteed to them over in
England. The country is being held up by a
political organisation and not a trade or
ganisation. I have always been in favour of
trade societies for collective bargaining.
Without them the workman would not
secure the position which they provide for
him. They would not have collective bargain.
ing for maintaining wages. But by no stretch
of imagination can I see or think that this is
a trade dispute. The miners were quite justified in withdrawing their labour. It is a
different thing for miners to withdraw, from
holding up the whole country-bus drivers,
tram drivers, the men who drive on the tube
railways.
I have been in conversation with many of
my own colleagues of the trade union movement since this strike commenced, and I have
yet to come across any sensible man who
agrees with it. I very much deplore it. We
in the North of Ireland have a Government
of our own, and in my opinion we should
take a very firm stand against those who incite willing workers to leave work as a matter
of sympathy with those on strike on the other
side. I do think the workers' representatives
in this part of the country are wise and
sensible and reasonable enough not to join
in a conflict such as this. I do say again I
am awfully sorry that the Government could
not stick to the Bill without introducing an
Amendment such as this, which is simply
conveying to the workers of the North of
Ireland that they are also entitled to join in
this conflict that at present exists on the
other side of the water.
Mr. LESLIE: I have listened with great
pleasure, as I am sure all the hon. Members
of the Senate have, to the speech which has
just been delivered, if I may say so, in so
eloquent terms by my hon. Friend on my left.
I could not but think that what he said as
regards the Amendment came from his heart,
but I wonder if he will pardon me if for a
moment I venture to appeal from his heart
to his head.
I am not quite sure whether owing to the
shortness of time available for considering
this Measure, the hon. Member has been able
to see that the Amendment introduced in
another place reproduces words from the Imperial Act of 1920. As these words are in the
Imperial Act I would venture to suggest that,
although I am not to be taken as having any
particular affection for that Statute which
the right hon. Gentleman the Minister of
Commerce mentioned, I am inclined to think
that as the Amendment introduced elsewhere
reproduces words from the Imperial Statute,
it would probably be found to assist the administration if it should unhappily become
necessary to exercise some of the powers being
conferred on the Northern Government. I
think it will be found to assist the administration if the Amendment referred to is
permitted to remain.
On general grounds I need scarcely say I
heartily support the Measure, and like my
hon. Friend I join in thanking the Government for having introduced it.
Mr. BARBOUR: I think the hon. Senator
Leslie, who has just sat down, has expressed
what was in the mind of the Government in
agreeing to this Amendment which was proposed in another place. The Government
feel, in bringing forward a Measure of this
kind and asking the public generally to repose so very large a degree of confidence in
their powers, that they must in the first
place be assured that no just right which is
already secured to them is being minimised
or reduced by a sort of round-the-corner
method.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Does this
not interfere with the rights of many trades?
Mr. BARBOUR: So the Government felt
that seeing this Clause is already in the
Emergency Powers Act passed in the Imperial
Parliament, when a demand was addressed
to them that it should be repeated in this
Act, sanction for which we are now soliciting, it was a request we could not reasonably refuse. We hope to enjoy during the
next few weeks the cordial co-operation of
every section of the community, and so we
would not like any section to feel aggrieved
by any part of this Act which they are being
G
Adjournment.

Emergency Powers Bill-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barbour]
asked to pass through at such short notice.
It is not conferring any new right upon
them. It is merely securing to them rights
which have already been conferred on them.
I sincerely hope any powers they may have
in that Act will not be exercised during the
next few weeks-I hope so at any rate-in
such a way as to interfere with the carrying
out of what is the main object of this Bill,
namely, to protect the interests and daily life
of especially the poorer portion of our population. I, therefore, hope the hon. Senator
will drop his objection to the inclusion of
the paragraph, by the insertion of which we
hope to secure the co-operation and goodwill
of one portion of the community at any rate.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be now
read a Second time) agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committed-
(Viscount Masserene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
agree to said Bill without any Amendment.
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adojurn
until to-morrow.-(Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard).
Colonel CRAWFORD: May I ask will
there be any sittings in this present week
after to-morrow?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I do not propose that the House
should sit after to-morrow until the following Tuesday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
a Quarter before Five of
the Clock.
197 Queen's University Bill—

6 MAY 1926
Second Reading.
[
FE
R
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 6th May, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Royal Assent.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am authorised by the Prime
inform the
Minister, on his behalf, to
Senate that the Lord Justices appointed for
the Government of Northern Ireland have
given, in the name of, and on behalf of
His Majesty the King, their Assent to the
following Bill:-
Emergency Powers.
Queen's University of Belfast Bill (S).
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move the Second Reading
of this very short Bill, the object of which
is to secure that representation on the
Senate of Queen's University may be given
to the City of Londonderry and the Councils
of the Six Counties. Hon. Members will
remember that about a year ago this Parliament passed an Act to enable the councils
of counties and county boroughs to make
grants to the University, and as several
councils are now doing so, directly out of
the rates, it is only proper that they should
be represented on the Senate.
The Constitution of the Senate of Queen's
University is set out in the First Schedule
(b) of the Irish Universities Act, 1908. This
Act does not provide for any variation or
amendment of that Constitution, and it has
been necessary, therefore, to introduce this
Bill.
Section 64, Sub-section 1 of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, states that no
law made by the Parliament of Northern
Ireland can have effect so as to alter the
Constitution of the Senate of Queen's
University until the proposed alteration is
approved by the Senate, the Academic
Council, and the Convocation of the
University. As the Preamble to the Bill
shows, the consent of these three bodies has
been secured.
The only other point to which, I think,
attention might be drawn is that the Schedule in the present Bill sets out more clearly
than the Schedule in the Irish Universities
Act, 1908, those members of the Senate who
are ex-officio members, nominated members,
and elected members. I do not think it is
necessary for me to say any more, except
to commend the Bill very heartily to the
favourable consideration of this House. I
beg to move.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I desire to support
this Bill and to commend it very heartily
to the favourable consideration of the Senate.
Perhaps I may be allowed to explain why
Queen's University has had to appeal to the
county councils for financial aid, in consequence of which representation on the Senate
of the University for the six county councils
and the City of Londonderry is now sought.
The University has an income of £10,000 a
year from the Church Temporalities Commissioners, but that is more in the nature
of an endowment than a direct grant from
the Exchequer. In addition, the Government
of Northern Ireland supplies £26,000 per
annum, but it has to be remembered that
whereas there were only 514 students attending the University before the war there are
now 1,169 students, so that hon. Senators
will realise what an immense increase in
the resources of the University is necessary
to cope with such an increase in the number
of students.
The Senate will be interested to know that
all modern Universities across the water receive a grant from the rates. For example,
Birmingham supplies £17,000 to its University, Bristol £19,000, Newcastle-on-Tyne
£9,000, Aberystwyth £12,000, and Belfast supplies its University with £250. It will thus
be seen that the contribution from the rates
from the city of Belfast and the Province is
comparatively insignificant. When I say that
last year we had to face a deficit of £5,500,
and in the previous year a deficit of £3,300,
hon. Members will understand how it comes
that we had to appeal to a wider area for
some means to supplement our annual income.
Notwithstanding the acute agricultural and
commercial depression the different county
councils most carefully considered this
matter, and some of the county councils
agreed to strike a farthing rate for the benefit of the University. Some of the others
have not yet decided on that point, but when
the City of Londonderry and the counties
who have not yet decided to contribute have
considered these facts perhaps they may see
their way to contribute in a similar way.
While we are very glad that the support
we have so far received gets us over part of
our difficulty, it does not enable us to carry
out our desire to have new teachers' chairs
and laboratories, which are necessary to keep
the University up-to-date and to meet the
ever-increasing demands of science. The
figures I have mentioned might be summarised by saving that Queen's University does
-199

Queen's University Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sinclair]
more work for less money than any other
University in the Kingdom. The truth of
this will be realised when we compare the
position of Queen's University with that of the
University at Aberystwyth, which is a University comparable with Queen's. The income of Queen's University is £11,000 less
than that of Aberystwyth, and the students
at Queen's number 400 more.
The discrepancy disclosed by these figures
is even worse than it appears, because across
the water scholarships are provided by the
local authorities. For instance, Birmingham
spends £730 per annum on scholarships, Newcastle-on-Tyne £752, and Aberystwyth £20,
whereas Queen's University has to spend more
than £3,000 out of its annual income for the
provision of scholarships.
I should like to remove a misapprehension
that is widespread throughout the country
districts, and that is that Queen's University
is a local institution, and that it makes a
profit out of the students coming to it. The
Six Counties supply roughly about half the
students in the University, and the City of
Belfast and environs supply the other half.
Therefore, it is not by any means a local
institution when half the students are drawn
from the counties. I think it is only right
and proper that the counties should feel that
the University is their University as well as
the property of the City of Belfast, and that
they should take a proper pride in it and
decide to secure its efficiency by furnishing
the means for its proper equipment and for
the provision of the Chairs for teachers that
may be necessary as time goes on.
Our expenditure at Queen's is £62 per
student per annum, and when I explain
that only one-third of that £62 is supplied
by the parents or guardians of students, and
the University supplies over two-thirds, it
will be seen how closely we have to finance
things in order to keep within our income.
The University is managed on very economical lines indeed. If we only spend £62
per student at Queen's, £91 per student is
spent in Birmingham, £127 in Bristol, £91
in Newcastle-on-Tyne, and £90 in Aberystwyth. That shows we have to finance our
University very closely indeed in order to
furnish our students with a University
education.
It will be seen why the University, on
account of the two deficits to which I have
referred, had to make an appeal to the
Counties, and I am very gratified to know
that some of them have responded at a
time when there is acute agricultural and
commercial depression. We are very grateful for what they have done, and we hope
their example will be followed by those
authorities who have not yet decided to
contribute. Let me say that a halfpenny
rate instead of a farthing rate would provide £10,000 a year additional income for
the University, and that would relieve us
of our immediate anxieties, and supply
something to enable us to increase our
teaching staff, and to provide residential
halls for the male students, which is the
crying need of the University, the generosity
of the Misses Riddel having provided hostels
for the women students.
For a long time the University was regarded as a mere Government institution,
and the general public took no interest in
it, but I think the time has come when they
should recognise that university education
is the anex of the educational pillar.
Although the Government provides for
elementary and secondary education, we
hope that this grant to the University may
be supplemented as a result of publicspirited action on the part of the county
councils, all of whom, we trust, will in
more prosperous times strike a halfpenny
rate, which would give the University an
additional income of £10,000 per annum, and
thus enable it to carry on with all possible
efficiency.
I very cordially commend this Measure
to the sympathetic consideration of the
Senate. I hope this House will see its way
to grant representation to the Counties and
to the City of Londonderry, and that we
may have representatives who will take a
lively interest in the management of the
University on its governing body. Queen's
University is not a mere local institution.
It belongs to all classes and creeds of the
community throughout the Province.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to thank the hon.
and gallant Gentleman for the very able
speech he has delivered, and for the information he has conveyed to the Senate. I
hope that information will go out to the
public of Northern Ireland, because if there
is one thing that is necessary for the efficient
carrying on of educational institutions in
any country it is that widespread interest
should be aroused in all the educational
institutions of that particular country. On
a question such as this I can, perhaps,
speak with more emphasis than most hon.
Senators, inasmuch as I have never had
the advantage of a university education,
my university having been the battle of
life and observation of my fellow-men
around me from day to day.
I have often lamented that the opportunity of acquiring a university education
was not brought within my reach in early
life. I think that if the facts given to us
to-day were widely disseminated amongst
the public of Northern Ireland they would
be calculated to arouse keen interest in the
welfare of Queen's University. The figures
the hon. and gallant Gentleman has given
to us prove beyond the possibility of doubt
that this University is working on an aver-
201 Queen's University Bill—

6 MAY 1926
Pensions Bill.

5.
age of two-thirds the cost of the various
other universities the hon. and gallant
Senator quoted in his speech. Therefore, it
affords me great pleasure to support this
particular Measure, especially in view of the
fact that in the Northern area
we have
for the first time had education brought
under the control of local representative
bodies, which really means bringing
education directly under the control of the
people.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: It gives
me great pleasure to support this Bill, because at one time the county with which I am
connected was one of the first supporters of
the old Queen's University. We were also
one of its first supporters under the new
scheme, and when times are more prosperous
I will do my best to induce the County Council
to strike a halfpenny rate, instead of the
present farthing rate, for the University.
There is one point I would like to mention,
and it is with regard to those county councils
which up to the present have refused to give
any grant from the rates to the University.
In my opinion a county or city which has not
given a grant should have no right to representation on the governing body of the University. The representatives of such counties
would probably not attend, and they would
not be any use. I think only those
authorities which support the University
should have representation on its governing
body, and it is not fair to them that those
bodies which have refused to contribute
should have an equal privilege.
a
I do not know whether it would be possible
to insert in the Bill a provision to the effect
that only those authorities which give a contribution from the rates should be entitled
to representation on the Senate of the University, but I am strongly of opinion that
that is what should be done. We quite recognise that Queen's University is in
different position to that it occupied before it
was separated from Trinity College.
number of medical and law students has
largely increased, and I have been wondering
whether it would be possible to change the
If it was
name of Queen's University.
possible to call it the University of Northern
Ireland people would begin to realise that
it was not merely a Belfast institution as
it has been looked upon in the past.
The
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I would like to make one remark in
reference to what the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman who has just sat down has said.
He very naturally wishes that those counties
which have not struck a rate in aid of the
funds of Queen's University should not be
represented on the Senate. I understand that
the authorities of Queen's University expressed the wish that all the counties should
be represented whether at the moment they
contributed or not-I presume with the view
that in the future they would contribute.
For that reason all the counties have been
given representation, even though at the
moment some of them do not contribute.
It
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: The
only objection I have is that once you get
them represented on the Senate of the University you will never get them off again, and
some of them will never contribute.
might be an incentive to them to contribute
something if it was pointed out that they
were not represented on the Governing body.
If they do get representation without giving
a grant their representatives will surely be
too ashamed to attend the meetings of the
Senate.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the whole
House for Wednesday next.

Pensions (Increase) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD:
That this Bill be
I beg to move,
now read the Third time. I promised yester-
One was
day to deal with two questions.
the Amendment moved by Senator Hill Dickson and also spoken to by Senator Colonel
Sharman Crawford, and the other was the
enquiry raised by Senator Glendinning, who
I regret is not here to-day.
The Amendment moved by Senator Hill
Dickson was in effect that pensioners amongst
the Petty Sessions clerks should be excluded
from the benefits of the Bill. He gave as his
reasons that these officers were for all practical purposes only part-time officers, and
that it was not right that their pensions
should be increased. In my remarks on the
Committee Stage I pointed out that the actual
increased charge on the Petty Sessions Fund
by reason of the Clause would be negligible,
Pensions Bill.
Third Reading.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
or very slight, but I am prepared to argue
the point on wider grounds.
In the first place it must be borne in mind
that the salaries of the majority of these
officers are even still very small, although
they have been increased by the cost-of-living
bonus. The salaries are small because it is
recognised that the appointment is in many
cases not a whole-time one, although it must
be remembered that a Petty Sessions clerk
very often has to do duties outside the
ordinary work of the Court on the days on
which it meets. The smallness of the salaries
is already reflected in the smallness of the
pensions; now the hon. Senator would wish
to penalise the pensioners a second time for
the part-time nature of their occupation by
withholding from them the slight increases
that in some cases would arise under this
Act. His arguments would equally have applied to the increases of which a number of
them are already in receipt under the
Pensions (Increase) Act, 1920.
In the second place, I would ask the hon.
Member to consider the position of a clerk
who retires at the present day. For purposes
of pension in his case account is taken of
the cost-of-living bonus that is added to his
salary. Now if this is a proper course to
take, then it equally follows that the prewar pensioner, who had not a cost-of-living
bonus added to the salary on which his pension was calculated, might justly claim that
his pension should be put into some nearer
relation to his present expenditure on the
bare necessities of life as is being in effect
done for the clerk who goes out on pension.
In the third place, it must be borne in
mind that very great limitations are in force
in connection with these increases, that is to
say, that before they can be given the private
means of the clerk must be taken into consideration. As many of these are, for example, police pensioners it follows that the
increase will only be given in a minimum of
cases.
Lastly, it seems to me that the argument of
the hon. Senator if carried to its logical conclusion really comes to this, that he considers
that owing to the part-time nature of a
Petty Sessions clerk's duty he should not be
pensionable at all. That, of course, is a
question which could not be discussed on the
present Bill. The Petty Sessions Acts themselves provide for a pension scheme for Petty
Sessions clerks, and until that provision is
altered we must accept the position as it
stands. For these reasons the Government
would not be prepared to accept an Amendment.
With regard to the second question, which
was put by Senator Glendinning, the question he raised was whether the present Bill
applies to national school teachers who were
The
pensioned in or subsequent to 1918.
reply to this is really contained in the Pensions Increase Act of 1920, that is to say, the
basic Act on which the present Bill is
drafted. The 1920 Act provides in effect that
the increases apply to all teachers who were
in receipt of pensions on the 16th August,
1920, but the increases are modified in the
case of those who were pensioned between the
4th August, 1914, and the 16th August, 1920,
in this way, namely, that if in the latter
case the pension was assessed on a salary
that had been increased after the 4th August,
1914, either by reason of war conditions or
by a general improvement in the scale of
emoluments, the increase to be granted when
added to the pension was not to bring the
pension to a higher figure than the same
pensioner would have got if he had been pensioned off before the 4th August, 1914, and had
received the full addition to that pension
allowed under the 1920 Act.
My reply, therefore, to the hon. Senator
is that unless I have full details of any particular pensioner who retired between 1914 and
1920 I cannot say how far he is entitled to
an increase under this Bill. I am sure, however, that if he approaches the Ministry of
Finance with full details they will supply
him with the necessary information. I beg to
move.
Colonel SHARMAN
CRAWFORD: The
only reason I objected to an increase of
Petty
pension being granted to certain
Sessions clerks was that from the speech of
the noble Viscount one would have thought
all Petty Sessions clerks did the same work,
whereas there is a very wide difference in
the work they do. I think Senator Hill
Dickson, who proposed the Amendment,
was referring to a Petty Sessions clerk who
was only engaged for an hour a month, and
it is carrying the matter too far when you
think of that man even receiving a pension
at all, whether large or small.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I might ask the
noble Viscount to tell us, when he is replying to the debate, whether the impression
which is in my mind is correct and that
is that the salaries of Petty Sessions clerks
vary very widely in different parts of the
Six Counties, and that the variation is due
to some attempt being made to estimate the
demand made on the time of the relative
individuals. In other cases, of course, a
Petty Sessions clerk is allowed to take other
work, such as acting as clerk to a council.
I remember the noble Marquess (the Marquess of Londonderry) when Leader of the
House, saying so on another Bill. It has
always been held that a Petty Sessions
clerk was a whole-time officer, and as such
was subject to being required to do work
outside the normal duties of a Petty Sessions clerk. The main point is whether the

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Member who has just
spoken is perfectly correct. The salaries of
Petty Sessions clerks vary with their work,

Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I do

increase of pension to Petty Sessions clerks
generally, but only to certain clerks in
country districts who are engaged only an
hour or so a month.

of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.

Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to ask the leave of the House
to postpone the Second Reading of this Bill
until to-morrow.
Ancient Monuments Bill [s].
Order for receiving Report of Amendments
read.
Moved: That this Report be now received.
-(The Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE 5 (Notice to be given by owners
of certain monuments).
(2) Where the owner of any ancient
monument which is included in any such
list of monuments as aforesaid, after being
notified as provided in the last preceding
sub-section, proposes to demolish or remove
in whole or in part, alter structurally, or
make additions to, the monument, or make
excavations in the neighbourhood thereof,
he shall forthwith give notice of his intention to the Ministry of Finance, and
shall not, except in the case of urgent
necessity, commence any work of demolition, removal, alteration, addition or
excavation, for a period of two months
after having given such notice; and any
person guilty of a contravention of or noncompliance with this provision shall be
liable, on conviction under the Summary
Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts, to a fine not
exceeding one hundred pounds, or to imimprisonment for a term not exceeding
three months, or to both.

Mf. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 5,
page 4, line 25, to leave out the words "one
hundred," and insert instead thereof the
word twenty." This is a small point which
I brought before the Committee when I was
moving another Amendment, which would
have dealt with this proposal somewhat indirectly. The point I desire to make is that
the penalty of £100 is somewhat large to
place in the power of a Magistrates' Court,
and it is somewhat in excess of the necessities of the case. I will tell the House
that originally, when the English Bill was
before the House of Commons, the penalty
suggested was £20, and that was subsequently altered to £100, owing to circumstances which are, perhaps, rather amusing
to those who care to dig into the Debates
of that period. I suggest we should reduce
the fine to £20, and I think we are entitled
to do that more so because we have now
made the period of notice two months instead of one month. I beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Senator, I
regret the Government do not see their way
to accept a diminution of this penalty, which
is the same as is provided for in Great
Britain. This matter was discussed on the
Committee Stage to a certain extent, and the
penalty is a fine not exceeding £100. After
all, what is the owner asked to do under
this section? He is to refrain from taking
steps which might lead to the ruin of a
monument, without giving due notice to the
Ministry of Finance, in order that the
Ministry, if it thinks fit, may take steps to
preserve the monument for the Province.
On the whole I cannot see any valid reason
why the hon. Senator wishes the penalty
reduced to £20, and I hope he will withdraw
his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I will not press the point.

CLAUSE 7 (Effect of Preservation Orders).
(1) While a Preservation Order is in
force, the monument to which the Order
relates shall not be demolished or removed,
nor shall any additions or alterations be
made thereto or any work carried out in
connection therewith, except with the
written consent of the Ministry of Finance.
(2) If, while a Preservation Order is in
force, it appears to the Ministry of Finance
that, owing to the neglect of the owner of
the monument, the monument is liable to
fall into decay, the Ministry of Finance
may make an Order appointing that Ministry guardian of the monument for the
period during which the Preservation
Order is in force, and in that case provisions of this Act shall, during that
period, take effect as if the Ministry of
Finance had been appointed guardian by
virtue of a deed executed by every person
seised or possessed of, or entitled to, an
estate or interest in the monument.
(3) Any order made under the last preceding sub-section may be revoked at any
time by the Ministry of Finance.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, in Clause 7, page 5,
line 34, after the word "work" (or any work)
to insert the word "be." This is purely a
drafting Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, in Clause 7, page 5,
line 39, after the word "owner" (owner of
the monument) to insert the words "or person having control." This again is a drafting Amendment, but I think I ought to explain to the House the necessity for it. The
word owner which occurs in sub-Section 2 of
this Clause is defined in sub-Section 4 of
Clause 1 of this Bill, and it is possible the
definition might narrow too much the intention of this Clause. In other words, the
actual owner might be able to prove that he
himself was not neglectful, and that some
other person who had control was really the
culprit. Accordingly the word "owner" has
been amplified in this Clause by the addition
of the words "or person having control." I
beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: As I spoke on the Committee Stage as regards the definition of sub-
Clause 4 of Clause 1, with special reference
to the fact that the term "owner" did not
seem to me to be sufficiently defined, I ought
perhaps to say that I quite concur in the
suggested Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause 11 (Powers of county councils with
respect to ancient monuments).
(1) The provisions of this Act with respect to the maintenance of and access, and
penalties for injury, to ancient monuments
shall apply in the case of any ancient
monument-
(a) of which a county council has, before the passing of this Act, become
guardian under sub-section (2) of section
nineteen of the Local Government (Ireland) Act, 1898; or
(b) which has been before the passing
of this Act, or thereafter, becomes vested
in a county council by virtue of an order
under sub-section (3) of section fourteen
of the Irish Land Act, 1903.
as if the county council were substituted
in those provisions for the Ministry of
Finance; and the expenses incurred by a
county council under this section shall be
raised by means of the poor rate as a
county at large charge.
(2) Where a county council is guardian
of an ancient monument, such council may,
with the consent of the owner of the monument, transfer the guardianship therdof
to the Ministry of Finance, and the provisions of this Act shall apply accordingly.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, in Clause 11, page
7, to leave out from the word "The" (The
provisions of this Act) to the end of sub-
Section (1) and insert instead thereof:-
"following provisions of this Act, namely-
Part I;
So much of Part II as relates to the gift,
devise or bequest of ancient monuments;
and
The foregoing provisions of this Part;
shall apply to a county council (including
the council of a county borough) as if the
county council were substituted in those
provisions for the Ministry of Finance;
and, in addition to its powers in relation
to monuments of which it is guardian, a
county council shall have power to maintain and preserve any monument-
(a) which has been accepted by that
council, or which it has agreed to maintain or preserve, under this Act; or
(b) which has been before the passing
of this Act, or thereafter becomes, vested
in that council by virtue of an order
under sub-section (3) of section fourteen
of the Irish Land Act, 1903;
The expenses incurred under this Act by
a county council shall, in the case of a
county borough council, be defrayed out
of any rate or fund which the Ministry
of Home Affairs may on the application of
the council approve, and shall, in the case
of any other county council, be defrayed
out of the county fund as a county at large
charge."
211 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Report Stage.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
This is an Amendment of the Clause which
I undertook on the Committee Stage to alter
at the request of the hon. Senators, providing for the enlargement of the scope of the
powers of county councils as compared with
the powers given in the Clause as it stands.
Generally speaking, the effect of this Amendment is that the county councils will have
exactly the same powers as the Ministry of
Finance in regard to the guardianship and
acceptance of ancient monuments, save that
they will not be allowed to purchase, nor
will they possess any power of making Preservation Orders. I may, however, add that
although there is no specific provision made
in this Bill, it is intended that the expert
advice of the Ancient Monuments Committee
and the Ministry of Finance will always be
available to the county councils if they desire to have recourse to these bodies. I beg
to move.
Mr. LESLIE: It is really a pleasure to
express my thanks to the Government for
having gone so far to meet the proposals
which I submitted not only on the Second
Reading but also on the Committee Stage.
The Amendment which has just been moved,
as the House will appreciate, gives very nearly
all that I asked for the other day as regards
local authorities. I am afraid there were
some thirty Amendments on that point,
twenty-nine of which were not moved. The
point of difference between this Amendment
and those I had put down is that I had suggested that power should be given to an
urban district. The Government has not
conceded that, or at all events it has not
yet conceded it, although perhaps in the
future that may happen.
As regards the rest of the Amendment, the
effect of it is to preserve for the county council the power which they had under the old
law of becoming guardian of any monument
which might be conveyed to them. That right
would have been taken away by the Bill
as it was first brought into this House. It
would have been taken away except as regards those cases where a power had
already been exercised which could not have
been exercised in the future. That has now
been set right and the Government, I observe,
has also given me what I asked for, that is,
they have extended the powers of Clause 10,
the powers to receive voluntary contributions
to the county councils as well as to the Ministry of Finance.
I notice that the noble Viscount referred
to the advice of the Ancient Monuments Committee being at the disposal of the county
councils. I think that is provided for sufficiently and satisfactorily, if I may speak for
a moment from the county council point of
view. The Clause I should refer the House
to is Sub-Clause 3 of Clause 13, which provides that the Ancient Monuments Committee may, if so requested by the owner of a
monument, give advice free of charge. In
some cases of course that will cover the
county councils, but in cases of guardianship, which no doubt are the cases the noble
Viscount had in his mind, perhaps it is
not quite so clear.
I do not think there is any other point
in the Amendment to which I should wish
to call the attention of the House, except
perhaps the fact that there is a definitely
new provision which enables the county councils to accept bequests and gifts of ancient
monuments. That, I think I am correct in
saying, is an entirely new power. They had
not got that power before, so that for practical purposes I think the only power they
have not got as regards acquisition is the
power to purchase. I think the insertion of
this Amendment will give great satisfaction
and perhaps will remove some of the objections which were urged in certain quarters
against some of the provisions of the Bill.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 12 (Public access to ancient monuments).
The public shall have access to any monument which is vested in the Ministry of
Finance or a county council, or of which
that Ministry or a county council is
guardian, at such times and under such
conditions as may from time to time be
prescribed by regulations of that Ministry
or such county council respectively:
Provided that-
(i) this section shall not apply to any
monument of which the Ministry of
Finance or a county Council has been appointed or constituted the guardian before
the commencement of this Act, except in
cases where the consent of the owner has
been given to the public having access to
the monument, either by the deed appointing or constituting that Ministry or such
county council the guardian, or where a
right of access has arisen through the
acquiescence of the owner or otherwise;
and
(ii) where the deed appointing the
Ministry of Finance the guardian of the
monument is executed after the commencement of this Act, if the deed so
provides, the public shall not have access
to the monument without the consent of
the owner of the monument.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 12,
to leave out paragraphs (i) and (ii) on pages
7 and 8, and insert instead thereof :-
"this section shall not-
(a) in the case of a monument which before the passing of this Act has been vested
in the Ministry of Finance or a County
Council or of which that Ministry or a
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Report Stage.
12 MAY 1926
pt
1 12

ACT
bet
B

DIGH
Te A
ta
FISP

County Council has become the guardian,
operate to confer any right of access other
than any such right which may exist at the
commencement of this Act; or
(b) in the case of a monument which
after the passing of this Act becomes
vested in the Ministry of Finance or a
County Council or of which that Ministry
of a County Council becomes guardian,
operate to confer any right of access other
than any such right to which the owner of
the monument and the occupier of any
land which it may be necessary to cross to
obtain access to the monument may consent."
This Amendment deals with the difficult
question of public access. I think we must
all agree that if a monument is to be maintained at all out of any form of public funds
it is necessary that the public should have
access to the monument, partly because if
the public are providing the money they are
entitled to see how it is being spent, and
partly because if you maintain a monument
from the point of view of education you
defeat your object if the monument is not
accessible.
As may be remembered, I put forward an
Amendment to this Clause in Committee,
and the noble Viscount stated at the commencement of the Committee's deliberations
that the Government were in sympathy with
my Amendment, but at a later stage the
noble Viscount said he did not quite see
how the Amendment read, and suggested it
might be better drafted. Accordingly, I
have had another look at it, and the result
is the words which I now suggest to the
House.
It is necessary, I am afraid, to examine
the Clause in some little detail to see what
it is we have to deal with. We want to
provide for access to a monument, whether
vested in the Ministry or in the County
Council, or whether those authorities are
simply the guardians. It is obvious, I
think, that, as regards the monuments that
are vested, or, rather, have been vested in
the past, we cannot give more powers by
this Bill than exist already, and, accordingly, I tried to deal with that in paragraph
(a) of my Amendment. Paragraph (b) is to
meet the cases of monuments which, after
the passing of the Act, become vested or
guarded, and I hope to manage to get away
from the difficulty as regards what I call
the Land Purchase Act cases, because, as
I understand it, the Land Purchase Acts do
not convey any right of public access to a
monument which has passed to the tenant
purchaser. That is subject to this qualification: that in individual cases it will
probably be found on an inspection of the
vesting Clause that some provisions have
been made to effect access, but Land Act
cases do present a distinct difficulty.
For the rest of the Amendment, which is
principally paragraph (d), I would suggest
that there should be a definite step taken
in order to prove consent rather than that,
as it originally appeared in the Bill, the
consent might arise negatively. I propose to
get rid of a rather troublesome expression
in the original Bill, the objection to which
is, as the House may remember, that if you
rely on the possible effect of equitable
acquiescence you leave the point at issue to
be settled in every individual case, and the
possibilities of law suits seem endless, and
nobody would be any better off.
Whether or not the House will prefer the
Amendment I now submit I do not know.
The point is a very difficult one, but one
may hope that, having regard to the good
nature of owners generally, difficulties may
not arise. But, as the Bill stands, I am
afraid there are possibilities of trouble,
which one would like to remove if possible.
I, accordingly, beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This is a point which the hon.
Senator dealt with pretty fully in Committee, and on which the hon. and gallant
Senator (Colonel Sharman Crawford) also
touched. I think the best way to explain
the matter is to go into this Clause in some
little detail. It will be remembered that two
rights have to be preserved. One is the
right of the taxpayer who is paying for the
maintenance of a monument to be able to
have access to it. The other is the right of
the owner, who has handed over a monument
to the guardianship of the Ministry of
Finance, to have inserted in the deed a
proviso that the public shall not have access.
Normally speaking the Ministry of Finance
would not accept the monument unless a
right of access was secured, but it may
happen that a monument is of such a nature,
for example, only of interest to the scientist,
or so situate by relation to surrounding property, that while it is very desirable that it
should be taken over from the point of view
of national importance, yet it would be unreasonable to have free access for the public
at large.
The same would apply to monuments taken
over by a County Council, and as regards the
particular point raised by the hon. Senator
all that I can say is if a monument is thought
to be of public interest and one to which the
public should have the right of free access,
then neither the State nor Local Authority
should take it over from the owner without
at the same time seeing to it that a means
of access exists.
As regards the words which the hon.
Senator, Mr. Leslie, criticised, I think in the
latter part of his speech, namely, "where a
right of access has arisen through the acquie-
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Report Stage.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
scence of the owner"-and he said it would
probably lead to a number of law suits-I
am advised by the legal advisers that the
wording of the Clause is accurate. If an
owner holds that he has not acquiesced in
such a right, he has his ordinary remedy in
the Courts of Law against the common trespasser. However, I do not anticipate there
will be many actions of this kind. For these
reasons the Government consider the Section
as it stands is better than the Amendment of
the hon. Member, and therefore I regret to
say I cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: With much that has been
said I am of course in agreement; in fact I
think we are almost getting at the stage of
being accused of being in dispute over mere
words. However, I think everybody will be
satisfied that this question is a very difficult
one, and I think everybody will agree that it
has been thoroughly well gone into. The
cases the noble Viscount alluded to I admit
might just as well have arisen under the
Amendment I propose as under the existing
Clause. In the circumstances I think hon.
Members will wish that I should withdraw
the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, in Clause 12, page 8
paragraph (ii), after the words "Finance"
(Ministry of Finance), insert "or a County
Council." This is consequential on the wider
powers given to County Councils under the
Amendment I previously moved.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 14 (Expenses of Ministry of
Finance and Advisory Committee).
There shall be paid out of moneys provided by Parliament such expenses as may
be incurred by the Ministry of Finance in
the exercise of its powers in connection
with-
(a) the inspection and maintenance of
ancient monuments, whether vested in
the Ministry of Finance or of which that
Ministry is guardian;
(b) the purchase by agreement of any
ancient monument; and
(c) the payment of compensation for
acts done under this Act involving interference with private rights of property;
and also such expenses of the Ancient
Monuments Advisory Committee as may
be sanctioned by the Ministry of Finance.
(d) the purchase or assistance towards
the purchase of archaeological objects of
which the finding has been reported to
the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the end of
Clause 14, to insert the following new Subsections:-
"(2) The cost of any deed required for the
purposes of the Act shall, if the Ministry
of Finance is a party to such deed, be paid
out of moneys provided by Parliament;
(3) Any deed required for the purposes
of this Act shall be exempt from stamp
duty."
These are two Sub-sections which perhaps
I ought to explain. They are directed, as the
House will appreciate, to facilitating transfer
to the Ministry for the benefit of the public
of monuments and sites of monuments. If a
monument is to be conveyed to the Ministry
a Deed is required and the cost of that Deed
may perhaps be material. The cost would be
dependent to some extent on the character
of the Title involved, and it occurred to me
that it was only reasonable that if the
Ministry of Finance is to benefit under the
Deed, the Ministry of Finance should pay for
it, of course out of moneys provided by
Parliament.
I venture to bring the Amendment before
the House although, as will be seen, it is an
Amendment on a strictly financial Clause,
and I recognise that supposing the Amendment should meet with the approval of
the House the paragraph containing it will
not appear in the Bill when it leaves this
House and starts on its voyage to another
place. Consequently I feel in some little
difficulty as to whether one should ask the
noble Viscount to assist us with his advice
on this matter, or whether it should not be
left entirely to the House.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Senator, I
think it is quite clear that it is not within
the discretion of this House to insert this
Amendment, because you are seeking to insert in the Bill Sub-sections proposing a
direct charge on the Exchequer, and I do not
think it is competent for the House to do
that.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am of opinion that this
Amendment is not in order.
Mr. LESLIE: I must, of course, bow to
your ruling, but I should have liked to
have submitted-
Mr. SPEAKER: While I will not alter
my ruling, I shall be very glad to hear what
the hon. Member has to say.
Mr. LESLIE: The point I desire to make
is that inasmuch as the House has no power
to increase a charge on the public, at the
same time the Bill, as it is brought into
this House, already contains a charge on the
public funds, and the charge on the public
funds which is already contained in Clause
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Report Stage.
12 MAY 1926
14 will have to be taken out of the Bill.
It seemed to me that as we were discussing
financial matters there is no objection to.
our amending a document-which, after all,
is our own private document-up to the time
when it leaves this House and goes to
another place. It was on those lines that I
ventured to bring the matter before this
House, but after what you, Mr. Speaker,
have said I cannot, of course, pursue the
subject.
CLAUSE 17 (Definition of "monument"
and "ancient monument," 45 and 46 Vic.,
e. 73).
In this Act the expression "monument"
erection
includes any site, structure or
(whether above or below the surface) other
than an ecclesiastical building which is
for the time being used for ecclesiastical
purposes; and the expression "ancient"
monument" includes any monument or
part or remains of a monument, the preservation of which is a matter of public interest by reason of the historic, architectural, traditional, artistic, or archæological
interest attaching thereto, and the site of
any such monument or of any remains
thereof, and any part of the adjoining land
which may be required for the purpose of
fencing, covering in, or otherwise preserving the monument from injury, or for the
purpose of preserving the amenities of the
monument, and also includes the means of
access thereto.
Mr. LESLIE: The next Amendment I beg
to move is in Clause 17, page 9, line 26, after
the word "purposes" (purposes; and the expression) insert, "or anything contained in
such ecclesiastical building." We are now
in the Definition Clause and it occurred to
me and I mentioned the point in Committee
but withdrew it because the noble Viscount
desired to look further into the questionthat as we are here dealing with churches
and as it is very possible that there might
be inside churches certain structures or erections the term is very wide-which are detached and are not part of the freehold, such
movable structures or erections would not be
covered by the exemptions already in the
Bill. As it would be very undesirable to
admit the exercise of any authority in
matters of that kind by any authority other
than the Church authority it might be worth
while to insert the simple words I suggest.
I confess I should like those words put in
the Bill, but if they are objected to there
is a protection which will occur to the House
and which I will not allude to at the moment.
I beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I may inform the hon. Senator that
an Amendment relating to anything contained
in an ecclesiastical building or in any building used for ecclesiastical purposes in addition to the building itself the Government
consider to be unnecessary, and for this
reason. If the contents referred to are monuments fixed to a wall or to the floor it would
be part of the fabric and, therefore, comprise the building itself. On the other hand,
anything movable in the building would
come under the definition of site, structure,
or erection, and therefore it would not be
considered a monument for the purposes of
the Bill. For that reason the Government
is not able to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry I did not catch
what the noble Viscount said. Did he say a
movable monument would be
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Would not be.
Mr. LESLIE: Evidently the Government
do not think it possible that there could be
a movable monument inside a church. However, the point of view I put forward is
possibly sufficiently defended in the Government of Ireland Act, although I would have
preferred that the words had gone into the
Bill. I will not press the point.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the end
of Clause 17 to add as a new Sub-Clause:--
"(2) For the purposes of this Act a monument shall not be deemed to be an ancient
monument unless it is mainly-
(a) of artificial construction or of
interest on account of artificial embellishment, and
(b) of a date prior to the accession of
King George the First."
We now approach a new Sub-clause put
down to enable us to discuss two questions
which really arise out of our discussion of
the Definition Clause on the Committee
Stage. The first proposal I have put down
is in consequence of the introduction of the
word "site.". The word "site" before
"structure or erection" does not occur in the
English Act, and is, if I may say so, a little
invention of our own. The insertion of that
word brings up at once the question of the
Giant's Causeway. The Giant's Causeway
was alluded to in the House of Lords during
the debates on these Measures there-not, I
think, the actual Act of 1913, but the Act of
the year before. Lord Sheffield asked the
question, but it does not appear from the
Debates that he got an answer.
I rather gather the matter was dealt with
privately and it was pointed out that owing
to the phraseology subsequently adopted in
the English Act the retention of the Schedule to the Act of 1882, about which so much
has already been said, there could be no
question that the Giant's Causeway was not
Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Report Stage.
[Mr. Leslie]
included. Therefore, for those reasons, even
had the Act of 1882 applied to Ireland, the
Giant's Causeway would not have been included. The question is whether our insertion of the word "site" has not brought in
the Giant's Causeway, and if so, whether
that is the desire of the Government. It
occurred to me that we might get over the
difficulty by inserting such words as I have
suggested.
I am not sure that I ought not to have
said "artificial construction or arrangement"
for fear one might interfere with some of
the Dolmens or standing stones, and also I
thought we must put in "artificial embellishment" in order to catch stones which have
I
been dealt with in some form or other
hope I have dealt sufficiently with the artificial question, but it may be that the noble
Viscount can assure us that the question of
the Giant's Causeway does not arise or that
it can be protected in some other way, in
which case there will be no further point
in considering the question of artificiality.
The second point as regards date arises
from the fact that we have no definition of
the word "ancient." Again and again it has
been pointed out that we have nothing to
guide us as to the word "ancient." On the
Committee Stage the noble Viscount told us
that it was the practice in England to date
anything ancient from the year 1714, which
was the year of the accession of George I.
It so chances that within the last few days
there has been a debate in the House of Lords
on some matter connected with ancient
monuments. I think some noble Lord moved
for Papers, and during that debate occasion
was taken to refer to the approaching loss of
a very valuable monument, Waterloo Bridge.
Waterloo Bridge, as will be remembered,
was only opened for traffic in, I think, the
year 1817, or two or three years after the
Battle of Waterloo, and during the debate
it was definitely stated on behalf of the Commissioners of Works that in dealing with
ancient monuments the noble Lord who
raised the question was just as much entitled to refer to Waterloo Bridge as
ancient monument as he was to Stonehenge.
an
Therefore, we now have it, so to speak,
officially stated in the House of Lords in
England that for the purposes of these Acts
the term "ancient" has gone entirelv, and
no question of age now arises. I think the
question we have now to consider is whether
we ought now to give any definition as regards date in our Bill, or whether we ought
to leave it to somebody-I am afraid I do
not know who, because the only authority
I can find in the Bill is the Ministry of
Finance, who will, perhaps, have something
to say. The Advisory Committee will report
ancient monuments, the Ministry may list
other ancient monuments, and the Ministry,
on receiving reports, may, if it appears that
the monument is an ancient monument, do
so and so.
There is nothing provided anywhere for
any outside authority to come in and say
whether a monument is ancient or not. I
do not know that, apart from the duties
of the auditor in connection with some item
of expenditure, the point is of any great
importance. However, I thought it was
point we ought to discuss, and I beg to move
the insertion of the words standing on the
Paper.
a
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: What the hon. Senator proposes to
do by this Amendment is to re-define what
is an ancient monument. I admit that the
definition of an ancient monument is a
matter of very considerable difficulty, but
we consider that the definition in the Bill
is the best we can do, and it is the result
of very careful consideration based on expert
advice. The hon. Member referred to the
word "site" in the course of his remarks,
and he seemed to have a dread that under
the term site"
might be included the
Giant's Causeway. I think the point is
this. A monument which is an ancient
monument has to be of historic, architectural, traditional, artistic, or archæological
interest, and the Giant's Causeway, I think
we may all safely say, is of scientific interest, and, therefore, would not come under
the definition of ancient monument, and it
is not considered so to do. I can tell the
hon. Member that definitely.
"
As regards the second part of the hon.
Member's Amendment, the insertion of any
date such as the year of the accession of
King George I, namely, 1714, which I mentioned in a speech on the Committee Stage,
as the date which it was the custom in
England to more or less accept as the date
of an ancient monument, is not considered
desirable, and it has not, as the hon. Member
is probably aware, been embodied in any
Act in Great Britain. Although, generally
speaking, nothing of a later date than the
year 1714, or, indeed, probably a good deal
earlier than that, would be preserved as an
ancient monument, it is quite possible that
work of a later date might be considered
worthy of preservation, and we do not wish
in this Bill to fetter posterity by the insertion of a rigid date. For that reason I
hope the hon. Member will withdraw what
we call an unnecessarily restrictive Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: After the very interesting
statement on the subject of the Giant's
Causeway, I think I have attained the object
I had in putting down the Amendment, and
I now ask leave to withdraw it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Report Stage.
12 MAY 1926
NEW CLAUSE.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, after Clause
7, to insert as a new Clause:-
"8. Sections five, six and seven of this
Act shall not apply-
(a) to any structure which appears to
the Ministry of Finance to be occupied
as a dwelling house (otherwise than by a
person employed as the caretaker thereof
or his family); or
(b) to any church, ecclesiastical building or structure which has been disposed
of under the provisions of sub-section four
of section twenty-five of the Irish Church
Aet, 1869; or
(c) to
any monument which after
the First of November, 1903, has been
before the passing of this Act or thereafter becomes vested in a purchaser under
the Land Purchase Acts or the Northern
Ireland Land Act, 1925."
This new Clause is the only Amendment I
have brought before the House this afternoon in which a question of principle is
really involved. I went into these matters
both on the Second Reading and in Committee, and the Clause I suggested in Committee was not perhaps altogether all that
might have been wished. I withdrew it because it contained a reference to the National
Trust which had become obsolete owing to
the course of the debate on another Section.
Sections 5, 6, and 7 of the Bill are what I
may describe as the bones of the law which
it is sought to bring into force in Northern
Ireland. They are the Sections relating to
Listing and Preservation Orders.
I want to ensure that, following the
English course, those Sections shall not
apply to any structure which is bona-fide
occupied as a dwelling-house by somebody
other than a caretaker who may have been
put in for the sake of making it a dwellinghouse. In England the whole of one part
of the Act is made not to apply, and as regards the Listing Clause, which is in a
separate part of the Act in England, there is
a special notice at the end that that Section
shall not apply. I think it is obvious that
a dwelling must be protected. It is possible
for there to be a dwelling-house in Northern
Ireland the preservation of which might be
a matter of public interest by reason of the
historic, architectural, traditional, artistic,
or archaeological interest attaching to it. I
really think there are such dwelling-houses
in Northern Ireland. There may not be so
many as in England, but there certainly are
houses that your Advisory Committee, a Committee which is not yet formed, and which is
not to be an elected body and for which
apparently nobody will be directly responsible to Parliament-most of them presumably antiquarians, might consider
should come within the definition, and they
might report to the Ministry that it was of
national importance and should be preserved,
and then the Minister must put it on his list.
He has no choice there.
If the Minister puts it on his list various
things happen that are described in Clause 5.
Then unfortunately the owner cannot be in
the ordinary way the owner of his own property and the property he inhabits. I do
not think it can be the intention of Parliament to impose that extraordinary restriction on a man as regards his own house
merely because certain people in Northern
Ireland think it ought to be managed by
them and not by him. Unless we put this
protection in the Bill the owner of a house
does stand to be subjected to unpleasant experiences.
paragraphs
to
The other
of the Clause
deal with the question of monuments
which, as I said before, have
offered
already been
a public body.
Under the Irish Church Act nothing
that was worth preserving could be disposed of until the Church Commissioners had
an opportunity of accepting it, and under the
Land Purchase Act nothing could be transferred to a tenant purchaser unless both the
Ministry of Finance and the County Council
had had an opportunity of acquiring it.
Those things have passed now.
As regards the operation of future Land
Purchase Acts the Ministry of Finance and
the County Councils will have an opportunity
of acquiring these ancient monuments, and if
they do not acquire them the purchaser must,
as I think, be left undisturbed. In none of
these cases has Parliament the moral right
to interfere unless it can do so by agreement.
I beg to move the new Clause in my name.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Member laid great stress
on the exclusion of a dwelling-house from
the operations of the Bill. At the present
moment the Government do not know of any
house in Northern Ireland which would come
within the definition of an ancient monument. If there was any such in Northern
Ireland it would be such a rarety in my
opinion that it would be worth preserving,
and possibly the best preservation for an
ancient dwelling is for somebody to live in it.
I do not think the hon. Member need fear
that the Government would place unnecessary
restrictions on the owner of such a dwelling
to the extent that he would not in effect be
the owner of his property. I think the hon.
Member may take it that if there was such a
dwelling-house in Northern Ireland and the
owner took reasonable care of it the Government would not be likely to put restrictions
on him, and tell him he had got to do this
and that, and make his life a burden to him.
Economy Bill- .

Ancient Monuments Bill-
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
As regards the other two Sub-sections of
this Clause, the Government does not
acquiesce in the view that because the State
or a local authority had power to accept a
monument under the series of Acts referred
to, and at some time did not elect to do
so, therefore, it is stopped for all time
from stepping in and preserving such a
monument, which is the effect the hon.
Member's Amendment would have. We all
know that scientific and historical knowledge has grown greatly in the last fifty
years, and what may at one time have
seemed an unsightly ruin may now be regarded as historically and scientifically of
considerable importance. Naturally we will
endeavour, whenever possible, to obtain
these things by agreement. For these
reasons I regret to say that the Government
cannot see their way to accept the Amendment.
Mr. GLENDINNING: With regard to
paragraph (a) of this Amendment, either
there are dwelling-houses which might be
considered by the Advisory Committee to
be worthy of preservation or there are not.
If there are it would be the duty of the
Government to have them preserved, and if
the owner was living in such a house he
would have to submit to a Preservation
Order by whatever means the Government
thought fit to impose. Therefore, there
would be Government interference with the
owner of this house. If he was not able
to spend whatever sum was necessary to
preserve the house in accordance with the
ideas of the Advisory Committee or the
Ministry of Finance, what would happen
then?
In a
case like that I think the
Government should buy the house. I would
be prepared to support this Amendment in
order to prevent Government interference
with a person's private property.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think there is much
left for me to say. I do not quite understand what the noble Viscount means by his
reference to these dwelling-houses.
He says
the Government understands there are no
dwelling-houses in Northern Ireland to which
this Bill could apply.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: So far as we know at present.
Mr. LESLIE: Are we to understand that
the Government comes before Parliament,
and asks for power to deal with they do not
know what? Again and again during the
discussion of this Bill have I asked the
Government to say what they were going
to preserve, and we have had no information.
I must stand by the old constitutional
maxim that a man's house is his castle, and,
as regards the other matters, I have a distinct preference for the sanctity of contracts.
If the Government have had opportunities
of acquiring these monuments in the past,
and have not done so, and if they desire
to take them over in the future it must be
by agreement. I have brought forward a
good many Amendments to this Measure,
and I do not think I have pressed my own
views on the House unduly, but it is impossible for me to withdraw this Amendment.
Question put: That the Clause be added
to the Bill.
The House divided: Contents, 5; Not
Contents, 11.

of the Ancient Monuments Fund set up under

The Ministry of Finance received its apportioned share of that fund, and that share

annum.

Under the general arrangements which have

much delayed repairs on the monuments in

the custody of the Ministry. Had the old

Economy Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 13th May, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM
(Deputy Speaker) in the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.

Second Annual Report of the Government
Inspectors of Explosives for Northern Ireland, being their Annual Report for the year
1925.

Presented (pursuant to Act) and ordered
