277 Teachers' Superannuation Bill.

20 MAY 1926
Firearms Bill.

THE SENATE.

Thursday, 20th May, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Teachers' Superannuation Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of the
House): I beg to move the Second Reading
of this Bill, which, as the title shows, is
concerned with the amalgamation into one
fund of certain pension funds for teachers,
and with provision for payments to be made
into and from that fund. It is purely
administrative in character, and does not
make any change in the rates of pension
payable to teachers. As hon. Senators are
aware, this House recently passed a Measure
dealing solely with that subject.
The Teachers' Pension Fund dates from
1879, in which year an Act was passed which
provided that the Commissioners of Church
Temporalities in Ireland should, for the
purpose of establishing a pension fund,
provide either a sum not exceeding £1,300,000
or annual interest on that sum at the rate
of 3 per cent. The Act also authorised the
Commissioners of Education to make deductions from teachers' salaries, such
deductions to be paid into the fund, and
provided that the Commissioners of the
National Debt might make advances to the
fund. As a matter of fact, the Treasury
was obliged more than once to come to the
aid of the fund in order to prevent it
becoming bankrupt. But as up to the war
teachers' salaries were not very great, the
were not
demands made upon the fund
excessive, and there was a possibility that
in time it might become solvent.
In 1920, however, as hon. Senators are
probably aware, the salaries of teachers in
Ireland were greatly increased, and though
the fund naturally profited from the corresponding increased contributions from serving
teachers, this addition was greatly outbalanced by the enhanced rates of pension
which became payable. In the year 1922 the
accrued income and capital of the fund was
apportioned between Northern Ireland and
the Irish Free State, our share amounting
to something over £500,000. We have continued to finance the fund, as was done by
the Imperial Treasury in the past, that is
to say, by annual grants from State
funds. The fund is still not solvent, and it
would require the interest from a capital
sum of £1,500,000, to enable liabilities to be
met in future.
As, however, the National Teachers' Pension Fund of Great Britain is designed to
meet circumstances similar to our own, and
is being supported by annual grants which
will probably vary proportionately with our
requirements, the Government of Northern
Ireland anticipate that under the Colwyn
award we shall receive such an amount as will
enable us for some time to meet the demands
upon our own fund. Hence it follows that we
should merely be locking up money unnecessarily if we were to set aside the sum of
a million and a half, which I have mentioned,
for the purpose of providing the necessary
money to meet the needs of the teachers'
pensions. I should like to emphasise the
a State
fact that the present Bill is
guarantee to the Teachers' Pensions, and
when this Measure is passed, teachers will
in future have the statutory security of the
Government for the continual payment of
their pensions.
Turning to the Bill itself it will be seen
that it authorises the amalgamation into
one fund of the various pension funds of
teachers of different classes of education,
that is, primary, secondary, technical, and
agricultural. Provision is made for investment, and for payments to be made into and
out of the fund. Clause 5 deals with the
payment of the contributions made by local
authorities, or the Ministry of Education
and the Ministry of Agriculture in respect
of the teachers employed by them, and also
provides that teachers may retire voluntarily
at the age of 60, but in any case must retire
when they reach 65. This is merely applying
one of the rules of the Civil Service.
Bill as a whole is clear, and hon. Senators
will not wish me to detain them with a
lengthy and detailed explanation. I beg
to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Firearms (Amendment) Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In rising to move the Second reading
of the Firearms (Amendment) Bill, I should
like to remind the House-although I am
sure the House will not need to be reminded
-that under the existing law, the Firearms
Act of 1920, all persons who have arms or
ammunition must obtain what is know as a
Firearm Certificate, and this certificate, as
Unemployment Insurance Bill. 280
Firearms Bill.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
the House knows, entitles the holder to have
in his possession, use, and carry a firearm.
These certificates can only be granted under
strict limitations, and the applicant must be
able to show that he has a good reason for requiring such certificate. Provision is made
for one class of certificate only, which not
only entitles the owner to have arms in his
possession, but also entitles him to carry
firearms.
I am sure the House will agree with me
when I say it is obviously desirable that the
carrying of arms should be restricted as far
as possible, and it is not proposed that the
present restrictions upon the grant of Firearm Certificates which, as I have said, entitles persons to carry arms, should be relaxed in any way, but it is considered that
there should be some means of enabling a
qualified permit to be given to allow persons
to have arms in their houses without
giving them the extended authority to carry
arms. There may be persons who for sentimental reasons desire to keep arms in their
houses, but have no desire to carry them.
to
I think the House will also agree with me
that the objection to the grant of a permit
to carry arms might not extend to
the grant of a permit merely
have arms in possession, and there is
no reason why law-abiding citizens should
not be allowed to retain their, own arms,
while not being permitted to carry them.
The main object, therefore, of this Bill is to
enable a permit to be granted to allow the
owner to keep arms on his premises if, in
the opinion of the authorities, such permit
can be granted without any danger to the
public. The fee charged for a Firearm
Certificate under the 1920 Act is 5s., in the
first instance, and 2s. 6d. for the annual renewal. For the limited permit under this
Bill the charge will be a nominal fee of
2s. 6d. for the first issue, and 1s. on each
annual renewal.
If hon. Members will look at Sub-secion 5
of Section 1 of the Bill they will see that
there is at the moment no specific power to
dispose of surrendered arms in cases where
the owner cannot be given a Firearm Certificate, and will not make arrangements for
the disposal of the arms, or in cases where
arms are found and the owner is unknown.
Moreover, in cases where arms are seized as
the result of some technical breach of the
law, and where, in the view of the Attorney-
General, the case is not of sufficient importance to justify a prosecution, the matter
can be really dealt with under this Section
by an order for the destruction or disposal
of the weapon in some other manner. The
existing powers of a court in respect to
orders for the disposal of arms are not, however, affected in any way.
At the present time there is no right of
appeal from the decision of the authority
granting the certificate, and we propose that
such a right should be given to the Minister
of Home Affairs where an applicant is dissatisfied with the decision which may have
been given by the authority by whom certificates are issued. I beg to move.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I would
like to say how pleased I am that the Government has introduced a Bill such as this. I have
during the past few months heard lamentations from men who have had firearms in
their possession for a long period, more because they were in the nature of memorials
or that they belonged to their grandfather or
some other predecessor, than for anything
else. These people were complaining very
bitterly that the authorities were removing
these ancient weapons from their homes.
This Bill will give those people an opportunity of applying for a permit which will
enable them to keep such weapons, which
are not dangerous, in their possession.
I am satisfied there are also cases where
a householder may require to keep firearms
in his house for defensive purposes, and in
the case of persons whose conduct has been
such as to justify the grant of a permit I
think they should be enabled to retain their
firearms.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I am in
agreement with the Bill, but there is one
question I would like to ask. In the old
days you had also got to have a permit to
have ammunition. Many people have shot
guns, and I have applied for permission to
obtain 200 cartridges, and when they are exhausted I want to know what steps I shall
have to take to get a new supply. Shall I
have to obtain a permit to go to a gun shop
to buy them; otherwise they may refuse to
supply me with any more.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I understand the hon. Senator wants
to know what happens in the case of a person
who has got a Firearms Certificate when his
ammunition runs out. That happened to me
this morning. I went to a certain gun-maker
in Belfast, and desired to buy certain cartridges for the purpose of shooting rooks.
There is on the Certificate a form to fill up,
and if the hon. Member signs that he can
get his ammunition without any difficulty.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I rise to move the Second Reading
Ancient Monuments Bill.
Adjournment.
20 MAY 1926
It is
of this Bill, and I do not think it is necessary for me to say anything on it.
a perfectly straightforward Bill, the object
of which is to extend the operation of the
Unemployment Insurance Act for a further
period that is, to the end of this year, or
such other time as Parliament may direct.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Ancient Monuments Bill [S].
for consideration of Commons
Amendment read.
Order
Commons Amendment:-
After Clause 13, insert the following new
Clause:-
14. (Expenses of Ministry of Finance and
Advisory Committee).
There shall be paid out of moneys provided by Parliament such expenses as may
be incurred by the Ministry of Finance in
the exercise of its powers in connection
with-
(a) the inspection and maintenance of
ancient monuments, whether vested in
the Ministry of Finance or of which that
Ministry is guardian;
:
(b) the purchase by agreement of any
ancient monument; and
(c) the payment of compensation for
acts done under this Act, involving interference with private rights of property;
and
(d) the purchase or assistance towards
the purchase of archæological objects of
which the finding has been reported to
the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee;
and also such expenses of the Ancient
Monuments Advisory Committee as may be
sanctioned by the Ministry of Finance.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The Commons
Amendment referred to in the Orders of the
Day is merely the re-insertion of Clause 14,
which deals with the expenses of the Ministry
of Finance and the Advisory Committee. On
a question of privilege, discussion on this
Clause was ruled out when this House passed
the Bill, and the Amendment only means
that it has been re-inserted in another place.
I beg to move that this Clause be agreed to.
Amendment agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them therewith.
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn
until Tuesday next.-(Viscount Charlemont.)
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: It would
be a great convenience to some of us who
may be going away to know what the Sittings
of the House are likely to be after Tuesday.
When will the House finish its business?
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I should say
probably on Thursday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twentyfive minutes before Four o'clock
until Tuesday next.
Jury Laws Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 25th May, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Message from the Commons.
That they have passed the Appropriation
Bill, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House.
Appropriation Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill ordered to be read a Second time tomorrow, and to be printed.
Jury Laws Amendment Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now' resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-
(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to
In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair].
CLAUSES 1 (Qualification of jurors), 2
(Preparation of lists of special jurors), 3
(Exemption from serving on juries), and
4 (Selection of grand jurors for Belfast City
Commission), ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Grand Juries at Courts of
Quarter Sessions).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move on page 5,
to leave out Clause 5. I must crave the
indulgence of the Committee this afternoon
while I endeavour to deal with a point of
some little difficulty, and a point of great
importance. What I am seeking to do, and
what I am asking the Committee to allow
me to do, is, as will be seen from the Paper,
to excise entirely from the Bill before us
the whole of Clause 5.
Clause 5, as we know, deals with the
abolition of Grand Juries at Courts of
Quarter Sessions. There was, as the Committee will remember, no Clause directed
to this end in the Bill originally brought in
in another place. Suggestions to that end
were made by private Members, and opposed,
I may say, with some vigour by representatives of the Government. Finally, however,
it was decided that the question should be
left to what I understand is termed a free
vote in another place, and on a free vote,
by 19 votes to 14, the Clause which is before
us, or, at least, the important part of the
Clause, was carried, and, accordingly, it
goes into the Bill.
The Clause itself purports to be based on
one of the recommendations in the report
of Lord Justice Andrews' Committee. I do
not think, if I may venture to say so, that
the question of the abolition of Grand Juries
at Courts of Quarter Sessions was strictly
within the terms of reference of the Commission. The exact terms of reference are
printed in the report, and I need not take
up time by reading them. The Clause was
based also, so far as I can see, on a Clause
which was inserted in a Bill brought before
the Imperial Parliament. The Imperial
Parliament has recently been dealing with
miscellaneous administrative provisions, and
a Clause was inserted in the Bill as introduced in the House of Commons at Westminster, directed towards the abolition of
Grand Juries at Quarter Sessions, and
that is the Clause, to some extent-for the
Clause before us does not follow it exactly-
I think the difference will be found to be of
great importance-but a Clause was inserted
in the Bill, and was discussed at some length
at Westminster. The same course was taken
there that was taken here. The matter was
left to a free vote of the House. The House
of Commons, which dealt with the matterit was on the Report Stage-consisted of 333
Members, and in that House of 333 the proposed Clause was cut out. Consequently
the abolition of Grand Juries at Quarter
Sessions has not yet been carried in the
English Parliament.
The Committee will remember that there
was, during the war, a short temporary
Measure passed in 1917, to suspend Grand
Juries, both at Assizes and Quarter Sessions.
That came to an end at the termination of
the War. I have suggested that the Clause
does not, to my mind, follow the English
abortive Clause. I think that we can only
examine the Clause as it has come before
us, on the assumption that it means exactly
what it says. I have some doubt on the
subject myself, but for the purpose of discussion I think we must assume that the
intention of the Clause is to bring about the
results which would follow from the adoption
of the Clause,
The Government, if I may venture to say
so, is bound to bring this Clause before us
this afternoon, inasmuch as the matter was
left to a free Vote in another place.
The
Jury Laws Bill-
25 MAY 1926
Committee.
Government is bound to explain the Clause
to us, and perhaps to put up such arguments
for it as may have been brought to their
notice. But I do not think that the Government is under any necessity to defend here
the Clause to which they so strenuously
objected elsewhere. Nor do I think that the
Government should endeavour to influence
the votes of this House, but I think that
the matter should be left here, as it was in
another place, to what they would call a
free vote, although that is an expression
which we do not use here. Now if the
majority in favour of this Clause had been
a large or a sizeable one, and if the matter
had been debated in detail elsewhere, and
if it could have been shown that there was
any popular demand for this serious constitutional change. I should not, myself, suggest that it would be for the Second Chamber
to interfere. I should rather go on to suggest one or two minor Amendments in the
Clause itself, of which I think it stands in
need. But I do not feel that I ought to
endeavour to take that course this afternoon.
I presume the noble Viscount will say that,
as the Government left the matter to a free
vote elsewhere, the Government could not
here accept Amendments of the details of
the Clause. The Committee's power in this
case, to put it bluntly, is to take it or leave
it.
My personal view is entirely against the
abolition of Grand Juries at Quarter Sessions, as well, of course, as at Assizes, were
that in question; and as distinguished
lawyers of such widely divergent political
views as the late Mr. Rawlinson, Chairman
of Cambridge Quarter Sessions; Sir Henry
Slessor, in the House of Commons, and when
the matter was referred to in the House of
Lords, Lords Parmoor and Phillimore, took
the same view that Grand Juries ought to
be retained at Quarter Sessions, I do not
think that I ought to be under the necessity
of apologising for my opinion. A system that
has been in existence since the time of Henry
V ought not to be interfered with, except in
deference to a distinct popular demand. I
say that the change it is desired to produce
cannot be worked alone. It belongs to the
whole system of Quarter Sessions.
I may, perhaps, remind the Committee that
for practical purposes there are two kinds
of Quarter Sessions, although they have, of
course, perfectly equal jurisdiction and perfectly equal powers of trial and punishment.
The two kinds of Quarter Sessions I am referring to are the ordinary Court of Quarter
Sessions held in the country, presided over
by the county court judge, and which is
composed of the county court judge and such
magistrates of the county as may choose to
attend. The other Quarter Sessions is the
Court presided over by the Recorder, sitting
as sole judge. The court that occurs to all
of us in the Recorder's Court in Belfast.
Both these courts, whether presided over by
a trained lawyer sitting as sole judge, or by
the county court judge, also a trained
lawyer, with the assistance of the ordinary
magistrates, have exactly the same powers
of trial and punishment.
It will be remembered that the powers of
Courts of Quarter Sessions in Northern
Ireland are in excess of the powers enjoyed
by Courts of Quarter Sessions in England.
In England, Courts of Quarter Sessions are
subject to a Regulating Act, which does not
operate in Northern Ireland. A Court of
Quarter Sessions in Northern Ireland can
in practice try almost any case. It is the
practice at a country Quarter Sessions, if a
serious or difficult charge has been returned
for trial there, for the Attorney-General
to move that the Bill be sent to the Assizes,
and that course is usually, though not
necessarily, followed. The same course is
not taken quite so often as regards the
Recorder's Court. I would sooner abolish
Quarter Sessions in country districts altogether than abolish Grand Juries at those
Quarter Sessions.
Now, what is the reason for this suggested
abolition? I say it is to relieve Grand
Juries who would be summoned to attend
the Belfast Recorder's Court. The Belfast
Recorder's Court sits four times a year, and
each Court requires 23 Grand Jurors. Therefore, the number of people concerned during
the year would only be 92. If it is necessary
to relieve Grand Jurors in that way we
shall find, if we return to the Report of
Lord Justice Andrews' Committee, that there
is a very important minute of reservation
which is signed by a member of the Committee, one of the most eminent, perhaps
the most eminent of our Civil Servants,
Mr. Watt. Mr. Watt points out that in
order to relieve the burden on Grand Jurors
it would be possible to apply to Northern
Ireland a section of the English Administration of Justice Act, which he quotes, and
which I need not re-quote. The effect is that
the Grand Jury shall be relieved of hearing
cases if it is certified to them that the man
concerned has already pleaded guilty. There
are also, as the Committee knows, relief Acts
already on our Statute Book which prevent
the summoning of Grand Juries if there is
no business for them to perform. Those
Acts are in frequent operation in the country
districts, but I cannot recollect that they
are ever applied in Belfast.
The origin of Grand Juries, whether et
Assizes or Quarter Sessions, is plainly indicated in the Report of the Committee to
287:

Jury Laws Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
which I have referred. It is traced back
to at least 1166, although I do not suggest
there were Quarter Sessions in operation
either in England or in Ireland during the
reign of Henry II. Obviously they came
into force later, but Grand Juries are distinctly referred to in Magna Charta. I
know there seems to be a popular impression
nowadays that if a man ventures to refer
to Magna Charta he might as well refer to
Grimm's Fairy Tales. But the fact remains
that Magna Charta still exists, and still has
considerable influence on the course of our
Jurisprudence. The expression used in
Magna Charta, in the well-known Clause
generally numbered 29-the Clause about the
free man being deprived of his liberty-is,
"Nec supra eum ibimus we will not
go upon him," and so forth and so on,
"except by the lawful judgment of his
Peers." Those words are a distinct recognition of the existence of the Grand Jury,
even in those days.
39_66
There is, fortunately I think, preserved
for us a charge delivered to the Grand Jury
at Westminster Sessions, in 1749, by Henry
Fielding, the author of "Tom Jones." I
venture to commend that charge to any
person who may be summoned on a Grand
Jury if he desires to know something of Mr.
Fielding's views on the duties of a Grand
Juror and the importance of the office to
which he is called. Amongst the things
which Mr. Fielding said is a eulogy of the
English system under which an Englishman,
so far from being convicted, cannot even
be put on his trial until twelve men at least
have said on their oath that there is probable
cause for his accusation. That is Mr. Fielding, who also quoted Magna Charta and gave
Coke's views upon it. Mr. Fielding supports the right of a man who has the
misfortune to be charged with an offence
to the right of the voice of at least twelve
men before he can be put on his trial, and
it does not matter whether the trial is at
the Assizes or at the Quarter Sessions. A
man can get many years penal servitude at
Quarter Sessions just as easily and just as
deservedly as he can at the Assizes. There
is, therefore, that right to the voice of
twelve men.
We have now to consider whether that
right is of any value. Perhaps we might
say we ought to consider whether the liberty
of one subject is to outweigh, shall we say,
the restriction on the liberty of twentythree subjects who are required to attend
as Grand Jurors. I suggest that that right
is of very great value. I reminded the
Committee just now that the powers of
Quarter Sessions in England are more restricted than in Northern Ireland. I was
thinking more particularly of the Act of
1885, under which certain offences against
women and girls are punishable. None of
those offences can in England be tried at
a Court of Quarter Sessions but in Northern
Ireland they can be so tried.
I did suggest, some two or three years ago,
that we should make a change in this
respect, but I did not press the proposal, as
I thought the occasion was not opportune.
The fact remains that if we accept this
Clause it will be possible for that class of
case, in which care is specially necessary,
to be tried at Quarter Sessions without
a Grand Jury. The injustice in certain
cases it may be a doctor, or it may be a
clergyman-may be very serious, and there
is always a chance of a person being undefended, or too poor to obtain legal assistance. To such a person a Grand Jury is a
great protection.
We pass from that to the results which
will follow if we adopt the Clause before
us. First of all, although it is a minor
matter, I have some doubt as to how much
of the preliminary work of preparing the
lists and issuing summonses would be
abolished. The suggestion is to abolish the
Grand Jury, but the Bill does not follow
the English precedents of 1917-the temporary Act-or the English Bill of the other
day, by definitely providing that no precept shall issue and so on. It may be that
there will be no difficulty in that matter,
but I thought it well to point it out. Then
we should lose the opportunity given to the
Recorder, or the Judge, to deliver his charge
to the Grand Jury. He could not charge a
Grand Jury if they were not there to be
charged. Those charges, delivered in
country districts by judges, are frequently of
some value, and the observations of the
Recorder of Belfast have also, from time to
time, obtained a considerable amount of
notice. I think, myself, that it would be a
distinct loss if those charges were not
delivered, but I do not say that that is of
first-class importance.
Then we come to the loss of right which a
Quarter Sessions Grand Jury possess the
right of presentment. I do not mean, of
course, the presentment of the old Grand
Jury expression, which rather implied the
power to make a rate, but I mean the presentment of nuisances and crimes within
their knowledge, and of encroachment on
Commons placed on them by Statute, and
although I do not remember a case of that
right being exercised in Ireland, it was
recognised in a case in England in 1910, and
it is a right it is not wise to take away.
Passing on, we come to what is called the
right of an ordinary person to present a Bill
of Indictment to a Grand Jury. That right
289 Jury Laws Bill-

25 MAY 1926
Committee.
is a common law right possessed by every
subject. It is almost his one chance of
appealing against a decision of a Court of
Magistrates which he thinks is adverse to
his interests. In the English Bill, and in
the English Statute the right of presentation of this private Bill was carefully preserved, and arrangements were made for
its presentation at Assizes. The Bill before
us makes no provision for this right whatever. It proposes to take away a common
law right without substituting anything at
all. Once again let me refer to Magna
Charta. If you deny a man the right to
present his case to a Court of Quarter Sessions you
are again infringing the old
principle.
Then we come to the expression "committed for trial," which will be found in
line 29 of the Clause. I want to pass that
over for a minute, because if I am right
in the contention that I am about to
submit to the Committee, it probably affords
the most glaring instance of imperfection in
the Clause before us. I propose to leave
that for a few minutes, while I go into other
items in the Clause. We come to the proviso
attempting to put on the presiding judge the
duty formerly performed by the Grand Jury.
We are not told what the judge is to do,
we are not told where he is to do it, whether
he is to do it in court or before he comes
into court. It will be obvious that if the
judge does the work in court, the saving of
time will not be great, and if he does not do
it in court, there will be those who will
impugn the manner in which the work is
done.
Mr. Watt pointed out that in England in
1917-and he might have added had he then
known, that neither in the Bill of 1925, was
it proposed to cast this duty on the judge.
Judges, as we know, already necessarily see
the depositions in order that they may charge
Grand Juries, but if a judge has to examine
the depositions with greater care to see
whether there is a true bill or not it is
obvious that he must take a great deal of
time over the business, and if a judge's
decisons should depend on the depositions,
he is losing the great help which is afforded
by actually seeing and hearing the witnesses
themselves. I can never agree that the
mere reading of the depositions is the same
thing as actually hearing the story from the
witnesses.
It is really a little doubtful as to what will
happen if the judge throws out the Bill, or
the jury disagree. I confess that I would
almost cut this proposal out altogether. I
think it is useless, and will impose an undue
amount of extra work on the Recorder of
Belfast, who is already supposed to be over-
K
worked, because in the Bill we make special
provision to enable him to get assistance.
Sub-clauses 2 and 3 of the Clause are ancillary
questions that need not, I think, be inserted
in the Bill, were it not on account of the
alteration made earlier. The third subclause will be found in the English Act of
1925. It is required there because of what
I may call the automatic true bill to be
returned in England in cases where a man
has been found guilty.
Now I return to the question of the committal for trial. The words of sub-clause
1 are:
As from the appointed day on which
this section comes into force Grand Juries
at Courts of Quarter Sessions shall be
abolished, and thereafter an indictment
may be presented to a Court of Quarter
Sessions without having been found by a
Grand Jury, if it is an indictment against
a person committed for trial at that court.


Now, it is not for me to make a dogmatic
assertion on a point of law, but I venture
to say that it is for those who bring this
Clause forward to defend every word in it.
The expression, 'committed for trial,"
seems to me to exclude entirely some 75 per
cent. of the cases which come to Quarter
Sessions. That is the case where the prisoner
or defendant is out on bail. I think that
75 per cent. of the defendants at Quarter
Sessions must be on bail. I cannot find
that the expression "committed for trial "
in Northern Ireland includes the cases of
people on bail. In England it is not so.
In
England, by virtue of a special section, to
which, I have no doubt, those responsible
for this Clause have already referred, it
is provided that in England and Wales the
expression, committed for trial," shall include persons on bail. I cannot, as I have
said before, trace that a similar arrangement is in operation in Northern Ireland,
and, having regard to the wording of our
Petty Sessions Act, in which the two expressions, "committed for trial," and
"admitted to bail," are juxtaposed, I am
afraid that the Clause would require amendment in that respect. It may be that there
is some answer, which I have been unable
to find my resources are limited-and, if so,
I shall be glad to hear it. If, however, I
am right it will become apparent that the
Clause, even if we could accept it, could not
come into operation until we have resorted
to some, perhaps, not too creditable makeshifts, such as putting off the appointed day
until we had time to alter our law. But I
think the better course is to reject the Clause
altogether, and leave it to come before us
again, if it is desired that it should do so, as
part of a considered scheme dealing with
Jury Laws Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
Quarter Sessions practice as a whole. If that
were done we could have a Bill dealing with
Quarter Sessions and the powers of those
Sessions, and dealing also with those minor
matters to which I have referred. As the
matter stands at present, I cannot see that
any case has been made out for changing the
ancient practice of the English Constitution,
on account of the free vote of a majority
of five in our House of Commons. I beg to
move.
cannot
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I
hope, in my reply on behalf of the Government, to give nearly such an interesting
exposition of Magna Charta and the works
of Henry Fielding as the hon. Senator (Mr.
Leslie) has done. I am afraid my speech
will lose a great deal by comparison with
his. With regard to the attitude of the
Government as to possible Amendments in
this Clause, I may say that the Clause must
now be counted as the Government's own
Clause, as it was left to a free vote
in another place. I think the Government
logically committed themselves to being
bound by the result of that vote, irrespective
of the opinion of individuals who voted
against it. I am afraid, therefore, that as the
hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie) said, it is a question of either taking it or leaving it.
In regard to the various points raised,
the report of the Andrews Committee
contained very wide terms, and the Clause
in question is well within those. As this
Bill is really based on that report the
Government are bound to adhere to it as
much as possible, and this they have done.
If you will read the report you will find,
I think, that the Andrews Committee, as a
whole, were very much in favour of the
abolition of Grand Juries at Quarter
Sessions. The hon. Senator, in his speech,
made reference to Magna Charta and other
things I should not like to say that he did
so to confuse the issue. I am perfectly
certain no confusion could possibly exist, even
where so many things were said within a
comparatively short time, but, at the
same time, other hon. Senators might come
to the conclusion, from the speech of the
hon. Senator, that the Andrews Committee recommended the abolition of Grand
Juries at Assizes as well. It was very much
the reverse of that. One point mentioned
was the importance to the public of the
charge by the judge to Grand Juries. Nobody
under-estimates the advantage and importance of that, and the opportunity will still
exist at Assizes.
I would like to take the points one by
one, as far as I can. As to the one raised
about offences against women and girls under
the Criminal Law Amendment Act in England, I would point out that the magistrate
who sends up the case for trial to Quarter
Sessions is as likely to judge of the rights
and wrongs of the case as any Grand Jury.
He is learned in the law, and is more likely,
or,
at least, as likely, to be able to judge
of such matters as the Grand Jury is. I do
not think, therefore that any infringement
of the rights of the subject would occur
through the abolition of Grand Juries. The
real reason for their elimination, as far as the
report goes, is that they have become more
or less of an anachronism. Their sphere of
usefulness, although they date back to Magna
Charta, and possibly before that, has been
exhausted. In the old days the administration of the law was rather haphazard, and
possibly depended a good deal on the state of
temper of the examining magistrate. Now
that these possibilities have been eliminated
I do not think that the rights of the subject
will be infringed by the abolition of the
Grand Juries.
As to the bill of indictment, which the
hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie) refers to, this can
be preferred at Assizes as well as at Quarter
Sessions, so that his argument in that respect
falls to the ground.
I think I have covered, rather briefly,
most of the points mentioned. The
object of the Bill is for the relief of a
number of jurors. I think that the figures
quoted by the hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie) were
rather low. What is really the fact is that
the average attendance of jurors in the
counties is 300, and in Belfast 400. I think
the hon. Senator mentioned ninety-three.
Mr. LESLIE: Ninety-two. There are
twenty-three jurors required four times in
the year.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The object of
this Clause is the abolition of Grand Juries
at Quarter Sessions, no matter how much
they may be required at Assizes. I think
that is about all I can say on the matter.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: When
this Bill was first introduced in another
place, I was rather inclined to support the
hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie), but since the Bill
has been presented to us in its present form,
following the Andrews' report by giving
the judge the same powers as the Grand
Jury, I have altered my view. The Grand
Jury was formed in the old days in case an
innocent person would have the indignity
of being tried. Now power is being given to
the judge to say whether it is a case of true
bill or no bill. It seems to me that a judge
would be in a far better position to say
whether it should be no bill or true bill,
than the ordinary Grand Jury would be.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) The Grand
#
Jury Laws Bill--
Committee.
25 MAY 1926
Jury, after all, have no depositions before
them, but witnesses are produced before
them to give evidence. I have known witnesses brought before a Grand Jury to give
evidence in favour of a person's
innocence, yet when they have been
cross-examined by a Grand Juror they
have given evidence which showed
that the person should be convicted. I do
not know much about the Quarter Sessions
Grand Jury, but I have been for a long
time connected with Grand Juries at Assizes,
and I think there have been only two or
three cases in which a true bill has not been
found. I think there has been an outcry
for many years against the uselessness of
summoning members of the Grand Jury,
whose time has been wasted by simply going
into the box and finding true bills. That is
nearly always the case at Quarter Sessions.
The judge, being a legal man, is in a far
better position, after reading the depositions, to say whether there is a case to go
before the jury or not. I am sure the hon.
Senator on the other side (Mr. Leslie) would
have been quite satisfied, with his legal
knowledge, to say whether it is a true bill
to go before the jury or not. I think it is
worthy of the past, and as one who appreciates old things, I am sorry to see the
Grand Jury done away with. But in these
days of advance, I think it is an advance,
and I think that justice is even more likely
to be done.
Mr. LESLIE: May I have a reply to my
point as regards bail, whether the expression
in the Clause deals with that or not? I do
not know if I may also ask as regards something that fell from the right hon. and
gallant Gentleman. I rather understood
him to say that witnesses came before the
Grand Jury to establish a person's innocence.
Colonel CRAWFORD: They came for the
purpose of proving innocence, but by the
time they were done with they gave evidence
to show guilt.
Mr. LESLIE: Witnesses for the prosecution?
Colonel CRAWFORD: Yes.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I regret that
I forgot to answer the hon. Senator about
bail. I am informed that the expression
"committed for trial" in Northern Ireland
includes a person on bail.
Mr. LESLIE: I think it would be the
general desire that I should not press this
matter to a division. I, therefore, ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 6 (Selection of jurors to be returned for service); 7 (Revision of jurors'
lists); 8 (Temporary appointment of additional judge in connection with revision of
jurors' lists for Belfast and Antrim); 9
(Areas for which lists of jurors are to be
prepared); 10 (Form of precept to be issued
to local officers, &c); and 11 (Chargeability of
local officers in connection with jurors'
lists), ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 12 (Remuneration of existing
local officers in connection with jurors'
lists).
Mr. BARBOUR: I should like to ask if
the Government have any intention to alter
an anomaly that exists at present. I raise
this question under the heading-Remuneration of existing local officers. The system
of drawing up jurors' and voters' lists is a
very cumbrous one. The major portion of
the work is done by county secretaries. Then
the work is simply re-arranged and put in
another form by the Clerk of the Crown
and Peace. This same work is done in the
case of the City of Belfast, I believe, by one
officer. Now, I cannot see why it should not
be done by the secretary of the county
council. It would save time, save expense,
and it would save two people being responsible for the same work. I would like to
know if the Government have any intention
in the near future of remedying something
which I think is rather an anomaly.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I should
be obliged if the hon. Senator would bring
this matter up on the Third Reading.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 13 (Provisions as to release and
excusing of jurors), 14 (Imposition of fines
on defaulting jurors), 15 (Remuneration and
other expenses of jurors, etc., in civil trials),
16 (Detention of jurors in civil trials during
interval for refreshment), 17 (Duties of
jurors upon coroner's inquest), and 18 (Short
title, construction, bringing of Act into
operation, and repeal), ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
First, Second, Third, Fourth, Fifth and
Sixth Schedues agreed to.
Mr. BARBOUR: Might I ask-
The CHAIRMAN: I have put the Question.
It is too late to speak on the Schedules.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Mr. BARBOUR: I should like to know
if I will have an opportunity of raising
this question again before the Bill is finally
disposed of.

Adjournment.
CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move: That this House do now adjourn until
to-morrow, when Standing Orders will be
suspended for the consideration of the Appropriation Bill.
Mr. GLENDINNING: The Prime Minister stated with regard to legislation which it
is intended to introduce in another place in
the Autumn, that copies of the Bills would
be issued to hon. Members as and when
printed during the interim. I would like
to suggest that copies should also be sent to
Members of the Senate. Frequently Members of the Senate have not very much time
to make themselves acquainted with legislation coming along-(Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.and if we get these Bills at the same
time as Members of the other House we
would, at all events, have some idea of the
character of the Bills, and would be more
prepared when they do come before us to
debate them, and to understand them. I
suggest to the Government that copies be
also sent to Members of this House.
Mr. SPEAKER: Copies of the Bills will be
sent to Members of this House.
Colonel CRAWFORD: May I ask, as you
are moving that Standing Orders be suspended to-morrow, does that mean that the
Session will come to an end to-morrow?
Bill will be finished to-morrow evening?
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Yes. The
Adjournment will then take place until
Tuesday, at 12-15 o'clock, for the Royal
Assent.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
Minutes after Four o'clock.

Jury Laws Amendment Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): In rising to move the Third
Reading of the Jury Laws Amendment Bill
I would ask the permission of the House to
reply to a point raised yesterday, and also
to a question of which I have received
private notice. The first point was raised by
Senator Barbour, who, I regret, is absent
to-day. He asked whether the functions of
the Clerks of the Crown and Peace in the
preparation of the Jury Lists could not be
equally well performed by the County
Secretaries. I think his point is founded on
a misapprehension of the position. The list
is prepared for the whole area included in
the County, including the Urban Districts
and Unions where there is a Clerk who was
appointed before the year 1898. The lists are
prepared in the case of Urban Districts by
the Clerk of the Urban District, and in the
case of Unions where there is an existing
Clerk by the Clerk of the Union. The
County Secretary, therefore, has no jurisdiction over the whole area for which the list
is prepared. He is only in a position to
furnish the list so far as a portion of the
County is concerned.
The work of the Clerk of the Crown and
Peace consists in consolidating the lists
received from the various local officials, that
is, the County Secretaries, Clerks of Urban
Districts, and existing Clerks of Unions, and
preparing these for submission to the County
Court Judge, who is the revising authority.
This is work of a judicial character, and is
clearly appropriate to be done by an official
under the County Court Judge. County
Secretaries and other similar local officials
attend the revision in the character of witnesses to give assistance to the County Court
Judge in revising the lists, and clearly such
officials would not be in a position to act
in the character of Clerk of the Court.
There is a further point, that the salaries
of the Clerks of the Crown and Peace were
fixed expressly to include work in connec
tion with the revision of Jury Lists, and if
the work were put upon the County Secretaries they would require increased remuneration, and, at the same time, the Government would be bound to continue the
existing salaries of the Clerks of the Crown
and Peace.
With regard to the point raised by Senator
Hill Dickson, as to where the medical
certificates to be issued by doctors in respect
of patients who desire to be excused from
jury service may be obtained, I wish to say
that the form of medical certificate will be
in the same position as any other form prescribed by Act of Parliament. The medical
certificate need not be on the printed form
at all provided it follows the form prescribed
by the Act. The Government will, however,
take steps to provide such forms, and will
make arrangements for them to be made
available to the public at a nominal charge.
I beg to move.

Firearms (Amendment) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
FER-
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
RARD: I beg to move, That this Bill be
now read the Third time.
Mr. DORMAN: I did not intervene on the
Second Reading of this Bill, but I wish to
intervene at this stage, and to record my
opposition to the passing of this Measure.
I think it is a Bill that is entirely uncalled
for, and is a move in the wrong direction,
so far as the people of Northern Ireland, or,
for that matter, of any part of Ireland, are
concerned. I happen to have had to move
about in the various districts of this city a
few years ago when firearms were being
widely used by the populace, and on more
than one occasion, through asserting my
right to move through all parts of the city,
I was fired at by both sides. (Laughter.)
Hon. Senators may laugh, but to me it was
no laughing matter.
Mr. McDOWELL:
what you were.
They did not know
Mr. DORMAN: I was a peaceable citizen
of Ulster asserting my right to move about
on the surface of my native country, and I
may say for the information of hon.
Senators that day after day I deliberately
and with malice aforethought walked
through the different parts of the city to
assert my citizen rights in the midst of the
devilry that was going on.
Mr. HILL DICKSON:
shots.
They were bad
Mr. DORMAN: Fortunately they were.
They were not as good shots as I would have
been had I had a revolver in my hand. We
are in a peculiar position in Ireland, and
particularly in Northern Ireland. We have
people on both sides who are mainly stimulated by and act upon the traditions of their
forefathers. We have a state of affairs that
probably does not exist in any other city of
the same dimensions in any other part of the
world. Because of the traditions of our forefathers we actually refuse to reside in the
same geographical area of our native land,
and for the Government to make it easier
for any of the citizens to get possession of
and carry lethal instruments of any description is, in my opinion, fatal so far as the
well-being of the community is concerned.
Even our City Council has had to change
the surface of our thoroughfares to prevent
the populace utilising the surface of the
streets for the purpose of attacking and injuring one another. In face of that we have
a Government presenting a Bill of this description, reducing by 50 per cent. the charge
made for a permit. The hon. Senator
(Senator Leslie) shakes his head, but I
believe I am correct in saying that the present charge is 5s, and it is proposed to
reduce it to 2s 6d, and the charge for renewal
is less. I wish to take this opportunity of
recording my protest against a Measure of
this kind, which, in my estimation, is going
to make it easier for the lawless and
irresponsible class to become permanent possessors of firearms to the detriment of the
public peace, and which will endanger the
life of peaceful citizens in the future, as it
has undoubtedly done in the past. I beg to
offer my protest against the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I merely rise to explain
that the reason I endeavoured to indicate
negation to the remark made by the hon.
Senator, who has just sat down, was because
the charge to which he referred as a charge
that is about to be reduced is the charge for
a certificate to have and to carry firearms.
The Bill before us, as I think was clearly
stated on the Second Reading, is limited to
the issue of a permit-which is not the same
thing as a certificate under the 1920 Actenabling a man to have firearms in his
dwelling-house, but that is as far as this Bill
takes us. The firearm has to be kept in the
man's dwelling-house. The effect of the
possession of a certificate under the 1920 Act
is to enable a man not only to have firearms
in his dwelling-house but to carry firearms
outside. That is the reason why I expressed
dissent to the hon. Senator's remark.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: No one regrets, I am sure, more
than I do that the hon. Senator should have
been fired at in the troublous periods, and I
303 Unemployment Insurance Bill.

THE SENATE
Adjournment.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
rejoice to see that those who fired at him
were very bad shots. I cannot, however,
agree with the hon. Senator in his views as
to the effect of this Bill. He stated in the
course of his speech that this Bill simply
means that lawless and irresponsible persons
will be enabled to get firearms. It is exactly
the contrary. No lawless or irresponsible
person will be able under this Bill to obtain
a permit. The object of the Bill is to allow
only those to have them to whom the
authorities think they can be lawfully and
rightly entrusted, and who are fit persons to
be allowed to have firearms in their houses.
Question put, That this Bill be now read
the Third time.

Contents, 1.

Colonel CRAWFORD: Can the Leader of
the House tell us at what date after 12th
October, when the lower House meets, will
the Senate be likely to meet? I suppose it
will not be for sometime after the lower
House assembles, as there will not be anything for us to do.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I think
about a week after the lower House meets.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five Minutes before Four o'clock.
Papers Presented.
The Education Act.

VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I am authorised by the Prime
Minister, on his behalf, to inform the Senate
that His Grace the Governor of Northern
Ireland, in the name and on behalf of His
Majesty the King, has been pleased to give
his assent to the following Bills:-
Mines (Working Facilities, Support, &c.).
Pensions (Increase).
Economy Exchequer Relief.

Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn
until such date as Mr. Speaker may appoint.
-(Viscount Charlemont.)
Mr. DORMAN: I feel it my duty to avail
myself of this opportunity of expressing my
opinion and my appreciation of the treatment that I have received since I entered
this House, at the hands of the late Leader
of the Senate, the right hon., the Marquess
of Londonderry. When I entered this House
I was entirely ignorant of Parliamentary
procedure, and on the occasion of my maiden
speech I well remember how courteously,
unostentatiously the noble Marquess approached me at the close of my remarks,
and drew my attention to the fact that in his
opinion most of what I had said was out of
order. I realised fully the kindness of the
treatment I received at his hands as the
Leader of the House, but I also appreciate
perhaps more than that the benefits that his
Lordship has conferred upon Northern
Ireland in his capacity as Minister of Education. When one looks back at the educational
history of Ireland one is amazed at the
ignorance that prevails amongst the people
as to the past history of their native country
from that particular aspect. At one period
this little island of ours was regarded as the
home of saints and scholars. But the education that our people have received for many
generations has been of such a character
that it has very largely diverted the minds
of the people from the true history of the
country, and has failed to keep green in their
memory the most important epochs of the
past. We certainly remember in this part of
the country, and also at the other end of
Ireland, a particular historical epoch which
we shall very soon be commemorating, an
epoch that, from an educational point of
view, was probably the most disastrous in the
history of our native land.
I have often wondered, when I have seen
thousands demonstrating in memory of that
particular epoch, how very few of them would
cast their minds back a thousand years before that period, and realise the condition of
the country at that particular time and the
state of education that prevailed amongst our
forefathers. Had some of those of us who
will probably be demonstrating in memory of
that particular epoch stood upon one of the
headlands of Northern Ireland a thousand
years before the period we will be commemorating, and examined the passengers and
freight of some small sailing craft making
its way out from one of the loughs of
Northern Ireland, they would have found
amongst the passengers scholars and
missionaries, and amongst the cargo manuscripts of the classics of Greece and Rome,
and manuscripts of the Holy Scriptures. Out
from this particular part of Ireland at that
period there were going scholars and missionaries to educate and civilise the people of
Great Britain and of the Continent of
Europe. On the other hand had they been
privileged to examine some of the incoming
The Education Act.
The Education Act.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
craft they would have found the passengers
to be composed perhaps of the sons of some
chief or some prince from Great Britain or
some part of the Continent of Europe making
their way across the Irish Sea to the shores
of Northern Ireland to take advantage of the
higher education that the sons of Ireland
could confer upon them and which they could
not obtain in their native country.
We come down then from that period to
another period in the educational history of
our country-the period with which will be
associated the name of a predecessor of the
late Leader of the House, a predecessor of the
noble Lord the Minister of Education, the
period from 1782 to 1800. The name of the
noble Marquess's great ancestor will never
be obliterated for his activities in the
interests of his country of that particular
period. At that period Ireland again stood
in the forefront amongst the nations of the
world from an educational point of view,
because when the Census began to be taken
in the middle part of the last century it was
discovered that of those who were children
of school age during that period of eighteen
years in Ireland, a larger proportion were
able to read and write than existed amongst
the population of any other country in
Europe.
I mention those matters in order to express
my appreciation of the services that the noble
Marquess has rendered to Northern Ireland
by successfully placing upon the Statute
Book the Education Act with which his name
will be associated in the future. That Act
may not have reached the Statute Book, and
probably has not reached the Statute Book,
in that perfect form the right hon. Gentleman
would have desired. In its passage to the
Statute Book it has been more or less disfigured through the storms of sectarian
bitterness and the waves of bibliolatry
with which it had to contend, but the noble
Lord has conferred not only a right but a
duty on the parents of the children of
Northern Ireland. The Act has conferred on
them for the first time the right to have a
voice in the selection of the men who will
control the educational machinery through
which their children must pass in the course
of their ordinary education.
It is true that so far there is no widespread manifestation of serious interest
in the duty that has been imposed upon
the electors and the parents of Northern
Ireland by that Act of Parliament, but
I believe a genuine interest in education
as a whole will be the natural result
of that Act. It has at least brought
within the reach of some of the best qualified
children of the working classes the opportunity of passing from the primary school
to the university, and thereby taking advantage of the benefits that the Act has conferred upon the people. Though the Marquess of Londonderry no doubt looks on this
question from an entirely different standpoint from that from which I look at it, I
still feel that it is my duty to avail myself
of this opportunity of expressing my appreciation of the services rendered by his Lordship
in this particular matter.
I look forward to the time when the
result of this Education Act will have been
such that the successors of the present
Minister of Education may build upon the
foundations that the right hon. Gentleman
has laid in that Act an educational superstructure which will not only afford an
opportunity of higher education for some
children, but which will widen the opportunities of education and afford the right
to every child to ascend the educational
ladder from the lowest rung to the highest.
I claim to be connected with a party who
appreciate most fully the loss that we have
sustained through not having had the opportunities of higher education in our youth.
Probably we appreciate the loss we have
sustained far greater than those who have
had the opportunity of higher education
appreciate the advantages they have obtained
through having had access to the channels of
higher education.
I look forward to the time when education
will have been so disseminated in Northern
Ireland that the people will realise that
knowledge is power, and that the people will
be so widely and so highly educated that they
will recognise that wisdom and truth are
more valuable than gold and silver. I believe
that in the future, when educationists come
to study the value of the educational
machinery which will no doubt be evolved
on the foundations we have laid in this Act,
the name of the noble Marquess, the late
Leader of this House and Minister of Education, will be looked back upon as the pioneer
of those who laid the foundations that
resulted in a lasting blessing to the people
and the State of Northern Ireland. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.)
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I do not
think I can reply adequately to the hon.
Senator's most interesting speech. I am sure
everybody in this House will agree with the
sentiments he has expressed towards the late
Leader of the House. With regard to the hon.
Senator's remarks about the Education Act
generally, there is only one thing he said
that I disagree with. I do not think that
anyone who is engaged in the administration
of the Act can possibly complain of any lack
of interest in it on the part of the people of
the Province. The Ministry of Education
has been subjected to a good deal of criticism, and in the Press every morning a great
many persons have something to say about the
1 JUNE 1926
The Education Act

Adjournment.
benefit of this Act and of the super-structure
that may be built upon it. Most of us here,
I think, will not live to see the full effect of
the Act for which the noble Marquess, the
late Minister of Education, was responsible.
administration of the Act, generally of a
rather depreciatory nature. However, I think
all that testifies to the interest that is taken
in the working of the Act, because people do
not, as a rule, criticise or abuse an Act in
which they take no interest, and I think this
criticism is a very healthy sign.
One must always remember that the Education Act is like a new machine that has only
just started to run. A certain amount of
grit must necessarily be in the bearings, and
things do not work so well as they will when
it has been running for a number of years.
I hope that in the future the educational
standard of this country will attain the
height Senator Dorman hopes it will, but I
think that if we in Ulster have one failing
it is that we expect too much too
quickly. It is not possible that every one of
the present generation will receive the full
I would like to thank Senator Dorman for
his speech, for in my opinion he has transformed an official proceeding with a certain
lack of interest that characterises almost all
official proceedings into a very interesting
occasion, and I will certainly remember what
occasion. I shall certainly remember what
he said to-day when I commemorate next
month the anniversary to which the hon.
Member alluded.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at twentyfive minutes before One o'clock
until such date as Mr. Speaker
may appoint.

MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
The Commons have passed the Loans
Guarantee (No. 2) Bill, the Expiring Laws
Continuance Bill, the Industrial Assurance
(Juvenile Societies) Bill, and the Malone
Training School Bill, to which they desire
the concurrence of the Senate.
LOANS GUARANTEE (No. 2) BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
EXPIRING LAWS CONTINUANCE BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time. (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
INDUSTRIAL ASSURANCE (JUVENILE
SOCIETIES) BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
