CLAUSE 16 (Provisions as to Assignment
of Functions to Police Officers and Constables).
(2) The Minister of Home Affairs shall,
subject to the approval of the Ministry of
Finance, have power if he considers that
it is necessary so to do, for the purposes
of this section-
(a) to alter the number and distribution
of the officers and constables of the
Royal Ulster Constabulary as determined by sub-section (2) of section one
of the Constabulary Act (Northern
Ireland), 1922, but so that the general
strength of the Force shall not exceed
the maximum number fixed by the
First Schedule to the said Constabulary
Act; and
(b) to extend, in the case of persons having
special qualifications for the performance of duties to which this section
relates, the limit of age fixed by paragraph (d) of sub-section (1) of section
seven of the said Constabulary Act.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I beg to
move in page 16, lines 1 to 5, to leave out
paragraph (b).
This Clause gives the Ministry power to
extend the age limit for the Constabulary
for the purpose of carrying out the operations of this Act. I think it would be a
wrong step to take to increase the age limit
at a time such as the present. Indeed, the
feeling of the electors all through the
country is in the contrary direction, and a
great deal of argument has been put up in
favour of men leaving their, positions a
little earlier in life to make room for others
who are mo.e in need of employment,
instead of extending the age limit.
I hardly think it would be satisfactory
from a Constabulary point of view, even to
be inclined to make exceptions in the case
of some constables or officers as against
others. It would be liable to cause ill-feeling
and dissatisfaction if there were
a few
favoured people who obtained special positions. At the present time there is no need
whatever for extending the age limit. There
are such considerable numbers in all trades
and professions unemployed that I think
there would be no difficulty whatever in getting the most suitable men possible for the
positions which the Ministry may require
to fill. It is an understood thing that there
is no man indispensable, and wherever there
is a good man there is always a better to be
had. In this case I think it would be no
harm, indeed I think it would be advisable,
that this Clause should be deleted, so that
there would be no possible chance of extending the age limit. We may be told that it
is only in exceptional or in very rare cases
that this may be done, but at the same time
it is dangerous to place a power like this in
the hands of the Ministry, and it would
probably turn out very unsatisfactorily. I
move that this Clause be deleted.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I desire to support
the Amendment which has been proposed by
the hon. Senator opposite. This is the
second occasion on which the Government
have tried to increase the Constabulary.
In 1923 I proposed an Amendment trying to
keep the age within the limits of the Constabulary Act, that is thirty-five years, and
17 NOVEMBER 1926
Motor Vehicles Bill-

Committee
may put out of his mind the idea that
there is any ulterior motive in this, or any
object except to get the very best men we
possibly can, and I can assure him that if
all these men can be obtained from the
ranks of the Royal Ulster Constabulary we
shall be so much the more pleased.
now again when the Motor Bill comes along
they vote in the other House a sum of-I
think the right hon. Gentleman (Sir Dawson
Bates) told us-£12,000. I would like to
know if these gentlemen who are to be appointed are Ulstermen, and what are their
qualifications, that we cannot supply men
at the age of thirty-five years in our own
country. With the dearth of employment
that there is at present, I certainly will
support the Amendment.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think my hon.
Friend (Mr. Joseph Cunningham) is under
a misunderstanding in regard to the object
of this Clause. As he is no doubt aware we
are precluded from taking any man into the
police force who is over twenty-seven years
of age, and we propose to ask for power to
increase that for this particular purpose,
but we do not propose to increase the retiring age of any individual.
With regard to the point raised by my
hon. Friend (Mr. Hill Dickson), he suggests
that we have actually persons nominated
and ready for these appointments. I would
like to give a most emphatic denial to that.
I do not know yet how many men in the
Royal Ulster Constabulary will be fit to
carry out this work, or whether or not it
will be necessary to bring in men with
technical skill and experience in motors.
Of course it will only be a temporary
Measure, because I hope that in the course
of time men will be recruited generally into
the force and ultimately get these positions.
I would remind my hon. Friends that the
whole success of the administration of this
Act depends upon getting suitable individuals.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: You will
have no trouble.
Sir DAWSON BATES: My hon. Friend
says I will have no trouble. He may know
the details and the professional experience
in the motor world that the men of the
Royal Ulster Constabulary have. I do not.
If there are sufficient men in the Royal
Ulster Constabulary with sufficient experience no men will be brought in at all, but
do not forget this: that men's livelihoods,
and poor men's livelihoods, may depend
upon the reports of these inspectors. Several
machines, I am certain, will be ordered off
the road as a result of these reports, and I
want to be in a position, if the matter is
raised in this House, or in the lower House,
and I am challenged as to why the police
have refused a licence to the owner of a
hackney vehicle, to be able to say what the
qualifications of the inspector concerned
are. The only object of this Clause is to
get the best men we can. I hope we will
be able to get them all in Ulster. I do not
see why we should not. My hon. Friend
Mr. HILL DICKSON: It is a question of
age. It is a question of appointing men of
that age to a pensionable job.
Sir DAWSON BATES: May I point out
that it does not affect the pension rights
one iota, because a man going into the police
force will have to retire at the same age as
the others who have been in the force
earlier will have to retire, and, of course,
his pension will be so much less.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: If I am
to understand that an outsider will have an
opportunity, and that the positions are not
to be strictly confined to a few, I will be
very well satisfied. It must be taken into
consideration that those at present in the
Royal Ulster Constabulary have enjoyed very
comfortable steady employment for a good
number of years, while there are many
people outside who are quite as competent,
and who have been unemployed. I am
speaking now as an engineer with some
knowledge of the motor trade and motor
business, and I do not think there would
be any difficulty in getting thoroughly
capable men to fill these positions, but
after the explanation given by the right
hon. Gentleman I have no hesitation in
withdrawing.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

vehicles of any particular class, that is

fares to be charged for or on such
vehicles for passengers and luggage,
as well within the said distance of ten

甭
Before I move the Amendment standing
in my name I would like just to express the
pleasure it is to me and to the Committee,
I am sure, to have the Minister of Home
Affairs (Sir Dawson Bates) here to-day to
answer these questions and to make such
clear statements. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) In moving this Amendment the principle that I want to secure is that when a
by-law is once passed by the Ministry of
Home Affairs it may come up for review.
As the Clause stands now I would be afraid
that, once it was passed, it never could be
reviewed by the Home Office, and if we
would only say that the Belfast Corporation,
say, or the county council by whom that
by-law was applied for could bring it up
and have it altered, it would meet the case
I think it would be an advantage that it
should be renewed by the Home Office. If
it is renewed by the Home Office then
Parliament has something to say in the
matter, and we could see these by-laws
and ask questions on them. Without saying
more or keeping the Committee at this late
hour, I just beg to move.
Sir DAWSON BATES: With regard to the
right hon. Senator's Amendment, I entirely
agree with the principle which he has laid
down. He drew my attention to the matter
some time ago and I told him then, as I
tell him now, that the Ministry's intention
is not to sanction any by-law for a longer
period than one year, so that every case will
come up. I might explain to the Committee
that when the Corporation, or other local
authorities, apply for by-laws, a public inquiry will be held, after due notice of at
least a month, to enable every interested
party, whether bus owners, or the general
public, or anybody else, to come before the
inspector and express their views, and then,
after that, it comes before the Ministry for
confirmation or not, as the case may be. I
would suggest to my right hon. Friend that
it would be better not to move his Amendment, but to leave the matter as it is, because circumstances may arise where the
Ministry may wish to make the confirmation for even a shorter period than one year.
After all, we are in an entirely experimental
stage, especially in regard to these by-laws,
and we want to keep ourselves as unfettered
as possible, so that these by-laws can be
brought up and revised at least once a year.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I would
be quite in agreement with the right hon.
Senator who moved this Amendment, though
I would suggest that now that we have got a
proper assurance from the Minister of Home
Affairs he should not press it. I think it
would meet his case if words were inserted
to make the confirmation for a period of not
longer than one year.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I will be very glad
to look into this point and see if it can be
legally fixed in that way.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: On that
assurance I ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 18,
page 18, line 12, to leave out from the beginning of the line to "and" in line 13.
The words which I suggest should be left
out are, on the face of them, rather extraordinary words to appear in this Bill. It
would seem that the local authority, which
in this case is probably likely to be the
Belfast Corporation, is to have power to fix
the maximum rates or fares for various
vehicles on the route of any tramway belonging to "such council or commissioners,"
and that seems to apply whether or not the
tramway is within the boundary of their
district. The words cannot possibly have
relation to tramcars, because they are excepted from the Bill altogether. I confess I
do not know what the words mean, though
there may be some simple explanation, but
on the face of it, it looks as if there was an
attempt, in the interests of some tramway
system, to impose rates of fares and so on,
on other vehicles, in order to bring them out
of competition with the tramways. I hope
this is not the case, but I must confess that
on the face of it, it looks as if it is.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I would like to
remove any idea in the minds of hon.
Senators that this Bill is for the purpose
of bolstering up the Belfast trams or any
other means of locomotion. That is not the
case. It is for the purpose of securing the
safety of the public. In reference to this
Motor Vehicles Bill-
17 NOVEMBER 1926
Committee.
particular Clause, the Government do not
propose to take control of the traffic in any
of the local authorities' areas, but, as I
pointed out, when these by-laws are made
anyone who is interested will have the right
to appear, put forward his objections, and
have the whole thing thoroughly thrashed
out. Then I have already given an undertaking that these by-laws will not be in
force for a period of more than one year. 1
cannot imagine that any injustice will be
done to anyone.
Mr. LESLIE: If the tramway is within
the bounds of the district it is apparently
already dealt with by this proposal, but the
route of the tramway may be outside the
district. I cannot understand the necessity
for the words, and I am afraid-
Sir DAWSON BATES: I do not think it
makes any difference.
Mr. LESLIE: Well, what are the words
for?
Sir DAWSON BATES: 1 will look into the
matter again. I do not want to appear as
if I brush aside the arguments put forward
by my hon. and learned Friend. Perhaps
in the circumstances he will withdraw, and
I will consider the question before the Report
stage.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 19 (Regulations by Ministry of
Home Affairs for purposes of Part II of
Act) and 20 (Payments into and out of
Road Fund [Northern Ireland]) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 21 (Penalties and legal proceedings)-
(4) Any police officer or constable who
appears on any information, complaint or
application before a court of summary
jurisdiction under this Act or under any
Act mentioned in sub-section (3) of this
section, shall have a right of appeal against
any order made by such court in the
matter of the information, complaint or
application, and the provisions of section
twenty-four of the Petty Sessions (Ireland)
Act, 1851, relating to appeals (except provisions as to recognizances) shall apply
accordingly.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 21,
page 21, lines 17 to 26, to leave out sub-
Section (4).
The sub-Clause I desire to leave out is a
most extraordinary Clause. It apparently
proposes-I do not say that it effects the
purpose that any police officer or constable
who appears on any information, complaint,
or application before a court of summary
jurisdiction, if he loses his case, is to have
the right of appeal. Such a proposal was
never heard of. There are only three exceptions that I know of. Under the ordinary
law of England, and the English-speaking
world I may say, if a man has once been
tried before a court of competent jurisdiction
he is not to be vexed again for the same
cause. Under this Clause a man may be
charged with some offence, such as overcrowding a char-a-banc, or whatever it may
be, and if the magistrates dismiss the case
this extraordinary Clause proposes to give
the police officer or constable the right of
appeal before the county court judge against
the dismiss-a most extraordinary provision.
I presume that is the meaning of it. I
presume that any order made by such
court" is meant to include a dismissal.
a
There are only three exceptions I know
of where appeals are allowed in criminal
cases as opposed to civil cases.
In every
civil case that comes before the magistrates
an appeal has always been allowed to either
party, but not in criminal cases where
there has never been any right of appeal
against a dismiss. We have instances
where an appeal is given under the
Fisheries Act, where the question is
quasi-civil matter, and under the Cattle
Diseases Act of 1894, where again the
question is of general application, and we
have, I am sorry to say, an instance in
Northern Ireland in the Intoxicating
Liquor Act, 1923, where something of this
sort was put in. I objected at the time to
the Clause in this House. The noble Viscount, the Parliamentary Secretary (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard) told us-I
remember distinctly-that he hoped I was
not going to move an Amendment, as great
care would be taken to see that the Clause
was not used for any oppressive purpose.
As any questions likely to be dealt with
under that Act were to be dealt with by
two resident magistrates, I thought that perhaps an appeal from two resident magistrates on a technical matter would not do
much harm, and I did not move an
Amendment. I believe I am correct in
saying there was only one appeal under
that Section which happened the other day,
when the decision was reversed.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Two
Mr. LESLIE: That is not a very big bag.
I must press, in accordance with the whole
tenor of the common Law of England, that
this particular sub-Clause be taken out.
Mr. LONG: As the right hon. Senator,
Colonel Sharman Crawford, will recollect,
the Roads Advisory Committee were very
strongly in favour of this provision, and it
seems to me personally it is only fair and
reasonable that a police officer should get the
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
same consideration and be allowed the same
rights and privileges in the matter of an
appeal as any ordinary individual. It is
hardly fair that he should be penalised because he is a police officer. We are all fully
agreed that we can trust the constabulary
not to bring a vexatious prosecution or to
appeal in frivolous cases. It is never done,
and I appeal to the hon. Senator to withdraw his Amendment; at least, I hope it will
not be accepted.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I hope my hon.
Friend will not see his way to press this
Amendment. I think it is a most necessary
and correct Clause to be put in any Bill. I
quoted some figures earlier this afternoon
to show how hopeless the administration is
at the present time. After all, we are in
earnest over this Bill or we are not. I can
assure him that the power of appeal will
only be used by the police in the case of a
gross miscarriage of justice. The fact that
in connection with the Intoxicating Liquor
Act only two or three cases of appeal have
been taken goes to show that the police do
not desire to do anything in a vindictive
spirit or to harass the public. It seems to
me that without this Clause the Bill may
very well be scrapped.
Mr. LESLIE: I am very sorry I cannot
agree. If a case is brought forward and
the magistrates dismiss the case, and the
police propose to take it to a further court.
they put the man charged to any amount of
inconvenience and expense. He may be out
of the country or in the country. The police
have the extraordinary advantage before
the re-hearing-because an appeal to the
county court judge is a re-hearing-of having the man's defence before them. They
put on somebody the responsibility of carrying out the provisions of Section 20 of the
Petty Sessions Act. Those are the provisions
which, as I am sure some of my hearers will
remember, require that if either party demand it, the magistrates, or one of them,
shall take a note of the evidence. It is
obvious that if this Clause were applied
every person charged with an offence under
this Act will require the magistrates to
take a note of the evidence in order, if the
case is dismissed and the police appeal, that
they may be able to question witnesses as
to what they said in the court below.
There is no appeal, speaking generally,
from a verdict of acquittal in a criminal
charge. It would be a most retrograde step
if we were to adopt this in Northern Ireland
If the right hon. Gentleman is convinced
that the magistrates' courts are inefficient
there is a proper course to take change
them. But as long as you have the courts
trust them, or try to trust them. Remember
that they have heard the evidence in a
particular case which you have not, and
do not, even on the advice of the Roads
Advisory Committee, which is not Parliament, trample on the common law of England. It was laid down by Blackstone, a
greater man than we have to-day, more than
a century ago, that where a man has once
been tried by a court of competent jurisdiction he is not to be vexed again for the
same cause. I cannot withdraw this
Amendment.
On Question, That this sub-Section be
left out."
The Committee divided: Contents, 3; Not
Contents, 7.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 21,
page 21, line 26, at end (end of Clause) to
add the following new sub-section:
(5) Where a period of not less than
three years has elapsed between two consecutive convictions of the same person,
the later conviction of that person shall
not for the purpose of this Act be deemed
to be a second or subsequent conviction.
This new sub-Clause is really to carry out
what I think is the intention of the Roads
Act, 1920, as regards the issue of licences
free from endorsements. It is provided in
the Roads Act, and the provision is repeated
Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.
Mr. W. J. McDowell.
in the Bill before us, that where a licence
is clean for three years a man may have a
new licence free from any endorsements of
an anterior date. What I am proposing
here is that as we are introducing
minimum penalties for second offences we
should provide that if a man has not been
convicted for a period of three years a conviction subsequent to those three years
should not be regarded as a second offence.
That is, if a first offence is committed in
1926, and the second is not committed
till 1930, the offence of 1930 shall not
for the
purposes of this Act be
deemed to be a second conviction. I
17 NOVEMBER 1926
Motor Vehicles Bill-

Committee.
ber of vehicles used, or as to the character,
weight or horse-power of any vehicle, or
as to the number of persons seated by a
vehicle or as to the purpose for which any
vehicle has been used, the burden of proof
in respect of the matter in question shall
lie on the defendant.
think that is a reasonable thing. As the
thing stands at present a man who was convicted ten years ago and got a clean sheet
for ten years might have that old conviction
still stand against him. I do not think that
is right. I think we would get over that by
inserting the sub-Section.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I do not know
whether the hon. Senator has overlooked the
effect of Clause 4 (3). That is as far as we
can go. Hon. Members will see by that
Clause that where there has been no conviction endorsed on the licence for the previous three years, the individual will get a
clean sheet.
Mr. LESLIE: Yes, I recognise that under
the sub-Section they get a licence free from
endorsement, but they do not get a complete
whitewashing. It is possible to prove a
conviction otherwise than by endorsement.
Sir DAWSON BATES: That raises entirely another point. In this case we are
prepared to issue a clean sheet at the end
of three years, provided they have no new
endorsement. What my hon. Friend wishes
to do is supposing a person has committed
three offences and is convicted, then by
some wiping off he is to get pardon for the
previous offences. If a person goes to jail
for theft, at the end of three years, and he
has not stolen anybody's purse, he is to
get a clean bill of health and be treated as
if he had never been convicted.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid the right hon.
Gentleman is not fairly representing the
purport of the Amendment. The Amendment is only for the purpose of this Act,
and there is no comparison between this and
a person guilty of stealing. I shall really
press the Amendment.
Amendment negatived.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 22 (False statements and forgery
or fraudulent use of licences, etc.).
(1) If any person in connection with an
application for a licence under any provision of this Act makes a declaration
which to his knowledge is false or in any
material respect misleading, or if any
person, being required by this Act or regulations made thereunder to furnish
particulars in connection with any such
application or otherwise in connection
with any vehicle, furnishes any particulars which to his knowledge are false or
in any material respect misleading, such
person shall be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding fifty pounds
or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months.
(2) If in any proceedings under this
section any question arises as to the num-
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 21,
line 33, leave out from "application" ("application or otherwise") to the end of the line.
This is a Clause dealing with applications
for licences, and it is, as hon. Members who
have looked into the matter will recognise,
based on Section 13 of the Roads Act, 1920.
Sub-Section 2 dealt with licensing matters,
and we seem to have followed that until we
come to the words I suggest leaving out for
the moment-" or otherwise in connection
with any vehicle." These words do not
seem to me to mean much, but if they are
being deliberately put in they probably
mean something. Although I move to leave
them out, I am prepared to withdraw the
Amendment if the right hon. Gentleman
will tell us what he really wants the words
for. What is the intention?
Mr. LONG: If I correctly understand the
purport of the words, they seem to be
necessary for the purpose of requiring
correct information about any machine in
cases of accident. Fords are very much
alike. They are much of a muchness, and
it is only when the cover is lifted and the
engine number examined that you can detect
the difference between one Ford and another.
Cases I know have occurred in which an
accident was due to defective machinery in
one car, and the owner substituted and
submitted another car for examination. 1
know, as chairman of the licensing authority in County Derry, that instances have
occurred where char-a-banc proprietors have
only four licences, and yet they own six
vehicles. I think it is absolutely necessary
that a provision of this sort should be put
in requiring full and correct particulars
regarding the application. They are especially necessary in cases of accidents.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Having
some knowledge of the subject, I am in agreement with my Friend (Mr. Long). There
has been a great deal of trouble with people
who are driving cars and substituting
vehicles for other ones.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government
cannot accept this Amendment. It is absolutely necessary that full power should be
given to the Government to find out exactly
the weight of the machine, because on that
may depend people's lives, the number of
the machine, and generally all particulars
relating to the vehicle. My hon. Friend (Mr.
Leslie) wants to know why we put in the
words "or otherwise in connection with any
Motor Vehicles Bill.
Finance Bill.
THE SENATE
[Sir Dawson Bates]
vehicle." We may want this information at
some other time; not at the time the application is made. I have known cases similar
to that referred to by Senator Long. There is
a certain scale, according to the weight of
the machine, and Fords are one example
where there is fraud. Another case is where
a man has 16 or 17 machines, and perhaps
he only takes out licences for nine. That is
a fraud on the revenue, and it is also gambling with people's lives. I hope under the
circumstances that the hon. Member will
withdraw the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I asked for an explanation,
and I seem to have got it. At all events I
must take it. This is only required in connection with applications for licences. It
does not apply to anything else for the
purposes of the Act?
Sir DAWSON BATES: The information
may not be wanted at the time the application is made.
Mr. LESLIE: I have much pleasure in
asking leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 21,
line 40, leave out from the first "as" ("as
to the number') to the end of the line.
This is an inquiry as to the number of
vehicles used. Is that material to the application for a licence under this Section?
Sir DAWSON BATES: It is a most
necessary thing. I recall one case that came
to
knowledge in connection
my
with
Down County Council, where a man had
about four or five vehicles, and where it

Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Mr. LONG: Hon. Members know that this
Bill relates to the position of barristers who
have been educated at King's Inns and been
called to the Southern Bar, and are now also
being called to the Northern Bar. I have no
objection whatever to the remission of the
dues which they may be called upon to pay,
because they have been called upon to
by
pay
the Southern Bar. What I
should like to ascertain from the mover of
the Third Reading is if any reciprocal
arrangement exists in the Southern Bar to
what is proposed in the Northern Bar.
would be exceedingly undesirable that watertight compartments should be set up between
the two Bars-the Northern area and the
Southern area-in legal matters. It is
exceedingly desirable that barristers practising in the North should be permitted to
appear in the courts in the South and vice
versa. I trust the benchers of the Northern
Inn will co-operate with the benchers of the
Southern Inn to secure if possible such
reciprocity.

Mr. LESLIE: May I ask the noble
Viscount if he proposes to adjourn the
House until to-morrow? I think we may take
it that the suspension of Standing Orders
will be necessary in order to clear up the
business of the session to-morrow afternoon,
with the exception, of course, of giving the
Royal Assent. I think we have nothing left
but the Report stage and Third Reading of
the Bill, which has occupied the attention
of the House this afternoon. The Amendments that may come before us for consideration to-morrow do not appear to be
vital. It may be possible to dispose of the
remaining business to-morrow, and if that
is so it will obviate the necessity of a sitting on Friday. But if we have to sit after
to-morrow it would be more convenient to
have a sitting on Friday than to come back
in the following week.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: To-morrow
I shall move the suspension of Standing
Orders, and I think that will enable us to
clear up all the outstanding business on that
day.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

o'clock.
Writs of Ejectment. 484
Writs of Ejectment.
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 18th November, 1926.
The House met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
SUSPENSION OF STANDING ORDER.
Moved:

'That Standing Order No. XXV
be suspended for this day's Sitting, in respect
of the Motor Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Bill."-(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
COSTS OF WRITS OF EJECTMENT.
The following question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Dorman:
"To ask the Leader of the House whether
it is a fact that the costs in the case of
Writs of Ejectment are excessive, and in
many cases exceed the amount of the rent
due; and whether the Government will
take steps to remedy this state of affairs."
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to draw the attention of the Government to the question I
have placed upon the Paper. The attention
of the Government was drawn to this
particular question during the last Session
of Parliament, and apparently nothing has
been done to amend the unfair conditions to
which the working class occupants of houses
in Belfast are subjected when an order for
ejectment is proceeded with against them.
The particular case which has drawn my
attention to this matter is the case of a
working man who has been unemployed, and
who was proceeded against for arrears of
rent, the sum of rent due being £2 16s., and
the costs in connection with the action being
£3 17s. 3d. On having his attention drawn
to this matter the learned judge referred to
the fact that he had, on several occasions,
mentioned this unnecessary burden of costs
that was being placed upon the shoulders of
the working class when ejectment orders
were obtained against them
The conditions existing in regard to the
housing of the working classes in Belfast
to-day are of such a character that in the
near future we may anticipate a large
number of attempts being made to eject them
from their homes because of arrears of rent.
It would be unfair that men who, through
no deliberate action of their Own to
avoid paying a just rent, but who through
unfavourable circumstances and unemployment have been compelled to run a month or
two in arrear should be subjected, if ejectment orders are obtained against them, to a
continuation of these abnormal expenses in
the form of costs for such ejections. The
record of Belfast at present is, unfortunately,
very bad in that respect. When one considers that during the interim between the
two censuses the proportion of one-room
dwellers in the borough area has been increased by between 600 per cent. and 700 per
cent., one can see how hard pressed these
poor people are to obtain homes.
In illustration of this I would like to
mention one particular case out of many.
I occupy a house of six apartments, for
which I pay £24 a year rent. A personal
friend of mine is paying no less than £31 4s.
for a single apartment almost in the centre
of the city, and he has been looking out for
a house for some years now. These circumstances make it imperative that those persons who are compelled to live in one room
should offer the exceedingly high rent that
the present occupiers of houses are paying
under the Rent and Mortgage Interest
Restrictions Act. These conditions will become a great incentive to the houseowning class to take advantage of the
economic conditions, and will increase
abnormally the amount of applications for
ejectment for the non-payment of rent among
the working class of Belfast.
Indeed, the Government themselves, I
understand, have been a victim in this particular matter. I have read in the papers
that they got the Royal Ulster Constabulary
barrack on Cregagh Road for which they
were paying £65 a year. I understand that
for some reason or other they failed to make
application before the expiration of the year
for a renewal of the tenancy, and that the
landlord has succeeded in exacting from the
Government, instead of a rental of £65 a
rental of £265 for the barrack, showing
plainly that even a powerful Government
cannot resist the avarice and greed of a landlord if he is disposed to take advantage of
any little failure on the part of a tenant to
comply fully with the terms of the letting or
agreement.
I take it for granted that the chief object
of all Governments should be the establishment of peace and a just relationship between
the citizens, and the establishment of a peaceful
and a just relationship between the citizens
and the various Departments of the State.
I cannot conceive how any man who owes rent
and who is exposed to costs similar to those
mentioned by the learned Recorder can dwell
in a state of peace of mind with regard to
the legal branch of the State. There must
necessarily be rankling in the mind of any
working man subject to an exorbitant fee of
this description a sense of injustice and indignation that the Government should allow
a condition such as this to prevail. I have
very much pleasure in asking the question.
18 NOVEMBER 1926
Writs of Ejectment.

Writs of Ejectment.
to be a ridiculous position. Surely our
Government ought to be able to get out of
the difficulty and try to remove this terrible
hardship that is weighing on the poor people
at the present time. I would make a special
appeal to the Government to consider this
matter very carefully.
Mr. MAGEE: It is not always that the hon.
Senator who has just spoken and I agree, but
on this subject we do agree. The hon.
Senator has spoken of the state of affairs
that exists from one side of the question. I
can speak of it from another. It has been
my misfortune on many occasions, I regret
to say, to have been the means of proceedings for ejectment being taken against
tenants. In very many cases, as the hon.
Senator has just said, this default is not due
to any wilfulness on the part of the tenants.
Very frequently the tenants go to some
charitable organisation, and what is the position when the tenant does that? A decree
has been got against the poor, unfortunate
man whose rent is 5/- or 6/- a week. The
first thing that must be done before the
decree is set aside or waived is that £6 10s.
must be put down. I have already said in
this House that it is a cruel thing-it is a
thing that goes to my heart to have to enrich the lawyers and the courts and the
sheriffs by the sum of £6 10s. If a man is
unable to pay 6/- a week how on earth can
he expect to raise the amount of money required to pay the enormous costs?
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. MAGEE: There is a very simple procedure, that is Petty Sessions jurisdiction.
I moved an Amendment to the Rent Restrictions Bill, the object of which was to extend
the Petty Sessions jurisdiction. The answer
that came was," Oh, this cannot be done,
it is a separate matter; it must be done in a
separate Bill." There is a Bill coming before us to-day in which it is proposed-I
think Senator Joseph Cunningham tried to
amend it yesterday-to alter the Constabulary Act. Surely to goodness no one can
say the Constabulary Act had anything to
do with motors. But still if it suits-I do
not know whom it suits, it does not seem to
suit anybody-why on earth if an extraneous
matter can be brought in in one Measure,
cannot it be brought in in another Measure?
I really feel very strongly that the hon.
Senator, who asked this question, has a case
which no one, on due consideration, can say
is a bad one.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I would
also like to associate myself with the remarks
of Senator Dorman and the Mayor of Derry.
There is no one, I think, who knows the
position better than the working-class who
are living among the working-class people.
It is, undoubtedly, a terrible hardship at the
present time to find that people who are
unable to pay their small weekly rent, when
they are taken before the court instead of
small weekly amounts being imposed on
them, which they are able to pay, have costs
heaped up against them which make it
impossible for them to pay. It seems
Mr. BARCLAY: I would also like to
associate myself with the hon. Senators who
have just spoken. I know, from personal
experience, of a number of poor people,
honest, respectable, good citizens, who, unfortunately, owing to the depression of trade
have been unemployed. There are none of
us here to-day but can have some realisation of what this means to a man who has
got a small family, say three or four, or
perhaps more. Those children are not fit to
work. Neither are they fit to want. It
seems very hard when that man is prosecuted by a landlord for the non-payment of
rent that he should find the costs of the
prosecution are more than the rent due.
This is where the hardship comes in, that
when the case goes against this poor man,
and a decree is granted, he and his wife and
children and their few sticks of furniture
are left upon the street.
Is it any wonder that we have the condition of affairs we have to-day in the city
of Belfast, where, as Senator Dorman has
pointed out, there are so many families all
crowding together to try to make a home
for themselves. Now, I hope that this question will get due consideration from the
Government in the near future, and that
something will be done to lighten the burden
that these poor people are suffering from
at the present time. Times will not always
be like this. We are living in hope that
trade and industry in this country will
revive. When that time comes these people
will be in a good position, and perhaps be able
to pay some of the rent that is against them
at the present time. I hope the Government
will take steps in that direction.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I think the point
that has been put forward by the Mayor
of Derry and the hon. Senator (Mr. Dorman)
is one that the Government will have to
consider. I do not know what reply will be
given to the question to-day, but I hold that
it is no reply to say that it is not included
in the Act. When the Government can include an increased age limit as regards the
police in the Motor Act, a Clause of a
similar kind could surely be included in
this Act to cover the question as to costs
in the eviction of tenants.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): Scales of costs are fixed by the
courts, and I have no reason to believe that
the costs in cases of the nature indicated
487 Motor Vehicles Bill--

THE SENATE
Report Stage.
[Viscount Charlemont]
in this question are excessive, although they
may possibly, in certain cases, exceed the
amount of the rent due.
I may point out, however, for the information of the hon. Senator, that the question which he now raises is closely allied
with a matter raised in another place on
the 14th December, 1925, when in answer
to a question of Mr. Kyle, the Minister of
Home Affairs stated that the matter would
be borne in mind in considering certain
other questions connected with summary
jurisdiction. The point in question has not
been lost sight of, but it is not by any means
as simple as would appear at first sight. I
may say, speaking for myself, that in view
of the very strong representations which
have been put up by the hon. Senators
to-day I will recommend the question
to the further favourable consideration of
the Government. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: May 1
just say, as regards the answer given by
the noble Viscount, that I am very glad
he has given this assurance, and I hope he
will carry it out, because I am certain that
if it had been put to a vote of the House.
to-day there would have been a unanimous.
decision in favour of it. I know that there
are great grievances on this subject, and 1
have great sympathy with the people.
hope the Government will take a note of the
matter, and do something to curb the extraordinary costs that are fixed.

Order for Report of Amendments read.

On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE (Suspension and endorsement of
licence and disqualification).
In cases where a conviction has been upheld on appeal, this sub-section shall apply
as if for references to the conviction and
the convicting court there were substituted references to the decision given on
the appeal and to the court by whom that
decision was given.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 8, line
16, leave out "and the convicting court."
The first Amendment standing in my name
on the Paper is, as hon. Member's will recollect, a suggested Amendment which was
left over from the Committee stage yesterday afternoon, in order that the matter
raised might be further considered. The
point at issue in so far as it is at issue is
really one simply of administration. The
idea underlying my original proposal was to
insure that in a case where an appeal for the
removal of suspension, imposed itself by a
court of appeal, came up, the notice which
is necessary to be given to the police should
be given to the district-inspector of police for
the district where the offence was originally
charged, and where the first conviction took
place. It seems to me that, perhaps, it would
be more convenient for the district-inspector
to be notified rather than that the other
district-inspector operating in the immediate
vicinity of the court of appeal should receive
notification. Since the Amendment was
withdrawn by me yesterday afternoon, discussions, if I may say so, have been going on
behind the Chair, and I understand that the
right hon. Gentleman (Sir Dawson Bates)
will now explain to us exactly how it is proposed that the machinery introduced by this
Clause should operate, and I think it will
probably be the case when the House has
heard the right hon. Gentleman that we
shall see that this is a case where the
original Amendment should not be pressed,
but for the moment I must move the words
as they stand.
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS (Sir
Dawson Bates): Since yesterday I have gone
into the point which was raised by the
learned Senator, and the effect of the Clause
as it now stands is, shortly, this. Assume a
man committed an offence in Ballymoney,
which will be familiar to the learned Senator,
and had his licence suspended for say twelve
months. He appeals to the higher court, the
higher court being the county court judge
sitting at Ballymena. Assuming the county
court judge confirmed that conviction, it
would still be open to the defendant, at the
end of three months, to apply to the convicting court, which in this case would be
the county court judge, to remit part of the
twelve months' suspension, but before the
applicant could make application to the
court he would have to serve notice, and
under the Bill it is proposed that the district-inspector under the convicting court,
which would be Ballymena and not Ballymoney, would be served with notice of the
intended application. From the administrative point of view what would happen is
that when the district-inspector at Ballymena got the notice of the proposed application the first thing he would do would be to
send the particulars up to headquarters,
because it is proposed under this Bill to have
a Central Register of every motorist who
holds a licence. There will be a complete
record as to what are his misdeeds from the
time the licence was issued. Full particu-
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Third Reading.
18 NOVEMBER 1926
lars will be sent to the Central Registry. It
would be obviously desirable, in the interests
of justice, that the police should ascertain
how the defendant, who is applying to get
this remission of twelve months suspension,
was behaving himself, and whether or not
he may not have in the meantime committed some further offence at Ballymoney.
I think that if the Amendment was pressed
it would, instead of improving the Measure,
only more cramp and fetter the administration of the Act. I am sure hon. Senators are
aware that cases have occurred where a man
got a licence in Tyrone and had it suspended,
and then that individual came to Belfast and
got a new licence. Under this Act that will
be absolutely impossible with the Central
Registry, and every information in the interests of the police and the administration
of the Act will be given to the districtinspector who originally started the case
against the defendant.
Mr. MAGEE: May I ask if the present Bill
applies to offences committed before the
passing thereof, or what is the procedure in
such cases?
Sir DAWSON BATES: No, it will only
apply to subsequent offences.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that the whole
House will join with me in feeling gratified
at the statement which has just been made
by the right hon. Gentleman. I am very
glad to hear about the Central Registry. I
beg leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 18 (Powers of Councils of County
Boroughs, and Urban Districts, &c., for
Regulation of Traffic).
(f) For fixing the maximum rates or fares
to be charged for or on such vehicles for
passengers and luggage, as well within
the said distance of ten miles as within
the boundaries of the district, and the
minimum rates or fares to be charged on
such vehicles for passengers and luggage
within the boundaries of the district or
on the route of any tramway belonging to
such council or commissioners, and for
preventing the drivers or conductors of
any such vehicles from demanding more
than the maximum, or accepting less than
the minimum, rates or fares so fixed.
#

*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 18,
line 12, leave out or on the route of any
tramway belonging to such council or commissioners." This again is a small matter
which stood over from yesterday. I proposed
to omit certain words, and I now repeat the
proposal. It seems to me that the words are
redundant, and possibly open to misconception and are misleading. I think, therefore,
it would be better, if only for some of the
reasons I gave yesterday, that they should be
withdrawn.
Sir DAWSON BATES: On further consideration the Government are prepared to
accept the Amendment which has been moved
by my learned Friend. I agree with him that
the words to a very large extent are redundant and unnecessary.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: Are we
quite assured now that the Clause as it stands
will prevent corporations from having their
buses or tramways outside their own district?
There were cases where buses were owned by
the Corporation, and the County Council had
to take action to prevent them from running.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I can assure my
hon. Friend that this Bill in no way affects
the rights of corporations or
any other
authority to go outside. They have the
right to go outside. This Bill does not
affect them.
Amendment agreed to
Moved:
That this Bill be now read the
Third time.--(Viscount Charlemont.)
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill which we are now
about to read for the Third time is one of
great interest, and we have been for some
time discussing it. I do not want to go
back on anything that has been said during
the past few days on the matter, but I
think, perhaps, I might refer for a few
moments to an effect which the Bill is likely
to have on magistrates. Under this Bill the
powers of magistrates to some extent have
been-I will not say curtailed, but circumscribed and it may be that there is some
feeling in the minds of the magistrates about
it. As some remarks were made which, perhaps, magistrates as a whole might deprecate,
I should like, as one who perhaps to some
extent put up the case for the magistrates
to draw the attention of the House to the
fact that magistrates are unpaid public
servants, that their difficulties are very
great, and that those of them appointed
in recent years, by which I mean since 1908,
when the Children's Act came in-
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Oh!
Mr. LESLIE: I take that date because it
is
a convenient date to reckon from; so
very much has happened since.
The year
1908 is eleven years from the year 1897,
which was the date of the last issue of
"Humphrey's Justice of the Peace." The
point is that since 1897, in spite of the
Children's Act and other miscellaneous
statutes which came into operation later, a
magistrate has no handbook to which he can
refer in order to learn his business. The
only book which has been published since
is a somewhat monumental work in two
Motor Vehicles Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
volumes by O'Connor, and of this the second
edition was published in 1916, and is, therefore, somewhat out of date by now.
I would like to say also that it must be
recognised by the magistrates concerned
that where a discretion remains vested in
them they must exercise a sound discretion
guided by law, and must not give effect to
their own whims and fancies. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) That is to say, if I may take
an easy example, it may possibly become my
duty to administer a minimum fine, and 1
shall certainly do so, not forgetting that if
the circumstances warrant it I am not to
be regarded as being limited to that minimum fine, and under some of the Clauses
which occupied our attention yesterday I do
not think I should wish to limit myself to
the minimum penalty provided. I hope this
Bill will be received in a friendly spirit by
all classes, by the magistrates by whom it
will have to be administered, by the motorists
themselves, and by the general public,
especially by the last mentioned, as it is a
measure mainly devised to ensure their
safety. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I did
not mean to intervene, but the hon. Senator
(Mr. Leslie) always seems to give me an opportunity of doing so. He has now raised a
new point about magistrates, in connection
with which I may tell a little story which
does not quite carry out his views. My father,
who was an eminent barrister, when he came
down to live in this part of the world, and
was made a magistrate, was complimented
by some of his brother magistrates on the
fact that as a barrister and a lawyer he
would be a great help to the Bench. I remember he told me that the words he used
in reply were: "Well, gentleman, I can tell
you that the less law magistrates know the
better, and let them use their commonsense. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I would like
to say that I am certain that the magistrates in connection with this Motor Bill
will use at least as much commonsense in
the future as I hope they have done in the
past. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Charlemont.)

I think the Prorogation takes place on the

Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Does
that mean that when these Amendments go

unless there are some fresh Amendments

VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: That is so.

30 NOVEMBER 1926
Royal Assent to Bills.
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 30th November, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Eleven
of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
PAPER PRESENTED.
Ministry of Labour:
National Health Insurance: Deposit Contributors Amendment Regulations (Northern
Ireland), 1926.
Presented (pursuant to Act), and ordered
to lie on the Table.
the House of Commons on the 23rd November, 1926, as reported in the Parliamentary
Debates, columns 2376, 2386, 2390 and 2410, on
consideration of the Senate Amendments to
the various Bills then before that House.
Mr. SPEAKER: My reply to the right hon.
Member only, of course, can apply to the
practice in this House, and that must be
clearly understood. The use of offensive or
insulting words against the character or
proceedings of the other House is out of
order. But, though I fully appreciate the
spirit which prompted the question of the
right hon. Gentleman, I do not feel really
that, as long as this House carries out
conscientiously the duties for which it was
created, we need concern ourselves too much
with criticism, however ill-mannered.
Question of Privilege.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: As the
first item of the business to-day, I have,
with your permission, to ask a question of
which I have given private notice. It is
common knowledge with the Members of the
Senate that on certain Amendments from
this House being sent forward to the Lower
House, certain remarks were made by an
hon. Member of that House which not only
affect the individual Members of the Senate
-I think individual Members of the Senate
can, as far as they are concerned take care
of themselves-but went a good deal further
than that, because when discourteous and
insulting remarks are used by a Member of
the Lower House it not only affects the
officials but it affects the whole authority
and prerogative, as it were, of the Senate.
I think, therefore, the time has come to
make some remonstrance at any rate, and
as the hon. Member is an old Parliamentarian who really ought to have known the
constitutional procedure better, I am
astonished at him. But after all, when he
makes the remarks he did, he is in fact
really asking for the abolition of this House,
and, I think if it came to a question of
brains and common sense, it might be better
for the country to have the abolition not of
this House, but the abolition of the other
House, and to conduct the business here.
I will not detain you further. I am sure
you will give us a satisfactory answer to
this question, and that it will be taken
notice of in the proper manner.
tion I wish to ask is:

(Traffic and Regulation) Bill, without any

Members of the

House resumed.
495 Prorogation of Parliament.

THE SENATE
King's Speech.
Prorogation of Parliament.
His Grace the Governor, being seated on
the Throne, commanded the Gentleman
Usher of the Black Rod to let the Commons
know, "It is his Grace's pleasure that they
attend him in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar, with their
Speaker, his Grace delivered the following
Speech from the Throne:-
KING'S SPEECH.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons:
The unemployment situation continues
to be the most important problem confronting Northern Ireland, and it is a
matter for regret that the improvement in
trade which was apparent in the early
months of this year has received a serious
set-back as a result of the unhappy dispute
in the Coal Mining Industry in Great
Britain. Notwithstanding, however, the
great difficulties which our industries have
experienced in carrying on their business
during this critical period, it is encouraging to note that 30,000 persons have been
placed in employment through the agency
of the Employment Exchanges since the
beginning of the year, or 6,000 more than
during the corresponding period of last
year.
I am glad to observe that since the Session
opened 86 schemes of public utility promoted by local authorities have been
approved as unemployment relief schemes
for Government grants, involving a total
expenditure of £380,000, and that the
Government has taken power to extend its
assistance to the carrying out of works of
capital construction by increasing the
limit of capital which may be guaranteed
under the Loans Guarantee Acts to
£9,000,000, and extending the operation of
these Acts to the 31st March, 1928. This is
proving of great benefit to the Shipbuilding
Industry and has also been instrumental
in the establishment of an Artificial Silk
Factory and the re-opening of certain
Woollen Mills.
I also note with satisfaction that contracts
have recently been placed in Belfast for
several new vessels. This will give muchneeded employment to those associated with
an industry which has suffered seriously
from the protracted general depression.
Some slight improvement during the past
few months has also been apparent in the
linen trade.
I am gratified that legislative effect has
been given to the arrangements reached
with the British Government in regard
to the financial position of the Unemploy
ment Insurance Fund and that the helpfulness of the Widows', Orphans' and Old
Age Contributory Pensions Scheme continues to extend; the number of persons in
Northern Ireland now enjoying benefits
totalling 16,000.
The passing of the Motor Vehicles
(Traffic and Regulation) Act will, I hope,
result in the prevention of regrettable
accidents involving loss of life. I sincerely
trust that all sections of the public, including pedestrians themselves, will energetically second the efforts of the authorities in
endeavouring to secure safety on our public
highways.
It was with satisfaction that I learnt of
the proposal of the Government to continue
the Housing Acts for another twelve
months, and I hope that during that period
the joint efforts of local authorities and of
private enterprise, with the financial assistance accorded by the Government, will have
the effect of alleviating in a marked degree
the hardship and inconvenience so long
endured by the working classes owing to
the shortage of houses.
The establishment of an Inn of Court
for Northern Ireland and the recognition
by my Government of the necessity of such
an Institution by giving financial assistance
towards its educational work will give
widespread satisfaction.
Members of the House of Commons:
I thank you for the provision of supplies
for the public services.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons:
I am glad to learn that the arrangements
in connection with the transfer of certain
services to Northern Ireland to which I
referred in my Speech at the opening of
the Session and which were embodied in
the Administrative Provisions Act
(Northern Ireland), 1926, have proceeded
smoothly since the appointed day for the
transfer.
It is a source of great satisfaction to
know that Northern Ireland continues to
be free from abnormal crime and that law
and order have been effectively maintained.
I pray that the Blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your labours.
Then the Proclamation Proroguing the
present Parliament of Northern Ireland was
read by the Speaker as followeth :-
Proclamation.
End of Session.
30 NOVEMBER 1926
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION
For Proroguing the present Parliament of
Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by Section eleven
of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, as
modified by and in pursuance of certain
enactments amending the said Act, that the
Governor of Northern Ireland shall in His
Majesty's name summon, prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of Northern Ireland.
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that this
present Parliament of Northern Ireland
should be prorogued to the eighth day of
March, One Thousand Nine Hundred and
Twenty-seven.
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD,
DUKE OF ABERCORN, Governor of Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation do publish
and declare His Majesty's Pleasure that the
said Parliament be, as from the reading of
this Proclamation in the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, prorogued to Tuesday,
the eighth day of March, One Thousand
Nine Hundred and Twenty-seven.
AND I do hereby further declare His
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to be that
the said Parliament shall on the said eighth
day of March, One Thousand Nine Hundred
and Twenty-seven, assemble and be holden for
the dispatch of divers urgent and important
affairs, and the Senators and Members of the
House of Commons of Northern Ireland are
hereby required to give their attendance
accordingly.
Given at Government House, Hillsborough,
this twenty-ninth day of November, One
Thousand Nine Hundred and Twenty-six.
Signed by his own hand,
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
His Grace, the Governor, then retired.
End of the Second Session (opened 9th
March, 1926) of the Second Parliament of
Northern Ireland, in the Seventeenth year
of the Reign of His Majesty King George
the Fifth.
