Tuesday, 9th March, 1926.
The Second Session of the Second Parliament of Northern Ireland, appointed to
meet 9th March, 1926, in the sixteenth year
in the reign of King George V, was opened
by the Governor (His Grace the Duke of
Abercorn, K.P.) in person.
The House met at Twelve of the clock,
Mr. SPEAKER (Most hon. the Marquess of
Dufferin and Ava, D.SO., D.L.) in the Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace the Governor being seated on
the Throne, commanded the Gentleman
Usher of the Black Rod to let the Commons
know "It is His Grace's pleasure that they
attend him immediately in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with their
Speaker, His Gracc delivered the following
Speech from the Throne, and then retired :-
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons:
I am deeply gratified at the satisfactory
arrangement reached with the British
Government in regard to the future of the
Unemployment Insurance Fund. Following
upon the procedure in the Imperial Parliament a Bill will be laid before you to give
full effect to the agreement at an early
date.
It is a matter of profound regret that I
am not yet able to comment more favourably on the question of Unemployment,
though I am glad to note that there have
recently appeared some slight indications
of an improvement in the volume of work
generally, and I learn with pleasure that
advantage continues to be taken of facilities
afforded under the Loans Guarantee Acts.
Every practicable step will be adopted to
assist industry by means of these Acts and
by Grants to Local Authorities and Public
Bodies in necessitous areas to carryout works
of permanent public utility. The measures
which my Government are taking in cooperation with the trade to stimulate a
demand for linen goods will necessarily
require time to achieve their full object.
Bills will be submitted to you dealing
with National Health and Unemployment
Insurance and for the purpose of securing
authority to extend for a further period
of one year the provisions of the Loans
Guarantee Acts.
It is gratifying to record that the great
measure of social reform-the Widows',
Orphans' and Old Age Contributory
Pensions Act-which was passed last
Session, has been brought into operation
so speedily and smoothly, and to learn
that no fewer than 3,350 widows and 8,300
dependent children and orphans are
already receiving benefits under the Act.
As it was not found possible to completc
the necessary arrangements to admit of
Measures for the purpose of amending the
provisions of the existing Juries Acts, and
the improvement of Mining facilities being
dealt with during last Session, I trust that
King's Speech.
Papers Presented.
THE SENATE
the Bills which are now to be laid before
you will receive your earnest consideration.
A Measure for the Preservation of
Ancient Monuments was duly introduced
in the Senate last Session and circulated.
A Bill relating to this matter will be laid
before you and proceeded with this
Session.
Proposals will be laid before you to
secure the more effective control of motor
traffic on public roads in order to minimise
the risks of accident to persons and property-particular regard being had to those
vehicles carrying passengers for hire...
Your assent will be invited to the
extension for another year of the Housing
Acts which have proved so useful in encouraging the activities of Local Authorities and private builders in the erection of
houses for the working classes.
Legislation will be submitted to you
dealing with minor difficulties which have
arisen in the interpretation and administration of the Intoxicating Liquor Act,
and Measures for the provision of better
accommodation for Juvenile Offenders and
for the Protection of Animal and Bird Life
will be laid before you.
I am glad to observe that the Marketing
of Eggs Act, which came into operation at
the beginning of last year, has already had
a beneficial effect on this important industry. Some revision is necessary, and
accordingly appropriate legislation will be
introduced during the Session.
It is gratifying to learn that the results
which have attended the first Season's work
of the Society formed last year to promote
the cultivation of pedigree flax seed
throughout the Empire, towards which you
have afforded and still continue to afford
financial assistance, are encouraging, and
that a sufficient stock of seed has now been
accumulated in Somersetshire to permit of
the allocation of appreciable guaranteed
quantities to Northern Ireland, Kenya,
Canada, and other parts of the Empire.
There are also numerous and important
problems relating to animal nutrition
which await solution. Having regard. to
the special conditions which prevail in
Northern Ireland, the time has arrived
when the investigation of such problems
should be undertaken, and with this object
in view you will be asked to make provision for the establishment of a Research
Experimental Farm, which will be conducted by a Board of Trustees.
Other Bills will be laid before you dealing with the following matters:-
The finance of Teachers' Pensions and
the increase of low-rated pensions of
certain state and local authorities'
pensioners.
Members of the House of Commons:
Estimates for the Public Services will be
laid before you in due course. Every
effort is being made by my Ministers to
reduce expenditure to the minimum
amount consistent with efficiency.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons:
Arising out of the agreement made last
December between the Governments of
Great Britain, the Irish Free State and
Northern Ireland, certain services the control of which remained in the hands of the
Imperial Authorities, will fall shortly to
be administered by the Government of
Northern Ireland. In consequence of this
you will be asked to pass a measure for
the purpose of assigning these functions to
the appropriate departments and making
certain consequential alterations in the
law.
I pray that Almighty God may
bestow His abundant blessing upon your
deliberations.
Then His Grace was pleased to retire, and
the Commons withdrew.
The House adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed.
Papers Presented.
Ministry of Commerce.
Report by Ministry of Commerce of proceedings under the General Pier and Harbour
Act, 1861 (24 and 25 Vict., c. 45) and the
General Pier and Harbour Act, 1861, Amendment Act (25 Vict., c. 19).
Ministry of Finance.
The Queen's University of Belfast. Abstract of Accounts for year ended 31st July,
1925.
Return by the Official Assignee of the
Belfast Local Bankruptcy Court for the year
ended 31st December, 1925.
Ministry of Home Affairs.
Road Fund (Northern Ireland) Account for
the year ended 31st March, 1925.
Annual Report for the year 1925 of the
Retreat established in Northern Ireland
under the Inebriates Acts, 1879-1899.
Annual Report of the Inspectors of
Lunatics, Northern Ireland, for the year
1924.
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
9 MARCH 1926
Ministry of Education.
Regulations governing the certification,
salaries and conditions of employment of
teachers in Preparatory, Intermediate and
Secondary Schools.
Presented (pursuant to Act) and ordered to
lie on the Table.
Mr. SPEAKER having read the King's
Speech,
Address in Reply to His Majesty's
Most Gracious Speech.
Viscount BANGOR (in morning dress): I
beg to move: That a humble Address be
presented to his Grace in the following
words:-
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to your Grace for
the Speech which your Grace has addressed
to both Houses of Parliament.
I am very sensible of the honour which has
been conferred on me when I was invited to
move the Address to the Throne, and I am
very conscious of my inability to deal adequately with the important and technical
matters contained in the Gracious Speech.
I will, therefore, appeal to right hon. and
hon. Members of this House to deal lightly
with my shortcomings, and to extend to me
the indulgence which is invariably given to
the Mover of the Address.
The first subject to which attention is
drawn in the Gracious Speech is that of the
prevailing unemployment.
I understand,
however, that there is a slight improvement
in this respect and that industry has been
materially assisted by means of the Loan
Guarantee Acts. There appears to be a good
deal of useful work through the country,
which I should imagine could be allotted to
the unemployed in the way of drainage,
repairs to roads, especially on the coast where
damage has occurred by the action of the
sea, and other similar services. I can only
hope that the Government will not relax any
effort in dealing with this much vexed
question.
a
It is most reassuring to look upon
pleasanter picture, and I allude to the
Widows', Orphans', and Old Age Contributory Pensions Act which was passed last
session. It is an admirable measure and the
fruits of it are already apparent in a great
number of cases.
The gracious Speech deals with a bill for
the Preservation of Ancient Monuments.
The country is full of them, especially a great
number of those fine old castles built for
defensive purposes by Sir John de Courcy.
I believe about the time of King Henry II.
Some of these castles are in a very shaky
state, and it seems a thousand pities that
these fine old monuments of a bygone age
should be allowed to fall into a state of
dilapidation and decay. Here is some work
which might possibly be suitable for the unemployed.
Allusion is made to the Agreement made
last December between the Governments of
Great Britain, the Irish Free State and
Northern Ireland, and to certain services now
under the Imperial authorities which will be
administered shortly by the Government of
Northern Ireland. I cannot leave this subject without saying how much I consider that
all of us in the Six Counties are indebted to
the Prime Minister (Sir James Craig) for
his ability, courage, and statesmanship in
preserving our Border intact. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) Those of us who do
not live near the Border can hardly realise,
I should imagine, the intense relief it must
have been to those upon the frontier when it
was settled that there was to be no deviation
from the boundary line established by the
Act of 1920.
The measures dealing with the more effective control of motor traffic are of the
highest importance, and I should say of the
utmost urgency. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) The number of motor accidents, recorded in the newspapers, and these are of
daily occurrence, is little short of appalling,
and a very large percentage of these mishaps
is due to preventable causes. There are two
main causes of accidents one careless
driving, and the other pedestrians not looking the right direction when they cross the
road. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
With regard to the first cause, I can
honestly say that a great number of really
bad and reckless drivers are to be met every
day upon the road and they constitute a very
serious danger to life and property. It is
perfectly true that it is a horrible thing to
meet these people on the road because they
are a very great danger to the community.
Anybody can receive a motor licence provided
he is of the requisite age and has got the
sum of 5s. in his pocket. I believe that a
licence was once issued to a blind man.
think there would be very much fewer accidents on the road if drivers had to undergo
a qualifying test before they were allowed
to hold a licence. I think the police in the
city are grappling with the traffic question
very well. It is a difficult problem, especially
when there is such a variety of vehicles and
all moving at a different speed.
I
The second cause of accidents is very
noticeable in Belfast where the majority of
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Bangor]
foot passengers crossing the streets never look
where they are going. I drive about a good
deal in Belfast and whenever I return home
I am always very thankful that I have not
run over anybody. I would like to say a
word about the question of motor vehicles
used in carrying passengers for hire. When
I allude to that class of vehicle I mean the
motor 'buses and chars-a-bancs that one sees
in great numbers on the roads. There is no
doubt whatever that drivers of these vehicles
very often are guilty of exceeding the speed
limit. I was told a day or two ago that one
of these motor 'buses covered the distance
between Downpatrick and Belfast in forty
minutes. Now I call that a most outrageous
speed and a very great danger to the community. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I
also heard that one of these drivers when
he was brought up for exceeding the speed
limit gave as his excuse that the engine in
his car was such a powerful one he could
not go slower than twenty miles an hour.
Therefore, I say that it is very necessary that
we should have some immediate legislation
about these vehicles. They occupy an enormous amount of the lateral space of the road.
It is difficult to get by them as I know from
experience. There are drivers who have a
most rooted disinclination to leave the middle
of the road.
I notice that there will be legislation in
connection with the erection of houses
for the working classes, the administration of
the Intoxicating Liquor Act and the provision of better accommodation for juvenile
offenders, and I am also glad to see that
measures for the protection of animal and
bird life will be brought forward. A great
many wild fowl are killed in the month of
July, and I feel certain that a good deal of
this could be stopped if only the police were
given fuller powers to apprehend persons
found in pursuit of game during the close
season.
I am glad to see that mention has been
made of an important industry in Ulster,
that of egg production. The Ministry of
Agriculture has done a great deal to encourage poultry farmers to produce the best
breeds of laying fowls and to ensure the
clean marketing of their eggs.
Finally, I see that the establishment of a
Research Experimental Farm is in contemplation for the purpose of dealing with problems connected with animal nutrition. This
should be of great assistance to farmers to
show them the most approved and up to date
methods of feeding. Ulster is essentially an
agricultural country, and it is all-important
to keep abreast of the times and not to be
left behind or out-distanced in the race by
the agriculturists of Continental nations.
I beg to move.
Sir WILLIAM TURNER (in morning
dress): I esteem it a high honour to have
been asked to second this address of thanks
to His Grace the Governor, which has been
so ably proposed by the noble lord, Viscount
Bangor. I do not take the compliment to
myself personally, rather do I regard it as a
recognition of the office which I hold as Lord
Mayor of the City of Belfast, and I am quite
sure that the citizens will appreciate the
honour thus done me.
I should like at the outset to give expression to our loyalty and devotion to His
Majesty the King. We, in Ulster, have
always been noted for our attachment to the
Crown and Constitution. Our people are
amongst the most loyal and patriotic in the
whole British Empire.
I am certain I am voicing the feelings of
all the right hon. and hon. Members of this
Chamber when I say that we deeply regret
the indisposition of her Grace the Duchess
of Abercorn, whose charm of manner, kindly
nature and gracious actions have won all our
hearts. We are, however, delighted to learn
that the Duchess is on the way to recovery,
and sincerely hope that before long she will
soon be about again.
We miss from our assembly to-day the
presence of the Marquess of Londonderry.
It was with regret we heard of his Lordship's
resignation from the leadership of this
Chamber and the position of Minister of
Education, but we know the reasons which
induced him to take that step. His great
commercial interests across the water require
closer attention, and already his influence in
the coal crisis is having a distinct effect.
We cordially welcome his successor, Lord
Charlemont. We know the keen practical
interest which the noble lord takes in industrial enterprises, and we trust that as
chairman of the Royal Commission on the
development of the natural and industrial
resources of Northern Ireland, he will be
able to suggest schemes and make recommendations which will be the means not only of
reviving and expanding old industries but
opening up new ones. Under ordinary circumstances his Lordship would have
difficult task in following Lord Londonderry,
but his lordship must feel comfort in the fact
that the spadework has been done by his
predecessor and that it now only falls to his
lot to see that the Act which has revolutionised our educational system is justly and
impartially administered.
a
This is the beginning of the second Session
of our second Parliament and it is gratifying
to know that we meet in an atmosphere of
peace and goodwill. I well remember the
gracious words spoken by His Majesty the
King when he visited Belfast in June, 1921,
to open our Northern Parliament. He said:-
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
9 MARCH 1926
"I pray that my coming to Ireland may
prove to be the first step towards an end
of strife amongst her people whatever their
race or creed. In that hope I appeal to all
Irishmen to pause, to stretch out the hand
of forbearance and conciliation, to forgive
and to forget and to join in making for the
land which they love a new era of peace,
contentment and goodwill."
That prayer of His Majesty has been
wonderfully answered, and perhaps never
before has such a feeling of friendliness and
forbearance existed between North and
South. We freely recognise each other's
difficulties and while our ideals and ambitions may be different, we acknowledge that
there are many matters on which we can
mutually co-operate for the welfare and
prosperity of the whole country.
Of course, the settlement of the Boundary
dispute is largely responsible for that
happier and brighter state of affairs, and in
this connection I cannot refrain from congratulating the Prime Minister (Sir James
Craig) who throughout the negotiations
proved himself to be a most prudent and farsighted statesman. Indeed, it was mainly
due to his diplomacy, firmness and courage
that Ulster's attitude
triumphantly
vindicated, and the best way in which we
can repay his services is by placing in him
our unqualified trust and confidence.
was
After the admirable speech of the noble
Viscount covering so many subjects it is not
necessary for me to refer in detail to the
gracious Speech from the Throne, but there
are one or two things to which 1 should like
to draw attention. I am sure we are all
pleased with the arrangements come
between our Government and the British
Government in regard to the Unemployment
Insurance Fund, ensuring that the workers
in Northern Ireland shall obtain the same
benefits as those of Great Britain.
to
Then through the operation of the Loans
Guarantee Acts, much is being done to relieve unemployment. For instance, in the
shipbuilding industry advantage has been
taken of those Acts and work provided for
some 9,000 men. The provisions of the Acts
are to be extended for another year and I
trust they will be widely availed of.
Our linen trade is at a low ebb, due largely
to intensified foreign competition, diminished
demand for finished goods and world-trade
depression. Here again, our Government has
come to the rescue, and by means of grants
towards improving the strains of flaxseed,
more scientific methods of production and
advertising the advantages of linen goods
have afforded practical and material assistance.
Agriculture, which is perhaps the chief
industry in Northern Ireland, has not been
forgotten. Methods of securing increased
production have been fostered and the farmers have been helped in many ways.
It is gratifying to note from the gracious
Speech that in every direction the Government is doing, and will continue to do, all
it can to aid the unemployed. By its cooperation with the local authorities works of
public utility involving a total cost of about
£4,200,000 have been put in hand and as a
result employment for some 22,000 men has
been found at various periods. I mention
these figures to show that no effort is being
spared to lift the plague of unemployment.
Reference is made in the gracious Speech
to the Widows', Orphans' and Old Age Contributory Pensions Act. I regard that
measure as one of the best pieces of legislation passed by the Government. It is the
coping stone of the arch of social insurance
and already has brought comfort and happiness to many homes. Our Government deserve great credit for their magnificent work
in this respect.
I notice that we are to have an Act dealing
with the jury system. This is a matter that
was to have been settled last vear but it was
impossible to complete the necessary arrangements. I hope nothing will occur this
Session to cause delay. We all recognise that
the present jury method entails hardship for
many people, and if anything can be done to
grant relief and put the system on a more
equitable basis, it will be heartily welcomed.
Then proposals are to be laid before us for
the better control of motor traffic. Now that
this mode of conveyance has become SO
popular it is our duty to do everything possible to safeguard the public. The risk of
accidents since the motor 'buses have become
so numerous has considerably increased, and
the traffic must be properly regulated.
I am sure it is with pleasure we note from
the gracious Speech that the Housing Acts
are to be continued for another year. There
is still a great shortage of houses, and if
anything can be done to encourage building,
either by the local authorities or by private
enterprise, it will be for the benefit of the
community.
It is stated that the Intoxicating Liquor
Act requires slight amendment. That is a
thorny problem, but I hope the proposed
alterations will be of such a nature that the
objections, if any, will be slight.
Many other important topics are mentioned
in the gracious Speech, but I will not trespass
further on your time. I am quite certain
that the varicus matters will be carefully
considered and dealt with when they come
before this Chamber.
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Sir William Turner]
I cannot conclude without congratulating
the Prime Minister on the skilful way in
which he has piloted the Government from
its inception, and if what he has already
accomplished is a foretaste of what we may
expect, then we shall have no reason to complain at the end of the Session. I beg to
second.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of the
House): I feel sure that all hon. Members of
this House are in agreement with what the
Lord Mayor of Belfast has just said, and that
they will join with me in offering our respectful sympathy to his Grace the Governor in
the anxious time through which he has been
passing. I am sure all will join with me in
congratulating him on the progress which
her Grace the Duchess is making towards
recovery, and I know that I am voicing the
feeling of the whole Province when I express
the hope that that good progress may be
maintained.
It is with a feeling of the greatest diffidence that I address this House for the first
time as its Leader. That diffidence is not
due altogether to a sense of unfamiliarity
with my task, for I am confident that hon.
Members will freely grant in my own case
the indulgence and sympathy which they are
wont to bestow upon one of their number who
plays for the first time a difficult part in
their deliberations. But when I reflect upon
the attributes of Lord Londonderry, in whose
place I stand here to-day, I cannot but feel
that I have indeed inherited the giant's robe
without having the necessary stature to wear
it properly. It would be superfluous for me
to speak at length upon his virtues to a
House which for almost five years has enjoyed
full experience of him as guide and friend,
and which is aware of the great debt which
Northern Ireland owes and always must owe
to him. It will be sufficient to observe that
his recent decision to relinquish office here
serves only to throw into greater light that
unfailing sense of public duty with which
Lord Londonderry has ever been animated.
He has not left us until he was satisfied of
the political well-being of this Province, and
it is but typical of him that he should now
devote himself to a problem which in complexity and magnitude is, perhaps, no less
great than that which he has helped to bring
to a safe issue in this country. I can pay
him no greater compliment than to say that
it will always be my endeavour so long as
I stand in this place to maintain the high
standard which has been set by my illustrious
predecessor, and my efforts to maintain that
standard will be lightened, I hope, by the
ready sympathy and assistance which will
be accorded to me by all Members of this
House. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
It is my privilege to congratulate the mover
and seconder upon the manner in which they
have accomplished their task. The noble
Viscount who moved the Address is an
original Member, and as such is well known
to this House, but we have not hitherto often
enjoyed the advantage of listening to his
speeches. That is a disability which I myself
must also share with Lord Bangor, though I
cannot claim, like him, the advantage of
being one of the first Members of the Senate.
I am sure that I am voicing the views of
all hon. Members when I say that I hope he
will often hereafter take an opportunity of
assisting us in our debates. We are not
ignorant of the great services which he
rendered to his country in time of war, services for which he received appropriate
honour at the hands of his King. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear). In these less spectacular days of peace he has consistently
taken an active part in the public life of our
Province, and it is to me doubly gratifying
that the important committee which has
charge of education in the great county of
Down should have him as its chairman.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
It is not necessary for me to direct attention to the merits of the seconder, whose
speech was in every way worthy of one who
is the first citizen in this great town. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) Sir William Turner
has now entered upon his fourth successive
year of office as head of the City Council.
His activities for the civic good are manifold
and widespread, and we rejoice to know that
his labours have been fittingly recognised and
that his name has recently been inscribed
upon the roll of the Freemen of the City of
Belfast. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
It is a great source of strength to this
House that we can command at all times the
services of the first citizens of our two cities
of Belfast and Londonderry; and we are to be
congratulated upon the personalities of those
who have hitherto sat with us in their
capacity as Lord Mayor of Belfast or Mayor
of Londonderry. I am sure the House will
agree with me when I say that fortunate
though we have been in this respect, we have
never been more fortunate than to-day.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
The legislation foreshadowed in the gracious
Speech to which we have had the honour
of listening is varied, and many aspects of
the social and public life of Northern Ireland
will be affected. Some of the Bills which will
be laid before you will be of an amending
nature, and this House will agree with me
that it is proper that legislative effect should
be given at the earliest possible moment to
any changes which the actual working of an
Act has shown to be desirable.
Amongst those services which have hitherto
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
9 MARCH 1926
own
been under the control of the Imperial
authorities, and which will shortly be transferred to our
Government, is the
administration of our sea and inland fishing
industries. The importance of these industries is appreciated, as are also the many
and complex difficulties with which they are
confronted, and I can assure hon. Members
that the interests of the fishermen of
Northern Ireland will in no way suffer by
the change in administration which takes
place next month.
The noble Viscount who moved the Address
to the gracious Speech from the Throne
rightly directed attention to the importance
of the Bill which you will be asked to consider in connection with the more effective
control of the motor traffic. The development of motor traffic in Northern Ireland
during the past few years has been remarkable, not only in the urban areas but also in
the rural districts, and it is essential that
legislation should be introduced which may
help to solve some of the many new problems
which that development has created. Lord
Bangor told us that he has heard of a licence
to drive a motor car being granted to a blind
man. I can assure him that provision has
been made in the Bill which will come before
us to prevent the possibility of such an
occurrence in the future. At the same time
I cannot but feel that the advertisement
which has been given to the activities of
this person may improve the passenger traffic
of the railways that serve the area in which
he operates.
a
The House will agree with me that it is
a matter of profound regret that Northern
Ireland is still suffering from severe industrial depression and consequent unemployment. The latest figures show that on 1st
March there were over 61,500 persons unemployed, including workers employed on
systematic short-time basis. I am glad to
say, however, that since the beginning of the
year there has been a decrease of nearly
3,000 in the number of those who cannot find
work. The Government has encouraged, by
all the means at its disposal, the provision of
work for those who are unemployed, and, in
co-operation with local authorities and other
public bodies, relief schemes to a total estimated value of over £4,000,000 have been promoted. 2,062 men are engaged on the schemes
at present in operation, apart from those
who indirectly benefit from the additional
work occasioned by the supply of materials
and transport. I may say that the assistance
rendered by the Belfast Corporation in the
promotion of relief schemes for the unemployed in the city is greatly appreciated by
the Government.
Mention has been made in the gracious
Speech that under the Widows', Orphans' and
Old Age Contributory Pensions Act, passed
last Session, about 3,350 widows and 8,300
dependent children have been benefited.
Claims are still being received in considerable
numbers and there is no doubt that these
figures will be largely increased in the future,
although the estimate furnished by the
actuary may prove to have been slightly
exaggerated. It is impossible for me to put
into words the relief and comfort which must
have been brought, even during the short
period since the Act came into force, to those
homes where previously distress and suffering
existed.
The activities of the Ministry of Agriculture
continue to be devoted to the improvement of
the output of our agricultural resources. At
the moment a census is being taken of the
agricultural production of Northern Ireland;
and the information obtained regarding the
various branches of crop and animal
husbandry will be of the utmost value in
assisting the formulation of agricultural
policy.
Owing to recent unfavourable seasons, many
farmers incurred serious losses of live stock.
To meet the situation, the Ministry designed
a scheme of loans at a low rate of interest to
enable farmers, more particularly in the
poorer districts, to replace their losses. Under
the scheme, which was brought into operation
last summer, a total sum of £46,000 has been
advanced to necessitous farmers.
You will be interested to learn that the Act
regulating the marketing of eggs has already
conferred very material benefit on the farmers
of Northern Ireland, and the Ministry hopes
to consider, at a future date, the question
of regulating the marketing of potatoes by a
similar measure.
a
Turning to the linen industry, I am glad
to note that there has been
welcome
improvement since the House rose, unemployment having been reduced by eight per cent.
in January as compared with December, and
by nearly thirteen per cent, as compared with
last July. Moreover, exports from the United
Kingdom during the first month of the year
were higher than during any month since
April last. More promising reports are also
being received from America-the industry's
greatest overseas market-as to the orders
which are being placed for linen goods, and it
is hoped that the merits of Irish linen will
once more assert themselves and resume their
former importance in that country.
The outlook in shipbuilding continues
serious, the basic fact being that there are
more ships and fewer cargoes than before the
war. Freights are down to pre-war level, but
the cost of running ships has practically
doubled itself since 1914. In spite of this,
however, it is encouraging to find that the
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
tonnage laid up in United Kingdom ports
was less at the end of last year than at the
end of 1924. It is a matter of some satisfaction that our local shipyards are able to
secure more than their share of any orders
for new vessels that are being placed, and it
is very gratifying to learn of the
which has attended their efforts in the
modern development of motor vessels.
success
In the realm of Local Government, administration is proceeding normally pending the
Report of the Local Government Commission.
There has been one important change,
however, regulations having been made
prescribing the labelling and composition of
dried and condensed milk-an important
matter for the health of the children and the
poorer classes who use tinned milk largely
in place of fresh milk.
The machinery for bringing into operation
the Pharmacy and Poisons Act (Northern
Ireland), 1925, has been set up. A Council
of the Pharmaceutical Society has been appointed and is at present engaged in making
ali arrangements for holding the necessary
examinations under the Act.
The Government scheme for subsidising
the building of houses has proved a decided
success, and Parliament will be asked this
year to extend for a further year the system
of granting subsidy. For the period, from
1st March, 1924, to the present date, 3,600
houses have been approved for erection by
private builders, and of these over 2,000 have
already been completed. The corresponding
figures in regard to houses being erected by
local authorities are 1,396 and 703 respectively.
I come now to my own Ministry, the Ministry of Education, the responsibility of which
I have assumed in succession to Lord Londonderry. I have already spoken of my task in
succeeding the noble Marquess in the performance of the duties of Leader of the
Senate. I can only say that my task in
following him as Minister of Education is
no less severe. I think I should say at once,
and I am sure that this House and the
country outside will be glad to know, that
the change of Ministers means no change of
policy, and that it is my intention to follow
my predecessor in allowing the machinery
set up by the Education Act of 1923 to develop
and to gain strength without pressing forward to the perfection of our educational
system faster than we can fully use its advantages or before we can afford them. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) I am happy to be
able to say that at the close of the present
financial year, which will mark the end of
the second year of the full enjoyment of their
powers, the educational authorities in the
cities of Belfast and Londonderry, and, in
particular, in the city of Belfast, where the
problem is so vast and so urgent, will have
achieved a definite and substantial advance
upon the line of progress before them-a long
and difficult march, which will call for the
exercise of patience and perseverance. The
same date will mark the close of the first
year of complete administration by the local
education authorities in the counties. My
predecessor, Lord Londonderry, was called
upon over and over again to defend these
new local authorities in the counties, and he
never for one moment allowed his confidence
in their future to be shaken. We are only
at the beginning, but I think we may already
say that Lord Londonderry's trust was not
misplaced and that we may look forward
most hopefully to the useful exercise of their
functions by the education authorities in
every part of the Province.
There is no one here to-day, I am sure, who
does not rejoice whole-heartedly at the knowledge that the difficult question of the
Boundary between Northern Ireland and the
Irish Free State has now been satisfactorily
settled. It is the dearest wish of all Irishmen who love their country that North and
South should be able to go their ways without
rancour or suspicion, and the removal of
what for the past few years has constituted a
source of deep anxiety and disquietude will
do much towards promoting that feeling
of friendliness which is essential to the wellbeing of our two communities.
The
prosperity of Southern Ireland is closely
related to our own, and there are many
matters of vital importance to both countries
which demand co-operation. This co-operation is now assured by the recent Agreement,
which will always be observed by Northern
Ireland both in the letter and in the spirit
in which it was drawn up.
I heartily endorse every word which fell
from the seconder to the Address (Sir Wm.
Turner) with reference to the Prime Minister.
Since the establishment of our Government
we have passed through troublous times, and
a man was needed in whom should be
combined the quality of statesmanship, the
virtue of unflinching honesty, and courage as
well. "The time brings forth the man," and
in Sir James Craig we have a Prime Minister
to whom is due, before all others, the fact
that Northern Ireland has successfully
surmounted the many difficulties with which
it has been faced. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear).
Many of you listened with pleasure to the
able speech from the distinguished statesman
who visited Belfast last
week. Mr.
Churchill spoke a timely word of warning
against the unpatriotic depreciation of the
thoroughly sound financial position of Great
Britain, and showed that a country which
was steadily reducing its capital liabilities
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
9 MARCH 1926
could not in any sense be said to be heading
towards bankruptcy. He also gave a clear
indication that trade was steadily improving, "basing himself," as he said, "on the
consolidation of peace in Europe, coupled
with the evidently growing power of the
League of Nations, the favourable harvests
throughout the world, and the successful
achievement of the gold standard in Great
Britain." It is heartening to know that
there is a spirit of conciliation and comradeship abroad in the world, and that the
gloomy days of depression are gradually
passing, to be succeeded, we hope, by a
period of prosperity in which Northern Ireland, in common with other parts of the
Empire, may surely be trusted to take her
full share. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Mr. DORMAN: I wish in the first place
to congratulate the noble Viscount and the
right hon. the Lord Mayor on the very able
manner in which they respectively proposed
and seconded the Address. I also wish to
congratulate the noble Viscount, the Leader
of the House. One would not like to introduce any jarring note on an occasion such
as this, but as was said by an eminent Member in another place this morning, King's
Speeches are drawn up to convey the minimum amount of information and the minimum amount of critical matter to the House
at the first meeting called for their
consideration.
I think that there are several things mentioned in the Speech well worthy of serious
consideration, and having that in view I
propose at the close of my remarks to move
as an Amendment to the Address the addition of the following words:
But regret that the Government do not
propose to make arrangements for the provision of work or maintenance for those
who are able and willing to work, or failing
such provision for keeping intact the homes
of those who are being deprived of their
unemployment benefits; and further that
the Government have not proposed any
legislation to amend the Rent and Mortgage Interest Restrictions Act, which fails
to prevent extensive profiteering on the reletting of uncontrolled houses.
At present, as the Leader of the House has
told us, there are over 61,000 men, women
and youths upon the live register in Northern Ireland, mainly due to the international
relationships in regard to trade at the close
of the war-due to the fact also that the
staple industries in the area over which the
Government have jurisdiction had been mainly
carrying on an international trade prior to
the war. I believe it is because our statesmen did not see the inevitable complications
that would arise in international trade due
to the reparations levied upon our late
enemy, Germany, that the present impasse
exists in Northern Ireland and Great Britain
to-day. One of our staple industries in
Northern Ireland is the shipbuilding industry. I remember well while working in the
shipyard some eight years ago predicting to
my fellow workers there that the
close of the peace negotiations and
the imposition of reparations in the
way of shipping to Great Britain as a
result of the war would inevitably bring
about a worse state of affairs in Northern
Ireland than in any other part of Great
Britain.
The Government no doubt will say they are
doing all they possibly can in this matter.
They profess to move on parallel lines with
Great Britain on all these matters. We find,
however, that the authorities in Northern
Ireland are not on parallel lines with
Great Britain SO far as unemployment
is concerned. I know several instances in
Belfast where men have been deprived of
their unemployment benefit. Young men with
wives and a few children depending on them
have no unemployment benefit to succour
them and keep their homes together. They
have made application to the Belfast Board
of Guardians for relief and have been offered
the alternative of breaking up their homes
and entering the workhouse, thereby stigmatising their offspring as paupers in the years
to come.
I think I am right in saying that men who
are to-day being turned off from the right to
extended benefit in Great Britain-those who
have wives and one or two children-are
given in outdoor relief a weekly sum which
exceeds that which they would have been
entitled to had they continued to be on the
list of unemployed. In Belfast, as I have
already said, if an instance of that kind
arises a man has no option but to relinquish
his little property in the form of furniture
in order to justify his claim for destitution
and thereby become entitled to enter the
workhouse as an inmate.
I rejoice to see that the Government takes
credit on itself for succouring industry in
the form of loans. In this particular part of
Ireland we continually hear the changes
rung and the glories of private enterprise
pronounced. Even in the gracious Speech
from the Throne I am surprised to find that
in such an important matter as the standard
of flax the Government have found it
necessary to set up a socialistic scheme for
the purpose of providing the flax growers
with pure seed.
With regard to the Rent Restrictions Act,
I would like to inform the House that at
the present time what was predicted by a
small group in another place when the Rent
Restriction Act was before Parliament in the
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
last Session, is going to take place in Belfast.
The working classes who are clamouring for
homes are being asked to pay from 100 to
150 per cent. more rent for houses that are
becoming decontrolled owing to the fact that
that Act was placed on the Statute book last
year. There is before my mind one particular
house which was recently vacated and the
rent of which was about 10s per week. The
landlord is now asking a minimum rent of
25s a week before he will let that house for
occupation. It is a working-class house in
a distinctly working-class neighbourhood.
I think the Government ought to take into
consideration the advisability of taking some
steps to restrain this profiteering on the
homes of the poorer people. Hundreds of
these cases are occurring in Belfast. In
consequence of the Government of Northern
Ireland not making some provision for the
unemployed turned off from benefits, and in
consequence of the profiteering permitted to
go on on houses in Belfast to-day I have
much pleasure in proposing the Amendment
to the Address which I have already read.
Mr. LESLIE: Before I approach the
primary object which has brought me to my
feet this afternoon, namely to support the
Address which has been moved and seconded
in the customary manner, I hope the House
will permit me for a moment or two to say
with what great pleasure I listened to the
speech of the Noble Viscount in moving the
Address, and also to the weighty words of the
right hon. the Lord Mayor in seconding it.
The noble Viscount allude to the question
of regulating heavy motor traffic, particularly
that kind of heavy motor traffic which
carries passengers for hire. I can only say
that my own recent experience has been
exactly that of the noble Viscount. I spent
more than five minutes the other afternoon
in a light travelling motor car endeavouring
to get past a vehicle limited by law to 12
miles an hour, which, if I have acquired any
judgment of speed during the last twenty
years or so was undoubtedly exceeding twentyfive miles an hour. It seems imperative in
any legislation to be introduced on this
subject an absolute requirement must be
inserted that such vehicles must carry a
conductor at the rear of the vehicle much in
the manner of the old fashioned London
omnibus.
In the Lord Mayor's speech perhaps the
most striking thing to me was the eloquent
tribute which he paid to our late Leader the
noble Marquess (the Marquess of Londonderry). It is, I think, impossible that any
of us should endeavour to address this House
to-day without recording our profound sense
of the depth of the obligation which we owe
to the Marquess of Londonderry, and our
sense of the debt which the whole people of
If
Northern Ireland also owe to the noble Mar
quess. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
was given to me as to others to be, by the
grace of our first Senator, the then Lord
Mayor, in the City Hall, on the occasion
of the first meeting of the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, and I remember distinctly
how the noble Marquess said that he envisaged the Ulster ship as a good ship which
had cast its moorings and was about to
voyage on seas not free from danger. The
Parliament of Northern Ireland-the shiphas weathered those dangers and difficulties.
How much does it not owe to its pilot? The
pilot of this House was the noble Marquess.
His services to Ulster were not limited to
the Leadership of this Senate. They were
not limited to the Ministry of Education,
nor to the production of the Measure which
will always be associated with his name.
The noble Marquess was, after the Prime
Minister himself, I imagine, unquestionably
the first Minister of the Cabinet. The work
of the noble Marquess is written in large
letters across the face of the Boundary Agreement, for the work that was done leading up
to that satisfactory settlement was as much
the work of the noble Marquess as any other
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). One
cannot help regretting, if only for the
sake of historical completeness, that on the
printed Act which records the result of those
Boundary discussions, the name of the noble
Marquess does not appear amongst the signatories. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) That,
however, is a matter of detail.
man.
Now that the noble Marquess has left us
as our Leader, though I am glad to say he
has not resigned his seat as a member of
this House, we have the privilege of welcoming his successor. The noble Viscount, who
acts for the first time as Leader of the Senate
this afternoon, comes to us, if I may say so,
with the record of an honoured Ulster name.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) The name is
one which if we look back through the past
centuries we shall find has always been
associated with causes peculiarly dear to
those who form the majority of this House.
We know that the noble Viscount has not
been with us as often as we could have
wished in the past, but we know that he had
duties outside, and that he had services to
Ulster to perform which prevented him
from attending our debates. The noble
Viscount has been with us whenhecould, and
we know he has spoken to us in Committee,
if not in the House, and he has made every
use of such opportunities as were at his command.
Moreover, the noble Viscount, the Leader
of the House, shares with the noble Viscount
who moved the Address a distinction which
I believe would otherwise be unequalled.
We find both of them are elected members
Debate on Address. 22
9 MARCH 1926
Debate on Address.
of two Second Chambers. It is distinctly
unusual, and I believe, but for the fact that
the Front Bench furnishes two instances
this afternoon, it would be unique. I can
only hope as regards the conduct of the business of the House, which is now in the hands
of the noble Viscount, we shall always receive
the utmost consideration, and that
shall be given time to consider matters
brought before us, and if, on the other
we
hand, it should
SO
is
chance that there
any little matter on which any
of us can render some slight assistance to
the noble Viscount, that assistance will be
rendered, not only because the rendering of
it will be a pleasure in itself, but also, perhaps, because any help that can be given in
that way will be some small attempt at repayment to our former Leader for all he has
done for us.
I turn more particularly to the Gracious
Speech itself, and incidentally to the Amendment to the Address, moved by the hon.
Member (Senator Dorman). The Speech
opens with a reference to unemployment,
which has already been alluded to, and to
questions connected partly with unemployment and partly with other interests of the
working classes, and it is against that part
of the Speech which deals with these matters
that the attack of the hon. Senator has been
particularly directed. The Amendment which
he would have the Senate accept asks us in
effect to censure the Government for not
giving any definite promise of finding employment for those who are out of work at
present. We are invited to censure the
Government for a general lack of effective
action, and all that in the face of the wording
of the Speech itself, which incidentally bears
very complete testimony to the work which
has already been done.
We are reminded in the Gracious Speech
of the facilities afforded by the Loans
Guarantee Acts. I think I am correct-and
there are those here who can correct me if
I am wrong-in saying that it is due to the
operation of one of those Acts that the shipbuilding industry in Belfast has been kept
at work to a greater extent than it otherwise would. I think I am correct again in
saying that the facilities offered to local
authorities in the way of loans and grants
have been of great assistance in promoting
the permanent improvement of our roads. I
wonder whether those who attack the
Government for so-called inaction in these
matters are aware that one of our counties
had
far more ambitious road-making
scheme before it some three years ago than
the scheme it carried out, and it was
obliged to postpone the complete adoption of
that scheme because it could not get the
necessary money advanced to it all at one
time. That is an example, I think, of the
great difficulty that must always be faced.
a
There is always some public work to be done
or some public improvement which it would
be possible to do if the means for paying for
it were forthcoming.
Neither the Government nor the taxpayer
nor the ratepayer has a purse unlimited,
and the resources of Northern Ireland have
been strained already to endeavour to keep
our loyal population at work, and so far as
I know I believe it is the intention of this
Senate that in so far as its power in matters
of finance extend they will not allow the
strain to be relaxed so long as there is a
call upon it. Housing and matters of that
kind which are in the interest of the working classes will, I am convinced, receive the
utmost consideration and assistance, and to
that the Gracious Speech before us bears
witness.
Passing to another matter mentioned in
the Speech and to which the noble Viscount
who moved the Address alluded. I refer to
the preservation of ancient monuments. I
am sure all of us who are proud of the
Northern area are extremely anxious that
any really historic place and really historic
monuments shall be preserved for the benefit
of future generations. We are, I am sure,
quite willing to do anything that can be done
to make towards that end. The proposals
laid before us must be proposals which will
carry public confidence and public sympathy.
The Bill when we see it will, I hope, be
found to give the utmost encouragement to
private effort and to the effort of municipal
bodies. At present our County Councils
have powers in that way, but I
am afraid they do not use them
very much. Perhaps they have not the
temptation. I am convinced that working on
lines which have been found satisfactory
across the water, there is a great deal to be
done in that way.
I cannot refrain from recording my satisfaction that something further in the way of
alleviating distress to the smaller creatures is
contemplated during the Session. There are
some Measures on the Imperial Statute Book
relating to animals, and also to small birds
which are not yet on ours, and I hope that
the reference in the Speech means that that
will be brought before us during the coming
Session.
I cannot but regret that the Government is
not bringing in a Bill somewhat on the lines
of the private Bill which I brought before the
House last year, dealing with the better protection of unfortunate married women
and maintenance for their children, but
perhaps that will be dealt with later on.
There are many other matters which may
call for attention and which, no doubt, will
receive attention in the years to come. For
the present, as it seems to me, the programme put before us is a business pro-
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.

THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
gramme which will make for reform and improvement, and I have much pleasure in
supporting the Address. I cannot say that I
think it will gain anything by the addition
of the words suggested by the hon. Member
(Senator Dorman).
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: In rising
to speak on the Motion before us this afternoon I should like to take advantage of the
opportunity to express my regret at the loss
to the House of the leadership of the
Marquess of Londonderry. We will, undoubtedly, miss the noble Marquess in this
House. We were fortunate in having him
from the beginning to give us the benefit of
his advice and assistance. The Education
Bill will be a long-standing memorial to his
efforts. But while we miss him we have
some consolation in knowing that he is giving his services in a wider field, and I trust
he will be instrumental in bringing about
a settlement of that much vexed questionthe miner's question. I hope our present
leader will not be long before he endears himself to the Members of this House.
There is really nothing in the Gracious
Speech to which I wish to refer, but I must
congratulate the mover (Viscount Bangor)
and the seconder (Sir William Turner) on
the able way in which they have moved and
seconded the Address to his Grace the
Governor. They both certainly delivered
very fine speeches. As I say, there is nothing
in the Gracious Speech that one could criticise
to any extent, but I see mention made of the
Intoxicating Liquor Act. I hope nothing of
a contentious nature is intended that would
bring the extreme sides into collision again.
I am glad to find that arrangements have
been made with the Imperial Government to
share the burden of unemployment benefit.
That is only fair. We-the workers of Ulster,
together with those of Great Britaincontributed into one common fund from 1912
up to 1921, and now that we are under stress
and difficulties I think the Imperial Government should share part of the terrible burden
we have to carry.
Reference is made to the Jury Act. I do
hope that something may be done to relieve
the hardships that exist so far as jurymen
are concerned. I have had some experience
of serving on a jury when I could not by any
means afford either the time or loss of wages.
There are others in a worse position than
myself and unfortunately helpless. Something
should be done to amend the Jury Act and
compensate those enduring hardships for the
welfare of other members of the community.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
On the occasion when I served on a jury
I was a weekly worker, and my wages were
much required, but I was robbed of my
wages and unfortunately got no remuneration from the State. I sincerely hope something will be done in this respect.
I
Allusion has been made by Senator
Leslie to the Bill which he introduced last
year. I am sorry to say there is one slight
matter which requires alteration or
improvement in the Widows', Orphans', and
Old Age (Contributory) Pensions Act.
find that widows who have left Scotland and
have arrived in the North of Ireland and who
have children born out of wedlock can draw
benefit in Northern Ireland simply because
the children were legitimised afterwards. In
the North that is not the law, and something should be done to remedy this state of
affairs and place the North of Ireland in the
same position, or as good a position, as Scotland or England.
Another instance only came before my
notice the other day. It is that of a widow
whose child, unfortunately, was born before
her marriage took place, with the result
that she has been deprived both of the
widows' pension and the orphans' allowance.
I trust Senator Leslie will persevere and
seek for some recognition of the Bill which
he introduced last Session. I am much
surprised that we have an Amendment regretting that the Government have not
taken steps to provide work. We all know
that the Government have done their best
in this matter, and that it is impossible for
them to provide more work than is being
done at the present time. Corporations,
municipalities, and councils throughout the
North of Ireland are co-operating and
assisting to provide work, and yet we have
an Amendment brought forward by Senator
Dorman censuring the Government,
Reference is made to the fact that when a
person's benefit is exhausted he is deprived
of poor-law assistance. That is a question into
which I recently inquired, because I greatly
regret that anybody should be struck off the
unemployment roll, but it is quite evident
that benefit could not go on for years. I must
admit that the Poor-law Guardians assist in
deserving cases brought before their notice,
and the amount of relief has in some cases
been in excess of the unemployment benefit
drawn. I want to state that out of every ten
applications made, seven are assisted. It is
not fair, therefore, to state to the public at
large that the Poor-law Guardians are soulless and careless with regard to the welfare
of the poorer classes. They are just as
anxious and desirous of extending help as any
other body.
I think that is all I have to say. I am
pleased that there is nothing of a contentious
nature in the King's Speech. I may congratulate Senator Dorman on again proving
himself a successful prophet. He has told us
Debate on Address.
Bills Presented.
9 MARCH 1926
more than once that many years ago he
informed his fellow-workers at the shipyards
of events that would take place many years
hence. I also desire to congratulate the
Government on adopting a Socialistic scheme
to provide seed for the farmer to grow flax.
However, I do not think that is a sign that
we are drifting into Socialism. The Unionist
party is by no means a stick-in-the-mud
party. It is prepared to progress and march
forward and provide assistance where private
enterprise failed.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I think Senator Joseph Cunningham has really answered
all the points raised by the Amendment and
it will not be necessary for me to make more
than a few remarks. I think the Government is doing everything possible to relieve
unemployment, both by unemployment
grants and under the Loans Guarantee
Acts.
With reference to the latter, hon,
Members may be interested in learning that,
so far as the shipyards are concerned, the
total amount of loans guaranteed is
£3,638,500; enabling five ships to be completed, while six are under construction.
The latest returns show that some 8,281 men
have thus been employed. With regard to
relief schemes, the Development Commission
Division No. 1.

has already issued a good many reports
dealing with road improvement schemes, with

employed, Coleraine Harbour and one or
two other projects. The hon. Senator can
be assured that the Government will spare
no pains in rendering any assistance they
can to lessen unemployment. I do not think
they can fairly be blamed for any failure to
provide relief for those deprived of benefit.
That is a matter for the Guardians, and I
certain they will give any assistance
they can. With regard to profiteering in
houses, I might point out that working class
houses up to a rental of £26 yearly, are still
restricted, except they pass into the possession of the landlord.
If they become
vacant they are automatically de-controlled.
De-control must start sometime, and this is
believed to be the best way to commence.
do not think I can add anything more to the

The House divided Contents, 1; Not-Contents, 15.

Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Cunningham, The Right Hon. S.

Resolved: That a humble Address be presented to his Grace in the following words-
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and loyal
subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, beg leave to offer our
humble thanks to your Grace for the
Speech which your Grace has addressed to
both Houses of Parliament.
Bills Presented.
PHARMACY (TEMPORARY PROVISIONS)
BILL (S.)
"to amend Section 7 and sub-Section 2 of
Section 19 of the Pharmacy and Poisons Act
(Northern Ireland), 1925," presented by Viscount Massereene and Ferrard, read the First
time, and ordered to be read the Second
time on Tuesday next and to be printed.

Long, Mr. J. A.

Adjournment.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: In moving
the adjournment of the House, I would like
to say that we have had a very interesting
debate this afternoon, and we are much indebted to the mover and seconder of the
Address, and also to those hon. Senators who
have spoken. I beg to move that this House
be adjourned until Tuesday, March 16, for
the consideration of Bills already read a
First time.

minutes before Five of the
clock.
