Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave out
from the beginning of line 6 (and that
Ministry shall), to "of" in line 8, and
insert:-
Provided that, before any monument is
included in any such list, the Ministry
shall give the owner of the monument (if
such owner's address be known or ascertainable) an opportunity of being heard, and
shall notify such owner of his right to
compensation, and"
These words are intended to ensure for the
owner, whatever class of owner he may be,
an opportunity of being heard before his
monument is put on the list. It may be
remembered that when these matters were
discussed in the House of Lords in 1912 and
1913 a great point was made of the fact that
it was apparently proposed to list a man's
monument without consulting him on the subject, and it was thought that that was not
right. When these matters were under consideration in England the question of compensation was not, perhaps, so necessarily important as it is here. In Northern Ireland,
as we know, we cannot take property of any
kind without compensation. If we cannot
take property neither can we take control of
that property without compensation. You
may be imposing a pecuniary loss on him
by assuming control of his property.
It might be a monument he could sell by
transporting elsewhere. It has been possible,
as the Committee will remember, to move
such things as Temple Bar and Cleopatra's
Needle. As the Committee will also remember, we are legislating here only for
Northern Ireland. We cannot go beyond
that, and if a man wants to sell his monument it seems to me that before the monument is put on the list the man should be
given an opportunity of saying anything he
may have to say, and he should also have the
right to compensation if the monument is
put on the list without his consent.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: On second thoughts, perhaps, the
hon. Senator will see that this Amendment
is not necessary. It goes somewhat beyond
the English Act of 1913, and it seems to me
it will create delay. As I understand the
matter, it would not be an advantage to the
owner at all and the Government are not
prepared to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I cannot see that the
delay on which the noble Viscount lays such
stress is of such importance. After all there
is some sort of postal system in Northern
Ireland. I reminded the House the other
day that Lord Curzon drew attention to this
point in the House of Lords. However, if the
noble Viscount is very anxious not to insert
these words, and if nobody else is anxious
that they should be inserted I shall not
detain the Committee any longer.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: There is no great point in this
Amendment, but I am prepared to reconsider it with the object of seeing if some
other Amendment can be drawn up for the
Report Stage.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
a
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in lines 9
and 10, to leave out "the next succeeding
sub-section," and insert "this Act."
This
raises another question. We provide in the
following Clause
penalty for offences
under the next succeeding sub-section. I
suggest that instead of dealing here with
offences under the next sub-section we should
make it an offence under this Act, because
that would provide for the possibility of
offences committed under Clause 7 when a
monument is under a Preservation Order.
As the matter stands at present, there is
no provision for imposing a penalty for
contravention of Clause 7 by a man who is
the owner of a monument. Therefore, it is
desirable to alter the words as I suggest,
but if there is any objection it is not a
point I should desire to press.
a
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I gather that what the hon. Member proposes is to fix
single general
penalty for all offences under the Act. If
that is so the Government cannot accept the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I will withdraw the Amendment. I am not very keen on it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 16
to leave out from "monument" to "he" in
line 17 (or make excavations in the neighbourhood thereof). There is no such provision in the English Act, and the English Act,
I think, ought to be referred to. The noble
Viscount refers to it now and again when
he likes it, and he does not refer to it when
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Committee.
4 MAY 1926


t


!
he does not like it. In the English Act this
provision is not included, and I cannot help
thinking the reason it was not included in
the English Act was because it was not
wanted. There is quite sufficient protection
given elsewhere in the Bill to meet this case
of excavation. If you leave it as it stands
let us see what it says. "Or make excavations in the neighbourhood thereof." What
is meant by neighbourhood? Half an acre,
half a mile, or half a county? How is it
proposed to justify interference with the man
to whom the monument belongs if he wants
to conduct his own excavations? Why should
he not do so?
The English Act does not contain these
words, and I cannot see that we ought to
retain them. We must recognise some rights
of the owner. I know it may be said that
excavations, except under the control of
the Minister of Finance, may lead to awful
results, but I have yet to learn that the
Ministry of Finance is such a universally
received authority as to justify us setting
them up against the original rights of property. In moving the Amendment I direct
particular attention to the expression
"neighbourhood."
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: At any rate to water down this subsection very much. The learned societies
is to put an absolute embargo on any excavations.
Mr. LESLIE: Oh, no.
a
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: At any rate, to water down this subsection very much. The learned societies
here are very strong on the point that excavation in injudicious hands may do irreparable damage to
monument, and
muddle up the neighbourhood to such an extent that scientific excavations would be impossible. As regards the expression "neighbourhood," I think the Committee may take
it that the Advisory Committee is not going
to strain at the meaning of that. It will
only mean the vicinity of the monument.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to support the inclusion of the Clause as it is. It is quite
possible that owing to an obsession a person
may do great harm to a monument, and
grievous harm has been done to one of the
most ancient monuments in Ireland by a
private owner. As a Socialist I do not stand
here as a supporter of property, or to speak
on behalf of property, but I do stand here to
try to show the absurdity of moving an
Amendment such as this.
One of the most ancient monuments in Ireland, which will be venerated by all Irishmen so long as Ireland retains the name of
Ireland, is where on the 1st May all the
lights of Ireland which were put out are relit under Druidical administration. The
owner of that monument was obsessed with
the British-Israelite movement, and got the
idea into his head that the Ark of the
Covenant was buried under Tara Hill.
The CHAIRMAN: I want to allow every
latitude for discussion, but I cannot see
that the Ark of the Covenant has anything
whatever to do with the Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I am
afraid I disagree with the Amendment on
the ground that such a monument is not the
property of one man, but the property of the
people of the country. I do think the owner
should be treated with all justice, but it is
much better that the country, the Government of the country and not the owner,
should be the chief object in these ancient
places.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount and his
allies are perhaps too strong for me.
Colonel CRAWFORD: I do not think it
is Parliamentary language to call us his
allies. We are not allies of the Government.
Mr. LESLIE : I did not wish to give any
offence. I was about to say more seriously
that the insertion of the words does not
really matter greatly, because the power of
the Ministry to deal with excavations is
limited.
Therefore, I would ask leave to
withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 4,
line 17, to leave out from "forthwith" ("he
shall forthwith give notice") to "the" in
line 18 ("to the Ministry of Finance") and
to insert "so inform." This is a small
difficulty. I thought the Bill as we originally saw it had been altered in one place so
as to get rid of the juxtaposition of the
purpose" and "intention." I rather think
the point is a very small one. I think
having spoken here of the purpose to
demolish we ought not to go on to talk
about intention. Therefore, I suggest a
small alteration to leave out from "forthwith" and insert "so inform."
"
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This is a very small point, but still
I think the wording of the Government
draftsman is more suitable than the wording
of the hon. Senator.
Mr. LESLIE: It is the inconsistency
I object to. That is all.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
143 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
Line 20, after "alteration" to insert "or."
Lines 20 and 21, to leave out "or excavation."
Amendments not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 21, to
leave out "two months" ("period of two
months after"), and insert "one month." I
suggest here that the Minister of Finance
ought to be able to make up his mind if he
has anything to say within a period of one
month, as had been originally proposed. The
Ministry should not take two months to deal
with Northern Ireland when Great Britain
manages to deal with Great Britain in one
month, and as the noble Viscount has just
reminded us how important haste is that is
to say, we have not really got time to consult
the owner-I really cannot see why we want
two months instead of one month. You must
remember if a man really does want to commence any work of removal, alteration, or
addition, he may want to do it at once.
presume, of course, that he will get compensation for the delay. But at all events I
suggest that we take out "two months" and
insert "one month," following the English
Act.
I
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The reason why we have inserted
two months is that although it is not in the
Act, it was suggested by the authorities in
Great Britain as a more satisfactory period
than one month. That is the reason why we
considered two months better. There is not
very much in it. Two months is a better
period than one.
Mr. LESLIE: Really I am disposed to press
this matter. We really must have some regard to the different conditions. The
English authorities are not concerned with
legislation in Northern Ireland. I suggest
to the noble Viscount that one month"
should be inserted instead of "two months."
6"
On Question, Amendment negatived.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Lines 21 and 22, leave out "having given
such notice" and insert "so informing the
Ministry."
Line 22, leave out from "notice" to the end
of sub-section (2).
Mr. LESLIE: This first Amendment has
already been dealt with, and the next one has
for practical purposes also been dealt with.
I may draw attention to it on the Report
Stage.
Amendments not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
New Clause:
The Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee may with the approval of the
Ministry publish from time to time a list
containing such monuments in Northern
Ireland as are vested in the Ministry, or
in a local authority or in the National
Trust, or of which the Ministry or a local
authority has been appointed guardian, or
in respect of which a Preservation Order
is in force. (Mr. Leslie.)
Brought up, and read a First time.
Mr. LESLIE : I beg to move that this
Clause be read a Second time. The object of
it is to enable the Advisory Committee to let
the people in Northern Ireland know something about the monuments there really are.
As the matter stands at present we are in
the position that the ordinary person has no
knowledge of the monuments in Northern
Ireland which need the protection of the
Ministry or of the local authority or of the
National Trust. It appears to me that this
Clause would be of some use, and it is necessary. I have put it in as a new Clause, otherwise some other provisions I have suggested
would have become applicable to it. There
is no objection, I think, to including the
local authority in this new Clause. The local
authority may be the owners, that is to say,
they may have monuments vested in them
under the provisions of the Land Purchase
Acts. I think that this Clause might possibly be of some use, and subject to what
may be said by others, I do not see that it
does any serious harm.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I venture to think that this
Amendment is really not necessary. Any individual person who is interested in this
matter can get from the Finance Ministry a
list of the monuments which are of national
importance, and, in any case, if we were to
accept this Amendment the words "or in the
National Trust" could hardly be included.
The National Trust works, as the hon.
Senator knows, under Imperial Acts, and
has not expressed any desire to be included
in this Act of ours. I really do not think
that this new Clause is necessary.
Mr. LESLIE : The new Clause was intended to do something towards popularising
ancient monuments, but if the noble
Viscount objects I ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 6 (Orders placing ancient monuments under protection of Ministry of
Finance).
(1) If the Ancient Monuments Advisory
Committee report to the Ministry of
Finance that any monument is in danger
of destruction or removal or damage from
neglect or injudicious treatment, and that
the preservation of the monument is of
national importance, the Ministry of
145 Ancient Monuments Bill-

4 MAY 1926
Committee.

tha
ال
Finance may, if it appears that the monument is an ancient monument within the
meaning of this Act, make an order (in
this Act referred to as a Preservation
Order) placing the monument under the
protection of that Ministry:
Provided that, if in any case the Ministry
of Finance considers that the making of
such an order is a matter of immediate
urgency, the order may be made without
receiving any such report as aforesaid.
(2) Where the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee have reason to believe
that any monument is in danger as aforesaid, and that the preservation of the
monument is of national importance, they
may themselves, or by any person authorised in writing by them, enter at any
reasonable time upon any premises for the
purposes of enabling them to determine by
inspection of the monument whether it is
proper for them to report to the Ministry
of Finance:
Provided that, unless the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee consider that
the inspection of the monument is a matter
of immediate urgency, they shall give not
less than seven clear days' notice to the occupier of the premises of their intention so
to enter upon them.
(3) A Preservation Order shall have effect
until revoked by the Ministry of Finance.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Page 4, line 32, to leave out "of national
importance" and insert "proper to be held
for preservation for the benefit of the people
of Northern Ireland."
Mr. LESLIE: This Amendment has already been dealt with and falls.
A mendment not moved.
The following Amendment also stood on
the Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Line 3, to leave out from "aforesaid" to
"they" in line 4.
Mr. LESLIE: I had intended to move an
Amendment there, but I think as things are
I had better not move that Amendment. We
will leave it as it is.
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 14, at
end ("so to enter upon them") to insert
(3) The Ministry shall forthwith notify the
owner of the monument and the occupier
of the premises wherein the monument is
situated of the making of a Preservation
Order under this section, and of his right
to apply for the revocation of such Order,
and of the penalties which may be incurred
under this Act.
(4) Any owner or occupier aggrieved by
the making of a Preservation Order under
this section may, within one month after
he has been notified under the last preceding sub-section, apply to the Ministry to
revoke such Order.
(5) On the receipt of such application the
Ministry shall either revoke the Order or
shall make a fresh Order providing that
the Order shall lapse eighteen months from
the time the first Order was made, unless
during such cighteen months the monument
shall be purchased by the Ministry by
agreement or a Bill for the compulsory
purchase of the monument shall be in
Parliament.
I suggest here these three sub-Clauses to
endeavour to meet the very difficult point of
taking dominion over another man's property
without the authority of Parliament. It will
be remembered that the English Act provides
that the Commissioners of Works with the
approval of the Treasury may make a Preservation Order in certain circumstances, and
that Preservation Order shall only be valid
for eighteen months unless in the meantime
it is confirmed by Parliament, and that the
Commissioners of Works may, if they so
desire, bring in a private Bill to confirm
the Preservation Order, and a private Bill so
brought in shall have the right to be referred
to a joint Committee, and the owner shall
be given an opportunity of being heard and
the ordinary procedure of a private Bill
Committee follows. When moving this sub-
Clause in England, Lord Beauchamp, who,
perhaps, we would not expect to find a very
strong supporter of the rights of private
property, pointed out that in his opinion
nothing short of the authority of Parliament gave complete protection. As the Bill
stands a Preservation Order can be made,
and apparently can be enforced. That is entirely unconstitutional, and I do not think
this Committee would be likely to allow that
to happen. I may say I endeavoured to find
out how many preservation Orders had been
made in England after the 1913 Act was
passed. I could not find one, but I since
learned that one was made in 1924. I do
not think many preservation Orders are
likely to be made, but I do feel very strongly
it is quite important for us not to allow a
man to be deprived to some extent of his
property without the authority of Parliament. Accordingly, I move sub-Clauses first
of all, providing that where a preservation
Order is made the man whose monument it
applies to shall have the right to be notified
and shall subsequently have the right of
application to the Ministry for a revocation
of the Order, and, finally, if the Ministry
and the owner of the monument cannot agree,
that the Order shall not stay in force for
more than 18 months unless during that time
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
the monument is purchased or a Bill for its
purchase has been brought into Parliament.
I cannot agree that you can take a man's
monument unless you are prepared to pay.
That is the purpose of the sub-section I have
moved. Whether it may be accepted I do
not know. I do think the principle is of the
very greatest importance.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I agree with the principle embodied
in this Amendinent. The hon. Senator
referred to the fact that there has only been
one Preservation Order made since 1913, but
I think the reason for that must be very
obvious, namely, that Preservation Orders
are not made because of the necessity in
Great Britain of following them up by a
Bill before Parliament. There is an Amendment down in my name on behalf of the
Government, to insure that no injustice is
done to the owner of a monument, and I
consider that the Amendment, as set down
in my name is more suitable than the
Amendment moved by the hon. Senator.
My Amendment follows his Amendment on
the Paper, and does provide for Parliamentary sanction. Therefore, I would ask
the hon. Member to withdraw his Amendment and accept mine.
Mr. LESLIE: My Amendment puts the
onus of moving on the Minister, and the
noble Viscount's Amendment proposes to
put the onus on the owner. The owner,
after all, is not represented in Parliament,
and the noble Viscount does not provide an
opportunity for him being heard, nor does
he provide anything in the nature of Private
Bill procedure. I do not know whether the
Committee would like me to withdraw this
Amendment in order that we might perhaps
re-consider it at a later stage. It is obvious
that some few words in the first of the new
sub-sections could not stand-the words dealing with penalties on account of alterations,
which the Government have not accepted in
Clause 5. This is a matter on which I would
ask hon. Senators to give us a little help, so
that we may see what really is their desire.
Mr. LONG: I think you might withdraw
your Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: The view seems to be that
the Amendment should be withdrawn.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, on page 5, lines 15
and 16, to leave out Sub-Section (3) and
insert:-
"Every Preservation Order shall be laid
before both Houses of Parliament as soon
as may be after it is made, and if either
of those Houses within the statutory period
next after any Preservation Order is laid
before it by resolution annuls the same,
such Preservation Order shall forthwith
become void, but without prejudice to the
validity of anything which in the meantime
may have been done thereunder. Subject
as aforesaid, a Preservation Order shall
have effect until revoked by the Ministry
of Finance.
For the purpose of this sub-section the
expression "statutory period" means a
period comprising five days at least on
which the House in question has sat, but
not being in any case shorter in duration
than ten days, any days comprised in more
than one session of Parliament may be
reckoned for that purpose."
I have already explained, in answer to
Senator Leslie, the necessity for this
Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Page 5, line 15, after "(3)" insert "subject
as aforesaid."
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount has incorporated this in his Amendment.
Amendment not moved.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Effect of Preservation Orders).
(1) While a Preservation Order is in
force, the monument to which the Order
relates shall not be demolished or removed,
nor shall any additions or alterations be
made thereto, or any work carried out in
connection therewith, except with the
written consent of the Ministry of Finance.
(2) If, while a Preservation Order is in
force, it appears to the Ministry of Finance
that, owing to the neglect of the owner of
the monument, the monument is liable to
fall into decay, the Ministry of Finance
may make an order appointing that Ministry guardian of the monument for the
period during which the Preservation Order
is in force, and in that case the provisions
of this Act shall, during that period, take
effect as if the Ministry of Finance had
been appointed guardian by virtue of a
deed executed by every person seized or
possessed of, or entitled to, an estate or
interest in the monument.
(3) Any Order made under the last preceding sub-section may be revoked at any
time by the Ministry of Finance.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Clause 7, page 5, line 19, after "nor," insert
"except in the case of urgent necessity."
Amendment not moved.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 5,
line 30, after "Act" (Act shall), to insert,
"other than those relating to public access."
The Amendment I have put down has to deal
with a difficulty which will arise as regards
public access. If the Ministry proceeds to
make a guardianship order, apparently the
provisions as regards public access are to take
effect as if the Ministry had been appointed
by virtue of every estate existing in the
monument, that is, the person in possession
and the person who may come into possession
in the future. I do not know if that is the
intention. We have had some difficulty with
the word owner, but for the moment it seems
to me that we had better cover ourselves
here by saying "other than those relating to
public access," because we shall be dealing
with the question of public access again in
a few minutes, and I need not anticipate
what I am going to say about that.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: With all respect to the hon. Senator,
I do not think there is very much in his
Amendment. I can see no reason for inserting the words he suggests when, after all,
the matter is so serious that the State has
to step in and assume the whole responsibility. I do not see any necessity for the
Amendment, because the State only steps in
at the eleventh hour.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think it is important to pursue that matter at the moment,
and I ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 5, line 31, after "guardian," insert
"during that period."
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page 5,
line 35, after "may" (may be revoked) to
insert "with the consent of the owner of the
monument." The point that occurred to me
was that if you allow the Ministry to come
in and make a Preservation Order, and if
it should so happen that the Ministry finds
its £600 a year is exhausted before it has
completed something that wants doing while
the Preservation Order is in force, the owner
cannot make any repairs and he cannot do
anything without the consent of the Ministry
of Finance. If during the Preservation Order
the monument falls into disrepair the Ministry, having no money to spend, can turn
round and revoke this Preservation Order,
and leave the monument in a worse state
than they found it. That is a possibility
under the Bill and, therefore, I suggest that
if a Preservation Order has been made it
should not be revoked without the consent
of the owner of the monument.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I think the contingency the hon.
Member has in mind is so unlikely that it
will never occur. At the same time, I cannot see why the owner's consent is necessary
to a revocation by the Ministry of its own
Order. I do not think the contingency the
hon. Member has in mind will ever occur.
Mr. LESLIE: Obviously the noble Viscount does not intend to give the owner any
protection whatever. I will not pursue the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Penalty for injuring ancient
monuments).
If any person injures or defaces any
monument of which the Ministry of
Finance or a county council is the owner or
guardian, or which is the subject of a Preservation Order, or which has been included
in a list of monuments published by the
Ministry of Finance under this part of this
Act, that person shall, on conviction under
the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts,
be liable to a fine not exceeding five pounds,
and, in addition to the fine, to pay such
sums as the court by whom he is tried think
just for the purpose of repairing any damages caused by him, or to imprisonment for
a term not exceeding one month.
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Page 5, line 38, leave out "county council"
and insert "local authority or the National
Trust."
Page 6, line 4, leave out "five" and insert
"ten."
Line 8, leave out "one month" and insert
"three months."
Amendments not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Compensation for infringement
of private rights).
(1) Where, under the powers conferred by
this Act, any act is done involving interference with private rights of property, compensation shall be payable by the Ministry
of Finance.
(2) If any dispute arises as to the amount
of such compensation, such question shall
be referred to, and determined by, an
official arbitrator under the Acquisition of
Land (Assessment of Compensation) Act,
1919, and sections three and six of that Act
shall, apply accordingly.
151 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, on page 6,
line 17, at the end of the Clause, to insert
the following new Sub-section :-

For the purposes of this section the inclusion of a monument in any list published under this Act, or the making of a
Preservation Order shall be deemed to be
an act done involving interference with
private rights of property."
I find some slight difficulty in Clause 9.
This is the Clause, as the Committee will
see, dealing with compensation. It does not
definitely state that compensation shall
be paid in respect of the inclusion of a monument in the list, or in respect of the making
of a Preservation Order. Yet both of these
proceedings would be to my mind proceedings which involve interference with the
private rights of property, and, therefore,
compensation ought to be payable. There is
not provided any means of setting in operation the compensation machinery, and I
think we could get over that. I, therefore,
suggest that "for the purposes of this Section
the inclusion of a monument in any list
published under this Act, or the making of a
Preservation Order, shall be deemed to
be an Act done involving interference with
private rights of property."
I am not sure that if something on the
lines of my suggestion was adopted, it is at
all clear that the expression "act is done"
would cover what I want. It is not an expression in the Public Health Act, and if you
look back to the earlier part of the Bill to
trace the expression "act" you find that "act"
means actually doing something yourself, not
making somebody else do it. "Doing such
acts and things as may be required for maintenance, and any other act or thing required
for the purpose of repairing a monument."
The words are used almost in a manual
sense, and I think it is necessary for the
protection of the owner, that words of the
kind I have suggested should be included.
VISCOUNT MASERREENE AND FER-
RARD: I oppose this Amendment, because
I think you may still leave it to the Tribunal to see that it is not an interference
with the rights of private property. It is
quite clear what the Tribunal would do,
and I think this Amendment is superfluous
and unnecessary.
Mr. LESLIE: I will not press the Amendment, but it seems to me to be another injustice to the owner.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 10 (Power to receive voluntary
contributions for maintenance of ancient
monuments.)
The Ministry of Finance may receive
voluntary contributions towards the cost
of the maintenance and preservation of
any monument which has become vested in
that Ministry, or of which that Ministry
has become the guardian under the provisions of this Act, and may enter into
an agreement with the owner of any such
monument, or with any other person, as to
the maintenance and preservation of the
monument and the cost thereof.
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 6, line 20, after "Ministry" insert "or
local authority".
Line 23, after "Ministry" in each case
where it occurs insert "or a local authority".
Amendments not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 11 (Powers of county councils
with respect to ancient monuments).
(1) The provisions of this Act with
respect to the maintenance of and access,
and penalties for injury, to ancient monuments shall apply in the case of any ancient
monument-
(a) of which a county council has, be
fore the passing of this Act, become
guardian under sub-section (2) of section
nineteen of the Local Government (Ireland) Act, 1898; or
(b) which has been before the passing
of this Act, or thereafter becomes, vested
in a county council by virtue of an order
under sub-section (3) of section fourteen
of the Irish Land Act, 1903,
as if the county council were substituted
in those provisions for the Ministry of
Finance; and the expenses incurred by a
county council under this section shall be
raised by means of the poor rate as a
county at large charge.
(2) Where a county council is guardian
of an ancient monument, such council may,
with the consent of the owner of the monument, transfer the guardianship thereof
to the Ministry of Finance, and the provisions of this Act shall apply accordingly.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 11,
page 7, line 5, to leave out "and" (and the
expenses), and insert "provided that." This
Amendment is to meet a possible objection
which I think will probably have some substance in it. We see that in certain circumstances the Bill is to be read as if the
county council is to be substituted for the
Ministry of Finance, and it goes on to say
that the expenses incurred by a county
council shall be raised so and so. I think
153 Ancient Monuments Bill-

4 MAY 1926
Committee.
that if those provisions which deal with
access, maintenance, and expenses of county
councils are to be read as though the county
council were to be substituted for the Ministry we will have some little difficulty in
Clause 14, because in Clause 14 we find that
such expense as may be incurred by the
Ministry of Finance in the exercise of its
powers in connection with the maintenance
of ancient monuments are to be defrayed out
of moneys provided by Parliament. Whether
that is the intention of the Government I
do not know. I do not think it
is. I rather think that it is the
intention of the Government that county
council expenses should be borne as hitherto
by the county councils themselves. If that
is the intention I do think the Bill has expressed itself very badly indeed. I suggest
that we put the matter beyond dispute by
simply leaving out the word "and" and
putting in "provided that." I do not think
it can be suggested that the expression paying out of expenses coupled with the maintenance of ancient monuments is anything
else than a provision in respect of maintenance. If the noble Viscount can assure me
that the Government itself proposes to bear
the expenditure of the county council, of
course, my Amendment falls to the ground
as unnecessary, but I am afraid my Amendment is required. I beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am not quite clear what the effect
of these words would be in the Bill, and if
the hon. Member would leave it over till the
Report Stage I will go into the matter.
Mr. LESLIE: By leave of the Committee,
I will withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Line 9, to leave out "county council" and
insert "local authority."
Line 10, to leave out "council" and insert
"local authority."
Amendments not moved.
Colonel CRAWFORD: Before you put the
Clause to the Committee, I take this opportunity of expressing the hope that in the
further consideration of this Bill the powers
of the county council should be more considered than they are under the Bill. After all,
the chief local authority in the county is the
county council. The district councils are
fully represented not only by their chairmen,
but by members from each district. I think
that where the county council is anxious, as
my county council is anxious to do and probably some of them are in a better position
to take charge of ancient monuments in their
district than the Ministry or Department,
which is to be formed, and might not have
the same information as we have, they
should have the power. I do hope in the
further consideration of the Bill the Leader
of the House will ask the Government to do
this, and we will be most happy to give any
advice. I am only speaking because my own
county council has taken the greatest interest in ancient monuments. I would hope
that the Government will consider the county
councils, because up to this they have not
been asked to say what their opinion is of
this Bill. I agree with the first part of the
Bill, but the county council should be given
more powers, and I trust the Government
will give a little more consideration to the
powers of these county councils, who wish to
do their duty in preserving what are con
sidered to be of the greatest interest, the
ancient monuments of Ireland.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the right hon. and
gallant Member, as I said before in the
previous part of this Committee Stage, I am
prepared on behalf of the Government to introduce a new Clause, a subsequent Clause,
on the Report Stage, giving the county
council more power, that is to say, enabling
them to become guardians of ancient monuments which are considered of national
importance. I am prepared to do so.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 12 (Public access to ancient
monuments).
The public shall have access to any monument which is vested in the Ministry of
Finance or a county council, or of which
that Ministry or a county council is
guardian, at such times and under such regulations as may from time to time be prescribed by that Ministry or such county
council respectively:
Provided that-
(i) this section shall not apply to any
monument of which the Ministry of
Finance or a county council has been
appointed or constituted the guardian before the commencement of this Act, except in cases where the consent of the
owner has been given to the public having
access to the monument either by the
deed appointing or constituting that
Ministry or such county council the
guardian, or where a right of access has
arisen through the acquiescence of the
owner or otherwise; and
(ii) where the deed appointing the
Ministry of Finance the guardian of the
monument is executed after the commencement of this Act, if the deed SO
provides, the public shall not have access
to the monument without the consent of
the owner of the monument.
155 Ancient Monuments Bill-
THE SENATE

Committee.
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 7, line 15, to leave out "county
council" and to insert "local authority."
Line 16, to leave out "county council" and
insert "local authority."
Amendments not moved.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move on page 7, line 17, to
leave out "regulations" ("under such regulations"), and insert "conditions." I think
"conditions" is a more general term, and has
a somewhat wider significance than regulations.
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask whether the noble
Viscount proposes to accept the following
Amendment, which stands in my name at
the top of the next page of Amendments.
The position is rather altered since I put my
Amendment down by the changing of the
word.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am not prepared to accept that
Amendment, because we consider "conditions"
enables us to make the change.
Mr. LESLIE: I really lay some stress on
that statement of the noble Viscount, that
in the opinion of the Government the new
word introduced does cover my proposal, that
is to say, there will be statutory provision
to charge for admission. I certainly support
the Amendment proposed by the noble Viscount. "Conditions" is much better than
"regulations."
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 7, line 17, after "regulations” ("under
such regulations"), to insert "which may
make provision among other things for the
route of access, and for imposing charges for
admission."
Mr. LESLIE: This Amendment falls after
what the noble Viscount said in moving his
Amendment.
Amendment not moved.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move in page 7, line 18,
after "by" ("prescribed by that Ministry")
to insert "regulations of." This comes in
consequence of the last Amendment.
The CHAIRMAN: The noble Viscount's
voice is getting lower and lower and lower.
I can hear not one word.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move after "by" to insert
"regulations of."
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Page 7, line 19, to leave out "county council" and insert "local authority."
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 21 to
leave out from "apply" ("shall not apply to
any") to the end of line 40, and insert "without the consent of the owner and the occupier
of the premises whereon the monument is
situated, and of the owner and occupier of
any other premises which it may be necessary to cross in order to obtain access to the
monument, and provided also that no right
of public access which may exist at the commencement of this Act shall be deemed to be
extinguished by reason only of the provisions
of this section."
This is an attempt on my part to deal with
the very difficult question of public access to
monuments. The Committee may appreciate
that the question of access to a monument
does not depend entirely on whether the land
one goes across to get to the monument belongs to the man to whom the monument
belongs or not. I confess I do not think that
the Clause, as it comes before us, is satisfactory. It seems to me to go very much
round the bush and not to arrive at the
proper point. The difficulties that particularly occur to me are these: What is to
happen, if this Clause is accepted, in Land
Purchase Act cases where a man has really
become the owner of a monument under the
Land Purchase Acts or where he does not?
What is to happen as regards the right of
public access, because the Land Purchase
Acts do not, as it seems to me, give any
right whatever of public access to a monument which is the subject of the Act. The
right of access given in the Land Purchase
Acts is the right of access which was given
by the original Act of 1882, and the access
given in the Act of 1882 was limited to access
of the Commissioner of Public Works for the
purpose of repairing a monument. It seems
to me if we pass the Clause as it stands we
are up against a difficulty there. Again, in
passing Preservation Orders there does not
seem to be any power under the Preservation
Order Clause to give public access. And
again we use an expression at the end of the
first Clause of the proviso which is quite
new to me. The section is not to apply where
the right of access has arisen through the
acquiescence of the owner. I really must ask
the noble Viscount, if he is going to oppose
my Amendment, what in the world he
means by acquiescence. We all know the
difficulties perpetually before us as to rights
of way. I think most of us are under the
impression that the mere voluntary courtesy
to A, B, C, or the whole alphabet, is no

foundation for a right of way. I think we
are also agreed that it is contrary to public
policy to make owners so extremely careful
that they are obliged to exclude everybody
for fear they may by acquiescence to use the
word in the Bill-establish a right of way.
I do not think the Clause as it stands in the
Bill is at all satisfactory. I think that,
perhaps, the method I have adopted of making it a little more definite, that the consent
of the owner and occupier must be given,
may help us. It does not seem to me to
matter when this consent is given
long as it is given. Therefore, I think we
should add that one and also put in a saving
Clause, but no right of public access which
is already there should be interfered with.
I do not know whether the Committee would
wish to accept this Clause that I am suggesting as it stands. I must freely confess that
it is rather doubtful. I cannot think that
we can accept the expression "arising through
acquiescence of the owner," and I do not
think some of the other difficulties I have
raised have been met. I beg to move.
SO
Colonel CRAWFORD: Before you put the
Question I hope the noble Leader of the
House will make the point clear, because the
case I have already mentioned, the case of
Nendrum, is partly under the county council
and partly under a private individual at
the present time. If the Clause does give
the right of access, probably the county council could get the owner out. There is divided
control. Something should be introduced to
enable them to do so.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In regard to the question asked by
the right hon. and gallant Member, I must
look into the matter of divided control and
give the right hon. Gentleman an answer at
a later date. As regards the Amendment of
the hon. Member, I confess I do not understand it. It does not seem to me to read
correctly. I think the Clause would be unintelligible if this were inserted. I may be
wrong, but so far I do not see what it would
mean. I have tried my best with the information I have got, and the legal advice at my
disposal, and they cannot make out what
actually it would mean. It would be better
if I went into this matter more fully. I
have not had time to consider this Clause,
but I will bring it up again. At the moment
I cannot gather what the Clause means.
Mr. LESLIE: I must, perhaps explain
that the Clause was meant to confer on the
public a definite right of access to a monument, but not to access without the consent
of the owner, but I will gladly avail myself
of the noble Viscount's offer to look further
into the matter.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I understand what the hon. Senator
means, but I think the Amendment could be
better drafted.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 13 (Ancient Monuments Advisory
Committee).
(1) The Minister of Finance shall appoint
the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee, and that Committee shall consist
of members representing the bodies named
in the First Schedule to this Act, and such
other members (of whom the Deputy
Keeper of the Records of Northern Ireland
shall be one) as the said Minister may
appoint.
(2) The Governor of Northern Ireland
may, by Order in Council, alter the First
Schedule to this Act.
(3) The Ancient Monuments Advisory
Committee may, if so requested by the
owner of an ancient monument, give advice,
free of charge, except for out of pocket expenses, with reference to the treatment of
the monument.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 13,
page 8, line 2, to leave out "shall" (shall be
one), and insert "may."
This is a very small matter. The proposal is
that the Deputy Keeper of Records of
Northern Ireland shall always be a member
of the Advisory Committee. It is not usual,
I think, in Acts of Parliament to insert a
definite provision of that sort. I imagine the
proposal was made for two reasons, partly to
obtain the services of the present temporary
Keeper of Records, which can of course be
done without specially mentioning it in the
Bill, and to substitute the Schedule here for
the provision in the English Schedule, that a
member of the Historical Commission on
Ancient Monuments should be included.
It seems to me that if you leave it imperative there might conceivably be a difficulty at some future date, because it might
happen that the Deputy Keeper of Records
might not be the man best fitted to represent the Ministry on the Advisory Committee, and it might happen that the man
best fitted to represent the Ministry might
not be the best man to become Deputy
Keeper of Records. I suggest that a way out
of the difficulty is by leaving out "shall" and
substituting "may."
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The reason the Government draftsman has put in the word "shall" instead of
may, is because the Deputy Keeper of Records
is the one person in Northern Ireland who is
sure to be a member of the Advisory Com-
159 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
mittee, both from an official and an expert
point of view. He is the one who can supply
official information, and if we put in the
word "may" the Ministry may appoint anyone else at their discretion. We desire that
the Deputy Keeper of Records should be the
one person to be appointed.
Mr. LESLIE: The knot the noble Viscount
seems to tie can always be untied, and I ask
leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I beg to
move, in page 8, line 2, after "one" (shall
be one) to insert "being a citizen of Northern
Ireland or Great Britain." The Clause says
the Ministry of Finance shall appoint an
Advisory Committee, and then the Schedule
gives the names of some associations that
are to be represented on that Committee.
Amongst these is the Royal Society of
Antiquarians of Ireland and the Royal Irish
Academy. I think that whatever representative is appointed for these societies should
not be a foreigner. I think there are some
members of these societies in Germany and
I want to prevent a foreigner being appointed a representative on this Committee
by the insertion of the words in the Amendment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am sorry I cannot accept the
Amendment of the right hon. Senator for the
reason that the Ministry of Finance may
desire temporarily to appoint on the Committee some eminent expert who was in
Northern Ireland at the time. The Ministry
might wish him to be a member of this
Committee temporarily, but if we insert the
Amendment we exclude any distinguished
person who is not a citizen of Northern Ireland or Great Britain from giving us the
benefit of his experience and ability. For
that reason I ask the right hon. Gentleman
to withdraw his Amendment.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: After
this explanation, and taking it for granted
that it is not the intention of the Ministry
of Finance to appoint a foreigner on the
Committee except for special reasons, I withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 14 (Expenses of Ministry of
Finance and Advisory Committee).
There shall be paid out of moneys provided by Parliament such expenses as may
be incurred by the Ministry of Finance in
the exercise of its powers in connection
with-
(a) the inspection and maintenance of
ancient monuments, whether vested in
the Ministry of Finance or of which that
Ministry is guardian;
(b) the purchase by agreement of any
ancient monument; and
(c) the payment of compensation for
acts done under this Act involving interference with private rights of property;
and also such expenses of the Ancient
Monuments Advisory Committee as may
be sanctioned by the Ministry of Finance.
(d) the purchase or assistance towards
the purchase of archaeological objects of
which the finding has been reported to
the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee.
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 8, line 17, at end add "or which are
the subject of a Preservation Order."
Line 19, leave out "and."
Line 22, at end add "and (d) the purchase
or assistance towards the purchase of archæological objects."
Lines. 25 to 28, leave out paragraph (d).
Line 28, at end insert the following new
sub-sections:-
"(2) The cost of any conveyances required for the purposes of this Act shall
be paid by the Ministry or the local authority concerned, and in so far as such cost
is payable by the Ministry it shall be paid
out of moneys provided by Parliament.
(3) Any such conveyances shall be exempt from Stamp Duty.
(4) No entertainments duty shall be payable in respect of any charge for admission
which may be imposed by virtue of section
twelve of this Act."
Mr. LESLIE: I think the Amendments
on Clause 14 might better be discussed on
the Report Stage, and I think they might be
withdrawn to-day. That applies to all the
Amendments on Clause 14.
Amendments not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 15 (Advice and superintendence
by Ministry of Finance).
(1) The Ministry of Finance may, if it
thinks fit, give advice with reference to the
treatment of any ancient monument, and
shall, as and when required, give that
advice with reference to the treatment of
any monument which is placed under their
protection by virtue of a Preservation
Order.

(2) The Ministry of Finance may superintend any work in connection with any
ancient monument if invited to do so by

CLAUSE 16 (Power of Councils to make
by-laws regulating Amendments, 7 Edw. 7,

NEW CLAUSES.
The following new Clauses stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
After Clause 16, insert the following new
Clause:-
In this Act, unless the context otherwise
requires, the expression "local authority"
means the council of a county, county of a
city, or of an urban district.
After Clause 16, insert the following new
Clause:-
The expenses of a local authority under
this Act shall be raised in like manner as
that local authority raises funds for the
relief of the poor.
After Clause 16, insert the following new
Clause:-
The expression "the "National Trust"
means the National Trust for places of
historic interest or national beauty incorporated by the National Trust Act, 1907.
After Clause 16, insert the following new
Clause:-
Nothing in this Act shall, without the
consent of the owners and occupiers concerned apply (a) to any structure or monument which appears to the Ministry to be
occupied as a dwelling-house (otherwise
than by a person employed as the caretaken thereof or his family); or
(b) to any monument which has passed
under the provisions of Section Twentyfive of the Irish Church Act, 1869, or
under the provisions of the Irish Land
Act, 1903, or any enactments amending
the same; or
(c) to any premises or monuments
vested in the National Trust.
After Clause 16, insert the following new
Clause:-
Any person guilty of any contravention
of or non-compliance with the provisions
of this Act shall be liable, on conviction
under the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland)
Acts, to a fine not exceeding fifty pounds,
or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three months.
After Clause 16, insert the following new
Clause:-
"Any expenditure to be incurred by a
local authority under this Act shall be
limited to an amount from time to time
approved by the Ministry of Home Affairs,
and shall-
(a) in the case of the council of a county
(not being a county borough) be defrayed
out of the county fund, and be raised by
means of the poor rate over so much of
the county as does not comprise any
urban district; and
(b) in the case of a council of a county
borough or urban district, be defrayed in
the same manner as expenses incurred in
the administration of the Public Health
(Ireland) Acts, 1878 to 1918."
After Clause 16 insert the following new
Clause:-
"In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires, the expression local authority' means the council of a county, county
of a city, or of an urban district, and the
expression 'the National Trust' means the
National Trust for places of Historic Interest or National Beauty, incorporated by
the National Trust Act, 1907."
Mr. LESLIE: The whole of the Amendments on page 6 of the Paper go out, although
I may draw attention to the last proposal on
that page with regard to expenditure incurred by the local authorities. If the Committee had accepted my second or third
Amendment to give certain power to local
authorities, it would have been necessary to
make provision for the expenses of those
authorities. I understand now that it is the
intention to give the local authority power
in some form or other, and I was glad to
notice the right hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel Sharman Crawford) was in
favour of that proposal. It will be necessary
in future to make provision for financing
F

THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
those authorities, but I think the noble
Viscount would prefer that this matter
should not be dealt with to-day. I think the
first Amendment on page 7 had also better
stand over until the local authority is dealt
with.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am obliged to the hon. Member
for the procedure he suggests. It will be
infinitely more suitable.
Amendments not moved.
NEW CLAUSE.
"Nothing in the Act shall, without the
consent of the owner and occupier concerned, apply-
(a) to any structure or monument
which appears to the Ministry to be оссиpied as a dwelling-house (otherwise than
by a person employed as the caretaker
thereof or his family); or
(b) to any church, ecclesiastical building or structure which has been disposed
of under the provisions of sub-section
four of section twenty-five of the Irish
Church Act, 1869; or
(c) to any monument which since the
first day of November, 1903, has become,
or may after the commencement of this
Act become, vested in a purchaser under
the Land Purchase Acts; or
(d) to any premises or monument
vested in the National Trust.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move the insertion
of this new Clause after Clause 16. I am
not sure whether the words "and occupier"
are now necessary, having regard to what
happened to sub-Section 4. There are two
cases where the protection is required. We
must have the protection given in 1913 to the
owner of a dwelling-house. In the Act of
1913 not only are the Commissioners of
Works and the local authority definitely prohibited from acquiring a dwelling-house, but
in the Clause dealing with this matter, and
I think with the Preservation Order, the
dwelling occupied by a person is protected.
It must be obvious, I think, that however
much you interfere with a man's property
without his consent there are rights, and if
a man happens to be living in a house which
is of historic interest unless you put a
saving Clause in you are exposing that man
to interference by the Ministry, and I do
not think Parliament intends to go so far
as that.
Next it is intended to protect any building
which has already been offered to the public
and not accepted, and so passed into private
ownership. The Committee will remember
that the provisions of Section 25 of the Irish
Church Act were that if any church or
ecclesiastical building was not in use for
ecclesiastical purposes and yet if the Commissioners for the purpose of the Irish
Church thought it was worthy of preservation as a national monument, it should be
offered to the Commissioners of Works for
maintenance as a national monument. If the
Commissioners did not accept it it was to be
disposed of as the Commissioners of the Irish
Church thought fit.
It seems to me that we cannot now go back
on any arrangement made there, except with
the definite consent of the man who has become the owner of that church, ecclesiastical
building, or structure. The third case is
likely to be more extensive. In the case of
the Land Purchase Acts-that is to say, those
that have come into operation since 1st
November, 1903, the day when the Wyndham
Act came into operation---it has been provided
by Parliament that if any farm is included
in any estate to be sold, and on that farm
there is anything which, in the opinion of
the Land Commission, ought to be preserved
as an ancient monument, that monument
shall not pass with the farm to the tenant
purchaser, but shall be offered to the Commissioners of Works for maintenance as a
national monument. If they accept the
monument it shall be vested in them, but if
the Commissioners of Works-now the Ministry of Finance-do not accept the monument it shall then be offered to the county
council, and if the county council accept it
it shall then be vested in the county council
Failing those two authorities the monument passed to the man who was the purchaser of the farm. It is impossible to go
back on that arrangement now, however much
local antiquarians may desire to get dominion
over that monument. Under the Land
Purchase Acts the monument has twice been
offered to the public and not accepted.
I say that you cannot consistently with
honouring a bargain go back on that bargain,
except with his consent, and that is the
proposal I have made. As regards the
fourth paragraph of the Clause, that is, "(d)
to any premises or monument vested in the
National Trust," I think after what the
noble Viscount said about the National
Trust, the Committee might prefer that that
should be dropped. Therefore, I only move
the new Clause with paragraphs (a), (b),
and (c).
VISCOUNT MASSERDENE AND FER-
RARD: I am afraid I do not see my way to
accept the hon. Member's Amendment. We
do not consider that there is any necessity to

exempt an inhabited house. It is very
doubtful, indeed, whether there is any inhabited house in Northern Ireland that from
the point of view of this Bill would deserve

think I may say, without any offence to
the hon. Senator that he has an eye more
to Great Britain in this matter than to
Northern Ireland. In this sense we have no
town like Chester, York, or Edinburgh,
where there would be dwelling-houses that
should be preserved. We have no place like
that. In any case, I think we must agree
that habitation is the best form of preservation. As regards the point the hon.
Senator raised as to the Land Purchase Act,
I think it must be quite clear to all of us
if we are to take over an ancient monument
of a tenant or occupier of a farm, for instance, we shall have to pay unless we come
to some agreement with him. We could
hardly do all these things by voluntary
agreement.
If a monument was affered
before, and not accepted, I think it must
be quite clear we could not take it over
without giving some terms to the tenant or
occupier. Therefore, I would ask the hon.
Member if he can see his way to withdraw
the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I greatly dislike to withdraw this Amendment. I did hope I should
have reached it at a time when the Committee was not quite so empty. I think,
perhaps, the noble Viscount, if I understood
correctly, will look into this matter again
and perhaps give us something on Report
to meet the point. I should have mentioned,
though I did not, as to how far, even without this Section, you are empowered to interfere with a monument, having regard to
the limitations of the Government of Ireland
Act. If the noble Viscount will look into
this matter, I would prefer to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 17 (Definition of "monument"
and "ancient monument." 45 and 46 Vic.,
c. 73).
In this Act the expression "monument"
includes any site, structure or erection
(whether above or below the ground) other
than ecclesiastical building which is, for
the time being, used for ecclesiastical purposes; and the expression "ancient monument" includes any monument or part or
remains of a monument, the preservation
of which is a matter of public interest, by
reason of the historic, architectural, traditional, artistic, or archaeological interest
attaching thereto, and the site of any such
monument or of any remains thereof, and
any part of the adjoining land which may
be required for the purpose of fencing,
covering in, or otherwise preserving the
monument from injury, or for the purpose
of preserving the amenities of the monument, and also includes the means of
access thereto.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 14 to
leave out "site" ("including any site, structure, or").
Clause 17 is the definition Clause and it
contains I am afraid, a good many matters
which will require our attention. The first
point which I would direct the attention of
the Committee to is the word "site" in line
14. The word "site" is quite new in this
sense, in the large body of statutes dealing
with ancient monuments. and it is in itself
a
word capable of very great expansion.
For example, when the expression "monument" includes any site we are not given any
idea whatever as to how much is meant.
The word has been put in in order to meet
some case which it was thought would not
otherwise be covered. I would propose in a
later Amendment to the same Clause, to
bring back the reference to the Schedule of
the Ancient Monuments Protection Act, 1882,
which still survives in England. The
Schedule has been kept alive, although the
Act has been repealed. I think it is fairly
plain to all of us that the original intention
was to keep in this Clause the reference to
the Ancient Monuments Act of 1882. I think
that expression would come under the term
"site," which is not, as I say, sufficiently
definite for our purpose. I think I may
move that the word "site" be omitted as
going much further than we desire.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The word "site" we consider to be
important, because, without the word "site"
I do not know that you could include the
ancient cave dwelling, for instance, or a
burying place. The hon. Member, I think,
said "site" is far too general a term and
might lead us to taking over a large tract
of country.
But really, I think the hon.
Member need have no fear on that score.
The Ministry of Finance are not going to go
seeking monuments, or to take up tracts of
country which they shall look after--as the
hon. Member rather seems to be suggesting.
I think he can rest assured that the word
"site" will not be abused in any way. Therefore, I would ask him to withdraw the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I have this difficulty, that
there are not enough Members to divide the
Committee.
The CHAIRMAN: Yes, there are.
Mr. LESLIE: Of course, if the Noble Viscount is adamant on the subject of site, it is
a little difficult to go on with some of the
other Amendments on this Clause. The noble
Viscount used cave as an illustration.
Reference to the Schedule of the Act of 1882,

which is what I want to keep in the Bill,
will show that the cave is definitely included

ground may be of tremendous size, and,
although I suppose, £600 a year might run
to buying few of these large ones, I think
it is rather to large a power to give. However, I do not know, as nobody else has
expressed any view on the matter. Therefore, I will not press this to a division, ir
the noble Viscount really prefers.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 15, to
leave out "ground" ("or below the ground")
and insert "surface." This is to cover the
case where it might be under water.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This is a small matter. We consider that "surface" would meet the point
rather than the word "ground." It is
merely a matter of wording. I am not
wedded to the term. I think "surface"
would meet the point.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Just
slight concession.

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-

Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 18,
after "monument" ("any monument or
part") to insert " irrespective of age." This
is an Amendment which I do not think is
very likely to be accepted. I put it down
in order to get from the Government, if I
can, some definition as to what they expect
or desire the Committee and the public to
understand by the word "ancient."
"Ancient," as we know, is commonly used
simply as the antithesis of "modern," one
word being as vague and as indefinite as
the other. An ancient monument, for this
purpose, was first defined in the Act of 1881,
as I said the other day and it takes us back to
the eleventh century. Then we are carried
on by subsequent Acts until the Middle
Ages, and now, by inserting the expression,
historic, or architectural, or artistic interest,
we have really brought it down to the monu
ment which was erected the week before
last. I do not know whether it will be the
desire of the Government to make any suggestion as to what they understand constitutes ancient, but I think it will be remembered that the term "ancient monument"
is left to the interpretation of the Advisory
Committee and the Ministry itself in the
earlier part of the Bill, and if we can do
anything to guide them, I think we ought
to do so. It is a point for discussion, and 1
beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I do not think the Government can
accept this Amendment. The term "ancient"
is a matter of interpretation. As far as the
British procedure goes I understand that
nothing is ancient after about the year 1740
or 1714. I think that is the rule they adopt
across the water, and while I do not say we
should follow it we will be guided to a certain extent by the experience across the
water.
Mr. LESLIE: I will withdraw the Amendment, but I will make a suggestion on the
Report Stage. There was a precedent established when we made our Records. I think
we made them old from before the year
1700.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in line 18,
after "monument" (includes any monument) to insert "specified in the Schedule
to the Ancient Monuments Protection Act,
1882, and any other monuments or things

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave out
from the beginning of line 26 to "and" in
line 27.
This is a more serious matter because here
again you touch the private property of the
owner. It is a new proposal altogether, and
does not appear in the English Act. The
words that ought to come out are altogether
too vague. How far do you want to go? Who
is to be the authority as to whether the
amenities of a monument are in danger or
not? The words in the Section are terribly
vague, because I am afraid we will have to
remember that it is possible a Preservation
Order might be made in respect of this. I
think it is going much further than anything
done across the water. I beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I think the word "amenities" is
really important. The hon. Member says it
has too wide a significance, but there might
be cases where that expression might be
very useful. It is quite true the term has
not been used before, but I do not think
the hon. Member need fear that any harm
will come from the use of those words.
"
Mr. LESLIE: It is not a question of amenities; it is a question of area. It says any
part," etc. We are not told who is to

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in line 27.

other."
"
This again is a question of access. The
expression is exactly as it appears in the
English Act of 1913, and it still leaves us
in doubt as to the access provided in the
case of monuments which have passed under
the Land Purchase Acts. It seems to me
that we ought to clear that up in some
way; otherwise sooner or later somebody
will be landed in a law suit, and it is not
desirable that our Acts should not be plain.
As it stands I am not satisfied that anything to which the Land Purchase Acts
apply is covered by the access provided in
this Bill. I suggest we should make our
meaning clear, by inserting such words as
these.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I do not think these words are
necessary, but I will look into the matter
further.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

McCALDIN'S DIVORCE BILL.
Brought from the Commons.
Adjournment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD. In rising to move that this House
do now adjourn, I have to inform the House
that owing to the serious state of affairs in
Great Britain, the Government are introducing an Emergency Powers Bill tomorrow, which they desire to pass through
all its stages on that day. Therefore, I must
crave the indulgence of the Senate to suspend Standing Orders, in order to pass this
Bill through the House to-morrow.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Six
o'clock.

Property Purchased with Public Funds.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Senator Samuel
Cunningham.
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government will introduce legislation
by which the dimensions, terms, and prices
of all property purchased with public funds
must be gazetted before the title is completed.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I ask the
Question which stands in my name on the
ground of recent events which are well
known to all the inhabitants of Northern Ireland. I think it would be a great
advantage if we knew at once what property had been bought, so that, if necessary,
a protest could be entered against any misappropriation of public money. Yesterday,
on another question, I had the assurance
that the Government were considering these
points, and I hope also to receive the assurance that this special point with regard to
the purchase of property will receive the
consideration of the Government, and that
they will deal with it in the Bill they propose to bring before Parliament.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD. In reply to the right hon. Gentleman, I understand that the procedure suggested by him relates in the main to proposals for purchase of land, buildings, etc.,
In the first place the meetings of local
authorities at which any such proposals
would be discussed are usually open to the
public, and their proceedings are generally
reported in the public Press.
In the second place, in every case in which
a local authority proposes to purchase prop
erty, that proposal usually comes before the
central authority concerned in the shape of
an application for sanction to the borrowing
of the sums necessary to make the purchase,
and in all such cases, except under exceрtional circumstances, the application forms
the subject of a Public Inquiry, held by the
officers of the Ministry concerned. At that
Inquiry it is customary for the authority
applying for sanction to the loan to be called
upon to explain publicly all details in com
nection with the works they propose to
embark on, including full particulars as to
any lands or buildings which it is proposed
to acquire.
In all the circumstances, therefore, it is
not considered that any further or essential
safeguards would be provided or secured by
making it a condition that particulars of
the works, including particulars of lands,
etc., to be acquired by a local authority,
should be published in the official Gazette,
a document which is not read, as a rule,
by the general body of ratepayers.
As the hon. Senator may also have in his
mind procedure in connection with purchases of property by Government Departments, I may point out that it is almost the
invariable practice that no new work or purchase of land for State purposes is embarked
on until an opportunity has been afforded
for debate in another place by the inclusion
of the necessary estimated money provision
in the Estimates for the services of the year
with an indication, if practicable, of the
property proposed to be acquired. It has
on rare occasions happened that by reason
of urgency the Ministry of Finance has
sanctioned a purchase not provided for in
the Estimates, but in such circumstances,
if the amount involved is material, it is
the practice to bring the matter to the
notice of Parliament by means of a Supplementary Estimate.
Furthermore, in the Appropriation Accounts of the expenditure of the preceding
year full details are given of all purchases
Vising of Passports.

Comptroller and Auditor-General not be

and the Accounting Officer of the Vote will

action of his Department. I am sure, therefore, that the hon. Senator will agree that
as regards purchases by the State ample
publicity is at present given.

Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:

Afforestation in Northern Ireland.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Samuel Cunningham:-
as
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government has taken or intends to
take, any steps to carry out the recommendation
to afforestation submitted
on 3rd December, 1924, by the Commission
on the Natural and Industrial Resources of
Northern Ireland, in its Report, which
stressed the urgent necessity for action
by the State; whether since the supply
of home-grown timber is steadily declining,
they will adopt the comprehensive scheme
of afforestation for Northern Ireland contained in the Development Commission
Report, and make adequate monetary provision for carrying out the same, in order
ultimately to ensure to this country its
own supply of soft woods; and whether
they will take steps to ascertain where,
in Northern Ireland areas which are suitable for afforestation and which could be
leased, can be found such as are mentioned
in paragraph 12 of the Commission Report,
particularly in the Antrim Hills, the
Sperrin Mountains and the Mourne
Mountains.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: The second
question which stands in my name on the
Order Paper is one to which I wish to call
the attention of the House. At this time,
when there is so much unemployment, I
think it would be a great advantage if a
scheme such as that suggested in the Report
of the Development Commission could be
carried out. It would take men off the dole
and give them useful employment. During
the past few days we have seen what can
happen in regard to supplies, and if there
was another war like the last I think it
would be a great advantage to have more
home-grown timber. I hope the Government
will be in a position to give us a satisfactory
reply as to whether or not they are likely
to carry out the recommendations of the
Development Commission on this subject.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I regret that the answer I have to
give to the right hon. Gentleman is not as
full as I should like to give him, and it
may not altogether satisfy him. In answer
to his question I have to say that the recommendations as to afforestation submitted
by the Commission on the Natural and Industrial Resources of Northern Ireland are
still under consideration. Information as to
land suitable for forestry purposes continues
to be collected by the Ministry of Agriculture
and will be available for consideration when
additional funds for the acquisition of extra
land for forestry purposes are available.
Vising of Passports at Londonderry Port
The following question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Long:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
it is a fact that persons sailing from the
United States direct to the port of Londonderry are required to have their passports
visaed by an official of the Irish Free State
at the port of embarkation, and pay a fee
of ten dollars, and, if so, whether the
Northern Government acquiesce in this
arrangement.
Mr. LONG: I do not intend to ask the
question standing in my name at the present
time, and I ask permission to withdraw it.
