CLAUSES 1 (Power to grant right to work
minerals which would otherwise be unworkable), and 2 (Power to grant right to work
minerals in case of agreement as to adjustment of boundaries between two mines),
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Power to grant ancillary
rights for facilitating the working of
minerals).
a
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In the debate on the Second Reading of this Bill, Senator Leslie raised
question as to what would happen in the
event of waste water from mines flowing into
streams. As this matter is referred to in
this Clause, I should like to inform the hon.
Member, that with regard to his question as
to the effect upon fishing interests of rights
to dispose of water, or other liquid matter,
obtained from mines being granted under
Section 1 of this Clause, I think we may
assume that the Tribunal would have regard
to the nature of the water to be disposed of,
and to the character of the stream into which
it was to be carried, before granting any
such rights. Also, I may explain that
Section 80 of the Fishery (Ireland) Act,
1842, prohibits the throwing, emptying, or
release into any river, lake, or portion of
tidal waters any dye-stuffs or other deleterious or poisonous liquid. This, I think,
covers the point the hon. Senator raised.
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the noble
Viscount for so kindly redeeming his promise, and for telling us the result of his
investigations. I feel quite sure that the
Section of the Fishery Act to which the noble
Viscount referred will, coupled with reasonable intelligence on the part of the Tribunal,
prevent any possible difficulty in this connection.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 4 (Limitation on power of
granting rights), 5 (Applications for rights),
6 (Power of Ministry of Commerce to grant
rights after reference to the Tribunal), and
7 (Provisions in case of several applications
in respect of the same rights), ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Restrictions on
minerals required for support).
working
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I should like to take this opportunity of informing Senator Leslie, who on
the Second Reading raised the question of
the protection of lateral support, that if he
will read Clause 8 of the Bill he will see
that this Clause gives power to impose restrictions upon the working of minerals required for the support of buildings or works,
and this support includes both vertical and
lateral support, as the text of the Clause
states.
Mr. LESLIE: Again I am obliged to the
noble Viscount. My question was really more
directed to secure lateral support for railways, but, as the noble Viscount told us the
other day, railways are not covered by this
Bill.
an assessor

(1) As from the first day of April, nineteen hundred and twenty-six, the schedule
to the Pensions (Increase) Act, 1920 (in
this Act referred to as "the principal Act")
shall, as respects pensions not exceeding
one hundred pounds payable out of the
Petty Sessions Clerks Fund, or out of the
Teachers' Pension Fund, have effect as if
the percentages by which existing pensions
may be increased were-
*

*
Mr. DICKSON: I beg to move in Clause
2, page 2, lines 16 and 17, to leave out
the words "out of the Petty Sessions
Clerks' Fund, or." I do not move this
Amendment with the object of affecting the
Petty Sessions clerks who are employed full
time, such as those in the City of Belfast
and in some other places, but I refer particularly to country districts where the
Petty Sessions only sit once a month, and
where the Petty Sessions clerk is occupied
perhaps for one hour a month, or, if he has
two districts,
couple of hours
a
in
the month. Why this man's pension
should be increased is more than I can
understand, any more than I can understand
why he should get a pension at all.
I do not think the country can afford to
increase the pensions of men who are not
full time officers, and are only occupied for
an hour or two in the month on these
duties. I really think it is absurd to
suggest increasing the pensions of such men.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The effect of the Amendment proposed by the hon. Member would be to
exclude Petty Sessions clerk pensioners from
the scope of the Bill. The hon. Senator
has another Amendment on the same subject. He states he has proposed the Amendment on the ground that these clerks are
almost all part time officials, and that,
therefore, they should not be given pension
rights. But this would be quite contrary
to the policy of previous Acts of Parliament.
While they are part time officials their
salaries are calculated on a part time basis
and are regulated by the amount of work
they perform in each district. The salaries
are not large, and as the persons appointed
are usually of middle age they are not
likely to earn large pensions owing to the
comparatively small number of years they
can serve.
The effect of this Bill, 60 far as the
Petty Sessions clerks in Northern Ireland
are concerned, is very small; I might almost say trifling. Only two cases will be
affected, and the amount of the increase will
be small, as in both cases the existing
pensions are small, and the Bill would only
give an increase of about 10 per cent. In
these circumstances I hope the hon. Member
will not press his Amendment
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I support the Amendment, and I quite agree with
what Senator Hill Dickson has said. 1
know of my own knowledge what very little
work some Petty Sessions clerks have to do.
I know of one case where the Petty Sessions
sits only once a month, and sometimes there
is no business at all and no magistrates
attend. The whole thing is ridiculous,
and I hope the Government will make some
differentiation between the full time Petty
Sessions clerks, who are entitled to a pension, and those in country districts to whom
I am referring. It would be a great injustice if increased pensions were given in
these cases. I hope the hon. Senator will
press the matter to a Division.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: My only reply to the hon. Senator
is what I have already stated.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Is it
not possible that some difference can be
made in the Bill between those Petty Sessions clerks who are fully employed and
those who are not? Some of these clerks
are entitled to the increased pension, but
a large proportion are not, and if some
differentiation could be made it would get
over the difficulty.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I will look into the matter and
see if we can bring up something on the
Report Stage.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Mr. GLENDINNING: Am I in order in
asking a question with regard to this? I am
informed that this Bill is confined in its effect
to pensioners who retired previous to 1914pre-war pensioners. I should like to know if
the primary school teachers who retired between 1914 and 1920 are affected by this Bill
and if so in what way.

Mr. GLENDINNING: Thank you very
much.

Ancient Monuments Bill.
House again in Committee (according to
Order).
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 18 (Finding of archæological
articles)-
(1) The finder of any archæological
object shall, within fourteen days of such
finding, report the finding and character of
the object and the circumstances of the
finding to the Ancient Monuments Advisory
Committee.
(2) Any person, being a finder of an
archaeological object, who fails to report
the finding of such object as provided by
sub-section (1) of this section or who refuses to give information or gives false information of the character of the object
found, or of the circumstances of the finding, to the Ancient Monuments Advisory
Committee or to such person or persons as
may be prescribed by the Ministry of
Finance shall be liable on conviction under
the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts
to a fine not exceeding five pounds, or to
imprisonment for a term not exceeding one
month.
(3) In this section the expressior
"archæological object" means an object
being a chattel whether in a manufactured
or unmanufactured state, which, by reason
of its archæological or historic interest, has
a value substantially greater than its in
trinsic value or the value of the materials
of which it is composed.
(4) The Ministry of Finance may, on the
recommendation of the Ancient Monuments
Advisory Committee, purchase or give
assistance towards the purchase of archaeological objects, the finding of which has
been reported under the section, and such
objects when purchased shall be deposited
for public exhibition in such places and
subject to such conditions, as the Ministry
may direct.

the
The following Amendment stood on
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 9, line 29, to leave out "shall, within
fourteen days of such finding" and insert
"may."
Mr. LESLIE: I think it would perhaps
make for convenience if I ask leave to alter
slightly the first Amendment standing in my
name, so that for the moment I may confine
myself to the Motion to leave out the word
"shall" instead of proceeding further. I
think that it is not impossible that the main
part of our discussion of this Clause can be
bound by our decision on that suitable substitution. The object of taking out the word
"shall "
The CHAIRMAN: May I ask is the hon.
Member leaving out the remainder of the
Amendment to insert "may"?
Mr. LESLIE: I was proposing to leave
out these words for the moment. I should
necessarily have to move to insert may"
afterwards. The object of the proposal is
of course to render the whole Section
Clause permissive instead of as it is at present subject to a penalty. The Committee
will remember that this Clause, according to
notice given to the public by means of the
Press, was deliberately brought forth
in order to ascertain how public feeling regarded such an innovation in our general
law. To require that any individual should
be required under penalty to report the finding of something which was not treasure
trove, and not, therefore prima facie the
property of the Crown, but was simply some
object possibly of interest to antiquaries or
the reverse-there is no precedent that I
know of for a Clause of this kind. But there
Committee.
Ancient Monuments Bill-

is no reason whatever why if it really is desired to make it so that this House should
not take the power. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) The question is then whether we are
entitled to add another to the already large
list of offences dealt with by criminal law,
and whether there is sufficient justification
for doing it in this case. After all who is it
that takes any particular interest in the preservation of any such articles as those with
which this Clause purports to deal? It is
only, I think, a very small section of the
commuity.
Colonel CRAWFORD: No, no.
Mr. LESLIE: I repeat that I think it is
only a very small section of the community.
Colonel CRAWFORD: No.
Mr. LESLIE: And I have yet to learn that
it is in accordance with our Constitution
that the very small minority should be put
over the vast majority. There is not the
least reason why anybody should not proceed
to study archæology if they wish to do so.
The only point is they should not endeavour
to impose upon others the task of assisting
them in the cultivation of their particular
taste. Passing from that is it gravely
thought that in this twentieth century if you
want to get help from the people of Northern
Ireland you can only get it by exposing them
to the risk of fine and imprisonment if they
do not help? I confess I have myself much
higher confidence in our people than that
course of action would suggest. I believe if
you want articles of archæological interest
preserved, the right thing to do is to endeavour to get help in preserving them by
cultivating the spirit of politeness, and by
lectures in towns and villages, but do not
bring in a new penal Clause without further
justification. Moreover, if you do insert a
penal Clause in the Act it is generally desirable that you should so arrange matters
that there is some chance of your desires becoming effective. I think it is fairly plain
that although these penalties may be inserted
in the Bill, the chance of securing conviction
is remarkably small. That is perhaps rather
a side issue, and I need not dwell upon it.
I understand this Clause may be of some use
to antiquaries if it were made permissive instead of treating it as a compulsory penal
Clause. I object to its inclusion. Accordingly, as a first step, to render it permissive,
I beg to move that the word "shall" be left
out and the word "may" inserted.
Colonel CRAWFORD: I am sorry I am at
a complete difference with him in every point
the hon. Senator has made in his speech. I
am under the impression-I will probably
be corrected afterwards-I am not certain of
that-but my opinion of this is that he is
quite wrong in the statement he made about
few people being interested in the discovery
and preservation of things which have lasted
for many years, the destruction of which is
such a disgrace to the country. Instead of
finding a minority, I think in this House you
will find a large majority. If this is put to
a vote it will be proved that the Committee
is entirely against him. In my humble
opinion taking out the word "shall," and
putting in the word "may," would destroy
the whole value of this Bill. We need not
be afraid of innocent persons being punished.
I am sure most people on whose land these
things may be found would only be too glad
to assist in every way, and it is only against
those who have no right to them that action
would be brought, and who would be rightly
punished. I am sure if I were on the Bench
when they were brought forward they would
be very heavily punished. I think the hon
Senator is quite wrong in his conclusions.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to support the retention of the word "shall" instead of the
proposed Amendment. I think I would be
right in saying most of these objects of
archæological interest are invariably discovered, not by the owners of the property
but by citizens interested in the preservation
of such objects in the interests of the community in the future. Circumstances might
arise that a member of one of these societies
mentioned for representation on the Committee to advise the Minister might discover
on certain property indications of a valuable
relic and if the word "shall" is taken out
here, it would debar him from restraining
the proprietor from proceeding to destroy
what he considered his property. In the
public interest, therefore, the removal of the
word "shall" would be more or less against
the object of the Bill. Therefore, I support
the retention of the word "shall."
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The speeches on this Amendment
made by the right hon. and gallant Senator,
Colonel Sharman Crawford, and the hon.
Senator Dorman really leave me very little
to say. They fully answered the speech of
the hon. Senator Leslie. But the hon. Senator Leslie, I gather, feels very strongly on
this matter, and thinks that "shall" is a
great infringement of private rights. After
all, what does this Clause do? It lays a very
trifling restraint on the liberty of the finder.
It merely delays the sale until he has reported, and it may, so it can be argued, do
a favour to the finder. What does it do? It
ensures that if he reports the matter he gets
expert information as to the value of the
find, and may indicate possible purchasers at
a fair value. Also, I may say, that this
Clause is not going to injure any innocent
person. There is an Amendment which
stands in my name, which comes on later, on
which I can raise that point. It is quite
183 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
true if the word "may" is inserted instead of
"shall" it will absolutely destroy the whole
value of the Clause as the hon. and gallant
Senator says. Therefore, on behalf of the
Government, I resist the Amendment.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I wish to
ask, just for information, if I find an Edward
III. or Edward I. coin would I be bound
under that Clause to proceed within fourteen
days to inform the Advisory Committee about
it. It is just for information I want to ask
that.
Colonel CRAWFORD: Under the law at
present you are.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The right hon. Senator raised the
question of treasure trove. Treasure trove is
gold or silver bullion or coins, and under
the present law the right hon. Senator would
have to report that matter. But as regards
treasure trove, perhaps I may not be out of
order in stating that an arrangement is being
come to with the Crown to the effect that in
respect of treasure trove in Northern Ireland
the Crown is prepared to waive its rights on
condition that the treasure trove is deposited
in the care of a public body or museum in
Northern Ireland. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear).
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I would
like to thank the Leader of the House for
the information. I was under the impression
that treasure trove had to be gold. Treasury
notes are not treasure trove. (Laughter).
Mr. LESLIE: There seem to be one or two
small matters left for me to do. I must
apologise to the noble Viscount for not quite
catching what he said about gold and silver
coins. Secondly, as regards the suggestion
of, I think, the right hon. and gallant Gentleman that these alterations would upset the
whole balance of the Bill, I must point out
that this Clause is dealing not with ancient
monuments but with archæological objects.
The provisions of the Bill applicable to
ancient monuments are not necessarily applicable to archæological objects.
Colonel CRAWFORD: That is a mere
quibble.
Mr. LESLIE: The hon. and gallant Gentleman says it is a mere quibble. I hope, as
we are all allowed to speak more than once,
he will elaborate that for us. I think also
that somewhat the same point was raised by
hon. Senator Dorman. I might point out
that he seemed to be under the impression
that it was the person to whom the object
was due who was under the liability under
the Act. The person to whom any object may
be passed is, I am told, not the finder. The
only person to whom the Clause of the Bill
would appear to apply is the person who
actually finds the object. I do not know
really what course the noble Viscount desires
to take on this matter, whether for the sake
of obtaining public interest through the newspapers he wishes to divide. Perhaps he would
tell us. I have not received sufficient support
from others.
The CHAIRMAN: I think the question
whether the Committee divides would not
rest with the noble Viscount. It is entirely
with the hon. Member.
Mr. LESLIE: I must apologise for making
the discussion so conversational. That being
so, I think I had better withdraw and leavo
the defence of the liberties of the people to
the popularly elected Chamber to whom this
Bill will have to go.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Line 32, to leave out from the beginning of
the line to "or" in line 39.
Amendment not moved.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move on page 9, line 34,
after "who" ("object, who fails to") to insert
"without reasonable cause." The object of
this Amendment is to allow some latitude,
to give a certain amount of freedom where an
individual may have some object which may
be of archæological interest, but from ignorance he may not be aware of the fact. We
do not want to penalise any innocent person.
We consider the words "without reasonable
cause" will fully cover this. I beg to move.

Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 9, line 40, to leave out from
"Ministry" to the end of line 3 of page 10.
Page 10, line 4, to leave out "Section" and
insert "Act."
Line 7, to leave out "has" and insert
"appears to have."
Line 11, to leave out from "may" to "purchase" in line 13.
Amendments not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 13,
after "purchase" ("Committee, purchase
or") to insert by agreement." The object
of bringing that Amendment before the Committee is for the sake of symmetry, if I may
use the expression, with Clause 3 of the Bill.
I do not think it requires a speech. It is
obvious that if you allow the Ministry of
Finance to acquire an ancient monument by
purchase or agreement under Clause 3, and
if you are going to allow the Ministry to purchase an archæological object under Clause
18, it would be desirable to have purchase by
agreement in each case. I beg to move.
Ancient Monuments Bill-

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This is a matter on which I do not
really mind very much one way or the other.
The Government consider the words "by
agreement" are really unnecessary, but if the
hon. Member insists I will agree.
Mr. LESLIE: I thank the noble Viscount.
I would not ask to insert it only for that
reason.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am prepared to accept the Amendment if the hon. Member wishes it.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, page 10, line 18, at
end (end of Sub-section (4)) to add the following new Sub-sections :-
(5) The Ministry of Finance may make
regulations-
(a) prescribing any officer of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary as the person to
whom reports are to be made or other
information given with respect to archæological objects;
(b) prescribing the body or bodies in
whose custod archæological objects may
be deposited; and
(c) generally for carrying this section
into effect.
(6) Nothing in this section shall be construed as affecting treasure trove, provided
that regulations under this section may be
made so as to apply to any archæological
object which is treasure trove, if the rights
of the Crown in such object have been
waived.
The object of this sub-Section is really to
provide machinery for carrying the provisions into effect. We prescribe an officer of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary as the person
to whom reports are to be made. The Ministry of Finance can make regulations about
the body or bodies into whose custody archæological objects may be deposited, and generally carrying this section into effect. Perhaps
I should mention the reason why we put in
the Royal Ulster Constabulary. It is in
deference to a recommendation from the
other side in regard to treasure trove. It
was thought there that an officer of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary should be the proper
person to receive it. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Samuel
Cunningham:-
Page 10, line 18, at end to add the following new sub-section :-
(5) Property held under this Act will be
exempt from taxes and rates.
Amendment not moved.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSES 19 (Repeal) and 20 (Short title)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
FIRST SCHEDULE.
The Ministry of Education for Northern
Ireland.
The Queen's University, Belfast.
The Royal Irish Academy.
The Royal Institute of British Architects.
The Royal Society of Antiquaries of Ireland.
The Belfast Natural History and Philosophical Society.
The Belfast Naturalists' Field Club.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, page 11, line
2, to insert "Trinity College, Dublin." As
I mentioned the other day it is a new idea
in schedules of this sort to give university
representation. University representation was
not given in England prior to 1913. Such representation is given in the National Trust
Act dealing with the constitution of the
council of the Trust. And if we are to
decide to give university representation in
Northern Ireland, to which I do not presume
to offer any objection, it seems to me that if
we include Queen's University, Belfast, we
cannot refrain from including also the old
University of Trinity College, Dublin, which
has so many distinguished representatives
in Northern Ireland. Accordingly, I suggest
we should add after Ministry of Education
for Northern Ireland the words "Trinity
College, Dublin."
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I fully appreciate the motives which
caused Senator Leslie to wish to include
Trinity College in the Schedule of this
Bill. But I might point out if we include
Trinity College, a college outside the jurisdiction of Northern Ireland, it might raise
an awkward precedent with regard to including on some future day in some future Bill
other institutions in the Free State. I might
mention that it is practically certain that
there will be a certain graduate of Trinity
College on the Advisory Committee. For that
reason on behalf of the Government I resist
the Amendment.
Colonel CRAWFORD: Perhaps, as Trinity
College has been mentioned, I might say that
I, unfortunately, on account of age, am one of
the senior graduates of Trinity College in
this part of the world, and as representing
the rest of the graduates here I might say
they would not care at all to have this Clause
put in, and would be quite satisfied with
what the Leader of the House suggested, representation on the Advisory Committee. The
graduates of Trinity College here would not
care for it.
187 Ancient Monuments Bill-
Committee A 188
THE SENATE
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 7, to
leave out "The Belfast Naturalists' Field
Club" and insert the "National Trust, The
Corporation of the City of Londonderry, The
Association of Ulster County Councils." This
Amendment is on somewhat different lines.
We have just declined to give representation
to Trinity College, Dublin, but it is given to
the Royal Irish Academy and the Royal
Society of Antiquaries and so on. The point
I do suggest is that there is no precedent that
I know of for including such an institution
as the Belfast Naturalists' Field Club in the
schedule of an Act of Parliament. In the
Bill before us we are providing an Advisory
Committee to advise the Ministry on the
question whether this or that monument is of
national importance. I am afraid I cannot
see that the inclusion of this Belfast Club
would help to inspire public confidence in
that Committee. I assume that the Belfast
Naturalists' Field Club differs from the
Natural History and Philosophical Society
in that it has nothing in the shape of a
Charter, and I also assume that the
membership of this Field Club is not entirely
under its control. I confess I cannot see any
reason for including this Field Club in the
First Schedule, and accordingly for the sake
of discussion, I beg to move its omission.
Before sitting down I should like, if I may,
to say how pleased I am to see the Minister
in charge of the Bill paying his first visit
to the Senate. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Mr. DORMAN: I do not know what reason
animated the Government when they put the
Belfast Naturalists' Field Club in the Bill,
but I presume it was mainly because the
Belfast Naturalists' Field Club would be regarded as pioneers in this work and as being
a body of individuals interested in the antiquities in Northern Ireland, and possibly
they would be the means of discovering most
of the monuments which are worthy of being
preserved by the Government for the people
as a whole. I think it would be a great
mistake to obliterate such a body as this
which is a voluntary society, and because of
their enthusiasm in the interests of ancient
monuments devote their own time to research.
In my judgment they would be a very valuable body to co-operate with the Government
in the preservation of ancient monuments
in the future.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I should like to support what Senator Dorman has said. It does
not seem to me to be of much consequence
whether the body has a Charter or not. The
Belfast Naturalists' Field Club has been in
existence a very long time and has done valuable work. It is a very active body indeed,
and as Senator Dorman pointed out, it is
very likely to make discoveries of interesting
monuments, and it should not be disqualified
on the mere technical point of having no
Charter. I therefore hope the Committee
will allow the name of the Belfast
Naturalists' Field Club to remain in the
Schedule.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I think it is the feeling of the Committee that the name of the Belfast
Naturalists' Field Club should remain in
the First Schedule, and with every word that
has been spoken by Senator Dorman and
Senator Colonel Sinclair I am in entire agreement.
As a matter of fact, the Belfast
Naturalists' Field Club has got an anti
quarian and archæological section which is
doing very useful work, and they were consulted to some extent with reference to this
Bill. To say the least it would be very ungracious on our part now to object to their
inclusion in this Schedule.
They will be
very useful on the Advisory Committee, and
for these reasons the Government desire to
retain their name in the First Schedule.
Mr. LESLIE: After what has been said
by the noble Viscount and Senator Colonel
Sinclair, I ask leave to withdraw the Amendment, and I do not move the insertion of the
other words.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
First Schedule agreed to.
SECOND SCHEDULE.
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Item 1, column 3, add "except the Schedule."
Item 3, leave out the reference.
After item 4 insert "7 Edwd. 7, ch. cxxxvi,
The National Trust Act, 1907, section 35."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not propose to move
any of the Amendments standing in my name
on the Second Schedule, because they are
consequential on other matters, but I would
like to ask a question as regards the Ancient
Monuments Protection Act of 1892. Will the
noble Viscount say what will happen under
this Bill to Section 2 of that Act? Section 2
was the Section that provided that moneys
from the Church Temporalities Account
might be used for the maintenance of monuments which had passed under the Irish
Church Act, but the Committee will
member that there was some alteration made
with regard to the method of accounting for
Church Temporalities receipts. I confess 1
do not know what will happen if we repeal
the whole of that Act, and being anxious,
as I am sure hon. Members will realise, to
obtain as much money as possible for the
maintenance of such ancient monuments as
may be ultimately decided upon, I am loath
to give up any money if it happens to be
available.

Stage.

Ordered: That the Bill be reported with

Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendments.
Ordered: That the Report of the said
Amendments be received on Wednesday next,
and that the Bill be printed as amended.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Emergency
Powers Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
Emergency Powers Bill.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, That this Bill be now
read a First time.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Before
the Motion is put, I should like to point out
that we have not even got a copy of the Bill
before us, and if copies of the Bill have been
available in the other House I think this
House should have been treated with a little
more courtesy.
Mr. SPEAKER: I understand that Bills
are coming down in a few minutes, but as a
matter of fact on the First Reading it is not
necessary to have a print of the Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I am
satisfied so long as the Bill will be before us
for the Second Reading.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I would ask the Minister of Commerce to move the Second Reading of this
Bill. I think this is the first time the right
hon. Gentleman has been in this House and
we welcome him very heartily. I am sure
we will listen with great interest to the
speech he will make on the Second Reading
of this Bill, and we hope to see him here on
future occasions.
The MINISTER OF COMMERCE (Mr.
Barbour): In response to a question that was
asked in another place yesterday afternoon
I announced the Government's intention of
seeking powers to protect the interests of the
community owing to the conditions which
have arisen in consequence of the very serious
strike which has broken out on the other
side of the Channel. The Government here
felt it was necessary to immediately take
such steps as might seem to them desirable
in order to protect the interests of the community generally, and especially of those
who, through lack of financial means, are
least able to face the hardships which an
upheaval such as is occurring across the
Channel inevitably brings in its train even
to us who are at this comparatively remote
distance.
The peculiar feature of the present
position is that the difficulties which face
us do not arise through any industrial dispute or disagreement within our own borders,
where, I am glad to say, amicable relations
continue in every section of industry. Unfortunately, however, our sources of coal and
certain imported foodstuffs have, for the
time being, been seriously interfered with,
and, although the general public throughout
Northern Ireland has taken the position
calmly, and risen to the occasion, there has
been a tendency on the part of some to
endeavour to secure as large stocks of supplies as possible, regardless of the interests
of the general community.
There are in our midst a number whose
means of subsistence are not such as to enable them to take thought for the morrow
by laying in adequate stocks of fuel and food
while these were freely available. These
less fortunate members of the community
must be protected, not only against any
difficulty in securing their daily needs, which
would be likely to arise from panic purchases on an unnecessarily large scale by
those who can afford to do so, but also so
far as this is practicable by means of regulation against any unwarranted increase
in prices of essential commodities.
Turning to the Measure, which is now in
the hands of hon. Members, I hope hon.
Members will forgive me the brief time given
for its consideration. They will realise that
if the steps we have in view are to be a
success they must be taken now. They will
eventually give the Government very ample
powers, and it is axiomatic that only the
existence of very exceptional circumstances
would justify the Government asking for
powers to intervene in the general commercial life of the community.
a
In 1920 the Imperial Parliament enacted
a Measure entitled the Emergency Powers
Act, to deal with
situation somewhat
similar to that which faces us to-day. That
Act we have adapted to local conditions.
Stated briefly, the Bill empowers the
Governor, when satisfied that a state of
emergency has arisen, to issue a proclama-
191 Emergency Powers Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[The Minister of Commerce (Mr. Barbour)]
tion that such emergency exists, and thereafter to make regulations for securing the
essentials of life to the community and for
regulating the supply and distribution of
food and other necessities.
I would like to draw the attention of hon.
Members to an addition that was made at
the last moment in another place. It comes
in in line 19, and I am sorry time did not
permit the addition being made in the copies
of the Bill circulated in this House. I am
glad to see it has been made in some of the
copies, but to ensure that it will come before
all Members of this House I will read the
addition that has been made, and which was
taken from the British Act. It was thought
when first framing the Bill that it would
not be necessary to insert this particular
proviso here, as it merely repeats a right that
is already secured, to those who wish to take
advantage of it, by legislation. To ensure
that their rights under the Trades Disputes
Act with regard to peaceful picketing shall
not be interfered with by any Regulations
that may be made under this Act it is repeated here that those powers are not overridden by the Emergency Powers Bill, the
Second Reading of which I am now moving.
In line 19 there have been inserted these
words:-
Provided that no such Regulation shall
make it an offence for any person or persons to take part in a strike or peacefully
to persuade any other person or persons
to take part in a strike.
Before sitting down I should like to take
advantage of this opportunity of appealing
to the people of Ulster to take to heart the
appeal of the Prime Minister of Great Britain
that the community at large should bear
with fortitude and resolution any inconveniences which they may be called upon to
face, and so facilitate and support the efforts
that the Government is making to safeguard
the supply of the essentials of life for all
classes of the community. I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to support the
Second Reading of this Measure, and to
thank the Government for inserting the
Amendment suggested in another place. I
am sure we all regret the introduction of
strife, and especially of strife which is calculated to interfere with the normal carrying
on of business. In this connection there
comes to my mind a remarkable statement
made by a celebrated Irishman who was an
authority on the subject of legislation. He
said Government was a device of human
wisdom for the supply of human needs. It
appears to me that this Measure is calculated
to enable the Government to function in
regard to the preservation of the supply of
the needs of the people inside the area of
Northern Ireland, regardless of what may be
taking place outside.
It is unfortunate that these circumstances
should arise, but they have arisen from
causes that I think authorities in the past
have recognised as being more or less justified. One of the most celebrated economists
of the last century stated that the example
of any individual or body of individuals who
willingly consent to a reduction in their
wages or in their standard of comfort should
never be held up for public approbation,
but everything that could be done should be
done to make such conduct become disgraceful in the interests of the community as a
whole. I make this statement, because I think
there is a tendency in the community to cast
all the blame on one of the parties engaged
in this particular strife, and to exonerate the
other and most powerful party from all
blame. The standard of comfort of the
miners will eventually be considerably reduced. I will not give hon. Senators exact
particulars, but I can assure them that in
the vast majority of mining districts in
Great Britain-
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: On a point of order, has a dissert
ation on the standard of living of the miners
anything to do with this Bill?
Mr. SPEAKER: A little wider latitude is
given on a Second Reading, and I propose to
allow the hon. Member to proceed.
Mr. DORMAN: My object was to show
that the miners are not absolutely to blame.
because, as I understand the situation across
the water, they have consented to enter into
negotiations provided that the undertaking
suggested in the Report of the Mines Commission is carried out. I think this Bill is
necessary, because only on Monday night 1
was in a more fortunate suburban area of
this city, and I discovered coal in large quantities being delivered at 9-30 and 10 p.m.,
which shows that steps were necessary on the
part of the Government to prevent the more
fortunate section of the community depriving
their poorer neighbours of some of the necessaries and comforts of life. I thank the
Government for accepting the Amendment in
another place, and I support the Bill.
to
of
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I would
also like to say how pleased I am
that the Government has taken steps
see that the poorer people
the community are provided for
during the time of this conflict between the
Communists and the Government. I am
sorry at the same time that the Government
found it necessary to insert an Amendment in
the Bill such has been inserted. It amounts
to this, in my opinion, that it goes out to
the people of Northern Ireland to tell them
that they are entitled to go on strike the same
as those on the other side. It is a reminder
to the working men of the North of Ireland
that they also are entitled to join in it.
193 Emergency Powers Bill-
Second Reading.
5 MAY 1926

We know that an Emergency Powers Act
does not prevent them from doing anything
legal and constitutional. I fail to see the
necessity for putting an Amendment like that
in. We should be thankful-extremely thankful-that the people in Northern Ireland, the
trade unionists and those connected with the
labour unions, are so wise that they are
taking no part in this conflict. I say I do not
agree with it all. The hon. Member who has
just sat down may claim that he is a representative of the workers, but I can say this-
I have been connected with the trade union
movement for the past 30 years, and been a
very active member, and I think I have done
all I possibly could to assist the worker to
improve his position and maintain his condition by collective bargaining. At the
present time I am a paying member of one
of the largest societies, and proud of it. But
this is not a trade dispute or an industrial
dispute. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
When I was more actively connected with
the society, and as the head of the society,
when we had a dispute between employers
and workers, the workman was entitled to
withdraw his labour. What is the position
to-day? It is not a dispute between the
mineowners and miners. It is a penalty
being inflicted upon the community. The
whole community is being held up. Workers
of every description at the present time are
put to the greatest possible inconvenience.
Food is in no way guaranteed to them over in
England. The country is being held up by a
political organisation and not a trade organisation. I have always been in favour of
trade societies for collective bargaining.
Without them the workman would not
secure the position which they provide for
him. They would not have collective bargaining for maintaining wages. But by no stretch
of imagination can I see or think that this is
a trade dispute. The miners were quite justified in withdrawing their labour. It is a
different thing for miners to withdraw, from
holding up the whole country-bus drivers,
tram drivers, the men who drive on the tube
railways.
I have been in conversation with many of
my own colleagues of the trade union movement since this strike commenced, and I have
yet to come across any sensible man who
agrees with it. I very much deplore it. We
in the North of Ireland have a Government
of our own, and in my opinion we should
take a very firm stand against those who incite willing workers to leave work as a matter
of sympathy with those on strike on the other
side. I do think the workers' representatives
in this part of the country are wise and
sensible and reasonable enough not to join
in a conflict such as this. I do say again I
am awfully sorry that the Government could
not stick to the Bill without introducing an
Amendment such as this, which is simply
conveying to the workers of the North of
Ireland that they are also entitled to join in
this conflict that at present exists on the
other side of the water.
Mr. LESLIE: I have listened with great
pleasure, as I am sure all the hon. Members
of the Senate have, to the speech which has
just been delivered, if I may say so, in so
eloquent terms by my hon. Friend on my left.
I could not but think that what he said as
regards the Amendment came from his heart.
but I wonder if he will pardon me if for a
moment I venture to appeal from his heart
to his head.
I am not quite sure whether owing to the
shortness of time available for considering
this Measure, the hon. Member has been able
to see that the Amendment introduced in
another place reproduces words from the Imperial Act of 1920. As these words are in the
Imperial Act I would venture to suggest that,
although I am not to be taken as having any
particular affection for that Statute which
the right hon. Gentleman the Minister of
Commerce mentioned, I am inclined to think
that as the Amendment introduced elsewhere
reproduces words from the Imperial Statute,
it would probably be found to assist the administration if it should unhappily become
necessary to exercise some of the powers being
conferred on the Northern Government. I
think it will be found to assist the administration if the Amendment referred to is
permitted to remain.
On general grounds I need scarcely say I
heartily support the Measure, and like my
hon. Friend I join in thanking the Government for having introduced it.
Mr. BARBOUR: I think the hon. Senator
Leslie, who has just sat down, has expressed
what was in the mind of the Government in
agreeing to this Amendment which was proposed in another place. The Government
feel, in bringing forward a Measure of this
kind and asking the public generally to repose so very large a degree of confidence in
their powers, that they must in the first
place be assured that no just right which is
already secured to them is being minimised
or reduced by a sort of round-the-corner
method.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Does this
not interfere with the rights of many trades?
Mr. BARBOUR: So the Government felt
that seeing this Clause is already in the
Emergency Powers Act passed in the Imperial
Parliament, when a demand was addressed
to them that it should be repeated in this
Act, sanction for which we are now soliciting, it was a request we could not reasonably refuse. We hope to enjoy during the
next few weeks the cordial co-operation of
every section of the community, and so we
would not like any section to feel aggrieved
by any part of this Act which they are being
G
195 Emergency Powers Bill-

THE SENATE
Adjournment.
[Mr. Barbour]
asked to pass through at such short notice.
It is not conferring any new right upon
them. It is merely securing to them rights
which have already been conferred on them.
I sincerely hope any powers they may have
in that Act will not be exercised during the
next few weeks-I hope so at any rate-in
such a way as to interfere with the carrying
out of what is the main object of this Bill,
namely, to protect the interests and daily life
of especially the poorer portion of our population. I, therefore, hope the hon. Senator
will drop his objection to the inclusion of
the paragraph, by the insertion of which we
hope to secure the co-operation and goodwill
of one portion of the community at any rate.

197 Queen's University Bill-

6 MAY 1926
Second Reading.

ND

thet
ore!
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 6th May, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
VISCOUNT
Royal Assent.
MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Ι am authorised by the Prime
Minister, on his behalf, to inform the
Senate that the Lord Justices appointed for
the Government of Northern Ireland have
given, in the name of, and on behalf of
His Majesty the King, their Assent to the
following Bill:-
Emergency Powers.
Queen's University of Belfast Bill (S).
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move the Second Reading
of this very short Bill, the object of which
is to secure that representation on the
Senate of Queen's University may be given
to the City of Londonderry and the Councils
of the Six Counties. Hon. Members will
remember that about a year ago this Parliament passed an Act to enable the councils
of counties and county boroughs to make
grants to the University, and as several
councils are now doing so, directly out of
the rates, it is only proper that they should
be represented on the Senate.
The Constitution of the Senate of Queen's
University is set out in the First Schedule
(b) of the Irish Universities Act, 1908. This
Act does not provide for any variation or
amendment of that Constitution, and it has
been necessary, therefore, to introduce this
Bill.
Section 64, Sub-section 1 of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, states that no
law made by the Parliament of Northern
Ireland can have effect so as to alter the
Constitution of the Senate of Queen's
University until the proposed alteration is
approved by the Senate, the Academic
Council, and the Convocation of the
University. As the Preamble to the Bill
shows, the consent of these three bodies has
been secured.
The only other point to which, I think,
attention might be drawn is that the Schedule in the present Bill sets out more clearly
than the Schedule in the Irish Universities
Act, 1908, those members of the Senate who
are ex-officio members, nominated members,
and elected members. I do not think it is
necessary for me to say any more, except
to commend the Bill very heartily to the
favourable consideration of this House. I
beg to move.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I desire to support
this Bill and to commend it very heartily
to the favourable consideration of the Senate.
Perhaps I may be allowed to explain why
Queen's University has had to appeal to the
county councils for financial aid, in consequence of which representation on the Senate
of the University for the six county councils
and the City of Londonderry is now sought.
The University has an income of £10,000 a
year from the Church Temporalities Commissioners, but that is more in the nature
of an endowment than a direct grant from
the Exchequer. In addition, the Government
of Northern Ireland supplies £26,000 per
annum, but it has to be remembered that
whereas there were only 514 students attending the University before the war there are
now 1,169 students, so that hon. Senators
will realise what an immense increase in
the resources of the University is necessary
to cope with such an increase in the number
of students.
The Senate will be interested to know that
all modern Universities across the water receive a grant from the rates. For example,
Birmingham supplies £17,000 to its University, Bristol £19,000, Newcastle-on-Tyne
£9,000, Aberystwyth £12,000, and Belfast supplies its University with £250. It will thus
be seen that the contribution from the rates
from the city of Belfast and the Province is
comparatively insignificant. When I say that
last year we had to face a deficit of £5,500,
and in the previous year a deficit of £3,300,
hon. Members will understand how it comes
that we had to appeal to a wider area for
some means to supplement our annual income.
Notwithstanding the acute agricultural and
commercial depression the different county
councils most carefully considered this
matter, and some of the county councils
agreed to strike a farthing rate for the benefit of the University. Some of the others
have not yet decided on that point, but when
the City of Londonderry and the counties
who have not yet decided to contribute have
considered these facts perhaps they may see
their way to contribute in a similar way.
While we are very glad that the support
we have so far received gets us over part of
our difficulty, it does not enable us to carry
out our desire to have new teachers' chairs
and laboratories, which are necessary to keep
the University up-to-date and to meet the
ever-increasing demands of science. The
figures I have mentioned might be summarised by saving that Queen's University does
Queen's University Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sinclair]
more work for less money than any other
University in the Kingdom. The truth of
this will be realised when we compare the
position of Queen's University with that of the
University at Aberystwyth, which is a University comparable with Queen's. The income of Queen's University is £11,000 less
than that of Aberystwyth, and the students
at Queen's number 400 more.
The discrepancy disclosed by these figures
is even worse than it appears, because across
the water scholarships are provided by the
local authorities. For instance, Birmingham
spends £730 per annum on scholarships, Newcastle-on-Tyne £752, and Aberystwyth £20,
whereas Queen's University has to spend more
than £3,000 out of its annual income for the
provision of scholarships.
I should like to remove a misapprehension
that is widespread throughout the country
districts, and that is that Queen's University
is a local institution, and that it makes a
profit out of the students coming to it. The
Six Counties supply roughly about half the
students in the University, and the City of
Belfast and environs supply the other half.
Therefore, it is not by any means a local
institution when half the students are drawn
from the counties. I think it is only right
and proper that the counties should feel that
the University is their University as well as
the property of the City of Belfast, and that
they should take a proper pride in it and
decide to secure its efficiency by furnishing
the means for its proper equipment and for
the provision of the Chairs for teachers that
may be necessary as time goes on.
Our expenditure at Queen's is £62 per
student per annum, and when I explain
that only one-third of that £62 is supplied
by the parents or guardians of students, and
the University supplies over two-thirds, it
will be seen how closely we have to finance
things in order to keep within our income.
The University is managed on very economical lines indeed. If we only spend £62
per student at Queen's, £91 per student is
spent in Birmingham, £127 in Bristol, £91
in Newcastle-on-Tyne, and £90 in Aberystwyth. That shows we have to finance our
University very closely indeed in order to
furnish our students with a University
education.
It will be seen why the University, on
account of the two deficits to which I have
referred, had to make an appeal to the
Counties, and I am very gratified to know
that some of them have responded at a
time when there is acute agricultural and
commercial depression. We are very grateful for what they have done, and we hope
their example will be followed by those
authorities who have not yet decided to
contribute. Let me say that a halfpenny
rate instead of a farthing rate would provide £10,000 a year additional income for
the University, and that would relieve us
of our immediate anxieties, and supply
something to enable us to increase our
teaching staff, and to provide residential
halls for the male students, which is the
crying need of the University, the generosity
of the Misses Riddel having provided hostels
for the women students.
For a long time the University was regarded as a mere Government institution,
and the general public took no interest in
it, but I think the time has come when they
should recognise that university education
is the avex of the educational pillar.
Although the Government provides for
elementary and secondary education, we
hope that this grant to the University may
be supplemented as a result of publicspirited action on the part of the county
councils, all of whom, we trust, will in
more prosperous times strike a halfpenny
rate, which would give the University an
additional income of £10,000 per annum, and
thus enable it to carry on with all possible
efficiency.
I very cordially commend this Measure
to the sympathetic consideration of the
Senate. I hope this House will see its waj
to grant representation to the Counties and
to the City of Londonderry, and that we
may have representatives who will take a
lively interest in the management of the
University on its governing body. Queen's
University is not a mere local institution.
It belongs to all classes and creeds of the
community throughout the Province.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to thank the hon.
and gallant Gentleman for the very able
speech he has delivered, and for the information he has conveyed to the Senate. I
hope that information will go out to the
public of Northern Ireland, because if there
is one thing that is necessary for the efficient
carrying on of educational institutions in
any country it is that widespread interest
should be aroused in all the educational
institutions of that particular country. On
a question such as this I can, perhaps,
speak with more emphasis than most hon.
Senators, inasmuch as I have never had
the advantage of a university education,
my university having been the battle of
life and observation of my fellow-men
around me from day to day.
I have often lamented that the opportunity of acquiring a university education
was not brought within my reach in early
life. I think that if the facts given to us
to-day were widely disseminated amongst
the public of Northern Ireland they would
be calculated to arouse keen interest in the
welfare of Queen's University. The figures
the hon. and gallant Gentleman has given
to us prove beyond the possibility of doubt
that this University is working on an aver-
Queen's University Bill-
Pensions Bill.
6 MAY 1926

of 1
the

i

to
Q
کرم
ti
age of two-thirds the cost of the various
other universities the hon. and gallant
Senator quoted in his speech. Therefore, it
affords me great pleasure to support this
particular Measure, especially in view of the
fact that in the Northern area we have
for the first time had education brought
under the control of local representative
bodies, which really means bringing
education directly under the control of the
people.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: It gives
me great pleasure to support this Bill, because at one time the county with which I am
connected was one of the first supporters of
the old Queen's University. We were also
one of its first supporters under the new
scheme, and when times are more prosperous
I will do my best to induce the County Council
to strike a halfpenny rate, instead of the
present farthing rate, for the University.
There is one point I would like to mention,
and it is with regard to those county councils
which up to the present have refused to give
any grant from the rates to the University.
In my opinion a county or city which has not
given a grant should have no right to representation on the governing body of the University. The representatives of such counties
would probably not attend, and they would
not be any use. I think only those
authorities which support the University
should have representation on its governing
body, and it is not fair to them that those
bodies which have refused to contribute
should have an equal privilege.
a
I do not know whether it would be possible
to insert in the Bill a provision to the effect
that only those authorities which give a contribution from the rates should be entitled
to representation on the Senate of the University, but I am strongly of opinion that
that is what should be done. We quite recognise that Queen's University is in
different position to that it occupied before it
was separated from Trinity College. The
number of medical and law students has
largely increased, and I have been wondering
whether it would be possible to change the
name of Queen's University. If it was
possible to call it the University of Northern
Ireland people would begin to realise that
it was not merely a Belfast institution as
it has been looked upon in the past.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I would like to make one remark in
reference to what the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman who has just sat down has said.
He very naturally wishes that those counties
which have not struck a rate in aid of the
funds of Queen's University should not be
represented on the Senate. I understand that
the authorities of Queen's University expressed the wish that all the counties should
be represented whether at the moment they
contributed or not-I presume with the view
that in the future they would contribute.
For that reason all the counties have been
given representation, even though at the
moment some of them do not contribute.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: The
only objection I have is that once you get
them represented on the Senate of the University you will never get them off again, and
some of them will never contribute. It
might be an incentive to them to contribute
something if it was pointed out that they
were not represented on the Governing body.
If they do get representation without giving
a grant their representatives will surely be
too ashamed to attend the meetings of the
Senate.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the wholo
House for Wednesday next.
Mines (Working Facilities, Support,

Order for Third Reading read.
Moved that this Bill be now read a Third
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.

(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate agree to

Pensions (Increase) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, That this Bill be
now read the Third time. I promised yesterday to deal with two questions. One was
the Amendment moved by Senator Hill Dickson and also spoken to by Senator Colonel
Sharman Crawford, and the other was the
enquiry raised by Senator Glendinning, who
I regret is not here to-day.
The Amendment moved by Senator Hill
Dickson was in effect that pensioners amongst
the Petty Sessions clerks should be excluded
from the benefits of the Bill. He gave as his
reasons that these officers were for all prac
tical purposes only part-time officers, and
that it was not right that their pensions
should be increased. In my remarks on the
Committee Stage I pointed out that the actual
increased charge on the Petty Sessions Fund
by reason of the Clause would be negligible,
Third Reading.
Pensions Bill.

THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
or very slight, but I am prepared to argue
the point on wider grounds.
In the first place it must be borne in mind
that the salaries of the majority of these
officers are even still very small, although
they have been increased by the cost-of-living
bonus. The salaries are small because it is
recognised that the appointment is in many
cases not a whole-time one, although it must
be remembered that a Petty Sessions clerk
very often has to do duties outside the
ordinary work of the Court on the days on
which it meets. The smallness of the salaries
is already reflected in the smallness of the
pensions; now the hon. Senator would wish
to penalise the pensioners a second time for
the part-time nature of their occupation by
withholding from them the slight increases
that in some cases would arise under this
Act. His arguments would equally have applied to the increases of which a number of
them are already in receipt under the
Pensions (Increase) Act, 1920.
In the second place, I would ask the hon.
Member to consider the position of a clerk
who retires at the present day. For purposes
of pension in his case account is taken of
the cost-of-living bonus that is added to his
salary. Now if this is a proper course to
take, then it equally follows that the prewar pensioner, who had not a cost-of-living
bonus added to the salary on which his pension was calculated, might justly claim that
his pension should be put into some nearer
relation to his present expenditure on the
bare necessities of life as is being in effect
done for the clerk who goes out on pension.
In the third place, it must be borne in
mind that very great limitations are in force
in connection with these increases, that is to
say, that before they can be given the private
means of the clerk must be taken into consideration. As many of these are, for example, police pensioners it follows that the
increase will only be given in a minimum of
cases.
Lastly, it seems to me that the argument of
the hon. Senator if carried to its logical conclusion really comes to this, that he considers
that owing to the part-time nature of a
Petty Sessions clerk's duty he should not be
pensionable at all. That, of course, is a
question which could not be discussed on the
present Bill. The Petty Sessions Acts themselves provide for a pension scheme for Petty
Sessions clerks, and until that provision is
altered we must accept the position as it
stands. For these reasons the Government
would not be prepared to accept an Amendment.
With regard to the second question, which
was put by Senator Glendinning, the question he raised was whether the present Bill
applies to national school teachers who were
pensioned in or subsequent to 1918. The
reply to this is really contained in the Pensions Increase Act of 1920, that is to say, the
basic Act on which the present Bill is
drafted. The 1920 Act provides in effect that
the increases apply to all teachers who were
in receipt of pensions on the 16th August,
1920, but the increases are modified in the
case of those who were pensioned between the
4th August, 1914, and the 16th August, 1920,
in this way, namely, that if in the latter
case the pension was assessed on a salary
that had been increased after the 4th August,
1914, either by reason of war conditions or
by a general improvement in the scale of
emoluments, the increase to be granted when
added to the pension was not to bring the
pension to a higher figure than the same
pensioner would have got if he had been pensioned off before the 4th August, 1914, and had
received the full addition to that pension
allowed under the 1920 Act.
My reply, therefore, to the hon. Senator
is that unless I have full details of any particular pensioner who retired between 1914 and
1920 I cannot say how far he is entitled to
an increase under this Bill. I am sure, however, that if he approaches the Ministry of
Finance with full details they will supply
him with the necessary information. I beg to
move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: The
only reason I objected to an increase of
pension being granted to certain Petty
Sessions clerks was that from the speech of
the noble Viscount one would have thought
all Petty Sessions clerks did the same work,
whereas there is a very wide difference in
the work they do. I think Senator Hill
Dickson, who proposed the Amendment,
was referring to a Petty Sessions clerk who
was only engaged for an hour a month, and
it is carrying the matter too far when you
think of that man even receiving a pension
at all, whether large or small.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I might ask the
noble Viscount to tell us, when he is replying to the debate, whether the impression
which is in my mind is correct and that
is that the salaries of Petty Sessions clerks
vary very widely in different parts of the
Six Counties, and that the variation is due
to some attempt being made to estimate the
demand made on the time of the relativo
individuals. In other cases, of course, a
Petty Sessions clerk is allowed to take other
work, such as acting as clerk to a council.
I remember the noble Marquess (the Marquess of Londonderry) when Leader of the
House, saying so on another Bill. It has
always been held that a Petty Sessions
clerk was a whole-time officer, and as such
was subject to being required to do work
outside the normal duties of a Petty Sessions clerk. The main point is whether the
Pensions Bill-
6 MAY 1926
Adjournment.
tow

the


ted
orig
the
salaries of Petty Sessions clerks do not vary
according to station and circumstances.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Member who has just
spoken is perfectly correct. The salaries of
Petty Sessions clerks vary with their work,
and their pensions vary accordingly.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I do
not wish it to be taken that I object to an
increase of pension to Petty Sessions clerks
generally, but only to certain clerks in
country districts who are engaged only an
hour or so a month.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed agreed to to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate
agree to
the said Bill without any Amendment.

207 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE

THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 12th May, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Return showing the number and the nature
of the experiments performed on Living
Animals during the year 1925, under Licences
granted in conformity with the Act 39 and
40 Vic., c. 77.
The Labourers (Northern Ireland) Order,
1926.1711
Regulations under Section 3 of the Pharmacy and Poisons Acts (Northern Ireland),
1925.
The Poisons and Pharmacy (Northern
Ireland) Regulations, 1926.
Ministry of Education:
Regulations governing the Certification,
Recognition, Payment of Salaries, and Conditions of Employment of Teachers of
Technical Schools and Classes.
Presented (pursuant to Act), and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Ministry of Commerce:
Commission on the Natural and Industrial
Resources of Northern Ireland:-
Report on the Practicability of Establishing the Sugar Beet Industry in Northern
Ireland.
Report on the Power Resources of Northern
Ireland.
Presented (by Command), and ordered to
lie on the Table.
Economy (Exchequer Relief) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to ask the leave of the House
to postpone the Second Reading of this Bill
until to-morrow.
Ancient Monuments Bill [S].
Order for receiving Report of Amendments
read.
Moved: That this Report be now received.
-(The Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE 5 (Notice to be given by owners
of certain monuments).
(2) Where the owner of any ancient
monument which is included in any such
list of monuments as aforesaid, after being
notified as provided in the last preceding
sub-section, proposes to demolish or remove
in whole or in part, alter structurally, or
make additions to, the monument, or make
excavations in the neighbourhood thereof,
he shall forthwith give notice of his intention to the Ministry of Finance, and
shall not, except in the case of urgent
necessity, commence any work of demolition, removal, alteration, addition or
excavation, for a period of two months
after having given such notice; and any
person guilty of a contravention of or noncompliance with this provision shall be
liable, on conviction under the Summary
Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts, to a fine not
exceeding one hundred pounds, or to imimprisonment for a term not exceeding
three months, or to both.
"
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 5,
page 4, line 25, to leave out the words one
hundred," and insert instead thereof the
word "twenty." This is a small point which
I brought before the Committee when I was
moving another Amendment, which would
have dealt with this proposal somewhat indirectly. The point I desire to make is that
the penalty of £100 is somewhat large to
place in the power of a Magistrates' Court,
and it is somewhat in excess of the necessities of the case. I will tell the House
that originally, when the English Bill was
before the House of Commons, the penalty
suggested was £20, and that was subsequently altered to £100, owing to circumstances which are, perhaps, rather amusing
to those who care to dig into the Debates
of that period. I suggest we should reduce
the fine to £20, and I think we are entitled
to do that more so because we have
made the period of notice two months instead of one month. I beg to move.
now
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Senator, I
regret the Government do not see their way
to accept a diminution of this penalty, which
is the same as is provided for in Great
Britain. This matter was discussed on the
Committee Stage to a certain extent, and the
penalty is a fine not exceeding £100. After
all, what is the owner asked to do under
this section? He is to refrain from taking
steps which might lead to the ruin of a
monument, without giving due notice to the
Ministry of Finance, in order that the
Ministry, if it thinks fit, may take steps to
preserve the monument for the Province.
On the whole I cannot see any valid reason
why the hon. Senator wishes the penalty
reduced to £20, and I hope he will withdraw
his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I will not press the point.
