Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 7 (Effect of Preservation Orders).
(1) While a Preservation Order is in
force, the monument to which the Order
relates shall not be demolished or removed,
nor shall any additions or alterations be
made thereto or any work carried out in
connection therewith, except with the
written consent of the Ministry of Finance.
(2) If, while a Preservation Order is in
force, it appears to the Ministry of Finance
that, owing to the neglect of the owner of
the monument, the monument is liable to
fall into decay, the Ministry of Finance
may make an Order appointing that Ministry guardian of the monument for the
period during which the Preservation
Order is in force, and in that case provisions of this Act shall, during that
period, take effect as if the Ministry of
Finance had been appointed guardian by
virtue of a deed executed by every person
seised or possessed of, or entitled to, an
estate or interest in the monument.
(3) Any order made under the last preceding sub-section may be revoked at any
time by the Ministry of Finance.

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, in Clause 7, page 5,
line 34, after the word "work" (or any work)
to insert the word "be." This is purely a
drafting Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, in Clause 7, page 5,
line 39, after the word "owner" (owner of
the monument) to insert the words "or person having control." This again is a drafting Amendment, but I think I ought to explain to the House the necessity for it. The
word owner which occurs in sub-Section 2 of
this Clause is defined in sub-Section 4 of
Clause 1 of this Bill, and it is possible the
definition might narrow too much the intention of this Clause. In other words, the
actual owner might be able to prove that he
himself was not neglectful, and that some
other person who had control was really the
culprit. Accordingly the word "owner" has
been amplified in this Clause by the addition
of the words "or person having control." I
beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: As I spoke on the Committee Stage as regards the definition of sub-
Clause 4 of Clause 1, with special reference
to the fact that the term "owner" did not
seem to me to be sufficiently defined, I ought
perhaps to say that I quite concur in the
suggested Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause 11 (Powers of county councils with
respect to ancient monuments).
(1) The provisions of this Act with respect to the maintenance of and access, and
penalties for injury, to ancient monuments
shall apply in the case of any ancient 1.018
monument-
(a) of which a county council has, before the passing of this Act, become
guardian under sub-section (2) of section
nineteen of the Local Government (Ireland) Act, 1898; or
(b) which has been before the passing
of this Act, or thereafter, becomes vested
in a county council by virtue of an order
under sub-section (3) of section fourteen
of the Irish Land Act, 1903.
as if the county council were substituted
in those provisions for the Ministry of
Finance; and the expenses incurred by a
county council under this section shall be
raised by means of the poor rate as a
county at large charge.
(2) Where a county council is guardian
of an ancient monument, such council may,
with the consent of the owner of the monument, transfer the guardianship therof
to the Ministry of Finance, and the provisions of this Act shall apply accordingly.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, in Clause 11, page
7, to leave out from the word "The" (The
provisions of this Act) to the end of sub-
Section (1) and insert instead thereof:-
"following provisions of this Act, namely
Part I;
So much of Part II as relates to the gift,
devise or bequest of ancient monuments;
and
The foregoing provisions of this Part;
shall apply to a county council (including
the council of a county borough) as if the
county council were substituted in those
provisions for the Ministry of Finance;
and, in addition to its powers in relation
to monuments of which it is guardian, a
county council shall have power to maintain and preserve any monument--
(a) which has been accepted by that
council, or which it has agreed to maintain or preserve, under this Act; or
(b) which has been before the passing
of this Act, or thereafter becomes, vested
in that council by virtue of an order
under sub-section (3) of section fourteen
of the Irish Land Act, 1903;
The expenses incurred under this Act by
a county council shall, in the case of a
county borough council, be defrayed out
of any rate or fund which the Ministry
of Home Affairs may on the application of
the council approve, and shall, in the case
of any other county council, be defrayed
out of the county fund as a county at large
charge."
211 Ancient Monuments Bill-
A 212
THE SENATE 2
Report Stage.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
This is an Amendment of the Clause which
I undertook on the Committee Stage to alter
210 at the request of the hon. Senators, provid
ing for the enlargement of the scope of the
powers of county councils as compared with
the powers given in the Clause as it stands.
Generally speaking, the effect of this Amendment is that the county councils will have
exactly the same powers as the Ministry of
Finance in regard to the guardianship and
acceptance of ancient monuments, save that
they will not be allowed to purchase, nor
will they possess any power of making Preservation Orders. I may, however, add that
although there is no specific provision made
in this Bill, it is intended that the expert
advice of the Ancient Monuments Committee
and the Ministry of Finance will always be
available to the county councils if they desire to have recourse to these bodies. I beg
to move.
Mr. LESLIE: It is really a pleasure to
express my thanks to the Government for
having gone so far to meet the proposals
which I submitted not only on the Second
Reading but also on the Committee Stage.
The Amendment which has just been moved,
as the House will appreciate, gives very nearly
all that I asked for the other day as regards
local authorities. I am afraid there were
some thirty Amendments on that point,
twenty-nine of which were not moved. The
point of difference between this Amendment
and those I had put down is that I had suggested that power should be given to an
urban district. The Government has not
conceded that, or at all events it has not
yet conceded it, although perhaps in the
future that may happen.
As regards the rest of the Amendment, the
effect of it is to preserve for the county council the power which they had under the old
law of becoming guardian of any monument
which might be conveyed to them. That right
would have been taken away by the Bill
as it was first brought into this House. It
would have been taken away except as regards those cases where
power had
already been exercised which could not have
been exercised in the future. That has now
been set right and the Government, I observe,
has also given me what I asked for, that is,
they have extended the powers of Clause 10,
the powers to receive voluntary contributions
to the county councils as well as to the Ministry of Finance.
a
I notice that the noble Viscount referred
to the advice of the Ancient Monuments Committee being at the disposal of the county
councils. I think that is provided for sufficiently and satisfactorily, if I may speak for
a moment from the county council point of
view. The Clause I should refer the House
to is Sub-Clause 3 of Clause 13, which provides that the Ancient Monuments Committee may, if so requested by the owner of a
monument, give advice free of charge. In
some cases of course that will cover the
county councils, but in cases of guardianship, which no doubt are the cases the noble
Viscount had in his mind, perhaps it is
not quite so clear.
I do not think there is any other point
in the Amendment to which I should wish
to call the attention of the House, except
perhaps the fact that there is a definitely
new provision which enables the county councils to accept bequests and gifts of ancient
monuments. That, I think I am correct in
saying, is an entirely new power. They had
not got that power before, so that for practical purposes I think the only power they
have not got as regards acquisition is the
power to purchase. I think the insertion of
this Amendment will give great satisfaction
and perhaps will remove some of the objections which were urged in certain quarters
against some of the provisions of the Bill.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 12 (Public access to ancient monuments).
The public shall have access to any monument which is vested in the Ministry of
Finance or a county council, or of which
that Ministry or a county council is
guardian, at such times and under such
conditions as may from time to time be
prescribed by regulations of that Ministry
or such county council respectively:
Provided that-
(i) this section shall not apply to any
monument of which the Ministry of
Finance or a county Council has been appointed or constituted the guardian before
the commencement of this Act, except in
cases where the consent of the owner has
been given to the public having access to
the monument, either by the deed appointing or constituting that Ministry or such
county council the guardian, or where a
right of access has arisen through the
acquiescence of the owner or otherwise;
and
(ii) where the deed appointing the
Ministry of Finance the guardian of the
monument is executed after the commencement of this Act, if the deed so
provides, the public shall not have access
to the monument without the consent of
the owner of the monument.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 12.
to leave out paragraphs (i) and (ii) on pages
7 and 8, and insert instead thereof:-
"this section shall not-
(a) in the case of a monument which before the passing of this Act has been vested
in the Ministry of Finance or a County
Council or of which that Ministry or a
213 Ancient Monuments Bill-
12 MAY 1926

Report Stage.
di

Ty
County Council has become the guardian,
operate to confer any right of access other
than any such right which may exist at the
commencement of this Act; or
(b) in the case of a monument which
after the passing of this Act becomes
vested in the Ministry of Finance or a
County Council or of which that Ministry
of a County Council becomes guardian,
operate to confer any right of access other
than any such right to which the owner of
the monument and the occupier of any
land which it may be necessary to cross to
obtain access to the monument may consent."
This Amendment deals with the difficult
question of public access. I think we must
all agree that if a monument is to be maintained at all out of any form of public funds
it is necessary that the public should have
access to the monument, partly because if
the public are providing the money they are
tel entitled to see how it is being spent, and
partly because if you maintain a monument
from the point of view of education you
il defeat your object if the monument is not
accessible.
As may be remembered, I put forward an
Amendment to this Clause in Committee,
dtand the noble Viscount stated at the commencement of the Committee's deliberations
that the Government were in sympathy with
my Amendment, but at a later stage the
noble Viscount said he did not quite see
how the Amendment read, and suggested it
might be better drafted. Accordingly, I
have had another look at it, and the result
is the words which I now suggest to the
House.
-1

It is necessary, I am afraid, to examine
the Clause in some little detail to see what
it is we have to deal with. We want to
provide for access to a monument, whether
vested in the Ministry or in the County
Council, or whether those authorities are
simply the guardians. It is obvious, I
think, that, as regards the monuments that
are vested, or, rather, have been vested in
the past, we cannot give more powers by
this Bill than exist already, and, accordingly, I tried to deal with that in paragraph
(a) of my Amendment. Paragraph (b) is to
meet the cases of monuments which, after
the passing of the Act, become vested or
guarded, and I hope to manage to get away
from the difficulty as regards what I call
the Land Purchase Act cases, because, as
I understand it, the Land Purchase Acts do
not convey any right of public access to a
monument which has passed to the tenant
purchaser. That is subject to this qualification: that in individual cases it will
probably be found on an inspection of the
vesting Clause that some provisions have
been made to effect access, but Land Act
cases do present a distinct difficulty.
For the rest of the Amendment, which is
principally paragraph (d), I would suggest
that there should be a definite step taken
in order to prove consent rather than that, 1401S,
as it originally appeared in the Bill, the
consent might arise negatively. I propose to
get rid of a rather troublesome expression
in the original Bill, the objection to which
is, as the House may remember, that if you
rely on the possible effect of equitable
acquiescence you leave the point at issue to
be settled in every individual case, and the
possibilities of law suits seem endless, and
nobody would be any better off.
Whether or not the House will prefer the
Amendment I now submit I do not know.
The point is a very difficult one, but one
may hope that, having regard to the good
nature of owners generally, difficulties may
not arise. But, as the Bill stands, I am
afraid there are possibilities of trouble,
which one would like to remove if possible.
I, accordingly, beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This is a point which the hon.
Senator dealt with pretty fully in Committee, and on which the hon. and gallant
Senator (Colonel Sharman Crawford) also
touched. I think the best way to explain
the matter is to go into this Clause in some
little detail. It will be remembered that two
rights have to be preserved. One is the
right of the taxpayer who is paying for the
maintenance of a monument to be able to
have access to it. The other is the right of
the owner, who has handed over a monument
to the guardianship of the Ministry of
Finance, to have inserted in the deed
proviso that the public shall not have access.
a
Normally speaking the Ministry of Finance
would not accept the monument unless a
right of access was secured, but it may
happen that a monument is of such a nature,
for example, only of interest to the scientist,
or so situate by relation to surrounding property, that while it is very desirable that it
should be taken over from the point of view
of national importance, yet it would be unreasonable to have free access for the public
at large.
The same would apply to monuments taken
over by a County Council, and as regards the
particular point raised by the hon. Senator
all that I can say is if a monument is thought
to be of public interest and one to which the
public should have the right of free access,
then neither the State nor Local Authority
should take it over from the owner without
at the same time seeing to it that a means
of access exists.
As regards the words which the hon.
Senator, Mr. Leslie, criticised, I think in the
latter part of his speech, namely, "where a
right of access has arisen through the acquie-
215 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Report Stage.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
scence of the owner"--and he said it would
probably lead to a number of law suits-I
20am advised by the legal advisers that the
wording of the Clause is accurate. If an
owner holds that he has not acquiesced in
such a right, he has his ordinary remedy in
the Courts of Law against the common trespasser. However, I do not anticipate there
will be many actions of this kind. For these
reasons the Government consider the Section
as it stands is better than the Amendment of
the hon. Member, and therefore I regret to
say I cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: With much that has been
said I am of course in agreement; in fact I
think we are almost getting at the stage of
being accused of being in dispute over mere
words. However, I think everybody will be
satisfied that this question is a very difficult
one, and I think everybody will agree that it
has been thoroughly well gone into. The
cases the noble Viscount alluded to I admit
might just as well have arisen under the
Amendment I propose as under the existing
Clause. In the circumstances I think hon.
Members will wish that I should withdraw
the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, in Clause 12, page 8
paragraph (ii), after the words "Finance"
(Ministry of Finance), insert "or a County
Council." This is consequential on the wider
powers given to County Councils under the
Amendment I previously moved.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 14 (Expenses of Ministry of
Finance and Advisory Committee).
There shall be paid out of moneys provided by Parliament such expenses as may
be incurred by the Ministry of Finance in
the exercise of its powers in connection
with-
(a) the inspection and maintenance of
ancient monuments, whether vested in
the Ministry of Finance or of which that
Ministry is guardian;
(b) the purchase by agreement of any
ancient monument; and
(c) the payment of compensation for
acts done under this Act involving interference with private rights of property;
and also such expenses of the Ancient
Monuments Advisory Committee as may
be sanctioned by the Ministry of Finance.
(d) the purchase or assistance towards
the purchase of archaeological objects of
which the finding has been reported to
the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the end of
Clause 14, to insert the following new Subsections:-
"(2) The cost of any deed required for the
purposes of the Act shall, if the Ministry
of Finance is a party to such deed, be paid
out of moneys provided by Parliament;
(3) Any deed required for the purposes
of this Act shall be exempt from stamp
duty."
These are two Sub-sections which perhaps
I ought to explain. They are directed, as the
House will appreciate, to facilitating transfer
to the Ministry for the benefit of the public
of monuments and sites of monuments. If a
monument is to be conveyed to the Ministry
a Deed is required and the cost of that Deed
may perhaps be material. The cost would be
dependent to some extent on the character
of the Title involved, and it occurred to me
that it was only reasonable that if the
Ministry of Finance is to benefit under the
Deed, the Ministry of Finance should pay for
it, of course out of moneys provided by
Parliament.
I venture to bring the Amendment before
the House although, as will be seen, it is an
Amendment on a strictly financial Clause,
and I recognise that supposing the Amendment should meet with the approval of
the House the paragraph containing it will
not appear in the Bill when it leaves this
House and starts on its voyage to another
place. Consequently I feel in some little
difficulty as to whether one should ask the
noble Viscount to assist us with his advice
on this matter, or whether it should not be
left entirely to the House.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Senator, I
think it is quite clear that it is not within
the discretion of this House to insert this
Amendment, because you are seeking to insert in the Bill Sub-sections proposing a
direct charge on the Exchequer, and I do not
think it is competent for the House to do
that.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am of opinion that this
Amendment is not in order.
Mr. LESLIE: I must, of course, bow to
your ruling, but I should have liked to
have submitted-
Mr. SPEAKER: While I will not alter
my ruling, I shall be very glad to hear what
the hon. Member has to say.
Mr. LESLIE: The point I desire to make
is that inasmuch as the House has no power
to increase a charge on the public, at the
same time the Bill, as it is brought into
this House, already contains a charge on the
public funds, and the charge on the public
funds which is already contained in Clause

14 will have to be taken out of the Bill.
It seemed to me that as we were discussing
financial matters there is no objection to
our amending a document which, after all,
is our own private document up to the time
when it leaves this House and goes to
another place. It was on those lines that 1
ventured to bring the matter before this
House, but after what you, Mr. Speaker,
have said I cannot, of course, pursue the
subject.
CLAUSE 17 (Definition of "monument"
and "ancient monument," 45 and 46 Vic.,
c. 73).
or
In this Act the expression "monument"
includes any site, structure or erection
(whether above or below the surface) other
than an ecclesiastical building which is
for the time being used for ecclesiastical
purposes; and the expression "ancient"
monument" includes any monument
part or remains of a monument, the preservation of which is a matter of public interest by reason of the historic, architectural, traditional, artistic, or archæological
interest attaching thereto, and the site of
any such monument or of any remains
thereof, and any part of the adjoining land
which may be required for the purpose of
fencing, covering in, or otherwise preserving the monument from injury, or for the
purpose of preserving the amenities of the
monument, and also includes the means of
access thereto.
Mr. LESLIE: The next Amendment I beg
to move is in Clause 17, page 9, line 26, after
the word "purposes" (purposes; and the expression) insert, "or anything contained in
such ecclesiastical building." We are now
in the Definition Clause and it occurred to
me and I mentioned the point in Committee
but withdrew it because the noble Viscount
desired to look further into the questionthat as we are here dealing with churches
and as it is very possible that there might
be inside churches certain structures or erections the term is very wide-which are detached and are not part of the freehold, such
movable structures or erections would not be
covered by the exemptions already in the
Bill. As it would be very undesirable to
admit the exercise of any authority in
matters of that kind by any authority other
than the Church authority it might be worth
while to insert the simple words I suggest.
I confess I should like those words put in
the Bill, but if they are objected to there
is a protection which will occur to the House
and which I will not allude to at the moment.
I beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I may inform the hon. Senator that
an Amendment relating to anything contained
in an ecclesiastical building or in any building used for ecclesiastical purposes in addition to the building itself the Government
consider to be unnecessary, and for this
reason. If the contents referred to are monuments fixed to a wall or to the floor it would
be part of the fabric and, therefore, comprise the building itself. On the other hand,
anything movable in the building would
come under the definition of site, structure,
or erection, and therefore it would not be
considered a monument for the purposes of
the Bill. For that reason the Government
is not able to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry I did not catch
what the noble Viscount said. Did he say a
movable monument would be
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Would not be.
Mr. LESLIE: Evidently the Government
do not think it possible that there could be
a movable monument inside a church. However, the point of view I put forward is
possibly sufficiently defended in the Government of Ireland Act, although I would have
preferred that the words had gone into the
Bill. I will not press the point.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the end
of Clause 17 to add as a new Sub-Clause:-
"(2) For the purposes of this Act a monument shall not be deemed to be an ancient
monument unless it is mainlyof
(a) of artificial construction or
interest on account of artificial embellishment, and
(b) of a date prior to the accession of
King George the First."
We now approach a new Sub-clause put
down to enable us to discuss two questions
which really arise out of our discussion of
the Definition Clause on the Committee
Stage. The first proposal I have put down
is in consequence of the introduction of the
word "site." The word "site" before
"structure or erection" does not occur in the
English Act, and is, if I may say so, a little
invention of our own. The insertion of that
word brings up at once the question of the
Giant's Causeway. The Giant's Causeway
was alluded to in the House of Lords during
the debates on these Measures there-not, I
think, the actual Act of 1913, but the Act of
the year before. Lord Sheffield asked the
question, but it does not appear from the
Debates that he got an answer.
I rather gather the matter was dealt with
privately and it was pointed out that owing
to the phraseology subsequently adopted in
the English Act the retention of the Schedule to the Act of 1882, about which so much
has already been said, there could be no
question that the Giant's Causeway was not
219 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Report Stage.
[Mr. Leslie]
included. Therefore, for those reasons, even
had the Act of 1882 applied to Ireland, the
Giant's Causeway would not have been included. The question is whether our insertion of the word "site" has not brought in
the Giant's Causeway, and if so, whether
that is the desire of the Government. It
occurred to me that we might get over the
difficulty by inserting such words as I have
suggested.
I am not sure that I ought not to have
said "artificial construction or arrangement"
for fear one might interfere with some of
the Dolmens or standing stones, and also I
thought we must put in "artificial embellishment" in order to catch stones which have
been dealt with in some form or other
hope I have dealt sufficiently with the artificial question, but it may be that the noble
Viscount can assure us that the question of
the Giant's Causeway does not arise or that
it can be protected in some other way, in
which case there will be no further point
in considering the question of artificiality.
I
The second point as regards date arises
from the fact that we have no definition of
the word "ancient." Again and again it has
been pointed out that we have nothing to
guide us as to the word "ancient." On the
Committee Stage the noble Viscount told us
that it was the practice in England to date
anything ancient from the year 1714, which
was the year of the accession of George I.
It so chances that within the last few days
there has been a debate in the House of Lords
on some matter connected with ancient
monuments. I think some noble Lord moved
for Papers, and during that debate occasion
was taken to refer to the approaching loss of
a very valuable monument, Waterloo Bridge.
Waterloo Bridge, as will be remembered,
was only opened for traffic in, I think, the
year 1817, or two or three years after the
Battle of Waterloo, and during the debate
it was definitely stated on behalf of the Commissioners of Works that in dealing with
ancient monuments the noble Lord who
raised the question was just as much entitled to refer to Waterloo Bridge as an
ancient monument as he was to Stonehenge.
Therefore, we now have it, so to speak,
officially stated in the House of Lords in
England that for the purposes of these Acts
the term "ancient" has gone entirely, and
no question of age now arises. I think the
question we have now to consider is whether
we ought now to give any definition as regards date in our Bill, or whether we ought
to leave it to somebody-I am afraid I do
not know who, because the only authority
I can find in the Bill is the Ministry of
Finance, who will, perhaps, have something
to say. The Advisory Committee will report
ancient monuments, the Ministry may list
other ancient monuments, and the Ministry,
on receiving reports, may, if it appears that
the monument is an ancient monument, do
so and so.
There is nothing provided anywhere for
any outside authority to come in and say
whether a monument is ancient or not. I
do not know that, apart from the duties
of the auditor in connection with some item
of expenditure, the point is of any great
importance. However, I thought it was a
point we ought to discuss, and I beg to move
the insertion of the words standing on the
Paper.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: What the hon. Senator proposes to
do by this Amendment is to re-define what
is an ancient monument. I admit that the
definition of an ancient monument is a
matter of very considerable difficulty, but
we consider that the definition in the Bill
is the best we can do, and it is the result
of very careful consideration based on expert
advice. The hon. Member referred to the
word "site" in the course of his remarks,
and he seemed to have a dread that under
the term "site might be included the
Giant's Causeway. I think the point is
this. A monument which is an ancient
monument has to be of historic, architec
tural, traditional, artistic, or archæological
interest, and the Giant's Causeway, I think
we may all safely say, is of scientific interest, and, therefore, would not come under
the definition of ancient monument, and it
is not considered so to do. I can tell the
hon. Member that definitely.
دو
As regards the second part of the hon.
Member's Amendment, the insertion of ang
date such as the year of the accession of
King George I, namely, 1714, which I mentioned in a speech on the Committee Stage,
as the date which it was the custom in
England to more or less accept as the date
of an ancient monument, is not considered
desirable, and it has not, as the hon. Member
is probably aware, been embodied in any
Act in Great Britain. Although, generally
speaking, nothing of a later date than the
year 1714, or, indeed, probably a good deal
earlier than that, would be preserved as an
ancient monument, it is quite possible that
work of a later date might be considered
worthy of preservation, and we do not wish
in this Bill to fetter posterity by the insertion of a rigid date. For that reason I
hope the hon. Member will withdraw what
we call an unnecessarily restrictive Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: After the very interesting
statement on the subject of the Giant's
Causeway, I think I have attained the object
I had in putting down the Amendment, and
I now ask leave to withdraw it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

NEW CLAUSE.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, after Clause
7, to insert as a new Clause:-
"8. Sections five, six and seven of this
Act shall not apply-
(a) to any structure which appears to
the Ministry of Finance to be occupied
as a dwelling house (otherwise than by a
person employed as the caretaker thereof
or his family); or
(b) to any church, ecclesiastical building or structure which has been disposed
of under the provisions of sub-section four
of section twenty-five of the Irish Church
Act, 1869; or
(c) to any monument which after
the First of November, 1903, has been
before the passing of this Act or thereafter becomes vested in a purchaser under
the Land Purchase Acts or the Northern
Ireland Land Act, 1925."
This new Clause is the only Amendment I
have brought before the House this afternoon in which a question of principle is
really involved. I went into these matters
both on the Second Reading and in Committee, and the Clause I suggested in Committee was not perhaps altogether all that
might have been wished. I withdrew it because it contained a reference to the National
Trust which had become obsolete owing to
the course of the debate on another Section.
Sections 5, 6, and 7 of the Bill are what I
may describe as the bones of the law which
it is sought to bring into force in Northern
Ireland. They are the Sections relating to
Listing and Preservation Orders.
I want to ensure that, following the
English course, those Sections shall not
apply to any structure which is bona-fide
occupied as a dwelling-house by somebody
other than a caretaker who may have been
put in for the sake of making it a dwellinghouse. In England the whole of one part
of the Act is made not to apply, and as regards the Listing Clause, which is in a
separate part of the Act in England, there is
a special notice at the end that that Section
shall not apply. I think it is obvious that
a dwelling must be protected. It is possible
for there to be a dwelling-house in Northern
Ireland the preservation of which might be
a matter of public interest by reason of the
historic, architectural, traditional, artistic,
or archaeological interest attaching to it. I
really think there are such dwelling-houses
in Northern Ireland. There may not be so
many as in England, but there certainly are
houses that your Advisory Committee, a Committee which is not yet formed, and which is
not to be an elected body and for which
apparently nobody will be directly
sponsible to Parliament-most of them presumably antiquarians, might consider
reshould come within the definition, and they
might report to the Ministry that it was of
national importance and should be preserved,
and then the Minister must put it on his list.
He has no choice there.
If the Minister puts it on his list various
things happen that are described in Clause 5.
Then unfortunately the owner cannot be in
the ordinary way the owner of his own property and the property he inhabits. I do
not think it can be the intention of Parliament to impose that extraordinary restriction on a man as regards his own house
merely because certain people in Northern
Ireland think it ought to be managed by
them and not by him. Unless we put this
protection in the Bill the owner of a house
does stand to be subjected to unpleasant experiences.
The other paragraphs of the Clause
deal with the question of monuments
which, as I said before, have
already been offered to a public body.
Under the Irish Church Act nothing
that was worth preserving could be disposed of until the Church Commissioners had
an opportunity of accepting it, and under the
Land Purchase Act nothing could be transferred to a tenant purchaser unless both the
Ministry of Finance and the County Council
had had an opportunity of acquiring it.
Those things have passed now.
As regards the operation of future Land
Purchase Acts the Ministry of Finance and
the County Councils will have an opportunity
of acquiring these ancient monuments, and if
they do not acquire them the purchaser must,
as I think, be left undisturbed. In none of
these cases has Parliament the moral right
to interfere unless it can do so by agreement.
I beg to move the new Clause in my name.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Member laid great stress
on the exclusion of a dwelling-house from
the operations of the Bill. At the present
moment the Government do not know of any
house in Northern Ireland which would come
within the definition of an ancient monument. If there was any such in Northern
Ireland it would be such a rarety in my
opinion that it would be worth preserving,
and possibly the best preservation for an
ancient dwelling is for somebody to live in it.
I do not think the hon. Member need fear
that the Government would place unnecessary
restrictions on the owner of such a dwelling
to the extent that he would not in effect be
the owner of his property. I think the hon.
Member may take it that if there was such a
dwelling-house in Northern Ireland and the
owner took reasonable care of it the Government would not be likely to put restrictions
on him, and tell him he had got to do this
and that, and make his life a burden to him.

[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard] Sande
As regards the other two Sub-sections of
this Clause, the Government does not
acquiesce in the view that because the State
or a local authority had power to accept a
monument under the series of Acts referred
to, and at some time did not elect to do
so, therefore, it is stopped for all time
from stepping in and preserving such a
monument, which is the effect the hon.
Member's Amendment would have. We all
know that scientific and historical knowledge has grown greatly in the last fifty
years, and what may at one time have
seemed an unsightly ruin may now be regarded as historically and scientifically of
considerable importance. Naturally we will
endeavour, whenever possible, to obtain
these things by agreement. For these
reasons I regret to say that the Government
cannot see their way to accept the Amendment.
a
Mr. GLENDINNING: With regard to
paragraph (a) of this Amendment, either
there are dwelling-houses which might be
considered by the Advisory Committee to
be worthy of preservation or there are not.
If there are it would be the duty of the
Government to have them preserved, and if
the owner was living in such a house he
would have to submit to
Preservation
Order by whatever means the Government
thought fit to impose. Therefore, there
would be Government interference with the
owner of this house. If he was not able
to spend whatever sum was necessary to
preserve the house in accordance with the
ideas of the Advisory Committee or the
Ministry of Finance, what would happen
then? In a case like that I think the
Mr. H. Milne Barbour.

Government should buy the house. I would

Mr. LESLIE: I do not think there is much
left for me to say. I do not quite understand what the noble Viscount means by his
reference to these dwelling-houses. He says
the Government understands there are no
dwelling-houses in Northern Ireland to which
this Bill could apply.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER
RARD: So far as we know at present.
Mr. LESLIE: Are we to understand that
the Government comes before Parliament,
and asks for power to deal with they do not
know what? Again and again during the
discussion of this Bill have I asked the
Government to say what they were going
to preserve, and we have had no information.
I must stand by the old constitutional
maxim that a man's house is his castle, and,
as regards the other matters, I have a distinct preference for the sanctity of contracts.
If the Government have had opportunities
of acquiring these monuments in the past,
and have not done so, and if they desire
to take them over in the future it must be
by agreement. I have brought forward a
good many Amendments to this Measure,
and I do not think I have pressed my own
views on the House unduly, but it is impossible for me to withdraw this Amendment.
Question put: That the Clause be added
to the Bill.

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: There is one point I would like to
refer to on the Second Schedule. I promised
on the Committee Stage that I would go into
the points raised by Senator Leslie with regard to his inquiry as to what has become
of the Ancient Monuments Fund set up under
Section 2 of the Ancient Monuments Act,
1892, to provide for the maintenance of monuments taken over by the Office of Works in
Dublin under the Irish Church Act, 1869.
The Ministry of Finance received its apportioned share of that fund, and that share
Colonel T. Sinclair.
Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.

yielded an income of £117 per annum,
Under the general arrangements which have
been in force since the Northern Government
was established, by which all apportioned
funds have been merged into a single Capital
Fund, of which the income is paid into the
Exchequer while the service previously
maintained from the fund is financied from
sums voted annually by Parliament, the
apportioned Ancient Monuments Fund has
been merged into the Capital Fund and the
necessary provision for the maintenance of
ancient monuments is voted annually. I may
point out that the provision in the current
year is for a sum of £600, the amount being
increased in order to overtake some very
much delayed repairs on the monuments in

Second Reading.
Economy Bill-

THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 13th May, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM
(Deputy Speaker) in the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Second Annual Report of the Government
Inspectors of Explosives for Northern Ireland, being their Annual Report for the year
1925.
Ministry of Education:
Instruction in Extra and Special Subjects
in Public Elementary Schools.
Presented (pursuant to Act) and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Economy (Exchequer Relief) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second Reading of
this Bill I beg leave to ask the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Finance, to take
charge of this Bill. I think I am right in
saying that this is the first time he has
graced this House with his presence. We are
all very glad to see him here to-day. He is
a very busy man and we are very pleased
indeed that he should be able to find the
time to come here and explain this Bill,
which is one of great interest and importance. The right hon. Gentleman will be able
to explain it far better than I could, having
regard to the short time I have had to study
the Measure. I beg to move.
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): I am glad to have the opportunity of appearing before your House to explain a Measure which has involved a good
deal of discussion and, in some places, apprehension.
Owing to the exigencies of British national
finance, and what Mr. Punch humourously
calls his ruthless quest for revenue, the Imperial Chancellor of the Exchequer found it
necessary to reduce the State contribution in
the case of two of the great industrial insurance schemes of the country, namely,
National Health Insurance and Unemployment Insurance. Mr. Churchill defended
the reduction of the Government's contribution to National Health Insurance
three grounds which he regarded as very
cogent. The first was that the State needed the
money involved, amounting to £2,750,000, and
that the interest of the State must be paraon
mount and transcend all other considerations.
The second argument used by the Chancellor
of the Exchequer was that the vast
accumulation of surplus funds of the
National Health Insurance Society
amounting
as it was estimated to
£65,000,000 proved that the Government could reduce its contribution without
affecting the solvency of the funds. The
third argument used by Mr. Churchill, and
probably the most cogent of all, was that no
member would by the Government's action
lose any benefit to which he was entitled
That really was probably the most important
assurance that could be given for National
Health Insurance.
In regard to Unemployment Insurance, the
Chancellor contended that although the funds
showed a deficit and owed the Government
some £7,000,000, yet the condition of employment showed such signs of improving that
he estimated that before very long a condition
of normality would result, when the income
on benefits would more than balance the
outlay. For this reason the Chancellor felt
he was justified in reducing the Government
contribution to the fund, whilst at the same
time giving a guarantee that no beneficiary
would suffer by reason of that reduction. He
expected by this step to effect a saving of be
tween £4,000,000 and £5,000,000.
The decision to reduce the contribution to
these two funds, together with certain other
economy steps were given effect to in a
Measure known as the Economy Bill which
passed the British House of Commons without
Amendment.
This Bill will have an
immediate and definite effect on our own
financial position in Ulster. The saving
which was effected by the reduction of the
contribution to these two Insurance Funds
in Britain amounted approximately to
£8,000,000. On the general principle govern.
ing our financial relation with Great Britain
as determined by the Colwyn Award we
would, unless we followed the British lead,
certainly have to find a considerable sum of
money out of our own resources, and I need
hardly say we are not in such affluent circumstances at the present time as to be able to
afford to lose any considerable sum of money
or submit to sacrifices.
But apart from any question of finance,
however, important and indeed vital this
may be, and is, we cannot overlook the fact
that the relations of Ulster and Great
Britain in the working of these two great
Insurance Schemes are so inter-related and
interwoven that they must, as far as possible,
be on a common basis. That is really the
great argument I use as to the necessity for
this Measure which I am now presenting. In
the case of Unemployment Insurance, as is
well-known, our Government, for a valuable
consideration, and because we were driven to
it on account of the abnormal deficit in our
own Insurance Fund, amounting to approximately £4,000,000, entered into an agreement
Second Reading.

Report Stage.
13 MAY 1926

ble
andi
bes
with the British Government that we would
in all respects maintain the same standards
of benefits and contribution as were provided
by the British scheme. We have, therefore,
no option in the matter, and the Bill which
I am presenting to the House for Second
Reading is the necessary outcome of that
obligation to which I have referred. There
are also other matters in our own Bill connected with our own local authorities, and
this part of the subject I will touch upon
later.
our
This Bill, therefore, which is before the
House is divided into two parts. Clauses
1-8 deal with the Amendment of the law in
regard to the State's Contribution to the Contributory Insurance Schemes of which I have
spoken, and the balance of the Measure treats
of the readjustment to a small extent of the
State's financial obligation to
local
authorities. Taking these matters in their
order, I will touch for a few minutes on the
question of the position of National Health
Insurance. Without going into the Measure
Clause by Clause I propose to deal with
Clauses 1 to 7 as one part of the Bill, and
with Clause 8 relating to Unemployment Insurance. The administration and machinery
of the scheme is contained in the Clauses
which follow the first Clause.
I would like to remind the House in regard to Health Insurance that the proposed
reduction in the contribution is from twoninths to one-seventh of the cost of benefits
and administration in the case of men, and
to one-fifth in the case of women. There has
been a good deal of expressed apprehension
on the part of opponents of the Measure that
the Government was not entitled to make
these reductions in its contributions to the
fund, but I think it will be necessary to
refer to the inception of the scheme in order
to have a due appreciation of the Government's position in this matter.
The financial system of the scheme was introduced in 1911, and the provisions were
based upon actuarial investigation which
assumed an average yield of three per cent.
on the investments of the society. As a
matter of fact the investments on the funds
are now yielding, and have for some time
yielded 4 per cent. to 5 per cent., and the
Government is in effect paying the difference
between 31 per cent. and approximately 5 per
cent. because, of course, the investments
are made in Government funds. The increase
alone, by reason of these additions to the return on the investments, amounts to
£2,000,000 a year, and this fact has therefore
greatly accelerated the rate of increase of the
big surplus which I have referred to as
£65,000,000.
There is another feature of the matter.
The Widows' and Orphans' Contributory
Pensions Act of last year, in addition to
placing a very heavy new burden on the
State, provided a large measure of relief in
this respect, that it removed from the purview of the National Health Insurance Fund
members who reached the age of 65 years, or
those between 65 and 70 years of age, and it
is calculated that this alone will relieve the
burden on the National Health Insurance
Fund to a very large extent. Since the beginning of the fund in 1911 the Government
has made certain changes from time to time
to meet particular circumstances as they
arose, and therefore the Government feels
that it is entitled to take the step to which
I am referring when the exigencies of the
State require it. It has to be remembered
that the Government guarantee is behind the
undertaking that no one entitled to benefit
will be in the slightest degree affected, as full
provision is made for the solvency of all
Approved Societies, no matter how small and
unimportant they may be. I do not think I
need labour this argument in view of the fact
that the whole weight and authority of the
British Government is behind the financial
obligations of the Societies, and that therefore
their position is assured.
Having dealt with National Health Insurance, I should like to shortly touch upon that
important fund known as the Unemployment
Insurance Fund. The proposed reduction
in this case is from 61d. to 6d. per week in
the case of men, with corresponding reductions in the case of women and young
persons, the provisions in respect of which
are dealt with in Clause 8. The Unemployment Insurance Fund differs in many respects from that of Health Insurance. The
latter fund is rich above all dreams and
expectations, whilst the Unemployment Insurance Fund in Northern Ireland owes
large sums of money to the Government.
The accumulated deficit on the Ulster Fund
last year, as hon. Members probably know,
reached a sum of approximately £4,000,000.
At that time the deficit on the British Fund
was about £7,000,000, so one can very readily
mentally estimate the difference in the conditions obtaining in Northern Ireland and in
Great Britain. It was quite obvious that
this burden of £4,000,000 was entirely too
heavy for this little State to bear, and as
no provision had been made for such a contingency in the original financial agreement
upon which the financial Clause of the
Government of Ireland Act was based
we contended with the British Government
that it was unfair that we should be
burdened with, at that time, such an unknown and heavy liability.
We, therefore, urged for an amalgamation
of the British and Ulster funds so that the
one fund would be operated in whatever way
might be arranged, This, however, the
British Government refused to agree to.
Economy Bill—
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Pollock]
They would not discuss with us the question
of amalgamation, and after very serious and
prolonged negotiations we reached this agreement with the British Government; that they
would undertake that if the accumulated
deficit of £4,000,000 was held in reserve they
would contribute 75 per cent, of the interest
cost of that accumulated deficit, and further
that they would also provide 75 per cent.
of the continuing deficit, assuming the deficit
to continue.
On the basis of the present figures of unemployment, I estimate that our liability
to the funds in the current year will be at
least £500,000, while the British contribution
to it will not fall far short of £1,000,000.
Considering, therefore, that our obligations
to the British Government in this matter
are so heavy we have no option-none whatever-but to keep step with the movement in
Great Britain. The moment we drop apart
we break the undertaking we entered into
and the whole scheme falls into the melting
pot. In this case, as with Health Insurance,
the Government's undertaking holds goodthat no beneficiary will suffer by the proposed changes any reduction of statutory
benefits.
I would like to stress this point in connection with Unemployment Insurance.
Last year we paid a State contribution to
the fund of £265,000, and if the changes proposed in this Bill had not become operative,
our State contribution would have exceeded
£300,000. The effect of this Bill will be to
reduce the Estimate in the current year to
£232,000 so far as any estimate can be made
in view of the upward and downward trend
of unemployment. In addition, however, to
this large sum of £232,000, the House must not
overlook the fact that we have to provide an
additional half million in order that benefits
may be secured to beneficiaries. It will thus
be seen that our total obligations to the Unemployment Fund in Northern Ireland
amount to a sum of approximately £750,000
this year in addition to the British Government's assistance of not far from £1,000,000.
These are very serious figures as you can
see, and obviously they place one like myself,
who is responsible for the finances of the
Province, in a somewhat difficult position as
regards where the money is to be found to
meet these heavy claims. Therefore, in presenting this Measure I am sure the Members
of this House, with the knowledge and information they have, will not feel that
this small reduction that is being made in
the Government's contribution is something
that cannot be defended.
Let me also stress this point: that this
contribution of £500,000 is not in the nature
of a loan that would or might be repaid,
assuming that the Unemployment Insurance
Fund became solvent and was able to repay
loans that had been made to it. This is not
a loan, but it is in the nature of an outand-out grant which the taxpayers of Northern Ireland are providing in order that
those who are beneficiaries under the Unemployment Insurance Fund may not be
deprived of the necessaries of life. Of course,
there is also another burden, and that is
that the deficit of £4,000,000 which has accumulated must in the natural order of things
be extinguished. It cannot remain for ever
as a dead weight, a millstone round the necks
of the people of this Province, and it must
be redeemed by the provision of a Sinking
Fund. That is all I propose to say in regard
to these great insurance schemes.
I now pass on to the second part of the
Measure, which begins at Clause 9 and
carries on to the end of the Bill. This part
of the Measure, as I explained before,
is concerned with the relations of the Government to our local authorities in respect
of two comparatively small matters. Perhaps
I should at this juncture say something about
the system that has obtained in the past in
respect of grants to local authorities. Our
present system of local Government is the
result of a long process of evolution, Conse
quently the financial relationship between
the State and local authorities has never
at any time been definitely defined. It has
simply grown over time.
In the early days of local government
definite sources of national revenue were earmarked for local services because the prin.
ciple of taxation of those days was that each
tax was levied to carry on a particular
service. This has given way to the modern
practice of paying all national revenue into
a central Exchequer, from which all issues
are made as they are required. This principle has regulated all modern financial
arrangements with local authorities, and,
if we wish to give some form of assistance,
we simply vote a grant from the Exchequer.
Examples of what I mean are to be found
in the case of Housing, Public Health, and
grants for Educational Services.
Touching upon Clause 9, to which I have
referred, when the proceeds of the Dogs'
Licence Duty were made available for local
authorities, such grants as I have mentioned were not in existence, that is to say,
direct grants from the Exchequer. Since
that time grants for specific purposes have
multiplied to such an extent that the
Government feel that local authorities have
not the same lien on the Dogs' Licence Duty
receipts, but I am concerned not to increase
their burdens unduly at the present time;
far from it, because I contend that it
matters little to a man whether he pays
his liabilities in the form of taxes or in the
form of rates. It is something he has to
pay, and so I say I have no tendency
towards increasing their burden unduly.
Therefore, in this matter, while I have explained the principle that should apply, I
am proposing only to keep in the Exchequer
Economy Bill-
Second Reading.
13 MAY 1926

-d

itia
the

کان
a sum equal to one-third of the annual
balance of the Dogs' Fund. This will
amount to about £4,000 a year, while the
local authorities will receive £8,000.
In speaking of the annual balance of the
Dogs' Fund, I should like to explain more
clearly what is implied in this phrase, because it is a little complex, and, perhaps,
from the reading of the Clause it is not fully
understood. There are expenses connected
with the issue of dogs' licences. The Inland
Revenue authorities issue stamps, the Petty
Sessions clerks collect the duty, and these
expenses have to be paid from the proceeds
before anything is handed over to the local
authorities. Then the police safeguard the
revenue, examine the licences, prosecute
offenders, and so on. In fact, the value of
the tax as a source of revenue is dependent
entirely on their vigilance. I have, therefore, decided that the expenses of the R.U.C.
should in all fairness be a deduction from
the proceeds before the balance is struck.
This is provided for in Sub-section (2) of
Clause 9.
There is still another provision of the
Clause which requires some explanation.
Sub-section (1) refers to fines on defaulting
jurors. I may say that this has nothing
whatever to do with the Bill now receiving
so much attention in another place. It
amounts only to this-that the police have
hitherto received a small proportion of these
fines as a recompense for their work in
tracking down defaulting jurors. In future
I propose that we must regard this as part
of the normal duties of the police, and so
I propose that in future these sums should
be credited to the Police Vote as an appropriation in aid.
In fairness to jurors, I must say that the
sum accruing from fines of this character
is not large, as will be expected. That
explains the features of Clause 9.
I come now to Clause 10 of the Bill, which
deals with another side of our relations
with the local authorities. Indeed, it is
so plain from the reading of the Clause
itself that it requires very little explanation
from me. But let me say that at the present
time we are bound by statute to pay annually
the sum of £42,881 to road authorities in aid
of expenditure on roads and bridges. This
grant dates from a long period antecedent
to the establishment of the Road Fund,
which introduced an entirely new method
of financing road expenditure, and, therefore, the old principle has become a decided
anomaly.
It is proposed, therefore, that
this expenditure should now fall into line
with the other expenditure of the same
nature, and the Clause proposes to make
provisions to this end.
I suggest it is an anachronism that from
the Exchequer we should provide a sum of
£43,000 for road work, and that out of the
Road Fund a contribution should be made
to the local authorities for this purpose.
Simplicity and business method require that
this should be dealt with through one
central avenue, and for that purpose I propose that the amount of £42,881, instead of
issuing from the Exchequer, shall issue from
the Road Fund towards the maintenance and
upkeep of the roads of the Province. This
should tend to simplify the accounts between
the State and the local authorities. These
two changes will relieve the Exchequer to
the extent of approximately £49,000.
In my earlier remarks I have endeavoured
to show the extent of the State's necessities
at the present time in its effort to provide
for the heavy abnormal obligations that
are thrown upon it, obligations that were
never contemplated when we undertook the
Government of Northern Ireland, and of
which the whole of the Province receives
the benefit. The vast sums which are paid
in relief of unemployment naturally relieve
the local authorities of heavy financial responsibilities in respect of poor-law relief,
and when discussing the financial relations
between these two bodies regard must be
had to the burden which is thrown upon the
State by abnormal conditions that obtain.
The credit of the State must be maintained. The State in this case, of course,
means the people, and whether the burden
is borne by the State or the local authorities it must be carried, otherwise all
government comes to an end.
The principle of equal rights with our
fellow countrymen in Britain has been contended for by the Government, and we have
met with success, but the local authorities
must bear in mind that as compared with
similar bodies in Britain they occupy a very
privileged position. I cannot but forcibly
stress this point, because we meet it in our
discussions with the British Government re-
'garding financial matters. If we claim
equal rights for our people in many social
services, it is difficult to resist the British
claim that we should equally bear the same
burdens, and, although local authorities are
laudably exercised to reduce the burden of
rates, they must not overlook the fact that
in respect of such matters as Education,
Police, Valuation, etc., they are much more
favourably circumstanced than similar
bodies in Britain.
I doubt very much whether local bodies
appreciate the importance of this, and the
fact that the Northern Government, in conducting negotiations with the British Government, have this difficulty to face. Indeed, in
my own negotiations with the Treasury on
the other side, and when arguing our case
before the different Tribunals that have determined our financial destiny, I found it
exceedingly difficult to meet that argument.
Economy Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Pollock]
Therefore, I think that in the small matters
which I have referred to in this Bill, which
do not impose any material burden on the
local authorities, the House will feel that the
Government is acting not only fairly but
generously towards local bodies. If any of
the details of the Measure require any
further explanation I shall be happy to give
it.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to crave the indulgence of the House in the few remarks I
wish to make upon this Measure as I shall
probably not participate in the further stages
of the Bill. I wish to congratulate the
right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Finance, on the eloquent manner in which
he has explained the provisions of this Bill
from the point of view of the Government,
but like all Bills this Bill has two sides. It
can be looked at from the side of those who
are disinterested, inasmuch as their homes,
their comforts, their happiness, and their
health would not be influenced by the provisions of the Measure.
I approach the consideration of the Bill,
not from a disinterested point of view, but
from the point of view of a Member of an
Approved Society and from the point of view
of one who was engaged at the inception of
the National Health Insurance Act in inducing as many as possible of his fellow
countrymen to join the Approved Societies
on the distinct understanding that there was
a guarantee being given to them on the part
of their employers and the State in return
for their contribution, that would be fulfilled in the letter and in the spirit. I do
not think this Bill is continuing the fulfilment of that guarantee which was given to
the people who were induced to join Approved
Societies.
I would ask the House to permit me to
take a bird's-eye view of this particular
question, because this is distinctly an Economy Bill, and the basis on which the
Societies whose funds are going to be affected
by this Measure was established should be
taken into consideration, to see that no guarantee then given is now withdrawn. The
National Health Insurance Act was instituted before the lamentable War and the
effect of the War has necessarily been that,
notwithstanding the fact that the Treasury
has guaranteed the present basis of benefits
and the present reserves, the present abnormal reserves are not the result of what
would have been the normal operation of the
Act as originally placed upon the Statute
Book. That is due to the fact that large
numbers of individuals had guaranteed to
their Approved Societies an exchequer reserve which was equivalent to the reserve
they would have to their credit had they
been insured persons from the age of 16 years.
I understand the Government has taken steps
to safeguard these reserves in so far as they
were connected with those of our fellow
countrymen who participated in and lost
their lives in the War.
Mr. POLLOCK: Hear, hear.
Mr. DORMAN: But I do not think the
Government has given due and proper con
sideration to the contributions which were
paid, contributions the obligations and benefits in respect of which have been removed
from the purview of the Approved Societies
and which have necessarily enabled them to
build up vast reserves which, as the right
hon. Gentleman has mentioned, comes to the
enormous sum of £65,000,000. These funds
are subject periodically to special examination
by qualified actuaries, and the Societies must
hold in reserve a sufficient proportion of
whatever profits they have made for the purpose of meeting the future liabilities that
will necessarily accrue during the operation
of the Act.
This Bill applies to a section of the community that I do not think any person will
deny has made considerable economies, from
the point of view that their standard of com
fort and subsistence has been reduced during
the period that the Act has been in operation. I do not wish to take up the time of
the House unnecessarily, but I would like to
say that during the period of the Great War,
the economy effected by the class who will be
affected by the provisions of this Measure,
was never in parity with the inflated cost of
living. The other class, who can look upon
this Bill from a completely disinterested
point of view, inasmuch as their lives are not
affected by its provisions, derived enormous
advantages during the same period.
The late Mr. Bonar Law, when Chancellor
of the Exchequer, called upon one of the most
eminent statisticians in Great Britain to give
him an estimate of the fortunes that had been
increased during that period, and it was
found by this celebrated statistician that the
income of that class of the community, who
were not subject to State Insurance, amounted
to the vast sum of over £5,000,000,000. The
Revenue authorities presented a White Book,
and it was found that the actual returns
made for income tax and super-tax were over
£4,000,000,000, or a sum equivalent to 50 per
cent. of the total personal wealth of the
citizens before the War.
Since the period of the War, the class who
will be affected by this Measure has made
considerable economies, without in any degree
dislocating the business of the State, or
striking against a reduction in their wages.;
In the year 1921, that particular class of the
community was subjected to a reduction in
their weekly earnings of over £6,024,500 per
week. During 1921-22 they were subjected to

Second Reading.

F
a
reduction of ever £4,209,500 per week in
their weekly wages, and during the financial
year, 1923, they were subjected to a reduction
of over £317,000 per week in their weekly
wages. Last year-1 am quoting from
memory-they were subjected to an economy
of £79,000 per week in their weekly wages.
I mention this because this is the class of the
community whose interests are going to be
adversely affected by the provisions of this
particular Measure.
Take the present position. During and
after the war the various Approved Societies,
with the consent of the Government, increased the sick benefits of a male adult by
50 per cent. That 50 per cent. increase is not
in parity with the pre-war benefit of 10s a
male adult received by a sum of 1s 3d. They
also increased the sick benefit that was given
to female members of Approved Societies, and
they increased the maternity benefit. I
understand that the present purchasing
capacity of the increase of 25 per cent. given
in maternity benefit is approximately about
8s short of being in parity with the maternity
benefit which a member of an Approved
society would have received before the war
broke out. I have interviewed representatives of Approved Societies representing over
200,000 members in Northern Ireland, and
with one voice they expressed the opinion
that this Bill is going to have a disastrous
effect upon the future of the members of
their societies.
a
At its last valuation
society with
approximately 20,000 members had a surplus
of £5,000. The head of that society informs
me that the effect of this Measure on the
Members of his society will be that they will
have to suffer a loss of about £1,000 per year
during the next five years, so that at the end
of five years, assuming they continue their
present rate of benefits to their members,
they will have absorbed that surplus of £5,000
over and above that which they actually considered necessary for meeting future
liabilities. I believe the Minister of
Finance's heart would never have permitted
him to introduce a Measure such as this in
Northern Ireland if it had not been preceded
by the Bill passed in the Imperial Parliament.
Mr. POLLOCK: Hear, hear.
Mr. DORMAN: I am glad to hear that
expression from the right hon. Gentleman.
It is a Bill that is due entirely to the
exigencies of the Budget in Great Britain.
Mr. POLLOCK: Hear, hear.
Mr. DORMAN: When the present Imperial
Chancellor of the Exchequer came into office
it was estimated he would have a balance in
his favour at the end of the financial year of
approximately £40,000,000. The right hon.
Gentleman, of course, looked round, as all
Chancellors do, to see how he could best dispose of that vast surplus, and he disposed of
it by giving relief to the income-tax and
super-tax payers. His predecessor had disposed of a surplus of about three-quarters of
that amount by relieving the breakfast table
of the mass of the working classes of Great
Britain and Northern Ireland.
It is not my duty, and I do not intend, in any
degree, to criticise the Chancellor of the Imperial Exchequer, but it appears to me that
it would be a more just and equitable, and a
more humane step if, when he found that
owing to the unexpected industrial struggle
in the coal trade it was necessary to pay a
subsidy to carry on that particular basic
industry, he had looked forward to reimposing the income tax and super-tax he had
taken off, for the purpose of meeting the deficiency, instead of raiding the future reserves
of these Approved Societies. Not one member
of the community who was contributing to
the upkeep of the State by paying incometax and super-tax would be affected at the
breakfast or dinner table owing to the reimposition of that portion of income-tax and
super-tax that was taken off by the Chancellor, but on the other hand this Bill will
affect the breakfast, dinner and tea table of
hundreds of thousands of the class least able
to bear a burden of this kind.
I would like to quote a statement from a
man whom I conceive to be one of the best
authorities on this particular subject. I
understand he was an official connected with
the Insurance Department, and he now occupies the eminent position of Chairman of the
National Amalgamated Approved Society, a
Society which I can say from my own personal knowledge had an enormous advantage
at the inception of the National Health Insurance scheme over any of the Societies that
were set up in Northern Ireland. Without
having to expend any money this Society had
to hand a qualified staff, ready to reap a rich
harvest of members in Northern Ireland.
Writing in the "Belfast News-Letter" of
28th April, this gentleman said:
Speaking as I do for an Approved
Society with a membership in Northern
Ireland exceeding 20,000, I desire to express
the very deep concern of the committee of
management of this Society with what they
believe will be the disastrous results of the
Bill. It is well known that in consequence
of the absence of medical benefit in Northern Ireland the claim experience of the
majority of Approved Societies is considerably in excess of the experience in England,
with the result that so far as Northern
Irish membership is concerned this Society
has been working at a loss. The consequences of the reduction in the State grant,
Economy Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
therefore, will be to increase our loss, and
I would earnestly ask the Government to
consider whether it would not be far
better to scrap the Act altogether.
Further on, he stated:-
It was recently stated before the Departmental Commission on Local Government
Administration in Northern Ireland that a
comparison of vital statistics in the
different countries of the United Kingdom
clearly demonstrated that in many respects
Northern Ireland was a bad last, and it
seems to me that it is unwise for the
Government to choose this time to make
matters worse.
I also have here an expression of opinion
from an official of one of the largest Approved
Societies in Northern Ireland. He says:-
At the inception of the Insurance Act
Societies were urged to set a high administration standard before them, and
they were assured that if on valuation a
disposable surplus remained (after providing for future liabilities) such surplus
could be utilised for additional benefits for
members. Not a few Societies, acting on the
advice given, have been fortunate in having
certified surpluses for disposal, and they
have formulated schemes of "treatment"
benefits, such as dental, hospital, optical,
and convalescent home benefits, all of
which have proved of immense value to
members, many of whom could not have
obtained such benefits except through the
help given by Insurance Societies. I fail
to see how Societies can hope to continue
these useful and important benefits in the
near future if the proposals now before
Parliament become law.
I well remember lecturing on several occasions, and being successful in bringing into
Approved Societies four or five whole Battalions of His Majesty's Forces in Northern
Ireland, with the exception of some twenty
men. One of the things I was authorised to
say at those meetings was that the benefits
promised would be secured, and that all the
surplus that would accrue would be set apart
in the future for the purpose of increasing
the very low standard of benefit which was
granted under the original scheme, because
10s a week for a man with a family was
absolutely insufficient to enable him to live
anything like a happy life, especially when
he had dependants to support.
I wonder if the Chancellor of the Exchequer
in Great Britain has taken into consideration
the wrong-I use the word wrong advisedlyhe has inflicted upon hundreds of thousands
of members of Approved Societies by this
Bill. Take, for instance, the wrong that will
be inflicted in Northern Ireland on a man
who has a wife and three children, and who
has not exhausted his guaranteed benefit at
the Unemployment Exchange. If that man,
through not having sufficient means to pur.
chase the necessary food to keep his vitality
up, becomes sick, he is bound, if he is an
honest man and a loyal citizen, immediately
to acquaint the authorities at the Labour
Exchange that he is making application for
benefit under the National Insurance Act.
Having made that application he is not, and
cannot be, genuinely looking for work. The
income he has to bring into his home during
the time he is drawing benefit from the
Approved Society is 15s per week. He has
been in receipt of Unemployment Benefit to
the amount of 29s per week, and his benefit
is not exhausted, but as an honest and loyal
man he is conscientiously bound to inform
the Labour Exchange authorities that he is
drawing benefits from the Approved Society,
and thus submit to having to accept the
miserable pittance granted under the State
Insurance scheme.
I do not wish to detain the House further,
but as one who is a member of an Approved
Society, and who has taken a deep interest
in this particular question, I wish to deliberately and boldly state that I regard this as
the most partisan and the most outrageous
Bill that has ever been brought before the
Parliament of the Six Counties.
Mr. BARBOUR: I would like to join with
Senator Dorman in congratulating our Chancellor of the Exchequer on the very lucid and
clear way he has explained the main points
of the Bill before us. I am sure each
one of us in this House is sympathetic towards those who are not so well placed in life
as we may happen to be, and we all welcome
the promise the right hon. Gentleman gave
us that the interests of these people, who are
likely to have recourse to these funds, will
not be prejudiced in any way.
However, it was not on that point that I
wished to say a word or two. It was more in
connection with the troubles of some of our
local bodies. Unfortunately we have not at
our disposal ability such as the Minister of
Finance can command and provide for looking after his Budget, and I would venture
to remind him that, with that less fortunate
measure of ability, we also have to take our
small share in preparing a lesser Budget,
which is, perhaps, just as difficult and just
as important to us as his Budget is to him.
I would also remind the right hon. Gentleman that we have been disappointed once or
twice, and have had grave misgivings about
proportionate grants that were promised to
us, and our memory is singularly tender as
regards these things.
As a matter of fact, this year all our public
bodies, with great difficulty, had just produced their Budgets, and one of the first
Economy Bill-
Second Reading.

things that happened afterwards was an
addition to the Budget Fund. Unfortunately
that brought in its train certain sacrifices
which we would have to make which really
prejudiced our case and added a good deal to
our difficulties. I would put it to the
Minister of Finance that with the lesser
abilities and powers we had at our command
he should sympathetically consider our plea.
It is very unfortunate when a Budget is being
produced to find that certain income one
expected has been taken away, and at one
period of this Bill's existence certain
additional expenditure was proposed to be
forced upon us.
The Minister of Finance knows that none
of the public bodies in Northern Ireland
would add to his difficulties in any way, and
we realise quite fully that he would be the last
person to put any difficulties in our way,
but we would ask him, if it is possible in
the future when these alterations are contemplated-and we are quite ready to admit
there are very strong arguments in their
favour and that they do seem to be necessary-he would take local authorities a little
more into his confidence. If he does he
will find a very ready measure of support
and willingness to sympathise with his difficulties. Some of these bodies I believe have
been alluded to as cave dwellers, but the
Minister might find that even cave dwellers
have a heart and might also have generous
sentiments.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to join with
other members of the Senate in saying how
glad we are to see the Minister of Finance
in this House and how much we have enjoyed listening to the speech he
given
us.
