Mr. DORMAN: I rise to express my
approval of this Bill, and I think the thanks
of the Government and people of Northern
Ireland are due to the Trustees of the
Balmoral School for so generously handing
over their school to the Government for the
reclamation of the social derelicts of the
community of Northern Ireland. I believe
it was entirely wrong in the past to allow
such derelicts, who were mainly such in consequence of the economic condition of their
parents. In the past it has been found that
most of the children who have become incarcerated in schools of this kind and who
became criminals were largely from the ranks
of the widowed mothers of the working
classes.
The gentleman who is responsible for the
introduction of widowed mothers' and
orphans' pensions, and who for many months
occupied the position of Judge in the
Children's Court in the State of New York,
carried on an extensive investigation in that
particular State, and he was driven to the
conclusion that most of the juveniles charged
with crimes were from the ranks of the
Orphans of working-class mothers of the
through economic stress and difficulty, had
almost to consent, unwillingly, to their
children pursuing a course of crime in order
to bring the necessaries of life to the home.
Therefore, I think this Measure deserves the
support of every shade of opinion, and 1
trust it will have the result the Government
dis looking forward to in the reclamation of
many of the children who sway unfortunately
in the future be led into a questionable
course of life.
gth
рета
Mr. LESLIE: It has been said that everything comes to the man who knows how to
wait, and I think that, perhaps, that old saying might be not unfittingly applied to the
Minister of Home Affairs in connection with
this particular Bill. All of us know that the
due disposal of boys and young men who
ought to be sent to a Borstal Institution or
to a Reformatory School has been beset with
difficulties for many years past. The causes
of the difficulties have been outlined to some
extent by the noble Viscount. I am sure,
from my own practical experience as a
✓ magistrate, that the difficulties of having
suitable and accessible places to send a boy
to have very often a good deal to do with the
magistrates' decision to apply the provisions
کا
of the First Offenders Acts to cases for which
I have sometimes thought those Acts were
never in reality intended.
Therefore, in congratulating the Minister
hs of Home Affairs to-day on the success which
has at last crowned his efforts. I am glad to
val find myself for once in agreement with the
hon. Member on my left (Mr. Dorman). But
L
I cannot go so far as to agree with him that
the young people who it is hoped will benefit
by these schools can properly be described
as derelicts. I think it is rather unjust and
perhaps a little against our modern spirit
to describe as derelicts young people of the
ages to which these schools apply. They are
comparatively young. I think the latest age
is twenty-one, and to describe a young fellow
of under twenty-one as a derelict because
he has for once offended, and been caught,
is perhaps a little more severe than the hon.
Member intended.
Mr. DORMAN: What I said was that they
were social derelicts.
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot agree that they are
derelicts at all. I think they are brands to
be snatched from the burning, and I hope
this school is going to help to snatch them.
Passing from that to the details, to some
extent, of the Bill, I do not see anything in
the Measure bearing on Part 10 of the Education Act. That is the part dealing with
Reformatories, and which enables the Ministers of Education and Home Affairs, in consultation, to make arrangements for the
education of these children. Therefore, I
presume it is not intended that the Education Act should be in any way affected, and
if it should turn out that mutual arrangements can be made they will be made.
Another small point is even more curious.
In starting this new Industrial School we
are embarking on an absolutely new undertaking in Northern Ireland. We have
nothing to guide us. There was an old Irish
Reformatory Act which was repealed, and
there was the Clonmel Institution, and what
I am wondering is whether the Minister of
Home Affairs has asked for sufficient powers
in the matter of the appointment of officers.
The question seems to me to be what we
mean by officers, and whether it is necessary
to define that very general term on this
occasion. If it is it can only be because we
have nothing to guide us. If you look back
at the Prisons Acts you will find there is no
definition, and there is no definition in our
Education Act or in the Children Act, but
if you turn to the Local Government Act of
1898, you will find that for some inexplicable
reason Parliament went out of its way to
define officers, and said that the expression
"officers" should be construed accordingly.
Therefore, I think we ought, perhaps-and
I would like the Government to make sure,
in order that if further powers are necessary
they should be taken to make sure that our
words are wide enough to cover part-time
officers, such, for instance, as Chaplains, if
it is necessary to appoint them, and it may
not be, because there is a provision in the
Children Act which might meet the case.
There is the possible case of Chaplains, and
323 Malone Training School Bill.
Ill-treated Married Women. 324
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]

the possible case of doctors. I should like to
be quite sure that the Government is satisfied they have taken every necessary power.
Another point I want to draw attention to
is of a very different character. We say
towards the end of the Bill that those pensioners, who are at present drawing pensions
in respect of their past employment at the
Malone Training School, are to have those
pensions continued to them during their
lifetime out of moneys provided by Parliament. That is an equitable provision, but
the same Clause which gives them that continuing right, and which deals also with
superannuation claims that may arise, ends
up by providing, or purporting to provide,
that if any question arises the matter is to
be settled by the Minister of Finance. Prima
facie, that would seem to be a case in which
the pensioner drawing his lawful pension will
be debarred, if any dispute arises, from his
ordinary rights of recourse to the courts.
Probably we shall be told that either there
are no cases or that the question does not
arise, but I think the House will see there
is a suggestion there of a principle which we
should be slow to admit. It might be better
to deal with that point at a later stage in
order that it may be looked into. Those
matters are details of a very small character.
On the whole I should like to conclude by
saying I believe this Bill will be found to be
of very great and lasting benefit to us in
Northern Ireland.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I welcome the Bill in every way, but there is
just one small point I would like to raise,
and it is a very important one. The failure
of certain negotiations is really the chief
reason why these schools have been in such
a poor state financially in the past. One of
the reasons why a decreased number of boys
has been sent to reformatory schools was
that the county councils and the Ministry of
Finance could never agree as to what share
of the expenses should be borne by the
Government and the county councils respectively. I quite admit that the county
councils should bear some of the expense,
but under the Bill it is left entirely in the
hands of the Ministry of Finance to decide
what amount the county councils shall pay
for the maintenance of these boys. In that
case the old crux will arise again, and the
county councils and the magistrates will find
that the rates cannot afford to bear the expense without assistance from the Government.
If this Bill is to be a success the county
councils will have to receive some financial
assistance from the Government, and not be
called upon to bear the whole of the expense.
It is quite clear from the Bill that, at the
present time, the Ministry of Finance can,
without any consultation with the county
councils, settle what the county councils
will have to pay for the maintenance of these
boys, and when the Committee stage of the
Bill is reached, I hope it will be made clear
that the county councils will receive some
financial assistance from the Government.
Otherwise I do not think the Bill will be the
success it ought to be.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: With regard
to the points raised by hon. Senators, I
would be glad if they would again mention
these points on the Committee stage, by
which time I shall have had an opportunity
of looking into them.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the whole
House for to-morrow
Protection of Ill-treated Married
Women.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
any representations have been received from
the Belfast Women's Advisory Council as
regards the desirability of legislation for the
better protection of ill-treated married
women and their children, and, if so, whether
it is the intention of the Government to take
any action in the matter.
Mr. LESLIE: In rising to ask the question
which stands in my name on the Paper, and
which relates to a representation made by
the Belfast Women's Advisory Council, I may
say at once that I recognise the House as a
whole is aware that the Belfast Women's
Advisory Council has a great knowledge of
matters relating particularly to women, as
they have numerous sources of information.
The question of the desirability of legislation
for the better protection of ill-treated
married women and their children has been
before this House on more than one occasion
before. I may perhaps venture to recall that
I drew attention to the matter when speaking
to the debate on the Address to the Gracions
Speech in Parliament last year, and subsequently, in the month of May, in accordance with the scheme which I had outlined
for myself in that debate, I introduced a
Bill into this House and this House did me
the honour of giving the Bill a Second
Reading.
That Bill was based on Mr. Byrne's private
Bill of 1895, which subsequently became an
Act, and that was applicable to England, but
not, unfortunately, to Northern Ireland.
When the Bill was read a Second time it was
agreed by common consent and I think
nobody could say it more readily than myself
-that it was not desirable or possible that
325 Ill-treated Married Women.
Ill-treated Married Women. 326
3 NOVEMBER 1926
the Bill should go further at that time,
because there were difficulties in the way.
One was that a Bill amending the English
law was at that moment actually under discussion in the Imperial House of Commons,
and that Bill, I may say parenthetically, was
ultimately passed with Amendments made in
the House of Lords. We were told we had to
wait until that Bill was through, and there
was also a small difficulty as to whether an
application of the old Act of 1878 did or did
not apply to Ireland. There seemed some
little doubt about that but it has now been
set at rest for ever because the Act in
question has been repealed by the Parliament
of the United Kingdom.
as
At a later date in the year, in the month
of June, before we rose for the Summer
Recess, I made inquiry as to whether the
Government had received any intimation
that public opinion regarded the Bill as a
matter of urgency, or of necessity, or even
of desirability, and I did not receive a reply
which, if I had had any personal feeling in
the matter, I could have regarded
pecularily satisfactory to myself. I am
sorry to say that the noble Marquess who
was then leading this House felt constrained
to tell us that he was afraid it must be confessed that the public had not taken the
slightest interest in the Bill. However, the
noble Marquess said also that the Government were obtaining the opinion of a Resident Magistrate of experience.
I
I am sure the experience of Resident
Magistrates would be of great value to us
with regard to cases that had come before
them, but I am not sure that it is to the
Resident Magistrate you must look if you
want information as to what is happening
in cases that do not come before him.
think it is rather to your Ministers of religion, your district visitors, your district
nurses, and vour dispensary doctors you must
go if you desire information as to the sufferings of women and children. Since then we
have heard no more about it until the other
day, when it would appear from the newspapers that the Women's Council I have referred to, approached the Home Office on the
subject.
of
The main point at issue is that the woman
in Northern Ireland has not the same protection that she would have if she resided in
England, for in England she would have
the right
something approaching
a judicial separation, as the phrase goes
there. in the case of an aggravated assault
upon her by her husband, or of persistent
cruelty by her husband to her or her children, and she would be able to get provision,
according to means, not only for herself but
separately for her children, and she would
have the services of an officer of the Court to
secure the regular payment of those sums.
That is, a married woman would be in at
least as good a position as regards her children as an unmarried woman has been
placed in as regards her children.
In Northern Ireland we have only one Act
to depend on, and that is the Act which does
require a man who has deserted his wife to
pay something up to £2. That desertion has
had to be twisted by decisions of the Courts
and has now got to such an extent that a
man is held to have deserted his wife if his
wife has in fact deserted him, it being held
that it is by his cruelty and so on, that he
has compelled his wife to leave him. The
old Act of 1886 was not considered satisfactory, and I do not myself see why it should be
regarded as sufficient for Northern Ireland.
However, I am perhaps going a little beyond
what I ought to do in asking the question
as to whether these representations have been
received, and whether it is the intention of
the Government to do anything now or in
the comparatively immediate future.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Before
the Leader of the House replies to the question, I should like to say that I quite agree
with the remarks of the hon. Senator. However, there are two sides to every question,
and the side that Senator Leslie did not
mention was the poor weak man who suffers
from the violence of his wife. I hope that
in any legislation that may be brought forward, such a man will receive equal protection. (Laughter.)
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The question
raised by the hon. Member is one of great
importance and many technical considerations are involved, and I must warn the
House that the length of my reply will be
commensurate with the importance of the
question raised..
Representations have been received from
the Belfast Women's Advisory Council
advocating the application of the Guardianship of Infants Act, 1925, and the summary
Jurisdiction (Separation and Maintenance)
Act, 1925, to Northern Ireland. My right hon.
Friend. the Minister of Home Affairs, saw a
deputation from the Council and discussed
the matter with them at considerable length.
The subject is one which is full of technicalities. and I am not at all sure that the
Council are fully cognizant of the present
state of the law or that they appreciate
the full significance of the changes which
they advocate.
We might, perhaps, leave the Guardianship
of Infants Act out of account for the moment,
as it was undoubtedly passed as a result
of the separation and maintenance legislation
which was not made applicable to Northern
Ireland. With regard to the separation and
maintenance legislation, I do not think it
327 Ill-treated Married Wemen
Ill-treated Married Women 328
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
will be suggested that the Government should
embark on legislation of this kind unless it
has been found by experience that the existing law has been found wanting in an
appreciable number of cases. On the contrary, if the existing law has proved itself by
experience to be capable of meeting the
reasonable needs of the community the
House will, I am sure, agree with me that it
would be a mistake to discard a code which
is showing itself to be reasonably efficient and
to introduce a new code which may be excellent in Great Britain, but may not be at all
suitable to the needs of the people here.
The main distinctions between the English
law on the subject and the law in force in
Northern Ireland are twofold, namely:-1.
the grounds on which the Order may be made,
and 2, the nature of the Order. The legal
advisers of the Government have considered
this matter carefully, and we have consulted
persons with extensive experience in the
practical administration of the law on this
point. We are satisfied that the difference
between English and Irish law with regard to
the point which I first mentioned, namely,
the grounds on which the Order may be made,
are far more apparent than real, though I
freely admit that the layman would not
arrive at this conclusion from a perusal of
the various Acts.
The English Code sets forth a great many
specific grounds on which a Separation Order
can be made, including assault, desertion,
habitual drunkenness, cruelty to the
children and
SO forth; whereas the
Irish
Code simply makes desertion
practically the only ground. But there
have been numerous judicial decisions
on the meaning of this expression and it has
been extended to cover much that the layman would not have understood by the word.
We are assured that in practice the Section
in the Irish Act is sufficiently wide to cover
almost any case that would be covered by the
specific grounds set out at length in the
English Act. Perhaps I might give an instance of what I mean. It has been decided
that cruelty carried out with the intention
of compelling the wife to leave home constitutes desertion, and generally speaking, if
the husband, by his conduct, compels the
wife to leave home, it is desertion on the part
of the husband.
There is one point that might at first sight
appear of great importance, and considerable
stress has been laid upon it from time to
The Irish law contains no specific
time.
provision for the children, whereas the English law enables the Court to make an Order
that the husband shall contribute up to a
maximum of 10s per week for each child.
At first this appears to be a very cogent
argument for amending the legislation, but
the force of the contention is dissipated when
the matter is examined in the light of praetical experience. The Courts have power to
make an Order in favour of the wife up to a
maximum of £2 per week, and in fixing the
amount, the children can be taken into
account. In how many cases could an Order
for more than £2 a week be enforced against
a man who is brought into Court under this
Code? To make an Order of a greater
amount is merely to ask for evasion.
You cannot give a man the capacity to
pay three or four pounds a week by saying
in an Act of Parliament that the Court shall
have power to make an Order that he is to
pay that amount.
a
I may add that there is
provision
in the Children Act, 1908, that where
the father is convicted of cruelty to
his children or neglect to maintain them, he
can be ordered to pay up to 20s per week for
the maintenance of each child, and the custody of the children can be given to the wife.
In practice it is very seldom that this power
is invoked, which undoubtedly goes to show
that the provisions relating to the grant of
maintenance to the mother provide a remedy
as great as it is practicable to enforce.
I now come to the second aspect of the
matter, namely, the nature of the Order that
can be made, and it is admitted that in this
respect there is a very real and practical
difference between English legislation and
that in force here, but the Government is not
at all convinced that it would be wise to
adopt the English system in Northern Ire
land. Under the English Acts of 1895 and
1925, Separation Orders in the nature of a
divorce a mensa et thoro can be granted by
English Courts of Summary Jurisdiction,
and in order to provide for the custody of
children, where Separation Orders are made,
the jurisdiction of the High Court and
County Courts, under the Guardianship of
Infants Act, 1886, were extended to Courts of
Summary Jurisdiction by the Guardianship
of Infants Acts, 1925.
Hon. Members will easily appreciate what
a drastic change the application of this
legislation to Northern Ireland would
mean, and we have no evidence that
there is any substantial body of public
opinion in favour of the change. The advocates of the proposal to put the law of
Northern Ireland on a par with the law of
England on this point, including, I think,
the hon. Senator who has asked this question,
have disclaimed any desire to promote any
Measure weakening the marriage tie, but I
fail to see how it can be argued that a proposal to grant to Courts of Summary Jurisdiction power which would enable them to
give what practically amounts to a divorce
a mensa et thoro would not have this effect.
In the year 1924, there were 163 applications for Maintenance Orders in Northern
Treland, of which 152 were in the City of
329 il-treated Married Women.

3 NOVEMBER 1926
Adjournment.

Belfast. Orders were made in 125 cases, of
which 117 were in the City of Belfast, and
20 Orders for imprisonment to enforce Maintenance Orders were made during the same
year. I would point out that, of course,
there were very many more applications for
Orders to enforce Maintenance Orders, but
it was only found necessary to proceed to
extreme measures in 20 cases. Another interesting fact which I might mention to the
House is that although in a period of 12
months there were numerous applications to
- enforce Maintenance Orders, in no more than
6 cases was the Order more than two years'
old.

الو

These statistics go to confirm the opinion
of the persons to whom I referred before as
having great practical experience in this
matter, that what is required in Northern
Ireland is something which will temporarily
bridge over the comparatively short period
during which there is difficulty between
husband and wife. It is highly desirable
that the form of the Order should not in any
way make it more difficult for the husband
and wife to come together again, and the
statistics which I quoted seemed to show
that this is what occurs in the very great
majority of cases here.
The provisions of the English Code in our
opinion, and in the opinion of those competent to advise us, do undoubtedly tend to
make the separation permanent, and this is
a thing which we are very anxious to avoid.
I am not prepared to say that isolated cases
may not arise at infrequent intervals which
could be more advantageously dealt with
under the English Code, but they are of very
rare occurrence, and so far, in the Government's opinion, no case has been made which
would justify them in introducing legislation
which would fundamentally change our law,
in order to deal with very exceptional cases.
There is the further question which I need
not labour, namely, whether it is desirable
to entrust these drastic powers to Magistrates in Northern Ireland, and I think hon.
Members can see for themselves that there
are considerations with regard to this subject
which have to be taken account of here,
but which probably do not apply with equal
force in England.
With regard to the Guardianship of Infants Act, 1925, I do not propose to deal with
that at any length; it is undoubtedly a development of the Code regarding Separation
Orders in England. Under the Guardianship
of Infants Act, 1886, the Court, on the application of the mother, may make an Order
regarding the custody of an infant, having
regard to the welfare of the infant and the
conduct of the parents, and to the wishes as
well of the mother as of the father. The
mother may appoint a guardian to act after
the death of both parents, or jointly with the
father if he survives her, and the Court can
confirm this appointment or supersede the
father if it thinks right. In all cases the welfare of the child is the paramount consideration.
This iurisdiction is exercised in Northern
Ireland both by the High Court and the
County Courts. The latter Court furnishes
a cheap and expeditious method of dealing
with the applications of persons of small
means, and the Government cannot see any
sufficient reason for altering the law in this
respect.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
minutes after Four o'clock.

THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 4th November, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
The Commons have passed the Protection
of Animals (Miscellaneous Provisions) Bill,
the Horse Breeding Bill, and the Housing
(No. 2) Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
PROTECTION OF ANIMALS (MISCEL-
LANEOUS PROVISIONS) BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
HORSE BREEDING BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
HOUSING (No. 2) BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Loans Guarantee (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill be now
read the Third time.
Mr. LONG: I desire to support the Third
Reading of this Bill, believing that it will be
an eminently useful Measure and one which
will enable many firms and industrial concerns
to carry on and provide much needed employment. My object is not only to support the
Bill but to elicit information from the noble
Lord who has moved the Third Reading. I
should like to ascertain whether these loans
are applicable to those engaged in agriculture, or whether there is any fund in
Northern Ireland analagous to the old Board
of Works which functioned in Dublin and
from which farmers could obtain loans for
the drainage of their land, the improvement
of their buildings, the erection of hay sheds,
and for various other purposes. Numerons
inquiries have been addressed to me as to
whether there was anything of that nature
in operation at the present time. I must
confess that I was unable to give the information, and I should be glad to be furnished
with it now.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I shall be very pleased to give the
hon. Senator the information he is seeking.
I think he is labouring under a slight misapprehension when he thinks that the facilities provided by the Loans Guarantee Act
apply to farmers who require accommodation
for various purposes. I would like to emphasise one point, which perhaps I did not
make sufficiently clear when moving the
Second Reading of this Bill yesterday. That
is this; that the facilities granted under the
Loans Guarantee Act are guarantees, and
merely enable the borrower to obtain loans
on reasonable terms from the Banks, Insurance Companies, and other lenders of money.
It will be easily seen that without a guarantee public companies would have to make a
public issue involving a much higher rate of
interest than they would ordinarily be prepared to pay.
I would like to assure the hon. Senator
that under several existing enactments
farmers are enabled to borrow money from
the Government Loans Fund for a variety of
purposes. In pre-transfer days, as the hon.
Member said, these loans were known as
Board of Works loans and the terms of such
loans are no doubt very familiar to the hon.
Member himself. Amongst the purposes for
which these loans may be granted I may
mention the erection of hay sheds, the
development and improvement of agricultural land, the purchase of agricultural
machinery, drainage, and so forth. I trust
I have made the position perfectly clear to
the hon. Member and that any doubt which
may have been present in his mind has now
been satisfactorily removed. I would also
like to say that I understand there is at
the present moment a Bill being introduced
in another place which has for its object
increasing the capital of the Government
Loan Fund from two million pounds to four
and a half million pounds.
Mr. DORMAN: I desire to give my support
to this Measure, but I also would like a
little information. I presume that this Bill
is particularly intended to stimulate trade
and encourage the employment of the unemployed in Northern Ireland. It seems to
me to be quite possible for companies to
obtain loans in excess of their subscribed
capital, and I would like to know what
security the Government obtains under such
circumstances. Where a company obtains a
loan in excess of its subscribed capital
would it not be advisable that the Govern-
ading

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Senator, I would
like to mention that before any guarantee
is given the first essential is that there must
be absolute security for the amount of the
guarantee. All these matters are investigated by a committee of experts, who advise
the Minister of Finance whether the security
for the guarantee is sound or not, and I
think it must be quite obvious to the hon.
Senator himself that a guarantee for an
amount exceeding the capital of a company
is scarcely likely to be recommended by a
committee of experts.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: With
regard to the reply given by the noble
Viscount (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard)
to Senator Long, I should like a little more
information. I think that most of us are
not familiar with the Government Loans
Fund, and the people who are most interested
do not know to whom to apply for a loan.
If the noble Viscount would supply that information to Senator Long it would be of
great value to the agricultural community.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I shall have pleasure in doing so.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)

335 Collections at Customs House.

THE SENATE

[Colonel Sinclair]
to attend on Wednesday when the Bill was

Mr. SPEAKER: As the Government, for
the convenience of the House, have agreed
to postpone the Committee Stage of this Bill
the hon. Member would not be in order in

Collections at Customs House Steps
MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to move,
that this House do now adjourn.
Mr. DORMAN: I desire to draw the
attention of the House to a matter in which
I am personally interested, and in which I
have taken a considerable interest for many
years. I have been the recipient of a number
of summonses during the Recess, and I have
been cited before the Court of Petty Session
for taking up a collection at the Customs
House steps. I have been accustomed to
take up this collection for a period of three
years under the auspices of a meeting
originally started by the Rev. J. Bruce
Wallace and myself, with the distinct object
of bringing together the Catholics and Protestants of Belfast as common Christians.
For a period of three years no objection has
been taken to the taking up of such
collection, but since the Recess I have received four summonses.
a
On the first two summonses I satisfied the
Court of Petty Session that the meetings
were of a religious character and therefore
did not come under the order of the Governor
of Northern Ireland regulating public meetings in Northern Ireland. On the third
summons I was fined, and on the fourth I
gave an undertaking not to continue the collection. I understand that the Orders across
the water do not embrace open-air meetings
at all, and I am personally acquainted with
the circumstances that led up to Orders of
this kind being made. They were made
owing to the number of frauds that were
perpetrated on the public during the latter
period of the war and the post-war period by
individuals who were deliberately defrauding
the public by representing that they were
obtaining subscriptions for the purpose of
alleviating the condition of those who had
suffered in the war.
I have taken the trouble to obtain a copy
of the Orders made in Great Britain, and I
find that the Order made in the metropolitan
area of London is entirely different from the
Order made in Northern Ireland. The Order
made in London does not interfere with any
stated set of public meetings or collections
taken up at any such meetings. There are a
large number of organisations connected
with the working-class movement in Great
Britain that have been accustomed, as we
have been accustomed in Belfast, to take up
collections at such meetings, and there they
have not been interfered with. The exact
wording of the Order made in the metropolitan area of London is as follows, and the
House will see-
Mr. SPEAKER: I think I must interrupt
the hon. Member if he is about to suggest
an alteration in the existing law, because he
is out of order in doing so on the motion for
the adjournment. It is open to him either
to bring in a Private Bill on the subject or
else to suggest to the Government, by means
of a question on the Paper, that such an
alteration of the law should be made. To
suggest, on the motion for the adjournment,
that an alteration should be made in the
present law, is out of order. If the hon.
Member was only going to complain of the
action of the police, as I at first thought he
was, that would be quite in order, but I think
I am correct in assuming that he proposed
to suggest an alteration in the law as it at
present stands.
Mr. DORMAN: My object is to draw
attention to the disabilities to which a large
proportion of the people of Northern Ireland
are subject under this particular Order.
For instance, I belong to two organisations,
both of which have ramifications throughout
Great Britain and Northern Ireiand. If I
were across the water I could with impunity
take up à collection, and it is to the disabilities to which we in Northern Ireland are
subject that I wish to draw attention. I make
no suggestion as to an amendment of the law,
but possibly the Government might be led to
amend the Order. I make no suggestion as
to any change--
Mr. SPEAKER: I must rule that this
question is out of order at the present
moment, and I should be obliged if the hon.
Senator would bring up the subject by
question.

Tuesday, 9th November, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Labour:
The Trade Boards (Northern Ireland)
District Trade Committee (Boot and Shoe
Repairing Trade) Regulations, 1926, dated
1st October, 1926, made by the Ministry of
Labour, under Section 18 of the Trade
Boards Act (Northern Ireland), 1923.
Ministry of Home Affairs :
The Northern Ireland Winter Assize
Order, 1926.
Ministry of Commerce:
The Rostrevor Electricity Special Order,
1926.
Presented (pursuant to Act), and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Marketing
of Eggs Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
MARKETING OF EGGS BILL.
Moved: "That this Bill be now read a First
tine."-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Protection of Animals (Miscellaneous
Provisions) Bill.

Order for Second Reading read.
Bill,
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move that this Bill be.
read a Second time. I do not think there is
any Member of this House who will not be
in sincere sympathy with the spirit of the
It is based on humane legislation, which
has been passed by the Imperial Parliament
the date of the transfer of legislative
er to the Parliament of Northern Ire-
1, and is designed to bring the law in
respect into conformity with that in
at Britain.
since
Sand,
this
Great!
As
divide
restric
hon. Members will see, the Bill is
ded into four parts. Part One deals with
relation to performing
crictions in
animals, and applies the provisions of the
Performing Animals Act, which was passed
by the Imperial Parliament last year.
While we have no evidence to show that conditions prevail in Northern Ireland similar
to those which necessitated the passing in
Great Britain of the Act to which I have
referred, yet it will be appreciated that
the very existence of restrictions on the
other side might give rise to abuses here.
It is in order to guard against such an
eventuality that this Bill proposes to impose
the same restrictions as those which are now
in force in Great Britain.
The Bill requires that all persons exhibiting
or training performing animals
shall, in future, be registered. The
fee for registration is in no case
to exceed one guinea, and provision is
made for the charging of a reduced fee to
persons who are already registered in Great
Britain, and who may desire to exhibit their
performing animals in Northern Ireland for
a short period of time. It will be noted that
the registry will be kept by the Inspector-
General of the Royal Ulster Constabulary,
and not by the local authorities, as is laid
down in the Imperial Act. The Government
are of opinion that only one register will be
required for Northern Ireland, and that
being so it will be conceded, I think, that the
Inspector-General is the proper authority to
keep it and to carry out the necessary
administration.
Hon. Members will also note that Part
One of the Bill does not apply to the training
of animals for military, agricultural, or
sporting purposes. This does not mean, of
course, that persons engaged in training
animals for these purposes will have free
licence to employ any cruel means they may
think fit; it merely indicates that the present Bill is concerned with those animals
which are being trained solely for performing purposes with a view to the financial
profit of the owner and trainer.
I come now to Part Two. This deals with
the protection of birds, and applies the provisions of two Acts passed by the Imperial
Government, namely, the Protection of Birds
Act, 1925, and the Captive Birds Shooting
(Prohibition) Act, 1921. In Clause 7 it is
made an offence for any person to employ
tethered or confined live birds as decoys or
to use bird-lime for the purpose of catching
birds. Clause 8 prohibits the keeping of a
bird in a cage or receptacle, which is not big
enough to allow it to stretch its wings;
while Clause 9 will put an end to a form of
so-called sport which, I am glad to say, is
already dying out in Northern Ireland. I
refer to pigeon shoots. I am sure that all
hon. Members will be in hearty agreement
with the provisions of these three Clauses.
339 Protection of Animals Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE

[Viscount Charlemont]
Part Three amends the Protection of
Animals Act, 1911. Under that Act the
coursing or hunting of a captive animal was
exempted from the provisions of the Act,
provided that the animal hunted or coursed
was not released in an exhausted condition.
In 1921 the Imperial Parliament passed a
short Act amending the Act of 1911, and prohibiting the coursing or hunting of a captive
animal in a place from which the animal
had no reasonable chance of escape. In the
Bill now before the House we are doing for
Northern Ireland exactly what the Imperial
Parliament did for Great Britain in its
amending Act of 1921.
Part Four provides for the application of
the procedure of the Summary Jurisdiction
Acts, and states that registration of trainers
and exhibitors of performing animals will
come into force in the New Year. The Bill,
as a whole, is simple and clear, and I do not
doubt that it will receive the hearty support
of this House. I beg to move.
a
Mr. LESLIE: I am only at
loss to
apportion the satisfaction which I am sure
must have been felt by the noble Viscount,
the Leader of the House, in his capacity, it
I may say so, of a vice-president of the Royal
Ulster Society for the Prevention of Cruelty
to Animals, in moving the Second Reading of
this Measure, and that felt by Mr. Speaker
in his capacity as president of the same
Society. I am quite satisfied that this Bill
is one that has been wanted, clamantly
wanted, for some years, and I rejoice that
we are taking practically the earliest opportunity of giving effect to the proposals which
it embodies.
I think myself that Great Britain owes a
great debt of gratitude to Brigadier-General
Cockerill, the Member for the Reigate
Division of Surrey, in the Imperial Parliament, for having brought before the Imperial
Parliament as a Private Bill the Measure on
which the first part of this Bill is based. It
was General Cockerill who drew attention
to the necessity for regulating the performance and training of animals for profit. As
regards the Bill itself, as a whole, I think
will be found of great practical convenience
in administration that we have found it possible to comprise in
Measure four
separate statutes. The result will be that
our code in Northern Ireland for dealing
with cruelty will be practically confined to
two statutes, the main statute of 1911-the
Protection of Animals Act-and this
Bill. Subject to a few Regulations made
under the Poultry and Diseases of Animals
Acts our entire code will be comprised in
these two statutes.
one
new
That result has not been attained without
a good deal of trouble in the way of drafting,
and it was found necessary, I understand,
to make some trivial alterations in the
Measure in another place. One of those
alterations at least will require an ancillary
alteration in the Committee Stage, but it is
not a matter that is likely to occupy our
attention for more than a few moments.
Taking the Bill as a whole I am sure it will
- be received with great satisfaction by all
people of humane feeling throughout the
Province. I have the greatest possible
pleasure in supporting it.
Mr. DORMAN: I should like to say that
this Bill has my whole-hearted approval. 1
think it is a Measure that is long overdue.
I had some experience some years ago of a
neighbour who was engaged in training dogs
for the purpose of exhibition, and I can assure this House that numerous individuals
have been engaged in that particular practice throughout the country in past years,
and that inhuman cruelties were inflicted
upon animals by isolated individuals here
and there, who, under this Bill, will be compelled to register and give an account of
their proceedings in the training of animals
for the purpose of profit. Needless to say,
the Party I represent in this House is wholeheartedly in favour of the protection of life
in the case of all animals.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I, like
the other hon. Members who have spoken,
very heartily approve of this Bill. The Bill
is for the protection of animals, and there
is one point I would like to draw attention
to. Those who have motored throughout
Ireland must have noticed that it is a common practice to tie goats' and sheeps' legs by
a string, which, when the animal moves,
cuts into the flesh. If these animals have
got to be tethered I think there should be
some provision made for the movement of
the goats or sheep so that the string would
not cut into the flesh as deep as the bone, as
it does in some cases at present.
There is another matter I intend to cal
attention to on the Committee stage of the
Bill. One part of the Measure refers to
animals, and I should like to know whether
that includes birds. If it does not I think
birds should be included, and, if necessary,
I will move an Amendment to that effect in
Committee. I welcome this Bill, and I hope
that if it is necessary even more stringent
measures will be taken to prevent cruelty to
animals. The less young people see of
cruelty the less they will think of it, and the
more we can do to abolish it the better.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: With regard
to the second point raised by Senator Samuel
Cunningham, I shall be glad if he will kindly
mention it again on the Committee stage.
As regards the first point with reference

Horse Breeding Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In rising to ask the House to give
this Bill a Second Reading to-day, I do not
think it requires many words of mine to
commend the Measure to the House. The
Bill, as hon. Members will see, is concerned
with the improvement of the breeding of
horses in Northern Ireland. The Bill proposes to repeal, as respects Northern Ireland, the Horse Breeding Act of 1918, and
to enact legislation which, it is hoped, will
prove more effective in raising the standard
of horse-breeding in this country. Under
the former Act which, as hon. Members are
aware, is still in force to-day, stallions are
not required to be licensed unless used for
stud purposes, and even then certain exemptions can be granted. For example,
stallion which is used exclusively for the
service of mares belonging to the owner of
the stallion, is exempted.
a
a
There are in Northern Ireland to-day,
as many hon. Senators are aware,
nmber of stallions which, while not
licensed under the 1918 Act, are
clarly being used for stud purposes.
I
think you will all agree that it is very
dificult, for various reasons which the
Huse will understand, to obtain definite
proof that these animals are so being used,
an, accordingly, it is proposed to put an
en to these evasions by introducing fresh
legislation. The present Bill is designed for
that purpose and provides that no stallion
to know
does n
and
ent to that
B
egen won
to prera
can
be kept, whether used for stud or not,
unless the owner holds either a licence or a
Fermit under conditions which the Bill lays
down. The ordinary fee for the grant of a
licence is not to exceed one guinea. Hon.
Menbers will find this in Clause 2.
max
shill
it the Clau
FONT:
the d
fair,
The
ximum charge for a permit will be two
Alings and sixpence. This is contained in
use 5. Provision is also made, as is only
f, for the charging of an extra fee when
plication for a licence is made after the
escribed time, or when a stallion is not
esented for inspection in accordance with
Ministry's directions. A permit, I may
explain, will be granted in respect of
animals such as polo ponies, race horses.
etc., which, though stallions, are not employed for stud purposes, while a licence is
intended for stallions which are used for
service.

It will be noted that in lieu of the penalty
not exceeding £20" provided by the Act
of 1918, the present Bill proposes a statutory
penalty of £2 for a first offence and £5 for
a second offence. It is also laid down that
the keeping of an unlicensed stallion after
a second conviction, shall constitute a continuing offence, the penalty for which will
be £2 for each week or part of a week during
which the offence continues. Hon. Members will agree that some powers are necessary to deal with that class of people who
persist in infringing the law, and it is considered that the best way of dealing with
them is to make their action a continuing
offence, thus obviating the need for further
prosecutions, with the knowledge that in the
meantime unsuitable stallions are being used.
I beg to move.
Mr. LONG: I beg to support the Bill
before the House, and I am satisfied that in
the course of time it will result in a better
class of animals being procured in Northern
Ireland. There is no doubt that the Bill as
it stands does not offer much inducement to
enterprising men to procure or import
stallions of a very good class. There is so
much uncertainty about the licence being
renewed at the end of the year. However,
the Ministry of Agriculture have adopted
regulations which, I think, show their enterprise and their desire that we should have the
very best stallions that can be procured.
Under the new Regulations the Ministry
propose to supply two-thirds of the price of
a stallion that is imported or procured. Of
course, the animal must be subject to the
approval of the Ministry and the price also
must be subject to their approval, but if a
person purchases a horse for £600 the
Ministry will grant £400 and all the purchaser has to find is £200. He must keep
the stallion for, I think, five years, and at
the expiration of that time it is his absolute
property. These Regulations, coupled with
this Bill, will, I am satisfied, make for an
improvement in the standard of horse breeding in Northern Ireland.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: There
is one point I should like to refer to because
I am not sure that it is covered by the Bill.
It affects Government stallions. A certain
number of stallions are registered for service
every year and are approved by the Government. Some of them have proved unfruitful,
and in the list of stallions registered for
service in my own county I notice that
animals that have given most unsatisfactory
results continue to be selected by the Govern-
343 Horse Breeding Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Colonel Sharman Crawford]
ment. I think those stallions should be
taken off the list and should not again
receive the premium.
of
Mr. LESLIE: The two Senators who have
just addressed the House are authorities on
the matter of horse breeding and on the
extent to which the breeding of horses can
properly be assisted by local funds under
County Council control, with the assistance,
course, of the Ministry. They have
supported the Bill and I should be the last to
disagree with either of them, for though 1
am not myself very conversant with horse
breeding or particularly interested in it, I
recognise that the supply of proper stallions
and horses is of great importance to the
agriculture of Ulster and to the supply, to
some extent, of what we may describe as
urban traction, and again, to some extent,
to the export trade.
J
But I should have liked, before being asked
to pass, or to consent to the passing of this
Bill, to have been given a little more information as to the size of the problem.
should like to know how many horses there
are in the Six Counties at present licensed
under the existing Act of 1918, and I should
like some indication of the size of the
problem as regards the unlicensed stallion.
It would appear from the printed reports of
the debates in another place that quite a
number of these animals in use on upcountry farms are for breeding and other
purposes and they will obviously be very
difficult to bring under control.
It is apparently now proposed to enforce
the provisions of this Bill, if it becomes an
Act, by introducing the principle of the
minimum fine. Before the Noble Viscount
spoke I must confess I was in grave doubt as
to whether the intention of the Bill was to
impose a minimum fine or whether it was
simply to indicate to the magistrates that
that was the fine that the Department and
Parliament expected them to impose.
Whether the words do or do not carry out
the suggestion of the noble Viscount must be
settled elsewhere, but I think we must,
after what has been stated, assume that the
intention is to impose a minimum fine.
To the principle of the minimum fine, 1
need scarcely say, I am inflexibly opposed.
Such fines have been abandoned for, I think,
three-quarters of a century. They survive
in revenue cases, which are in a class by
themselves; in fact, they have been imposed
in very recent years, and they also survive
to some extent where quasi civil injury has
been inflicted. A fairly recent example will
be found in the Public Health Act of 1878,
where there is a distinct penalty of £20
payable to, let us say, an Urban District
Council, in respect of a house built over a
sewer. There are also in the same Act
provisions for fines of not less than a shilling
and not more than two shillings that will
be imposed on those shall we say, law breaking people who permit their animals to
trespass in a public cemetery. If we go back
we find other instances which I will not
pause to recount this afternoon as they will
perhaps be more fitting to be mentioned at
a later date.
I think we are entitled to ask the noble
Viscount to tell us, before he asks us to
consent to such a reversal of the normal
practice, how many prosecutions there have
been under the Act of 1918, and under that
same Act in how many cases has the same
offender been brought up again. The object
of that inquiry, of course, is to ascertain
whether such penalties as have been inflicted
in times past have proved sufficient to stem
the evil. Unless it can be shown that the
steps taken by law in the past have proved
insufficient, there would seem no reason for
such an alteration in principle.
a
Passing from that there is a matter which
runs pretty well through the Bill and which
has, I imagine, not escaped attention, and
that is the additional duties it is proposed
to impose on the Police Force. The Royal
Ulster Constabulary is not
very large
Force. Some of us who live in the country
districts are of opinion that we do not see
His Majesty's uniform of that pattern quite
as often as we would wish. It is a great
question whether any further duties, apart
from their proper police duties, ought to be
cast upon the police. I know that, to some
extent, under our more recent Livestock
Breeding Act, the police are asked to come
in occasionally, but there is no precedent I
know of for giving any police officer or constable power to inspect and examine any
stallion, and I do not think that is a power
any police officer or constable ought to have.
The Minister provides for the services of an
authorised person, and I think he should,
speaking generally, be content with that.
To some extent this is a Bill for registration more than anything else, and, looking
at the method by which that is proposed to
be attained, the intention is to divide the
horses into two classes of licence and permit. When the Bill made its first appearance, and when it was circulated to us all
during the Recess, there was a distinct
exemption for what I may describe as private horses, horses to be used only for the
purposes of the owner. That has disappeared during the course of the Bill elsewhere, and it recurs in a somewhat different
form in another place. The alteration was
made, I understand, at the instance of a
private Member, and I must admit that it
seems to me to introduce a good deal of
administrative difficulty which was not
there before.
345 Horse Breeding Bill-
Second Reading.
9 NOVEMBER 1926


1-
I should like to know whether the Government feels itself committed to the exact
method of expression of that class of exemption. I am sure, from an examination of the
Clause itself, that they cannot possibly intend to be wedded to the particular form of
words used, and I should like to know
whether they will be prepared to admit an
alteration there which would bring back
more the sense of the Bill in its earlier form.
I do not think there will be any desire to
oppose the Bill in this House; in fact we
have had evidence this afternoon that there
is no desire to oppose the principle of the
Measure, nor do I think any great fault will
be found with it in the country.
At the same time, I am not sure that the
country will wish it to be brought into force
as early as January next. After all, we are
now in November, and the time it will take
to get this Bill into the heads of country
people who have these stallions for some
purpose of their own, is likely to be rather
more than six weeks. I do not know whether
it could be arranged that the commencement
of the Measure could be postponed until 1st
April, which is the date usually taken as
the commencement of the financial year. If
so it would presumably be necessary to readjust the periods for the issue of licences.
Taking the Bill as a whole, and with a view
of doing anything that can properly be done
to improve the standard of our horses, I
support the Measure.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Generally speaking,
I desire to give my support to this Bill. I
think there was a mistake made in the other
House when it reduced the penalty to £2.
In the first place the penalty was £10, which
was subsequently reduced to £5 and then to
£2. That, I think, was a mistake. Then
again, the Bill allows a man to use an
unsound stallion for his own purposes. 1
think that is a ridiculous suggestion. We
have to protect a man against himself as
well as against the public. In the Livestock
Breeding Act there is
such thing as
allowing a man to use an unlicensed bull
for his own purposes. This is a matter that
will
tremendous difficulty to the
Department. Senator Leslie suggested
change in the date of the commencement of
the Act to 1st April. The commencement of
the financial year has nothing to do with the
service of mares.
cause
no
Mr. LESLIE: Nor has the 1st January.
a
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Yes it has. Our
stock are served for foals about 1st January.
The exhibition of stallions comes on at
Balmoral in March, and if the Bill is to
apply next year at all it must come into
force on 1st January. With regard to the
point raised by the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman (Colonel Sharman Crawford) it
is one of very great importance, but I think
the question of unfruitful stallions is met
in the Bill by the licence being withdrawn in
the following year. Having drawn attention
to these matters I desire to support the Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: I also desire to offer my
support to this Measure. It is a wonderful
illustration of the results of the unrestricted
incentive for gain and competition, very
often spoken of as being the foundation of
all our prosperity and success. We now find
it necessary as a legislature to interfere with
this incentive to gain, because undoubtedly
it is an incentive to gain that has caused
those who are engaged in the breeding of
animals to keep, for the purpose of service,
worn out and unsuitable animals. I rejoice
that the legislature has realised its responsibility in this matter, and in so far as they
have realised their responsibility are
supporting the principle I stand here for and
in pleading for which I am like a voice crying in the wilderness, namely, that it is not
desirable that the incentive for gain and
competition should be allowed to run
unrestricted.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I think I may say that on the whole
this Bill has received a favourable reception
from the House. Several points have been
raised and I will endeavour to answer some
of them. Senator Leslie asked how many
licensed and unlicensed stallions there were
in Northern Ireland. The information I
have is that the numbers are approximately
120 licensed and 40 unlicensed. The hon.
Senator also asked for the number of
prosecutions under the Act of 1918. There
were only two prosecutions brought. Other
cases were brought, but evidence sufficient to
convict could not be obtained.
a
or
The right hon. and gallant Gentleman
(Colonel Sharman Crawford) raised the
question of unfruitful stallions. I think that
point is covered in the Bill, because a licence
is renewable every year, and the right hon.
and gallant Gentleman will see that under
Clause 2 the Ministry have power to refuse
to grant
licence and may revoke
suspend a licence in respect of a stallion if
it appears to the Ministry that the stallion
has proved to be inadequately prolific or
begets unsuitable stock. If a stallion proves
to be unsuitable or unfruitful I do not think
the Ministry would be likely to continue to
issue a licence in respect of him. Senator
Leslie referred to the use of the police in
carrying out the provisions of the Bill, but
the services of the police are already utilised
under the Livestock Breeding Act in the case
of the licensing of bulls.
Mr. LESLIE: Not for examination.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I do not think any difficulty will
arise by the Constabulary being employed
347 Malone Training School Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
under this Bill. As regards the suggestion
that the commencement of the Act should
date from 1st April, instead of 1st January,
I think Senator Hill Dickson has given a
sufficient answer to that proposal.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill commited to a Committee of the whole

Malone Training School Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I propose to
ask the permission of the House to take the
Malone Training School Bill now, instead of
in the order in which it appears on the
Paper.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-
(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Confirmation of agreement and
consequential provisions).
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I should like
to say a word of explanation on a question
raised by Senator Leslie as to the meaning of
"officers." I am advised that the expression is a general one and it covers parf-time
officers as well.
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the noble
Viscount for giving us that explanation. It
will be remembered that I only raised the
question on account of a special definition it
had been thought necessary to insert in the
Local Government Act, and I was anxious
that we should not make any mistake.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Existing and pensioned officers
of Reformatory School).
was
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: There
another point raised by Senator Leslie to the
effect that Clause 2, sub-Section 5, would enable the Minister of Finance to deprive an
existing pensioner of his rights. This is not
the case. The fourth sub-Clause lays it
down that every person who, on the prescribed date, was in receipt of a pension in
respect of past employment by the Committee
of Management at the Reformatory School
should, during his lifetime, continue to receive such pension. There is, in fact, only
one pensioner, who, I understand, is eightysix years of age and receives 10s per week,
and under no circumstances could he be deprived of his pension.
Mr. LESLIE: Again I should not propose
to pursue the matter. I entirely agree with
the noble Viscount that a claimant's right
is very fully dealt with in sub-Section 4, but
if any question does arise respecting the
claim, the decision is left, under sub-Clause
5, with the Minister of Finance. In view of
the explanation that there is but one pensioner, of very considerable years, I think
it is unnecessary to pursue the matter, but
I think it was right and necessary to call
the attention of the House to it, because I
am sure the House would be very slow to
allow anybody to be deprived of any right
of access to the Courts, and to have their
claim, whether they liked it or not, transferred to the adjudication of a Department.
But I am sure we will all agree that the
explanation is satisfactory.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Financial Provisions).
(1) There shall be paid out of moneys
provided by Parliament all such expenses
as may be incurred with the sanction of
the Ministry of Finance in connection with
the management and maintenance of
the Reformatory School and the Borstal
Institution, and in connection with the
maintenance of offenders in the said
School and Institution.
(2) The liabilities of the trustee and
the committee of management in connection with the Reformatory School outstanding on the date of the passing of
this Act, and any special bounties payable
under the provisions of this Act, shall be
charged upon the investments and funds
specified in the fourth schedule to the
agreement, and the balance of the said
investments and funds (if any) remaining
after all such charges have been satisfied,
shall be paid into, and form part of, the
Capital Fund established under section
four of the Exchequer and Financial Provisions Act (Northern Ireland), 1923.
(3) Where a youthful offender is ordered
to be sent to the Reformatory School,
the council of the county or county
borough in which he resides (to be specified in the order) shall pay towards the
expenses of such youthful offender, whilst
detained in the Reformatory School, a
contribution at such rate, and payable
at such times, as may be determined by
regulations made by the Ministry of Home
Affairs with the approval of the Ministry
of Finance; and the amount of any such
contribution shall be defrayed by the
council as expenses incurred by them
under Part IV. of the Children Act, 1908,

The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Clause 3, page 5, leave out Clause 3.
Mr. LESLIE: The rather drastic Amendment standing in my name requires, I
think, rather more explanation than an
ordinary verbal Amendment. It will be seen
that what I am asking the Committee to do
is to take out of the Bill the whole of
Clause 3; that is to say, to take out all the
financial provisions of the Bill. The reason
why I suggest this course is that a point
was raised on the Second Reading discussion by the right hon. and gallant Gentleman, Senator Colonel Sharman Crawford,
the Chairman not only of the Down County
Council but also of the Association of County
Councils, as regards the liability of the County
Councils, as representing their ratepayers,
for the maintenance of the Malone Reformatory School and for the maintenance of
any boys that might be sent to this Reformatory School. After the discussion in the
House the right hon. and gallant Gentleman was so kind as to draw my attention,
and the attention of others, to a Clause in
the Departmental Report on Reformatory
and Industrial Schools in Northern Ireland
which was presented to this House in June
1923.
I was aware, as I have no doubt other
hon. Members were, of the existence of this
Report, but Clause 4 in the summary of
Recommendations I must admit had entirely
escaped my recollection. That Clause recommended the adoption of the English and
Scottish system of financing these schools
whereby the cost would be divided equally
between State funds and local authorities'
contributions. That is to say, the local
authority could not be mulcted in
than 50 per cent. of the cost of maintenance
their, shall we say, patients in those
schools. This Report of 1923 is, of course,
subsequent to the Children Act of 1908,
Section 73 of which dealt with this matter
of the responsibility of the County Councils
and reference to that, without recollection
of
ate, and lea
Minister

thi
in
of
more
the Departmental Report, would, I think,
ead anybody to imagine that the proposal
in the Bill was perfectly satisfactory. However, with this report before us I do not
think anybody representing the County
Councils here to-day or who, in the past,
has served on County Councils, could agree
with the Bill as it stands,
to
It seems to me that the case is impregnable and that the claim is unanswerable
that the County Councils ought not to give
more than fifty per cent. of the cost,
and it might well be that they ought
not to contribute even so much as that.
If that be admitted the question arises
how this Senate can give effect
its determination to do the right thing.
and the moment one begins to consider that
question one is brought up against the rights
of this Senate on financial subjects. We
know that our rights in dealing with money
matters are defined in Section 16 of the
Government of Ireland Act, which is our
Constitution, and it might be thought that
merely to add a Clause here providing for
what I might call a fifty-fifty basis would be
within our powers. But I think that reference to the authorities and further
consideration would show that it would be
undesirable to suggest such a course here. It
would be most repugnant to us to do anything that could be deemed to infringe the
privileges of our colleagues who serve the
Constitution in another place, and it would
be a very undesirable thing to do if it could
be avoided.
There is a perfectly constitutional course
sanctioned by long usage in the Second
Chamber across the water. Where the House
of Lords cannot accept a financial proposal
as it stands the practice has been to simply
excise the entire Clause and leave it to those
whose financial responsibility is more
apparent to send back a new Clause. It is
for that reason that instead of suggesting a
merely verbal alteration I am suggesting to
the Committee that the entire Clause should
be excised. I accordingly beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: As the
Senator who originally drew attention to
what this Clause would mean to the County
Councils, I should like to say a few words.
The question of the contributions of the
Government and the County Councils for the
maintenance of these Reformatory and
Industrial Schools in Ireland has been a
source of contention between the County
Councils and the Government for many years.
The Clause as it stands puts the County
Councils in a very false position. I suppose
Finance Ministers are human and try to get
as much as they can, and perhaps some of
them try to get more than they ought to.
It seems to me that in this case, instead of
the basis of contribution being fifty-fifty as
in the past, a rapacious Finance Minister
could claim the whole of the expenses from
the County Councils.
That is not as it should be, and therefore
I thought it my duty to raise the question.
I would be one of the last people to interfere
with the privileges of the other House, but, as
I intimated to some of the officials concerned,
351 Malone Training School Bill- THE SENATE

Committee.
[Colonel Sharman Crawford]
if the Clause is not amended in such a way
as to make it acceptable to the County
Councils I shall move an Amendment to the
Clause. However, I hope the Leader of the
House will be able to give us such an assurance as will render such a step on my part
unnecessary.
Mr. LONG: I can corroberate the statement of the right hon. and gallant Gentleman that the relations of the local authorities with the Government on this question
of the contributions in respect of children
sent to Reformatory Schools have not been
happy in the past, and I think it is exceedingly desirable that the Government should
define in this Bill the liability of the local
authorities in this matter. It would
simplify matters in the future, and I
earnestly hope the Government will accede
to our request.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I desire to support
the Amendment proposed by Senator Leslie,
and I hope the Noble Leader of the House
will give us a guarantee that our request
will be granted, and that the respective contributions of the Government and the
County Councils will in the future be on a
fifty-fifty basis, and that the contributions of
the County Councils will not exceed fifty per
cent. We had a previous experience of this
kind when we took over the lunatics from
the different Workhouses.
We spent
enormous sums to make accommodation for
them, and we were promised a Capitation
grant that came from one particular source
of revenue. That source of revenue dropped
and our Capitation grant ceased with it.
As this Bill now stands, unless we have
some guarantee, we are liable to the same
treatment. The treatment I have referred
to
was experienced under the Imperial
Government. No person would doubt the
word of the present Minister of Finance or
the Minister of Home Affairs, who have
charge of this matter, but they may in time
be raised to a higher sphere, and we may
have someone else who may not be able to
finance the scheme. When the Malone Reformatory is taken over by the Government,
the first thing they will do will be to increase the salaries of the staff and place
them on a pensionable basis, and the School
will be carried on in an extravagant manner
and the cost of administration will be more
than ever it was. Therefore, the county
councils must have some guarantee that
their contribution will not exceed fifty per
cent.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: With regard
to the Amendment proposed by Senator
Leslie, I have had an opportunity of communicating with my right hon. Friends, the
Minister of Home Affairs and the Minister
of Finance. The Minister of Home Affairs,
with the consent of the Minister of Finance,
has authorised me to give a specific undertaking to the House that the weekly contribution to be paid by the local authority in
respect of each Reformatory boy, for whom
the authority is liable, will not exceed half
the average weekly cost per head of Reformatory boys in the institution. I am,
therefore, prepared to give the House an
unqualified undertaking to this effect, and I
trust the hon. Senator, in view of this very
definite pledge, will feel himself able to withdraw his Amendment.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Before
the hon. Member who moved the Amendment speaks again, I should like to ask one
question. So far as I can see the undertaking given by the noble Viscount is perfectly satisfactory, but I think the Bill, with
that alteration, still leaves it open to the
county councils to object to pay any expenses that they consider are not justified.
The county councils are not bound strictly
to pay fifty per cent. of the cost. If they
think the school is being managed extravagantly they will still have the right to object
to pay what they consider is unnecessary
expenditure.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I do not
think I could answer that question without
consulting the Minister concerned.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I think
that is so. Perhaps you would give us a
further assurance on that point on the Third
Reading of the Bill.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: From the
wording of the Bill it would appear that the
county councils have that right, but I will
mention the matter again on the Third
Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: I think it would be appropriate for me, in the first instance, to express
my thanks to the noble Viscount for so
kindly acting on behalf of the House as
intermediary in this matter, and I think we
may congratulate ourselves on the success
that has crowned his efforts. The undertaking the Leader of the House has given
really gives me something more than I
originally asked for, and I think it will be
the will of the Committee that the Amendment should be withdrawn.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Short title and construction)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
SCHEDULE agreed to.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported, without Amendment, to the House,

Housing (No. 2) Bill, 1926.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I do not think it is necessary for
me to make a lengthy speech in moving
that this Bill be read a Second time. Its
object is merely to extend for a further
year-that is, until March, 1928-the operation of the various Housing Acts which
have been passed by this Parliament.
Perhaps I may be allowed to recapitulate,
very briefly, the effect of these Acts.
Under the various Acts the Government is
empowered to grant subsidies to private
builders or urban authorities in respect of
houses of
certain type built by them.
These houses must be of the kitchen or
small parlour type with a superficial area,
ranging, except in special conditions, from
600 to 900 square feet, and must be completed in accordance with Rules made by
the Ministry of Home Affairs.
a
a
The subsidy given for each house is £60,
but that figure may be increased to £80 if
the house is built in six months. In
addition to this subsidy the Acts also empower local authorities to subsidise the
builder of this type of house up to £40 per
house or, alternatively, to grant him free
of interest
loan not exceeding £100.
Furthermore, the local authorities may, if
they so desire, exempt the houses from the
payment of rates for not more than ten
years. Up to the present the number of
houses approved under these Acts and
built by private builders is 3,022, while
1,782 have been approved but not yet completed. The urban authorities have
already completed 1,046, and have received
approval in respect of
further 914.
During the past twelve months more than
2,000 of these houses have been built by
both private builders and urban authoritjes, while last month 150 were approved
19 erection by private builders. While
Mese are very satisfactory figures, it is
clearly recognised that the housing prob-
Mem is, as yet, by no means solved, and
hence the Government feel that they need
make no apology in asking for the renewal
of the Acts for another year. I beg to
move.
M
a
Mr. DORMAN: As I am acquainted with
the housing conditions in Belfast I consider
it my duty to support this Measure. I can
assure hon. Senators that there is a lamentable condition of affairs existing in Belfast
at the present time in regard to the housing
of the working classes. When examining
the recent Census returns I made the
surprising discovery that Belfast has deteriorated probably more than any other great
industrial centre in regard to housing.
because between the Census of 1911 and that
of 1926 the number of one-room dwellings in
the city had increased by six hundred per
cent. I know numerous cases where six,
seven, eight, nine, ten and twelve human
beings belonging to a family are residing in
one room, and I also know cases where such
working class families are paying as much
as 12s 6d a week rental for the occupancy of
a single apartment. I therefore feel it my
duty to offer my support to any Measure
calculated to stimulate the building of
houses for the working classes of Belfast and
Northern Ireland

whole House for to-morrow.
