and fraudulent use of licence, &c.), 9 (Burden
of proof), 10 (Power to make rules), 11 (Prosecutions and fines), 12 (Interpretation), 13
(Commencement of Act and repeal of 8 and 9
Geo. 5, c. 13), and 14 (Short title) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported, with
Amendment, to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendment.
Motor Vehicles (Traffic and
Regulation) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move the Second Reading of the Motor
Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Bill. It
has for its object the improvement of traffic
conditions in Northern Ireland, and the reduction of accidents due to motor vehicles on
the roads. The law relating to motor offences
and penalties has remained unchanged
since 1903, when the Motor Car Act of that
year was passed, but in the meantime there
has been an inordinate development of motor
traffic on our roads in Northern Ireland, with
a consequent increase of motor accidents.
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Second Reading..
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
The growth of motor traffic in Northern
Ireland may be appreciated from the fact
that the number of motor vehicles licensed
in Northern Ireland in 1921 was 13.350,
whereas it is estimated that there are to-day
approximately 30,000 vehicles similarly
licensed.
A record has been kept of accidents which
have occurred in Northern Ireland during
the year ended 30th June, 1926, in connection
with motor vehicles, and it has been found
that in that period no less than 1,300 accidents have occurred in which motor vehicles
were either directly or indirectly concerned.
These accidents involved a loss of life in no
less than 59 cases and serious injury to person
in 759 other cases. It is obvious that it is the
duty of the Government and Parliament to
remedy this very serious state of affairs, and
it is hoped that the present Bill. will have
that effect to a very considerable extent.
The Government of Northern Ireland, in
consultation with the Imperial Government,
have had this matter under serious consideration for the last two or three years, and
have invited the views and recommendations
in their respective countries of special committees appointed for this purpose.
I may add that the proposals embodied in
this Bill are to a considerable extent framed
on recommendations made to the Minister of
Home Affairs by the Roads Advisory Committee of Northern Ireland, two of the
members of which happen to be hon.
Members of this House.
As will be seen from the structure of
the Bill, it is divided into three parts.
The first part deals in the main with
motor offences and penalties in respect
thereof, while the second part deals with
a wholly new principle in Northern Ireland,
i.e., the central examintion and licensing
of public service vehicles. In other words
the two main objects of the Bill are-first,
to make provision for a more comprehensive
and more effective control of motor traffic
generally on the roads, and, secondly, to
ensure that the motor vehicles on the roads
-and particularly those vehicles which are
used for carrying passengers are in a fit
and efficient condition, and are not subject
to any defects which might conduce to the
danger of the persons using them.
Turning to Part I of the Bill, I think it
well, my Lord, to explain at the outset that
in drafting this part of the Bill an opportunity has been taken of codifying as far
as possible the law in relation to motor
offences, and with this object the Bill completely repeals the Motor Car Act of 1903,
while at the same time it incorporates in
itself the more essential features of that
Bill.
Hon. Senators who are familiar with the
provisions of the Act of 1903 are aware that
all offences under that Act or under allied
Acts were grouped under one penal Clause,
and offenders were liable to a general
penalty not exceeding £20 for the first
offence. The result of this grouping
arrangement was that there has been no
clear distinction in the minds of motorists
or of the Magistrates as between serious
and technical offences, and, consequently, it
has often been the case that a motorist who
was guilty of an offence of dangerous or
reckless driving was let off with the same
penalty as that imposed on a motorist who
in a moment of inadvertence left his licence
behind him.
a
The present Bill adopts
different
principle, and an attempt has been made to
take out and place by themselves the more
serious offences that is to say, the offences
which are conducive in themselves to
danger to life, limb and property. For
these major offences special penalties are
prescribed in the Bill. The first is that of
reckless, wanton or dangerous driving, and it
will be observed that in the case of a conviction on this charge the delinquent will
render himself liable to a minimum penalty
of £1 for the first offence with a maximum
penalty of £5, and (2) increased minimum
and maximum penalties for subsequent
offences. Similarly, it will be seen from
Clause 2 that substantial minimum and
maximum penalties are being imposed for
the very serious offence of drunkenness
while in charge of a motor-car.
of
Another offence which is regarded as being
a major character is that of driving
while disqualified by the Court from driving, and in that case a definite penalty of
imprisonment is imposed, but here it is to
be remembered that before a person becomes liable to this severe penalty, he must
have, in the first instance, committed
a crime which in the opinion of the Court
was of such a serious character as to have
his licence suspended, and in the second
place he must have flouted the authority of
the Court and have driven while under
suspension.
Over and above these three major offences
it is also considered essential to impose
minimum penalties in the case of the
drivers of omnibuses who exceed the speed
limit prescribed by the Ministry of Home
Affairs. The present speed limit for these
heavy vehicles is 12 miles per hour, but the
Minister of Home Affairs has already given
an assurance that that speed limit will be
increased to 20 miles per hour, always
subject to the passing of this Bill with the
penalties for breach which are prescribed in
Sub-section (2) of Clause 6.
Having dealt with the major offences, it
will be seen from Clause 20 (1) of the Bill
389 Motor Vehicles Bill-

11 NOVEMBER 1926
Second Reading.


TE
-1
that in the case of the minor or what some
people might regard as the technical
offences, the maximum penalty of £20
which was prescribed by the Act of 1903
has been reduced to £5, and there is no provision regarding imprisonment such as
appeared in the older Act.
The next point of interest in connection
with Part I of the Bill is the question of
the licensing of drivers, in connection with
which I would refer the House to Clause 3.
This Clause is generally based on the old
Section in the Act of 1903 in relation to
licensing of drivers, but a new principle is
being introduced, in that applicants for
licences will in future be required to make a
statutory declaration to the effect that they
are not suffering from any physical defect
which would render them incapable of
driving a car with efficiency.
It will also be observed that the Clause
empowers the Ministry of Home Affairs to
prescribe the form of application and the
form of licence, and in this connection it
may be of interest to this House to know
that it is the intention of my right hon.
Friend the Minister of Home Affairs to require that every motor licence will in
future carry on it a photograph of the
licencee, as in the case of a passport, in
order that a stop may be put once and for
all to the abuses which have prevailed in
the past in connection with these licences.
The old law in connection with endorsement of licences has also been somewhat
altered, and it will be found in the Bill that
the Courts are granted a discretion in connection with endorsing licences, save in regard to the serious offences to which I have
already referred, in which case they are
if required to endorse the licences and to
furnish particulars of the endorsements to
the County Councils concerned and to the
- Ministry of Home Affairs. The object and
utility of this provision will be apparent, I
✔am sure, to the Members of this House, as
it will enable a more careful watch to be
kept of habitual offenders.
I would also draw attention to Clause 8
of the Bill, which confers a new power on
the Ministry of Home Affairs of prescribing
by regulation the carrying of lights or re-
-flecting discs at the rear by bicycles and
other vehicles on our roads, which are not
at present required by statute to show
light to the rear. That concludes the most
important features of Part I of the Bill.
a
Turning to Part II of the Bill, it will be
observed that it deals with the licensing
and regulation of public service vehicles,
and I might explain that its general effect
is to empower the Ministry of Home Affairs
to establish a system of examination and
of licensing of public service vehicles in
Northern Ireland, similar to that which has
worked with such conspicuous success in
London under the auspices of the Metropolitan police. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Up to the present the system of examination of public service vehicles-that is to
say, of chars-a-banes, motor omnibuses, taxicabs, and motor hackneys generally, has been
of a cursory and disconnected character.
The only bodies in Northern Ireland
possessed of powers in connection with these
vehicles have been the urban authorities,
and, consequently, in the case of a motor
hackney vehicle domiciled in a rural area
in one of our counties, there has been no
system in force under which that vehicle
became subjected to examination by
responsible authority.
a
Further, even where the urban authorities
possess powers to examine, test and license
such vehicles, their powers in this respect
were much hampered owing to the extent
of the cost involved in the employment of
competent inspectors, while, still further, it
has not been possible to secure a uniformity
throughout Northern Ireland in connection
with the Regulations or conditions to be
observed in each urban district.
On the whole, therefore, having regard to
the very rapid development of this form
of transport in Northern Ireland, and to
the fact that approximately 20,000,000 people
use these forms of transport each year, the
Government is satisfied that a central coordinated and comprehensive system of
testing and of licensing is essential.
Hon. Members of this House are aware
that all carrying undertakings, such as
railway and steamship companies, are compelled by law to conform to certain Government requirements in connection with their
undertakings, and there can be no objection
on the part of any person to the adoption
of a similar principle in connection with
this new and growing industry. Part II of
the Bill, accordingly, empowers the Ministry
to frame conditions under which these
passenger carrying vehicles will be licensed,
and to take the necessary steps for the
examination of the vehicles, and for the
issue of licences to such vehicles as may
pass the required tests.
Over and above
that the Bill introduces a further principle
in connection with the drivers and conductors of these vehicles.
Having regard to the fact that these employees are responsible in the main for the
lives of the passengers carried in the vehicles
controlled by them, it is considered proper
that before they are allowed to drive and
operate them they should pass certain
definite tests of character and of general
391 Motor Vehicles Bill.

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Viscount Charlemont]
ability as prescribed by the Ministry of
Home Affairs, and it will be observed that
substantial penalties are imposed in the Bill
in the case of vehicles being used for carrying passengers unless in the first place the
vehicles are licensed, and in the second place
the drivers and conductors are licensed.
This part of the Bill also deals with the
very important and pressing problem of
over-crowding. Up to the present it has not
been possible under existing laws to control
this difficulty properly. The imposition of
nominal fines has not proved a sufficient
deterrent, and it is a matter of daily occurrence to find vehicles which are licensed to
carry 32 passengers having as many as 50
passengers in them.
This offence is one that must be stopped
for many reasons. In the first place the
over-crowding tends to increase the danger to
the passengers in the event of any accident
happening. In the second place the vehicles
are not constructed to carry large numbers
in excess of the proper quota, while in the
third place the extra weight imposed by the
additional passengers tends to increase
abnormally the wear and tear on our public
roads.
Another factor which, perhaps, is of equal
importance is that the owner of the vehicle
by allowing additional passengers into the
vehicle is, in effect, defrauding the State,
having regard to the fact that he pays his
motor tax on the basis of the number of
passengers for whom the vehicle is designed.
It will be observed from Clause 16 of the
Bill that it is intended to carry out this
system of licensing and inspection through
the medium of the Royal Ulster Constabulary, but Sub-section (2) of this particular
Clause makes it clear that the personnel
employed is to be found out of the existing
establishment of the Force, and no increase
is to be made beyond the statutory strength.
By the transfer from the local authorities
to the Ministry of the power of licensing
these public service vehicles the local authorities become automatically deprived of certain powers which they possess by virtue of
their powers of licensing. In order to
preserve such powers as were necessary and
to confer on the local authorities such other
powers as are desirable and appropriate for
the better regulation of local traffic, a Clause
(18) has been inserted of the desired effect.
It will be observed, however, that these
powers are to be exercised by the local
authorities concerned, subject to the approval
of the Ministry of Home Affairs.
Part III of the Bill is of a supplemental
character, and calls for no particular
comment. I beg to move.
Mr. LONG: While the noble Viscount, the
Leader of the House, was moving the Second

Reading of this Bill I examined my licence
to drive a motor-car, and I found to my great
surprise that it expired on the 27th day of
August, 1926-(laughter)--and, therefore, for
that reason I would be inclined to deal
leniently with an unintentional offence of
that nature. However, I am strongly in
support of the general principles of the Bill.
I would like, if I am permitted, to refer to
two allegations that were made regarding
this Bill by a Member of Parliament for
Northern Ireland. This gentleman stated to
me that the Bill was promoted at the instigation of the railway companies. I want to
give a most emphatic denial to a statement of
that nature, because, as a member of the
Roads Advisory Committee, I am the inno
cent cause of the inception of the Bill. At a
meeting in December, 1924, I brought forward questions relating to motor accidents
and the speed of chars-a-banes. As a matter
of fact, the Roads Advisory Committee were
engaged during the whole of last winter con
the details of the principles of this Bill. The
railways were not represented, and the railway officials were not responsible in the
slightest degree for the inception of the
Measure.
Another statement that that Member of
Parliament made was that the heavy motor
and char-a-banc owners were not consulted.
That also is a misapprehension, because the
Roads Advisory Committee had before them
on several occasions representatives of the
Automobile Club-at least they obtained their
views on the matter--and they also had a
deputation of six from the Motor Coach
Association. These six members of the Motor
Coach Association, including the chairman,
welcomed the principles of the Bill, but, of
course, suggested minor Amendments, many
of which have been carried out.
an
It seems a very Irish thing, but that
Member of Parliament also stated to me that
the result of this Bill would be to prolong
the time of the journey from Derry to Port
rush and from Derry to Belfast. It seems
very Irish thing to say that increasing the
speed limit from twelve miles to twenty miles
hour would entail longer time, but his
explanation of that was that the members of
the Constabulary and the officials of the
Ministry had not really enforced the twelve
miles an hour limit. They winked at
breaches of the law. However, I think we
will all admit that a reform in this conne
tion was due, and that twelve miles an hour
was a ridiculous speed to require a vehicle
with pneumatic tyres to travel at.
With regard to the question of minimum
fines, about which there seems to be a differ
ence of opinion, and on which Senator Leslie
feels very strongly. I might mention that the
Roads Advisory Committee gave this matter
very careful consideration. It was repre

Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Although
I have no interest in the railway companies
or in bus traffic I am extremely pleased to
see the Government introducing this legislation for the regulation and conduct of
motor traffic. When we hear from the noble
Viscount, the Leader of the House, the great
number of casualties that occurred during
the past few years, it is quite evident that
it is necessary that the restrictions and
regulations in connection with this particulas traffic should be tightened up. There is
one thing which, unfortunately, is not in the
Bill up to the present, and I believe it has
been
Tesponsible for more accidents than
the anything else--that is the bright headlights
exhibited by the motor-cars. I understand
imit. W it might be difficult to introduce any par-
Betorestriction until somethinny par
es to
at a
doubte
weed to the from t
Some
the gene think

satisfa ctory is put on the market, but, un-
Ledly, there have been more accidents
that source than from any other.
me men who have motor-cars appear to
k the road is entirely their own, and
are so selfish that they are only coned with their own skins, being quite inat to the dangers which hundreds of
people are running. Last year I
happened to be one of a party in a char-abane coming from Larne, and I never saw
those lights exhibited more than on that
occasion. We, unfortunately, had been kept
till after dark, and when coming up the
Shore Road a motor-car with a driver and no
passengers in it exhibited very strong, bright,
glaring headlights. The result was that the
person driving the char-a-banc, which had no
fewer than thirty-two passengers on board,
was compelled to stop. He was so affected by
the bright lights that had he gone any
further he undoubtedly would have run into
a very dangerous part of the road, and so
have caused either loss of life or injury to
his passengers.
Something should be done in this respect.
The Government have made splendid attempts to correct many irregularities, but I
think this one should be attended to at the
earliest possible moment. I have noticed
very thoughtful people driving cars and
when they see someone approaching they put
their lights out, which makes it all the
better for those approaching. When motor
cycles and side-cars are going in the opposite
direction to these motor-cars with bright
lights there is certainly extreme danger. I
do hope the Government will give that matter
some attention.
There is another thing which I see mentioned in the Bill on which I would like to
have some information. I find that the
Ministry of Home Affairs intends with the
powers of this Bill-(Clause 16) to extend the
age limit in the Constabulary Act for the
purpose of the inspection and so on of these
machines. I for one fail to see the necessity
for extending the age limit. After the recommendation of the Committee that considered that particular question and after the
Act has been carefully drawn up, surely it
is not necessary. The men have been favourably situated in the police, or even police
workshops, for a number of years enjoying
the best of pay and are in a comfortable,
constant position, while
considerable
number of other workers are experiencing
the greatest possible hardship through the
terrible depression of trade. I can hardly
see that it is necessary to extend the age
limit for the purpose of getting some particular person to inspect or examine cars.
There may be good men in the Force; I have
always been of the opinion that there never
was a good man, but there was a better to
be had. I for one would like to hear some
explanation of the proposed extension of the
age limit, or I will be very much opposed to
it.
a
There is another matter which the Home
Secretary referred to in another place, but
which is not dealt with in this Bill. It
appears that the Home Secretary is very
much opposed to pillion-riding, or prejudiced
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
「Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
against it, although possibly I should not use
that word in connection with the Minister
of Home Affairs. Evidently he is not a
motor cyclist himself; he is probably served
with better conveyances, and, therefore, he
does not altogether appreciate the great pleasure that hundreds of young men and young
women derive from motor cycling and pillionriding. Nothing that could be done would
inflict greater hardship on the working
people than the prohibition of pillion-riding.
It is only the working class people who indulge in it. Unfortunately they cannot afford
either a side-car or a motor-car. A motorcycle is the only conveyance that they can
keep in an ordinary dwelling-house. The
door leading into the back of the premises
is only about 3 feet or 3 feet 6 inches wide,
and the door at the front is about the same
width, so that it is impossible for them to
keep any kind of a machine except a motor
bicycle. The motor cycle can be brought into
the dwelling through the ordinary doorway
at the back of the premises, and, therefore,
they are put to no inconvenience to house it.
To prohibit pillion-riding will prevent the
industrious young person who desires to see
the country from indulging in this pleasure.
It must be admitted that no young man, or
elderly man for that matter, cares to indulge
in motor cycling without a companion, and
I am quite satisfied that a motor cyclist.
could enjoy no better companionship than
that of a lady seated on the pillion. I do
wish to make an appeal to the Leader of the
House on this occasion to use his influence
with the Home Secretary to prevent interference with pillion-riding. I am satisfied
that the prohibition of pillion-riding would
impose a tremendous hardship on a consider.
able number of young men, and of elderly
men, too. I hope we will have an assurance
from the noble Leader of the House that we
need not worry any further on this matter.
Otherwise, I am strongly in favour of the
Second Reading of this splendid Bill for the
regulation of motor traffic.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we are all in
agreement with the statement of the noble
Viscount that the growth in mechanicallypropelled vehicular traffic, which has been
so enormous during the past few years,
necessitates a new examination of the restrictions imposed upon it, and an examination which must be made in the full light
of the conditions of the present day. When
the problem is considered it becomes, I think,
immediately apparent that there must be
two governing and over-riding considerations, and those two considerations are
the safety of the public, and, to some extent,
their convenience. When I am referring to
the safety and convenience of the public,
I wish it to be remembered that the
public includes
large proportion of
motorists. It is just as necessary to
a
protect the motorist as the pedestrian.
I sometimes wish that the pedestrian would
give the motor driver a little more assistance
than he does at present. The Government
has certainly done well in introducing this
Measure, after a good deal of preliminary
consideration, and I think it may fairly be
claimed that the Government has throughout the Measure kept well in mind those
two objects to which I have referred.
The noble Viscount said that something
in the nature of codification had been done
in the Measure. For myself I could wish
that there had been a great deal more codification. I should have liked to have got rid
of the old Act of 1896, so far as possible,
and of some parts of the Roads Act, and of
other statutes. I could have wished that
all our mechanically-propelled vehicle law
could have been compressed into one Act of
Parliament. The practical difficulties would
be very great, indeed, and I am not quite
sure that we have not really gone slightly
further in the direction I could wish for
than is safe at the moment, but I will speak
about that again.
The Bill before us has received the most
careful consideration in another place, and
that careful consideration will be a great
help to us now when we come to exercise
our duty of revision, a duty which will
necessarily be onerous, but a duty which
must be undertaken without fear and without favour, and with the fullest recognition,
if I may say so, of our individual responsibility. When this Bill becomes an Act,
and goes out to the public, and when this
Bill is abused, as it will be abused, by everybody on whose toes, metaphorically speaking, it treads, when that day comes it is
no use for an individual Member of Parlia
ment to say: "This is the Bill of the Govern
ment of Northern Ireland," because the
other person will retort, or at least I hope
he will, "You are a Member of Parliament.
Were you not in your place? Why did
you not call attention to this or that pro
vision? Why did you not put down Amendments? Why did you not weigh the thing
up, and get it put right?" that is, "get it
put right to please me"" before it became
an Act of Parliament?" There really is a
great responsibility on every Member of
this House.
One thing has emerged, and one great gain
has come to us, from that careful consideration of the Commons. When the Bill first
appeared there was no provision for laying
Regulations made by the Minister under the
Bill on the Table of the House. Had that
not been inserted at the instance of an hon.
Member, who sits elsewhere, we should have
been in the absurd position of having the
Minister of Home Affairs compelled to bring
before Parliament all new Regulations made
397 Motor Vehicles Bill-

11 NOVEMBER 1926
Second Reading.
E
2 under the original Act of 1896, and not so
compelled in respect of the Regulations made
under the new Act. The correction of that
oversight has saved us, I think, a large
amount of trouble.
dn

It is, I imagine, only under the powers
conferred by Section 6 of the 1896 Act that
it would be possible for the Minister of
Home Affairs to bring forward an Order
restricting the pastime or exercise of pillionriding, an Order to which my hon. Friend
on my left so strongly objects, and as regards
I which, I may say, I am heartily in support of my hon. Friend. That Section is
thirty years old, and when it was put into
the Act of 1896 I do not think there were
motor cyclists at all. I suppose, too, there
were no flappers; or at all events they were
not called by that name.
T

The Bill before us brings up divers new
正! proposals, and it will be for us very carefully
to consider and to make sure, if we can, that
those new proposals do not create new difficulties. We have to think of how far we can
support the proposals, and we have also to
consider how far those various proposals are
indlikely to bring about the end to which they
her!
are directed. Those new proposals include a
greater care and a greater supervision before
a driver's licence is issued, and that is a
matter in which I am sure the whole House
will be in support of the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Home Affairs.
heard the noble Viscount, whom I do not see
in his place to-day (Viscount Bangor), address
us at the opening of this Session. We heard
nd complaints of the issue of licences to the
blind, the maimed, and so on, and we know
that such a misuse of licences is likely to be
avoided in the future.

I understand from what has fallen from /
the noble Viscount (Viscount Charlemont)
that in future photographs will be placed on
driving licences, very much after the manner
of passports. To require a man to be photographed, and to carry his likeness about with
him for inspection by any policeman is a
provision which I, personally, most strongly
dislike, but in our particular circumstances
to-day I recognise that this putting of photographs on driving licences will be of the very
greatest possible assistance for purposes
far different from the enforcement of the
collection of the tax of, I think, five shillings.
I should certainly strongly support in the
House, and endeavour to defend outside, a
requirement which in itself I dislike extremely, because I dislike still more the
causes which render it to my mind a very
desirable precaution.
There are also many regulations as regards
public service vehicles. There are regulations
as regards inspection which I need not deal
with, because they are directed, as indeed
the noble Viscount claimed, towards the prevention of accidents. I rather wish we had
been told-perhaps the information may be
given later-what proportion of the total
death roll for the last eighteen months, or
any other convenient period, attributable to
motor vehicles is in connection with public
service vehicles. We all know that the
consequences of a catastrophe to a char-abane must necessarily be infinitely more
grave than the consequences of an accident
to an ordinary motor-car. We are all aware
of the dangers of overcrowding public service vehicles, and it is interesting to note
that proposals are in the Bill to deal with
that.
Again we have not my friend, but my
enemy-the minimum fine, and I shall hope
to attack that with more of the force at my
command than I did in the minor Measure,
the Horse Breeding Bill. We have not only
minimum fines, but minimum terms of imprisonment, and minimum terms for the suspension of licences. One has to remember
and one has to admit that the object of
punishment is primarily the protection of the
public, and when you are setting out deliberately to secure further public safety the time
has possibly come when you may properly
take a course which has the definite result
of protecting the public. It is quite obvious
that the definite protection of the public from
danger from motor-cars can be most efficiently secured by removing the motor driver
from the road for some period of time.
Another proposal in the Bill which I
shall not be able to support is the
proposal, in the case of
man who
has been charged before the magistrates with an offence and who has secured
the dismissal of the charge, to give the policeman who has appeared against him a right
of appeal. To that, I hope the house will
readily appreciate, I could not possibly give
my support.
a
Passing more directly to the Bill itself,
and without going more into the details,
one's attention is called naturally to the
very first Clause. It is known as the
dangerous driving Clause and it is the most
important, perhaps, in the whole Bill. The
Clause was originally based on the Clause
of the Act of 1903. It has been very greatly
improved elsewhere, although I am not
sure that it is quite all that we want. The
Clause has, I may confess, given me a great
deal of difficulty for more than twenty
years. The real trouble always was, and I
suppose always will be, that when a man
is charged with an offence under that
Clause or its predecessor the man is charged
with an offence which is not concrete but
abstract. He is not charged with actually
accomplishing anything. If he had run
399 Motor Vehicles Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Leslie]
down somebody and killed him he would
probably be charged with manslaughter,
and if he was convicted he would not be
subject to any minimum penalty because
there is not one for such a serious offence.
Whether a man is driving to the danger
of the public or not has always got to be
to a great extent a matter of opinion, based
on such evidence as may be obtained. It
may be that SO and SO did drive
dangerously, but so and so may be a motor
driver of twenty years' experience, and he
may know a great deal more about it than
the policeman. The difficulty is endless,
but the Clause before us is a better one
than we had before, although I believe it
might be possible to improve it still further.
If we find that impossible we shall have
to thankfully accept the Clause as an improvement on the original one.
a
man
Then we come to the question of the man
who is drunk in charge of a motor-car.
Being drunk in charge of a motor-car is,
I suppose, one of the most serious offencesusing the word offences as distinct from
crimes it is possible to conceive. Against
who really is drunk and who
really is driving a car you must protect the
public, but again we have that terrible conflict of opinion as regards the state of
drunkenness. That is a problem that has
perplexed magistrates for years and is
likely to go on doing so, but against such
a man there must be protection.
Now I come to the item which makes in
the direction of codification, and that is a
matter which has caused me great difficulty.
I hope there is an answer to what I am
going to say. I think there must be, but
I cannot find it in the Bill. It is proposed
to repeal the old Act of 1903, the original
Act under which we get our licence to-day,
and it is proposed to re-enact it in this Bill.
But the effect of repealing that Act, which
will not be done until the appointed day,
will be that amongst other provisions the
Section of the present Act dealing with
licencing will go. At present, as the House
knows,
licence to drive a motor-car
issuable in the County of Antrim is
accepted as good for driving in the County
of York. Conversely, a licence issued in
Lancashire is as good in Antrim.
a
If this Bill passes as it is, it is admitted
that a man coming into Northern Ireland
from Great Britain will require a new
licence, and provision is made for special
facilities for such a man, but what is to
happen to a man from Northern Ireland
who goes to England? His licence is
issued under the Act of 1926. To drive in
England you require a licence under the Act
of 1903, and to get such a licence in England you must be resident in the county
where you make the application. If you were
dealing purely with England or purely with
Northern Ireland the repeal of the old Act
would cause us no difficulty because of that
convenient provision in the Interpretation Act
that references to an old Act will carry on
in the new one. But here we are dealing
with a matter of two different Parliaments,
and there is no provision which will secure
reciprocity in that. Both countries have
their Interpretation Acts on identical lines.
but how can we ensure that a man from
Northern Ireland who wants to drive in
England, as many men do, will be able to do
so? How are we to arrange it? There may be
an answer, although I have not been able
to find it myself. The only alternative would
appear to be to postpone the appointed day
for the repeal of the old Act until reciprocity arrangements had been made by
Great Britain, presumably by Act of their
Parliament. It is a point of great importance, as the number of motor-cars that go
from Northern Ireland to England and Scotland is very great.
As regards the power of suspension, that
exists already. I think it ought not to be
forgotten that the power of suspending a
licence already exists, as does the power of
endorsement. What use the power of endorsement is I have never been very clear
about. The only object it seems to serve
is that if a man is charged and convicted
of an offence his licence can be produced,
if notice is given, and the magistrates know
the man has been convicted before-a
circumstance they ought not to know until
they have convicted him. I rather hope
the intention is to make more use of the
information so afforded.
I think we ought to limit the penalty for
a second or subsequent conviction. Offences
which have taken place within, say two
or three years of the first conviction, ought
not to stand against a man for five years,
so that if he is caught again, five years after,
he is still charged with the second offence.
It is a provision in the Roads Act of. I
think, 1920, that a licence which has no
endorsement less than three years' old can
be issued in a clean.form, and I think the
same thing should apply to a conviction
under this Bill,
The question of a local speed limit is one
that is always before us. These speed limits
are quite useless. They exist both in England and France, and I have generally found
that they are either regarded or disregarded.
They are regarded if there is a policeman
at each end, and they are also regarded if
the nature of the street or the circumstances
of the time require a slow speed.
Otherwise they are disregarded. There has been,
I know, a certain amount of agitation in
401 Motor Vehicles Bill-
11 November 1926

Second Reading.

i
IN
i
b

vehicle. That is also a point for further
elucidation.
I am in complete agreement
with the Government in putting the owner
of a public service vehicle to some extent in
the same position as a railway company in
the matter of liability for the acts of his
servants. I think that is necessary for the
protection of the public, and I have no objection to turning the public service vehicle
itself into a ship and dealing with it in the
same manner as ships are sometimes dealt
with. It is known to lawyers as an action in
rem. The cause follows the ship. The crime
occasionally is held to follow the car.
order to induce the Minister of Home Affairs
to remove the local speed limit entirely from
the Bill. I hope myself there will be no
more speed limits. I believe there are only
two at present, in Carrickfergus and Bangor,
and I should not like to suggest that the
power of imposing a local speed limit should
be entirely taken away, because I think it
would be infringing to some extent the
rights of the local authorities, whose opinion
in a matter of that kind ought to be regarded. Consequently, much as I dislike
local speed limits and believe them to be
generally useless, I should not advocate the
removal of the right to consider them.
The noble Viscount, the Leader of the
House, spoke of the proposals for the provision
of rear lights. I am sure that will have our
warmest concurrence. Speaking of lights reminds one of what my hon. Friend on the
left (Mr. Joseph Cunningham) said about
headlights. I can assure him that if anyone
will discover suitable headlights which will
light the road and will not dazzle the oncomer he will have no need of Regulations to
enforce their universal adoption. The danger
that exists when two motor-cars with powerful headlights are meeting each other is very
great. There is only one danger I believe to
be greater and that is when one of the cars
dims and the other does not. Dimmers have
been tried and found wanting. So far as the
Act is concerned there certainly is power by
Regulation to deal with the matter if a suitable solution can be found, and it will have
everybody's support when it comes.
Coming now more to the public vehicles
part of the Bill, I notice that in the description of a public service vehicle the expression,
"plying for hire at separate fares" has been
altered. I do not know whether that is intentional. We have had difficulties in various
ways with the Motor Char-a-bane Act because
something requires to be put into that Act
which was not noticed when we passed the
Measure, as to whether a man who keeps a
taxi cab and uses it in certain ways comes
under the Act or not. When it is a fourseater vehicle carrying six he has very often
been able to defend himself by saying, "I
let this car out to six men. I did not carry
them as separate fares." I should like to
have some information as to that point. It
would probably not be the least convenient
method if I put down some simple tentative
Amendment, but I must decline to bind myself to it at this stage.
As regards public service vehicles, it
appears to be clear that a man who wants
a public service vehicle must have a separate
licence to keep one. I assume that if he has
a licence to drive a public service vehicle
he can also drive an ordinary motor-car, but
I think it is clear that an ordinary motorcar licence will not cover a public service
The noble Viscount spoke about overcrowding. I must put before the House the country
point of view about that. We all must agree
that overcrowding is a dangerous nuisance,
but people at a country market on a wet day
will get home. You will not get so much
sympathy as regards overcrowding in the
country districts under this Bill as you
would if you were able to ensure that tram
cars in the city of Belfast were not overcrowded. Tram cars are excepted from the
operation of this Measure and the country
man will say, "I want to get home. Why
cannot I get into this bus?" You will say,
"You cannot. You will overcrowd the thing.
It is not possible to get in." You are putting
penalties on the conductor, who may be any
young man over the age of eighteen. He may
not be able to stem the crowd of would-be
getters home. In they get, the bus is started,
and the owner is fined. There is no provision
in this Bill for fining the person who
bores his way into an overcrowded vehicle.
I agree that there is very great difficulty if
30 people are found in a 20-seat bus to ascertain who are the offenders. We must have
care to have special regard to the point of
view of the country at night where there is
no regular service. It is easy to enforce
control of public service vehicles in Belfast;
it is not easy in the country.
I am glad the Ministry has asked for powers
to have proper officers for inspectors. I quite
agrée it is convenient that the people employed should be members of the Royal Ulster
Constabulary. I do not greatly care whether
these people are members of the Constabulary
at the time or if they become so. I do not
greatly care about their age. The point of
importance is that the persons selected
should understand the business. If such men
are not available, for any sake swear them
in. Never mind whether they are so many
inches round the chest.
The by-laws powers were a comparatively
late introduction elsewhere. I do not think
there will be any great objection, but I would
like to ask as regards that, where the necessary sanction to make these by-laws effective
is to be found in the Bill. It is rather difficult for the ordinary person whose library
is distinctly restricted to find out the by-laws
which are made in every urban district.
403 Motor Vehicles Bill-
Second Reading.

THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
I think the proposal to take appeals before
the county court judge sitting alone is a
most admirable provision. I should like to
see it extended. There has never been any
reason for many years in appealing from one
local bench to what is practically another.
I think that is a very desirable innovation.
Finally, while I support this Bill generally
I reserve to myself the fullest right to attack
not only the minimum penalties but also the
appeal provisions. I understand it is not
proposed to take the Committee stage until
Wednesday next, and I venture to hope that
during the intervening period we shall all
give further consideration to the Bill, and as
a result of that consideration further efforts
will be made to render it a still better
Measure than it is already.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I shall be
obliged if the hon. Senator-and I am sure
he will do so without any request from mewill put down some Amendments at a later
stage, and also I hope he will remember the
points he has raised to-day and so elucidate
information at that time. There are one or
two minor points which I can tell him at the
present moment as I have received information. The first is the proportion of accidents
due to public service vehicles. If he will
accept rough figures-
Mr. LESLIE: Oh, yes.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT
instead of
details, I will say about one-third. The
second thing is in regard to reciprocity in
driving here and in England. At present it
is the subject of an arrangement between the
Ministry of Home Affairs and the Ministry
of Transport in London. Further details
might be elicited later on. I have only that
bare fact. As regards the by-laws, sanction
is contained in the application of the Public
Health Act, section 18, sub-section (2).
as
of
Now I will turn to the question asked by
Senator Joseph Cunningham opposite.
Of course, anybody using the roads at night,
I do, knows of the danger
dazzling lights from which I have frequently
suffered when going home after the Senate
is over. The whole difficulty is that a proper
device for making any better state of things
has not yet been invented. I have information that the Royal Automobile Club in
London conducted a series of exhaustive experiments on the subject and the only conclusion reached was a negative one. They
say it is better to keep the lights always.up
rather than to dim and undim them, to the
danger of which hon. Senator Leslie referred.
I can only say that the Ministry is inquiring
into the subject and will continue to inquire.
Under this Act regulations can be made to
enforce any proper device that is put on the
market at such time as it shall be. That is
rather an indefinite answer, but I am afraid
it is the best I can give in the circumstances.
The second thing he asked was about pillionriding. Of course, it is quite certain that if
pillion-riding were prohibited it would be a
rather severe hardship on the people limited
to motor bicycles, and who could not take
ladies out any other way. The idea is
that safety should be conferred on the
actual people on the pillion, which is the
main point. In the present state of things
a great many accidents are due to the fact
that people fall off the pillion and break
their necks, and they have to be protected as
well as the actual driver. At the same time
pillion-riding is not in the Bill, so I might
say it really does not arise at the present
time. Then, as regards the extension of the
age limit for the R.U.C., it is only put in to
meet an emergency in cases where we have
not in the Royal Ulster Constabulary men of
sufficient experience or knowledge to act as
inspectors and we would have to employ
engineers over the normal age of entering.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: You could
pave the streets with engineers, they are so
numerous. Might I ask does the prohibition
of pillion-riding also apply to a married man
who has a child in front of him on the
handle-bars? Would that be prohibited
also? I have seen cases of married men
riding with their small son in front on the
handle-bars.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: That would
be as dangerous, if not more so, than pillionriding. I can only say that the whole subject
is under consideration at the present time.
On Question, Motion (That this Bill be now
read a Second time) agreed to.
a
Bill committed to Committee of the
Whole House for Wednesday next.
Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move the Second Reading
of the Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Bill, and I need only detain the House for
a few minutes.
Clause 1 proposes that the Ministry of
Finance be empowered to raise, by the issue
405 Exchequer Provisions Bill—

11 NOVEMBER 1926

Second Reading.
d.

an

her

of Ulster Loans Stock, a sum of £4,250,000 in
all, and consequently to lend up to that
amount for the various services financed
from the Government Loans Fund. That is
to say, the £900,000 margin under the Government Loans and Exchequer Provisions Act
of 1925 is on the verge of becoming exhausted, and authority is required to
advance a further sum of about two-and-aquarter million pounds during the next twoand-a-quarter years, that is, in the period up
to 31st March, 1929.
Clause 2 consolidates in a convenient form
the existing enactments relating to the
various classses of loans. A glance at the
Second Schedule to the Bill shows that the
present powers of the Ministry of Finance
in this respect are scattered through nine
various Acts. The present Bill repeals these
Acts to a certain extent and embodies in the
First Schedule a comprehensive list of lending purposes.
Clause 3 gives power to increase the amount
that may be spent on Police Barracks reconstruction from £375,000 to £800,000. The
original Estimate which was made was more
or less of a guess, and it has now been found
from contract prices and tenders received
that, on the basis of the improved accommodation which has been agreed as necessary,
£375,000 would be quite inadequate for carrying out the scheme as originally contemplated. The scheme will be carried out over
a period of ten years, and on this basis
average annual issues of £100,000 will be required from the Exchequer.
Clause 4 is a minor point. Technically
the Audit Office should disallow every
receipt submitted as a voucher for sums over
£2 if it does not bear a 2d. stamp. This is
occasionally not practicable and this dispensing power is accordingly given.
as
Clause 5 contains certain provisions in respect of the post of Comptroller and Auditor-
General. It is proposed that this officer shall
be placed on the same salary scale
attaches to a Secretary of a Ministry instead
of on a fixed salary of £1,350 as at present.
This officer's emoluments are charged not on
votes but on the Consolidated Fund, and
accordingly it is necessary to obtain statutory
confirmation before effect can be given to
this proposed change. Very little additional
expense is involved, as in the initial years
the occupant of the post will lose by the
change. A second important change is proposed, namely, the introduction of a condition requiring compulsory retirement at
sixty-five. Hitherto no age limit has been
fixed for this officer, who is not governed by
the ordinary Civil Service Regulations in
this respect. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committed.
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the
Third time on Tuesday next.
Local Government (War Service
Payments) Bill, 1926.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move the Second Reading
of this short one-clause Bill, the object of
which is to enable local authorities to pay
to their ex-Service officials or dependents
such proportion of war bonus and increment
of salary as may be decided.
During the late war, as hon. Members are
doubtless aware, many local authorities
undertook to make payments in respect of
salary or wages to their officers and employees
who enlisted in His Majesty's Forces for
the duration of the war. It was only in
1916, however, that statutory force was given
to these undertakings by the Local Government (Emergency Provisions) Act which
also gave the local authorities power, in the
event of the death of an employee whilst
serving with the Forces, to make payments
to his widow or other dependents.
The war brought with it, as we all know,
a great increase in the cost of living, which
necessitated a corresponding increase in the
salaries and wages of employees of local
authorities as, indeed, of most people in
general employment in these islands. In
some cases this increase took the form of a
war bonus, while in others a permanent increase of salary was made, but-and this is
where the necessity for the present Bill is
shown-the local authorities, in making
their contributions in respect of those employees serving in the war, had regard, not
to bonuses and increased salaries, which
were fixed after the date when their employee had left them, but to the actual
salaries and wages which those men had
enjoyed at the time when they joined the
forces. It is clear, therefore, I think, that
an employee who joined the Army towards
the end of 1914 might be receiving a smaller
payment from a local authority than his
fellow employee who perhaps did not join
the Army until 1916 or 1917 and who,
accordingly, had his contribution from the
same authority assessed on a salary which,
by reason of war bonus or increment, was
much greater than it had been in 1914.
After the end of the war the question was
raised as to whether these men who had
joined His Majesty's Forces were entitled to
407 Local Government Bill, 1926.

THE SENATE
Adjournment.
em-
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
the appropriate bonus or increment which
they naturally would have received had they
not left the service of the local authority
in order to serve their country. The case
was decided on Appeal in the House of
Lords, where it was ruled that these employees by joining the forces had, in effect,
severed their connection with their
ployers and that continuity of service was
thus broken. This decision, coupled with
the statutory limitation as to the time
within which such payments should be
made, has raised difficulties in the way of
local authorities making them without
Parliamentary sanction, and this Bill has
been introduced to remove any doubts which
may exist and to place the whole matter on
a proper legal basis. I trust that I have
made the position clear and that this House
will accord to the Bill their full and hearty
support. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I am perfectly certain that
the Bill which the noble Viscount has just
introduced will prove of great convenience to
our local authorities in enabling them to do
that which they know to be the right thing,
and a thing which their constituents expect
them to do. I am sure the whole of us are
most heartily in agreement with the principles of the Measure.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Bill committed to
Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.

THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 16th November, 1926.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Labour:
Factory and Workshop. The Docks Regulations (Northern Ireland), 1926, dated
October 28th, 1926, made by the Ministry of
Labour for Northern Ireland under Section
79 of the Factory and Workshop Act, 1901
(1 Edw. 7, c. 22) in respect of the processes
of Loading, Unloading, Moving and Handling Goods in, on or at any Dock, Wharf or
Quay, and the processes of Loading, Unloading and Coaling any Ship in any Dock,
Harbour or Canal.
Presented (pursuant to Act), and ordered
to lie on the Table.
THE CASE OF LIEUTENANT
CLENDINNING.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Dorman:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government is aware of the persistent
police prosecution of Lieutenant Cecil Clendinning in Belfast; whether the Government
have received any information from the
British authorities as to their refusal to
grant a public inquiry into the charges
made by Lieutenant Clendinning in connection with his treatment in India; and
whether the Government of Northern Ireland agree that an officer who has served in
His Majesty's Army should be treated in
this fashion.
Mr. DORMAN: I desire to draw the attention of the Government to the treatment
of the young officer, Lieutenant Clendinning,
who for the last nine years has been seeking
to obtain the right which is extended to
every British citizen before judgment is
passed upon him, the right of giving evidence
on his own behalf and testifying in person
before some tribunal as to the charge that
was brought against him while on active
service in His Majesty's Forces in India. I
do not wish to detain the House at any considerable length, but I desire to say that I
have had several interviews with this gentleman and I have carefully read the documents
which he is circulating on the public
thoroughfares with the object of arousing
public interest, so as to create a sufficient
demand to the authorities to extend to him
the right I have already mentioned, of calling witnesses and testifying in person to the
fact that he was not at any period of his
service suffering from mental instability or
insanity.
Any person who has read through the documents the lieutenant is circulating will see
that there is reasonable suspicion that this
young gentleman has been treated with scant
courtesy and consideration, and that he
has had injustices heaped upon him.
He still, I understand, holds His Majesty's
Commission as an officer in the Army, and
is, therefore, liable at any time to be called
up for service, and that service is to be
rendered in connection with
regiment
which I believe is now a distinctively Ulster
Regiment, namely, the late Royal Irish
Rifles, whose headquarters are now situated
in Belfast. In confirmation of what I say
I would just like to read three short extracts
relating to this case. The first is as follows:
a
The Chaplain of Cawnpore, on the 14th
March, 1917, interviewed the officer commanding Cawnpore Station, Colonel A. H.
Woods, and informed him that he found
me detained as an alleged mental case at
the Station Hospital whilst in a normal
state of mind, and requested him to intervene on my behalf. which he refused to
do.
In a letter, dated 25th September,
1917, and published by the Englishmen”
(Calcutta), the Chaplain states:-
"
"Having had long and frequent conversation with you. I must say I have
been struck by the clear and lucid way
you have always and at all times expressed yourself. I feel it is my duty
to testify to the fact that you have always
appeared to me in normal condition, and
that in the face of trying conditions. I
have come to this conclusion after months
of observation and careful thought
over your case."
That is signed by D. A. Canney. There
is a second passage in this document which
I would also like to read.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am afraid the hon.
Member is out of order. He can only at
present go into the question of the prosecution of this lieutenant by the police in
Belfast. He can also ask whether the
Government has any information from the
British authorities as to their refusing to
grant, etc., but he cannot go into the rights
and wrongs of the whole case. He must
adhere to the question as down on tho
Paper.
Finance Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
Mr. DORMAN: In that case I wish to
make this statement. This young officer is
liable, as I have already said, to be called
up for active service. I do not think
anyone of us can conceive of a superior
officer in command of an important undertaking in the future, having any suspicion
in his mind that such a subordinate officer
had at any time suffered from mental instability or insanity calling upon him, in
his official capacity as the officer in charge
of the operations, to take charge of a large
body of men. If only from that point of
view I think that as a people and as a
Parliament, considering that this young
officer is of Ulster parentage, we ought to
interest ourselves to see that this young
son of Ulster parents is not continuously
denied this right of being heard in his own
defence. Otherwise it is possible that in
the future the War Office in Great Britain
will in a similar manner deal out cruel
and harsh treatment to the child of some
other Ulster parents.
All I wish to say in conclusion is this.
The vilest criminal and the most atrocious
murderer in the community, after he has
been tried by twelve of his fellow countrymen and found guilty, and before the judge
assumes the black cap to pass sentence, is
given an opportunity of saying why sentence of death should not be passed upon
him. Here is a young man of Ulster parentago upon whose professional career sentence
of death has been passed, and I think it is
the duty of the Government to make some
gesture--

Mr. SPEAKER: I want to give the hon.
Senator as much latitude as I can, but he
is paying no attention whatever to my
ruling.
Mr. DORMAN: I will conclude by asking
what steps the Government propose to take
in view of the treatment doled out to this
young gentleman by the authorities across
the water.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): This is a matter entirely for
the Imperial Government. The Government
of Northern Ireland has received no information from the Imperial Government regarding Lieutenant Clendinning's claim for
an inquiry, and it does not feel called upon
to express any opinion on that matter.
Finance (Stamp Duty) Bill (Northern
Ireland), 1926.
Order for Second reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move the Second reading
of this Bill, which, as hon. Senators will see,
relates entirely to matters connected with
Stamps Revenue. With the permission of
the House I should like to give a short explanation of the three Clauses contained in
the Bill.
Clause 1 proposes to empower the Ministry of Finance, under certain conditions, to
exempt from Stamp Duty Grants or Letters
Patent issued under the Great Seal of Northern Ireland. The conditions necessary for
exemption are, as hon. Senators will note,
that the appointment shall not be an office
of profit, and that it is in the interests of
the public that such an appointment be
made. Examples which will illustrate the
meaning of the Clause are the Letters Patent
issued to the Lords Justices for the Government of Northern Ireland in the temporary
absence of His Grace the Governor, and the
Letters Patent empowering the Controller of
the Stationery Office to exercise the rights
and privileges in connection with Crown
copyright in Northern Ireland.
In such cases the Stamp Duty is technically payable by the parties concerned, but
in practice the Imperial Government has
always made the payments on their behalf.
When one Exchequer only was involved
there was no difficulty, but as the law stands
at present an actual cash payment must now
be made to the Exchequer of Northern Ireland by the British Government at whose
instigation such Letters Patent are issued.
Clearly this is inequitable, as the Imperial
Government is only making a certain ap
pointment for the convenience of administratration in Northern Ireland, and the Bill
now before the House proposes to relieve
the Imperial Government in future from
this obligation.
Clause 2 re-enacts for Northern Ireland
the rates of Stamp Duty imposed by the
Stamp Act of 1891 on admissions of persons
as students of the Inn of Court, and as
barristers. An additional exemption is introduced in respect of the barrister's fee with
a view to obviating hardship which might
otherwise result from the establishment of
the Northern Ireland Inn. This exemption
is in favour of those persons called to the
Northern Ireland Bar, who, having entered
the King's Inns in Dublin as duly recognised
Northern Ireland students before the date
of the establishment of the Northern Ireland Inn, were called to the Bar in Dublin,
and paid their barrister's fee there. It will
be recognised, I think, that it would be
unfair to demand a second payment from
these persons.
Clause 3 fixes the proportion of the
duties on these admissions which is to
be paid over to the new Inn of Court.
This I may mention is not a new departure, for the original Stamp Act of 1891
413 Appropriation Bill.

provided for somewhat similar payments to
the Society of the King's Inns in Dublin.
It will be noted that there is a maximum
contribution of Five Hundred pounds in any
one year. The number of admissions will
naturally vary from year to year, but it is
estimated that the average contribution will
be somewhere between Three hundred and
fifty pounds and the maximum of Five
hundred pounds. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill before us being
primarily a Stamp Duty measure would not,
I think, occasion any discussion in this
House in the ordinary way, but I do not
like to let the occasion pass without drawing
attention to the seal, so to speak, which this
Bill will give to an innovation which has
recently been made. Owing to the separation-perhaps I should say owing to the
division of Ireland recently made by the
Imperial Government it has been necessary
for us in Northern Ireland to set up our
own Inn of Court, that is to say, our own
university for the training of those who
wish to follow the profession of barrister.
Inns of Court in England, as the House
knows, date back to Tudor times and beyond,
and we are now setting up here for the first
time, in Northern Ireland, a new Inn,
which we all hope will follow the old tradition, and enable its alumni to shed as much
lustre upon the profession as in the
past, either the English Inns or the King's
Inns in Dublin. It is necessary, of course,
that some provision should be made for the
expense of the Inn. The Bill before us proposes that. I imagine myself that it will be
necessary for action to be taken in the direction of housing the members of that Inn,
and if any application is made in that regard
I venture to hope that Parliament will receive it in a friendly spirit and treat the
matter with the consideration it deserves.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committed.
-(Viscount Massercene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time to-morrow.

CLAUSE 6 (Duties of owners of stallionsi.
The owner of a stallion in respect of
which a licence or a permit is in force
under this Act shall
(a) give notice forthwith to the Ministry of Agriculture in the prescribed
manner and at the prescribed time of
any sale or letting or other change in
the ownership of the stallion or of its
castration or death; and
(b) submit the stallion to inspection
and examination by any officer of the
Ministry of Agriculture or authorised
person, if and when required by the
said Ministry; and
(c) return the licence and the certified copy (if any) or the permit to the
said Ministry forthwith on the expiration, revocation or suspension of the
licence or permit;
and if he fails to comply with any of the
requirements of this section he shall be
liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding five pounds.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, page 7, lines 19 and
20, to leave out "in respect of which a
licence or a permit is in force under" and
insert "of the age specified in Section one
of." The object of this Amendment is to
widen this Clause. As the Clause now stands
only the owner of the stallion in respect
of which a licence or a permit is in force
would have to comply with these conditions,
consequently it is necessary to widen the
Clause and to insert the words "of the age
specified in Section one of." I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, page 7, line 30, at the
beginning ("(c) return the") to insert "where
a licence or a permit has been in force."
This really is in a sense consequential on
the former Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

CLAUSE 7 (Inspection of Stallions).
Any officer of the Ministry of Agriculture, police officer or constable, or
authorised person, shall have power to
inspect and examine any stallion, and
shall for the purposes of this Act have
power to enter at all reasonable times any
premises where he has reason to believe
that any stallion is kept, and if any person refuses to allow any such officer, constable or authorised person (who, if so required, produces his authority) to inspect
and examine any stallion or to enter any
premises which he is entitled to enter
under this section, or obstructs or impedes
such officer, constable or authorised person
in the exercise of his powers under this
section, the person so refusing, obstructing or impeding shall be liable on conviction to fine not exceeding twenty
pounds.
a
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move page 7, line 39, after
"stallion" ("examine any stallion and ")
to insert "or other animal." As the Clause
now stands the police officer, constable, or
other authorised person has only power to
inspect and examine a stallion. If he went
to examine a stallion and found it was another animal he would be breaking the law.
I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: This Amendment which has
just been moved by the noble Viscount is
really the first of three Amendments on the
Paper, all of which are related to one another. They are being brought forward, I
think, to meet the difficulty which was put
up in this House by myself and other
Members when the Bill was in Committee,
and I desire to take the opportunity of saying
that the Amendments taken as a whole, as
they now stand, completely meet the views
which I then endeavoured to express. I should
like, if I may go further, to say that I consider
that I owe gratitude to the members of the
Department with whom I had an opportunity
of discussing the proposed alterations for
the time and attention which they devoted
to the question. I spent some time in investigating with them what really was required to meet the views of the Minister, on
the one hand, who wanted all necessary
powers and of this House on the other
who wanted to see that no unnecessary
powers were granted. I must say I was
greatly impressed with the wide views taken
by all concerned. I am sure that the result
of the efforts bestowed on it will materially
enhance the merits of the Measure.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
KARD: I beg to move, page 7, line 39, after
"stallion" to insert "but in the case of a
police officer or constable so far only as is
necessary for the purpose of requiring, reporting and giving evidence as to contraventions of this Act" This is really to meet
the wishes of the hon. Senator who has just
spoken. It clearly says that the police
officer who comes to any premises to inspect
will only do so for the purpose of requiring,
reporting, and giving evidence of contraventions of this Act.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move in page 8, lines 3 and
4, to leave out "to inspect and examine any
stallion or." This really has to do with the
same Amendment. It is consequential. I
beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Senator Leslie raised a
question about the castration of stallions,
and I promised I would give a full answer on
report. The facts are these. The tendency in
recent years among experienced veterinarians
is to perform this operation while the animal
is in a standing position. If the administration of a complete anæsthetic were made
compulsory this very useful method would,
to a great extent, have to be discarded.
An animal which has been castrated without an anæsthetic will, on being liberated,
commence to feed immediately, and given
asceptic conditions the wound will heal without untoward results.
On the other hand, an animal which has
been put under a complete anesthetic will,
on recovering consciousness remain dull and
nauseated for a fairly considerable period.
Further, the use of an anesthetic in this
connection would mean added cost to owners
and, in most cases, it would mean the casting and securing of the animal before the
administration of the anæsthetic. The result would be possible injury to limb and
spine, which possibility is obviated by the
standing method.
It is understood that the placing of this
operation in the first schedule to the Act of
1919 was considered when that Measure was
being passed in the British Parliament but,
after full consideration, the consent of that
body was withheld. The Government of
Northern Ireland do not consider it necessary
that it should be included in the Act of 1919.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time to-morrow.

Sir WILLIAM COATES: May I just for
one moment say I am very glad this Bill
has reached this stage in its passage through
the two Houses. The particular reason why
I am glad is because it will enable certain
local authorities in Northern Ireland to
carry out the promise which they made to
their employees in connection with the payment to ex-Servicemen of war bonus. The
promise has been made to these men that
the bonus will be paid, and the Bill will
enable it to be done. I can only hope that
the Royal Assent to the Bill will be granted,
if it passes its final stages now, at a very
early date, so as to make it possible for the
local authorities who have arranged to pay
actually to pay the money to the men before
Christmas.
Mr. DORMAN: The hon. Member has
spoken on behalf of the local authorities
who are the employers of these men, and I
would like on behalf of many of these men
to express my gratitude to the Government
for this Measure, and I hope it will become
law before Christmas.

On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time to-morrow.

CLAUSES 1 (Power of Ministry of Agriculture to refuse to grant, licences to wholesale dealers in eggs in certain cases), 2 (Evidence of authority to purchase on behalf of
licensed wholesale dealers), and 3 (Returns
with respect to dealings in preserved eggs)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Extension of power to make
rules.)
Question proposed: That Clause 4 stand
part of the Bill.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I did not rise to
propose an Amendment to this Clause, but
just to elicit from the Ministry whether
it will be necessary for a grocer or baker
who goes round the country collecting eggs,
as is generally done in Northern Ireland, to
have a licence for doing so. I can quite
understand that if he takes a number of
eggs from a farmer's house and then calls
in to certain wholesale merchants he should
have a permit. This man may take the
eggs to several grocers, and I would like to
know if it is necessary that he should have
a licence to do so.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Under the
Marketing of Eggs Act, 1924, every wholesale
dealer in eggs must be licensed by the
Ministry of Agriculture. The term "wholesale dealer" is defined in the Act as including
any person who carries on the business of
purchasing eggs for resale otherwise than
by retail. Thus a trader who purchases in
markets or by itinerant collection throughout the country need not be licensed so long
as all the eggs are sold retail, but if the eggs,
or any of them, are sold to another trader,
then the first trader must be licensed. The
determining factor is the manner in which
the merchant disposes of the eggs. In the
Bill at present before the Senate, Clause (2)
makes provision for exempting from these
provisions persons who act as purchasing
agents of wholesale dealers. Such accredited
agents will, in future, be required to hold a
of their employer's licence, duly
copy

Committee...!
[Viscount Charlemont]
counter-signed, as a mark of their authority
to act on his behalf, and need not be themselves licensed.
warm
Mr. DORMAN: There is a matter to which
I wish to draw the attention of the Ministry, and that is the custom which prevails
in the adjoining counties of bartering for
I am informed that a fairly large
eggs.
number of bread-van drivers take part payment for the bills due them for bread in the
form of eggs from their customers throughout the country.
It is quite possible that
the eggs are placed in a vehicle containing
bread, and the radiation of the
heat may injuriously affect the keeping
qualities of the eggs. There may not be
possible detection of that on this side before
they reach the other side. I would like to
know if the Minister's attention has been
drawn to this. According to the statement
of the right hon. Gentleman, the Leader of
the House, apparently such a man would be
compelled, under this Bill, to obtain
