Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Licensing and physical fitness
of drivers).
(1) A person shall not drive a motor car
on a public highway unless he is licensed
for the purpose under this section, and a
person shall not employ any person who
is not so licensed to drive a motor car. If
any person acts in contravention of this
provision he shall be guilty of an offence
under this Act.
If any person whose licence under this
section is suspended, or who is disqualified
for obtaining such licence or who has
obtained such licence whilst disqualified
for obtaining the same, drives a motor car
the public highway, he shall be
liable on summary conviction to imprisonment for a period of not less than one and
not more than six months.
on
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(4) A licence must be produced by any
person driving a motor car when demanded
by a police officer or constable.
If any
person fails to produce his licence he shall
be liable, on summary conviction, in respect
of each offence to a fine not exceeding five
pounds.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(6) Where a court of summary jurisdiction is satisfied, upon representation made
to it by a police officer or constable in such
manner, and after such notice to the
licence holder, as may be prescribed, that
a person, being resident in the petty sessions district for which such court acts,
who holds a licence under this section is,
owing to physical disability, unfit to drive
a motor car, the court shall have the same
powers of suspending the licence and declaring the person to be disqualified for
holding or obtaining a licence, and (on
being satisfied that the disability has
ceased) of removing the suspension or disqualification, as it would have if that
person had been convicted before it of an
offence under this Act.
(7) The Ministry of Home Affairs may
make regulations with respect to the
licences to be granted under this section,
and in particular-
*
*
*
*
*
*
(e) for providing special facilities for
granting licences to persons not resident in Northern Ireland.
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
(8) The council of a county or county
borough may charge in respect of the
issue of a new licence in place of a licence
lost or defaced such fee, not exceeding one
shilling, as the Ministry of Home Affairs
may prescribe.
(9) This section shall come into operation
on the appointed day, and as from that
day section three of the Motor Car Act,
1903, shall cease to have effect.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 3,
page 3, line 16, to leave out "less than one
and not".
A minimuni penalty in the form of imprisonment is very rare indeed, although
there are instances in the past of minimum
fines. I pass over such cases as when a man
is sent to jail, and may get not less than
seven days, or to penal servitude, which
shall not be less than three years. It seems to
me, although I agree that the offence for
which this imprisonment is to be imposed
is a very serious offence, that if a man is
to be sent to jail at all you must leave the
discretion to the magistrates. Let the magistrates say how long, up to six months, the
imprisonment shall be. It seems to me unnecessary to introduce a minimum sentence
of imprisonment of one month. I think you
ought to allow the court discretion in that
matter. It is going a long way to make
the offence punishable without the option,
and it will upset the whole Bill. I do think
it might be left to the discretion of the court.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am sorry I cannot
agree with the hon. and learned Senator.
This Clause was deliberately put into the
Bill as one of the many recommendations
of the Roads Advisory Committee, of which
two of the hon. Senators in this House are
members, and to whom we owe a great deal
in connection with the framing of this
Measure. I do not know that the hon.
Senator has fully appreciated what this
applies to. A minimum term of imprisonment is only applicable to the case of a man
whose licence has been taken away by the
court for a previous offence. That man
comes out deliberately with the knowledge
that his licence has been taken away, and
he flouts the order of the court. It is not
the case of a first offence, because before a
man can have his licence taken away he
has perhaps committed one or two
offences. His licence is taken away by
the court, and he knows he is deliberately
flouting the order of the court.
This is an
exceptional case, and it will destroy the
whole benefit of the Bill if we are going to
allow individuals, if the courts have said
they are not fit to have licences either
because they have been drunk or because
they have driven wantonly or recklessly, to
go out again and risk being caught again in
the hope that they would get off with a caution at the hands of a sympathetic Bench.
A man who does a thing of this sort deliberately, with his eyes open, ought not to be
the object of any sympathy.
Mr. LESLIE: I was fairly careful to disclaim any sympathy with the offence. My
case was that the Bench ought to have some
discretion. After all, the Bench will know
the circumstances of the case. I think it is
a very unusual practice indeed that Parliament should decree that there shall be a
minimum term of imprisonment of one
month, quite irrespective of the circumstances. All we know is that a man has
committed an offence, which we all agree is
a serious offence, and which we are prepared
to punish by imprisonment or fine. I think
we-
The CHAIRMAN: Does the hon. Member
move?
Mr. LESLIE: I was hoping I might prevail on the right hon. Gentleman to reconsider the matter.
The CHAIRMAN: Does the hon. Member
move?
Mr. LESLIE: I move to leave out the
words "less than one and not."
Amendment negatived.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I beg to move in
page 4, line 9, at end ("under this Act") add-
"Provided that, where the driver of a motor
vehicle is not in possession of his licence at
the time when it is demanded, he shall not
be guilty of an offence under this section if
he gives his correct name and address, and
produces his licence to the Police Authority
in the district in which he resides within
three days".
The Amendment which I move states that
the police will not have authority to prosecute unless the person does not give his correct name and address. I think it is giving
the police constable far too great authority
to arrest a man and bring him to a court
of summary jurisdiction simply because he
has left his licence at home in another coat.
That does not apply to a man who goes out
shooting. If he has forgotten his gun
licence he can produce it within three days.
But a man driving a motor unless he produces his licence immediately to the police
will be prosecuted although he may accidentally leave it at home. My hon. Friend
(Mr. Long) told us the last time he was
speaking on this subject that he let his
licence go three months out of date. I think
really that a man who forgets his licence on
one journey should not be subject to appearing in court. Of course, there is no minimum fine in this case, and the magistrates
449 Motor Vehicles Bill-

17 NOVEMBER 1926
Committee
上
the

to

d
would perhaps only impose a penalty of 1s,
but he would have to appear in court or be
represented there by a solicitor. I think
that is unreasonably hard.
a
Mr. LONG: Senator Hill Dickson has mentioned the fact that I had confessed to committing one offence. I have to confess
another offence. On the only occasion on
which I was stopped by a policeman he demanded my licence. I had not it with me at
the time, but I submitted it to the districtinspector for the area on the following day.
He was quite satisfied, and I was not summoned or proceeded against in any way. I
may just point out to Senator Dickson and
other Members of the Committee that
Regulation such as this has been in force
during the last twenty-three years and no
inconvenience has been caused. At the same
time I really do not see why a law that is
on the Statute Book should not be put into
effect by the police. This is a case where the
Regulations seem to over-ride the Act. The
1903 Act contained a provision similar to the
one in this Bill. For this reason then I do
not see that any harm could result from an
acceptance of the Amendment. It seems to
me that it would bring the law and the
Regulations into harmony, and I think that
is very desirable. Therefore, although I
must admit that the Constabulary administered the old Act with great discretion and
consideration for everyone, I really think
they would welcome this, and no possible
harm could be done by accepting this
Amendment.
Sir DAWSON BATES: As my hon. Friend
Senator Long has stated, this is simply a reenactment of the old Clause in the 1903 Act,
which has been in existence for the last
twenty-three years. I believe it is a most
useful Clause, and as time goes on it will
be seen how useful it is. Senator Hill
Dickson proposes that an individual who for-
- gets his licence is not to be guilty of an
offence provided he produces his licence
within three days. Imagine a case--and it
is not an uncommon case, and will become
more common later on as motor cars increase
-imagine a case of a man who steals a
motor car out on the stand. The policeman
has suspicions about him and he stops him.
The individual says to the policeman: “I am
sorry; my coat was wet; I had to change it
and forgot my licence, but I will bring it to
you within three days." He gives the name
of John Jones, perhaps the name of the
owner of the car. Of course, the gentleman
who stole the car never turns up. In the
administration of this Act and I think it
applies to all laws the police take a very
proper view of offences of this character. If
a case occurs where a man really does it
inadvertently the matter is reported to the
district-inspector who, having considered all
P
the circumstances, does not think it necessary
to bring a prosecution; but if he finds that
that individual again and again refuses to
produce his licence then it would be a matter for the police to act strictly and according to the circumstances. I do suggest, considering the increased number of motor cars,
that it is a most necessary thing. If it is
not inserted no one will carry his licence at
all. He will not take the trouble to do so.
Take the case of season-ticket holders on
railways. I venture to say that if a passenger forgets his season ticket he will be made
pay up at once. That is certainly the case
on the English railways. The result is that
the season-ticket holder does not forget his
ticket. For my own part I do not see why
an individual should not carry his licence.
Mr. LESLIE: It may interest some Members of the Committee to know that when
this Clause was inserted in the 1903 Bill, in
the House of Commons in London, the Committee stage was taken for the Government
of that day by the late Mr. Walter Long, as
he then was, and the late Sir Robert
Finlay, as he then was, turn and turn about.
An Amendment similar to that moved by
the hon. Member (Mr. Hill Dickson) was
moved in the Committee stage, and Mr. Long
made a definite statement.
I forget the
reference, but you will find it in Column
480 of the Imperial Hansard of the 7th
August, 1903. Mr. Walter Long, in response
to a suggestion that a man might be held
up in the North and had left his licence in
the South, said definitely that if the licence
is produced within a reasonable time, no
offence will be committed. It would be on
the same footing as a gun licence in that
respect.
There is another point that I think has
been mentioned. This Clause is objected to,
I know, but I think it can be defended. It
is sometimes said that it is very hard that
a man should be arrested. There is no
power in this Clause to arrest a man for
non-production of his licence. That is a
popular error which seems to have got into
the newspapers. That is not so. I think
that the rule is different.
The police do
not prosecute when the licence is produced
within a reasonable time, but, as I know
from magisterial experience, the man summoned does not take that trouble in the
matter; he gets summoned and then gets
fined.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I would point out
to the Minister that his argument in connection with stealing a motor car does not
apply, because the policeman has still power,
unless he knows the man has given a correct name
it.
Sir DAWSON BATES: He does not know
451 Motor Vehicles Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Then he takes
action and arrests him and does what he
likes with him.
Sir DAWSON BATES: May I clear up the
point? It is proposed, under our Regulations in the future, that each holder of a
licence must have his photograph on the
licence, and by means of that the policeman
will be able to identify the individual who
is in possession of the car. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.)
The CHAIRMAN: Does the hon. Member
move the Amendment?.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Yes, I think that
knocks the bottom out of Sir Dawson Bates'
case.
Amendment negatived.
"
c,
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 4, line
31, after "powers" ("have the same powers")
insert of endorsing and." This is a very
simple matter. It is a case of whether these
words are wanted or are not. It seems
to me that if they are wanted, having regard
to paragraph
of sub-Section 1, of
Section 4, and perhaps to sub-Section 5, it
is purely a matter of administration. It is
entirely a matter, I think, for the right
hon. Gentleman to tell us whether he wants
those words inserted or whether he does not.
The licence, of course, is the property of the
owner, and it seems to me you cannot, unless
you have power, very well endorse it without his consent. Do you want these words
to appear or not?
Sir DAWSON BATES: We do not want
the power of endorsement. We want to take
away the licence under this particular sub-
Clause 6. One cannot very well endorse a
licence which it is proposed to take away.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 5, line
14, at end ("Northern Ireland") add-
"and (f) for exempting in respect of
motor cycles, and notwithstanding the provisions of sub-section (5) of this section
any person over the age of 14 years who
is licensed to drive a motor cycle in Great
Britain from the disqualification in respect
of age imposed by that sub-section".
This proposal is to add a new paragraph
to the sub-clause dealing with the Minister's
power of making Regulations. The words
are on the Paper. What I want to do is to
save for every boy coming over to Northern
Ireland on holidays the right to use his
motor bicycle, which he has got in Great
Britain. I think in the case of boys who
want to ride their motor bicycles on holidays,
if they cannot ride them here they will not
come here. The law under this Bill will be
different to the law in England. Any boy of
14 years in England can get a motor cycle,
but he cannot do so here until he is 15 years,
I, therefore, suggest we should insert words
to give the right hon. Gentleman the power
which he would be glad to exercise in suitable cases and under proper Regulations to
exempt a person over 14 years who is
licensed in England from disqualification in
respect of age. I do not think it would do
any possible harm, and it might be an encouragement to some of our young friends.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The question of
the age limit for youths driving motor cars
received very careful consideration at the
hands of the Government, and we thought
we went very far when we fixed the limit
at 15 years of age. Many people think we
have gone too far in this direction. My hon.
and learned Friend suggests that we should
have a special exemption in regard to
English boys coming over to Ireland. I will
put a case to my hon. Friend. Supposing he
sends one boy to an Irish school, and another
bey to an English school, and supposing they
are twins of 15 years of age; the boy who
comes home from the English school, having
taken out a licence in England, will be able
to drive his motor car, but his brother will
not be. I do suggest that it would be absolutely impracticable to have a Regulation
under which an individual can go into
another country and get privileges which
are not accorded to the people living in that
country. I might as well suggest to my hon.
Friend, when he goes to England, that
because the public-houses are open all day
in Belfast he can go into a public-house in
London in the afternoon and have a drink,
while the local inhabitants are debarred
from doing so. I am sure my hon. Friend,
from that point of view, will see that it
would be unreasonable and unworkable to
insert the words.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 5,
line 20, to leave out from the word "day"
("as from that day"), to the end of line 21,
and to insert the words "licences to drive a
motor car shall be issued and shall be expressed to be issued under Section (3) of the
Motor Car Act, 1903, as that Section is
amended by this Act."
The Amendment on the Paper is, I venture
to think, one of very great importance, but
I have to admit that I did not put it down
in the expectation, or even the hope, that the
right hon. Gentleman (Sir Dawson Bates)
would accept it. I am not quite sure of what
would happen if he did accept it, and I am
afraid it has to be admitted on examination
that it would not really effect what I desire
should be effected. I have put down the
Amendment in order to get the right hon.
453 Motor Vehicles Bill-

17 NOVEMBER 1926
Committee.

Gentleman to tell us what the arrangements
are going to be as regards people in Northern Ireland who have licences to drive in
Northern Ireland, and who want to drive
their cars in Great Britain.
At the moment, as we know, there are no
4 special regulations at all. You take out your
licence in Northern Ireland, and the licence
☐ is held to be valid in England or in Scotland.
I say it is "held" to be valid. Under this
Bill, when it becomes an Act, the licence
will no longer purport to be issued under
the Act of 1903, which applied to Great
Britain and Ireland, and there may be difficulty in consequence. Under the Act of 1903
the person wishing to obtain a licence in
England must apply to the county council of
the county in which he happens to be resident. But a man from Northern Ireland is not
necessarily resident in England. The question
arises, what is going to happen when we
have repealed the Act of 1903, and how are
we to get over this difficulty, if at all? I am
afraid that the adoption of this Amendment
E
B


i would not have any actual effect, because it
would be trying to make our laws run in
Great Britain, which, of course, we cannot
do. But I would ask the right hon. Gentleman to consider this matter, and to satisfy
us that all is being done that can be done
in order to enable a man from Northern
Ireland to take his car over to Great Britain
and drive it as before. For the moment, I
beg to move.
Sir DAWSON BATES: My hon. and
learned Friend (Mr. Leslie) has asked me
what would happen if this Amendment were
accepted. My reply is, the whole Bill would
at have to be re-drafted. I think he is under
a misapprehension in regard to the whole
question of the inter-validity of licences between the two countries. That is not on the
statute at all, even at the present time.
When the Government of Ireland Act was
passed the administration of the 1903 Motor
Act passed over to Northern Ireland. At the
present time it is a matter of agreement between the two Governments as to whether
or not we are prepared to admit motorists
coming over here. We are delighted to see
them, and we are doing everything we can
to encourage them to come over here, and I
am glad to say that the British Government
are doing the same. It is entirely a matter
of agreement between the two countries. We
cannot legislate in regard to this matter and
compel the English people to accept our
licences. It is entirely a matter of agreement; it is not a question of the statute at
all. Whether this Amendment is passed or
not does not affect the present situation.
Mr. LESLIE: I ask leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: I want to ask a
question with regard to the age limit for the
issue of licences. I want to know what the
age limit is in Great Britain.
Sir DAWSON BATES: It is exactly the
same in both countries, except in regard to
motor bicycles. For motor bicycles the age
limit in Great Britain is fourteen, and
under this Bill we propose to make it fifteen
in Northern Ireland.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Suspension and endorsement
of licence, and disqualification).
(1) Without prejudice to the infliction of
any other penalty, any court before whom
a person is convicted of any offence under
this Act, or of any offence in connection
with the driving of a motor car punishable
(whether on indictment or summarily)
otherwise than under this Act-
*
*
(a) may, if the person convicted holds a
licence under this Part of this Act,
suspend that licence for such time as
the court thinks fit, and also declare
the person convicted to be disqualified
for obtaining such licence for such
further time after the expiration of the
licence as the court thinks fit;
*
*
Provided that-
*
*
*
*
(i) in the case of a second or subsequent
conviction for an offence under subsection (1) of section one of this Act,
and in the case of a third or subsequent conviction for exceeding a speed
limit fixed by regulations under this
Act or by or under any local enactment, the court shall (without prejudice to its power to order a longer
period of suspension) suspend for
period of not less than three months
the licence under this Part of this Act,
if any, held by the person convicted.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
a
In cases where a conviction has been upheld on appeal, this sub-section shall apply
as if for references to the conviction and
the convicting court there were substituted
references to the decision given on the
appeal and to the court by whom that
decision was given.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page 5,
line 29, to leave out the words part of
this" ("under this part of this Act').
I thought that this would be a convenient
place at which to ask the right hon. Gentleman to tell us, a little more clearly perhaps
that appears in the Bill, what is the intention of the Government in regard to the
issue of licences for the double purpose of
driving a motor car and driving a public
service vehicle. In the second part of the
Bill, which deals with public service
455 Motor Vehicles Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Mr. Leslie]
vehicles, no direct power is taken to grant
a licence to a man to drive a public service
vehicle. It is to be implied, of course.
There is direct power to licence the vehicle,
but not to licence the man to drive. There
is a Clause-the second sub-Section, I think,
of Clause 10-which seems to point pretty
plainly to the fact that the intention is that
there should be one licence to drive an ordinary motor car, and another licence to drive
a public service vehicle.
As I understand it, a man who wants to
drive a motor char-a-bane must first have
from the County Council a licence to drive
a motor car, and must then apply to the
Ministry of Home Affairs for a licence to
act as the driver of a char-a-banc, and the
Ministry will not issue a licence to drive a
char-a-banc except to a man who has got a
licence to drive a motor car. I think that
is so, subject to other conditions which do
not arise here. If that is so, it seems to me
that everything falls into its place, but, if
it is not so, then a good deal of this Clause
would require alteration, in order to give
it general application. I believe the Bill
has been so constructed that it has already
general application. It occurred to me
to raise this matter by way of an Amendment.
which certainly is not intended to be
accepted as it stands. Perhaps the right
hon. Gentleman will kindly explain to us
what is the intention of the Government.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The hon. and
learned Senator has really stated the facts
correctly. What will happen in regard to
public service vehicles is this, the individual
will require to have two licences-a licence
from the County Council as an ordinary
driver, and then a special licence which will
be issued by the Ministry of Home Affairs
through the police, provided that the applicant satisfies certain tests. For instance,
he must be of a certain age, and he must
pass a medical examination. We do not
propose to interfere at all with the issue of
licences as heretofore, in regard to public
service vehicles.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 6,
line 21, to leave out the word "three" ("not
less than three months"), and to insert instead thereof the word "one."
We are now dealing with the period of
suspension of licences. As I have said beforé, I attach very much more importance
to the power of suspending a licence than I
do to the actual amount of the fine. We are
now dealing with the case of a man who
has been twice convicted of the offence of
dangerous driving, or who has been three
times convicted of exceeding the speed limit,
and it is proposed to provide that
the court shall (without prejudice to its
power to order a longer period of suspension) suspend for a period of not less
than three months the licence under this
Part of this Act.
The Court must suspend the licence for not
less than three months. The man concerned,
of course, can appeal against that conviction, but he cannot appeal against the sentence. If the conviction is upheld the
suspension must run for three months.
The man is put in exactly the same position
as the drunken man in the next paragraph.
The drunken man is to be suspended for
twelve months, but at the end of every
three months he may go to the court at
which he was convicted and ask for a remission of the balance of his sentence. Of
course he may or he may not get it. The
point is, if this man is to have three months'
suspension, he is in exactly the same boat
as the drunken man. I think that is too
severe.
I rather hope that on this occasion I may
find the right hon. Gentleman in a more
merciful mood, and prepared to limit the
compulsory period of suspension to one
month. That will not in the least affect
the power of the magistrates to impose a
suspension of twelve months if the case is
a serious one. I think it is too hard to
require a minimum suspension of three
months. It may prove fatal to the man's
employment. I think this is a case in which
the magistrates might be given more discretion. It is not fair to make the minimum period of suspension the same in this
case as in the case of the drunken man,
a
Mr. LONG: This matter was very carefully considered by the Roads Advisory
Committee, and it came to the conclusion
that, as this was a question of
third
offence, the penalty of suspension for three
months was not too severe, and that it was
absolutely necessary in the interests of
public safety that punishment of a deterrent
character should be inflicted. I hope the
Home Secretary will not accept the Amendment.
Sir DAWSON BATES: This is a Clause
that the Government went into very carefully, and they came to the conclusion that
it was only a reasonable proposal, if they
were to do anything to put an end to the
evil of reckless driving and consequent loss
of life. I do not think that in the circumstances three months' suspension is by any
means unreasonable. Of course, as my hon.
Friend (Mr. Leslie) is aware, the individual
457 Motor Vehicles Bill-
17 NOVEMBER 1926

Committee.

D

concerned has an appeal to the county court
judge if he considers that he has been
harshly dealt with by the lower court.
Under the circumstances the Government
cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 8,
line 16, to leave out the words "and the
convicting court" ("to the conviction and
the convicting court").
Here, again, I am in some doubt as to
the intention of the words I am suggesting
to omit. The point is, we are dealing with
a man who comes to the court of appeal,
that is to say, the county court judge, to
obtain the removal or the reduction of a
sentence. It is provided, very properly, I
think, that notice must be given to the
police concerned in the matter. That is all
right and perfectly simple if you are appealing to the same court of petty sessions where
the conviction took place, because you have
the same district-inspector. But if you give
notice to the district-inspector in attendance
in the town where the county court
judge is situated you are not, I think,
giving notice to the proper officer, because
-it is all the same in Belfast, I have
no doubt-but in the country things are
different.
The petty sessions district has
one district-inspector and the appeal court is
in another police district with another inspector. It seems to me that the inspector
who ought to be notified is the inspector who
has knowledge of the circumstances of the
case, that is, the man who presumably appeared in the original prosecution. If the
matter arose in Ballymoney and the man
came back again to Ballymoney it would be
all right, but if his appeal is heard in Ballymena that is a different police district, and
the district-inspector would probably not be
in the appeal court at all. The person to be
advised is surely the district-inspector of
Ballymoney. It occurred to me if that were
so we might meet the case by leaving out the
words "and the convicting court". If the
right hon. Gentleman wishes I will allow
the matter to stand over until report.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I will look into the
point raised by the hon. and learned Senator.
I do not think there is anything in it of
substance, but I will go into it and see if I
can do anything to meet the point, if there
is anything in it.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Duty to stop in case of accident) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Regulations as to speed and
maximum weight of motor cars, and penalties for speed offences, &c)--
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(2) If any person drives a motor car
at a speed exceeding the speed limit fixed
by regulations under this section or fixed
by or under any local enactment he shall
be liable on summary conviction to a fine
of one pound or any greater sum not exceeding ten pounds, or in the case of a
second conviction to a fine of two pounds
or any greater sum not exceeding twenty
pounds, or in the case of a third or subsequent conviction to a fine of five pounds
or any greater sum not exceeding fifty
pounds, or in the discretion of the court
to imprisonment for a period not exceeding
three months, or to both such fine and
imprisonment.
(3) For the purposes of this section the
weight unladen of any vehicle shall be
determined in accordance with the provisions of sub-section (6) of section seven
of the Roads Act, 1920.
(4) Sub-section (4) of section twelve of the
Roads Act, 1920, shall have effect as if the
reference therein to regulations included
reference to orders made under subsection (4) of section seven of the said Act.
a
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Page 9, line 19, leave out from "fine" to
"not" in line 20.
Page 9, line 21, leave out from "fine" to
"not" in line 22.
Page 9, line 24, leave out from "fine" to
"not."
Amendments not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move page 9, line 35,
at end to add the following new sub-section:
(5) A person shall not be convicted
under this section for exceeding any limit
of speed merely on the opinion of one witness as to the rate of speed.
In the Act of 1903 there was a very valuable provision that a person should not be
459 Motor Vehicles Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Mr. Leslie]
convicted for exceeding any limit of speed
merely on the opinion of one witness. The
1903 Act, as you may remember, introduced
the speed limit of 20 miles per hour. That
is now gone. That may be the reason that
this protection given in the Act of 1903 is
gone also. I think it is still wanted, because
it will still be an offence to exceed the limit
of speed fixed under this section in any local
Act. The offence can arise in two ways. It
may be committed by an ordinary motor car,
I think, in Bangor or other place not up to
date, where they still have a speed limit, or
it may be committed by a public service
vehicle or motor lorty that is, owing to its
weight, limited in pace by regulations made
by the right hon. Gentleman.
In the circumstances I think it is essential
for the protection of the driver that he
should not be convicted under any limit of
speed merely on the opinion of one witness.
I need not point out, I think, that if a policeman is using a stop-watch, that is something
more than the opinion of one witness. That
question does not arise. I think we ought
Mr. R. Dorman.
Mr. J. G. Leslie.
Mr. W. Barclay.
Viscount Charlemont.
Sir George S. Clark, Bart.
Colonel The Right Hon. R. G.
Sharman-Crawford.
Amendment negatived.
to restore this sub-section which was in the
Act of 1903. I beg to move.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I really do not
think this Amendment is at all necessary. I
think it would be far more satisfactory not
to fetter the court by saying what opinion
they are to go by. There is a prevalent feeling abroad that a policeman's word is worth
more than that of an ordinary respectable
citizen. That is not so. I do think
that the protection individual motorists
have in the appeal to a higher court-to a
county court judge sitting alone makes this
entirely unnecessary. I can imagine in
certain circumstances the evidence might be
most useful and absolutely invaluable.
I
really do not see that the Government can
accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry I am unable
to withdraw this Amendment. My point has
not been met.
On Question, "That those words be there
inserted."

CLAUSE 11 (Grant of licence for public
service vehicles and penalties for use of unlicensed vehicles)-
*
*
*
*
*
(c) no such licence shall be issued unless
the applicant shows to the reasonable satisfaction of the Ministry of Home Affairs
that he will be in a position to satisfy all
lawful claims which may be made against
him in respect of damage to persons or
to property caused by the use upon public
highways of any public service vehicle
for which the licence is sought.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I beg
to move, page 11, line 34, to line 3 on page 12,
to leave out paragraph (c) and insert:
Mr. W. J. McDowell.
Mr. J. Porter-Portor.
(c) No such licence shall be issued unless
the applicant takes out an insurance policy
covering third party risks.
First of all, may I bear testimony to our
pleasure in seeing the Minister of Home
Affairs here to-day. I am one of those who
specially know what time he and also his
officials have devoted to this Bill. It has
been very carefully thought out. There is
only one point that my Amendment raises
and which I wish to draw attention to.
Both in the Lower House and in this Committee to-day we have heard a great deal
about the opinion of the Advisory Committee
being consulted. When this Clause came up
before the Advisory Committee I think it
was the Clause that had the most universal
support of all classes on the Advisory Committee who were present. It was their
opinion that this Clause should be introduced for the protection of the public.
I think that really the Amendment is a
Clause that would help the Minister in his
duty of dealing with whether a person was
qualified or not. Of course this is a case
where we do not aim at big companies and
people who are well known. This is for the
461 Motor Vehicles Bill-

17 NOVEMBER 1926
Committee.


a
I
11 1
дт
E
TA
protection of the public against the smaller
man, and also for the protection of the
smaller man who would do it himself.
What I mean by the smaller man is the
man who has a small bus or taxi which is
plying for public hire, and which he takes
out for his own profit. Some of these small
men are rather, as you must know, men of
straw. The machines probably are not good,
one way or another, and are more likely to
have accidents than the better kind of
machine. In that case it would mean that
the man who is in charge of one of these
inferior machines, with no capital, would
be able probably to pay the premium for
the third party risk, but if he had an accident he would not be able to pay damage if
he killed a person or injured a person
badly. He could not do it at all. The effect
a
a
- would probably be that he would become
bankrupt. He might then apply again to
get
licence to start again with his
machine, and begin killing people again.
I think this would be
most useful
Clause for everybody. I know it is the
universal wish of the Advisory Committee
that this should be put in. I have not
spoken to anyone of the general public,
whether drivers of machines or passengers
carried, who is not in favour of this Clause
going in. We know that this is only a Bill
in its first stage as it were, because if the
traffic goes on increasing as it has done,
probably far more stringent regulations will
have to be brought in in a short time. I
understand a Bill is about to be brought in
in the Imperial Houses of Parliament which
contains this Clause, and not only so, but
they are considering the question whether it
should not apply to owners of motor cars
as well. At present I think it is very important that this Amendment should be
carried for the protection of the public, and
really with no wish to show hostility to the
Government-they know how much I am
interested in the Bill-I do hope at any rate
that the Minister will be able to give some
that will satisfy me and my
friends. We only want to make the Bill
perfect, and I think this will make it a
better Bill. I beg to move.
assurance
Sir DAWSON BATES: It may interest the
■ Committee to know the circumstances under
which we introduced the principle contained
in paragraph (c), which my hon. and gallant
Friend proposes should be eliminated. The
proposal is that no such licence shall be
issued unless the applicant shows to the
reasonable satisfaction of the Ministry of
Home Affairs that he will be in a position
to satisfy all lawful claims which may be
made against him in respect of damage to
persons or to property, and so on. We introduced that principle after seeing the
practical effect of the working of a similar
provision in London, in Scotland Yard.
There they have a rule that when an individual comes up for a licence for a particular bus, that individual has to show the
police authorities there that he is in a
position-these are the words "that he is
in a financial position to meet a third party
claim," and they ask the individual does he
own the bus, and find out all about him, and
unless they have very good grounds for supposing that he is in a financial position to
meet the claim, they say you must take
out a third party insurance.”
They do not fetter themselves by making
it compulsory. For example, 1 can imagine
if this Clause is inserted here it would be
perfectly absurd to ask the Belfast Corporation to take out an insurance policy. In the
same way it would be entirely unnecessary
in London for the London General Omnibus Company who carry out their own insurance to take out a policy.
I can assure my hon. Friend that in administering this Act we will have to be very
certain of the financial stability of the individual who applies for a bus licence. Otherwise we will say, as they do in London,
""Take out a Third Party insurance." If you
take away our discretion I think you will
be rather handicapping us instead of enabling us to carry out the very thing we
want to do. I can give the Committee an
undertaking that where an individual is not
in a position to satisfy us that he is in a
financial position to meet a claim we will
insist on his taking out a Third Party insurance.
Mr. LONG: I am sorry I cannot agree with
the hon. Member who moved this Amendment. I think the sub-section the hon.
Member seeks to eliminate is of great value,
and I do not think anything can be achieved
by accepting the Amendment. However, it
would have this effect: it would shut out the
wealthy man who can be his own insurer.
The view is largely held that a man who is
in a solid financial position should be allowed
to be his own insurer if he finds it is an
economic proposition. While the Amendment would simplify matters for the Department in carrying out the Act I really think
the Clause as it stands is preferable to the
Amendment.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Would it apply retrospectively to those who are at present
driving a bus?
Sir DAWSON BATES: When the Act
comes into operation, individuals will have
to get a licence which will only be issued on
the conditions I have mentioned.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I am
not quite satisfied with the explanation.
With regard to buses owned by public bodies
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sharman Crawford]
and large companies an arrangement could
be made to exclude them from Third Party
insurance. My Amendment was only meant
to deal with the smaller men. This is really
only the first stage of the legislation that
will be required to deal with the problem
of motor traffic, and if the right hon. Gentleman finds that this Clause in the Bill does
not work satisfactorily perhaps at a later
stage, which may not be very far off, he will
make provision for Third Party insurance.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 12 (Licensing of drivers and conductors).
(1) No public service vehicle shall stand
or ply for hire or carry passengers for hire
unless the driver holds a licence granted
by the Ministry of Home Affairs, or (where
the vehicle is a vehicle of a class which is
required by regulations to carry a conductor) the driver and conductor, respectively, hold such licences.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(3) A licence to the driver or conductor
of a public service vehicle may be granted
upon payment of such fee (not exceeding five
shillings), and on such conditions, be in
such form, be subject to revocation or
suspension in such events, and generally
be dealt with, in such manner as may be
prescribed :
Provided that-
(a) a driver's or conductor's licence
shall, if not revoked or suspended, be
in force for one year;
(b) a driver's licence shall not be issued
to any person unless he is twenty-one
or more years of age and satisfies the
Ministry of Home Affairs that he is in
all respects fit to discharge the duties,
and is competent to drive a public
service vehicle of the type, for which
he requires the driver's licence;
(c) a conductor's licence shall not be
issued to any person unless he is
eighteen or more years of age and
satisfies the Ministry of Home Affairs
that he is capable of discharging the
duties required of him as such conductor.

to make matters a trifle more clear than
they are already. Following on the discussion on the first Amendment on this page,
"in
I suggest we should add the words,
addition to a licence to drive a motor car."
I think that clarifies the position.
I beg
to move.

are prepared to accept the Amendment.

In the case of the owner-

of two shillings and sixpence for each
person carried in excess of the prescribed number;

This is a slightly more difficult matter.
The words on the Paper almost express
themselves. The position of the whole matter seems to me to be that a man in the
country who has
Ford
which is
licensed to carry six persons, is sometimes
prosecuted under the provisions of the Char-
A difficulty does arise there.
a-banc Act.
I want to make sure that if no offence in
the matter referred to in paragraph (c) of
Clause 10 is committed, the man shall not
be prosecuted. Paragraph (c) of Clause 19
is a very useful paragraph for regulating
the number of passengers and the space
allotted which is usually, I believe, 16 inches
per head, or I should say, per tail. If you
put four people in the ordinary Ford, car
they will get their 16 inches, but if the car
is hired, as it is often hired in the country,
to take six people to the market, they do not
get their 16 inches each and an offence might
be held to have been committed. It is not
fair that a man should be fined for allowing
six people to go in the car, and I think that
if he keeps by his Excise licence, and is
hired by a particular party for a particular
job he should not be prosecuted.
Mr. LONG: I trust the Minister of Home
Affairs will not accept this Amendment.
The principle underlying the Clause is the
safety of the passengers and in my opinion
this is a wrecking Amendment and would
destroy the whole force of this Section of
the Bill. I might explain that in the case
of a char-a-bane, seating from 26 to 32 passengers, the licence duty would be £64. It
is only constructed to carry 26 persons,
but supposing 27 persons were found in
it, under the hon. Senator's Amendment no
offence would have been committed. The
owner of that vehicle, which was constructed
to carry only 26 passengers, could carry
32 passengers without committing an
offence. The only way that could be got
over would be to have a new law and a new
Finance Act to allow £2 per passenger to be
charged. That would require a new Act
and considerable delay would occur, and, in
my view, to legalise the carrying of any
passengers in
of the number for
which the vehicle is constructed, would be
very dangerous, and I trust the Committee
will not agree to it.
excess
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I quite
agree with the remarks of Senator Long. It
is very evident that Senator Leslie has not
the same knowledge as Senator Long and I
have of the dodging that has been going on
since the Bill dealing with chars-a-banes
was passed. We have had most flagrant
cases of evasion, and we have done our best
to stop them. The effect of the Amendment
would be to enable a car that is constructed
for seven passengers to carry fourteen, and
I think that would be highly dangerous and
most injudicious.
Sir DAWSON BATES: For the reasons
already expressed by Senator Long and
Senator Sharman Crawford the Government
cannot accept this Amendment. It would
cut across the whole of the financial arrangements regarding the licensing of these charsa-bancs.
I will give an illustration.
carry
If a
seven
machine was licensed to
passengers it would only pay a duty of £28
instead of a duty of £40. I think that is

is a far greater danger than that in the
Amendment. According to the latter part
of the Amendment, because
group of
individuals on an excursion hire a vehicle

a
Mr. LESLIE: Then may I ask if I am to
understand that Excise duty is exacted for
more than the number of passengers which
can properly be carried on a vehicle?
Sir DAWSON BATES: No.
Mr. LESLIE: My Amendment is that an
offence is not committed if the number of
persons carried is not in excess of the
number in respect of which Excise duty has
been paid. If a vehicle is licenced to carry
six persons, surely it must be allowed to
carry six passengers. I think my critics
have rather misunderstood me.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think that what
my hon. Friend has overlooked is the fact
that when this Bill becomes law the
Ministry will not have regard to the Excise
side of the question, but only as to whether
or not the vehicle is designed and suitable
for carrying a certain number of passengers.
When the machine comes up for examination and licence, the Ministry will not have

regard to the sixteen inches of space or anything of that sort, which seems to be in the

If the hon. Member says his Amendment
does not affect the financial side of the
question why does he move it?
Mr. LESLIE: I think from what the

(2) The Minister of Home Affairs shall,
subject to the approval of the Ministry of
Finance, have power if he considers that
it is necessary so to do, for the purposes
of this section-
(a) to alter the number and distribution
of the officers and constables of the
Royal Ulster Constabulary as determined by sub-section (2) of section one
of the Constabulary Act (Northern
Ireland), 1922, but so that the general
strength of the Force shall not exceed
the maximum number fixed by the
First Schedule to the said Constabulary
Act; and
(b) to extend, in the case of persons having
special qualifications for the performance of duties to which this section
relates, the limit of age fixed by paragraph (d) of sub-section (1) of section
seven of the said Constabulary Act.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I beg to
move in page 16, lines 1 to 5, to leave out
paragraph (b).
This Clause gives the Ministry power to
extend the age limit for the Constabulary
for the purpose of carrying out the operations of this Act. I think it would be a
wrong step to take to increase the age limit
at a time such as the present. Indeed, the
feeling of the electors all through the
country is in the contrary direction, and a
great deal of argument has been put up in
favour of men leaving their positions a
little earlier in life to make room for others
who are more in need of employment,
instead of extending the age limit.
a
few
I hardly think it would be satisfactory
from a Constabulary point of view, even to
be inclined to make exceptions in the case
of some constables or officers as against
others. It would be liable to cause ill-feeling
and dissatisfaction if there were
favoured people who obtained special positions. At the present time there is no need
whatever for extending the age limit. There
are such considerable numbers in all trades
and professions unemployed that I think
there would be no dificulty whatever in getting the most suitable men possible for the
positions which the Ministry may require
to fill. It is an understood thing that there
is no man indispensable, and wherever there
is a good man there is always a better to be
had. In this case I think it would be no
harm, indeed I think it would be advisable,
that this Clause should Le deleted, so that
there would be no possible chance of extending the age limit. We may be told that it
is only in exceptional or in very rare cases
that this may be done, but at the same time
it is dangerous to place a power like this in
the hands of the Ministry, and it would
probably turn out very unsatisfactorily. I
move that this Clause be deleted.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I desire to support
the Amendment which has been proposed by
the hon. Senator opposite. This is the
second occasion on which the Government
have tried to increase the Constabulary.
In 1923 I proposed an Amendment trying to
keep the age within the limits of the Constabulary Act, that is thirty-five years, and
469 Motor Vehicles Bili-

17 NOVEMBER 1926
Committee

now again when the Motor Bill comes along
they vote in the other House a sum of I
think the right hon. Gentleman (Sir Dawson
Bates) told us-£12,000. I would like to
know if these gentlemen who are to be appointed are Ulstermen, and what are their
qualifications, that we cannot supply men
at the age of thirty-five years in our own
country. With the dearth of employment
that there is at present, I certainly will
support the Amendment.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think my hon.
Friend (Mr. Joseph Cunningham) is under
a misunderstanding in regard to the object
of this Clause. As he is no doubt aware we
are precluded from taking any man into the
police force who is over twenty-seven years
of age, and we propose to ask for power to
increase that for this particular purpose,
but we do not propose to increase the retiring age of any individual.
With regard to the point raised by my
hon. Friend (Mr. Hill Dickson), he suggests
that we have actually persons nominated
aird ready for these appointments. I would
like to give a most emphatic denial to that.
I do not know yet how many men in the
Royal Ulster Constabulary will be fit to
carry out this work, or whether or not it
will be necessary to bring in men with
technical skill and experience in motors.
Of course it will only be a temporary
Measure, because I hope that in the course
of time men will be recruited generally into
the force and ultimately get these positions.
I would remind my hon. Friends that the
whole success of the administration of this
Act depends upon getting suitable individuals.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: You will
have no trouble.
Sir DAWSON BATES: My hon. Friend
says I will have no trouble. He may know
the details and the professional experience
in the motor world that the men of the
Royal Ulster Constabulary have. I do not.
If there are sufficient men in the Royal
Ulster Constabulary with sufficient experience no men will be brought in at all, but
do not forget this: that men's livelihoods,
and poor men's livelihoods, may depend
upon the reports of these inspectors. Several
machines, I am certain, will be ordered off
the road as a result of these reports, and I
want to be in a position, if the matter is
raised in this House, or in the lower House,
and I am challenged as to why the police
have refused a licence to the owner of a
hackney vehicle, to be able to say what the
qualifications of the inspector concerned
The only object of this Clause is to
get the best men we can. I hope we will
be able to get them all in Ulster. I do not
why we should not.
My hon. Friend
are.
see
may put out of his mind the idea that
there is any ulterior motive in this, or any
object except to get the very best men we
possibly can, and I can assure him that if
all these men can be obtained from the
ranks of the Royal Ulster Constabulary we
shall be so much the more pleased.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: It is a question of
age. It is a question of appointing men of
that age to a pensionable job.
Sir DAWSON BATES: May I point out
that it does not affect the pension rights
one iota, because a man going into the police
force will have to retire at the same age as
the others who have been in the force
earlier will have to retire, and, of course,
his pension will be so much less.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: If I am
to understand that an outsider will have an
opportunity, and that the positions are not
to be strictly confined to a few, I will be
very well satisfied. It must be taken into
consideration that those at present in the
Royal Ulster Constabulary have enjoyed very
comfortable steady employment for a good
number of years, while there are many
people outside who are quite as competent,
and who have been unemployed. I am
speaking now as an engineer with some
knowledge of the motor trade and motor
business, and I do not think there would
be any difficulty in getting thoroughly
capable men to fill these positions, but
after the explanation given by the right
hon. Gentleman I have no hesitation in
withdrawing.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

vehicles of any particular class, that is

Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I beg to

Before I move the Amendment standing
in my name I would like just to express the
pleasure it is to me and to the Committee,
I am sure, to have the Minister of Home
Affairs (Sir Dawson Bates) here to-day to
answer these questions and to make such
clear
statements. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) In moving this Amendment the principle that I want to secure is that when a
by-law is once passed by the Ministry of
Home Affairs it may come up for review.
As the Clause stands now I would be afraid
that, once it was passed, it never could be
reviewed by the Home Office, and if we
would only say that the Belfast Corporation,
say, or the county council by whom that
by-law was applied for could bring it up
and have it altered, it would meet the case
I think it would be an advantage that it
should be renewed by the Home Office. If
it is renewed by the Home Office then
Parliament has something to say in the
matter, and we could see these by-laws
and ask questions on them. Without saying
more or keeping the Committee at this late
hour, I just beg to move.
Sir DAWSON BATES: With regard to the
right hon. Senator's Amendment, I entirely
agree with the principle which he has laid
down. He drew my attention to the matter
some time ago and I told him then, as I
tell him now, that the Ministry's intention
is not to sanction any by-law for a longer
period than one year, so that every case will
come up. I might explain to the Committee
that when the Corporation, or other local
authorities, apply for by-laws, a public inquiry will be held, after due notice of at
least a month, to enable every interested
party, whether bus owners, or the general
public, or anybody else, to come before the
inspector and express their views, and then,
after that, it comes before the Ministry for
confirmation or not, as the case may be. I
would suggest to my right hon. Friend that
it would be better not to move his Amendment, but to leave the matter as it is, because circumstances may arise where the
Ministry may wish to make the confirmation for even a shorter period than one year.
After all, we are in an entirely experimental
stage, especially in regard to these by-laws,
and we want to keep ourselves as unfettered
as possible, so that these by-laws can be
brought up and revised at least once a year.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I would
be quite in agreement with the right hon.
Senator who moved this Amendment, though
I would suggest that now that we have got a
proper assurance from the Minister of Home
Affairs he should not press it. I think it
would meet his case if words were inserted
to make the confirmation for a period of not
longer than one year.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I will be very glad
to look into this point and see if it can be
legally fixed in that way.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: On that
assurance I ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 18,
page 18, line 12, to leave out from the beginning of the line to "and" in line 13.
The words which I suggest should be left
out are, on the face of them, rather extraordinary words to appear in this Bill. It
would seem that the local authority, which
in this case is probably likely to be the
Belfast Corporation, is to have power to fix
the maximum rates or fares for various
vehicles on the route of any tramway belonging to "such council or commissioners,"
and that seems to apply whether or not the
tramway is within the boundary of their
district. The words cannot possibly have
relation to tramcars, because they are excepted from the Bill altogether. I confess I
do not know what the words mean, though
there may be some simple explanation, but
on the face of it, it looks as if there was an
attempt, in the interests of some tramway
system, to impose rates of fares and so on,
on other vehicles, in order to bring them out
of competition with the tramways. I hope
this is not the case, but I must confess that
on the face of it, it looks as if it is.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I would like to
remove any idea in the minds of hon.
Senators that this Bill is for the purpose
of bolstering up the Belfast trams or any
other means of locomotion. That is not the
case. It is for the purpose of securing the
safety of the public. In reference to this
Motor Vehicles Bill-
17 NOVEMBER 1926
Committee.
particular Clause, the Government do not
propose to take control of the traffic in any
of the local authorities' areas, but, as 1
pointed out, when these by-laws are made
anyone who is interested will have the right
to appear, put forward his objections, and
have the whole thing thoroughly thrashed
out. Then I have already given an undertaking that these by-laws will not be in
force for a period of more than one year. 1
cannot imagine that any injustice will be
done to anyone.
Mr. LESLIE: If the tramway is within
the bounds of the district it is apparently
already dealt with by this proposal, but the
route of the tramway may be outside the
district. I cannot understand the necessity
for the words, and I am afraid—
Sir DAWSON BATES: I do not think it
makes any difference.
Mr. LESLIE: Well, what are the words
for?
Sir DAWSON BATES: 1 will look into the
matter again. I do not want to appear as
if I brush aside the arguments put forward
by my hon. and learned Friend. Perhaps
in the circumstances he will withdraw, and
I will consider the question before the Report
stage.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 19 (Regulations by Ministry of
Home Affairs for purposes of Part II of
Act) and 20 (Payments into and out of
Road Fund [Northern Ireland]) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 21 (Penalties and legal proceedings)-
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(4) Any police officer or constable who
appears on any information, complaint or
application before a court of summary
jurisdiction under this Act or under any
Act mentioned in sub-section (3) of this
section, shall have a right of appeal against
any order made by such court in the
matter of the information, complaint or
application, and the provisions of section
twenty-four of the Petty Sessions (Ireland)
Act, 1851, relating to appeals (except provisions as to recognizances) shall apply
accordingly.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 21,
page 21, lines 17 to 26, to leave out sub-
Section (4).
The sub-Clause I desire to leave out is a
most extraordinary Clause. It apparently
proposes-I do not say that it effects the
purpose that any police officer or constable
who appears on any information, complaint,
or application before a court of summary
jurisdiction, if he loses his case, is to have
the right of appeal. Such a proposal was
never heard of. There are only three exceptions that I know of. Under the ordinary
law of England, and the English-speaking
world I may say, if a man has once been
tried before a court of competent jurisdiction
he is not to be vexed again for the same
cause. Under this Clause a man may be
charged with some offence, such as overcrowding a char-a-banc, or whatever it may
be, and if the magistrates dismiss the case
this extraordinary Clause proposes to give
the police officer or constable the right of
appeal before the county court judge against
the dismiss-a most extraordinary provision.
I presume that is the meaning of it. I
presume that any order made by such
court" is meant to include a dismissal.
a
a
There are only three exceptions I know
of where appeals are allowed in criminal
cases as opposed to civil cases.
In every
civil case that comes before the magistrates
an appeal has always been allowed to either
party, but not in criminal cases where
there has never been any right of appeal
against
dismiss. We have instances
where an appeal is given under the
Fisheries Act, where the question is
quasi-civil matter, and under the Cattle
Diseases Act of 1894, where again the
question is of general application, and we
have, I am sorry to say, an instance in
Northern Ireland in the Intoxicating
Liquor Act, 1923, where something of this
sort was put in. I objected at the time to
the Clause in this House. The noble Viscount, the Parliamentary Secretary (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard) told us-I
remember distinctly--that he hoped I was
not going to move an Amendment, as great
care would be taken to see that the Clause
was not used for any oppressive purpose.
As any questions likely to be dealt with
under that Act were to be dealt with by
two resident magistrates, I thought that perhaps an appeal from two resident magistrates on a technical matter would not do
much harm, and I did not move an
Amendment. I believe I am correct in
saying there was only one appeal under
that Section which happened the other day,
when the decision was reversed.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Two.
Mr. LESLIE: That is not a very big bag.
I must press, in accordance with the whole
tenor of the common Law of England, that
this particular sub-Clause be taken out.
Mr. LONG: As the right hon. Senator,
Colonel Sharman Crawford, will recollect,
the Roads Advisory Committee were very
strongly in favour of this provision, and it
seems to me personally it is only fair and
reasonable that a police officer should get the
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
same consideration and be allowed the same
rights and privileges in the matter of an
appeal as any ordinary individual. It is
hardly fair that he should be penalised because he is a police officer. We are all fully
agreed that we can trust the constabulary
not to bring a vexatious prosecution or to
appeal in frivolous cases. It is never done,
and I appeal to the hon. Senator to withdraw his Amendment; at least, I hope it will
not be accepted.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I hope my hon.
Friend will not see his way to press this
Amendment. I think it is a most necessary
and correct Clause to be put in any Bill. I
quoted some figures earlier this afternoon
to show how hopeless the administration is
at the present time. After all, we are in
earnest over this Bill or we are not. I can
assure him that the power of appeal will
only be used by the police in the case of a
gross miscarriage of justice. The fact that
in connection with the Intoxicating Liquor
Act only two or three cases of appeal have
been taken goes to show that the police do
not desire to do anything in a vindictive
spirit or to harass the public. It seems to
me that without this Clause the Bill may
very well be scrapped.
Mr. LESLIE: I am very sorry I cannot
agree. If a case is brought forward and
the magistrates dismiss the case, and the
police propose to take it to a further court.
they put the man charged to any amount of
inconvenience and expense. He may be out
of the country or in the country. The police
have the extraordinary advantage before
the re-hearing-because an appeal to the
county court judge is a re-hearing-of hav-
Mr. J. Cunningham.
Mr. W. Barclay.
Viscount Charlemont.
Sir G. S. Clark.
ing the man's defence before them. They
put on somebody the responsibility of carrying out the provisions of Section 20 of the
Petty Sessions Act. Those are the provisions
which, as I am sure some of my hearers will
remember, require that if either party demand it, the magistrates, or one of them,
shall take a note of the evidence. It is
obvious that if this Clause were applied
every person charged with an offence under
this Act will require the magistrates to
take a note of the evidence in order, if the
case is dismissed and the police appeal, that
they may be able to question witnesses as
to what they said in the court below.
There is no appeal, speaking generally,
from a verdict of acquittal in a criminal
charge. It would be a most retrograde step
if we were to adopt this in Northern Ireland
If the right hon. Gentleman is convinced
that the magistrates' courts are inefficient
there is a proper course to take-change
them. But as long as you have the courts
trust them, or try to trust them. Remember
that they have heard the evidence in a
particular case which you have not, and
do not, even on the advice of the Roads
Advisory Committee, which is not Parliament, trample on the common law of England. It was laid down by Blackstone, a
greater man than we have to-day, more than
a century ago, that where a man has once
been tried by a court of competent jurisdiction he is not to be vexed again for the
same cause. I cannot withdraw this
Amendment.
On Question, "That this sub-Section be
left out."
The Committee divided: Contents, 3; Not
Contents, 7.
CONTENTS.
Mr. R. Dorman.
NOT CONTENTS.
Colonel The Right Hon. R. G.
Sharman-Crawford.
Mr. J. A. Long.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 21,
page 21, line 26, at end (end of Clause) to
add the following new sub-section:
(5) Where a period of not less than
three years has elapsed between two consecutive convictions of the same person,
the later conviction of that person shall
not for the purpose of this Act be deemed
to be a second or subsequent conviction.
This new sub-Clause is really to carry out
what I think is the intention of the Roads
Act, 1920, as regards the issue of licences
free from endorsements. It is provided in
the Roads Act, and the provision is repeated
| Mr. J. G. Leslie.
Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.
Mr. W. J. McDowell.
in the Bill before us, that where a licence
is clean for three years a man may have a
new licence free from any endorsements of
an anterior date.
What I am proposing
here is that
as we are introducing
minimum penalties for second offences we
should provide that if a man has not been
convicted for a period of three years a conviction subsequent to those three years
should not be regarded as a second offence.
That is, if a first offence is committed in
1926, and the second is not committed
till 1930, the offence of 1930 shall not
for the purposes of this Act be
deemed to be
second conviction. I
a
477 Motor Vehicles Bill-

17 NOVEMBER 1926
Committee.

think that is a reasonable thing. As the
thing stands at present a man who was convicted ten years ago and got a clean sheet
for ten years might have that old conviction
still stand against him. I do not think that
is right. I think we would get over that by
inserting the sub-Section.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I do not know
whether the hon. Senator has overlooked the
effect of Clause 4 (3). That is as far as we
can go. Hon. Members will see by that
Clause that where there has been no conviction endorsed on the licence for the previous three years, the individual will get a
clean sheet.
Mr. LESLIE: Yes, I recognise that under
the sub-Section they get a licence free from
endorsement, but they do not get a complete
whitewashing.
It is possible to prove a
conviction otherwise than by endorsement.
Sir DAWSON BATES: That raises entirely another point. In this case we are
prepared to issue a clean sheet at the end
of three years, provided they have no new
endorsement. What my hon. Friend wishes
to do is supposing a person has committed
three offences and is convicted, then by
some wiping off he is to get pardon for the
previous offences. If a person goes to jail
for theft, at the end of three years, and he
has not stolen anybody's purse, he is to
get a clean bill of health and be treated as
if he had never been convicted.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid the right hon.
Gentleman is not fairly representing the
purport of the Amendment. The Amendment is only for the purpose of this Act,
and there is no comparison between this and
a person guilty of stealing. I shall really
press the Amendment.
Amendment negatived.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 22 (False statements and forgery
or fraudulent use of licences, etc.).
or
(1) If any person in connection with an
application for a licence under any provision of this Act makes a declaration
which to his knowledge is false or in any
material respect misleading, if any
person, being required by this Act or regulations made thereunder to furnish
particulars in connection with any such
application or otherwise in connection
with any vehicle, furnishes any particulars which to his knowledge are false or
in any material respect misleading, such
person shall be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding fifty pounds
or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months.
(2) If in any proceedings under this
section any question arises as to the number of vehicles used, or as to the character,
weight or horse-power of any vehicle, or
as to the number of persons seated by a
vehicle or as to the purpose for which any
vehicle has been used, the burden of proof
in respect of the matter in question shall
lie on the defendant.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 21,
line 33, leave out from "application" ("application or otherwise") to the end of the line.
This is a Clause dealing with applications
for licences, and it is, as hon. Members who
have looked into the matter will recognise,
based on Section 13 of the Roads Act, 1920.
Sub-Section 2 dealt with licensing matters,
and we seem to have followed that until we
come to the words I suggest leaving out for
the moment-" or otherwise in connection
These words do not
with any vehicle."
seem to me to mean much, but if they are
being deliberately put in they probably
mean something. Although I move to leave
them out, I am prepared to withdraw the
Amendment if the right hon. Gentleman
will tell us what he really wants the words
for. What is the intention?
Mr. LONG: If I correctly understand the
purport of the words, they seem to be
necessary for the purpose of requiring
correct information about any machine in
cases of accident. Fords are very much
alike. They are much of a muchness, and
it is only when the cover is lifted and the
engine number examined that you can detect
the difference between one Ford and another.
Cases I know have occurred in which an
accident was due to defective machinery in
one car, and the owner substituted and
submitted another car for examination. 1
know, as chairman of the licensing authority in County Derry, that instances have
occurred where char-a-banc proprietors have
only four licences, and yet they own six
vehicles. I think it is absolutely necessary
that a provision of this sort should be put
in requiring full and correct particulars
regarding the application. They are especially necessary in cases of accidents.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Having
some knowledge of the subject, I am in agreement with my Friend (Mr. Long). There
has been a great deal of trouble with people
who are driving cars and substituting
vehicles for other ones.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government
cannot accept this Amendment. It is absolutely necessary that full power should be
given to the Government to find out exactly
the weight of the machine, because on that
may depend people's lives, the number of
the machine, and generally all particulars
relating to the vehicle. My hon. Friend (Mr.
Leslie) wants to know why we put in the
words or otherwise in connection with any
"
Motor Vehicles Bill.
Finance Bill.
THE SENATE
[Sir Dawson Bates]
vehicle." We may want this information at
some other time; not at the time the application is made. I have known cases similar
to that referred to by Senator Long. There is
a certain scale, according to the weight of
the machine, and Fords are one example
where there is fraud. Another case is where
a man has 16 or 17 machines, and perhaps
he only takes out licences for nine. That is
a fraud on the revenue, and it is also gambling with people's lives. I hope under the
circumstances that the hon. Member will
withdraw the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I asked for an explanation,
and I seem to have got it. At all events I
must take it. This is only required in connection with applications for licences.
does not apply to anything else for the
purposes of the Act?
It
Sir DAWSON BATES: The information
may not be wanted at the time the application is made.
Mr. LESLIE: I have much pleasure in
asking leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 21,
line 10, leave out from the first "as" ("as
to the number") to the end of the line.
This is an inquiry as to the number of
vehicles used. Is that material to the application for a licence under this Section?
a
Sir DAWSON BATES: It is
most
necessary thing. I recall one case that came
to
my knowledge in connection with
Down County Council, where a man had
about four or five vehicles, and where it

Finance (Stamp Duty) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time. - (Viscount Massereene and

Mr. LONG: Hon. Members know that this
Bill relates to the position of barristers who
have been educated at King's Inns and been
called to the Southern Bar, and are now also
being called to the Northern Bar. I have no
objection whatever to the remission of the
dues which they may be called upon to pay,
because they have been called upon to
pay by the Southern Bar. What I
should like to ascertain from the mover of
the Third Reading is if any reciprocal
arrangement exists in the Southern Bar to
what is proposed in the Northern Bar. It
would be exceedingly undesirable that watertight compartments should be set up between
the two Bars-the Northern area and the
Southern area in legal matters. It is
exceedingly desirable that barristers practising in the North should be permitted to
appear in the courts in the South and vice
versa. I trust the benchers of the Northern
Inn will co-operate with the benchers of the
Southern Inn to secure if possible such
néciprocity.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: What the hon. Senator desires is
I am sure what we probably all desire. The
question of making arrangements for what

whether either of these two Inns has raised

Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.
-(Viscount Charlemont.)
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask the noble
Viscount if he proposes to adjourn the
House until to-morrow? I think we may take
it that the suspension of Standing Orders
will be necessary in order to clear up the
business of the session to-morrow afternoon,
with the exception, of course, of giving the
Royal Assent. I think we have nothing left
but the Report stage and Third Reading of
the Bill, which has occupied the attention
of the House this afternoon. The Amendments that may come before us for consideration to-morrow do not appear to be
vital. It may be possible to dispose of the
remaining business to-morrow, and if that
is so it will obviate the necessity of a sitting on Friday. But if we have to sit after
to-morrow it would be more convenient to
have a sitting on Friday than to come back
in the following week.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: To-morrow
I shall move the suspension of Standing
Orders, and I think that will enable us to
clear up all the outstanding business on that
day.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Six
o'clock,
