Writs of Ejectment. 484
Writs of Ejectment.
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 18th November, 1926.
The House met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
SUSPENSION OF STANDING ORDER.
Moved: "That Standing Order No. XXV
be suspended for this day's Sitting, in respect
of the Motor Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Bill." (Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
COSTS OF WRITS OF EJECTMENT.
The following question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Dorman:
"To ask the Leader of the House whether
it is a fact that the costs in the case of
Writs of Ejectment are excessive, and in
many cases exceed the amount of the rent
due; and whether the Government will
take steps to remedy this state of affairs."
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to draw the attention of the Government to the question I
have placed upon the Paper. The attention
of the Government was drawn to this
particular question during the last Session
of Parliament, and apparently nothing has
been done to amend the unfair conditions to
which the working class occupants of houses
in Belfast are subjected when an order for
ejectment is proceeded with against them.
The particular case which has drawn my
attention to this matter is the case of a
working man who has been unemployed, and
who was proceeded against for arrears of
rent, the sum of rent due being £2 16s., and
the costs in connection with the action being
£3 17s. 3d. On having his attention drawn
to this matter the learned judge referred to
the fact that he had, on several occasions,
mentioned this unnecessary burden of costs
that was being placed upon the shoulders of
the working class when ejectment orders
were obtained against them.
near
The conditions existing in regard to the
housing of the working classes in Belfast
to-day are of such a character that in the
future we may anticipate a large
number of attempts being made to eject them
from their homes because of arrears of rent.
It would be unfair that men who, through
deliberate action
of their own
avoid paying a just rent, but who through
unfavourable circumstances and unemployment have been compelled to run a month or
two in arrear should be subjected, if ejectment orders are obtained against them, to a
no
to
continuation of these abnormal expenses in
the form of costs for such ejections. The
record of Belfast at present is, unfortunately,
very bad in that respect. When one considers that during the interim between the
two censuses the proportion of one-room
dwellers in the borough area has been increased by between 600 per cent. and 700 per
cent., one can see how hard pressed these
poor people are to obtain homes.
In illustration of this I would like to
mention one particular case out of many.
I occupy a house of six apartments, for
which I pay £24 a year rent. A personal
friend of mine is paying no less than £31 45.
for a single apartment almost in the centre
of the city, and he has been looking out for
a house for some years now. These circumstances make it imperative that those persons who are compelled to live in one room
should offer the exceedingly high rent that
the present occupiers of houses are paying
under the Rent and Mortgage Interest
Restrictions Act. These conditions will begreat incentive to the houseowning class to take advantage of the
economic conditions, and will increase
abnormally the amount of applications for
ejectment for the non-payment of rent among
the working class of Belfast.
come
a
Indeed, the Government themselves, I
understand, have been a victim in this particular matter. I have read in the papers
that they got the Royal Ulster Constabulary
barrack on Cregagh Road for which they
were paying £65 a year. I understand that
for some reason or other they failed to make
application before the expiration of the year
for a renewal of the tenancy, and that the
landlord has succeeded in exacting from the
Government, instead of a rental of £65 a
rental of £265 for the barrack, showing
plainly that even a powerful Government
cannot resist the avarice and greed of a landlord if he is disposed to take advantage of
any little failure on the part of a tenant to
comply fully with the terms of the letting or
agreement.
I take it for granted that the chief object
of all Governments should be the establishment of peace and a just relationship between
the citizens, and the establishment of a peaceful
and a just relationship between the citizens
and the various Departments of the State.
I cannot conceive how any man who owes rent
and who is exposed to costs similar to those
mentioned by the learned Recorder can dwell
in a state of peace of mind with regard to
the legal branch of the State. There must
necessarily be rankling in the mind of any
working man subject to an exorbitant fee of
this description a sense of injustice and indignation that the Government should allow
a condition such as this to prevail. I have
very much pleasure in asking the question.
435 Writs of Ejectment.
Writs of Ejectment.
18 NOVEMBER 1926

G

-
L

Mr. MAGEE: It is not always that the hon.
Senator who has just spoken and I agree, but
on
this subject we do agree. The hon.
Senator has spoken of the state of affairs
that exists from one side of the question. I
can speak of it from another. It has been
my misfortune on many occasions, I regret
to say, to have been the means of proceedings for ejectment being taken against
tenants. In very many cases, as the hon.
Senator has just said, this default is not due
to any wilfulness on the part of the tenants.
Very frequently the tenants go to some
charitable organisation, and what is the position when the tenant does that? A decree
has been got against the poor, unfortunate
man whose rent is 5/- or 6/- a week. The
first thing that must be done before the
decree is set aside or waived is that £6 10s.
must be put down. I have already said in
this House that it is a cruel thing it is a
thing that goes to my heart to have to enrich the lawyers and the courts and the
sheriffs by the sum of £6 10s. If a man is
unable to pay 6/- a week how on earth can
he expect to raise the amount of money required to pay the enormous costs?
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. MAGEE: There is a very simple procedure, that is Petty Sessions jurisdiction.
I moved an Amendment to the Rent Restrictions Bill, the object of which was to extend
the Petty Sessions jurisdiction. The answer
that came was, "Oh, this cannot be done,
it is a separate matter; it must be done in a
separate Bill." There is a Bill coming before us to-day in which it is proposed-I
think Senator Joseph Cunningham tried to
amend it yesterday-to alter the Constabulary Act. Surely to goodness no one can
say the Constabulary Act had anything to
do with motors. But still if it suits-I do
not know whom it suits, it does not seem to
suit anybody-why on earth if an extraneous
matter can be brought in in one Measure,
cannot it be brought in in another Measure?
I really feel very strongly that the hon.
Senator, who asked this question, has a case
which no one, on due consideration, can say
is a bad one.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I would
also like to associate myself with the remarks
of Senator Dorman and the Mayor of Derry.
There is no one, I think, who knows the
position better than the working-class who
are living among the working-class people.
It is, undoubtedly, a terrible hardship at the
present time to find that people who are
unable to pay their small weekly rent, when
they are taken before the court instead of
small weekly amounts being imposed on
them, which they are able to pay, have costs
heaped up against them which make it
impossible for them to pay. It seems
to be a ridiculous position. Surely our
Government ought to be able to get out of
the difficulty and try to remove this terrible
hardship that is weighing on the poor people
at the present time. I would make a special
appeal to the Government to consider this
matter very carefully.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would also like to
associate myself with the hon. Senators who
have just spoken. I know, from personal
experience, of a number of poor people,
honest, respectable, good citizens, who, unfortunately, owing to the depression of trade
have been unemployed. There are none of
us here to-day but can have some realisation of what this means to a man who has
got a small family, say three or four, or
perhaps more. Those children are not fit to
work. Neither are they fit to want. It
seems very hard when that man is prosecuted by a landlord for the non-payment of
rent that he should find the costs of the
prosecution are more than the rent due.
This is where the hardship comes in, that
when the case goes against this poor man,
and a decree is granted, he and his wife and
children and their few sticks of furniture
are left upon the street.
Is it any wonder that we have the condition of affairs we have to-day in the city
of Belfast, where, as Senator Dorman has
pointed out, there are so many families all
crowding together to try to make a home
for themselves. Now, I hope that this question will get due consideration from the
Government in the near future, and that
something will be done to lighten the burden
that these poor people are suffering from
at the present time. Times will not always
be like this. We are living in hope that
trade and industry in this country will
revive. When that time comes these people
will be in a good position, and perhaps be able
to pay some of the rent that is against them
at the present time. I hope the Government
will take steps in that direction.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I think the point
that has been put forward by the Mayor
of Derry and the hon. Senator (Mr. Dorman)
is one that the Government will have to
consider. I do not know what reply will be
given to the question to-day, but I hold that
it is no reply to say that it is not included
in the Act. When the Government can include an increased age limit as regards the
police in the Motor Act, a Clause of
similar kind could surely be included in
this Act to cover the question as to costs
in the eviction of tenants.
a
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): Scales of costs are fixed by the
courts, and I have no reason to believe that
the costs in cases of the nature indicated
487 Motor Vehicles Bill-

Report Stage.
[Viscount Charlemont]
in this question are excessive, although they

I may point out, however, for the information of the hon. Senator, that the question which he now raises is closely allied
with a matter raised in another place on
the 14th December, 1925, when in answer
to a question of Mr. Kyle, the Minister of
Home Affairs stated that the matter would

jurisdiction. The point in question has not
been lost sight of, but it is not by any means
as simple as would appear at first sight. 1
may say, speaking for myself, that in view
of the very strong representations which
have been put up by the hon. Senators
to-day I will recommend the question
to the further favourable consideration of
the Government. (Hon. Members: Hear.
hear.)

just say, as regards the answer given by

are great grievances on this subject, and 1
have great sympathy with the people.
hope the Government will take a note of the
matter, and do something to curb the extraordinary costs that are fixed.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 8, line
16, leave out "and the convicting court."
The first Amendment standing in my name
on the Paper is, as hon. Member's will recollect, a suggested Amendment which was
left over from the Committee stage yester-
The
day afternoon, in order that the matter
raised might be further considered. The
point at issue in so far as it is at issue is
really one simply of administration.
idea underlying my original proposal was to
insure that in a case where an appeal for the
removal of suspension, imposed itself by a
court of appeal, came up, the notice which
is necessary to be given to the police should
be given to the district-inspector of police for
the district where the offence was originally
charged, and where the first conviction took
place. It seems to me that, perhaps, it would
be more convenient for the district-inspector
to be notified rather than that the other
district-inspector operating in the immediate
vicinity of the court of appeal should receive
notification.
Since the Amendment was
withdrawn by me yesterday afternoon, discussions, if I may say so, have been going on
behind the Chair, and I understand that the
right hon. Gentleman (Sir Dawson Bates)
will now explain to us exactly how it is proposed that the machinery introduced by this
Clause should operate, and I think it will
probably be the case when the House has
heard the right hon. Gentleman that we
shall see that this is a case where the
original Amendment should not be pressed,
but for the moment I must move the words
as they stand.
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS (Sir
Dawson Bates): Since yesterday I have gone
into the point which was raised by the
learned Senator, and the effect of the Clause
as it now stands is, shortly, this. Assume a
man committed an offence in Ballymoney,
which will be familiar to the learned Senator,
and had his licence suspended for say twelve
months. He appeals to the higher court, the
higher court being the county court judge
sitting at Ballymena. Assuming the county
court judge confirmed that conviction, it
would still be open to the defendant, at the
end of three months, to apply to the convicting court, which in this case would be
the county court judge, to remit part of the
twelve months' suspension, but before the
applicant could make application to the
court he would have to serve notice, and
under the Bill it is proposed that the district-inspector under the convicting court,
which would be Ballymena and not Ballymoney, would be served with notice of the
intended application. From the administrative point of view what would happen is
that when the district-inspector at Ballymena got the notice of the proposed application the first thing he would do would be to
send the particulars up to headquarters,
because it is proposed under this Bill to have
a Central Register of every motorist who
holds a licence. There will be a complete
record as to what are his misdeeds from the
time the licence was issued. Full particu-
489 Motor Vehicles Bill-
Third Reading.

18 NOVEMBER 1926
コ
lars will be sent to the Central Registry. It
would be obviously desirable, in the interests
of justice, that the police should ascertain
how the defendant, who is applying to get
this remission of twelve months suspension,
was behaving himself, and whether or not
he may not have in the meantime committed some further offence at Ballymoney.
I think that if the Amendment was pressed
it would, instead of improving the Measure,
only more cramp and fetter the administration of the Act. I am sure hon. Senators are
aware that cases have occurred where a man
got a licence in Tyrone and had it suspended,
and then that individual came to Belfast and
got a new licence. Under this Act that will
be absolutely impossible with the Central
Registry, and every information in the interests of the police and the administration
of the Act will be given to the districtinspector who originally started the case
against the defendant.
Mr. MAGEE: May I ask if the present Bill
applies to offences committed before the
passing thereof, or what is the procedure in

Sir DAWSON BATES: No, it will only
apply to subsequent offences.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that the whole
House will join with me in feeling gratified
at the statement which has just been made
by the right hon. Gentleman. I am very
glad to hear about the Central Registry.
beg leave to withdraw the Amendment.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 18,
line 12, leave out or on the route of any

which stood over from yesterday. I proposed

it would be better, if only for some of the
reasons I gave yesterday, that they should be

Sir DAWSON BATES: On further consideration the Government are prepared to

by my learned Friend. I agree with him that
the words to a very large extent are redundant and unnecessary.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: Are we
quite assured now that the Clause as it stands
will prevent corporations from having their
buses or tramways outside their own district?
There were cases where buses were owned by
the Corporation, and the County Council had
to take action to prevent them from running.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I can assure my
hon. Friend that this Bill in no way affects
the rights of corporations or any other
authority to go outside. They have the
right to go outside. This Bill does not
affect them.

a
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill which we are now
about to read for the Third time is one of
great interest, and we have been for some
time discussing it. I do not want to go
back on anything that has been said during
the past few days on the matter, but I
think, perhaps, I might refer for
few
moments to an effect which the Bill is likely
to have on magistrates. Under this Bill the
powers of magistrates to some extent have
been-I will not say curtailed, but circumscribed and it may be that there is some
feeling in the minds of the magistrates about
it. As some remarks were made which, perhaps, magistrates as a whole might deprecate,
I should like, as one who perhaps to some
extent put up the case for the magistrates
to draw the attention of the House to the
fact that magistrates are unpaid public
servants, that their difficulties are very
great, and that those of them appointed
in recent years, by which I mean since 1908,
when the Children's Act came in--
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Oh!
a
Mr. LESLIE: I take that date because it
is
convenient date to reckon from; so
very much has happened since. The year
1908 is eleven years from the year 1897,
which was the date of the last issue of
"Humphrey's Justice of the Peace." The
point is that since 1897, in spite of the
Children's Act and other miscellaneous
statutes which came into operation later, a
magistrate has no handbook to which he can
refer in order to learn his business. The
only book which has been published since
is a somewhat monumental work in two

i
[Mr. Leslie]
volumes by O'Connor, and of this the second

I would like to say also that it must be
recognised by the magistrates concerned
that where a discretion remains vested in
them they must exercise a sound discretion
guided by law, and must not give effect to
their own whims and fancies. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) That is to say, if I may take
an easy example, it may possibly become my
duty to administer a minimum fine, and 1
shall certainly do so, not forgetting that if
the circumstances warrant it I am not to
be regarded as being limited to that minimum fine, and under some of the Clauses.
which occupied our attention yesterday I do
not think I should wish to limit myself to

their

Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I did
not mean to intervene, but the hon. Senator
(Mr. Leslie) always seems to give me an opportunity of doing so. He has now raised
new point about magistrates, in connection
with which I may tell a little story which
does not quite carry out his views. My father,
who was an eminent barrister, when he came
down to live in this part of the world, and
was made a magistrate, was complimented
by some of his brother magistrates on the
fact that as a barrister and a lawyer he
would be a great help to the Bench. I remember he told me that the words he used
in reply were: "Well, gentleman, I can tell
you that the less law magistrates know the
better, and let them use their commonsense." (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I would like
to say that I am certain that the magistrates in connection with this Motor Bill
will use at least as much commonsense in
the future as I hope they have done in the
past. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)

493 Question of Privilege.

30 NOVEMBER 1926
Royal Assent to Bills.

i
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 30th November, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Eleven
of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
PAPER PRESENTED.
Ministry of Labour:
National Health Insurance: Deposit Contributors Amendment Regulations (Northern
Ireland), 1926.
Presented (pursuant to Act), and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Question of Privilege.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: As the
first item of the business to-day, I have,
with your permission, to ask a question of
which I have given private notice. It is
common knowledge with the Members of the
Senate that on certain Amendments from
this House being sent forward to the Lower
House, certain remarks were made by an
hon. Member of that House which not only
affect the individual Members of the Senate
-I think individual Members of the Senate
can, as far as they are concerned take care
of themselves but went a good deal further
than that, because when discourteous and
insulting remarks are used by a Member of
the Lower House it not only affects the
officials but it affects the whole authority
and prerogative, as it were, of the Senate.
I think, therefore, the time has come to
make some remonstrance at any rate, and
as the hon. Member is an old Parliamentarian who really ought to have known the
constitutional procedure better, I am
astonished at him. But after all, when he
makes the remarks he did, he is in fact
really asking for the abolition of this House,
and, I think if it came to a question of
brains and common sense, it might be better
for the country to have the abolition not of
this House, but the abolition of the other
House, and to conduct the business here.
I will not detain you further. I am sure
you will give us a satisfactory answer to
this question, and that it will be taken
notice of in the proper manner. The question I wish to ask is:
con-
Whether it is not contrary to
stitutional practice for Members of the
House of Commons to refer to the
Senate and to Members of the Senate in
terms such as those used in the Debate in
the House of Commons on the 23rd November, 1926, as reported in the Parliamentary
Debates, columns 2376, 2386, 2390 and 2410, on
consideration of the Senate Amendments to
the various Bills then before that House.
Mr. SPEAKER: My reply to the right hon.
Member only, of course, can apply to the
practice in this House, and that must be
clearly understood. The use of offensive or
insulting words against the character or
proceedings of the other House is out of
order. But, though I fully appreciate the
spirit which prompted the question of the
right hon. Gentleman, I do not feel really
that, as long as this House carries out
conscientiously the duties for which it was
created, we need concern ourselves too much
with criticism, however ill-mannered.

495 Prorogation of Parliament.

THE SENATE
King's Speech.
Prorogation of Parliament.
His Grace the Governor, being seated on
the Throne, commanded the Gentleman
Usher of the Black Rod to let the Commons
know, "It is his Grace's pleasure that they
attend him in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar, with their
Speaker, his Grace delivered the following
Speech from the Throne:-
KING'S SPEECH.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons:
The unemployment situation continues
to be the most important problem confronting Northern Ireland, and it is a
matter for regret that the improvement in
trade which was apparent in the early
months of this year has received a serious
set-back as a result of the unhappy dispute
in the Coal Mining Industry in Great
Britain. Notwithstanding, however, the
great difficulties which our industries have
experienced in carrying on their business
during this critical period, it is encouraging to note that 30,000 persons have been
placed in employment through the agency
of the Employment Exchanges since the
beginning of the year, or 6,000 more than
during the corresponding period of last
year.
I am glad to observe that since the Session
opened 86 schemes of public utility promoted by local authorities have been
approved as unemployment relief schemes
for Government grants, involving a total
expenditure of £380,000, and that the
Government has taken power to extend its
assistance to the carrying out of works of
capital construction by increasing the
limit of capital which may be guaranteed
under the Loans Guarantee Acts to
£9,000,000, and extending the operation of
these Acts to the 31st March, 1928. This is
proving of great benefit to the Shipbuilding
Industry and has also been instrumental
in the establishment of an Artificial Silk
Factory and the re-opening of certain
Woollen Mills.
I also note with satisfaction that contracts
have recently been placed in Belfast for
several new vessels. This will give muchneeded employment to those associated with
an industry which has suffered seriously
from the protracted general depression.
Some slight improvement during the past
few months has also been apparent in the
linen trade.
I am gratified that legislative effect has
been given to the arrangements reached
with the British Government in regard
to the financial position of the Unemployment Insurance Fund and that the helpfulness of the Widows', Orphans' and Old
Age Contributory Pensions Scheme continues to extend; the number of persons in
Northern Ireland now enjoying benefits
totalling 16,000.
The passing of the Motor Vehicles
(Traffic and Regulation) Act will, I hope,
result in the prevention of regrettable
accidents involving loss of life. I sincerely
trust that all sections of the public, including pedestrians themselves, will energetically second the efforts of the authorities in
endeavouring to secure safety on our public
highways.
It was with satisfaction that I learnt of
the proposal of the Government to continue
the Housing Acts for another twelve
months, and I hope that during that period
the joint efforts of local authorities and of
private enterprise, with the financial assistance accorded by the Government, will have
the effect of alleviating in a marked degree
the hardship and inconvenience so long
endured by the working classes owing to
the shortage of houses.
The establishment of an Inn of Court
for Northern Ireland and the recognition
by my Government of the necessity of such
an Institution by giving financial assistance
towards its educational work will give
widespread satisfaction.
Members of the House of Commons:
I thank you for the provision of supplies
for the public services.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons:
I am glad to learn that the arrangements
in connection with the transfer of certain
services to Northern Ireland to which I
referred in my Speech at the opening of
the Session and which were embodied in
the
Act
Administrative Provisions
(Northern Ireland), 1926, have proceeded
smoothly since the appointed day for the
transfer.
It is a source of great satisfaction to
know that Northern Ireland continues to
be free from abnormal crime and that law
and order have been effectively maintained.
I pray that the Blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your labours.
Then the Proclamation Proroguing the
present Parliament of Northern Ireland was
read by the Speaker as followeth:-
Proclamation.
End of Session.
30 NOVEMBER 1926
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION
AND I do hereby further declare His
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to be that
the said Parliament shall on the said eighth
day of March, One Thousand Nine Hundred
and Twenty-seven, assemble and be holden for
the dispatch of divers urgent and important
affairs, and the Senators and Members of the
House of Commons of Northern Ireland are
hereby required to give their attendance
accordingly.
For Proroguing the present Parliament of
Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by Section eleven
of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, as
modified by and in pursuance of certain
enactments amending the said Act, that the
Governor of Northern Ireland shall in His
Majesty's name summon, prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of Northern Ireland.
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that this
present Parliament of Northern Ireland
should be prorogued to the eighth day of
March, One Thousand Nine Hundred and
Twenty-seven.
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD,
DUKE OF ABERCORN, Governor of Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation do publish
and declare His Majesty's Pleasure that the
said Parliament be, as from the reading of
this Proclamation in the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, prorogued to Tuesday,
the eighth day of March, One Thousand
Nine Hundred and Twenty-seven.
Given at Government House, Hillsborough,
this twenty-ninth day of November, One
Thousand Nine Hundred and Twenty-six.
Signed by his own hand,
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
His Grace, the Governor, then retired.
End of the Second Session (opened 9th
March, 1926) of the Second Parliament of
Northern Ireland, in the Seventeenth year
of the Reign of His Majesty King George
the Fifth.

T
INDEX.
