Message from the Governor.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of the
House) I have to report to the House that
His Grace the Governor has been pleased to
return the following Answer to the Address
from the Senate on Tuesday last :-
I have received with great satisfaction
the expression of your thanks for the
Speech with which I have opened the present Session of Parliament.
Pharmacy (Temporary Provisions)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In rising this afternoon to ask this
House to give a Second Reading to the
Pharmacy (Temporary Provisions) Bill, I do
not think it will be necessary for me to detain the House any length of time. It is a
short Bill, and, perhaps I may be allowed to
say, a very necessary one. It is a Bill, as
the title states, to amend an Act which this
Parliament passed in June last year. The
Bill aims at amending in certain respects the
Pharmacy and Poisons Act (Northern Ireland), which, as I have said, was passed in
June last year. Section 31, sub-section 1, of
that Act provided for the fixing of an appointed day in order to bring into operation
those parts of the Act which deal with the
holding of examinations and the issue of
annual licences. That Act provided that all
licences must be taken out on or before the
1st January; consequently, the appointed
day had to be fixed before that date in order
that there might be statutory authority for
collecting licence fees.
Second Reading.

Pharmacy Bill-
THE SENATE
IViscount Massereene and Ferrard]
I might mention that the fixing of the
appointed day automatically brought into
operation all the sections dealing with examinations, and at the same time debarred
the Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland from recognising the subsequent examinations of the Southern Society. It had
originally been hoped that the first meeting
of the Council of this Pharmaceutical Society
would have been held in the summer, soon
after the passing of the Act, but owing to
many unforessen difficulties this was found
to be impossible. As a matter of fact the
Council held its first meeting early in December, and, as hon. Members will appreciate,
the drawing up of a syllabus for examinations is a difficult and lengthy matter, and
it is obvious that some considerable time
must necessarily elapse before the Council is
in a position to bring its own system of
examinations into operation. Meanwhile,
there is no means open to candidates to
qualify for registration in Northern Ireland.
The present Bill provides for the recognition of the Southern Society's examinations
until a prescribed day, which must not be
later than the 30th of next September. The
Council will thus have ample time, not only
to arrange for their own examinations, but
also to give sufficient notice to intending
candidates regarding the new syllabus.
Opportunity has also been taken in this
Bill of rectifying another small matter.
Section 19, sub-section 2, of the Pharmacy
and Poisons Act (Northern Ireland), 1925,
permitted the executors of a deceased pharmaceutical chemist who was registered under
the Act to carry on his business subject to
certain conditions, but no provision was
made for a business being carried on by the
executors of a pharmaceutical chemist who
had died prior to the passing of the Act.
To rectify this the Bill provides for the business of a pharmaceutical chemist who died
before, or within nine months after the
passing of the Act-that is 9th June, 1925being carried on by executors provided that
a duly qualified manager is employed.
I do not think that I can usefully add
anything more in explanation of this Bill,
except to say that it in no way departs from
the original intention of the Act, but has
been framed in order to allow a reasonable
and sufficient time in which to carry one of
the main provisions of the Act into effect.
I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I desire to take advantage
of this opportunity to draw the attention of
the Government to some disabilities that are
suffered by individual citizens for whom we
are legislating in this amending Pharmacy
Bill. I understand that at present a chemist
who qualifies in Northern Ireland is not recognised by the Pharmaceutical Society of
Great Britain. Under the existing arrangements a qualified chemist may leave Northern
Ireland and proceed to one of the Colonies,
say New Zealand, become registered in new
Zealand, and immediately transfer to London
and be recognised there as a qualified
chemist. Because of his New Zealand qualification he can open an establishment in
London, but had he remained in Northern
Ireland, even as the proprietor of a chemist's
business, and afterwards proceeded to London, he would have been debarred from
carrying on his profession there or in any
other part of Great Britain.
Under the existing arrangement he would
not even be allowed to enter an examination,
although he might have practised as a pharmaceutical chemist in Northern Ireland for
many years, and been the proprietor of
an establishment. He would not be permitted to carry on his profession at all
unless he first agreed to become a junior
apprentice, and was prepared to devote at
least 4,000 hours' study under a qualified
chemist in Great Britain. I know this to be
true, because I am acquainted with a chemist
who has recently returned from London.
He approached the Society in Great Britain
with the object of opening an establishment
in London, and he was informed that it
would be necessary for him to become a
junior apprentice and devote 4,000 hours to
study under a qualified chemist before he
would be allowed to practice in London.
Then again, the English Society will not
recognise Irish apprentices. Assuming that
the parents of any of the youths of Northern
Ireland went to the trouble and expense of
giving them three years' study in order to
qualify them as chemists, and the parents
found it necessary to transfer their home
to Great Britain, the three years' study
which their child had undertaken in Northern Ireland would not be recognised in
Great Britain.
Considering that we in this particular area
are so fond of moving on parallel lines with
Great Britain, I want to ask the Government
to demand that some steps should be taken
so that chemists qualified in Northern Ireland should be recognised and be able to
practise in Great Britain. The Northern
Ireland Pharmacy Act passed last year has
left it open to the Society to offer reciprocal
treatment to chemists qualified in the
Commonwealth of Nations comprising the
British Empire, and I think I am justified
in asking for similar treatment on behalf of
the profession in Northern Ireland. I understand, indeed I know personally, that the
examinations of the old Irish Pharmaceutical
Society are quite as stiff as the examinations
which are undergone by apprentices in Great
Britain.
!

I
Municipal Corporations Bill -
Second Reading.
16 MARCH 1926
I therefore take this opportunity of asking
the noble Viscount to use his influence in
the direction of seeing that steps are taken
to afford chemists and apprentices in Northern Ireland the right to demand that their
status in Northern Ireland shall be recog
nised in Great Britain.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Senator who
has raised this somewhat important point,
it must be remembered that the Council of
the Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland has only just been constituted, and I
have no reason to suppose otherwise than
that they will correspond with the Pharmaceutical Society across the water, and that a
reciprocal arrangement will eventually be
arrived at. At present such is not the case,
but I will look into the matter. I am sure
we are all agreed that a chemist who is
qualified in Northern Ireland should be
equally qualified to practise his profession
in England.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Municipal Corporations Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move the Second Reading of this Bill, the
object of which is to make necessary amendments in the law relating to the granting of
Charters of Incorporation as Boroughs to
Urban District Councils. With the permission of this House I shall explain how the
necessity has arisen for these amendments.
Prior to the establishment of the Northern
Government a Petition for Incorporation
had to be presented to His Majesty the King,
who acted with the advice of the Privy
Council in granting the Charter of Incorporation. Under the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920, and the Irish Free State (Consequential Provisions) Act, 1922, the functions
of His Majesty in this respect have been
delegated to His Grace the Governor of
Northern Ireland. Through an oversight,
however, no consequential provision was
made as to the status of the Privy Council
of Northern Ireland in regard to advising
the Governor concerning Petitions for Incorporation. Hence, under the existing circumstances, if the inhabitants of one of our
more important urban districts desire that
their district should be constituted as a
Borough, the procedure which they must
adopt in order to obtain a Charter is indefinite and complicated. As it is understood
that in at least one case steps are being
taken to present a Petition for Incorporation, it becomes necessary to regularize the
situation and to establish the position of the
Northern Ireland Privy Council as the
advisers to the Governor in connection with
the granting of such Charters.
The present Bill amends Section 14 of the
Municipal Corporations (Ireland) Act, 1840,
by repealing that section and substituting a
new section, which enables His Grace the
Governor, acting with the advice of the Privy
Council of Northern Ireland, to grant a
Charter of Incorporation.
Another change, however, has been made.
Under the Act of 1840, the inhabitants of
any urban district, the population of which
exceeded three thousand, were allowed to present a Petition for Incorporation as a
Borough. In the Bill before you the minimum population is fixed at ten thousand.
Thus the privilege of becoming a Borough
will be invested with a dignity which would
be wanting if the lower limit of population
were prescribed. I may mention that during
the eighty-five years which have intervened
since the Act of 1840 was passed only one
Charter of Incorporation has been granted,
namely, to the Urban District of Wexford.
This town had a population of over ten
thousand inhabitants, and it seems to have
been understood that Charters would not be
granted to any smaller towns, as otherwise
the privilege of becoming a Borough would
lose much of its value.
Certain old Boroughs which were in existence prior to the passing of the Municipal
Corporations (Ireland) Act, 1840, will still
retain the right of presenting Petitions for
Incorporation, should they so desire. These
old Boroughs constitute the present urban
districts of Armagh, Carrickfergus, Coleraine, Dungannon, Enniskillen, and Strabane, and though the populations of these
districts are lower than ten thousand inhabitants, I think that this House will agree
with me that their historical associations
render it desirable that they should have the
right of presenting Petitions for Incorporation as Boroughs. I beg to move.
Mr. MAGEE: I should like to ask the
noble Viscount, the Leader of the House,
what are the reasons for this Bill. He has
given reasons for the presentation of the
Measure, but it must have been in consequence of some demand for it. There is a
Council of Municipal Authorities of Northern Ireland which takes an interest in all
legislation affecting municipalities in Northern Ireland, be they corporations or urban
councils, and as a matter of fact individual
members of the Government have recognised
that body by attending the annual conference. At the annual conference, held a short
time ago, it was suggested-I do not say by
any Member of the Government but by
members of our association--that it would be
B
25 Municipal Corporations Bill—
Second Reading.

THE SENATE
[Mr. Magee]
only proper and reasonable that legislation affecting municipalities in Northern
Ireland should not be dealt with until our
Council, which represents practically every
municipal body in Northern Ireland, had
been consulted.
I need not tell you that the Prime Minister
did not promise that he would consult us,
but it was not suggested that we should not
be consulted. I think that a Measure of
this kind, which intimately affects the whole
of the country, and particularly the bodies
which are represented by that association,
should not be proceeded with further until
they have been consulted.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I do not think it
would be out of place, or ungraceful on my
part, as the oldest Member representing a
borough which has the unique distinction,
outside the City of Belfast, of having its
mayor and corporation, if I should say a
few words in regard to this Bill. In the
first place, I must congratulate the Leader
of the House on having lost no time in introducing legislation. With regard to the
Bill itself, I immediately see there is a
necessity for such a Measure so as to give
the Government of Northern Ireland the
powers affecting Municipal Charters which
had been vested in the Imperial Parliament
and in His Majesty the King. I also recognise that although the towns mentioned by
the Leader of the House are not up to the
standard of population demanded by this Bill
it would be very ungracious if their old
standing as Boroughs was interfered with.
With regard to applications for new
Charters, I view the matter in this way. If,
say, Bangor wanted to raise the chairman of
the Urban Council to the dignity of Mayor,
and invest him and his aldermen and councillors with the flowing robes of office, I
think every town in the Province should
have the same privilege. We might have
Ballymena, Newry, Portadown, Lisburn,
and perhaps other towns raised to the
dignity of having a mayor and corporation.
It strikes me that the desire for such a
change shows a spirit of confidence in the
progress of their municipalities, and I see no
reason why they should not be given the
opportunity of attaining the dignity of
having a mayor and corporation. There is
no reason why this Senate should not support a Bill that will give such towns the
right to a Charter, and I have very great
pleasure in supporting the Measure.
Mr. LESLIE: I confess myself very much
in sympathy with the hon. Senator who has
just sat down in his desire to afford every
inducement to rising municipalities to receive this honour. The only thing approaching criticism which I should venture to pass
upon the Bill is to raise the question as to
whether we are quite wise in accepting the
last published census as the test of population of ten thousand. At the moment, basing
my remarks on the last census, which is
fifteen years old, apart from the ancient City
of Londonderry and the City of Belfast, we
have only five towns with a population exceeding ten thousand, according to the census
of 1911. Those five towns are Ballymena,
Lisburn, Lurgan, Portadown, and Newry,
but I have no doubt but that when our new
census comes to be taken in the middle of
next month, when we have the results we
shall find other towns which have risen above
the ten thousand mark, and it may be that
one of those towns is the town which Viscount Charlemont referred to as being
anxious to obtain a Charter.
There may also be other rising towns
which may not reach the population of ten
thousand. They may have a population of
9,500, and it may very well happen that if
there is the normal ten years' distance be
tween the census a town with a population
of 9,500 in 1926 may arrive at the ten thousand mark some years before a new census
can be taken, and therefore the ambition of
that town might be deferred, perhaps unduly. I think it is perhaps worth while to
consider at a later stage whether we shall
insist on the criterion of the last census,
and in making that suggestion I cannot close
my eyes to the fact that a public census
taken by experienced hands and worked out
with experience and care is really the only
satisfactory method of arriving at definite
figures. It would be possible to make other
proposals, but I admit the difficulty is very
great if you propose to substitute anything
for the authentic and official census. All the
same we might consider whether we might
not give this opportunity to a town which,
although it has not reached a population of
ten thousand at the time of the census, may
do so in a short time afterwards. Taking
the Bill as a whole I have nothing to do but
to support it.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: With regard
to what the last speaker has said, I shall be
obliged if he will raise the matter in Committee. In the speeches of the other two
hon. Senators who have spoken there appears to be a difference of opinion, as one
would like the Bill confined and the other
would like to extend its operation. In
general I should point out that this Bill
only aims at regularising and making easier
procedure which is already in existence.
Even at the present moment a town can
present its petition for incorporation, but
the law makes it unnecessarily complicated,
and I think it will be the opinion of this
House that laws which are made for the
betterment of the community in general
should be as little complicated as possible.
37 Municipal Corporations Bill.

16 MARCH 1926
Adjournment.
I think the procedure in this case should be
made as simple as possible, and the course
proposed by this Bill aims at saving both
time and money. I do not think the incorporation of other rising towns in Northern
Ireland will militate against the prestige
held by those places which have already
attained that dignity.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Adjournment.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That this House do now adjourn. I
may say that the Unemployment Insurance
(Agreement) Bill will not come to us from
another place until Thursday, but it is very
important that it should receive the Royal
Assent by Tuesday, 23rd March. I therefore
propose that we should meet on Thursday,
to take the Committee Stage of the Bills we
have just dealt with and to receive the
Unemployment Insurance Bill, and if possible give it a First Reading. Then it is
suggested that we should meet on Friday to
take the Bill through its remaining stages.
I know it is not customary for this House
to meet on Friday, but the circumstances
are rather exceptional. The alternative is
that Standing Orders should be suspended
and the Bill taken through all its stages on
Thursday. That would rather depend upon
the time at which the Bill arrived in this
House from another place, and at the
moment I cannot say what that time will be.
It obviously depends upon its progress
elsewhere.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
minutes before Four o'clock,
until Thursday next.
Municipal Corporations Bill 49
Pharmacy Bill.

Municipal Corporations Bill.

In Committee accordingly.

CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of s. 14 of 3 and 4
Vic., c. 108.)
"14.-(1) If a petition to grant a charter
of incorporation under this Act to the inhabitants of any urban district-
(a) consisting of or comprising any
borough named in the schedule (B) to this
Act annexed; or
(b) in which the population exceeds ten
thousand according to the last published
census for the time being.
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: In the first instance I have
to acquaint the Committee that the Amendment which I propose to move is not the
Amendment standing in my name on the
Paper. I found when I considered the
matter a little more that the Amendment
on the Paper, if accepted, would have had
an effect which was not intended, and therefore I propose to substitute wording which
is, I think, before you, Mr. Chairman. My
Motion is, in Clause 1, page 1, at the end of
line 18, to addor according to such statistics of later
date than the last published census as the
urban district council concerned may be
prepared to submit for the consideration
of the Privy Council of Northern Ireland.
The alteration, as the Committee will see,
is merely one of wording, and does not
affect the principle which I referred to when
speaking on the Second Reading of the Bill,
41 Municipal Corporations Bill--

18 MARCH 1926
Committee.
but it does avoid a little pitfall to which I
am afraid the earlier proposal was liable.
The point really is that we are at this
moment legislating for municipal corporations without any recent census returns to
guide us. Our last census is fifteen years
old, and we shall have another census next
month. It may very well be that if we were
discussing this matter in the autumn, with
the results of the census of next April
before us, we might find it was not necessary
to make any change, but not only are we
fifteen years behind as regards census returns, but we are entirely without knowledge as to the date when the next census
is likely to be taken. Normally in England
a ten years' interval elapses between the
taking of the censuses. There have been of
late years many proposals in England for curtailing that period. Some suggest seven
years, others five years, and others even
three years, and it may be that if it is decided in England to take a census again in
five years' time we should follow that
example. In any case it might happen that
the normal English census being taken in
1931, we should take another, although it
would be only five years since the census
fixed for the current year.
We have to accept the probability that
after taking the census this year we shall
not be taking another for ten years. That
is the best opinion we can form on the information before us. When the last census was
taken, in 1911, there were I am neglecting,
of course, the half dozen ancient towns referred to by the noble Viscount (Viscount
Charlemont) the other day-only five towns
with a population of 10,000, and only three
further towns with a population approximating to 10,000, and they did not approximate very closely. The urban district whose
population came nearest to 10,000 was Newtownards with a population of 9,500. Then
came Larne with just over 8,000, and Bangor
with a hundred or two less, and I think we
have no other urban district with a population exceeding 6,000. What the position
is to-day I do not know.
Under this Bill we are proposing to take
away from an urban district of 3,000 people
its right to apply for a Charter. Whether
an urban district with a population of over
3,000 would succeed in getting a Charter I
do not know. It is not a matter on which
we can form an opinion, because there is no
instance where an application has been made
to the Privy Council which would serve as
any guide. Presumably, the advice which
the Privy Council would tender to His
Grace the Governor would depend on all the
circumstances before the Council, and it
would really be impossible to give any accurate forecast of their decision. At the
moment an urban district with a population
of 3,000 can petition for a Charter, but if
this Bill becomes an Act the population
must be 10,000 instead of 3,000, at the date
of the last published census, which will, of
course, be that about to be taken.
If it should happen that there should be
an urban district with a population disclosed
by the census next month approximating to
10,000 but not reaching it, no petition for a
Charter can be put forward until the next
census has been taken and a population of
10,000 reached.
It seems to me that might possibly cause
some feeling of discontent in the Urban
District which happened to be concerned,
and the question which I think the Committee has to consider is whether that possibility is reasonably likely, and, if so, whether
we ought to take steps, either by the words
I now suggest or by some other means, to
obivate the occurrence. We cannot deny that
the last published census is the index
usually adopted, nor can we deny that an
official, published census is the best method
of ascertaining population, but I think there
might be some grievance if we stood solely
on the last published census and gave no
chance of intimating in the meantime that
the population had increased since the last
census. Accordingly, I beg to move the
Amendment.
To
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of the
House): I am afraid I must oppose the
Amendment on one or two grounds.
begin with, I think the arrangement the hon.
Senator contemplates might lead the Urban
District Council concerned into a good deal
of expense to prove by how much their popu
lation had increased, and I think the expense
would be rather out of proportion to the
advantage derived. In addition, I do not
think it would involve any great hardship
for that Urban District Council to wait until
the next census, because there is always the
possibility that the population of a town
might decrease. I think I am correct in
saying that the population of Larne has
decreased since the War.
The Imperial Parliament may possibly
introduce a system of quinquennial censuses
in the near future, in which case we might
follow suit. In that case the ground would
be cut from under the hon. Senator's feet.
In view of these circumstances I would ask
the hon. Member to withdraw his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: At all events it is certainly
not a matter to press at this stage, because
as regards this Bill we are the Chamber
initiating the legislation, and the matter can,
if necessary, be brought up again. If we
should have a census every five years, at
least half the ground is taken from me. I
Insurance Agreement Bill--

Message from the Commons.
That the Commons have passed the Unemployment Insurance (Agreement) Bill, to
which they desire the concurrence of this
House.
Unemployment Insurance (Agreement) Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: It is with
very great satisfaction that on behalf of
the Government, I move the Second Reading
of this Bill. Throughout the whole period
for which the Government has been responsible for the Unemployment Insurance service in Northern Ireland we have been faced
with a very grave unemployment problem.
The Government has adopted every course
within the limits of its financial resources
to stimulate employment in Northern Ireland. With the aid of the Loans Guarantee
Act, it has guaranteed loans for works of
capital construction amounting in all to
over £3,880,000, as a result of which a substantial amount of employment has been
given to our shipbuilding yards in particular, no fewer than 8,300 men being employed at the moment on ships, the building
of which has been undertaken with the
assistance of these Acts. A great amount of
employment has also been given on road
improvement works, and, with the assistance of local authorities, works of public
utility have been promoted with Government
aid to the total value of almost £4,200,000,
which about 22,000 jobs have been
obtained for our unemployed people.
on
Notwithstanding these efforts on behalf of
the Government, and others to which I need
not refer in detail at this moment, unemployment has continued very heavy and has
strained severely the resources of our Unemployment Fund. The Government might
have adopted the course that the benefits of
our unemployment scheme should have been
diminished, but this would have meant that
the social standards of our people would
have been reduced to a level lower than
that enjoyed by their fellows in the rest of
the United Kingdom. The Government,
however, felt that such a course was impossible, and it has followed as a consistent
policy throughout that the provisions of the
unemployment insurance scheme here
should be kept uniform with those across
the water. In consequence, the Government
has brought in successive Bills keeping the
unemployment scheme here on lines parallel
to those of the scheme in Great Britain, and,
at the same time, no opportunity has been
lost of impressing on the Imperial Government the view that it is only equitable that
the incidence of unemployment in Northern
Ireland should be spread in some way over
the whole of the United Kingdom, having
regard to the fact that economically and
industrially we are an integral part of the
United Kingdom.
I am very glad to say that this view has
been accepted by the Imperial Government,
and this Bill is designed to give effect to
this principle, which is all important to our
werkpeople here. An agreement has been
reached between the two Governments, which
is set out in the Schedule to this Bill, and
I need hardly point out that the agreement
supports entirely the wisdom of the course
45 Insurance Agreement Bill-

18 MARCH 1926
Second Reading.
which our Government has followed, and is
a guarantee that the social standards of
our people here will be maintained at the
same level as those of their fellows in Great
Britain.
A perusal of the Agreement would suggest
that it is highly technical, but in effect the
principles of the Agreement are clear. As
explained in the White Paper, which has
been published in connection with the Bill,
the Agreement provides for the two Unemployment Funds to be maintained in a state
of financial parity in respect of the period
from 1st October, 1925, to the end of the
current financial year, and keeps it in that
position for the four succeeding financial
years. In order to do this our Exchequer
is to make special contributions to our Unemployment Fund of an amount sufficient
to produce a deficit or surplus per head of
the insured population equal to that in
Great Britain. This having been done, the
total contributions, that is, the ordinary
State Grants and the special payment that
I have mentioned, made by the respective
Exchequers are spread over the total populations of the two countries, and threequarters in excess of the amount of this
average is paid over to the Northern Ireland
Exchequer. The debit balance on our Fund
at the 30th September last, so far as it exceeds the proportionate part for Northern Ireland of the British Fund's debt on that date,
is to be placed to a Suspense Account, but
the interest on this debt is to rank for
assistance from the Imperial Exchequer
under the terms of the Agreement.
The clauses in the Bill are simply machinery to give effect to this Agreement, and
I need not detain the House with a detailed
explanation of them clause by clause. I beg
to move.
Mr. DORMAN: 1 wish to welcome the
settlement that has been entered into by
Northern Ireland and the Imperial Exchequer on this particular question of unemployment. While admitting that the
Northern Government has moved on parallel
lines with Great Britain so far as concerns
unemployment contributions and benefits to
those who are unfortunately amongst the
ranks of the unemployed, I do not think we
are justified in claiming that
we have
afforded the same facilities of succour and
Northern
support to the unemployed in
Ireland as the unemployed in Great Britain
have been in receipt of for the last few years.
In illustration of this I wish to give one
case out of many that have come under my
own personal observation. It is the case of
a young man, 32 years of age, with a wife
and three children, who was not entitled,
in consequence of his specific employment,
to claim unemployment benefit, and who was
engaged in a branch of the linen industry
which, I understand, has recently been
abolished-designing linen patterns for the
consumption of the United States.
When noticed to vacate his home in which
he was lodging, with a widow, and left
destitute, he came to me, and I personally sent him to the relieving officer of the
district, from whom he received the sum of
10s 6d to carry him over the week-end.
When he appeared to claim the same succour
he would have been entitled to had he been
resident in any of the industrial centres of
Great Britain, he was offered the alternative of breaking up his home and entering
the workhouse as an inmate and separating
himself from his wife and family. That is
one of many instances that have come under
my personal observation of men who have
been turned off guaranteed benefits and
offered the alternative of going into the
workhouse.
Had that young man been in the ranks
of the unemployed in Glasgow, or in any
large city across the water, he would have
been entitled to augmentation sufficient to
bring him up to what the Poor-Law Guardians considered to be a minimum of subsistence. Therefore, I do not think the
Northern Government is entitled to claim
that it is moving on parallel lines with
Great Britain so far as the support of the
unemployed is concerned.
I am very glad to know that the Northern
Government is not responsible for the settlement imposed on Northern Ireland, a settlement which has, unfortunately, caused many
of those in the ranks of the unemployed to
misjudge and unjustifiably blame the
Northern Government for the impasse the
Unemployment Fund is in. Nor do I think
the settlement is all that we might consider
just, because I understand that at the present time the Ministry of Labour is paying
in excess of the amount contributed by the
employers and workers a sum equal to
£35,000 a week, which, if unemployment is
not relieved during the coming year, will
leave the Northern Government in debt to
the extent of £1,800,000.
As I understand the arrangement just
entered into, the Imperial Government is
not, under this Agreement, shouldering a
fair share of the enormous debt that the
Northern Government has had to contract
in order to maintain the unemployed in our
community. They are only undertaking to
meet a share of the interest upon that debt,
and I personally anticipate that at the close
of the present year, when this Agreement has
been in operation a year, the million pounds
which is guaranteed will have been exceeded,
and there will have to be some new arrangement entered into between the Imperial
47 Coleraine Technical School.
Coleraine Technical School. 48
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
Government and the Government of Northern Ireland. I hope that when that time
arrives the Northern Government will take
steps to demand, as a matter of justice, that
the Imperial Exchequer should shoulder a
reasonable proportion of that debt, because
at the time the funds were separated we
were a hundred per cent. worse off as regards
the charge for unemployment benefit in
Northern Ireland.
On behalf of those I represent in this
House I welcome this settlement, and if we
should continue to have such a large proportion of unemployed I hope that in any
future settlement the Imperial Parliament
will not merely undertake to meet their
share of the interest on the unemployment
debt, but will agree to liquidate a portion
of that debt and bring it into a state of
parity with the fund in Great Britain, as
it should have been at the time of the recent settlement.

Coleraine Technical School.
MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move: That this House do now adjourn.
Mr. LESLIE: I desire to take this opportunity of asking the noble Viscount, the
Leader of the House and Minister of
Education, a question of which I have given
him private notice. I wish to ask whether
the attention of the Ministry has been called
to the criticisms passed at Coleraine last
Tuesday, at a meeting of the Coleraine
Technical Committee, criticisms passed on
the Government generally and on the
Ministry of Education in particular in respect of an alleged delay in the completion
of the negotiations respecting the transfer
of Coleraine Technical School.
The speeches at the meeting to which I
have referred seem to have been couched in
somewhat violent language, and in language
which perhaps ean scarcely be described as
urbane. Apparently nothing was said as to
whether the negotiations have been accelerated from the outside by the Coleraine
people themselves; I mean nothing was said
as to whether their own action had been of
a rapid nature, nor was any consideration
apparently given to any administrative or
legal difficulties which might arise in considering any proposals for the transfer of
property from one public body to another.
Nor again does it appear to have occurred to
the Committee that at the moment there is
more than one application for transfer engaging the attention of the Ministry.
As to all those questions I cannot of course
argue, as I have no knowledge of them, but
as complaint has been made, and as that
complaint has attained a certain amount of
notoriety, owing to its publication in the
daily Press, I thought it was only right
that the matter should be considered at the
earliest possible moment, and that the
answer I hope to receive should also be communicated to the Press without loss of time.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am very
much obliged to the hon. Senator for raising
this question, and also for giving me private
notice concerning it.
It is true that the examination of the
Draft Agreement for the relinquishment of
the powers and duties of the Technical Instruction Committee in favour of the Education Authority has occupied a considerable
time.
On the 10th of November, 1925, the Draft
Agreement was received by the Ministry of
Education from the solicitors to the Urban
District Council, but it was not until the
2nd of December that the Ministry were
informed of the resolution of the County
Council to accept the proposed Agreement.
During December the proposals, which
raised many difficult legal questions, were
under examination in the Ministry. By the
1st of January, 1926, the Scheme had been
fully examined. The legal difficulties were
found to be so serious that the case was submitted to the Law Officers of the Government. Their reply, which was received on
the 13th of February, raised further difficulties, both legal and administrative, which
had to be examined in the Ministry of Education, and subsequent discussion was
necessary with the Chief Inspector of Audits
at the Ministry of Home Affairs.
Mr. SPEAKER: If the noble Leader of the
House wishes the Press to get this reply he
will have to speak a little more slowly.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I may add
that the urgency for getting the whole business disposed of by the end of this month
lay in the fact that the proposals were designed to take effect as from the 1st of April,
1926, and it was, accordingly, highly desirable that the matter should be concluded before the end of the current financial year.
49 Coleraine Technical School.

18 MARCH 1926
Adjournment.
It is fair also to assume that the negotiations between the local parties were of a
protracted character. The Education
Authority came into office on the 1st of April,
1925, and it was not until early in December
that the approval by that Authority of the
Draft Scheme was communicated to the
Ministry of Education.

Importation of Inferior Timber.
The following question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Senator Samuel Cunningham:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether,
in view of the prevalence of dry rot arising
from the importation of inferior timber, the
Government will consider the question of
introducing legislation to provide for the inspection of all imported timber, and the rejection of that found infected or faulty.
I ask
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
the Question which stands in my name,
because I realise there is great loss to
the country through the spread of dry
rot. Whether during recent years it has
been brought about on account of inferior
timber having been cut during war times,
carelessly stacked in the forests, and then
brought into the country, or whether it is
just the spread of the disease through our
houses is a question of which I have no
knowledge. I think it is a question which
the Government might investigate, and if it
is found that dry rot is caused by the importation of inferior timber, the Government
ought to introduce legislation to prevent
such timber being brought into the
country.
We have had experience during the past
year of the beneficial effects of legislation of
this kind. We all know the result of the
Act which was passed to prevent inferior eggs
being sent from this Province into other
markets. Formerly Irish eggs were only
considered of second quality in the Glasgow
markets, but I happen to know that Irish
eggs are now in better demand and
bring better prices in the Glasgow
markets than Danish or any other eggs.
When we see what useful legislation can do
in that respect we hope that if the Government find that dry rot is caused by inferior
and unripened timber, they will adopt similar means to prevent its importation into
Northern Ireland.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of what has been
said by my right hon. Friend, as regards
the efforts that might be promoted by the
Government towards the extirpation of dry
rot in the timber used in Northern Ireland.
I would like to ask the Government, when
considering this question, to bear in mind
that, although there are, as we are all aware,
certain restrictions imposed on the Parliament of Northern Ireland as regards importation, yet the same sub-Section of the
Government of Ireland Act which imposes
the restriction, also does empower this Par-
23 MARCH 1926
Inferior Timber.

Business of the House.
to-day and be read a First time, and it is
very important that we should take 11
through all its stages on Wednesday and
Thursday.
liament to make laws which are limited to
the protection of the traders of this part of
Ireland from fraud. I would, therefore,
venture to suggest that perhaps something
on those lines might be done.
We know we cannot go outside the limitations of the Act as regards importations
generally, because the restriction imposed
was meant to be a valuable restriction and
was used, for instance, in the Plumage Bill.
I remember the Parliamentary Secretary
reminding this House of this restriction
when we were dealing with the difficulty as
regards condensed milk of bad quality, and
in the case of milk we were obliged to limit
our Statute to giving power to make Regulations because of the restriction to which I
have alluded. I think that as faulty timber
is only imported here for sale in some form
or other, there is some hope of our being able
to do something under that paragraph of
that sub-section of the Government of Ireland Act, and I would like to press that,
so far as I can, on the Government.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The question
raised by the right hon. Senator (Mr.
Samuel Cunningham) would, of course, as
the last speaker mentioned, come within Section 4 of the Government of Ireland Act, and
with regard to the protection of users from
fraud, a fresh Bill would probably be necessary to effect that result. The Government
are of the opinion that in this particular
matter it would be rather difficult to bring
in restrictive legislation affecting one commodity alone. So far as eggs are concerned,
it was a question of export only, not of import, and I think that to take up the matter as Senator Leslie advises would mean
fresh legislation. However, if the question
was pushed here I am sure the Government
would be pleased to consider that possibility.

Business of the House.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I suggest
that this House do now adjourn during pleasure.
With regard to the arrangements for the
week, I am afraid the House will have to
sit to-morrow and on Thursday, because it
is very important that we should pass all
Bills by the end of the financial year. I
hope the Housing Bill will be received later
Mr. LESLIE: I am surprised to hear that
it is contemplated we should take the Housing Bill, which is not yet before us, through
its Second Reading to-morrow, because it is
scarcely possible for that Bill to be in the
hands of hon. Senators who reside at a distance before they are called upon to discuss
it in the afternoon; and if it should be that
any Amendment is contemplated, the time is
really not sufficient. It happens that I myself saw the Bill as it now is in another
place, if it has not been altered since this
morning, and I am not myself in any particular difficulty, but there are hon. Senators
who live at a great distance and whose
postal arrangements are such that they could
not receive the Bill, even if posted to-day,
before they leave to-morrow in order to
attend here to-morrow afternoon.
I am not supposed to know what is in the
Housing Bill, but it occurs to one that it is
a matter on which certain discussion might
arise, because if it is a Bill for extending
powers already given, hon. Senators might
like to discuss the use that has already been
made of those powers, and they might like to
ask for information. I am sure the noble
Viscount, the Leader of the House, is not
anxious to press the House unduly, but I
am bound to bring forward the difficulty.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: By leave of the House I would
like to answer what Senator Leslie has said.
I agree with the hon. Senator that it is not
only unusual but undesirable, if it can
possibly be avoided, that a Bill should be
received at a late stage to-day and then be
taken for Second Reading the following day.
Hon. Members know perfectly well that the
last thing the Government desire is to
give this House insufficient time properly to
consider Bills brought before it. I would
like to point out that if this Housing Bill
is not passed by the end of the financial
year it will be a very serious matter. The
only alternative I can suggest-and I understand the Leader of the House is agreeable
to this is that the Second Reading should
be taken on Thursday and the remaining
stages on Friday, but I do not see how it
will be possible to get the Bill through in
time if we give the House any further latitude.
House adjourned during pleasure.

The Senate met at a Quarter after Three

Housing Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In rising to move the Second Reading
of the Housing Bill, I would like respectfully to remind hon. Senators that three
Housing Acts have been passed since this
Government has been in existence. The
result of those three Acts up to date has on
the whole been fairly satisfactory, and,
although not sufficient to justify the assumption that the housing difficulty in Northern
Ireland has been solved, the result was
sufficiently satisfactory to justify the Government in asking Parliament to continue the
subsidy arrangements for a further year.
The effect of the Bill is to extend for a
further year, that is up to the 31st March,
1927, the operation of the Government grants
of £60 and £80, in the case of houses built
up to and including that date, and complying
with the conditions prescribed in the previous
Housing Acts and the Rules made under those
Acts by the Ministry, and at the same time
to continue for a similar period the powers
conferred by those Acts on local authorities
to supplement the Government grants by
making grants from the rates or advancing
loans free of interest, and also by remission
of rates.
As regards the progress that has been made
in the erection of houses, I should like if I
may to give the House some figures showing
what has been done. The figures for the last
two years, that is, since the coming into
operation of the housing policy of the
Government, are as follows:--
The numbers actually completed up to date
are not perhaps a true index of the success
of the Government's policy, as progress in the
initial stages of the working of the Acts was
necessarily slow, and the true effect of the
Government's policy can best be ascertained
by an examination of the present situation,
resulting from the recent 1925 Act passed,
increasing the Government grant from £60 to
£80 in the case of houses completed within
six months from the date of commencement.
For example-during the period from the
1st January, 1925-1st July, 1925, the number
of private builders' houses approved for
erection was 717, while for the following six
months, that is from the 1st July, 1925-
1st January, 1926, the number of private
builders' houses authorised was 1,006-that is
to say an increased rate of progress of about
50 per cent in the second half-year.
During the month of January, 1925, 189
private builders' houses were completed, and
the first week of the present month constituted a record in the progress of completion,
the total number reported as having been
completed during the week being 75. It is
anticipated that this rate of progress will be
more than maintained during the present
year.
The operations of local authorities are
naturally of a more intermittent character
than those of private builders, as the schemes
are formulated in groups of houses, and, consequently, the record of progress is not so
consistent, but progress is being satisfactorily
maintained, and in addition to the houses
already authorised several local authorities
in Northern Ireland have schemes in contemplation.
The policy of the Government, which has
been consistently followed, and which is embodied in the three Housing Acts already
passed, the 1923 Act, the 1921 Act and the
1925 Act, has been to encourage private enterprise, and to introduce as few hampering
restrictions as possible, and at the same time
to work in close co-operation with and offer
similar inducements to local authorities.
This policy, it is claimed, has worked successfully and the present Bill continues it in
every respect. That private enterprise is a
most important factor, is shown by the fact
that up to date the number of houses built
by private builders under our subsidy arrangements has reached over 2,000, and is
twice as great as the number completed by
local authorities.
At the same time I do not wish any hon.
Senator to imagine, nor do I wish it to go
Housing Bill--
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
out from this House, that the Government do
not fully appreciate the efforts made by the
local authorities. The efforts of the local
authorities cannot be in any way disparaged,
because we must bear in mind that
in addition to the operations which they
have actually undertaken by themselves
they have in the most generous fashion cooperated with the Government in encouraging private enterprise in respective areas.
In most areas the local authorities supplement the Government £60 and £89 grants by
additional grants ranging from £25 £40,
and in a considerable number of cases arrangements have been made by the local
authorities to remit the rates for periods
ranging from 5 to 10 years in the case of
new houses being built and coming within
the scope of the Northern Ireland Housing
Acts.
Before sitting down I should like to inform
the House that so far as the present Bill
is concerned, no compulsion is placed on the
local authorities to continue their encouragement and support to building operations in
their respective districts, but enabling
powers are accorded to them, and it is hopefully anticipated that they will renew, if not
actually increase, their efforts to induce
private builders to erect houses for the
working classes in their respective areas.
In the firm belief that this Bill will materially assist in the erection of houses for the
working classes, I commend it to your
favourable consideration.
Mr. LONG: On behalf of the local authorities I should like briefly to support the
Second Reading of this Bill. It is, no doubt,
true, as the noble Viscount has stated, that
considerable progress has been made in the
building of houses, but the want has not
yet been fully met. There is no possible
doubt that an acute housing shortage exists
both in the towns and in the country districts of Northern Ireland. It appears to me
to be inexplicable that, notwithstanding the
very heavy death rate due to the Great War,
and also notwithstanding the fact that there
are almost the same number of houses available in rural Ireland as there were ten years
ago, there exists a very marked shortage of
houses. I should like, therefore, very strongly
to support the proposal to continue the
powers given in this Bill to local authorities
for a further period.
I am sorry I cannot agree that our decision
to permit local authorities to remit rates on
houses has had a very large effect on building. From inquiries I have made I have
elicited the fact that in almost all cases the
houses would have been built without this
concession, and the result of the exemption
from rates has been that the speculative
builder has been able to receive a higher
price from the purchasers of the houses. Of
course, that power was not obligatory on
local authorities, but it was freely exercised,
with very scanty success. On the whole,
I support the Second Reading of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: It is not difficult to support
a Bill with so admirable an object as that
which is now before us, and it must be a
source of satisfaction to us all that the
policy which we have consistently supported
has so far justified itself as to encourage the
Government to come before us to ask for its
extension for another year. The last speaker
mentioned the difficulty as regards exemption
from rates, and it will be remembered that
it was not perhaps altogether without hesitation that this House accepted the proposal
to insert a power to give exemption from
rates in the Measure which came before us
last year. The proposal was adopted, but I
think none of us were very enthusiastic regarding it. We accepted it as an experiment,
and we wished it well, and we even allowed
the exemption to run for ten years, although
some of us thought it would be better in the
first instance to limit the period to five years.
However, in the interests of greater elasticity, and in order not to impose any restriction on local authorities, we accepted the ten
years, and I should like to ask the noble
Viscount (Viscount Massereene) to tell us,
not necessarily to-day, but perhaps on the
Third Reading, whether the power of going
the extreme limit of ten years has been
exercised in many cases. So far as Ι
remember the only instances were furnished by places so far distant as Lisburn,
Limavady, and Newry, and I think these
were places where the housing shortage was
acutely felt.
Mr. DICKSON: And Newtownards.
Mr. LESLIE: Whether in the town of
Newtownards the full period of ten years
was adopted I do not know, but I understand
the hon. Senator (Mr. Dickson) says it was.
I do not think anybody would propose to delay
the operation of this Measure by suggesting
any alteration at present either as regards
rates or any difficulty there may be in any
particular locality. The powers of the
Ministry are, of course, preserved. Everything in existence is preserved under this
Bill, and I am sure it will be received with
the greatest satisfaction.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: As a
worker I desire to support the Second Reading of this Bill. At the same time I am
somewhat disappointed with the result of the
legislation on this subject that has been previously passed by the Government. The progress of the building operations that it has
been sought to encourage has not been very
satisfactory, and to-day the want of proper
housing accommodation amongst the working classes is almost as bad as it was a few
years ago. Whether the inducement offered
is sufficient it is hard to say. I am sure the
previous Housing Acts must have had some
Housing Bill-
24 MARCH 1926
Second Reading.
encouraging effect on private builders, but
unless there is a greater attempt made to
supply the want I am afraid the Government
will be compelled to offer even greater inducements to encourage private builders, and
local authorities to supply the houses that
are required for the working classes.
Considerable controversy is going on on the
question of erecting cheaper houses for the
workers, and I feel that smaller and cheaper
houses is one solution of the problem. The
houses that are being erected at the present
time are let at rents out of the reach of the
poorer classes of workers, and they are the
very people who most require houses. Therefore, I believe that if there were smaller and
cheaper houses erected, and plenty of them,
it would relieve to some extent at least the
unfortunate position the working classes find
themselves in to-day. It has been said that
to put up smaller houses is only to create
slums. That is not the case. You might
have smaller houses occupied by most respectable people, and which would continue
to be occupied by them, provided there was
sufficient air space all round the house.
Slum property is generally property which
is congested in small narrow thoroughfares
where there is no fresh air or sunshine, but
if smaller houses are erected in such a way
that they would get plenty of fresh air and
sunshine I do not think there is any possibility of them falling within the category
of slum property. It would be far better
than having two or three families living in
a two- or three-roomed house. It was recently my unfortunate lot to visit a house
containing only a kitchen, and two rooms on
the second floor, and I found two families
living in it. One can scarcely realise how
bad such a condition of affairs is.
Working class people who have to live
under such conditions must be very liable
to contract the worst and most deadly
diseases. I should imagine that consumption
would be very likely to thrive in such a house
as that, and if the authorities would encourage the erection of a smaller house at
a rent the working classes could pay, and in
a position where plenty of air and sunshine
would be available, it would meet a want
very much felt. The Corporation have been
building nice little houses, although small,
but it is not the poorer people that are
occupying them but rather middle class
people. I am in favour of the Bill and I
hope it will do something further to encourage building and thus help to remove
this terrible housing shortage.
Mr. DORMAN: I think the object of this
Bill will meet with the whole-hearted approval
of every right thinking man in the community, but at the same time I think the
expectations that were formed as a result
of the facilities offered by the Government
to private builders and local authorities have
not been realised, and that even after the
few years these facilities to those engaged in
the building trade have been in existence
the building of houses is still lamentably
slow not only in Belfast and the various urban
areas in Northern Ireland, but also in the
country districts. That being so, it seems to
me that the Government may possibly in the
future be forced to follow the course
necessarily pursued by the Imperial Government with regard to housing in Scotland.
If the supply of houses is to reach a standard
equivalent to the demand the present system
of producing them is not likely to attain it.
and possibly the Government may have to set
up a Housing Commission and undertake the
building of houses for the people collectively.
I hope the Government will not encourage
the erection of two-roomed houses. I speak
with a very wide experience and I can say
without fear of contradiction that the unfortunate spread of consumption in Ireland
has been very largely due to the congestion
in the dwellings of the people. Even in the
city of Belfast circumstances arise where it
is utterly impossible for the head of a working class home to segregate or separate the
members of a family when that dread disease
comes into the house. I have in my view
just now some five or six families, various
members of which are suffering from tuberculosis and where the parents are compelled,
because of the smallness of the home, to
have, in at least three instances, children
who are suffering from tuberculosis sleeping
in the same bed as healthy members of the
family. Although I am not sufficiently well
informed to know personally, I have reason
to believe that very often that dread disease
is contracted from cough spray. From that
point of view, therefore, 1 hope that in the
interests of the health and well-being of the
people the Government will not at all encourage the building of two-roomed houses.
I would also like to say that the Government would, perhaps, have been wisely advised had they given special facilities to
local authorities. It appears to me that the
object of this Bill is to stimulate private
enterprise in this matter of housing in preference to building by local authorities but
I think that if we took a long view of it
we would see that if the figures given by
the noble Viscount of the number of houses
provided by private enterprise and the
number provided by local authorities were
reversed and the vast majority of the houses
had been erected by the local authorities it
would point to a brighter future for the
various urban districts of Northern Ireland,
because every house that has been built by
a local authority has practically been built
at a cheaper rate than houses erected by
private enterprise.
Mr. DICKSON: No, no.
THE SENATE
Housing Bill-

Second Reading.
classes. As Senator Joseph Cunningham has
said, the houses are occupied by middle-class
people. The great majority of the workers
in Belfast are unskilled men earning from
£1 to £2 a week, and I cannot see how it is
possible for a man with those wages to pay
up to 22s. a week for a house. Is there no
means of producing a cheaper house? Can
houses not be erected at a cheaper
rate than they are being built at
the present time? I think there
is a terrible waste of money somewhere,
although I do not know where, and that
houses can be erected more cheaply than they
are being produced to-day.
Mr. DORMAN: I think I am justified in
saying that the statistics collected in Great
Britain go to prove that the kitchen houses
that have been provided there by the local
authorities have been erected at a cheaper
rate than those supplied by private enterprise. But even assuming that houses built
by the local authorities were slightly dearer
than those built by private enterprise, looking to the future one can anticipate that
when the loans that necessarily had to be
raised for the construction of these houses
are repaid the community itself will become
the owner of that particular property and
the rentals will constitute an income to the
common good. Then the urban authorities
in the various districts, in times of stress and
strain or unemployment, may be in a position
to mitigate the sufferings of people by diminishing from week to week the rents of their
dwellings.
If we built seven-eights of the dwellings
of the working classes by private enterprise
the community would be deprived of that
means of relieving suffering, whereas if they
were built for local authorities by direct
labour we could look forward not only to an
income in relief of the rates but to an improvement in the circumstances of the people,
which would make it possible for us to meet
any crisis such as has arisen as an cutcome of
the Great War, when those who had control
of the means of life jumped their prices so
that the community itself had to interfere
and check the profiteering propensities of
these people. I welcome the Bill as I welcome
every Bill calculated to stimulate the production of houses
Mr. BARCLAY: I have great pleasure in
supporting the Second Reading of this Bill,
and I do so for two reasons. The first is
that the people require houses, and the second
is that it will relieve some hundreds of men
who are unemployed at the present time and
are receiving the dole from the Labour Exchange. Owing to the hold-up in the building
of houses that has taken place in the past
we have in the building trade some hundreds
of bricklayers, joiners, plumbers, painters,
and others, who are walking about the streets
idle, and I believe that by this scheme the
building trade will revive to such an extent
that these men will be fully employed in the
near future.
We know very well from what has been
stated to-day that there are a lot of people
crowded together in homes, but I am sorry
to say that so far as I can see from the Bill
before us the houses that are going to be
erected in the future will be the same as
those already put up. They are going to be
too dear and beyond the means of the working classes. We have been told that all the
Housing Acts that have been passed have
been passed for the benefit of the working
Whether they can be produced more
cheaply by private enterprise than by local
authorities I do not know, but I should think
they could be built more cheaply by private
enterprise because of the competition. To
my mind local authorities run with too loose
a rein. They know the money is coming
from a broad board, and they do not get the
production the private builder gets for the
same wages. Therefore, all the additional
money that is paid in wages and for material
is paid by the poor man who occupies the
house. He is a working man and he is the
man who pays the piper, and we want to do
something to ease his burden. I do not
agree with the proposal to build smaller
houses. I say houses should be large, because
a small house is not suitable for a mixed
family. We know that a great many families
are living in a two-roomed house. It is
all right for a few years at the beginning,
but those boys and girls grow up into manhood and womanhood and parents who have
any regard for the moral character of their
children will try to find a larger house for
them to live in.
This cannot be done if the cost of these
houses is going to be so great, because the
family is not earning very much money.
Say there are two or three boys, and their
father puts them to a trade. They have got
to serve anything from five to seven years,
during which time they may get from 8s. a
week to 19s a week in their last year. A
man cannot afford to go into a very expensive house when he has two or three boys
bringing in that money. As for the girls,
they bring in very little, so that the father
is not in a position to pay the rents at
present demanded. Therefore, I would like
the Government to take some steps to build
houses, larger houses if possible, that can
be let at a less rent than asked for to-day.
I have great pleasure in supporting the Bill.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I am pleased to note that on the whole the
House has given this Bill a satisfactory reception. With regard to Senator Leslie's
question as to the number of local authorities who have remitted rates for the full
Loans Guarantee Bill-
Second Reading.
24 MARCH 1926
period of ten years, I have before me a list
of those authorities. They are:-Urban:
Armagh, Lisburn, Omagh Warrenpoint;
Rural: Banbridge, Newry No. 1, Newry No.
2, Tandragee, and Newtownards. Senator
Joseph Cunningham referred to the necessity
for cheaper houses, and this was also advocated by Senator Barclay. If I may, I should
like to congratulate Senator Barclay on his
very able maiden speech to which this House
listened with very great pleasure. I sincerely
trust that we shall often hear the hon.
Senator in the future on questions of which
he has a knowledge.
As hon. Senators are probably aware, the
size of the house may be reduced from 600
superficial feet, which was the minimum
under the 1923 Act, to 550 superficial feet,
and under the 1925 Act that could be reduced
in rural districts to 500 superficial feet. I
think we are all agreed that that is the very
minimum size any house ought to be. We
all desire to see cheap houses provided for
the working classes, but we must all realise
that it is not an easy matter. The cost of
materials and everything else has gone up,
and in many cases the wages of the people
who occupy the houses have not increased
commensurate with the increased cost of the
rents. The Government are fully aware of
the shortage of houses, and the Ministry of
Home Affairs is always willing to consider
any scheme for the production of houses on a
cheaper scale than at present.
Senator Dorman is of the opinion that
more houses should be built by the local
authorities, but I am afraid I cannot agree
with him in the reasons he gives for that
policy. They are totally contrary to what I
hold and believe. If in any way the Government can produce cheaper houses, I can
assure this House they are only too anxious
and ready to do so.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be now
read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg to move
the Second Reading of the Loans Guarantee
Bill. It will be within the recollection of
This House that an Act was passed in 1922
to enable the Ministry of Finance to guarantee certain loans, provided that the aggregate capital amount did not exceed two
million pounds. Subsequent legislation has
extended the period during which the
original Act was operative, and has also increased the total amount of the loans which
could be guaranteed, so that at the present
moment the Ministry of Finance is empowered to guarantee the principal or interest of loans not exceeding in all six million
pounds. This power lapses at the end of the
current financial year.
Hon. Members will no doubt have seen the
returns presented from time to time by the
Ministry of Finance, and showing the extent
to which its powers in this respect have been
exercised. It will not be out of place, however, for me to mention that up to the end
of last month the total amount guaranteed
was more than four million pounds. Thus,
there is slightly less than two million pounds
still available. It has been considered desirable, however, to ask Parliament not only
to extend for another year the period during
which these powers may be exercised, but also
to increase the existing margin to three
million pounds on account of the possibility
of a large shipbuilding programme being
adopted in the coming year. (Hon. Members:
hear, hear.) The tendency is for shipowners
to depend more and more upon the
financial facilities afforded by the machinery
of the British Trades Facilities Act and the
Northern Ireland Loans Guarantee Act, and
the conditions prevailing in our own shipbuilding industry are such that we cannot
contemplate the withdrawal of the assistance
afforded by these guarantees so long as the
use of such credit is of some assistance in
countering the other forms of State assistance
rendered in competing countries.
I am sure this House will agree with
me that the use of this machinery in the shipbuilding industry has had a considerable
effect in keeping down unemployment. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) During the last few
months more than eight thousand men, on an
average, have been employed at ships, the
construction of which is being financed under
State guarantees, and if shipowners can see
their way to place further orders with our
shipbuilding firms through the help of the
Loans Guarantee machinery, Parliament will,
I feel sure, support the Government in its
desire to help towards this end.
Turning to Section 2, I should like to explain shortly the actual significance of the
proposals which are made there.
A new
feature is now being introduced for the first
time, and Section 2 contemplates the payment
of a small amount from Government funds
towards the interest chargeable upon
Northern Ireland Guarantee Loans. Up to
the present time, Government assistance has
taken the form of a guarantee only, and we
believe and hope that the issue of these guarantees will not involve the State in the loss
of a single penny.
The greater part of the sum of four million
pounds which has already been raised under
C
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
[Viscount Charlemont]
the Loans Guarantee Acts has been obtained
in conjunction with loans guaranteed by the
British Government and applicable to that
part of the expenditure on a ship which represents raw material manufactured in Great
Britain. I am glad to be able to inform the
House that in each case a Northern Ireland
guaranteed loan has been raised by the
company concerned on terms identical with
those governing the British guaranteed loans.
I am sure this House will agree with me that
it is a matter of considerable gratification to
us that Northern Ireland credit should stand
so high as it does, yet we must contemplate
the possibility of our guarantees being very
slightly less valuable than British guarantees,
for British credit, as you know, is second to
none throughout the world. If such circumstances should arise, a company borrowing
money on our guarantee would be obliged to
pay a slightly higher rate of interest than
would be necessary on a loan guaranteed by
the British Government. As it is considered
desirable that a shipowner, for instance, who
wishes to build a ship in Northern Ireland
under a Northern Ireland guarantee should
not have to pay a higher price for his money
than if his order had been placed in Great
Britain, provision has been made in Section
2 of the Bill before the House to give discretion within narrow limits to the Ministry
of Finance to contribute towards the interest
charges, if necessary, in order to assist in
bridging any gap which might conceivably
occur between the interests charged in
this country and in Great Britain. I
have no doubt that hon. Members will
appreciate the importance of this proposal,
and I do not think, therefore, that it is
necessary for me to dwell on the matter at
any great length. I beg to move.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am very
pleased to hear the report that the Loans
Guarantee Act has been so successful in the
North of Ireland. There was nothing pleased
me more than to hear the noble Leader of
the House state that the increase is due
principally to an extended shipbuilding programme in the near future. I am very
pleased, indeed, to hear that statement from
the noble Leader, and I trust that the increased amount will not be sufficient and that
the Government will be compelled to apply
for a further increase in the amount at their
disposal to assist in this shipping scheme
which has been referred to.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
Message from the Commons,
That they have passed the Administrative
Provisions Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
Administrative Provisions Bill.
and
Moved: "That this Bill be now read a
First time."-(Viscount Massereene
Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Moved: "That this House do now adjourn."
-(Viscount Charlemont).
