Business of the House. 70
Business of the House.
25 MARCH 1926
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 25th March, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Business of the House.
AND FER-
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE
RARD: I beg to move, That Standing Order
No. XXV be dispensed with for the purpose
of passing the Bills set out on the Paper,
and the Consolidated Fund Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure there is nobody
here who would desire in any way to obstruct
the course of public business, but when we
are presented with such a proposal as that
which the noble Viscount has just brought
forward I think it is incumbent on us to
examine for a few moments what it is that
that proposal really means and to what it
is that this House will be committing itself
if it agrees to the Motion. The Bills to
which the proposal of the noble Viscount
relate are set out in the Paper. There are
three of them in existence at the moment,
and I presume that the birth of a fourth
may shortly be expected.
The Housing Bill, which heads the list,
was read a Second time in this House yesterday, and I think it must be apparent to those
who followed the course of the debate that
it is at least possible that some hon. Senator
might desire to move some Amendment dealing perhaps with some of the subjects touched
upon in the debate. But a large proportion
of the Members of the Senate are entirely
debarred from moving any Amendment because they have not seen the Bill, and the
postal arrangements in the country are such
that it would be impossible either for them
to receive a copy of the Bill read a Second
time yesterday, or even to receive notice that
the Committee stage of that Measure was to
be taken this afternoon. Again, if by any
extraordinary activity on their part they
obtained possession of a copy of the Bill,
and did desire to move an Amendment, they
could not get the Amendment on the Paper
in time for it to be circulated and considered
by other Members of the Senate.
There is, I am afraid, a popular impression
that the sittings of this House are not of
very great value and that the Members of
this House have too little to do. It is. I
think, forgotten that the work of Members
of Parliament is not by any means confined
to attendance in the House itself. A great
deal of it has to be done in the Library
or in those places where it is possible to
consult other people. The actual sitting of
the House is not necessarily lengthy, and it
therefore follows that the extension of a day
or so which we are sometimes given is of
but little advantage. What we really want
is two or three days between the stages of
Bills in order that there may be time to
consult books of reference, to think out
Amendments, to consult anyone we may
desire to consult, and to put down notices
on the Paper in reasonable time for consideration by others before discussion takes place.
This House is always tolerant when appeals
for rapidity are made by the Government
towards the close of the financial year or the
close of a Session. The financial year closes
with unvarying regularity on the 31st March,
and the occurrence of the 31st March is one
which can be foreseen for a very considerable
period. There are various methods of obtaining more time for this House, but it is,
perhaps, not for us to go into them in detail.
We know, and we must admit, that the last
Session of Parliament was protracted almost
up to Christmas and, therefore, it cannot
reasonably be expected that Parliament
should re-assemble without an interval of
three months. I do not think we ought to
complain very much about that.
Passing to another of the Bills, the Administrative Provisions Bill, that is necessitated, as I think we have already been told,
owing to arrangements which were not made
until the month of December and the preparation of such a Measure would
necessarily occupy time. But I cannot but
think it is unfortunate that so complicated
a Measure should be presented to us for
complete discussion within a period of
forty-eight hours and without the possibility, to which I have alluded, of hon.
Senators in different parts of the Province
having an opportunity of becoming acquainted with its various Clauses. I do not
wish on this occasion to do more than I
have done and I do not think any Member
of this Senate would desire to press opposition to the proposal of the noble Viscount
to any greater length, but I do venture to
hope that every conceivable method will
be adopted whereby in future this House
may be given reasonable time to consider
Measures which are to be laid before us.
I will not oppose the Motion.
Mr LONG: With regard to the Administrative Provisions Bill, I should have
liked more time to consider it, and I agree
with Senator Leslie that it is very desirable
the Senate should be afforded more time
to consider Bills that come before it. I
know there are certain features of the
Diseases of Animals Act which do not seem
to me to operate well, but I cannot give
the House figures without consultation with
the local authorities in County Londonderry
Business of the House.
Housing Bill.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
and it would require some time to obtain
those figures. I will not go into details-
Mr. SPEAKER: Will the hon. Member
speak up?
Mr. LONG-but I feel that some good
would ensue if further time was given for
the consideration of this Measure, and I
support Senator Leslie in his request.
Mr. DORMAN: I desire to support the
hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) in his appeal.
In looking over this Bill I find it contains
nine pages of printed matter and two closely
printed pages of Schedule, and it was only
placed in my hands when I came here. As
I have done on previous occasions, I desire
to offer my conscientious objection to being
asked to vote on any Measure which I have
not had an opportunity of reading. How
is a layman like myself, a man unacquainted with Parliamentary language, who
must necessarily devote a very long time
to comparing the different Sections of the
Bill before he can grasp the meaning of it,
to be expected to exercise his right of conscientiously voting on such a Measure in
the time that is allowed? I hope the
Government will see its way to make such
arrangements as Senator Leslie has appealed
for so that Members of this House will be
given a reasonable opportunity to devote
some study to the Bills they are called
upon to vote on in this House from time to
time.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Neither I nor the noble Viscount,
the Leader of the House desire unduly to
ask this House to pass Bills which they have
not had sufficient time to properly consider.
The protest in the speech of Senator Leslie
was a very forcible one and one which I
am sure will be very seriously considered
by the Government. This Session has been
a somewhat short one, but it has been
crowded with legislation and it is unfortunate that owing to the exigencies of
time this House had been asked to pass
a lot of legislation in a very short time. I
can assure hon. Members that the Members
of the Government who sit in this Chamber
will make strong representations to the
Government on this matter, and I trust
that the complaint hon. Senators have made,
a right and just complaint, will never have
to be made again. (Hon Members: Hear,
hear.).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill Presented.
ANCIENT MONUMENTS BILL(S),
"to amend and extend the law relating to
Ancient Monuments, and to make provision
for the preservation of objects of archeological interest; and for other purposes in
connection therewith, was presented by
Viscount Massereene and Ferrard, read a
First time, and ordered to be read a Second
time and to be printed. (Bill No. 3).
Housing Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
House in Committee accordingly.
(The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.)
CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of s. 1 of 13 and
14 Geo. 5, c. 30, as to conditions for grants
in respect of construction of houses, 14 and
15 Geo. 5, c 28, 15 and 16 Geo. 5, c. 13),
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Short Title and Construction)
Mr. LESLIE: I only rise for a moment
before we part with Clause 2 to express my
thanks to the noble Viscount (Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard) for the intimation
he was good enough to give us yesterday as
regards the extent to which the power of
exemption from rates has been used by the
various councils throughout Northern lreland. The figures given us were of great
interest, and I am sure we will study and
consider them with a view to our guidance
as regards any action that may be taken
next year if it should be that a similar Bill
is again presented to us.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported without Amendment to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.
Moved: That this Report be now received.
--(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)

Loans Guarantee Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said Bill.---
(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time.-(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.--(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House of

agree to the said Bill without any Amendment.
Administrative Provisions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): In moving the Second Reading
of this Bill I should like to state that it is
a necessary complement to the Boundary
settlement which was made last December
involving, inter alia, the transfer from the
Imperial Government to the Government of
Northern Ireland of the functions known
as Council of Ireland Services. It is to
complete the distribution of these Services
amongst the appropriate Northern Ireland
Departments, to make the necessary adaptations in the law and incidentally to simplify
and clarify the existing law in some respects
that this Bill is now introduced. In order
that Departments may possess the full
statutory powers on the 1st April, being the
date of transfer settled by the Ireland (Confirmation of Agreement) Act, 1925, it is
essential that the present Measure be passed
before that date, and inasmuch as it is in
no sense controversial I hope that the House
will give it an easy passage and provide
every facility.
The Services which will be transferred are
those relating to Railways and Fisheries
and Contagious Diseases of Animals. Under
Section 10, sub-section 2, of the Government
of Ireland Act, 1920, it was provided that
with a view to uniform administration
throughout Ireland of these public Services
the powers relating thereto were to be exercised by a Council of Ireland, comprising
40 persons representing the Senate and the
House of Commons of Northern Ireland and
Southern Ireland. In the circumstances
which arose by reason of the setting up of
the Irish Free State this Council was impossible of attainment and as a temporary
measure by an Order in Council under the
Government of Ireland Act these Services
were retained under Imperial control, that
is under the Secretary of State of the Home
Office. In the fresh set of circumstances
that have now arisen these powers are passing to the Government of Northern Ireland
and in lieu the agreement of December last
provides that "The Governments of the Irish
Free State and of Northern Ireland shall
meet together, as and when necessary, for
the purpose of considering matters of
common interest arising out of, or connected
with, the exercise and administration of the
said powers." In my view this provision
is one which will be found of great practical
value in the future, and the parallel legislation dealing with agricultural matters,
which even before this Agreement had been
passed by this House and by the Free State
Parliament, indicates clearly that co-operation in this particular direction is necessary.
Turning now to the general provisions of
the Bill, the most important from a technical
point of view is the Schedule. This Schedule
operates in the same way as the various
Adaptation Orders made by His Majesty and
the Privy Council to give effect to various
provisions of the Government of Ireland Act.
It would have been possible to arrange for
similar Orders in Council being issued in
connection with the transfer of these Services without the intervention of this House,
but it seemed more proper that, inasmuch
as this legislative assembly has for some
years past been in full working, and is in a
position to deal more satisfactorily and fully
with adaptations of its own law, it should
itself, by means of this enactment, make the
necessary consequential changes in the law.
This arrangement has been effected with the
full agreement of His Majesty's Government.
In the body of the Bill are provisions
which go further than adaptation. They are
mainly necessary in order to bring the administrative and financial provisions of the
existing Acts into line with changes in the
law that have already been made by this
House. I may, for example, instance the
Government Loans Fund, which was established by our Government Loans and Exchequer Provisions Act of last Session.
Power is taken in the present Bill to enable
loans for Fishery and the other Services
covered by the Bill to be made from this
Fund instead of, as has been done in the
past, from the Endowment Fund of the
Department of Agriculture or from voted
moneys. Similarly the whole section of the
Bill headed "Contagious Diseases of Animals"
presents in a clear and easily understood
75 Administrative Provisions Bill-
THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Viscount Charlemont]
form the financial provisions of the various
Diseases of Animals Acts.
Briefly, it may be stated that these diseases
fall into two categories, and these categories
correspond to two separate accounts within
the Cattle Diseases Fund. One class of disease
is that relating to cattle pleuro-pneumonia,
foot and mouth disease and swine fever.
Generally speaking, the responsibility for
paying compensation in respect of destruction of cattle suffering from these diseases
is regarded as a State liability. On the
other hand all other cattle diseases are regarded as matters of local interest, and all
the expenses of the State are met by means
of a special rate struck by each county and
borough council, the proceeds being paid
over to the Cattle Diseases Fund. Disbursements are made thereout by the State and
one half of the expenditure of local authorities is itself met from the fund. There is,
however, one disease which recent legislation regards as more in the nature of a
State liability, that is to say Cattle Tuberculosis, and in regard to this provision is
made by which the entire cost of compensation for cattle slaughtered in such instances
is met from voted moneys. I think hon. Members will agree that in view of the prevalence
of tuberculosis it is only proper that this
should be a State charge. It has, in fact,
been so since last year as a result of an
Act passed in the British House of Commons,
which extended to Northern Ireland.
The only other matter which I think calls
for comment in the Bill is the provision in
Sections 7 and 8. This enables, if agreement
is reached with the Free State by means of
one of those discussions which the December
agreement mentions, licences for rod-fishing
to be available in either area without any
extra payment, and also provides for the
apportionment of property between Boards
of Conservators. This latter provision is
necessary by reason of the fact that within
the last few months some of the inland fishing districts which operate on both sides of
the Border have been re-arranged by agreement between the Irish Free State and the
British Government, in crder that each
Board of Conservators may be completely
within the jurisdiction of one Government.
Full effect has not yet been given to this
change, and the clause in the Bill provides
for necessary facilities for completing such
arrangements. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I venture to think it will
not be wholly out of place if I preface the
few remarks I am going to make about the
Bill before us by tendering congratulations
to the noble Viscount, the Leader of the
House, on the singular lucidity with which
he has explained to us the provisions of the
first important and complicated Bill it has

fallen to him to handle. If I may say so,
he drew attention to all the outstanding points
of the Measure, and his doing so will be an
assistance to those who have not had as
much time to devote to the study of the Bill
as could have been wished.
I complained a few minutes ago of the
celerity with which this House was expected to deal with this Measure, and I
should like now to congratulate those responsible on the celerity with which they
have produced and laid before this House
a revised print of the Bill as introduced and
amended in another place about four or five
o'clock yesterday afternoon. It shows that
our machinery has attained a high degree of
efficiency.
The Bill itself, as I understand it, contains practically no new provisions. There
are two matters to which I propose to direct
attention in Committee at a later stage, and
one of them has already been alluded to by
the noble Viscount, the Leader of the House.
Taking it as a whole, the Bill seems to me
to be a consolidation and re-enactment of
the sixteen existing statutes to which reference is made, and I think the claim made
in the Memorandum attached to the Bill
that something in the way of clarification
has been done has been fully made out.
There are important financial provisions in
the Bill. I think we are entitled to take it
that the somewhat complicated Second Clause
has been fully debated and considered in
another place, and that therefore we need
not concern ourselves with it.
I imagine that the dual effect of that
clause is to bring the loans which are now
to be made, or may be made, under the
authority of this Bill instead of under the
authority of former Bills, and to bring those
under the Schedule to the Government
Loans and Exchequer Provisions Act of last
Session. I do not anticipate for a moment
that any difficulty will arise as to that, and
I venture to say that the references to the
various Acts of Parliament and other enactments which are made in the Bill can be
followed if time is given for that purpose.
With the possible exceptions to which I
alluded just now there 18 nothing in
the Bill which can possibly cause objection
to be taken, and it is certainly an advantage, as we said when we were discussing
the terms of the Boundary Settlement, that
the important matters dealt with should be
in our own hands.
The suggestion made as to the form in which
assistance should be given towards compensation for cattle slaughtered on account of
tuberculosis infection is one which I am
sure will commend itself very much to the
country, for I think the country is very
much perturbed by the prevalence of tuberculosis. Indeed the matter was referred to
77 Administrative Provisions Bill-

25 MARCH 1926
Committee.
yesterday in connection with housing, and I
believe the opinion is gaining ground that
the spread of human tuberculosis is due to
infection from cows. It is not a matter on
which it is possible for a layman to speak
with any certainty, but it is a matter which
is engaging the attention of our local sanitary authorities, and I think the measures
proposed in this Bill will be of assistance
to them. Taking the Bill as a whole I have
pleasure in supporting it.
Mr. LONG: With regard to the Contagious
Diseases part of the Bill, I regret that
owing to the farce of having to proceed with
all stages of the Bill in one day, I am not
able to give the House any figures regarding
the working of the Diseases of Animals Act
in the past in the County of Londonderry,
but from recollection I do not think it was
at all satisfactory. Suppose a farmer in a
remote district reported that a cow of his
was suspected of tuberculosis. The veterinary inspector was sent, possibly a distance
of twenty miles, to make a test, and he had
to go back three or four times at certain
intervals. The result was that the compensation given for this animal when it was
found to be suffering from fuberculosis was
very much less than the veterinary inspector received for his attention to it.
That has been a glaring anomaly in the
County of Londonderry for a number of
years and the result has been that farmers
have concealed the existence of tuberculosis, and cases of suspected tuberculosis
in their herds for the reason that they
think they have been inadequately compensated, and I think so too. As a rule these
cattle are emaciated and the value put on
them by those who assess the value is very
small. In practice these men find that
they can sell such an animal to a low class
butcher at a much higher price than they
receive in compensation.
I think the object of the Government
should be to offer every inducement to the
farmer to stamp out this disease, which I
believe is liable to be communicated to
human beings. I hope the Government will
explore that part of this Bill with the
object of seeing if it is possible to amend
the Diseases of Animals Act so that the
people who own these infected animals will
be induced to report them to the authorities.
I think
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
Senator Long can be assured the Government will make every possible research into
this question, and possibly in the future
they will bring in legislation to produce
the result he desires.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.
-(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair].
CLAUSES 1 (Exercise of executive powers
of Council of Ireland), 2 (Loans for purposes of railways, fisheries and Diseases of
Animals Acts), 3 (Saving for powers as to
transit of animals and produce), 4 (Amendment of 13 & 14 Geo. 5, c. 31, s. 10 as to railway
and harbour charges), 5 (Grants for purposes of light railways), and 6 (Saving for
Railway and Canal Commission), ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Apportionment of property of
fishery conservators).
Mr. LESLIE: For the convenience of
debate I beg to move that Clause 7 be left
out of the Bill. This is the Clause to which
the noble Leader of the House drew attention
as being the Clause required to enable a
convenient apportionment of the property
and liabilities of the different Boards of
Conservators. It seems to me that the Clause
bristles with possible difficulties, and it also
seems to me that this is probably a Clause
which requires rather careful consideration.
I confess I am in some doubt as to whether
it would not be better for the Committee
to omit this Clause from the Bill for the
present and to leave the matter with which
it deals to be dealt with by a separate
Measure when the opportunity and the occasion occur.
If the Committee will read the clause they
cannot, I think, avoid the conclusion that we
are proposing to deal with property and with
liabilities of the extent of which we know
nothing. We have not at the moment any
intimation before us either as to the number
of Boards of Conservators in question or as
to the property and the liabilities they may
bring in. We are proposing in effect to make
our Minister of Commerce-and I confess I
know nobody to whom I would rather give
such an appointment-a plenipotentiary to
deal, in consultation with somebody appointed from another part of Ireland, with
the property of other persons and other
bodies.
In saying that, we must not forget that the
property to which I have referred is not
personal property in any way. There is no
question here of depriving any man of his
goods. Any property to which this clause
can relate can only be property which is
held by a public body in trust, whether expressed or implied, for certain public purposes, but any such property would have been
acquired presumably by the trustees out of
some rate or other, and the people who paid
that rate would perhaps seem entitled to be
consulted. There is no provision I can find
Administrative Provisions Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
here for consulting Boards of Conservators
themselves. One presumes the Minister would
do that before going into a meeting with the
Free State representative, but there is
nothing definitely in the Bill which requires
that that shall be done,
I do not know whether the noble Viscount
will be able to give us any indication that
this clause has been submitted to and adopted
in principle by some of the Boards of Conservators to whom it relates. If so, it would
go a long way to destroy any serious objection. It may well be that the noble Viscount
will suggest to us that it would be more convenient, and would better serve the main
purpose of the Bill, which deals with
the allocation of services between the
different parts of Ireland and to their
respective Departments, if the Committee
allowed the clause to stand for the present,
well knowing that circumstances may arise
in the future when those concerned in the
execution of the clause will require the
Government to come to Parliament again
for further powers or possibly for an amendment of the clause.
If that view should be taken I am sure
none of us will wish to cut the clause out
just for the sake of preventing a danger
arising from possibilities which are, perhaps,
rather remote. But as it stands at present
I must say the clause does not give one the
complete satisfaction that property and
duties are being fully protected and that the
jurisdiction of the ordinary Courts is not
being ousted. Mention of the ordinary
Courts brings before us the extreme difficulty
of dealing with any matter of this kind,
because it might very well be that a Board
concerned in this matter would be situated in
the Irish Free State and an action might
arise in their Courts, while Boards in Northern Ireland would necessarily have resort to
the Courts here, and very great difficulty
would arise. But we look forward to the
Bill, and the Treaty as a whole, as a means of
avoiding such an impracticable situation as
I have alluded to.
For the moment, therefore, I beg to move
that Clause 7 be omitted.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I think that
the contingency mentioned by hon. Senator
Leslie is a remote one, and for that reason
and for others I am afraid I cannot accept
the Amendment. We are, of course, legislating for the future, and it is impossible
to know precisely what difficulties may
occur. If there was any question of
any of the Boards mentioned suffering
detriment the Ministry of Commerce would
undoubtedly consult with them before action
was taken. So I would prefer the Amendment to be withdrawn for these reasons.
Mr. LESLIE: I think it would be the wish
of the Committee that the Amendment should
be withdrawn, as the noble Viscount suggests, as the matter has been considered and
discussed. If it does arise in the future it
can be reviewed. I ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 8 (Power to charge reduced
duties for licences to fish in certain cases),
9 (Grants in aid of fisheries), 10 (Establishment of new General Cattle Diseases Fund),
11 (Payments into General Account of the
Fund), 12 (Payments out of General Account
of the Fund), 13 (Payments into and out of
the Special Account of the Fund), 14 (Repeal
of enactments of 57 and 58 Vict., c. 57, 4 and
5 Geo. 5, c. 40, and 15 and 16 Geo. 5, c. 63),
15 (Short Title and Commencement), ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
SCHEDULE—
5. The Ministry of Commerce and the
Railway and Canal Commission shall consult the Ministry of Labour before making
any Order under the Railway Regulation
Act, 1893.
*
Mr. LESLIE: A question arises in Part II
of the Schedule, paragraph 5, at the top of
page 11. This, again, is a question which I
think might engage our attention for a few
moments before we part from the Schedule.
The paragraph requires--definitely requires
that the Ministry of Commerce and the Railway and Canal Commission shall consult the
Ministry of Labour before making any Order
under the Railway Regulation Act, 1893. The
Motion which I propose is again more for
the convenience of debate than for anything
else. The Motion is that the words, "and
the Railway and Canal Commission" be
left out. The position is that the Ministry
of Commerce as the successors, I think, of
the Ministry of Transport-who were themselves the successors of the Board of Trade
-are already required by the Act of 1893
which followed a long series of Acts which
need not be enumerated--already required to
take certain action when a complaint is
made before them. The complaint to
which the Act of 1893 was directed was a
complaint from railway employees that their
hours of work were too long, that they
were not given Sundays off, that the intervals between periods of labour was insufficient, and to meet that Parliament,
following, as I say, a series of older enactments in 1893 provided that the Board of
Trade on receiving such complaint should
be empowered to make inquiries and should
have power to order the companies concerned to furnish replies to such questions
81 Administrative Provisions Bill-

25 MARCH 1926
Committee.
as they might ask, and they should then
bring the question before the Railway and
Canal Commission before which the Board
of Trade was entitled to appear.
It appears then from that that the action
of the Railway and Canal Commission when
dealing with these matters is somewhat of
a judicial character-judicial I should be
inclined to think subject to any further
advice which might be given, rather than
administrative. And I confess I am not fond
of requiring a judicial body to consult anyone.
A judicial body may and should hear anybody representing parties concerned in the
matter. But when it comes to consultation
that is a somewhat different matter.
And again, although we can undoubtedly
properly require by statute that the
Ministry of Commerce shall do this or that,
I have some slight doubt as to whether this
Parliament is empowered to require the
Railway and Canal Commissioners to do
anything. That is a point, I understand,
which the Government has looked into, and
on which they will be in a position to advise
us in a few moments.
I feel just now that we have that double
difficulty of requiring a body which is at all
events a semi-judicial body to consult, and
also of imposing the will of Parliament on
the Railway and Canal Commission, a Commission which we did not appoint and which
we do not pay, and as to which I think the
Parliament of Northern Ireland has not yet
made provision, although, I understand, by
some adaptation orders there is power to do
something, but at all events that something
has not been done yet. I therefore suggest
that we should for the moment have in our
minds the proposal to leave out the reference to the Railway and Canal Commission.
If the Minister of Labour wishes to be consulted by the Minister of Commerce, I have
not the least objection. It undoubtedly concerns his Department rather more than any
other. But my difficulty is as to whether
we have power to make this requirement
and impose it on the Railway and Canal
Commission. I beg to move.
The hon.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
Senator has raised a very difficult legal
point, but I am informed by the Attorney-
General that the Government of Northern
Ireland has power over the Railway and
Canal Commission to the required extent,
and can order them if they so desire, which
is, I think, somewhat unlikely.
Mr. LESLIE: I think now that we have
been given in a definite statement information from so eminent an authority as the
Attorney-General, there is nothing more to
be said. The Committee, I am sure, will be
willing that the Amendment should be withdrawn, the more so as the question now-adays is not particularly likely to arise. The
main point is we have definite information
that we can do this and that we shall not
be legislating beyond our powers.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: That is so.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Schedule agreed to.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported without
Amendment to the House.

Bann Drainage Scheme.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the report relative to the Bann Drainage, re
ferred to by the Minister of Finance, when
speaking on the 21st October last in another
place, is now in the hands of the Government,
and if so, whether it is intended to present
that Report to Parliament.
Mr. LESLIE: The question which stands
in my name is one that has been suggested
to me by a Chamber of Commerce operating
in a Northern locality, in the County of
Antrim, and I may say at once that I am
asking the question not with any view of
precipitating a general debate on a very important subject, but rather bona fide to
obtain information as to the present position
of a matter which has been occupying attention for some considerable time. It seems
to me that the time has perhaps come when
the Government may not unreasonably be
asked to report progress as to the position
of the scheme for improving the drainage
of the Bann.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of the
House): I am informed that this Report
has been received. Having regard to the
importance and difficulty of the question my
hon. Friend will appreciate that the Report
needs careful consideration, and this it is
receiving. It is not in a form suitable for
publication in its entirety, but, without
making any promise at the moment, the
Government will consider how far, consis-
89 Local Bills-Standing Orders.
27 APRIL 1926
Local Bills-Standing Orders. 90
Amendment made.
tent with the public interest, it will be possible to make public later the more important
parts of the Report.
Standing Orders Relative to
Local Bills.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Amendments which
appear on the Paper to Standing Orders
relative to Local Bills I would like to inform the House that there are three slight
alterations to be made to the draft Amendments as they appear on the Paper in the
hands of hon. Members.
Order No. 38 (Deposit of Bills at Public
Departments).
On or before the twenty-first day of
December a printed copy shall be deposited:
(1) of every Local Bill, at the Ministries
of Finance, Home Affairs, Labour,
Education, Agriculture, and Commerce in
Belfast.
(2) of every Local Bill at the Office of His
Majesty's Treasury, at the General Post
Office, at the Home Office, at the Irish
Office, so long as it shall continue to exist,
and at the Office of Woods in London;
(3) of every local Bill affecting any dock,
harbour navigation, pier or port, foreshore
or tidal lands, at the Office of the Board of
Trade in London.
(4) of every Local Bill affecting any railway at the Ministry of Transport in
London.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Amendment to Standing Order 38 (2), I want to point out that in
the second line of the Amendment as it
appears on the Order Paper, the words "in
London" should be omitted. The Amendment will then read:-
Order No. 38 (2), leave out "at the Home
Office, at the Irish Office, so long as it shall
continue to exist, and."
After "Woods" insert at the War Office and
in duplicate at the Home Office.
This Standing Order merely enumerates
the offices in London at which the promoters
of Local Bills are required to deposit copies
of their Bills. The effect of this Amendment
is to take out the Irish Office, which no longer
exists, and to add the War Office, whose
interests might be affected. It also requires
the promoters to lodge a second copy at the
Home Office in place of the copy lodged with
the Imperial Secretary's Office, which has
ceased to exist. I beg to move.
Order No. 38 (2), leave out "at the Home
Office, at the Irish Office, so long as it shall
continue to exist, and"
After "Woods" insert "at the War Office
and in duplicate at the Home Office."
Order No. 43 (Estimates to be deposited
at the Ministry of Home Affairs).
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In the new Order which it is proposed to insert after Order No. 43 there is a
slight drafting Amendment needed in the
copy of the new Order before the House. In
line 2 of the new Order, after the letter (c)
there should be inserted the words, "proposed to be carried out." The first paragraph would then read:-
On or before the Thirty-first day of
December, copies of the estimate of expense
of any work or purpose described in the
appendix marked (c) proposed to be
carried out by any Municipal Corporation,"
etc.
The object of this Amendment, together
with the Appendix which follows it, is to
require very much more detailed estimates
from local authorities and Joint Boards of
permanent works proposed to be executed
under a Local Bill. The information which
these estimates would give is essential to the
Ministry, whose assent is required for the
raising of loans. I may also mention that
this Standing Order has recently been
included in the Standing Orders at Westminster, on which our procedure is modelled.
Before formally moving this Amendment I
ought to explain exactly what "other local
authority" in the first paragraph of this New
Order means. Other local authorities would
refer to Harbour Comissioners, Canal Commissioners, and any authority connected with
road transport or ferries. A road authority
is covered by Order 42 which just precedes
this Order and which says:-
On or before the thirty-first day of
December copies of the estimate for any
works proposed to be authorised by any
Bill in relation to harbours, canals, waterways and inland navigation shall be
deposited at the Ministry of Commerce.
I may also mention to the House that a
local authority referred to in this Order
does not comprise a County Council. I say
this because I understand an hon. Member,
who takes a great interest in every matter
that comes before this House, has drawn my
attention to the matter, and I thought it
better to explain it before the question was
raised. I beg to move, after Order No. 43,
to insert the new Order as set out on the
Paper.

On or before the thirty-first day of

the appendix marked (C) by any municipal corporation, district council, joint
board or joint committee, or other local
authority, shall be deposited at the office
of the Clerk of the Parliaments, the
Ministry of Finance, and the Ministry of

The estimates shall be in the form set
forth in the appendix marked (C), or as
near thereto as circumstances may permit,
and no other estimates shall be required
from the promoters.
If any moneys are required to be borrowed to meet any excess of expenditure
previously authorised by Parliament, or
any Government Department, there shall
be deposited with the said estimates a
statement for the purposes and reasons for
the borrowing.
Together with the said estimates there
shall be deposited a statement showing the
full patriculars with respect to the district
of the local authority, that is to say: (a)
area of the district; (b) population according to the last census; (c) rateable and
assessable value according to the last
valuation list; (d) rates made in the
district during the last preceding financial
year; (e) the sum of the balances of outstanding loans contracted by the local
authority; and (f) the amount of the outstanding loans to which the limitation of
Section 238 of the Public Health (Ireland)
Act, 1878, applies.
