Ordered: That the Standing Orders, relative to Local Bills, be printed, as amended.
Ancient Monuments Bill [S].
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move the Second Reading of this Bill, whi
is intended, in the main, to enact for Northern Ireland, with one or two Amendments,
which experience in Great Britain has
shown to be desirable, the Ancient Monuments Bill of 1913, which did not apply to
Ireland. The last Act of any importance
specially dealing with ancient monuments
in Ireland was that of 1892, and it will be
generally admitted that a revision is over.
due. Hon. Members will, I think, agree
that just as the written records of a community should not be allowed to perish, so
also those monuments, which speak of
a past for which there is no written record,
should not be allowed to disappear. But
when the question of who is actually to
look after these monuments is approached,
it will be found that at present, in the great
majority of cases, the whole responsibility
is left to the owner.
It will be freely admitted that many
owners, at their own trouble and expense,
do look after these monuments, preserve
them, and make them available to all. This
public spirit is greatly to be commended,
but there is another class of owner, which
is, perhaps, unaware of any special importance attaching to these structures and behaves accordingly. Others again may be so
pressed in financial circumstances as to be
unable to afford outlay on maintenance, or
may even be influenced by a desire to make
profit, or advantage, where such
can be
made, by the disposal of the monuments.
Everyone would deplore such occurrences,
but they will take place if there is no
authority entrusted with the guardianship
of these ancient monuments, and there is at
present no power in law in Northern Ireland to prevent, or even delay, destruction.
In the recent history of Northern Ireland,
stone circles have been used to mend field
dykes, prehistoric stone forts on hill tops
sold for road metal, and old castles, churches
and towers have been used as quarries for
building material.
The Commissioners of Works in Dublin included an Ancient Monuments Branch,
whose duties were confined to the maintenance of such monuments as had been entrusted to it, either by Act of Parliament or
by private donation. Twenty-four of these
monuments are in Northern Ireland, and
their management is now entrusted to a
branch of the Ministry of Finance, which
inherits the powers of the Commissioners
of Public Works. These monuments, which
include such well-known remains
as the
Giant's Ring, Inch Abbey, Devenish Abbey
and Round Tower, Arboe Cross and Dungiven Priory, may be taken to be reasonably
sure of preservation. It is possible, however, that in the public interest it may be
advisable that other monuments should be
placed in State charge, and Clauses 3 and
4 of the Bill enable the Minister of Finance
to accept or purchase other monuments of
this type. It is not anticipated that these
will be many, and I am sure that in view
of the fact that each such monument involves a certain financial charge, my right
hon. Friend, the Minister of Finance, in
whose discretion the matter will lie, will
not hastily accept.
By Clause 6 power is taken by means of a
Preservation Order to bring a threatened
monument under the protection of the
Ministry. The procedure will be found in
the English Act of 1913, and its effect is to
prevent alteration or demolition except
with the Ministry's consent. In the extreme
case where the owner's negligence is likely
to entail complete destruction, the Ministry
has power to convert protection into complete guardianship. These are, of course,
powers which would only be used in the last
95 Ancient Monuments Bill-
THE SENATE.

Second Reading.
[Viscount Charlemont]
resort, and where the monument was of
sufficient importance as to justify their exercise. It is not expected that they will be
used frequently, but the experience of all
countries in these matters has shown that
unless some such provision is inserted, the
community may have to look on helplessly
while a person who is for the time the
owner of some world-famous treasure, in
the exercise of his unchallengeable powers
at law, proceeds to mishandle, sell or destroy something which the general sentiment
of all wishes to preserve.
Of course, where action affecting private
property is taken, the owner is entitled to
compensation, and we propose to consider
the matter as on a par with the situation,
which arises when the community find it
compulsorily
necessary
to take private
property for the erection of works of public
utility. If an agreement cannot be reached
between the State and the proprietor, the
matter will, under Clause 9, be referred to
an impartial tribunal for the assessment of
compensation. But although these provisions are inserted as an ultimate safeguard,
we do not wish to proceed along these lines.
After all, the preservation of monuments
must, in the end, lie on much the same basis
as the maintenance of the public peace,
namely, the general support of all citizens.
It is proposed that a policy of spreading
knowledge and facilitating
should generally be adopted.
preservation
It is only right that all Monuments for the
maintenance of which the State undertakes
responsibility should be open to the general
public. This is provided for in Clause 12 of
the Bill, and Clause 8 provides a salutary
check to the depredations of tourists who
desire to immortalise their names by carving
them on these ancient monuments and the
Bill prescibes a penalty of five pounds, or a
month's imprisonment, for this class of
vandalism.
There is a large number of monuments
which, though interesting, are hardly
deserving of preservation at the expense of
the State, particularly if the owner can be
induced to take an enlightened view of his
duty in the matter. These are works which
would, in accordance with the fifth Clause of
the Bill, be included in a published list.
The effect of the listing will merely be that
the owner will be required to notify the
Ministry of Finance before taking any action
which might injuriously affect the structure.
The Ministry will have no power to prevent
any action which it thinks undesirable
unless by the extreme step of taking over the
monument, but it is thought that the notification will give time for consultation and
advice. The experience of learned societies
has shown that very few owners, when they
realize the importance of their possession,
are deaf to advice as to the manner of its
preservation, particularly if that advice is
backed by a strong public opinion.
Here the expert Advisory Committee to be
set up by Clause 13 will play an important
part. We propose that it should be mainly
quite unofficial. It will contain not merely
representatives
representatives
of learned bodies but
from each of the Six
Counties, and will without doubt play an
extremely useful part in arousing local
interest and bringing gentle persuasion to
bear upon owners. Here I think it well to
point out that the Bill does not propose in
any way to interfere with the extensive
powers relating to ancient monuments which
the local authorities already possess, and
which, I may say, they seldom exercise.
With the general mass of the smaller and
less important Monuments it will be impossible to deal in a general way, and it seems
that here the best plan is that which has
been adopted in England, Scotland and
Wales, namely, the securing of information
respecting them and the preservation of that
information in some permanent form.
Clause 5 makes provision for this. It would
be a hopeless task to maintain every rath,
ruined church or castle, but they can form
the subject of archaeological study, their
dimensions can be noted, photographs taken,
and what is known of their history noted
down. In this task the co-operation of the
Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee
will be most useful and from all experience
of the earnest unrewarded zeal of antiquarians it seems certain that, once the lead
is given and the effort organised, excellent
results will follow. It may be remembered
by some of my hearers that a private Society
in Dublin-the Georgian Society-recorded
in this way Dublin architecture, much of
which has since perished. No less than 18
Counties of Great Britain have been
similarly dealt with in a series of striking
publications produced by the Stationery
Office, and while we could hardly hope to
rival such works as these, it is quite within
the bounds of possibility to ensure at
moderate expense that a description shall be
kept for posterity of those Monuments of
Northern Ireland, the preservation of which
it is not proposed to secure under the Bill.
I come now to the more novel features of
the Bill. In the English Act of 1913 the
operation of a Preservation Order affecting
a Monument was limited to a period of 18
months, unless it was subsequently confirmed
by Parliament. I understand that this
requirement has proved hampering and
unnecessary, and accordingly the present
Bill proposes to leave the matter entirely at
the Minister's discretion. The Preser-
Ancient Monuments Bill-

27 APRIL 1926
Second Reading.
vation Order will be the act of a Minister
responsible to Parliament and if he exercises
power arbitrarily he is liable at any time to
be questioned in Parliament. The monetary
damage suffered by the owner will in most
cases be negligible, and in any event will be
assessable by an impartial tribunal.
Finally, I reach the requirement which
necessitates that the finding of archaeological
objects shall be reported to the Advisory
Committee which we are setting up. This
also is a step forward, which I am satisfied
that Great Britain will herself have to adopt
in the near future and which various foreign
countries, and at least one British possession have put in force. The purpose is
to secure that objects of this kind shall not
be spirited away without at least a local
museum or local learned body having a
chance to obtain them, and, I may add,
without the finder having some knowledge of
the worth of his find. What has only too
frequently happened in the past is this: A
ploughman or turfcutter finds some valuable object and sells it, perhaps, to the local
shopkeeper, for a shilling or two, not knowing its value. The shopkeeper sells it at a
large advance in price to some local resident,
who may, or may not, recognize its true
value. Other sales take place, the object
passes from hand to hand, and, perhaps,
leaves the country altogether. Meanwhile,
no public record of its nature, or the
circumstances of its finding, both of which
are so important for science, has been
obtained, and no public institution has even
had an opportunity for purchase.
Generally speaking, the Bill seems likely
to promote, at a trifling cost, the preservation of many historic remains which otherwise are likely to perish, and I commend it
very heartily to the consideration of the
House. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: The subject which the noble
Viscount has brought before us this afternoon is undoubtedly one of great interest,
and I think I may say that the case he has
put before us is one which will appeal to
and receive the sympathy of us all. I am
afraid I cannot add that I think the proposals will arouse any general enthusiasm
amongst the public, for I am very much
afraid that the general public do not pay
particular attention to relics of past ages,
nor is the general public very much interested in the preservation of ancient monuments. There is a sentimental feeling in
Ulster in favour of preserving anything that
has come down to us from what the country
people generally call "the long ago's." I
think that is a laudable sentiment and one
should, if one can, endeavour to support it
and give effect to it, provided that the relics
which it is desired to preserve are really
D
worth preservation, and provided that their
preservation works no injustice on anybody
concerned.
Our chief difficulty in preserving ancient
monuments is, as a well-known poet pointed
out at least a hundred years ago, the ravages
of time. Time will destroy all your ancient
monuments, whatever you do. They must
always remain, as Byron called them,
Fragments of stone reared by creatures of
clay.
You cannot preserve for all time against
the ravages of time, but you can to some
extent preserve them against ravages by
human nature, to which the noble Viscount
referred in terms which, indeed, he might
have made a little more forcible. The sort
of person who wishes to record his name or
initials on ancient monuments is not a person worthy of much consideration. However, if
we are to support our sentiments as regards
these ancient monuments we have to consider how we are to do it. Now, we are
frequently reminded, not always in the kindliest way, that the area of Northern Ireland
is less than the area of Yorkshire. It does
occur to me that before Parliament is asked
to bring in a Bill to deal with this matter,
Parliament ought to be made acquainted with
the ancient monuments which it is desired
to preserve. There cannot be any insuperable diffculty in preparing and submitting to
this House a list of monuments in Northern
Ireland to which it is desired that this Bill
should apply. After all, there are Ordnance
Maps, and they go into very considerable
detail.
Has,
I should like to know exactly what monuments, other than the twenty-four referred
to, the Government, or the Department behind the Government which is responsible.
for this matter, have in mind, because on that
seems to depend the answer to the question
one naturally asks, is this Bill necessary at
all? Who has asked for this Bill?
for instance, the Farmers' Union been consulted? I propose to show presently that the
Farmers' Union is very much interested in
this matter and very much concerned. Has
the Landlord's Convention been consulted, or
the Association of County Councils, or the
Association of Municipal Councils? Who has
been consulted? I think we are entitled to
know something more than we have yet been
told as to the necessity of adding another
Statute to our Statute Book.
We have to remember that in this matter
Northern Ireland has already been better
protected than were the people of Great
Britain before the Act of 1913 was passed.
We have in Northern Ireland as part of the
original whole Island, the Irish Church Act,
of 1869, which contained a section providing
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
that any Church not required for Ecclesiastical purposes which, in the opinion of the
Commissioners-that is the Commissioners
for carrying into execution the Irish Church
Act-was worthy of preservation as a national
monument and the term national monument
is worthy of remark-should be handed over
to the Commissioners of Works, which is now
the Ministry of Finance, as regards Northern
Ireland, and if they decline to accept it, it
should be disposed of in the ordinary way.
Anything, then, in the way of a disused
Church which passed under the provisions of
the Irish Church Act of 1869 has once already
been offered to the Public, and if it has not
been accepted and has passed into private
hands, it becomes a very grave question
whether, for the honour of Parliament, you
can seek to re-open that transaction. Following the Act of 1869, we had the general Act
of 1882, which applied to both England and
Ireland, the details of which I need not refer
to at this moment, except to point out that
the Bill gave a definition of ancient monuments, by means of reference to its three
Schedules, which were appended to the Bill,
showing the monuments in England, Scotland
and Ireland, respectively, to which the Bill
was meant to apply.
So far as Northern Ireland is concerned,
there were only three examples-the Giant's
Ring, at Ballylesson; the Earthen Fort, at
Downpatrick; and another one at Navan.
The items comprised in those Schedules were
limited to Barrows, Earthworks, Dolmens,
and Menhirs, or standing stones. There
was nothing included in those Schedules
in the Act of 1882, of an earlier date
than the Norman Conquést. The next
Act was that of 1892, which carried the
matter a little further, and permitted
the acquisition, by the Commissioners of
Works, of Medieval monuments. That
brought us a little nearer modern times, because I understand that medieval means
middle ages, which expired in 1483, and in
each of those cases, the ancient monuments
of the earlier Act, or the Medieval monuments of the later Act, there was a distinct
power given to the Commissioners of Works
to acquire monuments by agreement with the
owner, out of moneys provided by Parliament.
The Commissioners of Works, with the consent of the Treasury, could acquire monuments absolutely. They could also become
guardians of monuments, leaving the nominal
ownership with the owner, and then they
had certain privileges.
After that you got provisions in 1898
which again were a little in advance of the
English provisions. They did not get these
powers in England until later. Power was
given to the County Councils by the Local
Government Act, which gave their origin
to the County Councils, to become guardians
of any
monuments and to allow their
inspectors to report on such monuments as
were falling into disrepair. The County
Councils were also given power to prosecute
those name-inscribing people who have been
referred to, but the glamour of ownership,
so to speak, was never brought before the
County Councils. They could not acquire
actual ownership, and consequently they did
not get it.
There was the small Act of 1900 which
does not matter, and now we come to 1903,
and this is where the interests of the
farmers seem to come in. In that year was
passed the Irish Land Act, Wyndham's Act.
under that Act it was provided that where on
any farm on which the fee-simple was to pass
to the tenant there stood any ancient monument which in the opinion of the Land Commission was worthy of preservation, that
monument should not pass with the farm,
but should in the first instance be offered to
the Commissioners of Works for maintenance
as a public monument. If the Commissioners of Works did not take it it was
then to be offered to the County Council for
preservation as an ancient monument. If
neither of those things came off, then the
monument passed to the farmer, and in
many cases it is with him to-day.
Here again, exactly as in the case of the
Irish Church Act, I say you cannot for the
honour of Parliament tear up the arrange
ment then made. The public, through that
Department, have had opportunities of
acquiring these monuments, and if those
opportunities have been neglected, that must
prevail. I look in vain for any provision in
this Bill to safeguard interests which
may have arisen. After 1903 there was
an Act of Parliament which is, I am
afraid, too little known. It was an
Imperial Act, although it is a local
Act. That was the Act establishing what in
England is called the National Trust. Its
full name is the National Trust for the
Preservation of Places of Historic Interest
or Natural Beauty. It is a voluntary
association and is given many special powers.
It is maintained entirely by voluntary
contributions and it has done work in Great
Britain which is of the utmost value. It is
entirely due to the operations of the National
Trust across the water that the Roman
Villa at Chedworth, and, to take a more
recent instance, the monument to Viscount
Falkland on the field at Newbury, have been
acquired and maintained.
The National Trust is enabled, under its
Act, to operate in Northern Ireland if it
gets any encouragement to do so.
I understand it has no property in Northern Ireland,
27 APRIL 1926
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Second Reading.

respect to you, Mr. Speaker, I am afraid 1
must mention Helen's Tower also as being a
comparatively modern monument, and monuments in the streets of Belfast, such as the
South African Memorial, and every War
Memorial in the country. That is where the
expression ancient monuments, as extended
by this Bill has now brought us, and it is
worth considering if that is what we mean.
although it has one place in County Cork.
Those are the Statutes we have got at the
moment, but we have not got this Act of
1913. The Act of 1913 requires careful
comparison with the Bill now before us.
That Act extended the powers of local
authorities very considerably. While not
interfering with those powers of guardianship possessed by County Councils, it gave
the County Council, or the Council of a city,
the power of acquiring the actual ownership
and it gave the same power to a Borough
Council. That is, it extended the net. It
made it easier for monuments to be
preserved by extending the public
authorities which had power to acquire them.
It also brought in a Clause as regards the
listing of monuments on something of the
same lines as the Clause in the Bill now
before us, and it also provided for
Preservation Order.
a
But as the Noble Viscount, the Leader of
the House, plainly told us, the Preservation
Order, under the English Act, was only to
have effect for eighteen months unless confirmed by Parliament. The proposal in the
Bill before us is to substitute for the control
of Parliament, the control of the Ministry.
Under the Bill there is no appeal against a
Preservation Order made, doubtless, after
due inquiry, but there is no appeal against a
Preservation Order made by the Ministry.
In England the Preservation Order only lasts
until it is confirmed by Parliament. I shall
be speaking again on that proposal.
If it be decided that it is time to take
further action about our ancient monuments,
it seems 10 me that very great care is
required before we add another Act of
Parliament to the Statute Book. It seems
rather a curious point to make on a Second
Reading speech, but first of all one has to
direct attention to the very title of the Bill.
The Bill is said to deal with Ancient Monuments, and we would really have to consider
what we mean by ancient monuments, or in
other words, what sort of monument is it we
are going to cover by this expression. How
far do we mean to go? I have shown that in
1882 you were limited by the Schedules, and
that later on you were extended to the
Fifteenth Century, but now, following the
English Act, if we turn to the Definition
Clause of our Bill, we find that any monument, the preservation of which is a matter
of public interest, by reason of the historic,
architectural, traditional, artistic, or archaeological interest, is an ancient monument.
Away goes the idea of age entirely. You
have in the Bill before you a definition which
does undoubtedly cover monuments of such
widely varied dates as the Giant's Ring, the
Round Tower and the Steeple at Antrim,
Dunluce Castle, Derry Walls, and, with all
I have already said enough to prepare the
House for the Amendment that the word
"now" be omitted from the Motion and at
the end thereof be added the words, "this
day Six months." I propose this Amendment
not, as I am sure the House will appreciate,
necessarily as final, but because I think
that an Amendment of that sort is the best
step one can take in the interests of debate
and in order that a debate may follow and
that we may have the advantage of hearing
different views from the various parts of the
Province. The discussion which I hope will
ensue ought to afford us a reliable index of
public feeling as represented here on the
various important points of the scheme before us, and it will enable us to consider
whether the Bill ought to be given a Second
Reading or whether it should be withdrawn
in order that another one can be substituted,
or whether a rather drastic Amendment
should be accepted by the Government.
Now let me try to see rather more in detail what it is is wrong with the Bill. I say
that the faults of this Bill are excessive centralisation and too much big stick. Big stick
does not, as a rule, awaken public enthusiasm
or interest. It generally repels it. The extensive centralisation has, I think, been
carried to this point. You are going to make
the Ministry the authority to preserve ancient
monuments, which is presumably the purpose
of the Bill. The Bill has taken you to this
point. If the Ministry cannot preserve these
monuments, nobody else shall. That is the
effect of the Bill. You have left the County
Councils with their present powers, but you
have prohibited them from accepting guardianship in the future; and you have not
given them any power of acquiring monuments, nor have you followed the English
method of giving such power to your large
towns.
The whole possibility of success in this
Bill must turn in great measure on the prob
lem of finance. Now finance, as the House is
aware, is a matter which can only be discussed here, so to speak, by the courtesy of
those who sit elsewhere, and by the use of
a well-known Parliamentary device of italics
and dashes. But we cannot approach the
consideration of this subject without turning
our minds to the money matter. What
money is there likely to be available for the
purposes of this Bill? I understand the
103 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Leslie]
estimates for the coming year amount to
£600. What mortgages there are already on
that £600 I do not know. We have just heard
there are twenty-four monuments to be kept
up, and out of the balance we will have to pay
some expenses of the Advisory Committee
which is to be established, the expenses of
anything else required, whether in ownership
or guardianship, and I am not at all certain
that out of what may be left it is not the
intention of the Bill to repay the County
Councils some of the expenses they have incurred. I should hesitate to conclude that
that can be the meaning of the Bill, because
there are provisions in it which appear to
be to the contrary. I presume we are entitled to get, under the Colwyn terms, some
allowance in our Imperial contribution, because they do apply funds in Great Britain
to this purpose.
I understand that in 1913, when the present
Act was a Bill, it was stated in the House of
Commons there, that the estimate was £19,000
for the year. I understand that the estimate
now is for £250,000. The increase appears to
be startling, but at the moment we appear
to be starting with £600. If there are so
many of these monuments crying out for protection, and you have only got £600, is this
the time to shut off the County Councils, or
do anything that will prevent somebody else
coming in to help. You are taking powers
by the Bill to permit the Ministry to receive
help, and you have not given the same
power to the County Councils. I think
finance is one of the first things to which I
have to call attention. You have recently
given powers to the County Councils and to
Urban Councils to incur reasonable expenditure for the maintenance of War Memorials,
and I cannot see why, if you want to preserve other monuments, you should not give
more powers.
I should have preferred to start our new
Bill, if there must be a new Bill, with a
proposal that, without prejudice to existing
powers, the Ministry of Finance, the County
Council, or Council of a city, or urban district in Northern Ireland and the National
Trust should be the authorities for the purposes of this Bill, and I would give all those
authorities the same permissive powers that
have been confined to the Ministry of Finance.
I do not think I would give anybody, except
in very exceptional cases, any compulsory
powers. I would continue the old practice of
trying to deal with these things by agreement, because if you approach a man by
agreement it is wonderful what you can get
done, but if you wave the big stick at him,
you usually put his back up, and if you are
going to put a man's back up by the provisions of this Bill, your £600 will not go
very far.
Passing from finance to speak for a
moment more on the question of acquisition by agreement, we must admit, after
what we have heard, that there will be
cases where something, which it is really
desirable should be preserved, will not be
so taken care of. I believe that if an
owner is approached in a civil manner he
may be less implacable, but if there is a
case where you are satisfied you must have
a monument then the proper course to adopt
is the open constitutional course of coming
to Parliament and asking for a Private Bill.
The railways are always taking private
property, and they always have the authority of Parliament, and why should we
depart from the old constitutional practice
and introduce in Northern Ireland something that has not been introduced in
England, and which really is contrary to
a document which is quite as old as the
majority of these monuments we are talking
of preserving.
I do not want to extend a speech, which
is already rather long, by dealing more than
I ought at this stage with the details of
the Bill, but it is scarcely possible to avoid
referring to some of the clauses individually
for the sake of illustration. Parts 1 and 2
of the Bill are practically a reconstruction
of the existing law, and call for but little
special attention, except in so far as they
fail to give to local authorities those rights
for which I have already pleaded. I cannot pass from them without saying that
I am afraid the
new definition of
owner with which we are presented
in the first clause is one which is likely to
get us into trouble at a later stage of the
Bill, for it seems to me that the Bill is
taking as owner a man who is not the owner,
but simply is the man in possession. I think
there may be difficulties afterwards, but that
is a point which is really only of minor
importance, because we are not talking so
much about owners.
Then, having got away from Parts 1 and 2,
we come to Part 3 of the Bill, which is
really the new legislation. In the new part
of the Bill-Part 3-we are introduced for
the first time to the Advisory Committee. I
imagine that the Advisory Committee may
very well be of great assistance, but when
you are establishing an Advisory Committee to give advice about other people's
property it is, I think, incumbent on you to
see that your Advisory Committe is as representative and as properly constituted as
possible. Let us see how the committee is to
be made up. The Advisory Committee, of
course, is a Schedule to the English Act, and
a place is found for representatives of the
Royal Academy, the representatives of the
Royal Commission on Historical Monuments,
and also Trustees of the British Museum.
We cannot put those in our Bill, and I
105 Ancient Monuments Bill-

27 APRIL 1926
Second Reading.
suggest you have done all you can. We find
the committee in the First Schedule of the
Bill. We see that the Ministry of Education for Northern Ireland has been promoted to a position on the list very
different to that enjoyed by its older
brother in England, but that is a
detail. We see the Queen's University represented. In England they did not give
University representation. I cannot help
thinking, and I venture to mention it to-day
in order that it may be considered, that if
University representation is to come in at all
Trinity College, Dublin, ought not to be
forgotten. The Royal Societies are represented, naturally enough, and then we come
to two new other Societies, both of them in
Belfast.
I cannot say that I think there is any precedent for including private bodies of that
kind on a committee appointed by Statute.
Instances to the contrary are numerous,
although individual members of the necessary
acquirements can be represented. Having
passed from what this Advisory Committee
includes, one turns to what it does not include. There is not a single direct provincial representative there, although the noble
Viscount said the Committee would be representative of all the counties of Northern
Ireland. Representatives of the counties are
not in the Bill, and they ought to be. The
County Councils ought to claim representation, and the Corporation of the City of
Londonderry might have something to say,
and they are not on the committee.
Passing from the Advisory Committee we
find they are given certain powers which they
can only exercise if they see that the object
of their attention is what the Bill describes
as of National importance. That phrase,
National importance, is after all a very
strong phrase indeed. If you are going to
apply a term like that to a national monument it seems to me to affect closely the scope
of the Bill. The words "National importance" were put in the English Act, and I
venture to suggest that the phrase "National
importance" in England in 1913 had a very
different meaning to the phrase "National
importance" now, after the war. I should
not dwell on the words were it not that the
committee are entirely bound by the phrase,
and unless we can give some line to the
Advisory Committee as to how those words
are to be construed, we are rendering the
provision absolutely nugatory.
Turning away from that, I cannot refrain
from calling attention to the extraordinary
character of the whole of Clause 5. I told
the House that it was based on a clause of
the English Act. The English Parliament
in 1912 as well as in 1913 was very much
occupied on Bills and matters of this
description. There were, I think, in 1912, no
fewer than three Private Bills running at once
in the House of Lords, and the ultimate result was the Bill of 1913, and on one of those
occasions no less an authority than the late
Marquess Curzon pointed out the extraordinary manner in which this clause sought
to treat the owner of a monument. You
treat the man at the outset as a potential
criminal. You say the Ministry shall notify
the owner it is going to do such a thing, and
so on. After all, a monument would not be
there at all had it not been that the owner
and his predecessors had preserved it for
years without the assistance of Parliament,
and now the suggestion is that you should
turn round and treat him in that way.
There is no suggestion, I think, of consulting
him first to see what he has got to say about
it.
Neither is there any protection for a
dwelling-house. In the English Act, both in
Part 1, which dealt with acquisition-not, as
in our case, with guardianship, and in Part
3, there was a definite exception of a man's
dwelling-house. I cannot see why a dwellinghouse in Northern Ireland should not be protected, because if the Advisory Committee
says that this dwelling or castle is of
archaeological interest and puts it on the list
the owner could not instal electric light or
put in a new bath without the permission of
the Ministry of Finance. After all, this is a
free country, and that seems rather further
than this House is prepared to go. When
the
monument has got in
list the
owner is not to do anything until he has
given the Ministry of Finance two months'
notice. Why the Ministry of Finance wants
two months' notice when one month is sufficient in England I do not know. The only
reason I can suggest is that as our area is
only equal to that of Yorkshire we propose
to inspect it by foot instead of by motor car.
If the owner has to have his monument
listed at all and has to wait while he consults the Ministry at least he should have
his expenses. Something might be made of
that Clause, but it would have to be on permisive lines.
a
Clause 6 I will not trouble the House with
to-day, but I forget whether it was Clause 5
or this Clause, which the noble Viscount
alluded to as affording the right to photograph. There is no right to photograph any
monument given in the Bill.
Turning to Clause 7, I say definitely that
a Preservation Order of that sort, a prohibition for the demolition or removal of something which belongs to a man, is a definite
interference with his property, which cannot
be done merely at the will of the Ministry of
Finance. If there is to be a Preservation
Order at all-it may be a case where you
cannot agree with a man-it should only be
made subject to the approval of Parliament,
107 Ancient Monuments Bill-

THE SENATE
*
Second Reading.
[Mr. Leslie]
which is the English practice. As the Clause
stands at present you will be in this very
curious position. If your monument was
merely listed and if there was risk of
damage by storm, a condition of urgent
necessity would arise, and you would be able
to repair the monument, yet you find in the
Bill that no work shall be carried out without the consent of the Ministry of Finance.
That is a curious way of protecting a monument.
Then again, if the second part of that
Clause becomes operative, and the Ministry
appoints inself guardian it would seem, so
far as I can make out, that anybody entitled
to that monument in expectancy is ousted,
although in the earlier part of the Bill, we
find that the interests of reversionaries are
protected. However, compensation is provided under certain conditions, and the fact
that compensation is provided is, of course,
cast upon us as a necessity by the terms of
the Government of Ireland Act. I confess I
am not altogether satisfied with that Clause,
but the matter need not be gone into in detail
now. The main thing lacking seems to me
to be an indication as to how the Court of
Arbitration is to be set in motion. Power is
given to decide as to the amount, but there
does not seem to be any provision as to who
is to say whether or not a cause for action
has arisen, nor is it made clear-although I
have no reason to doubt it is the intentionthat the mere inclusion of a monument in
the list may be a cause of action, or that the
making of a Preservation Order, is to be the
subject for compensation. Those are matters
which I feel could be made more clear, but
they are not so serious as the main construction of the Bill.
Then we pass to the more general provisions of the Bill, as to which I am sure the
House will be glad to hear I have not got
very much to say. They do contain one or
two provisions which cast a doubt in my
mind as to who is to ultimately pay the
expenses incurred by a County Council.
They do not give a County Council power to
continue their beneficent work as guardian,
nor do they clear up the point as to property which passed under the Land Act, but
I am very much afraid that in the clause
dealing with public access, the Bill goes
beyond the powers of this Parliament, because I do not think there is any right of
public access given in respect of anything
which passed under the Act of 1903. That
is only access given by the Act of 1882, and
that, as you will see by reference, gave no
right of public access, whatever. It gave the
right to the Commissioners of Works to have
access by themselves, and their workmen, for
the purpose of repairing and maintaining
monuments, but it did not do any more than
that. When once you commence to deal with
legislation as regards rights of way, the
trouble you are inviting is very serious, and
if you have only £600 you cannot contest
many lawsuits.
If you want to provide those means of
access you may or you may not have to buy
the bed and soil of the land you want to traverse to get there. The question is one that
ought to be considered with the greatest care,
although it does not in principle present any
insuperable difficulty. As regards the other
matter dealt with in the Finance clause,
which, as I have said, we can only discuss by
the courtesy of those who sit in another place.
I should have liked a little something more
done to encourage preservation. I should
have liked, for instance, the insertion of provisions that where owners do give their
monuments to the public the deeds should be
free from duty, and so on. On that subject
something might also be done by allowing
local bodies to charge for admission to monuments, and admission in those cases might
be made free of Entertainments Duty. A
little help might be given there.
The question of the payment of compensation I have already spoken of, and now,
passing over other Clauses, I would just draw
attention to Clause 16. I only mention it as
showing that although the County Councils
have been treated with very scant consideration in the earlier Clauses of the Bill, here
you put in a Clause in which you directly
ask them to assist you. I dare say you will
get that assistance. Then you get a definition
which only invites attention because of the
new suggestion that a site may be a monument. I take the expression "site" to be
introduced for the very laudable purpose of
getting hold of some engraved stones or
similar objects. The best example I can think
of is the White Horse on the Berkshire
Hills. Unfortunately that is not in Northern
Ireland, but I draw attention to the
matter because I think it goes further than
Parliament will allow. It might cover fifty
or a hundred acres. I believe if we had
stuck more to the Act of 1882 we should have
found all we required. Now I have finished
with the first four parts of the Bill.
There remains only what ought to be the
fifth part of the Bill, that contained in
Clause 18 which is given a separate place in
the title as the preservation of Archaeological
objects. I believe it will be possible to make
this Clause of some service, but that could
not be done unless the big stick was taken
away and the Clause became permissive. As
the Clause stands, it simply invites a man
first of all to comply with the law, which of
course should always be done, but the man
can without the least trouble comply with the
law, defeat your object, and then laugh, and
you could not touch him. You cannot base
a criminal charge on petitio principii. The
109 Ancient Monuments Bill—

27 APRIL 1926
Second Reading.
man does not know it is an archaeological
object, and you ought not to require a
learned Professor who is writing a book who
may go and pick up something or other at
once to disclose it to the Advisory Committee
which is composed of his dearest enemies.
There is interference with the liberty of the
subject and it is not of much use.
If you want to preserve archaeological
objects why do you limit the archaeological
objects to those of which findings may be
reported under this Clause. The point of
importance is whether the object is of
interest or not. I think that if the House
decided to take the Bill further, that clause
also can be made of some serivce.
Now I have finished. I am afraid I have
made a great demand on the indulgence of
the House, but I appeal to the House to try
to take the public with you. Above all, take
that big stick, break it up and make a fire
of it and light something that will melt the
hard heart of the Ministry of Finance, and
perhaps he will give you a little more
money. If the Bill is put into Committee,
and if the Government were not in any way
implacable, I believe a Bill might be produced which would be of some use. The
Government will, I am sure, be much helped
by the criticisms of this House.
Mr. SPEAKER: What is the Amendment
the hon. Member moves?
Mr. LESLIE: The Amendment is that the
word "now" be omitted from the Motion,
and that at the end the words "this day six
months" be added.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to express general
approval of the objects of the Bill, and J
would like to thank the noble Viscount, the
Leader of the House, for the very able explanation he has given us of the various
sections of the Measure. I remember reading
in the American papers fifty years ago, a
statement made by your respected father, Mr.
Speaker, in delivering a speech which was
reported in the American papers at that
time. I remember being taunted very
severely by my shipmates of the lower deck,
in consequence of the story told by Lord
Dufferin. He stated that on leaving Great
Britain on one occasion to fill an important
position as Her Majesty's representative
abroad, he had left definite instructions for
the preservation of some monument on some
part of his estate, and before he was long
away he was informed that the person to
whom he had left the duty of preserving this
particular monument had proceeded to demolish it, in order to build a wall to preserve it. We were taunted on the lack of
veneration shown by our fellow countrymen
for ancient monuments, as displayed by the
individual in this particular case.
I think the word site is necessary in the
Bill. I think there are a lot of very valuable
monuments not embraced in any of the Acts
of Parliament passed up to the present. If
the present generation do not recognise the
value of them, at least future generations
will recognise their value when people become better educated. I refer not merely
to dwellings and historic monuments, but to
eskers and kitchen middens, monuments of
primitive times. Throughout the central
part of Ireland there is a series of eskers
which have been broken up by the County
Councils, and these eskers provide indisput
able evidence of the Ice Age in Ireland.
These ancient remains have been carted
away for the purpose of macadamising roads.
To future generations, when the people are
better educated and begin to study the
development of the physical surface of their
country, these relics will be intensely
interesting. These monuments will be irreplaceable and various specimens of that
particular kind ought to be preserved.
I have no doubt there are some interesting
monuments within the area
of Northern
Ireland. There is another class of monument, namely, kitchen middens. I am sure
there are specimens of these in this
particular part of Ireland, and they are
worthy of preservation. No attempt has
been made by Governments hitherto to
preserve kitchen middeus, and no attempt
has been made by any Government to preserve the monuments of the physical development of the surface of the country and
the early social development of the people.
I know for a fact that in many counties these
middens have been dug into by the farmers
who have taken the shells away, and decarbonised them for the purpose of making
manure. 1 mention these two matters because they come within the purview of the
word" site." While they could not be spoken
of as monuments they could probably be regarded as sites. I think the preservation of
these ancient archaeological specimens is well
worthy of the consideration of the Government and of the people, although the people
in general, to-day, manifest little interest in
such things. I have no hesitation whatever
in saying that future generations, if the
Government does not preserve these specimens, will lament the negligence of their forefathers in not preserving such specimens of
the social development of the people and the
physical development of the country of which
they are inhabitants.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I have to
thank the hon. Senator (Mr. Dorman) who
has just spoken, for his defence of the Bill
on general lines. I think that what the first
speaker (Mr. Leslie) was most afraid of was
compulsory powers and the big stick element.
I might say the big stick, if wielded by the
Ancient Monuments Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Minister of Finance, makes him accountable
to Parliament for any action in which he has
been over arbitrary. While the procedure in
England has been to pass private Bills for
this purpose, they are apt to be crowded
out by pressure of other legislation, and I
think the procedure that has been laid down
in this Bill is really a more practical one.
As I said, if the Minister acts in any manner
which is contrary to the public advantage, he
can be questioned in Parliament.
The great purpose of this Bill, apart from
the actual working of it, is to inspire public
interest which, as the last speaker has remarked, is very deficient at the present time.
County Councils have often more pressing
business to deal with than these ancient
monuments; they have not shown very great
interest in them, and there is great ignorance concerning them. I know myself
of the case of a stone which was standing
when I was a boy and which is now
lying in the position of the lintel of a
door. I do not know that it was a very
interesting monument, but at the same time
I think it was worthy of preservation. Under
the terms of this Bill that sort of thing will
be impossible in the future, or, at least, we
hope so.
In regard to finance, this Bill is concerned
more with the future than with the present.
The Minister of Finance has got his eye
on nothing at the present moment, that
is, to purchase compulsorily or
deliver
over to the care of the Government, and it
is hardly likely that with the small sum we
have got in hand the Minister will go into
the Six Counties to look for monuments he
can take over. It is for the future, as
Senator Dorman has mentioned, to discover
the kitchen middens and those other
interesting archæological objects. They have
not been under the care of the Government
in the past, but they will be in the future.
As far as the big stick is concerned, I
agree that in the main it is desirable that it
should be eliminated as much as possible,
but it seems to me that with modern methods
of government it is not always possible to do
so. Senator Leslie complained that the
owner under this Bill, if he received a notice
from the Minister of Finance concerning an
ancient monument in his ownership, might
regard himself as a potential criminal threatened with pains and penalties for not keeping
it. I may say that the Imperial Chancellor
of the Exchequer, when he writes to me about
income-tax, takes that very view himself. He
threatens me that if within twenty-one days
I do not give him notice of appeal he will
imprison me, and that notwithstanding the
fact that for years I have paid my incometax without fail. I do not think that an
owner under this Clause would find himself
much aggrieved at an action of that kind.
I do not know whether I would be in order
if I went into minor points which might, or
might not, be raised in Committee, but I
think the proposal that the Bill should be
read six months hence is tantamount to a
motion for rejection. At the same time,
I would say that there are various points
which Senator Leslie raised which I could
not possibly answer straight off, that is to
say, what the precise meaning of "owner" is
and also the question about access, which is
a very difficult point and will require a
certain amount of investigation on my part.
On general lines I think the Bill is required
if not for present at least for future
purposes, and I hope the motion for rejection
will not be proceeded with.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount has
appealed that the motion should be withdrawn, and has suggested that a good deal of
alteration can be made in Committee. I confess I should like to have heard more
definitely that the proposal to substitute
Parliamentary control for the control of the
Minister would be accepted. That is a very
great difficulty in my mind but, at the same
time, nobody else has spoken on the subject,
and it is always possible to reject a Bill on
the Third Reading if nothing could be done
in Committee. I think, if I can judge the
feeling of the House, it would meet with
more general approval that the motion
should be withdrawn to-day-
Mr. SPEAKER: May I point out the hon.
Member is not allowed to make two
speeches?
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I have transgressed and I apologise. I ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: As this Bill
is going to be read a Second time, there are
one or two points to which I would like to
draw the attention of the noble Lord, the
Leader of the House. One is as to the final
court of appeal. Could an appeal not be
carried from the Ministry of Finance to the
County Court Judge or the Recorder? The
other question I would like to draw attention
to is in the event of a gold ornament being
found. I understand that gold ornament at
the present time would go to England to the
Crown and would not be held by Northern
Ireland. Could it not be provided in the case
of a gold ornament being found that it would
go to Northern Ireland and not to the
Crown? The other point to which I draw
attention is this: In the Schedule as it is
now we have a representative of an all-Ire-
Ancient Monuments Bill.
27 APRIL 1926

Adjournment.
anybody from the National Trust in England being appointed, as one might be under
the present circumstances. We would rather
hope-although it is not mentioned in the
Bill to give representation on the Advisory
Committee to a member of the National Trust
in England if they would pursue their
labours over here. If representation on the
Committee were limited to people inhabiting
Northern Ireland it would prevent the
possibility of that being done.
land body-it is really two bodies. Would
that mean they could nominate a man who
was not a citizen of Northern Ireland to sit
on that Advisory Committee? I think they
should have power to nominate with the
qualification that the person nominated
should be a citizen of Northern Ireland. I
mention these three points so that if the
Government were drawing up Amendments
they would look into these matters.
Senator Leslie has mentioned, there is room
for a great many changes in the Bill, but I
hope it will pass.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT rose.
As

speak again by permission of the House.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I will find

of treasure trove, and I will also inquire into
the question of the final court of appeal. I
would like to reply to the suggestion as to
the constitution of the Advisory Committee.
If the suggestion were passed it would mean
that only an inhabitant of Northern Ireland
could be appointed, and that would prevent

Mines Bill-
#
THE SENATE
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 28th April, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair,
Mines (Working Facilities, Support
etc.) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD): In rising to move the Second Reading of the Mines (Working Facilities, Support,
etc.) Bill I do not propose to detain the House
at any great length. This Bill, as its title
sets forth, is designed to make provision for
facilitating the working of minerals in
Northern Ireland, and also for imposing restrictions on the working of those minerals
required for the support of buildings and
works. In those cases where there is a
possibility that minerals may be left permanently unworked, either through legal disability or for various other reasons, a right
to work them may be conferred on the person
who has an interest. This is very fully set
out in Clauses 1 and 2 of the Bill. In addition,
power is also taken in the Bill to grant
ancillary rights, such as way leaves, and the
right to a water supply. This is explicitly
explained in Clause 3.
All these powers to grant rights are vested
by the Bill in the Ministry of Commerce, and
if the Minister of Commerce considers, under
Clause 5, that a prima-facie case has not been
made out he has to refer the matter to a
Tribunal, which is set up under Clause 11,
and he has to act on the findings of that
Tribunal. If there should be any dissatisfaction with the findings of the Tribunal as
to compensation, under Clause 10 (1).
appeal may be made to the County Court
Judge, sitting with an Assessor.
Part II of the Bill is very short and concerns coal mining only. This Clause-Clause
18-states that Section 4 and Section 20 of the
Mining Industry Act, 1920, shall be deemed
never to have applied to Northern Ireland.
Under Section 4 of the Act of 1920 the Board
of Trade was required to set up Pit and
District Committee and Regional and National
Boards. This House will agree, I think, that
a provision of this nature is unnecessary
in the case of our one and only local coal
mine. The other section of the Act of 1920
relates to the establishment of a fund for the
improvement of the social welfare of colliery
workers. This fund is to be created from a
levy of one penny per ton of the output of
coal mines.
Hon. Members are well aware of the extent
of the coal mining industry in Great Britain,
and will appreciate that a sum so levied
would be very large and would permit in
Great Britain, as it has actually done, of the
inauguration of many schemes for improving
the social conditions of the workers in the
industry. Under the Act to which I have
referred, the fund was limited to a period of
five years, that is, up to March of this year,
but the provisions have subsequently been
re-enacted.
Hon. Members know the degree of development which has been reached by our one
coal mine in Northern Ireland, and I think
it will be quite clear that there could have
been no object in establishing the fund here
during the past two years, when the mine
was still in the development stage, and endeavouring to create elaborate machinery to
deal with it. Accordingly, under Section 18
of the Bill now before the House it is proposed
that Section 20 of the 1920 Act creating the
fund shall be deemed never to have applied
to Northern Ireland. When I say this, 1
desire to assure hon. Members that if the
coal-mining industry here develops to any
great extent-and we all hope it will become
a great industry in the country-a fund so
created would attain substantial proportions,
the subject will be very sympathetically reviewed by the Government. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I was not, I am afraid,
aware that this Bill was going to come before
us for Second Reading this afternoon, and,
therefore, I have not, as I should have done,
endeavoured to compare it with the English
Statute of, I think, 1923. The first observation that occurs to one, having regard to one's
recollection of that Statute, is that the noble
Viscount (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard)
has succeeded in compressing the English
Statute law into very much smaller space
than appears to be required in England, and
I presume I may take it that the omission
of so many pages of the English law is due
to the fact that the law was applicable to
minerals of a class that do not exist in
Northern Ireland.
Perhaps the noble Viscount could indicate
to us whether he is satisfied that there is
sufficient protection given in this Bill on the
ground of lateral support. Lateral support
is always one of the great difficulties that
have to be encountered by anybody who seeks
to bore into the earth's surface, and, not
having studied the Bill in detail, I do not
know whether any of those rights are protected. There is another matter to which
protection ought to be given, and no doubt
may be given, and that is, is there any protection for streams, for the fouling of those
streams by the effluvia of any mines that may
be opened in Northern Ireland. The same
remark applies there again.
Poisons Bill—'
Second Reading.
28 APRIL 1926
I do not know whether there is any chance
of seeking any mine in Northern Ireland the
works in connection of which would be likely
to be responsible for some more or less
deleterious fluid flowing into neighbouring
streams. If so, the fishery interests ought
to be protected. I have no doubt the noble
Viscount will be able to tell us something
more on those two points, although I do not
know that it will be necessary to press for
an immediate reply, as some later opportunity might be more convenient, but that
is a matter for the noble Viscount himself.
The general principle of the Bill will,
I think, commend itself to us all, because if
there is any real risk of valuable mineral
sources in Northern Ireland remaining unworked owing to the incapacity or the unwillingness of those interested to allow them
to be worked, it is certainly desirable that
the opportunity should be provided for getting
over those difficulties to allow the work to
take place, to the great benefit both of labour
in Northern Ireland and incidentally of
capital. If those difficulties can be got over
without working any injustice on anybody—
and I should say the provisions as regards
the Tribunal of Appeal are satisfactory-I
think the Bill will confer very great benefits,
and in that case we shall all wish to support
it.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to Senator Leslie, who
mentioned that the Government have compressed several of the clauses of the English
Act into a very small compass, I should like to
point out to him that we have not taken any
action in regard to Part 11 of the English
Act, which referred to minerals working
under railways. We do not propose to adopt
that in Northern Ireland. In addition, I may
mention that Part 11 of the English Act was
an agreed settlement. With reference to the
other point raised by the hon. Senator as
regards drainage from coal mines, I think he
was anxious about the protection of fisheries.
I think this question would be more properly
dealt with under the Fisheries Act, and not
under this Bill, but I will go more fully into
the matter and make a statement on a later
occasion.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the whole
House for Wednesday next.
Pensions (Increase) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of the
House): I beg to move the Second Reading
of this Bill. Before I come to a detailed
examination of the Bill, I think it necessary
to make reference to two Acts passed by the
Imperial Parliament, namely, the Pensions
Increase Act of 1920, and the Amending Act
of 1924. The great rise in the cost of living
subsequent to the outbreak of the war
imposed very considerable hardships on
pensioners whose fixed pensions largely lost
their purchasing power, and the first Act to
which I have referred-that of 1920-was
designed to relieve these hardships in some
measure. It did so by making certain scales
of increase, having first taken into account
the private means of the pensioner. Those
affected by the Act were pensioners paid from
State or semi-State funds, and those paid by
local authorities. In the case of the latter.
the Act enacted that the pensions were to be
increased only if the Imperial Treasury
authorised the application of the Act to the
particular local authority, and even then the
authority had the option of granting the
increase, either in whole or in part.
The Amending Act--the Act of 1924-brought
into force larger increases than those introduced in 1920 for persons whose pensions were
less than £100 per year. These increases were
the same as those provided for in Clause 2 of
the Bill now before this House. The principal
Act of 1920 was passed by the Imperial Parliament prior to the establishment of the
Government of Northern Ireland, and its
provisions applied, therefore, to those pensions which at that time were being paid from
Imperial funds to pensioners of the Royal
Irish Constabulary and officials who had
retired from Irish Departments of the Civil
Service. A proportion of the pensions and
of any consequential increases made under
the 1920 Act is recovered from Northern
Ireland funds by deduction from the Northern
Ireland Residuary Share of Taxes. When the
second Act-that of 1924-was passed, some
of these pensioners became entitled to claim
from the Imperial Government the increases
which were granted by that Act.
But though it was only right that these
increases should be made, my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Finance, established
the position with the Imperial Treasury that
they could not, without the consent of the
Government of Northern Ireland, add to the
burden on the Northern Ireland Exchequer
by deducting these increases of pensions from
the Residuary Share of Taxes, and it was
only by action on the part of Northern
Ireland that the increases, so far as they
affected Northern Ireland, could be lifted
from the British taxpayer.
Accordingly, the Ministry of Finance, pending legislative sanction by Parliament, gave
authority to the Imperial Treasury to deduct
the increases from the Residuary Share of
Taxes, before these were paid over to our
Exchequer, and hon. Members will see from
Clause 1 that that arrangement is now to be
confirmed by the Bill now before this House.
By far the major portion of the increase
Poisons Bill-
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
will be in connection with the pensioners of
the Royal Irish Constabulary, who will
account for about £6,000 per year-a sum
which, of course, will gradually diminish.
Clause 2 of the Bill grants similar increases
of pension to those pensioners who are paid
direct from Northern Ireland funds, namely,
petty sessions clerks and national school
teachers. This House will agree with me
that, as the other pensioners to whom I have
referred in connection with the 1924 Act have
had the increases granted, and the burden
of those increases assumed by us, we would
not be logical if we refused similar increases
of pensions in respect of those who draw their
pensions direct from Northern Ireland funds.
Under the first sub-section of the proviso
to Clause 2 it is ensured that no pension
shall be increased beyond the pension that
would be awarded to a pensioner retiring today with a similar length of service who has
cost-of-living bonus reckoned as a pensionable emolument. I feel sure that this House
will agree that this proviso is only fair.
With regard to the second sub-section of
the proviso, I may explain that a large
number of teachers who were not affected by
the Pensions Increase Act of 1920 were granted
increased pensions under Section 70 (3) of the
Education Act of 1923. In a few cases the terms
which it is now proposed should apply to such
teachers would be less liberal than those
granted by the Education Act, and it has
been necessary to include the sub-section
in the proviso in order that in these few
cases no decrease of pension may be made.
The purport of Clause 3 is quite clear, and
no comment, I think, is required.
Under Clause 4 the Ministry of Finance is
given power to apply the provisions respecting increased pension percentages to the
pensions payable by local or public authoriities. It will be noted that the clause is
permissive, and this House will agree that
it would not be right to compel local authorities to grant increases whether they were
willing or not. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the whole
House for Wednesday next.
Adjournment.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn until
Tuesday next."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty Minutes before
Four o'clock until Tuesday
next.
Public Appointments.
Irish Language in Schools.
4 MAY 1926
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 4th May, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
PAPER PRESENTED TO PARLIAMENT.
Ministry of Labour.
The National Health Insurance (Navy,
Army, and Air Force) Amendment Regulations, 1926, dated March 15th, 1926, made by
the National Health Insurance Joint Com-
Imittee, the Minister of Health, the Scottish
Board of Health, and the Ministry of Labour
for Northern Ireland, acting jointly under
the National Health Insurance Act, 1924 (14
and 15 Geo. 5, c. 38).
Presented (pursuant to Act), and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Appointments by Public Bodies.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Samuel Cunningham:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government would introduce legislation
to prevent public bodies from making any
appointments (other than temporary) without gazetting the name, address and qualification of the person appointed.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I ask the
question which stands in my name, because
I feel that the public are often at a
loss to know to whom to apply for any
information, and I think that in connection with all our public bodies we should
know who is the man in the office to apply to
for information required. Another reason
why these appointments should be gazetted
is that a public body which is paying salaries
and making appointments out of public
funds should be in a position to give the
public the names and qualifications of the
officials appointed by that body. The public
have a right to know why their servants are
appointed and are being paid a salary.
would not apply to one body alone, but to
every body employing an official who is paid
out of the public funds. I hope the Government will see its way to promise to introduce
legislation of this nature as soon as possible.
In addition to the particulars which I have
suggested should be gazetted, I think the
amount of the salary paid to any official
should also be included.
It
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Before replying to the question of the
right hon. Gentleman, I would like to take
this opportunity of informing the House that
I regret the noble Viscount, the Leader of the
House (Viscount Charlemont) is indisposed,
and cannot be in his place to-day nor
probably for the remainder of this week, but
he hopes to be here next week.
In reply to the question of the right hon.
Gentleman, I would say that as he is aware,
the whole question of the Local Government
administrative machinery is under consideration at the present moment by the Local
Government Commission, and as it is understood that this Commission is considering
amongst other matters the question of the
staffing of the various local authorities, à
think he will agree with me that it would be
premature to express any opinion on this
matter until the Report of this Commission
has been received.
Irish Language in Northern Ireland
Schools.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Barclay :-
To ask the Leader of the House if it is a
fact that the Government has given its consent to the teaching of the Irish language in
one hundred and forty-four schools in
Northern Ireland, at an annual cost of seven
shillings and sixpence a pupil; and, if so,
whether they will refuse to allow public
money to be spent on this object in future.
Mr. BARCLAY: This question arises out
of a speech that was delivered some time ago
by a gentleman who, I suppose, would call
himself a friend of the Government. This
speech also appeared in the public Press,
and a great many friends of the Government
on reading the speech seemed to take it
greatly to heart that they should be taxed
to the extent of 7/6 a pupil for the purpose
of teaching Irish in 144 schools in Northern
Ireland. A number of workmen came to me
about this matter. Of course, they had the
idea that if one took any part in politics
one should understand all the Acts of Parliament that were on the Statute book and
be able to explain them in all their details.
When this question was put to me I could
not give any explanation of it or say
whether it was a fact or not. I said I had
the impression that it was untrue, or,
perhaps, it was truth half told. I said no
doubt the Ministry of Education had given
grants to all those 144 schools for educational purposes, but whether this sum of
7/6 has been set aside definitely for the purpose of teaching Irish or not, I could not
tell. However, I said I would raise the
THE SENATE
Irish Language in Schools. 124
Irish Language in Schools.
[Mr. Barclay]
give the hon. Senator any more information
on the point.
question in the Senate, and as this statement
has appeared in the Press and has been
broadcast all over the country, the answer to
this question would also appear in the Press
and then people would know whether the
statement was true or not.
If it is a fact, I would protest here and
now, and as a working man I would protest
on the ground that it is a waste of public
money and that it is spending money for
which there will never be any return, as far
as this country is concerned. The Irish
language is all right for a pastime, but as
far as its commercial value is concerned it
is no good now, and it will not be any good
in the future. In the second place, I would
protest against the expenditure of public
money for this purpose because the teaching
of the Irish language is part and parcel of
the Irish Republican propaganda.
Irish Republicans have declared over and
over again that it is their intention to drive
out of Ireland everything that is English or
that has any relation to England, and this
is one way in which they are trying to drive
the English language out of Ireland. By
compulsion in the Free State and by slipping the Irish language into schools in
Northern Ireland they hope to train the
young to speak the Irish language, so that
when the day comes they will be able to remove the rest of us who cannot speak Irish.
The
It is not right that a man who is an Imperialist, or a Loyalist, or a Constitutionalist should be taxed, either directly or indirectly, in support of the Irish Republican
propaganda. Therefore, I would be very
much obliged to the noble Viscount if he
would give me an answer that would help to
ease the minds of a great many loyal subjects in the City of Belfast on this subject.
Mr. DORMAN: Am I in order in speaking
at this stage?
Mr. SPEAKER: Perhaps the noble Viscount had better reply first.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Senator opposite who raised this question, I have to inform the House that it is the case that Irish
is one of the extra and optional subjects
which may be taught outside the ordinary
school hours to children in the Third or
higher Standards in public elementary
schools, and for which fees at the rate referred to are payable to the teachers.
In the school year 1924-25, the extra
classes for Irish were taught in 132 schools
as compared with 144 in the previous year.
The question of continuing or modifying
the rules governing these payments is at
present under consideration in the Ministry
of Education. I am sorry I am not able to
Mr. DORMAN: Arising out of that answer,
I would like to set the hon. Senator's mind
right in regard to some of the statements he
made in his speech, which are decidedly uncalled for and untrue.
Hon. SENATORS: Order, order.
Mr. SPEAKER: I was talking to the Clerk,
and I am afraid I did not hear what
expression the hon. Senator used.
Mr. DORMAN: If I have transgressed the
Rules, I withdraw. To say that the teaching
of the Irish language is solely propaganda
and carried on exclusively by the Irish Re
publican Party is untrue-or perhaps incorrect is the proper word to use, if I wish to
keep myself in order. The most popular man
in Ireland to-day and for many years on this
great question of the Irish language is the
Rev. Canon Hanney, a man who could command an overflow meeting in a Protestant
place in the South of Ireland before the
advertised time. By no stretch of imagination could we apply to the reverend gentleman the charge of unconstitutional or illegal
conduct, or rebellion against the British
Constitution in any period in his history.
We are quite prepared to spend vast sums of
money in teaching our children languages
that are not spoken in any part of the world
to-day, and in all the Four Provinces of Ireland there are large areas in which the Irish
language is exclusively spoken. I, as an
Irishman, embrace the opportunity of saluting my fellow Senator, by asking him
"Cionnas ta tu," in the language of
his native country. I would like to
know why the Government should be debarred from teaching the children of Ireland
the ancient language of their forefathers and
thereby keeping green in their memory whatever is left of some of their ancient literature
for the interest and benefit of future
generations.
Bill Presented
QUEEN'S UNIVERSITY OF
BILL.
BELFAST
"to alter the constitution of the Senate of
