Jury Laws Amendment Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, That this Bill
be now read a Second time. The Bill, as
hon. Senators are aware, has excited a great
deal of public interest, proposing, as it does,
to effect important changes in the existing
code of Jury Laws. Therefore, I thought it
right that the Members of this House should
be afforded an opportunty of hearing a
detailed explanation of its various Clauses
from my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Home Affairs, whose reasoned exposition of
the Bill, in another place, has drawn upon
him the congratulations of all parties. The
right hon. Gentleman has been good enough
to accede to my request, and I am sure the
House will be grateful to him for doing so.
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS (Sir
Dawson Bates): May I, first of all, be permitted to express my appreciation of the
honour this House has done me in allowing
me to come here this afternoon and explain
some of the provisions of the Jury Bill. For
many years past grave dissatisfaction has
prevailed because of the inequalities which
existed in regard to the Jury Service. This
is due to the fact that the Jury Acts, the
last of which was passed in 1876, are, to a
very large extent, both obsolete and out-ofdate, and this, think, will become apparent
when the Bill before the House at present
is considered.
The subject is difficult and complicated,
and before introducing this Measure we felt
that the whole subject matter should be
explored from every point of view, and
accordingly the Government appointed a
Commission, with Lord Justice Andrews as
its Chairman, and in referring to this
matter, I would like to express the Government's appreciation of the services rendered
by these gentlemen in presenting such a
valuable and exhaustive report, which report
forms the basis of the present Bill. Hon.
Senators are well aware that under the
Government of Ireland Act 1920, certain
matters are reserved to the Imperial Government. The Jury Commission had a very
wide field of reference and their recommendations come under three heads :-
(1) Those matters that are clearly outside the competence of our own Parliament;
(2) Those matters which are clearly within our competence;
(3) Those matters in which there may be
some doubt as to whether they are within
our competence or not.
It has been suggested that the Commission
who made these recommendations would not
have made them if they did not think that
all their recommendations came within the
scope of our own Parliament. Such is not
the case. Lord Justice Andrews in his
report clearly points out that to give effect
to some of their recommendations an Act
of the Imperial Parliament would be
necessary. I think it right to emphasise
that fact, as some people seem to think that
19 MAY 1926
Jury Laws Bill-

Second Reading.
of the Land Purchase Acts has converted a
very large number of small judicial tenancies
into freeholds, and the result is that at the
present time a small farmer with a £10 valuation and with nobody to leave behind when he
leaves his farm is frequently called upon to
serve, while his neighbour, who may be a
judicial tenant up to £40 valuation and who
is in a very much better financial position
altogether, escapes.
there is a conflict of opinion between the
advisers of the Government and Lord Justice
Andrews as to what is and what is not inside
the competence of our Parliament. We have
excluded from this Bill those matters which
are clearly outside our province, and we are
dealing now with those matters which are
clearly within our competence or those in
regard to which there is a certain amount of
doubt. We have had several consultations
with the Imperial Home Secretary, and he
has undertaken to recommend the introduction of a Bill to put it beyond doubt that
what is contained in this Bill will be given
full legal effect to. With regard to the
matters which are clearly outside our competence, I am still in consultation with the
Home Secretary.
I think it would be a convenience to the
House if I was to state shortly the existing
law in regard to jury qualifications. The
qualifications are that every man between 21
and 65 and under the Sex Disqualification
Act which was passed in 1919, this also applies
to women-having the necessary qualifications, is liable to serve on all juries, the
qualifications being:-
(1) he should have a clear annual income
of £10 from freehold lands;
(2) that he should have a clear annual
income of £20 from leasehold lands;
(3) that he should either in his own
name, or as a member of a firm, be rated-
(a) in respect of lands and tenements of
a net annual valuation of £40;
or
(b) £10 in respect of a dwelling-house,
except in the case of Belfast and Derry,
where the valuation should be £20.
(4) that he should be a Director of any Incorporated Company;
(5) that he should be a member of a
Board of Harbour Commissioners.
With regard to the first of these two qualifications, that is, an income of £10 from freehold or £20 from leasehold lands, a great deal
of difficulty has arisen in regard to what this
exactly means.
Some Revising Barristers take the view that
the annual income must necessarily mean the
valuation; others interpret it as the actual income, and the result has been extremely unsatisfactory, but apart altogether from this
point of view, hon. Senators are aware that
the tenure of land in Northern Ireland has
altered very materially owing to the passing
In the
of the various Land Purchase Acts.
old days a £10 freeholder was very often a
man of substance and a man well able to bear
the burden of Jury Service. The operation
With regard to urban areas other
than Derry and Belfast, 50 years ago
a £10 valuation brought in a fairly
substantial man, but since that time
the standard of valuation has increased
enormously, and the result is that this particular qualification captures a large number
of individuals to whom Jury Service is a real
hardship. In the country the existing qualifications produce an unnecessarily large
number of jurors, while in the city the reverse is the case, and in the country, as the
result of the present system, a very large
number of individuals who can ill afford the
time are called upon to serve when there is
really no necessity for them to be summoned.
In the cities the number is too small, and
individuals are called upon to serve at
frequent intervals, and consequently the
burden is felt very severely, more particularly
in Belfast since the transfer of the Courts
from Dublin to Belfast, where the juries have
to try cases which arise from all parts of the
Six Counties.
In the present Bill we propose to simplify
the qualifications. The Commission recommended the entire abolition of the freeholder and the leaseholder qualification, and
the substitution of a simple rating qualification, and in this Bill we have adopted the
Commission's recommendation, although at
the same time we cannot agree with the rating
recommendations of the Commission, because
with the fuller statistics at our disposal, if
the recommendations of the Commission
were adopted, we would not be able to produce sufficient jurors. The new qualifications, therefore, under our Bill will be, in
the county boroughs £20--the same as it was
before. In the case of urban districts and
towns £20, and £16 for other parts of the
counties. I believe that in adopting these
figures we will be able to get a sufficient
number of jurors, so that no man will have
to serve more than once in three years, and
possibly once in four or five years. At the
same time we will relieve the man of small
means, especially those who are dependent
upon their daily wages, as well as farmers
and shopkeepers, who have no assistants to
work for them in their absence.
At the present time, individuals who are
qualified in Belfast, and who live inside the
radius of 12 miles, are called upon to serve
in the city, and not in the county, and we
propose to extend this radius from 12 miles
to 25 miles. We think it only right that
I
Jury Laws Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Sir Dawson Bates]
men who have their business and earn their
livelihood in Belfast should do their share
of jury service in the cities rather than in
the country. It has been found that there
has been considerable evasion of jury service. Men have transferred their property
to their wives in order to escape, and we
have made provision in the Bill to stop
this unfair practice, by enacting that the
husband is to be deemed to be rated in
respect of the premises for the purpose of
the Jury Law, unless it is shown-and the
onus will be on the husband-that the property came to the wife otherwise than by
gift from a husband, or by an arrangement
with him, and I think that this will possibly
put an end to a practice that has grown
into a considerable abuse.
Now we come to one of the most important parts of the Measure, the question of
exemptions, and hon. Senators will, if they
turn to the 3rd Schedule, find these set out.
In putting forward these exemptions we
have been guided by this principle; that no
citizen is entitled as a matter of privilege to
claim exemption, and that the only grounds
for exemption are the convenience of the
public or the interests of the State. It will
be noted that we have abolished the
exemption that has hitherto been given to
Civil Engineers and Pharmaceutical
Chemists, with the important exception in
regard to the latter that in the case of a
one-owner shop it would be in the interests
of the public that that shop should have
someone in a position to attend to the public.
It will also be seen that at the present time
all officers holding appointments under the
Government or any local authority are
exempt. There is no such general exemption
in England, and we do not see why the
exemption should be continued here, and
accordingly, with regard to our own Civil
Service, they will all have to serve, with the
exception of the Head and his Deputy in
each Ministry and a few officials in the
Home Office. As regards these latter, we
propose to exempt those officials who have
been dealing with criminal cases, as it is
obviously undesirable that individuals who
have had something to do with the preparation of a Crown case should also be asked to
serve on a Jury trying the case.
We also propose to exempt from service
those officials under the local authorities
who have the duty of preparing the Jury
Lists. They will be very few in number,
possibly three in the case of each local
authority.
We propose that, as regards Grand Juries
in Belfast, in the future these will be
selected by the Sheriff in the same way as is
done in the counties. It is proposed to do
away with Grand Juries at Quarter Sessions.
In the future when a prisoner is returned
to trial, the Bill will be sent to the County
Court Judge, who, after perusal of the
depositions, will communicate whether or
not in his opinion there is a prima facie
case. If he decides there is, such a case will
then go to the common jury for trial.
One of the causes of the difficulties in the
present Jury system arises from the method
of summoning jurors. The procedure is laid
down in the 1876 Act. It was clearly
intended at that time that no man should
have to serve more than once in 3 years, but
owing to the peculiar wording of the Section,
that is not the case, and I will try to
explain how this arose. When the Sheriff
gets his book for the first time he draws his
panel. He takes the first name at the letter
"A"; the next name commencing with "B,"
and so on until he gets his panel. He then
goes on. When he draws his next panel he
starts where he left off and works back.
But at the end of 3 years he has to give up
his book, and he gets a fresh book, and he
has to start all over again. The result is
that those individuals whose names appear
at the end of any letter, such as the name of
Arnot, or the name of McMullan, are never
called upon to serve at all-particularly in
the country, and the effect of Clause 6 is
to do away with this difficulty in the present
procedure. In the future a sheriff will continue to carry on until he has exhausted
the entire names and then he will go back
again. The result of all this has been that
the degree of Jury Service is largely
dependent on the chance of the initial
letters of a man's name.
One of the great difficulties in the present
system is the form of revision. As the law
now stands the name of every individual
who is prima facie entitled to serve is put
on the list each year, whether he is entitled
to exemption or not, and the result is that
an individual who has reached the age of
65 years, and who may be entitled to
exemption, appears year after year with
embarrassment both to the individual and
to the Court. Under the new machinery
which we propose to set up the first list
which will be prepared will contain the name
of every individual who is prima facie
entitled to serve. That will come before
the Revising Barrister, who will proceed to
consider claims for exemption, and each will
be ruled on by him. Where a case of
exemption is proved to his satisfaction,
either on the grounds of ill health or any
other cause, the Revising Barrister will strike
the name off the list and he will mark it
"permanent" or "temporary" as the case
may be. If marked "permanent" the name
will not appear on future lists, but if
marked "temporary" it will appear after a
reasonable time, but before it is ruled on
the individual will receive notice and be
able to appear before the Revising Barrister
Jury Laws Bill-
Second Reading.
19 MAY 1926
to show cause why he should still be
exempted.
At the present time under the Local Government Act of 1898 the expense of preparing
the Jurors' List is borne by the County
Council as a County at large charge, but at
the same time the Urban district councils
are also liable for the expense of preparing
their lists, and the result is that the Urban
Councils have to pay twice over, and we propose to remedy that.
One of the most important provisions
which has been inserted in the Bill is that
we give power to the presiding judge to divide the panel into two or more sections, and
this will enable him to excuse from attendance on any specified days during the sittings of the court the jurors whose names
appear in any one or more of those sections
into which the panel should have been
divided. The provision is one entirely in
the interests of the jurors, and the
arrangement, which I have indicated, will
obviate the very great inconvenience to
which jurors are subject at present, as it will
enable the judge to keep one section of the
panel for a certain period and avoid keeping
an unnecessarily large number of jurors hanging about the court. We propose to confer
powers on judges similar to those which
they have in England under which a judge,
after a very heavy trial-for example, a
murder trial-will be empowered to excuse
the jurors who have served from further service for a period of years, and I cannot but
think that this should appeal to the House
as an eminently fair provision.
We also provide machinery for the judges
to deal with cases where incapacity to serve
has escaped the notice of the Revising
Barrister or has arisen since the revision. At
the present time if a man who is otherwise
disqualified appears on the jury panel, while
the judge may on that occasion excuse him
from attendance at a particular assize, he has
no power to strike his name off the jury panel,
with the result that he will be called on the
following assizes. We propose to give the
judge power to direct the sheriff to pass over
such name during the currency of the list,
and we provide machinery for ensuring that
the circumstance will be brought before the
notice of the Revising Barrister at the next
revision.
It is felt, and I cannot but think not without reason, that sometimes medical certificates are given to jurors without sufficient
grounds, and as a result the judges are placed
in a somewhat difficult position. We propose
under this Bill that a juror is only to be
excused on the ground of ill-health on the
production of a proper medical certificate, the
form of which is set out in the schedule. It
is true that the Commission recommend that
a certificate should be accompanied by an
affidavit, but we feel that this would impose
expense and be a burden on the juror, and we
think that the matter will be rectified by
the present proposals. With regard to the
question of fines, we propose that fines should
be imposed by the judge unless some reasonable cause is shown for non-attendance, and
that immediately after the fine is imposed,
notice should be given to the defaulter and
subsequenty enforced, unless the fine is remitted by the Judge for cause shown. If
this new law is strictly enforced it will do
much to remove the causes of grievance on
the part of jurors who are anxious to do
their duty, while others seek to avoid it.
At the present time it is customary to
pay Petty Jurors, in common law cases, the
sum of £1 1s 0d, that is, for the whole Jury,
which means a sum of 1s 9d each. We now
propose that each juryman should receive
the sum of 5s. This remuneration will, of
course, be borne by the litigants. As regards
Special Juries, at the present time they receive a
sum of £1 1s 0d each, and it is
customary only to pay this fee when a jury
has found a verdict, or the case has been
settled after the jury has been sworn, and
we propose to make this custom legal. We
also propose that as regards the View Jury
each juryman should receive an additional
sum of £1 1s 0d. These fees will also be
paid by the litigants. It is felt that a
great loss of time has been incurred by juries
having to separate during the luncheon interval, and frequently jurymen are unable to
get luncheon, and we propose that in future,
instead of the jury separating to get
luncheon, luncheon shall be provided for
them at the expense of the litigants.
With regard to the Coroners' Court, we
propose that, unless the Coroner deems it
necessary, in the interests of justice, it
should be unnecessary for the jury to view
the body.
We also take power to bring the different
parts of the Act into force at different times.
This will enable us to put into force portions
of it which are essential for the preparation of the Jurors' Lists, even though the
arrangements with the Imperial Government with regard to other matters under
the Bill which I have mentioned may not
be completed in time.
I fear I must have made considerable
demands on the patience of the House, but
before sitting down I would like to refer to
certain of the recommendations which were
made by the Commission, and which we have
not been able to see our way to adopt. As
I said before, these come under two categories, those which are within our power to
deal with, and those which partly come
within our competence and partly outside,
Jury Laws Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Sir Dawson Bates]
The Committee recommended the payment
of expenses incurred by jurors in criminal
cases where they were brought over a distance of 10 miles. It was suggested by the
Committee that these expenses should be
paid out of the fines levied on absent jurors.
It is quite clear, however, that the fines on
jurors would not be anything like sufficient
to meet the demands on it, and some counties might have no Fine Fund at all. But
apart altogether from this aspect of the
question, this proposal would introduce an
entirely new principle of far reaching effect.
It is a principle which the Imperial Government, who will have to confirm this Bill, is
utterly opposed to.
Jury service in criminal cases has, from
the earliest times, been regarded as a duty
of citizenship which ought to be discharged
by the citizen without payment, and, while
citizenship has many privileges attaching to
it, it has also got its obligations. After all,
criminal law exists not merely for the
punishment of the criminal but it is also
designed for the purpose of protecting lawabiding citizens, and if such get the protection of the law they are entitled to do their
share in the administration of justice.
Apart altogether from this aspect of the
question, the present Bill is designed to ensure
that no one should be asked to serve more
than once in three years, or even five years,
and surely it is not too much to ask a citizen
to give a small portion of his time to the
service of the State. In addition, as I have
already pointed out, we are raising the
qualification, and the result will be that in
future we will not have to call upon the small
ratepayer to whom jury service is a real
hardship. We have also given powers to
the Judge to divide the panel into sections,
and this will mean that jurors will not be.
detained for such long periods as heretofore.
It is recommended by the Committee that
we should pay certain expenses incurred by
the Sheriff, but I would remind hon. Senators that this matter was fully considered
in 1920, when the salaries and duties of the
sub-sheriffs were considered and fixed, and
we did not consider that any new condition
of affairs had arisen to justify this additional expense being thrown upon the Crown.
There was a further recommendation that,
as regards women jurors, they should be
given the option of deciding whether they
should serve or not. Well, after all, the
Sex Disqualification Act of 1919, put women
on a par with men, giving them the same
privileges, and if it gives them the same
privileges, women should accept the same
obligations as men. This recommendation
would fly in the face of the modern idea of
equality of men and women. It is also recommended that in order to meet the case
of an individual who lives in an hotel or
boarding-house, and who thus escaped jury
service, there should be an income qualification, and I think hon. Senators who have
had the experience of meeting Income Tax
Assessors, and having to disclose their income, will realise how impracticable it would
be to set up machinery to investigate new
incomes. The Government would have to
set up a special Department to investigate
the incomes of such persons.
It is suggested (1) that we should reduce
juries from 12 to 6; (2) that we should reduce the number of challenges where persons are jointly charged; and (3) that we
should alter the form of oath. While these
matters are inside our competence, so far
as regards Quarter Sessions, they are clearly
outside our competence in so far as the
High Court is concerned. We have conferred
with the Imperial Government, who are
opposed to making any alteration on the
lines indicated, and in the circumstances I
do not think it is necessary to waste the
time of this House in discussing the merits of
them.
One of the most important recommendations which the Commission made was that
certain trials of a commercial character
should be tried by a judge without a jury.
This recommendation was based on certain
provisions of the Administration of Justice
Act, 1920, passed in England, but that Act
was subsequently repealed, as it was found
that this Act did not work satisfactorily.
We are, however, discussing the matter with
the Imperial Home Secretary, and I hope it
will be possible to have a Clause inserted
in an English Bill, whereby complicated civil
cases, requiring expert witnesses and examination of documents, should be tried by
a judge without a jury, but I have already
said that this can only be carried out by
arrangement with the Imperial Government.
In conclusion, I desire to point out that
the Government have endeavoured, so far
as possible, to make this Bill self-contained
and to avoid legislation by reference. It
was not possible to have a complete modification of the existing Acts, but I think, on the
whole, the present Bill will be a very good
guide on the subject of the jury laws. We have
tried to make the qualification of jurors fair
and equitable, and I think we have removed
the present inequalities in regard
Jury Service. We have endeavoured to stop
evasion, and I think we have improved the
administrative procedure of the preparation
of Jurors' Lists. I cannot but think that
when the Bill has come into full working
order it will be found to be a simple Bill, a
good Bill, and one that will remove the
burdens which at the present time press very
hard upon individuals who are not able
to
Jury Laws Bill—
Second Reading.
19 MAY 1926
to bear them. Again, I would like to thank
hon. Senators for the patient hearing they
have given me.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I am
sure all the Members of this House have
listened with great interest and admiration
to the lucid speech of the Minister of Home
Affairs. There are only two matters I would
like to refer to, and one of them the right
hon. Gentleman did not touch on at all.
In the Second Schedule the Province is
divided into two parts, and I do not understand why in the case of Antrim, Armagh
and Down the net annual value of the rating
qualification of special jurors is £100 or upwards in respect of lands, tenements, or
hereditaments, while in the Counties of Fermanagh, Londonderry, and Tyrone, the
qualification is only £70. I do not know
whether it is because the ratepayers in the
first three named counties are considered to
be superior to the ratepayers in the other
three counties, but I should have thought
the qualification would have been the same
in the case of all the counties.
Another matter that caused me some
difficulty was the retention in the Bill as it
was originally presented of the Grand
Jury at Quarter Sessions, but in the Bill as
now presented to this House that difficulty
has been removed by such Grand Juries
having been abolished. Under the Bill as
it now stands, before a bill goes to the Jury
the Judge at Quarter Sessions will have to
state whether in his opinion it is a true
bill or not. As the case will already have
been investigated by the Petty Sessions
Court or Resident Magistrates, the interests
of the person or persons concerned will be
quite safe. At the same time I am sorry to
see such a relic of old times disappear, but
I think the necessity for it has passed.
In the old days, when there were no Petty
Sessions Courts and no Resident Magistrates
any gentleman who was a magistrate might
have been interviewed by a police constable
and over a bottle of claret he might have
there and then returned a person to be tried
by a judge and jury. Those days have gone
and now all cases are properly investigated.
Another matter on which the Minister of
Home Affairs relieved my mind was that
under this Bill the advantage of one's name
commencing with "Mac" is done away with,
and that in future all persons who have that
illustrious cognomen to their name will
come into line with persons of other names
for the purpose of jury service. In the past
many of them never served on a jury at all,
and we will all rejoice to see them on the
jury in future.
Mr. DORMAN: I want to join with the
hon. Senator who has just sat down in congratulating the Minister of Home Affairs
on the very lucid explanation he has given
us of the provisions of this Bill. I think it
will be looked forward to with a considerable amount of interest by the class in the
community which has undoubtedly suffered
as a result of the state of anarchy which has
prevailed in connection with jury service in
the past. I am especially pleased to see that
the Government has provided some compensation for those who are summoned from a
distance to attend as jurors in criminal
cases.
My mind goes back to forty years ago when
the country was in a very disturbed condition and when I, as a young man, was
dependent on my earnings for the upkeep
of my home. I then had the misfortune to
be a juryman, and all the cases in the South
and West were being concentrated in the
county in which I resided. The Assizes
extended over three, four, and sometimes
five weeks, and after one Assize it took me
fully a year to recover from the injustice
inflicted upon me in endeavouring to comply
with the law by attending in the capital of
that particular county to perform my duties
as a juror. Therefore I am sure this Bill
will be welcomed by jurymen who live in
large centres where criminal cases are tried.
I would like to draw the attention of the
Minister to one matter. The Government
has exempted from jury service certain
officials in the Home Office because they will
necessarily be brought into contact with the
evidence that may be put forward by the
Crown when the case comes before a Judge
and jury. It seems to me that there is an
equally just claim to be made for the exemption of subordinate clerks in a solicitor's
office. The chief clerk and other clerks in a
solicitor's office are necessarily engaged from
day to day in drawing up briefs for the information of counsel, and from a psychological
standpoint it appears to me that their minds
will unconsciously become biased and they
could not be looked upon as unbiased jurors.
It is possible that a chief clerk in a
solicitor's office who is preparing a civil case
for his employer may be summoned to act
upon the jury, and it seems to me that some
provision ought to be made in the Bill for
the exemption of persons who will thus be
unnecessarily placed in such a position that
they cannot be looked upon as unbiased
jurors. I am sure a great many jurors in
Northern Ireland will welcome this Measure,
and it will remove a grievance many jurors
had in the past that they were doing more
than their fair share of jury service.
Jury Laws Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: Like the other speakers, I
have listened with great attention and
pleasure to the exposition of the Measure
before us which the Minister of Home Affairs
has done us the favour of addressing to us
this afternoon. If I may, I should like to
express the hope that the right hon. Gentleman will come to see us again at a date not
too distant. His visits are always welcome,
but in the past they have been somewhat
rare.
Passing to the subject of this afternoon's
discussion, I think one cannot but begin by
expressing something in the nature of eulogy
of the Report on which this Bill is based.
The Report itself is a most able one, and to
those of us who have read it it must have
afforded the greatest possible assistance
in getting a grip in our minds of a subject
which really is not free from complication
but which has a great deal of historic
interest attached to it. A great deal of the
history of our Jury Laws has been epitomised and condensed in the introductory
portion of the Report I hold in my hand. 1
do not know whether it is to the Secretary
of the Committee that we are indebted for
this Report, or whether the scholarly hand
of the Deputy Keeper of Records is to be
detected in some portion of it, but however
it may be, thanks are undoubtedly due from
us for a very good work well and carefully
performed.
As regards the various changes the right
hon. Gentleman indicated, I do not believe
this House will regard it as incumbent on
it as a Second Chamber to go very carefully
into the details of revision of valuation.
After all, those are matters of regulation,
and they have been dealt with elsewhere, and
I do not imagine there will be any disposition to criticise any of those details involving the slight changes indicated.
point that will concern and interest us is as
to whether the changes which are proposed
will supply the requisite number of jurors,
and I am thinking, of course, particularly of
jurors who have to serve in Belfast, because
it is on the jurors of Belfast that the great
work of the last few years has particularly
fallen.
The
I think the right hon. Gentleman alluded to
the great increase in the mass of jury work
which was caused by tba new arrangement
necessitating the hearing in Belfast of cases
which formerly were heard in Dublin. It is
obvious that anything that can be done to remove a grievance of that nature must be
done, and if the measures adumbrated in the
Bill do not prove sufficient to effect their
purpose then Parliament will have to give
more extensive powers. We might, I suppose, in the last resort have to adopt a plan
somewhat similar to that by which jurors
are provided in the Central Criminal Court
in London.. The arrangement there,
as the House probably knows, is that
persons in the county of Middlesex, and
in large parts of the counties of Surrey and
Kent, are liable for service, but they are
allowed to set off such service if they should
happen to be struck in the same year for
service in their own county.
The Minister of Home Affairs has not proposed to go anything like so far as that, but
he proposes to extend the twelve miles radius
to twenty-five miles in the case of jurors
whose business is in the city. I understood
the right hon. Gentleman to say that a
juror caught under that provision would not
be liable for service in his county as well.
Sir DAWSON BATES indicated assent.
Mr. LESLIE: I thought so, but I could
not find anything directly providing for that
in the Bill. It is a distinctly important
matter. As regards the exemptions, I am
with the right hon. Gentleman in keeping
exemptions down with a very strong hand,
because I am with him in thinking that jury
service, being one of those few definite duties
which the subject owes to the Crown, ought
not to be in any way cast aside and regarded
as an unfair burden. It is a duty of the
subject to the Crown, and I do not think
you can put it in any other way. Therefore,
the service should be performed by all as
their turn comes.
The right hon. Gentleman has made a distinct change in removing from the Civil
Service the exemption it formerly enjoyed.
As to that I can only say that if a different
view had been taken by the Government, and
if it had been decided to retain the exemption of civil servants, I should have had no
hesitation in supporting the Government. I
think, on the whole, that the effect of employing civil servants occasionally as jurymen, can only be to relieve some other member of the community from service, and in so
far as that happens there is a double favour.
Your civil servant is required to perform the
specific duties assigned to him, and if he is
taken away from his normal duties to serve
the Crown elsewhere-because no man can
serve in two places at once-it seems to me
that on that jury he is taking the place of
some other citizen who has thereby escaped
269:
270,
Jury Laws Bill-
Second Reading.
19 MAY 1926
his share of work. I am sure popular feeling, generally, will be with the right hon.
Gentleman, but I hope there will not be any
extension of the principle, and it has not
been taken very far.

There is another point regarding exemptions, and I am not sure that that is not a
point rather for you than for me, and that
is the exemption of servants of the Houses
of Parliament. They do not seem to me to
be exempt under the wording of the Bill, unless it is suggested that they are persons
exempted by virtue of any prescription" and
so on. I think it will be admitted that the
officials of the Houses of Parliament, in accordance with those ancient privileges
of Parliament, ought to be specifically
exempted. I do not suppose any objection will be taken as regards the
exemption of pharmaceutical chemists, for
their defence is impregnable; in fact I almost
thought the Minister of Home Affairs was
going to quote to us the reported, if not decided, case dealing with the exemption of
a person who had no qualified assistant.
Leaving the question of exemptions, one
comes to the proposal for varying the arrangements as regards the selection of Grand
Juries at the Belfast Commission. I am sure
everybody will agree that the course taken is
the right one, and I presume the Government
is satisfied that the new Clause is as watertight as it can be made. It is not very expressive in itself, but those who are
acquainted with the earlier Act of 1836 dealing with Grand Juries, will be aware that
that Act is not particularly explanatory
either.
Then one comes to that part of the Bill
which gives me the greatest difficulty, and
that is Clause 5-the proposal, to use the
words of the Bill, to abolish Grand Juries
at Courts of Quarter Sessions. That is not
a point on which I ought to detain the House
this afternoon, but I shall take the opportunity of bringing it forward again in a few
days time. I cannot agree with the right
hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel Sharman Crawford), who has just left us, as to
the reasons why Grand Juries were introduced, because it is a matter of history that
Grand Juries were infinitely older than the
Commission of the Peace. The origin of the
Grand Jury must be taken to be lost in the
Imists of the Saxon ages, but, at all events,
I will not deal further with that to-day beyond pointing out that there are other points
to be considered besides the mere question of
the trial of persons. There are still rights
of preferring an indictment to a Grand Jury
at Quarter Sessions by an ordinary person.
I do not know that those rights have been
exercised within living memory, but so far
as I know they still exist, and they were reenacted in 1908 in an Imperial Statute.
Generally, the provisions of the Bill deal
with the relief of individuals, and, as such,
are welcome, and I think there will be
general satisfaction that in the case of
Coroners' Juries provision has been made to
relieve some members of the jury from the
necessity of viewing the body. If I understand the right hon. Gentleman correctly
it will still be open to some of the jury to
view the body if they express any desire to
do so without the entire jury being required
to view the body. Nowadays there can be no
object in going through the whole gruesome
form of viewing the body, because evidence
as to the personality of the deceased can be
obtained otherwise.
There is a general absence of interference
about the Bill, due partly to the considerations alluded to by the right hon. Gentleman
-the difficulty of dealing with our own High
Court procedure. I am thinking particularly of non-interference with the service of
women jurors, the right of challenge, and
the method of swearing the jury. On the
whole, I am glad that no dislocation of the
existing practice on those matters has been
attempted, and apart from the one very
debatable question I have mentioned, I do
not think this Bill will be likely to give us
very great trouble. I feel sure it will be
received with general satisfaction throughout the Province, and I am sure that that
feeling of satisfaction will be tempered with
some feeling of regret that it has not been
found possible to give something to a juror
in criminal cases, even his railway fare. 1
think that if it had been possible to go so
far as that-and further I would not like to
suggest you should go, because you cannot
make service in criminal cases a matter of
payment, but you might perhaps have gone
so far as to pay a juror's railway fare. We
cannot suggest that course in this House, but
I think there will be a feeling that it should
be done. On general grounds I have pleasure
in supporting the Bill.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I should like to join
in the congratulations that have been so
happily expressed by other hon. Members
to the Minister of Home Affairs for his lucid
explanation of this Bill. I view with very
great satisfaction the introduction in the
Bill of the Fifth Schedule, where most specific information is required to be inserted in
a doctor's certificate which has to be given
in the case of a person seeking exemption on
the ground of ill-health. In times gone by
members of the medical profession have been
placed in a position of great embarrassment,
more particularly those members of the profession who are known as family attendants,
and I think the fact that they will have to
supply definite details, as set out in this
Schedule, will, in some respects, remove that
difficulty.
Second Reading. 272
Jury Laws Bill-
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sinclair]
My object in rising is to inquire to what
extent the members of the medical profession
can calculate on reasonable secrecy in the
handling of these certificates through the
various channels through which they may
pass. We are asked now to state in the
certificate the precise nature and probable
duration of the illness. That will be fully
set forth, but it will be a matter of satisfaction to know that all reasonable secrecy can
be counted upon both in respect of the person claiming exemption and the doctor who
issued the certificate. A great many cases
will arise in which the first paragraph in
the Schedule could not be adeqately filled in
-cases in which no definite disease can be
reported, but where a sort of general disability might be recognised by a doctor, and i
should like to know whether in a case involving that difficulty the filling in of paragraph 4 would answer the purpose. So far
as I can judge the Bill is a very good one,
and, in the main, I will support it.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I would
like to join with the other hon. Senators who
have spoken in welcoming the Minister of
Home Affairs to this House, and congratulating him on the interesting statement he has
made. His explanation of the provisions of
the Bill has made it fairly easy for such as
I to understand it. I am satisfied that this
Measure will remove a number of injustices
that have hitherto existed in the administration of the Jury Laws. I have had the experience-probably through being one of the
unfortunate people the initial of whose name
occurs early in the alphabet-of being summoned pretty often to the Assizes as a juror,
and I know what a hardship it is for a man
who can ill afford the time.
It has been stated here that it is an obligation on the part of a citizen to act on a jury,
but, apparently, it is not an obligation on
the part of all citizens. You have drawn a
line and you say a person under a certain
valuation is not entitled to serve on a jury
and give his services for the benefit of the
community. You have said to the people
on the other side of the line that it is
obligatory on them to act as jurors, and it
must be said that the persons on either side
of that line have considerable hardships to
contend with.
Take, for instance, the case of a man who
may have an industrious wife and a large
family. He has taken a shop, the valuation
of which just qualifies him to be summoned
as a juror. Simply beeause his wife is assisting him in very difficult times that man
is required to serve as a juror and has to lose
probably a week or a fortnight attending at
the courts. I would like to see something
done to relieve the hardship which falls on a
person who is earning a weekly wage. You
may argue that it is an obligation on the
part of citizens to act as jurymen, but you
want jurymen who are prepared to give their
services willingly, and who can devote the
necessary attention to the work.
It is impossible to think that a man who
is there under such conditions of hardship
as I have described can give proper attention
to the case that is being tried. Many men
have come to me and complained of their
position in connection with this matter. I
will give you an actual case that was quoted
to me of a man who had a shop and his wife
was ill. He was compelled to close his shop
in order to attend the Court as a juror, and
he was not by any means in a position to
stand the loss involved. I am of opinion
that the time has arrived when all jurymen
should be paid. It is all very well to say
that it is an obligation on them to render
this service to the State, but is there anyone
else who gives his services free in Assize
cases? The judge is paid, counsel on both
sides are paid, the police are paid; in fact no
one connected with a case gives his services
gratuitously except the juryman. A good
deal of agitation has been going on for a
considerable time in favour of the payment
of jurors.
The Minister of Home Affairs has told us
he has increased the payment from 1s 9d to
5s for each juryman in civil cases, but the
litigant is required to pay that. The Crown
are acting for the people of the whole
Province, and why should not the Crown
make provision for the payment of jurors?
A person who can well afford to act as a
juryman might be called upon to give his
services gratuitously, but other persons who
are unable to serve without considerable loss
to themselves should either be paid or
exempt. I feel strongly on this matter, because the first time I was called upon to
serve as a juror I could ill afford to lose a
fortnight's wages, and there was no dole to
draw. I have known other workmen who
were called upon because they happened to
come just within the £20 valuation, and I
know it meant a considerable sacrifice on
their part to serve.
Even at this time I think the right hon.
Gentleman should take this matter into consideration. I know this House has nothing
to do with the financial part of the Bill, but
the suggestions I have made are
carried out it would relieve many
poor men who will be called
upon
It is stated,
if
to
serve under this Bill.
of course, that this is a duty a man owes to
the State, but I maintain that a workman's
first duty is to his wife and family, and a
man who is called upon to forego a fortnight's
wages is not doing what is fair to his wife
and family. He is neglecting them to serve
the State.
Jury Laws Bill.
Economy Bill.
19 MAY 1926
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sure it is the will
of the House that the Minister of Home
Affairs should reply. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
Sir DAWSON BATES: The right hon.
and gallant Gentleman (Colonel Sharman
Crawford) drew the attention of the House
to the difference in the valuation regarding
special jurors in the Counties of Antrim,
Down, and Armagh, and Fermanagh, Londonderry, and Tyrone. In the latter three
counties the valuation is much lower than
in the other three counties, but in fixing
these figures we had in view the object of
getting a sufficient number of jurors. The
right hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel
Sinclair) raised a most interesting point as
to the degree of secrecy that could be
counted upon by the medical profession with
regard to the certificates they will be required to furnish in the case of a juror seeking exemption on the ground of ill-health.
The certificate the medical practitioner supplies will be given to the individual concerned, and that will go from him direct to
the judge, and I am sure the latter, occupying the high and responsible position he does,
will have every regard for the secrecy that
should necessarily be imposed in the case of
all such certificates issued by medical prac
titioners.
Senator Joseph Cunningham pointed out
the hardship that falls on the one-man business and the man who earns a weekly wage.
Everyone knows that some hardship must
arise in some of these cases, but I hope, as I
said before, that under the new system those
cases of hardship will be few and far between,
and that there will not be an undue burden
thrown on the shoulders of those who are
not fit to bear it. At the same time I am
sure the hon. Member will appreciate the
difficulty there is of knowing where to draw
the line. The higher you make the valuation the fewer jurors you have to call upon.
In the case he has referred to, where the wife
of a man owning a business is ill, I cannot
imagine any judge refusing to allow that
juror complete exemption for that particular
Assize.
Senator Dorman suggested that solicitors'
clerks should be exempted from jury service.
I do not know whether he proposed that because he thinks lawyers' clerks cannot be
trusted, but if so, I do not think it is a correct view to hold. If a solicitor's clerk is
called upon to serve on a jury in a criminal
case in which his employer is defending the
prisoner, the Crown will take good care to
stand that particular individual by, and
similarly in a civil case I am certain one
solicitor will take care that any clerk
of the opposing solicitor is not on the jury.
Therefore, so far as the public interest is
concerned I do not think the point arises.
A point was raised by Senator Leslie which
he said was not made quite clear in the Billthe case of an individual who resided within
the 25 miles' radius, and who would have to
serve as a juror in Belfast. I can assure the
hon. Member that the expression used in
Clause 1,"deemed to be resident," will
automatically exempt such a person from
serving in his own county, and he will only
have to serve in the city. Another point
mentioned by Senator Leslie concerned
officials of the Houses of Parliament. We
feel that if such an official is called upon to
serve during the time Parliament is sitting
there will not be the slightest difficulty in
securing exemption for those particular
Assizes, and when he was again summoned
probably Parliament would not be sitting.
The whole question of exemption is full of
difficulties, because the more people you
exempt the greater is the burden that will
fall on other people, and that is why we have
tightened matters up as much as possible.
Under the Bill it is quite clear there will in
future not be any obligation on the part of
a Coroner's Jury to view the body unless the
Coroner deems it necessary in the interests
of justice, and I hope those cases will be few
and far between. I think I have dealt with
the main points that have been raised, and I
would in conclusion again like to thank this
House for its kindness in allowing me to
appear before it.
On Question, Motion (That this Bill be now
read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Economy (Exchequer Relief) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.

Ferrard.)
Mr. LONG: There are two proposals in
this Bill to which I take strong exception.
One is the proposal of the Minister of
Finance to take a certain proportion of the
Dog Licence revenue which formerly accrued
to the county councils, and to which I referred on the Committee Stage. In the County
of Londonderry the amount received from
Dog Licences was 1,450 per annum, or thereabouts, and hon. Members will understand
that the proposal in the Bill will make a
considerable difference to the finances of the
local authority.
The other matter to which I object very
strongly is the capitalisation of the Road
Fund. It is quite true that the county
councils will in future receive an amount
not less than that they formerly received,
but I think it was a legitimate and natural

transport. I think this proposal is alto-

gether unjustified, if not immoral, and I very

277 Teachers' Superannuation Bill.

20 MAY 1926
Firearms Bill.
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 20th May, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Teachers' Superannuation Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of the
House): I beg to move the Second Reading
of this Bill, which, as the title shows, is
concerned with the amalgamation into one
fund of certain pension funds for teachers,
and with provision for payments to be made
into and from that fund. It is purely
administrative in character, and does not
make any change in the rates of pension
payable to teachers. As hon. Senators are
aware, this House recently passed a Measure
dealing solely with that subject.
The Teachers' Pension Fund dates from
1879, in which year an Act was passed which
provided that the Commissioners of Church
Temporalities in Ireland should, for the
purpose of establishing a pension fund,
provide either a sum not exceeding £1,300,000
or annual interest on that sum at the rate
of 3 per cent. The Act also authorised the
Commissioners of Education to make deductions from
teachers' salaries, such
deductions to be paid into the fund, and
provided that the Commissioners of the
National Debt might make advances to the
fund. As a matter of fact, the Treasury
was obliged more than once to come to the
aid of the fund in order to prevent it
becoming bankrupt. But as up to the war
teachers' salaries were not very great, the
demands made upon the fund were not
excessive, and there was a possibility that
in time it might become solvent.
are
In 1920, however, as hon. Senators
probably aware, the salaries of teachers in
Ireland were greatly increased, and though
the fund naturally profited from the corresponding increased contributions from serving
teachers, this addition was greatly outbalanced by the enhanced rates of pension
which became payable. In the year 1922 the
accrued income and capital of the fund was
apportioned between Northern Ireland and
the Irish Free State, our share amounting
to something over £500,000. We have continued to finance the fund, as was done by
the Imperial Treasury in the past, that is
to say, by annual grants from State
funds. The fund is still not solvent, and it
would require the interest from a capital
sum of £1,500,000, to enable liabilities to be
met in future.
As, however, the National Teachers' Pension Fund of Great Britain is designed to
meet circumstances similar to our own, and
is being supported by annual grants which
will probably vary proportionately with our
requirements, the Government of Northern
Ireland anticipate that under the Colwyn
award we shall receive such an amount as will
enable us for some time to meet the demands
upon our own fund. Hence it follows that we
should merely be locking up money unnecessarily if we were to set aside the sum of
a million and a half, which I have mentioned,
for the purpose of providing the necessary
money to meet the needs of the teachers'
pensions. I should like to emphasise the
fact
that the present Bill is a State
guarantee to the Teachers' Pensions, and
when this Measure is passed, teachers will
in future have the statutory security of the
Government for the continual payment of
their pensions.
Turning to the Bill itself it will be seen
that it authorises the amalgamation into
one fund of the various pension funds of
teachers of different classes of education,
that is, primary, secondary, technical, and
agricultural. Provision is made for investment, and for payments to be made into and
out of the fund. Clause 5 deals with the
payment of the contributions made by local
authorities, or the Ministry of Education
and the Ministry of Agriculture in respect
of the teachers employed by them, and also
provides that teachers may retire voluntarily
at the age of 60, but in any case must retire
when they reach 65. This is merely applying
one of the rules of the Civil Service. The
Bill as a whole is clear, and hon. Senators
will not wish me to detain them with a
lengthy and detailed explanation. I beg
to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Firearms (Amendment) Bill.
Order for Second reading read..
;
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In rising to move the Second reading
of the Firearms (Amendment) Bill, I should
like to remind the House-although I am
sure the House will not need to be reminded
-that under the existing law, the Firearms
Act of 1920, all persons who have arms or
ammunition must obtain what is know as a
Firearm Certificate, and this certificate, as
Unemployment Insurance Bill. 280
Firearms Bill.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
the House knows, entitles the holder to have
in his possession, use, and carry a firearm.
These certificates can only be granted under
strict limitations, and the applicant must be
able to show that he has a good reason for requiring such certificate. Provision is made
for one class of certificate only, which not
only entitles the owner to have arms in his
possession, but also entitles him to carry
firearms.
I am sure the House will agree with me
when I say it is obviously desirable that the
carrying of arms should be restricted as far
as possible, and it is not proposed that the
present restrictions upon the grant of Firearm Certificates which, as I have said, entitles persons to carry arms, should be relaxed in any way, but it is considered that
there should be some means of enabling a
qualified permit to be given to allow persons
to have arms in their houses withcut
giving them the extended authority to carry
arms. There may be persons who for sentimental reasons desire to keep arms in their
houses, but have no desire to carry them.
the
I think the House will also agree with me
that the objection to the grant of a perinit
to
carry arms might not extend to
grant of
permit
a
merely
to
have
arms in possession, and there is
no reason why law-abiding citizens should
not be allowed to retain their own arms,
while not being permitted to carry them.
The main object, therefore, of this Bill is to
enable a permit to be granted to allow the
owner to keep arms on his premises if, in
the opinion of the authorities, such permit
can be granted without any danger to the
public. The fee charged for a Firearm
Certificate under the 1920 Act is 5s., in the
first instance, and 2s. 6d. for the annual renewal. For the limited permit under this
Bill the charge will be a nominal fee of
2s. 6d. for the first issue, and 1s. on each
annual renewal.
If hon. Members will look at Sub-secion 5
of Section 1 of the Bill they will see that
there is at the moment no specific power to
dispose of surrendered arms in cases where
the owner cannot be given a Firearm Certificate, and will not make arrangements for
the disposal of the arms, or in cases where
arms are found and the owner is unknown.
Moreover, in cases where arms are seized as
the result of some technical breach of the
law, and where, in the view of the Attorney-
General, the case is not of sufficient importance to justify a prosecution, the matter
can be really dealt with under this Section
by an order for the destruction or disposal
of the weapon in some other manner. The
existing powers of a court in respect to
orders for the disposal of arms are not, however, affected in any way.
At the present time there is no right of
appeal from the decision of the authority
granting the certificate, and we propose that
such a right should be given to the Minister
of Home Affairs where an applicant is dissatisfied with the decision which may have
been given by the authority by whom certificates are issued. I beg to move.
I would
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
like to say how pleased I am that the Government has introduced a Bill such as this. I have
during the past few months heard lamenta.
tions from men who have had firearms in
their possession for a long period, more because they were in the nature of memorials
or that they belonged to their grandfather or
some other predecessor, than for anything
else. These people were complaining very
bitterly that the authorities were removing
these ancient weapons from their homes.
This Bill will give those people an opportunity of applying for a permit which will
enable them to keep such weapons, which
are not dangerous, in their possession.
I am satisfied there are also cases where
a householder may require to keep firearms
in his house for defensive purposes, and in
the case of persons whose conduct has been
such as to justify the grant of a permit I
think they should be enabled to retain their
firearms.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I am in
agreement with the Bill, but there is one
question I would like to ask. In the old
days you had also got to have a permit to
have ammunition. Many people have shot
guns, and I have applied for permission to
obtain 200 cartridges, and when they are exhausted I want to know what steps I shall
have to take to get a new supply. Shall I
have to obtain a permit to go to a gun shop
to buy them; otherwise they may refuse to
supply me with any more.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I understand the hon. Senator wants
to know what happens in the case of a person
who has got a Firearms Certificate when his
ammunition runs out. That happened to me
this morning. I went to a certain gun-maker
in Belfast, and desired to buy certain cartridges for the purpose of shooting rooks.
There is on the Certificate a form to fill up,
and if the hon. Member signs that he can
get his ammunition without any difficulty.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I rise to move the Second Reading
I-

Adjournment.
Ancient Monuments Bill.

of this Bill, and I do not think it is necessary for me to say anything on it.
It is
a perfectly straightforward Bill, the object
of which is to extend the operation of the
Unemployment Insurance Act for a further
period that is, to the end of this year, or

I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the

Ancient Monuments Bill [s].
consideration of Commons
for
Order
Amendment read.
Commons Amendment:-
After Clause 13, insert the following new
Clause:-
14. (Expenses of Ministry of Finance and
Advisory Committee).
There shall be paid out of moneys provided by Parliament such expenses as may
be incurred by the Ministry of Finance in
the exercise of its powers in connection
with-
(a) the inspection and maintenance of
ancient monuments, whether vested in
the Ministry of Finance or of which that
Ministry is guardian;
(b) the purchase by agreement of any
ancient monument; and
(c) the payment of compensation for
acts done under this Act, involving interference with private rights of property;
and
(d) the purchase or assistance towards
the purchase of archæological objects of
which the finding has been reported to
the Ancient Monuments Advisory Committee;
and also such expenses of the Ancient
Monuments Advisory Committee as may be
sanctioned by the Ministry of Finance.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The Commons
Amendment referred to in the Orders of the
Day is merely the re-insertion of Clause 14,
which deals with the expenses of the Ministry
of Finance and the Advisory Committee. On
a question of privilege, discussion on this
Clause was ruled out when this House passed
the Bill, and the Amendment only means
that it has been re-inserted in another place.
I beg to move that this Clause be agreed to.
Amendment agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them therewith.
Adjournment,
Moved: That this House do now adjourn
until Tuesday next.-(Viscount Charlemont.)
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: It would
be a great convenience to some of us who
may be going away to know what the Sittings
of the House are likely to be after Tuesday.
When will the House finish its business?
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I should say
probably on Thursday.
