CLAUSE 1 (Confirmation of agreement and
consequential provisions).
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I should like
to say a word of explanation on a question
raised by Senator Leslie as to the meaning of
"officers." I am advised that the expression is a general one and it covers part-time
officers as well.
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the noble
Viscount for giving us that explanation. It
will be remembered that I only raised the
question on account of a special definition it
had been thought necessary to insert in the
Local Government Act, and I was anxious
that we should not make any mistake.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Existing and pensioned officers
of Reformatory School).
was
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: There
another point raised by Senator Leslie to the
effect that Clause 2, sub-Section 5, would enable the Minister of Finance to deprive an
existing pensioner of his rights. This is not
the case. The fourth sub-Clause lays it
down that every person who, on the prescribed date, was in receipt of a pension in
respect of past employment by the Committee
of Management at the Reformatory School
should, during his lifetime, continue to receive such pension. There is, in fact, only
one pensioner, who, I understand, is eightysix years of age and receives 10s per week,
and under no circumstances could he be deprived of his pension.
Mr. LESLIE: Again I should not propose
to pursue the matter. I entirely agree with
the noble Viscount that a claimant's right
is very fully dealt with in sub-Section 4, but
if any question does arise respecting the
claim, the decision is left, under sub-Clause
5, with the Minister of Finance. In view of
the explanation that there is but one pensioner, of very considerable years, I think
it is unnecessary to pursue the matter, but
I think it was right and necessary to call
the attention of the House to it, because I
am sure the House would be very slow to
allow anybody to be deprived of any right
of access to the Courts, and to have their
claim, whether they liked it or not, transferred to the adjudication of a Department.
But I am sure we will all agree that the
explanation is satisfactory.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Financial Provisions).
(1) There shall be paid out of moneys
provided by Parliament all such expenses
as may be incurred with the sanction of
the Ministry of Finance in connection with
the management and maintenance of
the Reformatory School and the Borstal
Institution, and in connection with the
maintenance of offenders in the said
School and Institution.
(2) The liabilities of the trustee and
the committee of management in connection with the Reformatory School outstanding on the date of the passing of
this Act, and any special bounties payable
under the provisions of this Act, shall be
charged upon the investments and funds
specified in the fourth schedule to the
agreement, and the balance of the said
investments and funds (if any) remaining
after all such charges have been satisfied,
shall be paid into, and form part of, the
Capital Fund established under section
four of the Exchequer and Financial Provisions Act (Northern Ireland), 1923.
(3) Where a youthful offender is ordered
to be sent to the Reformatory School,
the council of the county or county
borough in which he resides (to be specified in the order) shall pay towards the
expenses of such youthful offender, whilst
detained in the Reformatory School, a
contribution at such rate, and payable
at such times, as may be determined by
regulations made by the Ministry of Home
Affairs with the approval of the Ministry
of Finance; and the amount of any such
contribution shall be defrayed by the
council as expenses incurred by them
under Part IV. of the Children Act, 1908,
319 Malone Training School Bill-

9 NOVEMBER 1926
Committee.
and shall, for the purposes of sub-section
(3) of section ten of the Local Government
Act (Northern Ireland), 1923, he a sum
due from the council to the Ministry of
Home Affairs, and be deductible from
any grants which may be payable to the
council under that section.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Clause 3, page 5, leave out Clause 3.
an
Mr. LESLIE: The rather drastic Amendment standing in my name requires, I
think, rather more explanation than
ordinary verbal Amendment. It will be seen
that what I am asking the Committee to do
is to take out of the Bill the whole of
Clause 3; that is to say, to take out all the
financial provisions of the Bill. The reason
why I suggest this course is that a point
was raised on the Second Reading discussion by the right hon. and gallant Gentleman, Senator Colonel Sharman Crawford,
the Chairman not only of the Down County
Council but also of the Association of County
Councils, as regards the liability of the County
Councils, as representing their ratepayers,
for the maintenance of the Malone Reformatory School and for the maintenance of
any boys that might be sent to this Reformatory School. After the discussion in the
House the right hon. and gallant Gentleman was so kind as to draw my attention,
and the attention of others, to a Clause in
the Departmental Report on Reformatory
and Industrial Schools in Northern Ireland
which was presented to this House in June
1923.
more
I was aware, as I have no doubt other
hon. Members were, of the existence of this
Report, but Clause 4 in the summary of
Recommendations I must admit had entirely
escaped my recollection. That Clause recommended the adoption of the English and
Scottish system of financing these schools
whereby the cost would be divided equally
between State funds and local authorities'
contributions. That is to say, the local
authority could not be mulcted in
than 50 per cent. of the cost of maintenance
of their, shall we say, patients in those
schools. This Report of 1923 is, of course,
subsequent to the Children Act of 1908,
Section 73 of which dealt with this matter
of the responsibility of the County Councils
and reference to that, without recollection
of the Departmental Report, would, I think,
lead anybody to imagine that the proposal
in the Bill was perfectly satisfactory. However, with this report before us I do not
think anybody representing the County
Councils here to-day or who, in the past,
has served on County Councils, could agree
with the Bill as it stands,
It seems to me that the case is impregnable and that the claim is unanswerable
that the County Councils ought not to givo
more than fifty per cent. of the cost,
and it might well be that they ought
not to contribute even so much as that.
If that be admitted the question arises
how this Senate can give effect to
its determination to do the right thing,
and the moment one begins to consider that
question one is brought up against the rights
of this Senate on financial subjects. We
know that our rights in dealing with money
matters are defined in Section 16 of the
Government of Ireland Act, which is our
Constitution, and it might be thought that
merely to add a Clause here providing for
what I might call a fifty-fifty basis would be
within our powers. But I think that reference to the authorities and further
consideration would show that it would be
undesirable to suggest such a course here. It
would be most repugnant to us to do anything that could be deemed to infringe the
privileges of our colleagues who serve the
Constitution in another place, and it would
be a very undesirable thing to do if it could
be avoided.
There is a perfectly constitutional course
sanctioned by long usage in the Second
Chamber across the water. Where the House
of Lords cannot accept a financial proposal
as it stands the practice has been to simply
excise the entire Clause and leave it to those
whose financial responsibility is more
apparent to send back a new Clause. It is
for that reason that instead of suggesting a
merely verbal alteration I am suggesting to
the Committee that the entire Clause should
be excised. I accordingly beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: As the
Senator who originally drew attention to
what this Clause would mean to the County
Councils, I should like to say a few words.
The question of the contributions of the
Government and the County Councils for the
maintenance of these Reformatory and
Industrial Schools in Ireland has been a
source of contention between the County
Councils and the Government for many years.
The Clause as it stands puts the County
Councils in a very false position. I suppose
Finance Ministers are human and try to get
as much as they can, and perhaps some of
them try to get more than they ought to.
It seems to me that in this case, instead of
the basis of contribution being fifty-fifty as
in the past, a rapacious Finance Minister
could claim the whole of the expenses from
the County Councils.
That is not as it should be, and therefore
I thought it my duty to raise the question.
I would be one of the last people to interfere
with the privileges of the other House, but, as
I intimated to some of the officials concerned,
Malone Training School Bill- THE SENATE
Committee.
[Colonel Sharman Crawford]
if the Clause is not amended in such a way
as to make it acceptable to the County
Councils I shall move an Amendment to the
Clause. However, I hope the Leader of the
House will be able to give us such an assurance as will render such a step on my part
unnecessary.
Mr. LONG: I can corroborate the stateinent of the right hon. and gallant Gentleman that the relations of the local authorities with the Government on this question
of the contributions in respect of children
sent to Reformatory Schools have not been
happy in the past, and I think it is exceedingly desirable that the Government should
define in this Bill the liability of the local
authorities in this matter. It would
simplify matters in the future, and I
earnestly hope the Government will accede
to our request.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I desire to support
the Amendment proposed by Senator Leslie,
and I hope the Noble Leader of the House
will give us a guarantee that our request
will be granted, and that the respective contributions of the Government and the
County Councils will in the future be on a
fifty-fifty basis, and that the contributions of
the County Councils will not exceed fifty per
cent. We had a previous experience of this
kind when we took over the lunatics from
the different Workhouses. We spent
enormous sums to make accommodation for
them, and we were promised a Capitation
grant that came from one particular source
of revenue. That source of revenue dropped
and our Capitation grant ceased with it.
As this Bill now stands, unless we have
some guarantee, we are liable to the same
treatment. The treatment I have referred
to was experienced under the Imperial
Government. No person would doubt the
word of the present Minister of Finance or
the Minister of Home Affairs, who have
charge of this matter, but they may in time
be raised to a higher sphere, and we may
have someone else who may not be able to
finance the scheme. When the Malone Reformatory is taken over by the Government,
the first thing they will do will be to inerease the salaries of the staff and place
them on a pensionable basis, and the School
will be carried on in an extravagant manner
and the cost of administration will be more
than ever it was. Therefore, the county
councils must have some guarantee that
their contribution will not exceed fifty per
cent.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: With regard
to the Amendment proposed by Senator
Leslie, I have had an opportunity of communicating with my right hon. Friends, the
Minister of Home Affairs and the Minister
of Finance. The Minister of Home Affairs,
with the consent of the Minister of Finance,
has authorised me to give a specific undertaking to the House that the weekly contribution to be paid by the local authority in
respect of each Reformatory boy, for whom
the authority is liable, will not exceed half
the average weekly cost per head of Reformatory boys in the institution.
I am,
therefore, prepared to give the House an
unqualified undertaking to this effect, and I
trust the hon. Senator, in view of this very
definite pledge, will feel himself able to withdraw his Amendment.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Before
the hon. Member who moved the Amendment speaks again, I should like to ask one
question. So far as I can see the undertaking given by the noble Viscount is perfectly satisfactory, but I think the Bill, with
that alteration, still leaves it open to the
county councils to object to pay any expenses that they consider are not justified.
The county councils are not bound strictly
to pay fifty per cent. of the cost. If they
think the school is being managed extravagantly they will still have the right to object
to pay what they consider is unnecessary
expenditure.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I do not
think I could answer that question without
consulting the Minister concerned.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I think
that is so.
Perhaps you would give us a
further assurance on that point on the Third
Reading of the Bill.
From the
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
wording of the Bill it would appear that the
county councils have that right, but I will
mention the matter again on the Third
Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: I think it would be appropriate for me, in the first instance, to express
my thanks to the noble Viscount for so
kindly acting on behalf of the House as
intermediary in this matter, and I think we
may congratulate ourselves on the success
that has crowned his efforts. The undertaking the Leader of the House has given
really gives me something more than I
originally asked for, and I think it will be
the will of the Committee that the Amendment should be withdrawn.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Short title and construction)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.

Preamble and Title agreed to.

Housing (No. 2) Bill, 1926.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I do not think it is necessary for
me to make a lengthy speech in moving
that this Bill be read a Second time. Its
object is merely to extend for a further
year-that is, until March, 1928-the operation of the various Housing Acts which
have been passed by this Parliament.
Perhaps I may be allowed to recapitulate,
very briefly, the effect
of these Acts.
Under the various Acts the Government is
empowered to grant subsidies to private
builders or urban authorities in respect of
houses of a certain type built by them.
These houses must be of the kitchen or
small parlour type with a superficial area,
ranging, except in special conditions, from
600 to 900 square feet, and must be completed in accordance with Rules made by
the Ministry of Home Affairs.
The subsidy given for each house is £60,
but that figure may be increased to £80 if
the house is built in six months. In
addition to this subsidy the Acts also empower local authorities to subsidise the
builder of this type of house up to £40 per
house or, alternatively, to grant him free
of interest a loan not exceeding £100.
Furthermore, the local authorities may, if
they so desire, exempt the houses from the
payment of rates for not more than ten
years. Up to the present the number of
houses approved under these Acts and
built by private builders is 3,022, while
1,782 have been approved but not yet completed. The urban authorities have
already completed 1,046, and have received
further 914.
approval in respect of
During the past twelve months more than
2,000 of these houses have been built by
both private builders and urban authorities, while last month 150 were approved
for erection by private builders. While
these are very satisfactory figures, it is
clearly recognised that the housing problem is, as yet, by no meaus solved, and
hence the Government feel that they need
make no apology in asking for the renewal
of the Acts for another year. I beg to
move.
M
a
Mr. DORMAN: As I am acquainted with
the housing conditions in Belfast I consider
it my duty to support this Measure. I can
assure hon. Senators that there is a lamentable condition of affairs existing in Belfast
at the present time in regard to the housing
of the working classes. When examining
the recent Census returns I made the
surprising discovery that Belfast has deteriorated probably more than any other great
industrial centre in regard to housing.
because between the Census of 1911 and that
of 1926 the number of one-room dwellings in
the city had increased by six hundred per
cent. I know numerous cases where six,
seven, eight, nine, ten and twelve human
beings belonging to a family are residing in
one room, and I also know cases where such
working class families are paying as much
as 12s 6d a week rental for the occupancy of
a single apartment. I therefore feel it my
duty to offer my support to any Measure
calculated to stimulate the building of
houses for the working classes of Belfast and
Northern Ireland

whole House for to-morrow.

Third time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate have agreed
to the said Bill without any Amendment.
355 Collections at Public Meetings.
Collections at Public Meetings. 356
THE SENATE
Collections at Public Meetings.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Dorman:
To ask the Leader of the House whether it
is a fact that a difference exists between the
Orders issued in Northern Ireland in regard
to collections at public meetings, and those
in force in Great Britain; and whether it
is the intention of the Government to bring
in legislation or amend the Orders so that
the citizens of Northern Ireland may obtain
the same facilities of taking up collections
as are enjoyed by their fellow-citizens in
Great Britain.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to ask the question
which stands in my name on the Paper,
drawing attention to the disabilities to which
the citizens of Northern Ireland are subject
in regard to taking collections at public
meetings. I had taken a very considerable
interest in this particular question for some
time before the Imperial Parliament thought
it necessary to intervene to protect the
public from fraudulent attempts to obtain
collections for the ostensible purpose of assisting soldiers who had served in the war.
My reason for taking an interest in the
matter was that I had five sons engaged in
the war and I had myself been engaged in
ministering to the comfort of the sons of
other men in connection with the Young
Men's Christian Association.
The regulations that now exist were put
into force for the specific purpose of preventing this system of fraudulently and deceitfully obtaining sums of money from the
public, some of which was spent in the payment of high hotel expenses and very often
in the entertainment of those who made the
collection. This practice had existed to an
enormous extent in Great Britain, and it
ultimately extended to the United States of
America. I believe it was in consequence of
the action taken in America that the
Imperial authorities saw fit to intervene in
the first instance.
I handed to the noble Leader of the House
a paper containing facts regarding fraudulent collections made by some persons who
occupy a very eminent position in British
society, whose names I do not intend to
mention, but whose photographs appeared in
American newspapers and were not reproduced in any newspaper in Great Britain or
Ireland. The American authorities thought
it necessary to deport a number of those
individuals. Some of them admitted
having collected up to 20,000 and 30,000
dollars, and in one case where 20.000 dollars
had been collected in New York the particular lady could only account for 4,000
dollars as having been transferred to England.
Mr. SPEAKER: I must ask the hon.
Member to stick a little more closely to the
question. I cannot see that what happened
in America has anything to do with the
question or the Paper.
Mr. DORMAN: I defer. The Order that
is issued in Northern Ireland is entirely
different from the Orders which have been
issued in England. The Orders that have
been issued in England do not deprive any
body of citizens who have been accustomed
to holding open-air meetings from taking
up collections at those meetings without let
or hindrance. They are not asked to give
any return to the police or to the authorities as to the amounts of these collections,
except in instances where permission is
obtained for holding meetings in the public
parks, and then only the correct amount
of the collection has to be stated to the
park ranger in charge of that particular
park.
I have the privilege of being connected
with a body that is affiliated with Great
Britain-with Scotland-the Independent
Labour Party, a distinctive Socialistic
party. I have communicated with that
particular party, and I have ascertained
that their meetings are not interfered with
at all across the water. The secretary and
the responsible officials of that particular
organisation have replied to me
follows:
as
"In answer to your inquiry I have to
inform you that it has never been
necessary in Scotland to ask for a permit
to hold open-air meetings and take
collections at such meetings. In Glasgow
alone the Independent Labour Party
holds almost 800 open-air meetings every
year, while through Scotland the number
amounts to many thousands, and in all
cases these are held and collections taken
without asking permission from the
authorities."
That is signed by William Stewart, secretary of that party, James Carmichael,
Scottish organiser, and John M'Lure,
Glasgow, organiser of the Independent
Labour Party, the members of which have
been summoned and fined for doing in
Northern Ireland what our comrades
across the water are permitted to do without let or hindrance.
I also hold in my hand a communication
from the Organiser of the same party in
London and the Southern Counties, and
in reply to my inquiry he writes as
follows:
"I understand that you desire to know
the procedure with regard to collections
at open-air meetings in London and the
South of England.
357 Collections at Public Meetings. 9 NOVEMBER 1926
Collections at Public Meetings. 358
(1) Collections can be taken at any
street corner in London and the Southern
towns. The police regulations specify
that the collector must remain in the
position where the meeting is being held
and not badger people on the pathway
away from the meeting. This is the universal procedure and causes no obstruction or police interference."
Then he goes, on to state that no collections
are allowed in the Royal Parks, but where
the meetings are held in municipal parks
and permission obtained there is no interference whatever with the right of those
conducting those meetings to take
collections at such meetings.
up
Since I placed this Notice upon the Paper
my attention has been drawn to the proceedings in another place, and I find that
the Minister of Home Affairs has stated
that he is not acquainted with the Regulations made in Great Britain. I have here
the Regulation of the London Metropolitan
police authorities relating to the same procedure over there, and it is diametrically
opposed to the Order issued by the police
authorities in Northern Ireland. The
Order under which I myself received a
summons and was fined-a fine which some
kindly-disposed person paid without
authority, and which I would not have
paid, as I do not believe in passive
obedience, and as I believe that the people
of Northern Ireland are not accustomed to
believe in passive obedience. They are
rather inclined to believe more, not in
passive resistance, but in actual armed and
forcible resistance when their wishes are
not complied with.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Active resistance.
Mr. DORMAN: I thank the noble Viscount
for the correction. This Order in Northern
Ireland states that no collection of money,
other than a collection taken at an open-air
religious meeting, or sale of any article for
the benefit of charitable or other purposes,
and so forth, shall take place without the
permission of the police authorities.
Order in Great Britain is entirely different.
It is as follows:-
The
"(1) No collection of money (other than
a collection taken at an open-air meeting)

plainly and distinctly exempting from the
Order in Great Britain all these customary
open-air meetings, at which the people are
not defrauded, for they know for what purpose they are making their contributions.
I also wish to state that in my opinion
this is a grave constitutional question. It
is a question of my right as a British
citizen to do in Northern Ireland that which
as a British citizen I would have a perfect
right to do were I resident in Great Britain
instead of in Northern Ireland. A few years
ago we in Northern Ireland entered into a
historic Solemn League and Covenant, and
our presence here to-day is concrete proof
that the first part of that Solemn League
and Covenant has been entirely passed and
done away with. The only portion of
that Solemn League and Covenant
which
remains for us to impliment
to-day is that particular portion of
it which applies to the preservation of
our rights as British citizens. I claim that
the Government of Northern Ireland, if
they are going to move on parallel lines
with Great Britain, ought to give us the
opportunity, seeing that we are a body
affiliated with the Independent Labour
Party of Great Britain, ought to give us the
same facilities for financing our political
propaganda as the Imperial Parliament
gives to our ranks over there. There are a
large number of organisations. There is
the Independent Labour Party; there is the
Social Democratic Federation; there is the
Socialist Party of Great Britain; there is
an organisation of unemployed men, and
there is the much despised and much abused
Communist Party, who have been refused
affiliation for the last two years by overwhelming majorities in connection with the
Labour Party. All these organisations--
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member is again
going very far away from the point of his
question. I must ask him to observe my
ruling.
Mr. DORMAN: I was trying to show for
the information of the Government what
rights are extended to these various bodies
across the water, and I am only trying to
emphasise how important it is that we
should demand similar rights in Northern
Ireland. I have been prosecuted for taking
up a collection. I have receipts for all
moneys that were collected at that meeting.
From Sunday to Sunday the amount of
money was not counted by me, but by the
public, in the presence of the police, and the
amount of money collected was announced,
and the purposes for which it was allocated.
In order to emphasise the difference of it
I may quote from the Hansard of the British
Parliament. At an unemployed meeting,
held as usual where collections were taken
up, the chairman of the meeting happened
to announce in public that fifty per cent. of
the collection about to be taken would be
allocated for charitable purposes, that is,
for the support of the wives and children
of the locked-out miners, that twenty-five
per cent. of it would go to the payment
of the speakers, and that the other twentyfive per cent. would be allocated to the
359 Collections at Public Meetings.
Collections at Public Meetings. 360
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
support of the unemployed organisation.
Major Kindersley asked the Home Secretary
"Whether he was aware that at a meeting held under the auspices of the
National Unemployed Workers' Committee movement near the Green
Man, Leytonstone, on Sunday, the
4th instant, a collection was made on behalf of the miners' children fund, that
the speaker who made the collection
publicly admitted that only fifty per
cent. of the money collected went to the
said fund, and that twenty-five per cent.
went to pay the miners' speakers, and
twenty-five per cent. to the organisation
he represented; and whether in view of
the proportion of the sum collected which
was retained for organisation purposes,
any steps were proposed to be taken in
this matter."
The Home Secretary replied as follows:
"The reply to the first part of the
question is in the affirmative. With regard to the last part, the Regulations as
to street collections do not apply to
collections taken at open-air meetings,
and I have, therefore, no power to take
action."
as
My object, and my only object, is to demand for our working-class people
citizens of Great Britain-a citizenship
which we have the right to claim inasmuch
as we have our Parliamentary representatives in the Imperial Parliament-and
their organisations in Northern Ireland
the same rights, the same freedom, and the
same liberties as are accorded to our
fellows across the water. I have no
hesitation in expressing the belief that
when the matter is brought under the
attention of the Cabinet they will possibly
accede to the demand, and grant to us the
same freedom here as is given across the
water.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Before the
Leader of the House answers this question I
would like to ask if he is aware that prior to
the present Regulations being put into force
street collections were really a nuisance in
the City of Belfast. Pedestrians were
accosted for financial support for every conceivable purpose. Particularly the place to
which my hon. Friend (Mr. Dorman) refers,
the Custom House Steps, on Sunday was a
terrible place for collections. There
four sides to the Custom House. On one side
we had one party, who sometimes opened
their meeting with a portion of Scripture,
and then read lessons from some other
paper---the "Daily Herald" or something
like that and then took up a collection to
defray the expenses, the election expenses, of
some person who may have been standare
ing either for the Corporation or for Parliament. Of course, there was the one consolation that in all cases these candidates were
workingmen's friends, and then they asked
for more liberal subscriptions on that
account. Round on the other side of the
same place we had another party who were
holding forth and asking for subscriptions
for the unemployed. I think I am quite
correct in saying that the subscriptions never
travelled the length of the really genuinely
unemployed.
Mr. DORMAN: Question.
The col-
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
lectors themselves, I know, were unemployed,
in fact they had never been employed during
their whole lifetimes. I do not think they
were ever guilty of working. I do not think
those collections ever got any further than
those directly responsible for collecting the
money.
Mr. DORMAN: I do not like to interrupt
the hon. Gentleman, but I happen to have
been on the Committee of this organisation,
and I can give a deliberate contradiction to
that. The moneys were accounted for.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am quite
prepared to accept that statement from my
hon. Friend, but not to deny the fact that
some of the collectors were unemployed all
their lives. I am quite in a position to state
emphatically that what I have said is quite
correct in that respect. As to where the
money may have drifted, there may bave
been some who did benefit, and I hope there
were, but I do want to say that it was becoming a terrible nuisance and I am very
glad that the Government took steps to put
a stop to it. Before any change is made I do
hope the Government will seriously consider
the position. It is now considerably improved, because any person has the privilege
of listening to our right hon. Friend (Mr.
Dorman) at the meetings on Sundays without
the danger of some one coming along with a
hat, shaking coppers in his face, and expecting him to pay for the good information he
may get.
Mr. DORMAN:
Friend.
I am not a right hon.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I do hope
the Government will be careful not to make
any change that will be likely to revert to
the system under which we did live for some
time. I am sure that the good work being
carried on at the Steps will just continue as
satisfactorily without the collections, if not
better than before, because, if the object of
that meeting is to bring about good fellowship and good feeling between Roman
Catholic and Protestant, as has been stated,
then I am sure we all wish him well if he
would only continue in that direction, but
361 Collections at Public Meetings-

9 NOVEMBER 1926
Adjournment.
unfortunately, if he brings Roman Catholic
and Protestant together then they do make
sure to create a tremendous division between
worker and employer, and what good is
being done in one direction is being destroyed
in another. I hope the Government will
carefully consider the position with regard
to collections before any alteration is made.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I hope the
hon. Senator who asked this question will
have an opportunity at the place mentioned
in it of having a more unrestrained appeal
to eloquence than he has in this House,
where I cannot help thinking the question
would have gained a good deal in point if we
had not had to go to America with certain
persons and be deported from there back.
I would like to say in general answer that
I regret that I have not sufficient information in my possession to enable me to reply
to the first part of the hon. Senator's question as regards a difference between the
Regulations for street collections in Northern Ireland and those in force in Great
Britain generally.
In the London Metropolitan Police District
the Regulations are practically identical,
with the one exception, that in the London
Metropolitan District all open-air meetings
are exempt from the Regulations, whereas
in Northern Ireland only those open-air
meetings which are of a religious character
are so exempt.
The Regulations now in force in Northern
Ireland were made by the Inspector-General,
R.U.C., in December, 1923, in substitution of
Regulations previously made by the Inspector-General, Royal Irish Constabulary,
in June, 1918, from which they do not differ
in any material respect.
Owing to the disturbed condition of the
country generally the enforcement of the
Regulations made by the Inspector-General,
R.I.C., was in abeyance for a considerable
period. I think every hon. Senator knows
perfectly well the reason for which the
Regulations were designed, and I do not
think I need go into that. The present
Regulations do not impose any disability on
any particular section, and apply to all
equally. They do not prohibit collections
at open-air meetings, but merely require
that permission should be obtained before a
collection is taken. As regards the undoubted difference that is allowed to exist
between the Regulations in Northern Ireland
and those in Great Britain, I do not consider that there is any body of popular
opinion-any really responsible body-which
would desire to change it, and I do not
think, therefore, that any general exemption
in favour of open-air meetings is desirable.
move,

THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 10th November, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance.-
Ministry of Finance Minute regarding the
audit of certain Accounts by the Comptroller
and Auditor-General under Section 20 of the
Exchequer and Audit Act (Northern Ireland), 1921 (12 Geo. V., c. 2) as extended by
Section 7 of the Exchequer and Financial
Provisions Act (Northern Ireland), 1923
(13 Geo. V., c. 4).
Ministry of Education.-
Technical School Examinations and Examinations for Teachers in manual Training, Domestic Economy, and Drawing.
Presented (pursuant to Act) and ordered
to lie on the Table.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
The Commons have passed the Motor
Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Bill, the
Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill,
and the Local Government (War Service
Payments) Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House.
MOTOR VEHICLES (TRAFFIC AND
REGULATION) BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
EXCHEQUER AND FINANCIAL
PROVISIONS BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, motion agreed to.
LOCAL GOVERNMENT (WAR SERVICE
PAYMENTS) BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Marketing of Eggs Bill, 1926.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move the Second
Reading of this Bill, which may be taken as
a natural corollary to the Marketing of
Eggs Act passed by this House two years
ago. The primary aim of that Act was, as
họn. Members are aware, to initiate improvements in the testing, grading, and
packing of eggs, as a first stage in the
development of the poultry industry of
Northern Ireland, and it was clearly
realised at that time that success in the
administration of the Act would necessitate
an extension of its scope at a later date.
In the great market which lies at our
door competition is to-day keener than ever,
and the imports of eggs from foreign countries to Great Britain have risen from seven
million great hundreds in 1920 to twentytwo million great hundreds in 1925. All the
exporting countries, whether foreign or
colonial, are paying greater attention to the
quality of the eggs they are supplying as
well as to the methods of packing, and there
are signs that the agricultural community
in Great Britain itself is awakening to the
opportunity which the home market affords.
It is imperative that we in Northern Ireland
should not fall behind our competitors, and
that no effort should be relaxed towards
placing on the British market eggs equal,
or if possible superior, to any in the world.
At the outset we have many advantages. We
produce eggs of the finest quality, and we
are in close proximity to the great centres
of consumption. It has already been shown
that we can meet successfully the competition from overseas, but if we are to develop
our industry to the utmost we must increase
qur hold on the better class trade.
The Act of 1924 was the first step in this
direction, and the Bill now before the House
will, it is hoped, take us a further stage.
Its provisions fall naturally into two groups.
First, there are the new principles of empowering the Ministry to apply to retailers
rules similar to those prescribed for shippers, of placing on the producer a share in
the responsibility for the cleanliness and
freshness of the eggs, and of prescribing
purchase by weight. The second group of
provisions is concerned with improvements
in the existing administrative machinery.
With regard to retailers, it may be said
at once that it is not the intention of the
Government to bring the operations of
retail traders generally within the scope of
this legislation. They will be affected only
so far as the interests of the industry as a
whole demand. For instance, where retailers
go to the markets for supplies they should
365 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

10 NOVEMBER 1926
Second Reading.
not be allowed to purchase eggs which the
shipper is obliged to refuse, nor should they
be allowed to collect their eggs in the open
wholesale
in
while the
wet weather,
dealer, with whom they compete, is compelled to have covered accommodation.
Hon. Members know, I am sure, that one
of the present difficulties in the egg industry
is the prevailing tendency to hold over
supplies in anticipation of a rising market.
The new Bill proposes to discourage this
practice by making the sale of stale eggs
an offence. Unclean eggs are also prohibited,
and those Members of this House who have
practical experience of the egg industry
will agree with me that, by the exercise of
simple precautions, cleanliness can easily
be ensured.
Clause 7 enacts that the purchase of eggs
for resale shall be by weight. Purchase by
weight was always desirable. It is of particular importance to wholesale dealers who
are compelled, under the Rules at present
in force, to sell their eggs in grades, which
are guaranteed as being certain weights, and
who are at present buying by the dozen and
selling by weight. I may mention here that
there are areas in Northern Ireland where
purchase by weight has been in operation
for some time past, and which are noted
for the size of the eggs they produce.
and
I come now to those provisions in the
Bill which are designed to improve the
administrative machinery. Whereas under
the Act of 1924 a licence is issuable on the
applicant merely undertaking to comply
with the prescribed conditions, the present
Bill proposes, in Clause 1, that new applihave
cants should
their premises
a licence is
equipment inspected before
granted. The same Clause also gives power
to the Ministry to refuse a licence to a
wholesaler whose licence has been revoked.
As things stand at present such a wholesaler may immediately apply for a fresh
licence, and there is nothing to prevent the
Ministry being forced to grant it.
With regard to Clause 5, I may remind
hon. Members that the only remedy at present for breaches of the rules is the suspension or revocation of licences. This is
obviously too drastic a course to take for
isolated offences, and the Ministry is faced
with the problem of allowing such offences
to go unchecked or applying a penalty which
is far too severe. The Clause in question
provides the necessary elasticity by enacting
that these offences can be tried in the
ordinary courts of law.
With regard to inspection of cases in
transit, it is not proposed to interfere
unduly with consignments. Cases will
be opened for examination, but arrangements will be made for having them repacked efficiently and forwarded without
delay. I may say that every country in the
world which has assumed the responsibility
of regulating its export trade in eggs
enforces more stringent transit inspection
conditions than those which are proposed
here.
Before I close I should like with the permission of the House, to say a few words
with regard to the prices which are being
obtained at present in Great Britain for
Ulster eggs. It has been said, and quite
truly, that the price has fallen of late, and
it has been argued that the Marketing of
Eggs Act of 1924 is responsible for the decline. I should have thought that those
who made this statement would be fully
cognisant of all the facts and that they
would know that the price of eggs in Great
Britain, whether home-produced or imported, has fallen in recent years. A
significant fact, which is not so widely
known as it should be, is that eggs from
Northern Ireland now command higher
prices in Great Britain than do Danish eggs,
and this fact is, I think, a clear indication
that the Act of 1924 has justified its
existence. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: The only objection I myself
have to this Bill is the proposal to impose
a minimum fine. It is somewhat different
to that which occupied our attention in another Measure yesterday, and I will draw
attention to it again at a later stage. I
would like to ask the Noble Viscount who
moved the Second Reading if I correctly
understood him to say that the Act of 1924
provided no remedy other than a suspension
of licence. I have not Sub-section 3 of
Section 8 before me at the moment, but I
was under the impression that there were
provisions in that Sub-section for a finenot of course for a minimum fine or a fixed
fine but an ordinary discretionary fine to
which we have been accustomed for so very
many years. The matter can, I am sure,
easily settled, but if there is no other course
open than that of the suspension of the
licence I quite agree that this House and
Parliament should take some action. But
if it is a case of substituting a minimum
fine for a discretionary fine the matter
assumes a different aspect.

With regard to the imposition of the minimum fine I think that that is advisable so

may have a great deal of effect upon the

CLAUSE 1 (Restriction on exhibition and
training of performing animals) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Powers of courts to prohibit
or restrict exhibition and training of performing animals).
(2) A court of quarter sessions shall, on
an appeal under this Act, have power to
make any order which a court of summary
jurisdiction could have made under this
section and to confirm, quash or vary an
order which has been appealed against.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 2,
page 3, lines 1 to 5, to leave out Sub-Section
(2). This Amendment is not as formidable
as it looks. It is not really a question of
excision but of transposition. It is desirable
that the Sub-section should be made of
general application, and for that purpose it
ought to appear in Part IV of the Bill as an
immediate adjunct of the provisions relating
to appeal with which it is connected. All
that is necessary is to leave it out here and
to reproduce it further on with the alteration
of the word "section" to "Act."
I am pre-
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
pared to accept the Amendment of the hon.
Gentleman.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Power to enter premises).
(1) Any police officer or constable or
authorised person may-
(a) enter at all reasonable times and
inspect any premises in which any performing animals are being trained or exhibited, or kept for training or exhibition,
and inspect any such animals found
therein;
(b) require any person whom he has
reason to believe to be a trainer or exhibitor of performing animals to produce
his certificate of registration.
(2) No police officer or constable or
authorised person shall be entitled under
this section to go on or behind the stage
during a public performance of performing
animals.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, page 3, line
24, after "which" ("in which any performing") to insert "he has cause to believe that."
It is usual when an inspector of any kind,
whether police officer or authorised person,
has power to enter premises for the purpose
of carrying out any Act to give him protection allowing him to enter if he has reason to
suppose that what he expects to find is there.
Without that protection if a man went into
one of these places to look for a performing
lion and the performing lion was not there
he would be a trespasser. Examples of this
protection are found in such widely varying
Measures as the Trades Wages Regulations,
the Livestock Breeding and all the Poultry
Acts. The reason why the words I suggest
were not in the Bill before us at the moment
is, I think, that they do not appear in the
English Measure from which this part of the
Act was taken, and the draftsman would
naturally not insert them without direct instructions. The reason why they do not
appear in the English Measure may be that
it was a private Bill, or it may be because they
use the services of an officer of the local
authority instead of an authorsied person. I
think it would be in accordance with the
general practice to insert these words. I
beg to move accordingly.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, page 5,
line 1, to leave out "(2)." This is a trifling
Amendment consequential on the first one

effect would be of course that the expression

rules and
(1) For the purposes of this Part of this

The expression "animal" does not include invertebrates:
The expression "exhibit" means exhibit
at any entertainment to which the public
are admitted, whether on payment of
money or otherwise, and the expression
"train" means train for the purpose of any
such exhibition, and the expression
"exhibitor" and "trainer" have respectively the corresponding meanings:
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I beg to
move, page 5, line 7, after "animal" ("expression 'animal' does") to insert "includes
birds but." I move this Amendment from
the feeling I had that birds were not included in it. I think the general opinion
is that animals do not include birds, but if
I am wrong-and I did not look up any
authorities, and I do not know the legal
definition of this word-and it does include
birds the Amendment will be unnecessary.
If it does not include birds I think it would
be an advantage to include them. The only
animals I know that fly are men, and
whether they are intended to be included in
this I do not know. I beg to move that
this Amendment be made, unless it is
definitely stated that the definition of the
word is sufficient.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am afraid
I must oppose that Amendment for the fact
is that the expression "animal" does cover
birds. Hon. Senators will see that the
first line of this Clause says the expression
"animal" does not include invertebrates, so
it would follow naturally that it does include all vertebrates. Birds are vertebrates
and therefore are included, and I think man
as well, but I am not competent to judge.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
leave to withdraw the Amendment.
statement is quite satisfactory.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
I beg

Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 6 (Exceptions from application
of Part I of Act), 7 (Illegal method of
taking birds), 8 (Illegal confinement of
birds), 9 (Prohibition of shooting of captive

CLAUSE 12 (Provisions as to prosecution
fines and appeals).
All offences against this Act shall be
prosecuted, and all fines thereunder shall
be recovered, in manner provided by the
Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts; and
a person aggrieved by a conviction or order
made by a court of summary jurisdiction
on determining any information or complaint under this Act may appeal in
manner provided by the said Summary
Jurisdiction Acts.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, page 7, line
30, at end to add the following new Subsection:
"(2) A court of quarter sessions shall, on
an appeal under this Act, have power to
make any Order which a court of summary
jurisdiction could have made under this Act,
and to confirm, quash or vary an Order which
has been appealed against:
Provided that no appeal shall lie against
an Order made under Part II of this Act for
the disposal by destruction of a live decoy
bird."
This is the Sub-section which we took out
a few minutes ago from an early part of the
Bill with the change I suggested to be made
to insert the word "Act" instead of the word
"section." But the opportunity has been
taken to add a few words, providing that no
appeal shall lie against an order for the disposal by destruction of a live decoy bird.
If hon. Senators will look at Clause 7 they
will see that the court has power to order
the destruction of any live bird, and if, as
might easily happen, the bird in question is
blind, maimed, or injured, it would be possible for the owner to defeat the ends of
the court by entering an appeal. A somewhat
similar difficulty occurred in the main Act,
the 1911 Act, for the Prevention of Cruelty
to Animals, where it is expressly provided
that no appeal shall lie against an order for
the destruction of an animal. Speaking for
myself I am not disposed to trust the humanity of the man who uses a maimed or injured
bird, and, therefore, I ask the Committee to
allow the words to be added to the Bill.

Malone Training School Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: In moving
the Third Reading of this Bill I should like
to answer a question put yesterday by the
right hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel
Sharman Crawford), who is not here to-day,
as to whether County Councils could make
an appeal against any future extravagance
in connection with the administration of
the Malone Reformatory School. The answer
is that they can do so, as the matter can
be raised in Parliament on the discussion
of the annual Vote. The Malone Training
School will appear as an item in the Vote,
and any question of extravagance can be
debated when that Vote is presented to the
House. I beg to move that the Bill be now
read the Third time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-
(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate have
agreed to the said Bill without any Amendment.
ADJOURNMENT.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, that this House do now adjourn. I
should like to add that the business for
to-morrow will include the Second Reading
of the Motor Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Bill, and the Second Reading of the
Local Government (War Service Payments)
Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
minutes before Four o'clock.

Horse Breeding Bill.
Committee.
11 NOVEMBER 1926
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 11th November, 1926.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
The Commons have passed the Finance
(Stamp Duty) Bill and the Appropriation
(No. 2) Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
FINANCE (STAMP DUTY) BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time.--(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
APPROPRIATION (No. 2) BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time.--(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Horse Breeding Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Licences and permits for
ownership or keeping of stallions).
Any person who, after the appointed day,
owns, keeps or has control of a stallion of
three years' old and upwards calculated
from first day of January, in the year of
its birth, shall be liable on conviction to
a fine of two pounds, unless the stallion is
licensed, or a permit has been granted in
respect thereof, under the provisions of
this Act; and any person who has been
convicted of owning, keeping or having
control of a stallion contrary to the provisions of this Act, shall, if after the expiration of seven days following such
conviction he owns, keeps or has control of
such stallion contrary to the provisions of
this Act, be liable on conviction to a fine
of five pounds; and, if after such second
conviction, he continues to own, keep or
have control of such stallion contrary to
the provisions of this Act, he shall be
guilty of a continuing offence and be liable
on conviction to a further penalty of forty
shillings for each week or part of a week
during which he is guilty of the continuing
offence.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 1,
page 3, line 8, to leave out the words "of
two" (a fine of two pounds), and insert instead thereof the words "not exceeding
twenty." In speaking on the Second Reading of this Bill I referred to the proposed
penalties to be imposed for a breach of the
conditions of Clause 1, and I am afraid that
perhaps I laid rather too much stress on
the fact that the proposal of a fine of £2
was a minimum fine. Undoubtedly the proposal is to impose a minimum fine, but it is
also a proposal to impose a maximum fine,
and the question at issue in this case,
therefore, is rather whether it is desirable to introduce a fixed fine or whether it is
desirable to introduce merely a maximum fine.
Whether the effect of the form of words
used is sufficient to ensure that a fine of £2
shall be inflicted
not is a question
which, if the magistrates concerned should
ever choose to raise it, would have to be
settled by the superior courts.
or
It has not been the practice in the past
in those very rare cases where a fixed or
minimum fine has been imposed to use the
form of words before us to-day. I do not,
however, want to dwell on that very much,
because the fine suggested here is such a
very small matter. It is merely £2, and the
circumstances are not such as to render it
reasonably probable that that fine can be an
unreasonably severe fine. After all, the
man who keeps a stallion can probably
afford to pay a fine of £2 if he does not conform to the laws governing the subject. I
am rather inclined to think that the mistake the Bill makes is in departing from
the principle of the original English Act of
1918, which imposes a penalty not exceeding £20, and the object of this Amendment
is to reintroduce that.
I can myself very readily see a case
where a fine of £2 would not be sufficient. I
think it is far more easy to imagine a case
where £2 is sufficient, but that is not a matter with which I will deal. Again, looking
at the matter as a fixed fine, we cannot
forget, or we ought not to forget, that the
fines to be imposed are for, amongst other
things, failure to take out a licence. The
moment you bring in the question of a
licence and the fee attached to it you raise
at once the question as to whether the
Measure is not a financial Measure, to some
extent a Revenue Measure, and, therefore,
Committee.

Horse Breeding Bill-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
ought it to be considered with regard to the
conditions usually accepted in regard to
such a Measure? It has always been the
practice, so far as one can trace, or at least
it has been the practice for many years,
when a distinctively Revenue Measure imposing a licence duty is passed to put in as the
penalty either three times the duty or such
and such a fixed sum. That is so in motorcar cases and in a good many others.
We have then to consider how far the proposal before us to-day comes within the
category of a Revenue Measure. I have been
referred, shortly before the Committee met
this afternoon, to the case of the Scutch Miils
Act passed recently by this Parliament in
which the same form of words is used as
that in the Bill before us and in which a
penalty of, I think, £10 is inserted. I have
been informed that cases have been heard
under that Act and that a fine of £10 has
been imposed, but that it has been the
practice of the Ministry to reduce the fine.
I do not think that is a satisfactory method
of dealing with such cases. It seems to me
that if a fine is imposed which presses
severely, the Court that imposed that fine
ought to have full discretion in the matter
and that if the offender who has been fined
is not satisfied his proper course is to appeal
to His Grace the Governor.
It is not right that a Department should
undertake a reduction of a fine imposed by
a Court of competent jurisdiction. To allow
that is to allow that Department to usurp
the functions of the Courts, and that is a
practice which is not encouraged by our
Constitution and of which I myself cannot
refrain from expressing my disapproval.
The point we have to consider to-day is
to ask the Government definitely whether
they prefer this minimum and fixed fine to
the older fine of £20 which was inserted in
the original English Act of 1918. The expression in that Act is "not exceeding
twenty pounds," and apparently it has
been completely efficacious in the past.
In eight years there have been only two
cases of offences which could be brought
before the Courts. There may or may not
have been possible-not to secure a
against this Act, but it does not seem to
have been possible not only to secure a
conviction but to obtain evidence sufficient
to bring the alleged offender into Court.
That being so we can, I think, admit there
have been no other cases than the two which
are cited by the Department.
If the Department thinks it cannot ensure the observance of the new provisions
except by imposing a fixed penalty of £2
it may be that the Committee will concur.
I cannot see that any case whatever has
been made out for departing from the
original provisions in the English Act of
1918, which made the penalty "not exceeding twenty pounds." That is, it left the
assessment of the penalty entirely to the
Court that heard the case. That being so,
I have put this Amendment on the Paper,
and I beg to move.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I cannot agree
with the Amendment proposed by the hon.
Senator. The purport of that Amendment
is to do away with the minimum fine of
£2. The hon. Senator suggests the alternative of a maximum fine of £20. That
would not prevent a Court of Petty
Sessions from fining a man half-a-crown.
We have had experience of Petty Session
Courts in dealing with their neighbours.
Mr. SPEAKER: Will the hon. Member
please speak up.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: The Licensing Bill
had to be taken out of the hands of the
local magistrates in the Petty Session
Courts and placed in the hands of the
resident magistrates, owing to the practice
of packing the benches. In all cases connected with the Department of Agriculture,
sheep dipping, the prevention of the spread
of scab, and that sort of thing, if a local
farmer is brought before his brother
farmers at a Court of Petty Session he is,
with very few exceptions, fined 2s 6d, and
goes home and repeats the offence the next
week. The fine originally in the Bill was
£10. It was reduced at the request of the
representatives of agriculture in another
place to £2. The Minister, in the first
instance, reduced it to £5, and afterwards,
representations from the agricultural
interests, to £2. I personally, as an agriculturist, think he was doing no service to
agricultural interests in reducing that fine
beyond the original intention of the Government. However, to leave it open to a petty
sessions court to fine a man half-a-crown
for committing an offence under this Bill
is what I could not support. I agree with
the Bill as it stands.
on
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: It is quite obvious from the speech
of my hon. Friend opposite (Mr. Leslie) that
he has a rooted objection to a fixed fine or a
minimum fine. To my mind, therefore, nothing I can say will alter that objection. The
hon, Senator spoke on the first Amendment.
The other two are consequential, so I may
take the three together. I am afraid I must
inform the hon. Senator that the Government cannot accept his Amendment. They
consider it would be a great mistake to delete
this fixed fine. I would like to point out
that since the Ministry functioned, in view
of the defects of the 1918 Horse Breeding Act,
it was found possible to institute proceedings
in only the two cases which the hon. Senator
mentioned. In each of these cases fines of
Horse Breeding Bill-
Committee.
11 NOVEMBER 1926
5s were imposed. The proposal in this Clause
is designed expressly to preclude in future
fines of a derisory nature.
I should like to point out also that the
principle of fixed penalties has been agreed
to in the Scutch Mills Act (Northern Ireland),
1923, which was, of course, passed by this
Parliament. Fixed penalties are also imposed in various orders under the destructive
The hon.
insects and pests legislation.
Senator went on to refer to the fact I do
not think I am misquoting him-that it
should not be left in the power of the
Ministry to reduce the fine. I think the hon.
Senator forgets that when a fixed penalty is
imposed by a court it is subject to be
modified on a memorial to the Governor
of Northern Ireland and naturally the
Governor gets the advice of his Ministers
and acts according as His Grace may think
fit. Perhaps I might also mention that in
the case of breaches of the provisions of
Black Scab Act there were 59 cases where the
statutory fine of £10 was imposed and the
fines were reduced to amounts varying from
£1 to £5. Similarly under the Scutch Mills
Act there were four cases of memorials presented against the fines that were imposed,
and the fines of £20 were reduced to £2 and
the £40 penalties were reduced to £4. The
Ministry, therefore, in asking for powers
under this Act similar to those given in the
legislation I have referred to, feel that the
exercise of such powers in the past should
inspire confidence in the Ministry for the
future. For these reasons I cannot accept
the hon. Senator's Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I gather from what has just
fallen from the noble Viscount that the
Government prefers a fixed penalty of £2 to
the possibility of a penalty of £20. I observe,
too, that the Government are supported in
their attitude by the hon. Senator from
County Down (Mr. Dickson), for whose
opinion on all matters relating to livestock
breeding I have the greatest respect. I can
only say that I regret to hear his attack
on the body of magistrates of Northern
Ireland. I think that I am correct in saying
that my hon. Friend is a member of that
body, and I must say that my own experience
during the past twenty years has not been
that of my hon. Friend. I do not pretend that
I invariably agree with my colleagues on the
magisterial bench, but I cannot say that
when I happen to differ it necessarily follows
that I am right and the others wrong. However, I think that the general opinion would
be that as the Ministry distinctly prefers the
course in the Bill there is no serious principle at issue, and no great harm will
be done. Nobody suggests that there would
be any savage persecution by the Ministry.
I am quite in agreement with the special
method for obtaining reductions in necessary
I do not think I am giving away any
cases.
principle, and I beg leave to withdraw the
Amendment. Of course, as the other two
Amendments are consequential they will not
be moved.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendments stood on the

Page 3, line 16, leave out "of five" and

Question proposed: That Clause 1 stand

an anæs-
Mr. LESLIE: The question has been
raised as to what is to happen to a
stallion which is condemned. There are
three courses open-destruction, export,
and castration. It is with the last I desire
to deal. The point is if there is an animal
that is over three years' old, castration
ought not to be effected except under an
anæsthetic. There is power as the Committee will remember under the Act of
1919 to require that animals subject to
certain operations shall not be subjected to
such operations except under
thetic. The operations are set out in
various schedules. The first of the
schedules relates to horses and castration
is not one of the operations. On the
other hand by Section 3 of the same Act
there is power given to the Ministry of
Agriculture to make orders for operations,
and it would therefore be possible for castration to be ordered if not already done.
I would like before we pass from this
Clause to ask the noble Viscount whether
he can give us an assurance that no horses
over three years of age will be castrated
except under an anesthetic.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I regret I am not at the present
moment in a position to give the hon.
Senator a definite reply. I am informed
that the veterinary officers consider
that for an animal over three years an
anæsthetic is unsafe. For a stallion it
would be unsafe to use an anæsthetic. I
will go into the matter more fully and give
the hon. Member full answer on Report.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill,
CLAUSE 2 (Power of Ministry of Agriculture to grant, revoke and suspend
licences).
(1) The Ministry of Agriculture shall
have power to grant, revoke and suspend
licences for the purposes of this Act.
(2) The Ministry of Agriculture shall, on
application being made in the prescribed

and also, if so requested, a certified copy
of the licence:

and may revoke or suspend a licence, in

It
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, page 4, line
1, after "(1)" to insert "subject to the provisions of this Act." This small Amendment
is not really a drafting Amendment,
though it is of a drafting nature.
is in reality consequential on an alteration made fn another place. I think
that the words I suggest are required
to avoid apparent contradiction in the Bill.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am obliged to the hon. Member.
I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, page 4, line
23, to leave out from "physique" ("physique;
or begets unsuitable stock") to the end
of line 24. This Amendment is one which
I am sure the Committee will recognise is
intended to afford an opportunity for
further elucidation of the Measure before
us. The Amendment as it stands is to take
away the power of the Ministry to revoke
or suspend a licence in respect of a stallion
which begets unsuitable stock. Now the
prevention of the use of a stallion which
begets unsuitable stock is, I imagine, one
of the principal objects of the Bill before
us. The words to which I draw attention
do not occur in the Horse Breeding Act of
1919. Therefore, it has occurred to some
of us that we would like a little more explanation from the Government as to how
it is intended to make the paragraph work.
It is obvious if you are to decide the
question, especially if there is opposition on
the part of the owner of the stallion, it will
be necessary to inspect not only the stallion
but also the mare which has produced the
progeny. Sometimes it may be possible to
do this by agreement. In other cases it
may not be possible. In any case the owner
of the stallion might like to claim some
right of appeal which apparently he would
not get under the general appeal provisions.
On the whole it would
that
little further explanation ought to be
given. In asking that I do not want the
Committee to think I have forgotten that
the whole Clause is governed by the fact
that the powers given by the various
Clauses are only to be exercised if it occurs
to the Ministry to do so and so. For the
moment I beg to move the Amendment.
seem
some
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Amendment is to leave out "or
begets unsuitable stock." Of course the
hon. Senator knows as well as I do that
the effect of this Amendment if passed
would be to prevent the Ministry having
power to revoke the licence of a stallion
that begets unsuitable stock I think the
way the paragraph would work would be
as follows: If, we will say, the owner of a
mare was dissatisfied with the foal produced he would probably raise a complaint
and the Ministry would send someone down
to inspect the foal. I would like to point
out to the hon. Member that the progeny
of stallions will be inspected periodically
by the Ministry. The inspector going
round-and not only the inspector but the
owners of mares-will very soon come to
notice that the stallion is producing unsuitable stock. That will very soon be
found out, and I do not think there will
be any real difficulty in the working of
this part of the Act. The very fact that
the progeny are inspected will make it
appear whether the stallion is begetting unsuitable stock.
soon
Mr. LONG: As a
matter of fact the
practice in the past was for the Department
to inspect the stock of a stallion which was
rejected for defective conformation, and the
procedure in that case was just as the noble
Viscount has indicated to the Committee. I
can see no difficulty whatever. However, I
am very glad to have got the information
which hon. Senator Leslie has elicited.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we are obliged
to the noble Viscount, and it only remains

CLAUSE 3 (Duration, Transfer, and Production of Licences).
re-
(1) A licence, unless suspended or
voked by the Ministry of Agriculture, shall
remain in force until the thirty-first day of
December of the year in respect of which
the licence is granted, but shall be renewable annually, and the same provisions shall
apply to the renewal of a licence as apply
to the grant of a licence:
Provided that in the case of a stallion
which has attained such age as may be
prescribed, and in respect of which a
licence has been in force for such number
of years as may be prescribed, the renewal
of the licence shall not be refused on the
ground only of the stallion being affected
in its wind.
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 3,

that in the case of a stallion which has
attained") to insert "that any licence granted
or renewed under the Horse Breeding Act,
1918, at any time between the 31st day of
