CLAUSE 6 (Duties of owners of stallions).
The owner of a stallion in respect of
which a licence or a permit is in force
under this Act shall
(a) give notice forthwith to the Ministry of Agriculture in the prescribed
manner and at the prescribed time of
any sale or letting or other change in
the ownership of the stallion or of its
castration or death; and
(b) submit the stallion to inspection
and examination by any officer of the
Ministry of Agriculture or authorised
person, if and when required by the
said Ministry; and
(c) return the licence and the certified copy (if any) or the permit to the
said Ministry forthwith on the expiration, revocation or suspension of the
licence or permit;
and if he fails to comply with any of the
requirements of this section he shall be
liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding five pounds.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD I beg to move, page 7, lines 19 and
20, to leave out "in respect of which a
licence or a permit is in force under" and
insert "of the age specified in Section one
of." The object of this Amendment is to
widen this Clause. As the Clause now stands
only the owner of the stallion in respect
of which a licence or a permit is in force
would have to comply with these conditions,
consequently it is necessary to widen the
Clause and to insert the words "of the age
specified in Section one of." I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, page 7, line 30, at the
beginning ("(c) return the") to insert "where
a licence or a permit has been in force."
This really is in a sense consequential on
the former Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

CLAUSE 7 (Inspection of Stallions).
or
Any officer of the Ministry of Agriculture, police officer or constable,
authorised person, shall have power to
inspect and examine any stallion, and
shall for the purposes of this Act have
power to enter at all reasonable times any

and examine any stallion or to enter any

under this section, or obstructs or impedes

pounds.
a
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move page 7, line 39, after
stallion" ("examine any stallion and ")
to insert "or other animal." As the Clause
now stands the police officer, constable, or
other authorised person has only power to
inspect and examine a stallion. If he went
to examine a stallion and found it was another animal he would be breaking the law.
I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: This Amendment which has
just been moved by the noble Viscount is
really the first of three Amendments on the
Paper, all of which are related to one another. They are being brought forward, I
think, to meet the difficulty which was put
up in this House by myself and other
Members when the Bill was in Committee,
and I desire to take the opportunity of saying
that the Amendments taken as a whole, as
they now stand, completely meet the views
which I then endeavoured to express. I should
like, if I may go further, to say that I consider
that I owe gratitude to the members of the
Department with whom I had an opportunity
of discussing the proposed alterations for
the time and attention which they devoted
to the question. I spent some time in investigating with them what really was required to meet the views of the Minister, on
the one hand, who wanted all necessary
powers and of this House on the other
who wanted to see that no unnecessary
powers were granted. I must say I was
greatly impressed with the wide views taken
by all concerned. I am sure that the result
of the efforts bestowed on it will materially
enhance the merits of the Measure.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, page 7, line 39, after
"stallion" to insert "but in the case of a
police officer or constable so far only as is
necessary for the purpose of requiring, reporting and giving evidence as to contraventions of this Act" This is really to meet
the wishes of the hon. Senator who has just
spoken. It clearly says that the police
officer who comes to any premises to inspect
will only do so for the purpose of requiring,
reporting, and giving evidence of contraventions of this Act.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move in page 8, lines 3 and
4, to leave out "to inspect and examine any
stallion or." This really has to do with the
same Amendment. It is consequential.
beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
I
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Senator Leslie raised a
question about the castration of stallions,
and I promised I would give a full answer on
report. The facts are these. The tendency in
recent years among experienced veterinarians
is to perform this operation while the animal
is in a standing position. If the administration of a complete anaesthetic were made
compulsory this very useful method would,
to a great extent, have to be discarded.
An animal which has been castrated without an anesthetic will, on being liberated,
commence to feed immediately, and given
asceptic conditions the wound will heal without untoward results.
On the other hand, an animal which has
been put under a complete anaesthetic will,
on recovering consciousness remain dull and
nauseated for a fairly considerable period.
Further, the use of an anesthetic in this
connection would mean added cost to owners
and, in most cases, it would mean the casting and securing of the animal before the
administration of the anaesthetic. The result would be possible injury to limb and
spine, which possibility is obviated by the
standing method.
It is understood that the placing of this
operation in the first schedule to the Act of
1919 was considered when that Measure was
being passed in the British Parliament but,
after full consideration, the consent of that
body was withheld. The Government of
Northern Ireland do not consider it necessary
that it should be included in the Act of 1919.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time to-morrow.

The
Sir WILLIAM COATES: May I just for
one moment say I am very glad this Bill
has reached this stage in its passage through
the two Houses. The particular reason why
I am glad is because it will enable certain
local authorities in Northern Ireland to
carry out the promise which they made to
their employees in connection with the payment to ex-Servicemen of war bonus.
promise has been made to these men that
the bonus will be paid, and the Bill will
enable it to be done. I can only hope that
the Royal Assent to the Bill will be granted,
if it passes its final stages now, at a very
early date, so as to make it possible for the
local authorities who have arranged to pay
actually to pay the money to the men before
Christmas.
Mr. DORMAN: The hon. Member has
spoken on behalf of the local authorities
who are the employers of these men, and I
would like on behalf of many of these men
to express my gratitude to the Government
for this Measure, and I hope it will become
law before Christmas.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

CLAUSES 1 (Power of Ministry of Agriculture to refuse to grant licences to wholesale dealers in eggs in certain cases), 2 (Evidence of authority to purchase on behalf of
licensed wholesale dealers), and 3 (Returns
with respect to dealings in preserved eggs)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Extension of power to make
rules.)
Question proposed: That Clause 4 stand
part of the Bill.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I did not rise to
propose an Amendment to this Clause, but
just to elicit from the Ministry whether
it will be necessary for a grocer or baker
who goes round the country collecting eggs,
as is generally done in Northern Ireland, to
have a licence for doing so. I can quite
understand that if he takes a number of
eggs from a farmer's house and then calls
in to certain wholesale merchants he should
have a permit. This man may take the
would like to
eggs to several grocers, and
know if it is necessary that he should have
a licence to do so.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Under the
Marketing of Eggs Act, 1924, every wholesale
dealer in eggs must be licensed by the
Ministry of Agriculture. The term "wholesale dealer" is defined in the Act as including
any person who carries on the business of
purchasing eggs for resale otherwise than
by retail. Thus a trader who purchases in
markets or by itinerant collection throughout the country need not be licensed so long
as all the eggs are sold retail, but if the eggs,
or any of them, are sold to another trader,
then the first trader must be licensed. The
determining factor is the manner in which
the merchant disposes of the eggs. In the
Bill at present before the Senate, Clause (2)
makes provision for exempting from these
provisions persons who act as purchasing
agents of wholesale dealers. Such accredited
agents will, in future, be required to hold a
copy
of their employer's licence, duly
419 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Viscount Charlemont]
counter-signed, as a mark of their authority
to act on his behalf, and need not be themselves licensed.
Mr. DORMAN: There is a matter to which
I wish to draw the attention of the Ministry, and that is the custom which prevails
in the adjoining counties of bartering for
eggs. I am informed that a fairly large
number of bread-van drivers take part payment for the bills due them for bread in the
form of eggs from their customers throughout the country. It is quite possible that
the eggs are placed in a vehicle containing
warm bread, and the radiation of the
heat may injuriously affect the keeping
qualities of the eggs. There may not be
possible detection of that on this side before
they reach the other side. I would like to
know if the Minister's attention has been
drawn to this. According to the statement
of the right hon. Gentleman, the Leader of
the House, apparently such a man would be
compelled, under this Bill, to obtain a
licence for buying eggs with the intention of
reselling them for distribution.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Selling eggs
retail does not require a licence. The
question of the incubator is a rather difficult one.
Mr. DORMAN: What I meant was, that
leaving eggs in the carts for a few hours is
equivalent to placing them in an incubator
before they go
to market. That deteriorates the keeping quality of the eggs.
I can quite see that a heated bread-van
would be equal to a heated incubator.
On question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE 5 (Provisions as to penalties and
offences).
(1) Section eight of the principal Act
shall have effect as if the following subsection were substituted for sub-section (3)
thereof:-
(3) Any person who is found guilty of
an offence against this Act shall be
liable, on conviction, for the first offence
to a fine of one pound or any greater
sum not exceeding twenty pounds, and
for the second offence to a fine of three
pounds or any greater sum not exceeding
fifty pounds, and for the third or any
subsequent offence to a fine of five
pounds or any greater sum not exceeding
one hundred pounds.
(2) Any person who fails to comply with
the provisions of any rules made by the
Ministry under the principal Act, or under
that Act as amended by this Act, shall be
guilty of an offence against the principal
Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 3,
lines 6 to 17, leave out sub-Section (1). I
have first of all to thank the noble Viscount,
the Parliamentary Secretary (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard), for consenting to postpone the Committee stage of this Bill
from last Thursday until to-day.
I had
rather hoped that in the interval.I might
have been able to bring myself to see that
the Amendment which stands on the Paper
in my name was an Amendment which,
perhaps, it was unnecessary to move. I
have done what I could in consultation and
otherwise, to form an opinion as to whether
it was necessary to trouble the Committee
again on a matter which raised the question
of the minimum fine. I would rather, as I
have said, have found it unnecessary, but I
regret to say that further investigation has
only strengthened my original opinion that
the minimum fine should not be introduced
on this occasion.
The objections to a minimum fine briefly
are, as I am sure every Member of the Committee is aware, that deliberately to put a
minimum fine in an Act of Parliament means
a lack of confidence in the magistrates, a
lack of confidence which was, if I may say
so, strongly deprecated by representatives
of the Government in another place two or
three years ago. Proposals were made, as
will be remembered, for inserting a minimum fine in the Education Act and also in
the Criminal Law Amendment Act. Both
those proposals were turned down at the instance in one case of a Cabinet Minister, and
in the other of a Parliamentary Secretary.
Another difficulty about the minimum fine
is that it is an attempt by Parliament to do
the work of courts of magistrates. That is
an interference with the judiciary, because
it is the judiciary whether it be the High
Court or whether it be the magistrates.
Again, from the practical point of view, it
is commonly said that if you insert a minipenalty that minimum tends to
become the maximum. That point of view
has always been accepted.
mum
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Why?
Mr. LESLIE: It was accepted, for instance,
last week in the case of the Horse Breeding
Bill. The arrangement there is to have a
fixed fine of £2 in place of the original fine
not exceeding £20. It is generally found
that if a fixed fine is to be imposed, as in
fishery cases, the magistrates will not go
beyond it. There are cases under most Acts
of Parliament-and it may well be that the
Marketing of Eggs Bill would not be an
exception-where the fine ought to be much
higher than £1, as suggested here for the
first offence. Then we come to the difficulty
which is concerned with the futility of trying
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committee.
16 NOVEMBER 1926
to put in a provision of this sort. After all,
the first duty of a magistrate is to decide
whether or not he has to convict in any
offence. Having done that his next duty is
to impose what he, in consultation with his
colleagues of course, considers a proper
penalty. If he will not do his duty in
one case he will not do it in the other.
If he is required to impose a penalty he
thinks too severe he will look round and
Can I not let the man off altogether?
say,
Am I perfectly sure about this? Is it not a
case where he must have the benefit of the
doubt?" If you try to press the magistrate
into doing that which is not what the
magistrate thinks right, he will probably
take such courses as I have indicated.

Again, to introduce a minimum penalty
for this or that offence has the effect of
upsetting the whole balance of your penal
code. You commit yourselves, for instance,
to say that an offence under the new Act
is so much worse than other offences that
you must have a minimum penalty. In this
particular case you are bound to say that
the offence, whatever it is, and under whatever Section it is, is a more serious offence
than the minor assault cases which are
frequently and usually punished with less
than a penalty of £1. You would be inserting in this Bill as a minimum a penalty
which is the maximum for failure to comply
with the Notification of Births Act, which
is an Act of some importance. It is not possible to standardise crimes and offences, but
you can standardise the penalties for those
offences. You can standardise the actual
penalty, but you cannot standardise the
effect of the penalty any more than you
can standardise the incidents of a particular
offence. If you put in a minimum fine as a
general obligation you are, therefore, in fact
prejudging a case which has not been heard.
It is setting up a theory against the practical
experience of whatever court may be trying
the case.
Almost every case that comes up for the
proposed insertion of a minimum fine has
to be examined from one or other of the
standpoints I have just mentioned. Sometimes it is one, and sometimes the other.
Every time a proposal comes forward the
whole question has to be examined in the
light of the particular circumstances. Let
us now see what will happen if this particular minimum fine is inserted? I pass over
for the moment the suggestion that without
the insertion of the sub-section I am trying
to delete the Ministry will be left without any
power other than the power of revoking the
licence. It seems to me that sub-Section (2)
of this Section would give them power to
deal with breaches of the provisions in their
Rules under the old penalties imposed by sub-
Section (3) of Section 8 of the principal Act.
But now let us see to what offences this
minimum fine would become applicable. It
would become applicable to every offence
which is found in the Marketing of Eggs
Act, 1924, as well as every offence which is
specified in the new Bill-the Bill now before
We shall find on looking into it that
the offences are of a very varied character,
and of very various degrees of gravity.
us.
Coming back to the Act of 1924, it became
law on the 19th May of that year, and it
came into operation on the 1st January,
1925, rather less than two years ago. We
find that the first offence it is possible to
commit is to carry on business as a wholesale
dealer without a licence, and also to deal
by way of wholesale purchase with any
wholesale dealer not so licensed We also
find that any person fraudulently altering or
using or permitting to be fraudulently altered
or used any licence or certified copy of a
licence shall be guilty of an offence. I think
it is plainly obvious that the offences under
sub-Section (4) of Section 1 of the earlier
Act are offences that are capable of almost
infinite gradation.
Passing on to the next section of the older
Act we find that the owner or occupier of
premises is required to register with the
Ministry, and to keep a record and to furnish
to the Ministry returns of various kinds, and
to hand those returns over for inspection by
the Ministry; and the person who wilfully
makes default, or who is guilty of sending in
a false statement, is again guilty of an
offence against the Act. Here again it
seems to me that the offences are capable of
great gradation.
Then we go on to the next Section, which
provides for persons dealing in preserved
eggs, and so on. It states that all packages
containing such eggs shall be branded with
99 66 Cold-Stored
the words "Preserved Eggs,"
Eggs," or "Pickled Eggs," and that the
letters of which the brand is composed shall
be capital block letters not less than two
inches square and distinctly legible. If those
letters are not two inches square, an offence
against the Act is committed, and the person
guilty is liable to a minimum fine of one
pound irrespective of his explanation.
Then towards the end of Section 3 we come
to the sub-section which deals with the question of warranty and with one of the Sections
of the Food and Drugs Act of 1899, which
provides that a person guilty of an offence
under that Section is liable to a penalty
not exceeding five pounds. What is going
to happen to that if we put on a minimum
fine here? I confess I am not very certain,
but at all events we shall be interfering
with it in our Bill, which we endeavour to
connect with the sale of food and drugs. The
whole tendency has been to make ordinary
rules under the Food and Drugs Acts, which
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
were passed to ensure that persons get the
food they pay for, but here we are going off
on lines of our own instead of adhering to
the general purport of that Act.
Again, further down in the older Act we
refer to a Section of the Public Health Act
about unsound meat, and say that that Act
shall be deemed to be in force in relation
to the sale of eggs. Now, in that Act there
are no minimum fines. There are penalties
not exceeding this and that, but no minimum fines. Still further on we have the
usual power to enter premises and so on,
and the minimum penalty is a pound, but
in that case one pound would be a very
lenient penalty indeed. Then there is the
making of rules, but in connection with that
there is no penalty under the older law.
Passing then from the Act to the Bill
before us, we see that a set of new offences
is introduced. We propose to require that
every licensed wholesale dealer shall notify
to the Ministry the name and address of
each person to whom a certified copy of the
licence is issued. This has to be done within
a prescribed time, and if it is not done
within that prescribed time, the dealer is
guilty of an offence against the Act, and the
minimum fine of one pound is imposed,
irrespective of the circumstances. We go on
to say more about the keeping of records
and such returns as may be prescribed, and
if that regulation is not complied with, the
minimum fine again comes into operation.
The next Clause, Clause 4, to which preceding speakers have made reference, provides that the Ministry may make rules
providing that persons purchasing eggs for
re-sale by retail, shall comply with certain
conditions laid down. If those rules are
made and not complied with, the minimum
fine will apply, and it is really rather an
extraordinary proceeding to fix a minimum
fine for an offence against rules which are
not yet made.
Then we go on to people selling or offering
for sale, eggs which are stale, and so on.
That is an offence which is clearly on the
lines of those against which the Food and
Drugs Act was directed, and we are putting
in there a minimum fine which may be very
much below the fine which ought to be imposed under the Food and Drugs Act. We
are doing it and the Food and Drugs Act
does not do it.
Then, again, we have the Clause about
obstruction or impeding an officer, and
another Clause making it an offence to purchase hen eggs except by weight. If anybody does purchase hen eggs except by
weight, the minimum fine again comes into
operation. It seems to me that no case has
been made out for departing from the
usage and practice of centuries. I do not
think, in fact, that we can say that on the
whole a case has been made out for bring
ing in a minimum fine for the infringment
set out here. The practice is almost universal not to have a minimum fine, but only
maximum fines.
There are instances under the Statutes of
cases where minimum fines are imposed.
My attention was called the other day to the
Colorado Beetle Act, as it was called, and
my mind was taken back to the days when
that menace was very much before the
British public. I remember well the pictures I saw in the illustrated papers, on
the panoramas, and so on, and that it was
used as a bogey to frighten children. That
was in 1877, and the Act that was passed
that year gave Her Majesty power, by Order
in Council, to extend the provisions of that
Act, and prescribed penalties of ten pounds
in various cases. I believe that that provision has been found of great benefit in
later years. That was an exception, however.
There are other exceptions in regard to
minimum fines. There is one on the Statute
Book, a fairly recent one, the Railway Act
of 1868, which is only applicable to railway
companies. Under that Act a railway
company may be fined not less than £200
and not more than £500, and the offence
for which that penalty may be imposed is
that of running a special train or of stopping a train for the convenience of those
who desire to attend prize fights. That
Statute is still in force.
There are older examples, of course.
There is an Irish Statute of 1695, imposing
a fine of one shilling for cursing or swearing
if the offender is a labourer, and two
shillings if he occupies a higher station in
life, and there is also the case of a butcher,
who arrives at an inn on a Sunday, for
which he has to pay five shillings. That is
the sort of thing that used to be dealt with
by a minimum fine, but I do not think we
should desire to add to such examples. I
suggest that in our legislative proceedings
we should exercise the same patience in our
desire to produce a new heaven and a new
earth in the poultry yard, as I am sure the
officers of the Department do when they are
brought into personal contact with those
engaged in the industry. I am sorry, as I
have stated, in view of the fact that this
matter has been before us more than once,
and is likely to be before us again, to trouble
the Committee with this Amendment, but
I cannot believe myself that it is desirable
to insert this sub-Clause into the Bill.
Therefore, I beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I, unfortunately, was not able to be present on the
16 NOVEMBER 1926
Marketing of Eggs Bill-

Committee.
tioned in this Bill, has worked very satisfactorily, and there have been no complaints.
On this question and on the question to
come before the House to-morrow I am in
favour of minimum fines, but I am entirely
opposed to the rates of minimum fines imposed in the Motor Bill.
Second Reading of this Bill, and, therefore,
I would just like to say a few words on it.
The hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie) who sits behind
me and I do not hold the same views at all
on the question of the minimum fine. There
uay be something in the latter part of his
remarks which were rather hard to follow,
but if it is his contention that this minimum
fine might apply to other things than are
mentioned in this Bill it might be a question
for consideration, because it might apply to
things to which it was not intended by the
framers of this Bill that minimum fines
should apply. However, it is a matter for
the Government to consider whether there is
anything in that or not.
But to go to the point germane of his
speech-which I believe he has made before,
and I hope he will consider that he has
inade the most of it when we come to it
the one
again to-morrow-namely, that
reason and the only reason for the mention
of minimum fines is, I regret to say, the
difference between the formation of benches
of magistrates in recent years and what
they used to be. I think that the figures
that the Leader of the House gave on the
Motor Traffic Bill of the perfectly miserable
and injudicious fines inflicted by magistrates
show that there are many benches of magistrates who do not do justice in the sentences
which they give. All this is leading to a
thing which is a burning question in many
cases, whether it would not be better to do
away with magistrates altogether. Magistrates used to be appointed on account of
their proper capacity for judicial duties. 1
am afraid nowadays the judicial part of their
appointment is not considered at all, but that
it simply has become a reward for giving
a vote for somebody, or some particular
reward you want to give to a man.
Another point is that benches are of such
a size now. When two or three magistrates
used to deal with a case there was generally
Justice done, but when fifteen or sixteen
magistrates attend on the bench human
nature comes in, and the bench is more or
less packed before the case is heard at all.
I believe myself that there are many magistrates who will gladly welcome the minimum
fine. We are human, and, of course, if your
neighbour is brought up you do not like to
punish him, whereas if there was a minimum fine a magistrate could go home and
say to his neighbour: "I am very sorry you
had to be fined so much, because we had to
do it under the law."
One way in which to my own knowledge a
minimum fine works very well indeed is in
the case of those who do not take out a
licence or who use a licence wrongly. There
is a minimum fine inflicted on them, which,
of course, the county council can reduce on
its merits. That fine, which is a very heavy
fine, and not at all like the small fine men-
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I thought it
was somewhat invidious of the right hon.
and gallant Gentleman (Colonel Sharman
Crawford) to look over at one Lord
Lieutenant, my noble Friend (Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard) on my left, and at
another Vice Lieutenant, who has the honour
to be Leader of this House. and to suggest
that the magistrates who are selected nowadays could under any circumstances be deterred by any kind of minimum fine or
maximum fine from doing their duty.
(Laughter.) However, I think I had better
pass that over, as it is a personal matter,
and not in question at the present moment.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: The appointment of magistrates is not left altogether to the Lords Lieutenant. It has been
more or less taken out of their hands.
a
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: That is
graceful compliment. The hon. Senator
Leslie in speaking on this matter of minimum fines the other day, rather resented
a charge of incapacity which was being made
by another hon. Senator. He resented the
suggestion that some magistrates might
not be trusted to impose the proper penalty, and he suggested to-day that sooner
than impose a penalty of which they did not
approve they might let off an obviously
guilty person. I think that is making a
charge against the magistracy which is a
somewhat regrettable thing to do. To come
to the actual point, the Government are
by no means wedded to this principle of a
minimum or fixed fine from this day forward.
They will only ask Parliament to sanction
the imposition of a minimum fine when the
circumstances of the legislation render it
clearly desirable.
I am afraid that by a good many people
in Ulster offences under the present Bill,
when it becomes an Act, might be regarded
as not very important. Offences against
the community are usually not looked upon
in the same light as offences against the individual. It is generally supposed to be a
less heavy kind of offence. It is for the
reason that the health of the community
must be safeguarded that the Government
have thought it necessary that minimum
fines should be imposed in this particular
piece of legislation. The question of
the minimum fine will doubtless reappear,
but I think that the Government have made
out a case for applying it in the present
cireumstances.
427 Marketing of Eggs Bill.
Protection of Linen Industry. 428
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I welcome the assertion
which has fallen from the noble Viscount
that the Government is not wedded to this
principle of minimum fines. I venture
to hope we are not going to meet them
very often again. I cannot help thinking
that perhaps the noble Viscount advances
a
little towards the trap I was trying
to indicate when he said that I was suggesting that the magistrates would let off
people who were obviously guilty. One of
my main points is that you cannot pre-judge
a case. It is only the magistrates who can
decide. What I was endeavouring to say was
that if a magistrate did not wish to impose a
minimum penalty he would be more prone
than would otherwise be the case to examine
the evidence from the point of making sure
that he was driven to a conviction by the
force of evidence, and that he would also
consider First Offenders Acts and matters
of that kind.
Passing from what the noble Viscount
said, I would venture to say that I was
surprised to hear the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman (Colonel Sharman Crawford)
who sits in front of me start off by saying he
did not hold my views, and to find as his
speech went on, how much he and I were
really in agreement. As regards the statement that some magistrates would welcome
a minimum fine, I ought perhaps to explain,
as the right hon. and gallant Gentleman
was not with us last week, that when spraking on a somewhat similar subject I was
very careful to differentiate Revenue cases
from the class to which the right hon. and
gallant Gentleman referred as being a class
of cases which stand wholly by themselves.
I said that for the protection of Revenue
minimum fines have always been imposed.
I can best sum up the argument against
him by quoting an old saying, and that is
that two wrongs do not make one right. However, I know perfectly well that this Bill
when it becomes an Act will not be harshly
administered. The noble Viscount bas indicated that the Government is in favour of
minimum fines, and as the opposition to
minimum fines has not found, any other supporter I think it will save trouble if I ask
leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

pass.-
And a Message was sent to the House of

agree to the said Bill, with some Amendments, to which they desire the concurrence
of the Commons.
The Linen Industry-Granting of
Protection.
The following question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Barclay:
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government will make representations
to the Imperial Government with a view to
the appointment of a Committee under the
Safeguarding of Industries Act, to inquire
into the necessity of granting some protection to the linen industry.
Mr. BARCLAY: I beg to ask the noble
Leader of the House this question on the
Order Paper in my name. I feel that something should be done regarding this very
important industry which we have in
Northern Ireland. As there has been something done for other industries in Great
Britain, and also in Northern Ireland, I
wondered if something of the same nature
could not be done for this very important
industry. This industry, like others, has
been greatly exposed to unfair competition.
Under the Safeguarding of Industries Act,
a Committee of Inquiry has been set up,
and they have found it necessary that these
other industries should be protected. The
industries which I refer to are the motor
industry, artificial silk industry, watch and
Protection of Linen Industry.
Adjournment.
16 NOVEMBER 1926
clock industry, and so on, and we find that
the protection which they have got has been
Several silk
of great benefit to them.
factories have been built in England, and
we in Northern Ireland have benefited to a
certain extent by one being opened up in
Ballymena.
We have also benefited to a certain extent
by the protection given to the motor industry. We can build motor cars in the
city of Belfast, and we can also build omnibuses as good as those that can be built in
any part of the world. I come back to this
linen industry. We are told by the Press
that we cannot compete with the foreigner,
that our market is open to the foreigner to
dump in his surplus production and compete with the home manufacturer. Practically all foreign nations that manufacture
linen are protected and have high tariff
walls built up against the industry of Belfast,
with the result that behind those tariff
walls they can manufacture goods for their
own home market at less than we can produce and sell them in that market. Behind
those tariff walls of theirs, as I said before,
they can produce goods by working longer
hours and paying less wages, and so put
those goods upon the home market at something like 40 per cent. to 100 per cent. cheaper
than we can manufacture them in the city
of Belfast and put them on the market.
Owing to our having an open port those
people can manufacture a surplus at this
cheap rate, and this surplus production is
dumped into our markets, I am told. almost
as cheap as our manufacturers can buy the
raw material.
Of course, when it appears in the shop
window we see it, and we buy it, and while
we buy this stuff and wear it and tear it
we are at the same time employing foreign
labour, we are paying a good dividend on
foreign capital, and we are also increasing
foreign capital. We are also allowing our
own men and women to be on the Labour
Exchanges getting what is practically a
starvation allowance there. We are allowing
these great buildings of brick and mortar
to decay and the machinery within those
buildings to rust and waste. We are allowing
all this because somebody-I do not know
who it is-will not take a proper stand and
see that our industry gets proper protection.
I brought this question forward to-day to
see if it would not be possible that under
this Act our linen industry should get the
protection that is needed. If it is possible,
I would earnestly ask the Government to
take steps at the earliest possible moment
to cause this inquiry to be made, so that
this industry may have this protection.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I fear that
there may be some slight misunderstanding
in the mind of the hon. Senator as to the
conditions under which applications for the
safeguarding of a particular industry are
made to the Imperial Authorities. These
conditions have been embodied in a White
Paper which sets forth in detail the procedure that must be adopted, and specifies certain rules to be applied by the Board of
Trade in deciding whether a prima facie
case for the setting up of a Committee of
Inquiry has been established.
As the hon. Senator will doubtless appreciate, the application must necessarily have
reference to the whole of the United Kingdom, since any measure of safeguarding
would naturally affect the entire area.
The activities of the Ministry of Commerce
in safeguarding questions lie mainly in the
direction of collecting and collating data for
the use of any industry which proposes to
submit an application to the Board of Trade.
Mr. BARCLAY: Thank you, my Lord.
Adjournment.
Moved: "That this House do now adjourn."
-(Viscount Charlemont.
Mr. LESLIE: I take the opportunity which
the rules of the House and the rules
of debate prevented me taking when
the noble Viscount, the Parliamentary
Secretary, was giving information in respect
of the possibility of administering anæsthetics to horses of a certain age before
subjecting them to a certain operation, of
thanking the noble Viscount for looking into
the matter. I may say that I am rather
in agreement with what was said. I think
that very often the pain of such an operation
is attributable rather to fright than to
physical suffering, and even if a local
anæsthetic could not be injected more perhaps could be done by curative methods after
the operation. I am sure we may take it,
from what the noble Viscount has said, that
it is the intention of the Department to
see that all possible precautions are taken,
and that these matters are dealt with by
really competent persons.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD indicated assent.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Half after Four o'clock.
431 Motor Vehicles Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 17th November, 1926.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Motor Vehicles (Traffic and
Regulation) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): Before moving formally that
this House do resolve itself into Committee
on the said Bill I should like to be allowed
to say that this House is grateful to the
Minister of Home Affairs for kindly
consenting to come and take charge of
the Bill through the Committee stage.
It is a highly technical Measure, and the
Minister, with his well-known lucidity
of argument, will be able to explain the
Clauses in a more satisfactory manner to this
House than I could myself. I beg to move
that this House do now resolve itself into
Committee on the said Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Reckless or wanton driving).
(1) If any person drives a motor car on
a public highway recklessly or wantonly,
or at a speed, or in a manner which is dangerous to the public, having regard to all
the circumstances of the case, that person
shall be liable on summary conviction to a
fine of one pound or any greater sum not
exceeding ten pounds, or in the case of a
second conviction to a fine of two pounds
or any greater sum not exceeding twenty
pounds, or, in the case of a third or subsequent conviction, to a fine of five pounds,
or any greater sum not exceeding fifty
pounds, or, in the discretion of the court,
to imprisonment for a period not exceeding
three months. or to both such fine and
imprisonment.
(2) Any police constable may apprehend
without warrant the driver of any car who
commits an offence under this section
within his view, if such driver refuses to
give his name and address or produce his
licence on demand, or if the motor car does
not bear the identification mark or sign
required in pursuance of the Roads Act,
1920.
(3) If the driver of any motor car who
commits an offence under this section refuses to give his name or address, or gives
a false name or address, he shall be guilty
of an offence under this Act, and it shall
be the duty of the owner of the car, if required, to give any information which it
is within his power to give, and which may
lead to the identification and apprehension
of the driver, and if the owner fails to do
so he also shall be guilty of an offence
under this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: Before entering on the
first Amendment standing in my name on
the Paper I trust I may be allowed to associate myself, as the saying goes, with what
was said by the noble Viscount, the Leader
of the House. in welcoming our
Friend, the right hon. Gentleman the
Minister of Home Affairs. We are, I am
sure, always glad to see the right hon.
Gentleman in this House, and, if I may
say so, we are particularly glad to see him
on this occasion, because we know that in
the preparation of the Bill before us and in
the guiding of its passage in another place
he has bestowed most unremitting care and
attention on the subject before us and he
has done all he could to meet the objections
which naturally are aroused by a Measure
of this character.
Passing now to the Amendment before us
1 beg to move, page 1, line 9, after "highway" ("a public highway recklessly or") to
to insert "either." The reason I make that
suggestion is because I want it made perfectly plain that if a man is charged under
this sub-section he shall know exactly what
it is with which he is charged, that is to
say, whether the charge against him is recklessness in speed or manner and so on. As
I understand the matter in England and
Scotland under the existing Act of 1903 it
is necessary for the prosecuting authority,
so to speak, to choose the horse on which
he proposes to declare to win. In Ireland
I understand there has been a decision given
in the courts before 1920 which raises some
difficulty in that way, and I further made
out from the printed reports of the proceedings in another place that there appeared
to be some discrepancy between what was
said by the right hon. Gentleman the
Minister of Home Affairs and the right hon.
Gentleman the Attorney-General. It could
only be an apparent discrepancy, but I
think we are entitled to ask whether the intention is that the offences under the subsection are to be separate. If they are we
shall gain a great deal by the shortening of
the summonses.
At present when a man is summoned
under that section of the 1903 Act the summons covers about as much space as the subsection itself in this page. I should like
17 NOVEMBER 1926
Motor Vehicles Bill-

Committee.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: There is just a
question I would like to ask the Minister
of Home Affairs whom we are pleased to
see here to-day. What is the definition of
"wantonly driving" a motor-car?
to get rid of that, and I should like it to be
perfectly plain that when a man is charged
he is charged with one offence and that he
should know which offence it is that is
charged against him. For that purpose I suggest putting in the word "either" which
I think would do it. I do not very much like
using "either " except in connection with
which of two, which is the more ordinary
use of the word. But there are plenty of
examples of either" followed by more than
one alternative. I do not think I need say
any more. I beg to move.

The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS (Sir
Dawson Bates): I would first of all like to
thank the noble Leader of this House and
the Senators for permitting me to come here
this afternoon. I should like particularly
to thank the learned Senator (Mr. Leslie)
for the very kind references he has made to
myself.
Now the Bill of course, as mentioned, is of
a somewhat complicated nature, and the
Government have taken the greatest possible
pains to try to make it as perfect as possible.
I do not believe there ever was a Bill perfect.
Taking that view the Government from the
very first sought the opinions of everyone,
and accepted many Amendments in the
Lower House which we believe have improved the Measure. But the hon. and
learned Senator in moving this Amendment
must have overlooked the fact that in the
Lower House as the Bill was originally
drafted the two main offences of reckless
driving and drunkenness were grouped together under one section. As the result of
representations the Government came to the
conclusion that it would be very much more
satisfactory for the very reason to which my
learned Friend has referred to divide that
first Clause into two, so that there would be
separate and distinct offences-one in regard
to reckless and wanton driving and a
separate offence in regard to drunkenness.
One great advantage of that is to meet the
very point my hon. and learned Friend referred to, that is, the length of the summons.
As a matter of fact in future it would be
quite open to the police when they are issuing a summons to refer to the offences constituted under the particular section, that
is Section 1, Sub-section (1) as a breach of
Section 1, Sub-section (1) of the Motor
Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Bill. I
understand that there is a conflict between
my hon. and learned Friend and the Parliamentary draftsman as to which is the best
form, and having regard to his own
admission that it is not an improvement
from the point of view of draftsmanship,
perhaps with this explanation my hon. and
learned Friend will withdraw his Amendment.
The CHAIRMAN: I do not think that
arises under this Amendment. It will come
under the next Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I certainly do not desire to
press this Amendment on the right hon.
Gentleman, but he has not quite answered
my question. It was not so much a matter
of drafting. What I want to know is this:
Is the whole thing from "recklessly" down
to case taken as one offence, or are we
going to separate them.
"
""
Sir DAWSON BATES: One offence.
Mr. LESLIE: You want to charge the
whole lot, I think, from what the right hon.
Gentleman has said?
Mr. DICKSON: I just wanted to ask the
Minister does the word "motor-car" in the
first line in Clause 1 include a motor cycle
or motor lorry or any other vehicle, and
why is "motor-car" used instead of "motor
vehicle."
The
CHAIRMAN: I do not think that
either comes under this Amendment. It
would be better to raise it on the Question
that Clause 1 stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the right hon.
Gentleman has really made it clear what is
meant, and I ask leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
"
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, page 1, line 9,
to leave out or wantonly." The words, as
we know, were inserted in another place in
substitution for the words "or negligently,"
and I am sure everybody would agree that
having got rid of the words "or negligently"
must be an improvement. But it seems to
me that the improvement did not go as far
as it might. I cannot see myself at the
moment any objection to leaving these
two words or wantonly" out. Like the
hon. Baronet, I do not know what they
mean. The word "wantonly is used
possibly as meaning nothing more than playfully, as Lovelace wrote, "the birds who
wanton in the air." Then from that you
get from playfulness to mischief, but you
always stop short of maliciously.
However, I do not know that the word
very much matters, as used in literature.
The important thing is the use of the word
in Acts of Parliament. I have traced it in
two Acts. You find the word "wantonly"
in an Act of 1847 in connection with the
discharge of firearms or fireworks or the
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
lighting of bonfires in the public streets,
and you find it again in a later sub-section
of the same Measure in exactly what I
might describe as a typical instance applied
to the ringing of door bells to the annoyance of the inhabitants. That, to my mind,
is exactly what wantonly" does mean. We
get it again in the general Prevention of
Cruelty to Animals Act, where you
"wantonly and unnecessarily cause suffering." That would seem to be an example
of the use of the word as something more
than playful and something less
than
malicious, something like taking a cat into
a cupboard to rub its fur and see the
sparks fly. It might mean as little as that,
or it might mean more. I think it is very
difficult to apply playfulness or mischievousness to driving a motor-car, and, therefore,
I do not think the Bill will be any the worse
if these words are taken out. At the same
time I am not to be taken as for one moment
suggesting that the word "negligently" should
be restored, but the point is, do we really
want these words, and, if so, what do they
mean.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I cannot pretend
to be the authority my hon. and learned
Friend is on the subject of the definition of
words, but before I came to the House I took
the precaution to consult one of the leading
dictionaries, and I have the definition of the
word "wanton" and also of the word
"reckless." The definition of the word
"wanton" is,
Undisciplined; indulging the natural impulses or appetites without restraint; free
from moral control.
The definition of "reckless" is,
Heedless of consequences; rash; negligent;
indifferent; regardless; unconcerned; inattentive.
I am sure the hon. Senator will appreciate
that according to these definitions the two
offences are of a different character.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: I think it is a pity
the Bill should contain a word nobody
seems to easily understand. I think the purpose of the Clause is served by the retention
of the word "reckless," and I support the
Amendment that the word "wantonly" should
be left out.
I
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
rather agree with the proposer of the Amendment. I think "reckless" is the pioper word
to have in the Clause, because the word
"wantonly" can be used in a very wrong sense.
I should think reckless driving includes
wanton driving. If there is any doubt about
the matter I think it would be better to have
it made clear.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think that what
was in the mind of the Lower House when
this word was inserted as an Amendment
was to make it absolutely clear that what
we aimed at was to get at the individual who
deliberately and without regard to the possible consequences took risks. Take the case
of a man who, although he might be an excellent driver, swings round a blind corner
at such a speed that he has to keep on the
wrong side of the white line. That driver
does that deliberately and recklessly. Then
take the case of another man who drives
along talking to a companion in the car.
He is absolutely indifferent to his surroundings and is not paying any attention. He is
not like the other individual who takes
deliberate risks.
According to the definition he is indifferent,
whereas the other man is not indifferent but
deliberately takes a risk with his eyes open.
The man who is indifferent probably does not
look round and refuses to put his hand out
to indicate that he intends to go round a
corner. It is difficult at the moment to quote
a clear example, but by the use of the word
"wantonly" we want to try to convey something beyond ordinary negligence. In the one
case the man is doing something he knows is
wrong, and in the other case the man is indifferent, although, he has a most dangerous
machine under his control. That is the man
the word "wantonly” is intended to apply to,
Mr. PORTER PORTER: I oppose the
Amendment for the simple reason that I
would use every word in the dictionary if it
would help the magistrates to convict a person who drives either recklessly or wantonly.
For that reason I think the words should be
left in the Clauso
Mr. LESLIE: The difficulty I am in over
this Bill seems to me to be most admirably
illustrated by the course of the debate. If
we introduce this word "wantonly" here it
will have to be construed by the courts. Nobody
seems to know what it means. This Clause
is a matter of summary jurisdiction, and the
courts will certainly have to construe it, and
how they will do it I do not know. I cannot
see that we gain anything by the insertion
of these two words, and I appeal to the right
hon. Gentleman to leave them out. It will
simplify things and he will not be giving
anything away. We agree in the case of the
man going round the corner that that is
recklessness, and I almost think that the
other case is driving to the danger of the
public. If a man is not looking where he is
going and is carrying on a conversation with
a companion in the car I should say he was
driving to the danger of the public. I do
suggest the right hon. Gentleman should
meet us by withdrawing these two words
from the Clause.
17 NOVEMBER 1926
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.

The Committee divided: Contents, 5; Not
Contents, 10.
Question put, "That these words be left
out."

Mr. LESLIE: The next Amendment standing in my name deals with a matter that
has been before us more than once of late.
It is in Clause 1, page 1, line 12, to leave
out from the word "fine" (fine of one pound)
to the word "not" (not exceeding ten
pounds) in line 13. I said yesterday when
speaking on the subject of the minimum fine
that one of the objections to it was that
when it made its appearance it had to be
dealt with on somewhat different lines according to the circumstances of the case.
I know perfectly well that the right hon.
Gentleman has a very strong opinion on the
subject of minimum fines. He knows, I
think, that I also have a strong opinion,
but one has to recognise that in this legislature there are two Chambers and it is
not always the duty of one who sits in the
Second Chamber to unduly press his personal views. Therefore, I think it would be
a mistake to insert the minimum fine here,
not that I am in any way anxious that
those who drive dangerously should get off
lightly. I am not. I think dangerous
driving, if it really is dangerous driving,
should be most rigorously repressed. We
are all agreed on that, particularly those
who go about in motor-cars.

While the fine is discretionary it is possible for the magistrates to inflict a small
fine, very often with the remark, I hope
it will be a warning to you," and very often
it is a warning to the offender. There is that
possibility. Then there is the other difficulty I mentioned yesterday. If a fixed fine
is to be inflicted the magistrate will look
round more and more before he convicts a
man. Let us take the case of two men
driving in company in a manner dangerous
to the public in a street in the town of
Antrim. They are both stopped and prosecuted, and they are both convicted. They
have to be fined one pound, because unless
the circumstances were exceptionally bad
the magistrates would not go beyond the
legal limit. They have both to pay the
costs of the summons which I think is now
about 4s.
One of them who may live in Antrim has
to pay £1 4s, and the other who may live
in Bangor may have to pay extra costs
Mr. J. Porter-Porter.
Colonel T. Sinclair.
amounting to 15s. It has occurred to me
that, perhaps, the right hon. Gentleman,
who has given way a good deal, might be
prepared to give us not only in regard to
this Clause but also in regard to others, a
new Clause on the Report stage saying that
where a minimum fine is fixed in this Bill
the costs should be taken into consideration in that fine. The magistrate is not
bound to the one pound fine, and if it is
a bad case I hope he will go beyond the one
pound. If it can be provided that in future
the fine should include the costs I think
we should avoid some injustices and some
inequalities which would otherwise result.
Without laying further stress on the question I put that to the right hon. Gentleman
in moving the Amendment. I think the
costs might be included in the fine at the
discretion of the magistrate.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: 1 unfortunately have got to make a few remarks
on this subject, though I thought the hon.
Senator (Mr. Leslie) and I had a sort of
arrangement that he had said all he wanted
to say on the subject yesterday. The only
fault I have to find with this Clause as
amended from the original Bill is that,
though at first I thought the fine a bit too
high. I now think the fine is not high
enough. That is my only objection to the
alteration from the original Bill. After all
it was not on the general subject of minimum
fines that I spoke yesterday but on this
particular motor traffic that has grown up
in such a recent period. It is in that connection that the penalties have not been.
in the opinion of most people, adequate to
the offences So far as the point about warning offenders is concerned I think it has no
effect at all, particularly on a young man.
Sometimes it does do good, but in a great
many cases they take it as a sign of weakness, and we find the same man repeating
the offence immediately afterwards. It is
far better to nip the thing in the bud at
once. As this Committee knows very well
I am strongly in favour of a minimum fine
in this Bill, and I, therefore, propose to
vote against the Amendment.
Sir DAWSON BATES: If this Amendment
were accepted it would cut across the whole
principle laid down in this Bill. I would remind the Committee that last year there
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Sir Dawson Bates]
were no less than 759 persons injured, and
no less than 59 persons killed in the same
period as a result of motor accidents. The
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) who
moved the Amendment suggested that if
this minimum penalty were inserted in the
Bill it would not give an opportunity to
the magistrates of warning young offenders.
Unfortunately our experience goes to show
that warning is no good at all to the
ordinary road hog. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
I have taken out some figures, because 1
think that in a case like this figures are
perhaps of more effect than anything else.
I find that out of 574 convictions for motoring offences during a period of twelve months
in no less than 25 cases were there only 1s.
fines imposed, while in 160 other cases fines
of 5s or less were imposed, and in 160
further cases the fines did not exceed 10s.
With regard to the point of warning young
offenders, I find that for the month of
October-that is last October-in cases for
excessive speeding out of 90 offences in only
twelve cases did the fines exceed 10s., while
in the majority of the cases the penalties
were 5s. or under. ln one case a man from
Downpatrick was fined on no less than three
occasions in that particular month, and the
magistrates evidently believed in the grading
system for on the first occasion in the month
he was fined 2s. 6d., on the next occasion
he was fined 5s., and on the third occasion
he was fined 10s., so that perhaps by next
month it will be graded up to £1 for the
sixth offence at that rate of going.
In another case a man from Ballynahinch
was fined three times during the month,
while a third man in County Down--
County Down seems to be pretty bad-
(laughter)—was fined on five occasions
during the same month-five occasions in
one month-the fines being 5s and under. 1
do suggest that so long as we have the present system it is absolutely necessary, unless
this Act is going to become a dead letter,
that there should be a minimum penalty. I
minimum
agree that the principle of a
fine is one that should only be exercised
with great care, but in this particular
case when we have something like 30,000
vehicles on the roads to-day in Northern
Ireland-the number has been trebled during
the last three years, and next year it may
be 40,000-we must do something I suggest
to prevent this terrible loss of life and injury-(hon. Members: Hear, hear)--and I
believe that the only way by which we can
do it is by having a minimum penalty.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I certainly
am strongly in favour of something being
done to deal with those who disregard
the safety of passengers or the safety
of pedestrians. A great deal has been
said about the attitude of the magistrates,
that they probably do not deal as fairly or
as justly as they should when they are
inflicting fines, and it must be admitted that
putting on a minimum fine certainly does
show a want of confidence in the magistrates who are dealing with these cases.
But there are a considerable number of
trivial cases likely to be brought up under
the administration of this Act, and it will
depend a good deal upon the temper of the
police constable or the police officer whether
these cases are brought forward. In the city,
for instance, a pointsman, if it is a pleasant
day and the sun is shining, is in a good temper. He will probably hold up a man who has
violated his directions, give him a caution
and let him pass; but if it happens to be
a very dirty miserable day and the policeman is in a bad temper he will probably
hold that man up, and if he does not happen
just to have seen his signal he will take his
name and address and take him before the
court.
There may be dozens of cases like that.
I did take notice one day at one of our
points, and I saw a policeman who had
been speaking to an officer alongside him
holding up his hand as a signal to the traffic
in a certain direction to stop. A cyclist
coming along had not exactly understood
the signal given and immediately he came
forward to the police officer the officer
stopped him and spoke to him. He was, I
suppose, arguing with him, but in a moment
the police officer said "Over there." Now it
was not the offence that had taken place
but whatever had occurred in the course of
the conversation that resulted in that, but
he ordered the cyclist over to the corner
to get his name and address, and I am sure
it was only a trivial offence. Under this
Bill such a trivial offence would mean a
pound to that man.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Might I clear up
that point? The minimum penalties only
apply for four offences under this Bill,
namely, recklessly or wantonly driving,
drunkenness, where a man whose licence has
been suspended by the court deliberately
goes out and drives a car, and, fourthly,
to the case of a public vehicle where the
speed limit is exceeded. The case to which
the hon. Senator (Mr. Joseph Cunningham)
refers is a trivial case and would be dealt
with as such. There is no minimum penalty
attached to that class of case.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It is not
treated as reckless driving?
Sir DAWSON BATES: No.
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman
(Sir Dawson Bates) did not say whether he
441 Motor Vehicles Bill-
17 NOVEMBER 1926

Committee
arwould consider the question I put to him
about the costs or not, whether some
rangement could not be come to whereby a
man, as I say, in County Antrim would not
have an unfair advantage over another man
living in Bangor or some other place in
County Down.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I do not quite
follow the point of my hon. and learned
Friend.
Mr. LESLIE :
I am sorry. What I
meant was that the Government should give
us on Report a general Clause providing that
in cases where the minimum penalties were
imposed under this Act the costs of court
and of service might be reckoned by the
magistrates as part of the fine. It would
have to be wrapped up in better language, of
course, but that is the idea I have in my
mind. It seems to me that that would very
possibly avoid a certain amount of hardship.
I know, of course, the right hon. Gentleman
wants to protect the public.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I suggest to my
hon. and learned Friend that it would be a
mistake to mix up the costs and the fine.
After all, cases may occur where the magistrates come to a decision not to inflict any
costs, and I think it would be very undesirable to mix the two things up. I cannot
quite follow the suggestion that a gentleman
in County Antrim would be in any better
position than another gentleman in Bangor.
Mr. LESLIE: The point is that the
offence occurs in County Antrim; one man
resides in County Antrim, say in the petty
sessions district, and is summoned to that
court; the other man lives in County Down,
and the summons has to be served on him
in County Down, so that, apart from the
extra railway travelling, there are extra
costs. If extra costs are incurred in serving
the summons they may run to fifteen or
sixteen shillings against that man from
County Down. Assuming that he is only
fined one pound he will be fined one pound
plus four shillings ordinary costs and fifteen
The
or sixteen shillings for extra costs.
magistrates have power, of course, to refuse
to allow costs, but they will have to be paid
by somebody, and I think it is not unfair
when you are imposing a minimum penalty
to take these things into consideration. If
the costs are included in such a fine and the
magistrates think a fine of one pound is not
enough then let them impose a fine of two
pounds. All cases of reckless driving are not
the same.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I thought my hon.
and learned Friend´objected to the principle
of a minimum fine.
Mr. LESLIE: So I do.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I rather suggest
to him that his suggestion is going to fetter
the magistrates in their discretion still
more. My hon. and learned Friend admits
that the magistrates have full power in regard to costs, and I really do not think that
any great hardship is going to be inflicted.
It is unfortunate that such a man should go
away for a joy ride out of his own county
and commit an offence amongst people who
are not his neighbours; but really I think
it is his own fault.
Mr. LESLIE: I ask leave to withdraw my
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendments stood on the

Page 1, line 14, leave out from "fine" to

Page 1, line 16, leave out from "fine" to

Page 2, lines 4 to 19, leave out sub-Sections
